text: Imperially Approved Four Treasuries Section Twelve of Miscellaneous The Records of the Strange Category Two of Miscellaneous [Strange Tales] Preface We officials respectfully examine that The Records of the Strange consists of fifty volumes, compiled by Hong Mai of the Song dynasty. His other work, Random Notes from the Rongzhai Studio, has already been separately recorded. This book records stories of the supernatural, hence it is named after the *Liezi* and the *Yijian Zhi* (Records of the Strange). According to the *Liezi*, it says that Yu the Great saw something, Bo Yi knew it and named it, and Yijian heard of it and recorded it. It means "precious birds and exotic animals like those in the *Shanhaijing*." Hong Mai recorded matters concerning immortals and ghosts and named the book after the *Liezi*, but this is not its original meaning. However, Zhang Shensu of the Tang dynasty had already used the name *Yijian Lu* (Records of the Strange), so Hong Mai's name also has a basis. Chen Zhensun's *Book Records* mentions The Records of the Strange with 200 volumes divided into 10 sections, another 100 volumes divided into 10 sections, and another 100 volumes divided into 10 sections, and finally 20 volumes divided into 2 sections, totaling 420 volumes. Zhao Yushi's *Record of Returning Guest* also mentions Hong Mai's The Records of the Strange, consisting of 32 volumes with 31 prefaces, none of which are repeated, and each section records the main points of the preface, quite detailed and comprehensive. This version only contains the first 50 volumes, from A to E, with the title simply *Yijian Zhi* and the original preface not included, compared with Zhao Yushi's version.

The text being described contains entries from "支甲" to "支戊," which are not the main or primary collections but are only mentioned in the records of the time. "支丙" is referred to as "支景," possibly to avoid a taboo name, specifically to avoid the name of a great-grandfather. However, in some versions, "丙" is still used instead of "景," suggesting that the change might have been made by the scribes. Hu Yinglin, in his work "Bi Cong" (Collection of Miscellaneous Notes), states that the version he possessed had a hundred volumes. Zhou Lianggong, in his "Shu Ying" (Book Images), mentions that Hu Yinglin's version only contained entries from "支甲" to "三甲." This suggests that the version Zhou describes might be the same as Hu's, but half of it is missing. Zhu Guozhen, in his "Yong Zhuang Xiao Pin" (Small Notes from the Floating Pavilion), did not know that it was one of the sections in the collection and claimed that "Yi Jian Zhi" originally had 420 volumes. The current version, however, has only 51 volumes, which he attributed to the text being too lengthy and disorganized, leading to its reduction. This is a significant error, as Chen Zhensun criticized Ma for misusing his intentions, and his criticism is quite accurate. Zhang Shinan, in his "Fang Huan Ji Wen" (Records of Observations from Official Life), suggested that Ma wanted to write a national history and used this as a way to practice his writing skills, though this explanation seems somewhat evasive. However, the text contains poems and verses that can often be used for reference, and it also includes unusual stories and events that can serve as moral lessons. Not all of it is useless for the heart and mind. As for the category of "novels" or "fiction," it has been recorded historically and does not need to be confined to a particular region. It is not fair to blame Ma alone for the text. This translation was reviewed and published in the 43rd year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign (1778). The chief editors were Ji Yun, Lu Xixiong, and Sun Shiyi, and the chief proofreader was Lu Feichi. ---

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 1 By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Lady of Zhang Xianggong Qian Lüdao, courtesy name Jiazhen, was from Jingzhao Xianyang. During the reign of Emperor Shizong of Jin (Jin Huitong, 1149–1161), he traveled to study in Shang and Guo, passing through Hu County. He was rushing on his journey without stopping, with only one servant accompanying him. As the sky turned dark, he could no longer see the path and let his horse wander. He arrived at a large mansion and knocked on the door to ask for lodging. A concubine came out from inside, surprised and said to him, "This place is near the mountains and has many wolves and tigers. Is it suitable to travel at night?" Qian replied, "Just happened to lose my way unintentionally. Dare to ask for a place to stay. But I don't know whose mansion this is." The concubine said, "It is the residence of Zhang Xianggong, the governor of Hedong Prefecture. The governor has passed away; only the lady is here. Permission must be sought." He then went in to inform her. After a short while, she invited him to meet. The tall and grand buildings were lit up by bright candles, and the cups and dishes were already arranged. The lady's appearance was graceful and beautiful, and her attire was splendid. They began to dine together, and the maidservants' dancing and singing were exquisite, never seen before. Qian felt it was a strange encounter like traveling to the fairy realm. His emotions were stirred, and he didn't know where he belonged. He stood respectfully and did not easily engage in conversation. The others thought he was rustic and foolish and looked at each other, laughing mockingly. After the meal, he went to bed. Soon after, the candles were lit again, and the lady came again. The others surrounded her and helped her onto the bed. Qian hurriedly stepped down to avoid, but she insisted repeatedly. They finally shared the bed. The next morning, they kept him for breakfast. Qian was originally a wandering traveler and was very satisfied. He did not mention leaving for several days. One evening during their joyful drinking, he heard the sound of people calling and guiding outside, saying the governor was coming. The lady quickly got up, and the other concubines all ran around. Qian hid in a dark room, dared not breathe or be afraid, and pretended to sleep for a long time. Foxes howled and crows cawed. The east became bright, and the people and houses had all disappeared. He found himself lying in a thicket of ancient tombs. He came out in a state of distress and met a farmer, finally finding the official road. The clothes still had a lingering fragrance that lasted for a month before fading. A Woman on the Road to Loufan

A villager from Fei Yang堡 in Yi Fang county of Lan Zhou, named Nan Yan, was traveling to Lou Fan county. Along the way, he stopped for a rest and encountered a woman dressed in simple clothes with a high hairstyle, around twenty years old. She bowed and asked, "I am traveling from Lou Fan to Lan Zhou alone and am lost. I don't know the way. Could you please give me directions?" After she gave the directions, she sighed deeply and fell to the ground. Nan Yan helped her up and saw that she was dead. He then went to an inn to seek help, but bystanders, fearing involvement, reported him to the local authorities. He was imprisoned and interrogated, falsely confessing to murder. However, there were no signs of injury on the corpse, and he could not specify what was stolen. The county sent an official named Ming Sheng to investigate. Ming called Nan Yan and asked, "Did you really kill her?" Nan Yan replied, "It is difficult to say." Ming Sheng did not understand and asked again. Nan Yan repeated his answer, leading Ming to suspect that the "difficult to say" referred to Nan Yan himself. Ming then returned to the county and reported to the magistrate, who ordered a new investigation. When the coffin was opened, only a piece of rotten wood was found, leading to Nan Yan's release. It is said that Nan Yan was known for his filial piety and respect in his community, suggesting he was spared due to divine protection. --- In the eastern part of Wucheng, there was a monk named Yuan Hui, a young man from a nearby village. After becoming a monk, he became a wandering monk, indulging in drinking and enjoying socializing. At the age of twenty-five, he fell ill and returned home, where he lay bedridden for a year. Suddenly, he fell unconscious, and his family wept around him. After a short time, he regained consciousness and complained of severe pain in his lower back and tailbone. A doctor named Kong Yan Zhang examined him and discovered a short donkey's tail protruding from his skin. His father, fearing the scandal, quickly covered it up, but the pain worsened. Yuan Hui then removed his clothes to show the donkey's tail, which stopped the pain.

A man grew a foot or so taller each day, and the next day, hair sprouted all over his body. His head and face already resembled a donkey, and after several days, his hooves and mane were all present. His ears stood up straight, and he roared and cried in a sad manner. His four limbs stood on the ground, upright, seemingly a real donkey. The family discussed killing him, but the temple monk said it was not advisable, stating that this was a sign from heaven to warn and punish future wrongdoers. If they killed him, they would be going against the principles of heaven and would be harmful to their household. Therefore, they kept him in the stable without putting a bridle and reins on him. The donkey kept braying and eating, and sometimes chewed on people in a disorderly manner. They tried to put a saddle in front of him, and he stood up in anticipation, as if with a joyful expression. He could carry heavy loads over long distances and could travel two hundred miles in a day. He lived for ten years before dying. In the year of Huangtong, during the reign of the Jin dynasty, the Jurchen army occupied the lands of Qi and Lu, and the county of Fengfu was renamed Taian. For many months, there was no rain. The Han general Liu, who was the governor, prayed to Mount Tai, but it did not respond. He then sacrificed to the Dragon Pond, and the sun became more intense. Liu grew even more angry and ordered laborers to transport stones and earth to fill the pond, making it flat. At night, he dreamed of a deity saying, "The sky has been without rain for a long time, but this is not my fault. Within the four seas, even a drop of water is governed by spirits, and I cannot participate. How dare I violate the laws of heaven and send rain without permission? Please understand this." Liu woke up even more angry. At dawn, he led more than a thousand soldiers and increased the transport of stones and earth, throwing them into the pond. By evening, the pond was flat, but by morning, it returned to its original state. Liu was completely unaware and exhausted human effort to do it. During the day, a strong cold wind suddenly arose, and lightning and thunder came out of the pond. Mountains and hills all shook, and the officials and people were very afraid. Liu still supervised the work without stopping. After several days, he suddenly died, and rain came abundantly.

The Incident at Shumu Palace Involving Horses During the Zhenglong era of Emperor Wanyan Liang, the governor of Tai'an, Beishile, arrived at the capital and paid respectful visits to the East Peak Temple, performing rituals at all the shrines. Upon reaching the Shumu Palace, he was shocked to see blood flowing on the ground. He personally led his subordinates to conduct a thorough inspection and found that a statue had a broken finger with blood continuously dripping. Additionally, various items such as jewelry, bracelets, and offerings had been stolen. Immediately, he apprehended the person in charge of the temple and imprisoned him for interrogation. Despite applying severe punishments, the true culprit could not be determined. Later, on the first day of the month, Beishile returned to the temple to offer sacrifices, burning incense and praying respectfully. He said, "The inner palace is strictly guarded and deeply fortified; how could thieves enter? Now that the case has been pending for so long, it must be harming the innocent. May the divine be extremely wise and enlightened, showing us who should be punished to satisfy the people's will." After the prayer, he returned to his carriage. That evening, the gatekeeper reported that in the western part of the palace, there were four horses painted on a plain wall that had suddenly disappeared. Everyone below heard the sound of horse hooves but could not see the horses. After a while, each horse carried a person: one monk, one child, and two servants. Beishile hurriedly rode his horse to the site, first thanking the divine for its power, and then questioned the four prisoners, binding them together under the eaves. It seemed as if some invisible force was holding them. At that time, there was a Jurchen thousand-household leader named Ashida, who was cruel and oppressive, demanding wealth and bribes from his subordinates. Two of them, unable to comply, conspired with a monk from Zhusan Temple to dig through the tiles and beams of the palace roof. It took ten days to reach the location, which is why outsiders did not know about it. The stolen items were hidden in the monk's place, and the child overheard the plan. Therefore, they presented a report to the court. Emperor Wanyan Liang ordered the execution of the thousand-household leader and the two servants by beating, and exiled the monk and the child to distant places.

Wang Er Wang Er was a man from Longzhou, residing in the Black Pine Forest Valley. His family had been engaged in hunting and shooting animals for generations, which earned him a reputation. One day, while following a group in pursuit of deer, he reached a deep ravine and lost his way, just when he was hesitating. Suddenly, he encountered a woman crossing water. She was a young woman who was beautiful but had no clothes. She looked at Wang and smiled. Wang, who was accustomed to living in the mountains and forests, had seen many strange creatures, but although he knew she was not human, he showed no fear. He scolded her, saying, "Are you a ghost or a monster?" The woman smiled again but did not answer. After a long time, she asked Wang, "Who are you?" Wang then slightly bowed and spoke respectfully, "I am a hunter from the mountains. Today while chasing a tiger, I got lost, and now life and death are just a moment apart. I beg you to pity me." The woman said, "Follow me, and I will show you the way back." So he followed her, and they climbed a very high, steep mountain peak, crossed a winding stream up to the knees, and walked on a rough path where his feet could not keep up. The woman did not wear shoes, but walked as fast as flying. They arrived at a cave with a large stone room. The place was quiet and secluded, like a hermit's abode, and the bedroom was especially clean and elegant. Seeing no one else around, Wang teased her, and she happily accepted. At night they shared a bed, and during the day she went out to find fruits to eat. They lived there for more than a month. Wang thought of his mother's lack of support and wept, telling her, "I want to return briefly, and we will meet again soon." The woman agreed and escorted him to the official road before parting. Wang was moved by her kindness and decided to visit her again one day. He tried talking to her and invited her to return home, and she did not refuse modestly. They held hands and reached home. Wang's wife Zhao had already given birth to three children, and this woman gave birth to two more children. They lived together very harmoniously with Zhao. When outsiders came, they would be surprised and hide, sometimes walking alone into the mountains and not returning for months. She never cooked food, and Wang also allowed her to come and go as she pleased. Twenty years later, she was still alive.

During the reign of Emperor Shizong of Jin, there was a severe drought in Hedong Prefecture. The governor, Li Jinwu, prayed but saw no effect. He heard that the monk Cihui of Xiyans Temple had strict adherence to monastic rules and was highly respected among the monastic community. He went to ask for help. The monk said, "I am old and cannot move the heavens and earth. But every day when I preach, an old man always comes to listen. I don't know where he comes from, and I suspect he is a dragon. You should try to question him. Please come here tomorrow morning with sincere intentions." Li agreed. As promised, the old man arrived. Li greeted him and said, "I have been praying, but the drought continues, and the five grains are not growing. The people will have no means of survival. I hope the dragon deity will show compassion and send rain. I will build a temple and offer regular worship to glorify the power and influence of the great deity. Please consider this." The old man remained silent for a while, then frowned and sighed, "Ah, it is the monk who has revealed my divine secrets. I will soon die." He then told Li, "Governor, do not worry. I will pledge my life in return for your favor." He turned to the monk and said, "I have been punished by the heavens because of the monk. I will immediately comply."

A man was punished and fell to the ground as a dead body, but he remained within this realm. He begged the monk to serve as a witness so that the entire county's people could hold a ritual for seven days and nights, hoping that by virtue of this merit, he might achieve transcendence. The monk agreed and then departed. As a result, it rained for three days. In a distant county of Yu, a dead dragon fell from a mountain. Li led the common people to summon a thousand monks to the place, built a ritual platform, and invited the compassionate master to perform the rites. After the rites were completed, the dragon appeared in the air and spoke human words, saying, "Although I was punished by heaven, I relied on the power of the law to be saved. By virtue of the supreme and wondrous cause, I have become a bodhisattva dragon." Li built a temple and requested permission from the court, naming the place 'Qinglong Valley.' A story tells of Master Xuanzhao preaching the Lotus Sutra on Mount Song. Three old men came daily to listen and said they were dragons. Xuanzhao, seeing a drought, commanded the rain to fall. The old men said, "The rain is forbidden; if we act without permission, we will be severely reprimanded." Only Master Sun, a recluse, can resolve our disciples' troubles. Xuanzhao went to see Sun Simiao and pleaded with him. That night, it rained for a thousand miles. The three old men transformed into otters and hid in the pond behind Sun's residence. They were caught by messengers, but Sun intervened and released them. This event is somewhat similar to others.

During the second year of the Tiande era of the Jin dynasty (1150 CE), in the fifth month, the city of Yan, known as Yan Mountain City, was narrow but crowded, and the authorities desired to expand it. The southeastern corner of the city was called Tongzhou Gate, and the southwestern corner was called Xijing Gate. Each had a high mound, commonly referred to as the "King of Yan's Mound," though it was unclear which dynasty or which king it belonged to. At this time, markers were set up to establish the foundation for the expansion. However, the eastern tomb was directly obstructing the planned layout, and it was proposed to move forward the northern side to add to the city walls. The construction work had not yet begun when, several days later, the citizens heard voices in the middle of the night claiming that the King of Yan was relocating the capital. They all went out to see and witnessed a procession of imperial carriages and ceremonial guards stretching for ten miles from the eastern mound to the western mound, illuminated by flickering lights and perfumed by fragrant winds. The procession then disappeared. The next evening, the same phenomenon occurred again. The citizens informed Zhang Jun, the governor of the region, who then petitioned the court to divert the construction to avoid the tomb.

**Translation:** The Fifth Young Master A man named Liu Xiang from the market in Hezhong married a woman from the Zheng family. She was known for her beauty. Liu Xiang was unable to support himself and lived a life of poverty and despair, spending his days drinking with his companions. Zheng endured hunger and cold, feeling lonely and resentful. Suddenly, she fell ill, her body wasting away, and she became unconscious. Later, she recovered slightly but remained alone in a room, sitting silently without speaking. Whenever Liu Xiang came, she would bite her teeth in anger and insult him. Liu Xiang was deeply depressed and eventually left, abandoning her. Zheng locked herself in and maintained her chastity, often seeming to talk to someone in private. The household members secretly peeped through cracks but saw nothing. After a long time, Liu Xiang returned home and entered the room, where he found the room filled with gold, silk, money, and fine fabrics. He asked how she had acquired these items. Zheng replied, "For several months now, every night when it is deep, a young man appears. He calls himself the Fifth Young Master and sleeps with me. All these items are gifts from him. I dare not hide this from you." Liu Xiang was furious but, having long been in poverty and hoping for a better life, he did not blame her. One day, during the daytime, the young man arrived, and Liu Xiang was present. The young man warned Liu Xiang not to be with his wife. Liu Xiang was afraid and moved to an outer residence, allowing the young man to do as he pleased. He also cast a golden statue and worshiped it morning and evening. Soon, Liu Xiang remarried, and since he had no children, he prayed to the young man to ask for one. The young man then secretly took the ninth son of the Western Commander to be Liu Xiang's heir. The Western Commander offered rewards and searched for the boy. They captured the boy's mother, a neighbor named Hu, and brought her to Liu Xiang's house. They found a baby wrapped in fine silk, suspecting it was not born in a common household. They reported this to the commander, who arrested Liu Xiang and Zheng, chained them, and interrogated them, seizing their property. The case was not yet decided when the spirit summoned the ghostly beings, opened heavy doors, and directly entered the prison, taking all the prisoners of the same time. They all escaped. The commander was furious and the next day, he seized Liu Xiang and his wife again, subjecting them to harsh beatings. That night, the spirit took Zheng back and set fire to the government buildings, towers, and grasslands, leaving them completely empty. Tiles, gravel, and bricks fell like rain, and no one could approach to put out the fire. The commander had no choice but to worship the spirit and no longer punish the two people. The Fifth Young Master ultimately occupied the Zheng family.

### Final Translation **Song Zhongzheng** A man from the state of Wei, Wang Wai, obtained the position of assistant teacher in the state through a donation of grain. He was wealthy but had a violent and arbitrary nature. Whenever he passed by a shrine, he never showed any respect, and sometimes he would curse and insult it. During the early years of the Zhenglong era in the Jin dynasty, a scholar paid him a visit and said, "I am Song Zhongzheng." After being invited in, the scholar freely discussed good and evil omens, and his words seemed to imply criticism and warning. Wang was displeased and replied, "Heaven has bestowed virtue upon me. How can good or evil affect me?" The scholar said, "Your stubbornness and refusal to accept advice will lead to disaster. The deity of Mars will soon descend upon your home. Hurry and perform a ritual to avert the disaster, and you may still avoid calamity." Wang responded, "If disaster can be averted through a ritual, then good fortune can also be prayed for. Do not use groundless words to deceive and confuse me." The scholar continued to speak insistently, but Wang shouted him away. After ten days, another scholar, dressed in red, also claimed to be named Song and spoke to Wang in the same manner as Song Zhongzheng. Wang said, "Ten days ago, a scholar named Song visited me and tried to frighten me. I firmly refused to listen to him. Are you his accomplice? I have always been straightforward and have no fear or respect for spirits. You are dressed in red and named Song. Are you not the essence of the deity of Mars?" He shouted him away again. The man left and shouted to the sky. Suddenly, a large fireball descended from the sky. People rushed to save Wang, but he remained arrogant and said, "It is too late to save me. This fire can only destroy my house." Soon, the flames rotated and spread into dozens of torches, destroying Wang's house and all nearby structures, even though they were made of gold and jade. Only the temple dedicated to Mars, located near his residence, remained undamaged. **Seven Maidens**

The great river flows eastward, cutting through the Taihang Mountains and encountering numerous steep rapids where the water is swift and fierce, making it impossible for fish and turtles to stay. One of these rapids is called Qiniangzi Rapids. On the mountain peak stands a temple dedicated to a dragon maiden, and at the foot of the mountain live a thousand households. During the summer floods, the waters reach the sky, and there is annual labor for flood defense, causing constant fear of being submerged. The elderly discuss the idea of moving their settlement to another location. A scholar named Han Yuanweng, respected and trusted by the community, goes to discuss the matter. Han Yuanweng says, "Our ancestors have lived here for generations, with tombs, houses, and other structures. To abandon this place would cause great unease. Let us appeal to the dragon goddess to see if she agrees to the move. It is not too late to make plans according to the situation." The community then pools money to prepare offerings and selects a day to visit the temple to request the relocation of the shrine to the riverside. They cast divination tools and receive a favorable omen. The community returns home after the ritual, preparing funds and considering expenses. That night, a violent storm erupts with thunder, wind, and heavy rain, and the sounds of foxes howling and ghosts wailing. By morning, the sky had cleared, and the atmosphere was peaceful. The temple was found to be intact on flat ground, with not a single tile or beam damaged. The walls, mud sculptures, and statues were all in perfect condition, and the foundation was solid, even better than before. From then on, the floodwaters reached the temple steps and stopped, and the people no longer had to worry about floods. In the 26th year of the Shaoxing era, the land of Huai and Song, where the autumn harvest was abundant like clouds, was suddenly plagued by a massive locust swarm that flew so densely it seemed to pierce the sky. The locusts cleared every field they encountered. Soon after, a water bird named *qu*, which resembles a wild duck but is larger, with a broad neck and long crop capable of holding several dou of food, appeared. The birds formed groups, calling to each other, and pecked up locusts to fill their crops, then spat them out. After spitting out the locusts, they would peck again. This happened in dozens of cities and towns. Within ten days, there were no remaining locusts, and the year was a great harvest. The officials in Xu and Si reported the matter to the Jin court, which then decreed that the *qu* be enfeoffed as the Great General of Fangguo.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume One, Series A *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Yijian Zhi* Volume Two, Series A Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Four Generals of Yangwu The lower embankment of the Yellow River in the south of Yangwu County, located in the northwest of Bianjing, was repeatedly destroyed by floods and rapid currents. Each time it was repaired, it required tens of thousands of laborers and a vast amount of gold. During the reign of Emperor Shizong of Jin, it once breached and overflowed, and soldiers were sent to block it. It was built in the morning and collapsed at night. The governor of Bianjing sought someone who could swim to explore the bottom of the water. An old fisherman claimed he could remain submerged for a day and night. The governor then ordered the preparation of sacrificial offerings and first worshipped the river god before sending him in. Half a day later, the fisherman emerged. There was a large water dragon causing damage, so the embankment could not be made firm. It was not possible to kill it without a precious sword. The dragon was sleeping in a hundred-foot deep abyss, and it would be easy to slay it. The governor took an ancient sword from the treasury and gave it to the fisherman. Before entering the water, the fisherman said, "Please gather all the boats on the shore to wait for me." At noon, the water turned red, and he was ordered to reach the middle of the river. By the appointed time, the water turned red, and the fisherman emerged carrying the dragon's head and leaped onto the boat. The great flood suddenly subsided, and the embankment became stable. The governor wanted to recommend him for a military title, but the fisherman declined. He also refused many gifts of gold and silk. Shortly after, he died. The governor built a shrine in his place and petitioned the court to posthumously honor him as the Four Generals, considering him the son of the dragon princess San Niang. Statues were erected beside the shrine, and its miraculous responses were very notable. When the fisherman's family was visited, no one knew him. His name was never inquired about, and only those who recognized him suspected he might be a deity.

A Donkey of Langzhong Du Du Jing, the Langzhong, was a native of Shangyuan Village, Ronghe County, Hedong Prefecture. His family had been in the medical profession for generations, and their assets were just sufficient to make ends meet. One day, he bought a donkey from a local resident named Wang. The donkey was only four feet tall, but it was strong, loved to bray, and could run far. Du Jing rode the donkey day and night, and he enjoyed getting drunk in the evening, galloping recklessly without restraint. If the donkey slowed down slightly, he would whip it harshly. As a result, every time he put the saddle on the donkey, it would tremble with fear and mournfully bray, out of dread. At first, Du Jing did not know that the donkey harbored resentment and thoughts of revenge. Once, he went to Ding Village for evening drinking and, halfway back, was still ten miles from home. Wanting to hurry back, he whipped the donkey more urgently. The young servant could not keep up, carrying clothes and a medicine box behind. Suddenly, the donkey stumbled and fell, throwing Du Jing to the ground. He was not yet able to get up when the donkey kicked and bit him, eating almost all of his thigh. His breath was not yet gone, but the donkey wandered aimlessly and returned alone. The family was surprised and said he must have suffered the misfortune of wolves and tigers. When they looked at the donkey's mouth, it was all bloody. The children carried torches to the place where the incident occurred. Du Jing was still in pain but could speak. They carried him to the house, where he died.

The Black Wind King The Temple of the Earth God in Fen County, located forty li south of the Fen River, stood majestically by the Hong River, with mountains forming its backdrop. This temple had been a significant site since the Han and Tang dynasties, with its palaces and halls adorned with grandeur. During the Shaoxing period (1131-1162), it fell under the control of the Jurchen people, who were led by a powerful commander known as the Black Wind King. He led an army of tens of thousands, intending to invade the regions of Liang and Yi, and stationed his troops near the temple. The temple was filled with foul odors and filth, piled up like a hill, and no one bothered to clean it. One night, the Black Wind King, drunk, attempted to enter the inner chambers of the temple, harboring disrespectful intentions. His attendants tried to dissuade him, but he ignored their warnings and proceeded with forty servants. Just as he was about to enter, a sudden burst of fire emerged amidst smoke and fog, accompanied by a cold wind so strong that it made people unable to stand still. Fearing for his life, the Black Wind King hastily rushed out of the temple. At that moment, the sky was clear, and the sun was high in the midday sky. Suddenly, a cloud rose from the temple, followed by thunder, lightning, and heavy rain. In an instant, the water rose several feet, washing away all the previous filth. The Black Wind King, realizing the divine retribution, performed a ritual of purification, offered a sacrifice, and donated five thousand strings of cash to atone for his actions. However, two-thirds of his soldiers perished in the event.

Wang De Ruo and the Dried Crab In Yidu, Qingzhou, Wang De Ruo built a new residence on the northern outskirts of the city. Once completed, various strange occurrences began to emerge, with supernatural beings appearing during the day amidst thick, dense smoke. These beings took on human form and moved in and out in a chaotic manner, lying down or sitting idly as if nothing was wrong. Wang De Ruo invited and called upon those skilled in the arts of magic and sorcery to apply their methods to control and treat the disturbances. Many attempts by several individuals were all ineffective, and he was unable to find peace or rest. He returned to his old house and posted a notice. He then sought out someone with courage and wisdom to investigate the situation. A dog butcher named Fan Wu, known for his bravery and strength, went to Wang De Ruo to request food and drink, and stayed alone for the night. Around midnight, a loud, sudden sound came from the west wing, and a person leaped out from the ground. This person had a short body and a hunched neck, wore red clothing, and was well-built and robust, appearing to be about thirty years old. The apparition clapped its hands and danced in the courtyard. Fan Wu grasped a knife and chased after it, following it until he reached the southeast corner where it disappeared. Fan Wu remembered the place and, the next morning, dug up the soil. He found a large and red dried-up crab. He crushed it with a hammer and threw it into the water. Afterward, everything became peaceful. The owner thanked Fan Wu generously, and thus found a peaceful place to live.

### Translation of the Source Text #### 李婆墓 In the territory of Xiapi, there is an ancient mound said to be the tomb of Lady Li. No one knows when it was built, but it is believed to contain many treasures. Eventually, it was coveted by a worthless and wicked man. In the year Ding Si of the Shaoxing era during the late period of the false Qi state, bandits ran rampant, burning down houses and digging up tombs almost every day. Thus, they set about the tomb of Li, gathering three hundred people and assembling shovels and picks. From morning to noon, they reached the underground chamber, and the coffins and coffers were all exposed. The crowd was exhausted and resting, some eating dry food. Soon, an old woman appeared, over seven chi tall, with white hair, dark complexion, and extremely ugly appearance. She wore a white robe and sat calmly on the coffin, flicking her fingers and emitting a long whistle. The sound reverberated through the forest and ravines, and the streams and brooks in the valleys all boiled up. The crowd was terrified and fled. Soon, smoke and mist enveloped everything, and spirits and ghosts appeared and disappeared. Some heard the sound of noisy chariots and horses, while others heard a faint rumbling like thunder. After some time, the sky cleared. One brave thief went back to look, but the coffins and coffers were no longer there; only an empty hole remained. He regretfully returned. Within fifty days, many died suddenly or met with misfortune. The villagers all covered the tomb and offered sacrifices. #### 宿迁诸尹 During the time of chaos, the prominent family of Yin in Susong gathered their clansmen and rose up to attack the encampment of the Jurchen generals Longhu. They captured the imperial portraits and various items from the palace and placed them in their home. Due to the obstruction of the roads, they did not have time to present these items to the court. Two scholars from the same village, Zhou and Guo, sought to obtain some of the goods but were not satisfied. They falsely accused the officials of privately hoarding items that belonged to the imperial palace, alleging that they were planning treason. The prison officials did not investigate further, and as a result, all the members of the Yin family were executed. Zhou was rewarded with the position of magistrate of his county, and Guo became the deputy magistrate, assisting in the plot. Those who aided in the plan were all promoted to higher ranks. Later, to avoid the war, many people from the town fled to Jingkou, and Zhou and Guo also moved south. They often met with their friend Zhu.

A boy named Zhu, who was in the market, was about six or seven years old. He saw five strong soldiers wearing blue and purple robes, drawing bows and holding arrows, looking back and feeling angry or resentful. They were in the main thoroughfare, wanting to shoot someone, and quickly entered a wine shop. Zhu did not notice, but from Long secretly told him, and then went out to look. All followed him westward. After the people finished drinking, they passed through a small alley on the south side and arrived at a narrow place. There, they met the five soldiers who had been there before, who straightened their bodies and shot arrows, hitting Zhou Guo's chest. Those who were traveling together saw nothing at all. The two boys immediately claimed to have heart pain, collapsed and could not get up. The crowd carried him back, and after a night, a boil grew on his back. The chest and back were pierced through to the diaphragm, revealing the five organs. More than a month later, he died. **Small Pearl Mountain Leftover Egg** East of Mizhou, one hundred and twenty miles, bordering the sea, there is a mountain called Small Pearl, with twin peaks towering, rising high into the clouds. In the middle, a stream, clear and sparkling, clear enough to see, called Round Top. In the autumn of the third year of the Zhenglong era of the Jin dynasty, a villager walked along the side of the mountain to the side of Round Top, saw an egg on the ground, capable of holding two dan of millet, with colorful and brilliant light. He found it strange and looked at it, then cut vines to tie it and carried it down the mountain. The whole village came to see, amazed, as it was something they had never heard of. Several young men took it away, and immediately cooked and ate it. After ten days, a hurricane blew up at night, shaking the heavens. The residents of more than a hundred households were lifted by the wind to the top of the mountain, then fell back to the side of Round Top. The young men who ate the egg died, while the rest, thousands of old and weak, were unharmed. Broken tiles and rotten wood still exist today.

Gao Gao, the eleventh Preserver of Righteousness, was a native of Fulü. In the early years of the Shaoxing era, he followed the governor-general of Chu Prefecture, Zhao Li, and rose to become a commander and chief of the garrison, standing six feet and two inches tall, brave and strong. By the thirty-first year of the Gengchen era, he was residing temporarily in Beishenyan and went to Xuyi to gather those who were destitute. He secretly crossed the Huai River and entered the eastern city of Si, where he robbed a wealthy household belonging to the Wang clan, obtaining two million strings of gold and silver. At this time, Wanyan Liang, who was arrogant and aggressive, sent official documents across the border to demand information and action. The prefectures and counties drew portraits and set generous rewards, but Gao calmly and without fear, with his gang, entered the city of Chu. He called a painter named Zhao Si, pointed at the drawing, and said, "What you have drawn does not match me; it should be changed." The prefect Lan Shiji sent someone to invite him, and he gladly accepted the command, answering fluently and carrying himself naturally. Lan thought him a remarkable man and released him due to his bravery, saying, "In times of crisis on the border, this person is truly useful." The following year, the governor of Xuyi, Zhou Cong, selected an effective envoy to come and capture him. Gao began to flee to the north of the Huai River and escaped to a village. The people of the Huai region always feared him and no one dared to inquire. Only Wang Yun, who scorned Gao's bravery, was willing to contribute. Zhou Cong issued an order to the official in charge of capturing bandits, Qiao Shun, who led the garrison troops and directly arrived at his place. It was getting late, and Gao heard someone call, "The officials are after you three times," and then the soldiers arrived, but he still led his followers to resist. One of them said, "Li Si defected and followed Wang Yun," and held a spear to stab his nose, causing him to bleed and faint, and thus he was captured. Wang Yun cut off his head, and his eyes remained open as if alive, looking at the crowd sideways, gritting his teeth, and making a sound. Those who saw it were shocked. It was extremely hot, and after two nights, he finally reached Xuyi, threw his head into the prefecture hall, and his eyes finally closed.

The Servant of Hu Huang Hu Huang, a man from Wangan County in Ba Prefecture, lived in the village of Mokinkou. His family was moderately well-off, and he was generous and devoted to righteousness, forgetting about personal gain. He had a servant named Yan An, who served him for twenty-eight years. Yan An was respectful, diligent, and trustworthy, never accepting payment without permission. Whenever Hu Huang gave him money, Yan An would say, "Please store it temporarily at the master's house. Take it when needed." He cherished the master's possessions and did not waste even a single coin. Hu Huang treated Yan An like a younger brother, and Yan An called Hu Huang elder brother and referred to his wife as elder sister. In the year 1130 (the reign of Emperor Gaozong of the Southern Song Dynasty) and 1156 (the reign of Emperor Zhengong of the Jin Dynasty), during the middle of spring, Yan An took Hu Huang's arm and led him into the bathhouse, saying, "I have a secret matter to tell you." Hu Huang laughed and said, "Are you not asking for accumulated wages? Or is your wife having an affair with someone else?" Yan An replied, "No, neither of those. Then what else could be so secret?" Yan An said, "Brother, you will die without dying a good death. From now on, within seventy days, you will be struck by lightning in the county market. I have a method to save you. Do you believe me?" Hu Huang highly valued Yan An and told his wife and children, who were all worried and did not know what to do. Sixty-three days later, Hu Huang asked Yan An, "If what you say is true, the deadline is approaching. What is this method you mentioned?" Yan An immediately gave him a secret incantation and said, "Once I save you from this calamity, I will leave this world." On the day of the predicted event, the sky was clear, and the fields were cloudless. Yan An stacked several tables and chairs, covered them with a monk's robe, and by noon, smoke and mist rose up, and thunder and lightning flashed. He led Hu Huang to hide under the table and instructed him to recite the incantation quickly, warning him to watch for any sparks that might fly out. A great spirit, wearing armor and wielding a halberd, called out to the spirits, "Hu Huang has nowhere to hide. The time has passed. He has already escaped the punishment of heaven and will live for another twelve years. What shall we do?" The spirits suddenly dispersed. It was still early afternoon, and Yan An was nowhere to be found. Hu Huang lived until the year 1192 before he finally died.

### Translation #### Danzhou Stone Mirror and Drum In the territory of Danzhou, there are two mountain fortresses known as East Pool and West Pool. West Pool has a sheer cliff that rises a hundred feet, so steep and rugged that people cannot climb it. Below the cliff lies a stone mirror and a stone drum. Inscriptions on the side of the drum read: "When the stone drum is struck, the clouds of soldiers gather; when the stone mirror reflects, the south is respected." During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song (1162–1189), although the land had fallen to the Jin dynasty, the Qin people gathered in rebellion, desiring to return to the Song dynasty. This uprising never ceased, and the fortress often housed ten thousand troops. Those who came would strike the drum, but it remained silent, and when they looked into the mirror, it was dim and dark. A local man named Cao Busi, who was poor and raised his parents by spinning and weaving, was called Busi by the locals. In the third year of the Tianjuan era of the Jin dynasty (1152), he returned to the West Pool fortress. Someone invited him to the site of the stone drum and mirror, where he struck the drum, and it rang out, shaking the surroundings. When he looked into the mirror, it suddenly became bright. Those who saw him noticed that his appearance remained unchanged, but he wore a crown and robe like a king. They gathered and bowed, taking him as their leader. After a long time, even the East Pool fortress submitted to him. The bandits in Guanzhong gathered in large numbers, but whenever they fought, they were defeated and retreated. After a year, his victorious troops numbered in the tens of thousands, and he took control of Yan'an, proclaiming himself king. However, he did not last two years and was killed by the Jin. The stone mirror then began to haunt. #### Nine Dragons Temple In the White Dragon Valley of Tongzhou, there was a family of potters named Liang, who had been engaged in pottery-making for generations. Their family was extremely prosperous, and they built ten kilns, all of which produced earthenware. Only one kiln produced the best wares, while the other nine often produced crooked and uneven pieces. When these pieces were sold in the market, people competed to buy them, even though they were of poor quality. The valley had an old temple called the White Dragon Temple, named after the valley. It was known for its spiritual influence, but it was quiet and not respected by the local community. One day, Liang dreamed of an old man who transformed into a human and came to him, saying, "I have nine sons, and they are all grown up. I have no place to leave them, so I will entrust them to your family. In the past, when we made steaming pots, we often exerted hidden effort."

A Dragon's Blessing and a Man's Redemption When Liang was struggling with the nine kilns, he had never obtained a single good piece of pottery. He often thought about it and wondered how he could improve. The dragon said to him, "How is it that you do not understand? You make poor-quality goods yet gain substantial profit. Is it not because of my son?" Liang immediately stood up in awe and thanked the dragon. "If you can help him build a temple, in the future, he will gain even greater blessings," the dragon said. Liang agreed and then woke up. Immediately, he called for craftsmen to prepare materials and built a new shrine on the old site. He carved an image of the old dragon in the center and arranged nine positions on either side to honor his son. When the work was completed, people from near and far came to worship and were pleased. Later, in a time of drought, Liang prayed for rain, and the rain fell without waiting for a specific day. Liang's livelihood improved even more than before. --- Wang Shihui Wang Yuan, with the courtesy name Shihui, was from Dong'e in the prefecture of Yunque. He became ill from excessive drinking but was extremely intelligent and kind. At around forty, he had not yet entered officialdom. In the height of summer, he played with friends and drank together, drinking so much that he fell asleep by the window. He dreamed of traveling to another place and heard that the country of indulgence had invaded. The residents carried the elderly and children and scattered. Wang was in a panic and hid. When he returned home, his entire family had been captured. He wandered alone in the mountains for years, with no place to settle. Suddenly, he met three old friends, Yan Zhongfu, Li Hengjia, and Wang Mianfu. They asked about each other's well-being and told each other that their families were safe. Wang was very happy and said to them, "I have been in distress for three years, suffering from hunger and cold, drifting from place to place, unable to plan for the next day. Every time I think of the joys of life, I drink several dou of wine. Now I just want one cup to quench my thirst, but there is no way to get it. Do you have any intention to help?" Zhongfu said, "A few miles ahead, there is a wine shop with green curtains. Two beautiful women are serving at the counter. Why don't we visit it together?" Wang was even happier and went to the market, and it was just as Zhongfu said. Wang drank the first cup first and then ordered.

Adding wine, a young girl holding a vessel looked sad. Yüan asked her with a stern look, "A wine server should be cheerful and happy when serving guests. Why do you look so gloomy?" The girl wept and said, "My elder doesn't know. The wine we just drank was not brewed from grains or yeast; it was made from human essence and blood. People in the mortal world trample on the remnants, accumulate misdeeds, and after death, their essence and blood seep into the fermentation vat and become wine." Yüan was skeptical and said, "I don't believe it." Shu then led him into the inner room to inspect. In the large room, there were rows of wine presses, and there were over a hundred people sitting naked, men and women mixed together. Two large demons were holding tools and placing them in the fermentation vat in turn. A large stone was pressing the vat, and blood flowed out from their mouths. Soon, it became wine. Yüan was frightened and trembling and awoke. A little boy was by his side, and guests were sitting casually, with the sound of pouring wine and clinking cups ringing out. He hurriedly told about the dream, and not even a moment had passed. He recalled the experience, and it seemed like several years had passed. Stories from the Tang Dynasty literature, such as "The Dream of the South柯," "The Story of the Cherry Blossom," and "The Yellow Millet Dream," are all similar. As recorded in the *Wenxian Lu*, the story of Zhang Sheng entering a defense is quite similar to this.

**常珤牛** Chang Bao, a native of Pingyang in Jinzhou, lost his father, a village official, at a young age. His mother remarried a wealthy man named Kang Dehui, who was a good-for-nothing and unreliable. Kang gave Chang Bao 3,000 strings of cash to set up a small shop, allowing him to sustain his livelihood. However, Chang Bao treated his stepfather with little gratitude. After several years, when Kang Dehui died, Chang Bao treated him like a stranger and stole all his wealth. At the age of thirty, Chang Bao was strong and healthy, but he suddenly bellowed like a cow and died. That night, one of Kang's cows gave birth to a calf with a single hoof on its back, and the calf's side was marked with Chang Bao's name in red. Kang's two sons, who were educated and understood right from wrong, could not expose this shameful act and hid the calf in the back of their house. The calf bellowed loudly, jumped wildly, broke through the fence, and rushed into the town. The townspeople, seeing this, learned about the incident. Kang sent several strong servants to chase the calf, but they could not catch it. The calf rushed directly into a deep valley and was never seen again. **野牛滩** The Wild Cow Beach is located near Bai Bo in Luoyang, adjacent to the Nine Ladies Temple. During the reign of Emperor Xizong of Jin in the autumn of the Huangtong era, a sudden flood occurred. Ten out of every seven households were submerged and drowned. People saw a group of dragons leaping and surging in the ravines, breaking peaks and shores, creating deep pools and washing away houses and fields. The rain continued for five days and nights without stopping. Suddenly, dozens of cows appeared on a high cliff, rushing downstream with the current. They fought with a dragon at the foot of the mountain. Black mist swirled around, and flames shot out. After one night, the rain stopped, and the water returned to its original course. A dragon, ten zhang long, was found dead at the temple. The governor of Luoyang, Meng Jun, led officials from Luoyang and Heyang to offer sacrifices by the river. Soon after, a dragon was seen in the clouds, its head raised as if rushing toward the crowd. Everyone looked up and realized that it had transformed into a cow to kill the dragon. A temple was then established at that place.

**Translation:** *Yijian Zhi Jiajuan Er* *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Yijian Zhi Jiajuan San* Song Dynasty, Hong Mai **The Residence of Lady Lü** In the early days of the Chunxi reign, the Imperial Guards were herding horses from Wu County to Pingwang, passing through Linping. The group had already stopped for the night, but the rear guard officer He Zhong and four soldiers were left behind. After traveling three li, they reached Jiangwan and lost their way. They asked a farmer, who advised them to take the left-hand main road. After half a li, they passed through a pine forest and came upon a large mansion. Several horses were tied up, all sturdy and handsome. They asked the gatekeeper whose residence it was. The gatekeeper replied, "It was the home of the former governor of Yongzhou, Lü, who has passed away. Now the lady is widowed." They asked if the horses were for sale. The gatekeeper replied, "I am looking for a buyer." They gave a small bribe to get him to go inside and report. After a long time, the lady came out, dressed simply in plain clothes, gracefully and naturally, with the demeanor of a hermit in the woods. She was around forty years old and had several dozen attendants. She invited them to sit and offered tea. They asked what they wanted and offered the horses. She smiled and said, "A trivial matter." Soon, she prepared a feast, with various performances of music and dance. After the entertainment, she invited them into the room and intended to sleep with them.

He, a simple and rustic military man, believed himself unworthy of being paired with a beautiful woman. The lady sighed and said, "I have been a widow for ten years, without children, living alongside maidservants, barely surviving. Tonight, unexpectedly, you arrived. Isn't this fate? You should not worry about it." She then stayed at the inn for three nights. When they parted, she gave him as a gift a five-colored horse and one hundred taels of silver, and the four soldiers each received a thousand coins as a gift. She also mentioned that her sister lived near Jingci Temple and asked him to deliver a letter. They held hands affectionately and then parted. Upon returning, He violated the military deadline and incurred punishment, leaving him in a state of fear with no way out. He pretended to be ill and delayed his return, claiming to have been delayed due to illness. His superior, seeing the horse, was pleased and did not question him, and He was promoted to a regular general. A few days later, a messenger from Lady Lu arrived. He's sister-in-law was startled but had no way to refuse. His sister-in-law's sister arrived, and the three of them sat together in a triangular formation. She ordered the messenger to call He into a small pavilion and severely reprimand him. He bowed and apologized, pleading his case for three nights until she forgave him. Half a year later, He's wife died, and the expenses for the funeral were all provided by Lady Lu. Through the matchmaker, he paid the bride price to marry her as his second wife. He asked to live with his sister-in-law. The next year on Cold Food Day, He's son visited his father's grave and went to visit his sister-in-law's house. His sister-in-law said that her sister had already returned to Linping. When he visited the house again the following year, the house and grounds were nowhere to be found, except for two ancient tombs in the pine forest. He's son was deeply moved, paid his respects, and then left.

**Translation:** The Wen Family Girl A man named Wen Shi lived near the judicial office in Yongzhou, and his wife, Min, gave birth to a daughter who was exceptionally graceful and beautiful, unlike ordinary girls in the marketplace. They named her Yunnu. In the year Dinghai of the Qian Dao era, she was thirteen years old and slept only with her grandmother on the same bed. One night, she dreamed of a Taoist priest who arrived. Her parents prepared a meal to entertain him. After eating, the Taoist priest called Yunnu forward and said, "I have been searching for you for a long time, and now I find you here." He reached into his chest and took out a small porcelain jar, from which he took a pill the size of a bean, green and blue in color and very beautiful. He placed it in his palm and said, "The power of this pill can reverse the workings of heaven, alter the course of nature, change yin and yang, and transform cold and heat. You have a predestined connection and should take this pill. Swallow it quickly." Yunnu was delighted and took the pill as soon as it was offered. As soon as it went down, a fragrant aroma filled the air. The Taoist priest then left, and Yunnu woke up dazedly. The pill was still in her stomach, and the fragrance did not dissipate. She told her grandmother, and shortly after, when she got up to use the restroom, she had already transformed into a man. At the time, I recorded this event in my work "Bing Zhi," and it differs slightly from what I wrote there. I sometimes write "Wen" as "Wen," but the story is not entirely fabricated. Liu Chengjie's Horse A man from Zhejiang West named Liu Chengjie returned home to Ganzhou after serving as a tax official in Ganzhou. He traveled east with his son and servant on horseback and stopped at an inn in Guixi, Jiangxi. There, he met several merchants carrying fragrant incense and asked where they were from, wanting to buy their incense. They offered to sell it, but Liu found it inferior. He said, "The incense I have, though not abundant, is superior to what you have." He showed them the silver he had, which amounted to about a hundred taels. The merchants secretly observed and decided to stay overnight. In the middle of the night, they armed themselves and entered Liu's room. Liu, who had served in the military, used his strength to cut off one of their arms. The others were frightened and fled. The innkeeper, who was part of the plot, told them, "He will not give up and might bring more accomplices, so we should not sleep."

If one does not understand, it is better to start the journey to avoid trouble. Liu did not doubt his deceit and urged the servant to rise, leaving without waiting for a full meal. They reached a high hill and met bandits. Although they resisted, the few could not match the many, and both the son and the servant died there. Just as a postal soldier passed by, he was also killed, and the bodies were thrown into a pit. The bandits divided their loot and fled. The horse he rode stumbled on the road. Just as the county clerk went out to inspect the fields, the horse approached the carriage, bowing its feet as if paying respect, then retreated and advanced again, repeating this six or seven times. The county clerk found it strange and said, "This must be a case of wrongful accusation." He sent several people to follow the horse, which led them to the edge of a hill. The horse pawed the ground, and the soil stood firm, leaving a trail of blood and a strong smell of death. Four bodies were found in the pit, and their limbs were still warm. The county clerk immediately ordered the village head to investigate and capture the culprits. By the end of the day, they were all caught and executed for their crimes. --- Serving as the county clerk of Jian'an, Yu was a man of exceptional learning who passed the imperial examination and was appointed to a position in Yixing. On the eve of taking office, he suddenly died. After a day, he revived and recounted his experience. He said that when he was initially ill and delirious, he saw a clerk bowing in the courtyard and saying, "The lord has a command." He followed the clerk for about a hundred miles, but his strength could not keep up, and he begged for a brief rest. After a long time, they arrived at a place resembling a temple of a mountain god. The main official was seated in the center, wearing a ceremonial cap and tassel. Yu knelt and pleaded, "I have no regrets about dying, but I think my father is old and has no one to care for him. I request to be granted the remaining life to fulfill his natural lifespan." The main official asked, "Do you know about your past life?" Yu replied, "I do not know." The official said, "Your name in a past life was Chen Chaola, and now you are borrowing his accumulated learning, which is why you are exceptionally wise. But in your entire life, you did not do any good deeds, so your blessings are shallow. The Heavenly Emperor, moved by your diligence in studying, granted you a degree. The reason you did not enjoy a long life and died early was that you never did any good deeds. Moved by your wish to care for your parents, I have petitioned the Heavenly Court to allow you to return for seven days to say goodbye to your father." Yu returned home, knowing he would not live long, and wept as he cared for his father. Seven days later, he indeed died.

The Grand Marshal Wang Xuan During the waning days of Emperor Gaozong's reign in the Southern Song Dynasty, Wang Xuan, a Western border general, distinguished himself by facing the Jin army and achieving military success. He later served as the commander of Xiangyang and passed away. Six months after his death, one of his former subordinates, Jiang Xun, who was responsible for training troops, left the city and went to Tanxi, where he drank by the water's edge. A soldier in yellow robes, holding a flag inscribed with the word "command," shouted, "The commander-in-chief summons Jiang Xun." When asked who was calling, Jiang replied, "It is General Wang." Recalling that Wang had already passed away, Jiang closely examined the soldier, recognizing him as an old acquaintance. Despite his doubts, he was urged to follow and proceeded to the south gate tower, where Wang Xuan appeared, no different from when he was alive. Jiang bowed twice, and Wang Xuan inquired about his family's well-being, to which Jiang responded that they were fine. Wang then asked if Jiang's horse was still there, and Jiang replied that it had been given to the commander-in-chief. Wang expressed regret, saying, "Too bad, too bad." He then asked if anyone had seen his horse, and Jiang answered that he had not. Wang then instructed someone to bring the green lion, which arrived shortly afterward. It turned out to be a green horse, extremely swift and strong. Wang made Jiang ride the horse, and as Jiang took the reins and mounted, the horse moved as fast as a bird, with its feet not touching the ground. It passed by human dwellings, leaping directly over rooftops. Jiang was surprised and bewildered, as if in a dream. After returning, Wang sent Jiang away and ordered a subordinate to escort him down the city. Dazedly, Jiang returned to his house and began talking to his wife and children before finishing. He then received five thousand coins in five types of currency and five jars of wine, which Wang Xuan said were a reward for training. Jiang intended to use official vouchers for the reward, but they had already disappeared. The next day, Jiang fell ill and recovered after five days.

Xiong Er's Disobedience In the Xingguo military district, there was a man named Xiong Er, who had a perverse and rebellious nature. His father, Ming, was a soldier who, by the time he was old, had left the military and could no longer support himself. Ming's wife died early, and he relied solely on his son for support, yet treated him as if he were a stranger. Eventually, Ming was reduced to begging, and he wept and pleaded with Xiong Er, but Xiong Er cursed him and would not listen. Ming intended to report Xiong Er to the authorities but could not bring himself to do it. Instead, he prayed to heaven every night, hoping that his son would repent and show filial piety. After two years of this, Xiong Er finally followed his friends, indulging in drinking and gambling. One day, the clear sky suddenly turned dark and gloomy, with rain falling like needles, and lightning and thunder so intense that people could barely see. Someone called out for Xiong Er, and after the storm cleared, he was nowhere to be found. They searched and found his body outside the city gate, with his eyes gouged out, his tongue cut, and the words "不孝之子" (disobedient son) written on his back. This incident occurred on September 7, 1176 AD. Zhang Wenbao In Jiankang, there was a military officer named Li Jin, who was strong and brave, serving as the flag bearer of his unit. He was only thirty years old when he contracted a feverish illness. The commanding officer ordered Zhang Wenbao, a medical officer, to treat him. Zhang Wenbao was not skilled in medicine but wanted to use his military title to protect his family. He misdiagnosed Li Jin's condition, prescribed a large dose of Aconite, and Li Jin suffered greatly, cursing Zhang Wenbao. Zhang Wenbao replied, "The Aconite burned me alive! I am doomed to die, and I will hold you accountable in the afterlife." Soon after, Li Jin's body turned black, and he was buried. Three days later, his family prepared wine and ritual offerings at his tomb. Li Jin returned as a ghost and said to Zhang Wenbao, "You killed me with your medicine. I am now a ghost." His wife ran to report this to the commander, who sent someone to open the tomb. The body was already decayed and unrecognizable. From then on, Zhang Wenbao saw Li Jin's ghost every day for two months before he died.

### 方禹寃 Fang Yu, a local official from Poyang County, had a conflict with Yang Wu, a wicked man. Yang Wu was involved in trade, and Fang Yu was frequently humiliated by him. Once, when they met while drunk, Fang Yu grabbed Yang Wu's clothes. Realizing he could not fight back, Fang Yu spoke humbly and begged for mercy, but Yang Wu ignored him, dragged him to the ground, and beat him severely. Bystanders tried to intervene, but Yang Wu continued to abuse Fang Yu. Eventually, the crowd carried Fang Yu home, where he was on the verge of death. He told his wife and children, "I have an ancient grudge with him, and now he has beaten me. If I don't survive, do not sue him in court or present my body for examination. Let my shame and pain spread, but place a pen and paper in my coffin; I will be judged by heaven." With these words, Fang Yu died. His wife and children harbored deep resentment and hatred, but no one else knew about it. Yang Wu, believing he had achieved his goal, became even more cruel and violent. Several months later, during the late autumn, at noon, Yang Wu saw Fang Yu coming from afar, accompanied by two spirits. The spirits approached and shouted at Yang Wu, saying, "You killed me without cause. I have appealed to the underworld, and with the help of these two messengers, we will capture you." Yang Wu tried to flee, but Fang Yu grabbed his hair, and the spirits struck him. Yang Wu cried out in pain and begged for forgiveness, saying, "When you were hurting me, I was in a desperate situation. Killing someone means paying with your life, and owing a debt means repaying it. Such idle words cannot save you." Passersby saw Yang Wu hanging his head, looking dejected, talking to himself, and slapping his face until he bled. They informed his family, who came to see him. Yang Wu could still describe what had happened, but soon after, he died. ### 汪乙鼋 Wang Yi and the Turtle (Note: The second section, "汪乙鼋," does not provide enough context for a complete translation. Additional information or the full text would be needed to translate this part accurately.)

A Poyang Commoner Named Wang Yi Wang Yi, a commoner from Poyang, lived near the warehouse gate and made a living by selling fish and turtles. In the autumn of the third year of the Gande era (1167), he went to the lake and, with two thousand coins, bought a turtle from a fisherman. The turtle weighed one hundred catties. He brought it home and placed it in his room. That night, he heard a very sad moaning sound. He lit a lamp to illuminate the room and saw that it was the turtle. The turtle raised its head and spoke human words, saying, "I wish to save my remaining life and be released back into the rivers and lakes. You should consider how to repay me." Wang Yi was foolish and cruel, thinking it was strange. He held a large stick and beat the turtle dozens of times, but the turtle still begged for its life without stopping. An elderly neighbor heard the commotion and found it strange. He got up, put on clothes, and called Wang Yi, urging him to release the turtle into the water. Wang Yi did not listen. The next morning, the old man again led other residents to jointly advise and stop him, and even wanted everyone to contribute money to compensate for the turtle's value, but Wang Yi still would not. Three days later, he killed the turtle. Soon after, he was imprisoned for a crime, suffered flogging, and his family became increasingly poor. He and his wife both starved to death. **Duan Xiang's Tavern** Duan Xiang, a local official from Poyang, was in charge of the wine business at a tavern located south of the county government office. The tavern had an old building with multiple stories, which had long been haunted by a mountain demon. For many years, no one dared to climb it. Every dark and rainy night, it would throw tiles and stones, break jars, and overturn water. In the third year of the Chunxi era (1176), Duan Xiang was deeply worried about this and gathered with other young men to discuss it. They secretly hid sharp knives, scattering them outside the door and by the stove. Several people entered the room to guard and watch. They also tied a large rooster and placed it by the jar. Duan Xiang personally held a short spear and sat upright waiting. It was the second watch of the night in the eighth month of autumn. They heard a breathing sound. Soon, a creature over six feet tall appeared, wearing soft shoes, a Tang-style hat, green robes, and black boots. Holding a hand tablet, it descended from the stairs, pushed the tablet aside, and rolled up its sleeves, intending to go to the jar to wash. Hearing the rooster flapping its wings, it transformed into a fox. It crouched on the ground, ready to attack the rooster. Duan Xiang thrust his spear into its throat, making a sound like a fox's howl. Everyone together captured and killed it. They skinned it, and the fur was beautifully patterned and lovely. They cooked the meat and ate it all. The strange occurrences in the building ceased.

**Jiang Yanrong** In the twelfth year of the Chongxi era (1185 AD), Jiang Yanrong, a physician from Poyang, moved to a residence within the city gate of Tai. One night, returning home, he stopped to sit alone with a candle and studied medical texts thoroughly. He saw an old man knock on the door and stand there, staring at him. When the old man disappeared, Jiang realized it was a strange occurrence but did not have the time to investigate its cause. On another night, after attending a banquet in the market and getting drunk, he encountered the old man again. The old man was clearly recognizable, with bushy eyebrows, a white beard, and a plain black-green robe. Jiang shouted at and scolded him, and the old man disappeared into the ground. Jiang said, "It must be something buried wanting to come out." Early the next morning, he dug up about two feet of soil and found a small bar of silver weighing twelve taels. He dug again and heard a clanging sound like metal or armor, which was too hard to penetrate. Jiang feared that seeking unexpected good fortune might instead bring disaster, so he stopped. **Zhang Catfish** Zhang, known in the market as "Catfish," was a man with a wide mouth and an unattractive face. In the tenth year of the Qiandao era (1173 AD), he owed a large amount of unpaid taxes and could not pay. He and his wife hanged themselves one after the other. He had a young son-in-law named Chen Fang who took charge of the household. In the tenth year of the Chongxi era (1180 AD), Fang went with a fellow villager named Zhu to the fish pond in Duchang to collect debts from fishermen. While drinking in a tavern, they saw Zhang come running in a yellow robe. Fang was startled, and Zhang shouted at him sternly, saying, "I know you are here, so I came to take your life." Fang bowed twice and waved to invite him to sit, but Zhang refused and said, "I will call your wife to settle accounts with you." A short while later, Zhang came with his wife, who was holding an infant, and swung an iron ruler to hit Fang. Zhu took away the ruler and said, "Your daughter entrusted her husband to follow me. If you kill him, what will happen?" Zhang said, "I have been dead for seven or eight years, imprisoned in the underworld and suffering endless torment. Your husband did not save me, and this infant also died prematurely because of a single blow. How can you bring this upon me?"

Encountering a dispute between two archers of the county magistrate's office, Zhang and his wife, along with the yellow-clad one, rented a place to stay near the wine shop. Fang and Zhu were in the same room as the archers, and there was no distinction between humans and ghosts. Zhang cursed Fang all night and sent the yellow-clad one to his home to fetch money. After a long time, 2,000 coins were brought. At first, the left door god rejected me, but the right door god relayed the message, so I obtained it. The next morning, Fang, Zhu, and Chen again took the same route to the northwest of Pó City, reaching the island of Bīn. Suddenly, Fang was no longer there, but he could still hear Zhang's voice clearly. Soon after, Fang fell ill and remained so for over ten days, eventually recovering and being fortunate to survive.

The Servant of the Bao Family In the Bao family of Poyang, which resided inside the Binfuzhou gate, they bought a horse and entrusted it to their servant Cheng San to take care of. They bathed the horse daily at the horse-bathing shoal, but often white-necked crows would land on its back and defecate. Deeply troubled by this, they drove the crows away but they kept coming back. So, he hammered a needle into a small hook and threaded it with a long thread, winding it from the horse's belly around to its back and hung bait on it. The crows pecked at the bait and swallowed the hook, unable to get free. Cheng removed their eyes and carried them back to his home, asking for wine from the master's house and drank it. From then on, his vision became increasingly sharp, and he could see ghosts and spirits in the void. Once, he was with Bao's maid in the kitchen and saw a ghost with bulging eyes, a protruding tongue, and a rope around its neck. The ghost stepped over the threshold and peered at Cheng, who struck it with a stick, causing it to cry out in fear and slowly sink into the ground and disappear. It was said that someone had hanged themselves there in the past. Afterward, whenever he went out into the wild, he would always see something. Although they resembled human forms, their limbs were often incomplete. Ghosts and spirits would often run away when they saw him. Some people say that the eyes of a thousand-year-old crow can see through things. Is what Cheng swallowed indeed such a thing?

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Three The *Four Treasuries* (Qinding Siku Quanshu) The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Four By Song Dynasty, Hong Mai The Lord of Commonalty In the eleventh month of the fourth year of the Shaoxing era (1134), Liu Si, a scholar from Luchong Village in Duchang County, Nan'kang Prefecture, suddenly died. Two officials led him to the underworld and brought him to the Hall of Yama. Below the hall, Liu looked up and saw the base of the throne, which was about ten feet high. Yama was seated behind a table, with his head resting on his hand. Before him stood a large sign with green characters that read, "Lord of Commonalty." Liu silently wondered, "How could there be such a surname in the world?" He had not yet spoken when Yama turned to the officials and said, "This one seems to have a bit of life." The officials questioned Liu, "What did you just say?" Liu dared not speak, saying, "I never opened my mouth." After repeated questioning, he finally revealed what he had thought. The official scolded him, "Do not leak this information. This is Hong, the Right Chancellor of Raozhou, who is now the King of Hell." Shortly after, Yama ordered the officials to escort Liu back. On the way, they passed through several prisons where Liu saw prisoners suffering greatly, which terrified him immensely. The official raised his sleeve to cover Liu's face, and he could see nothing. They traveled for more than thirty miles before the official removed his hand. There were eleven halls, each grand and imposing, with monks standing with their hands in prayer, ascending to high seats. Before each hall was a dark, ominous table.

A sheet of white paper was spread out, and several worms wriggled on it. The monk got up and held the *Suíqiúfǎ Huán* (Seeking the Dharma Ring), shaking it and making a sound. He asked Liu, "What good deeds did you perform in your life?" Liu replied, "There is nothing remarkable; I only recited the *Gāo Wáng Guānshìyīn Jīng* (High King Guanyin Sutra)." The monk said, "This is enough. Let you go." Liu had been there for three days, and the monk feared that Liu's wife and children would ruin the house, so he instructed them to return quickly. The official led the way, and they crossed the stream twice before Liu woke up. In February of the following year, Liu's neighbor Yè Bǎiyī came to the court to submit a petition, staying at the inn of Mr. Huáng. He spoke about this with someone, but I did not have time to personally investigate. Therefore, I recorded the deeds of Wén Huìgōng. During his travels to Guangzhou, the governor of the prefecture was Fang Wùdézī, who was retained to oversee the staff. He served alongside his younger brother Zhìchuān, whose name was Hóng. Officials and performers avoided mentioning his name, calling him Gong, an Inspector. The Hóng family originally descended from the Gònggōng clan, and therefore, in the *Zuǒzhuàn* (Commentary of Zuo), "Jìn Gòng Huá" and "Lǔ Gòng Lǚ" are read as "Gōng." By the Han dynasty, a water radical was added to the left side.

Zhang Zhen, the grandson of a former political official known as Zhongding, served as a clerk in the Anfu Office of Hubei Province.
