In the fourth year of the Shaoxing era (1134 AD), in November, the term of the Counselor-in-Advisory, Zhang Guang, came to an end. Zhang Zhen escorted him to the riverbank, where they stayed for a drink until the second watch of the night, when they returned to the city, which was just settling down for the night. However, a fire broke out in the city market. Zhang Zhen rose and followed the General and Privy Councilor to inspect the fire. By the time they returned, it was midnight, and before dawn, Zhang Zhen suddenly heard his wife, Qin Shi, repeatedly shouting for help. He called out to her, but she closed her eyes and did not speak. After a short time, she died. Ten days earlier, Zhang Zhen had sent a tattooed messenger with a letter to De Xing. On the way, the messenger felt a heavy weight on his shoulder, which increased day by day until it became almost unbearable. He once threw the letter to the ground, saying, "Is there a dead man's head inside? How can I carry it across the river?" He told the boatman, who tried to lift it and said he had never encountered a letter so heavy for a long journey. Upon arriving at the Zhang residence, the messenger delivered the letter to Zhang Zhen's father, the Governor of Tongzhou. Upon opening the letter, he found only dried deer meat. As soon as he stepped outside, he was struck by something and Zhang Zhen shouted at him, "How could you have insulted me on the road?" The messenger apologized and said he was not afraid. He then went directly to his parents' room, crying and saying, "Life and death are fated; there is nothing left to regret. Zhang Zhen has no children, and it is difficult for a young widow to remain unmarried. After the funeral, she requested to be sent back to her family. My older sister has been widowed for a long time, even though she has a son, it is not a long-term solution. I wish to seek another match for a lifetime commitment." The messenger suddenly fell to the ground and revived after some time. The Governor of Tongzhou was startled and worried, especially since Zhang Zhen was the son of his wife, Fan Shi, and he felt particularly sorrowful. Two days later, the news of Zhang Zhen's death arrived. Thus, when the messenger came from the west, Zhang Zhen's spirit had already left his body. Truly sad, he died at the age of thirty-five. Qin Shi was a daughter of the family.

### Translation **Double-Headed Lotus** Gao Nie, from Poyang, took up residence at the county magistrate's guesthouse, using the "No Litigation Hall" as a school. In the pond, a lotus flower bloomed with two heads. The county magistrate, Jia Fang, saw it and said, "This is an auspicious omen." My father, during the Dazhong Xiangfu period (1109-1110), while studying at the rural school in Wu County, produced this flower behind his study. The guards informed the headmaster, who then brought the students to see it. They picked it and carefully examined it; there were faint golden characters spelling out my father's name in three characters on the leaves. The others all offered congratulatory words and encouraged him to study diligently, hoping he would be the top scholar the following year. The frivolous young men laughed in unison and said, "Jia Laizi becoming the top scholar would mean there are no true scholars in the world." At that time, he was suffering from sores all over his body, hence the name. By autumn, he was recommended and went to the imperial examination, where he came in first place. Jia's name was Anzai. **Hua Yan** Hua Yan, a scholar from Chuzhou, whose courtesy name was Qingchang, entered the Imperial Academy to study. Once, he gathered with his fellow students in the Tizhen Study on the furnace pavilion. A servant from the study reported that Chen Guanren from Chuzhou was coming to the study. Hua leaped up joyfully, his face beaming with happiness. The others were surprised and asked him why. He said, "Last year, I passed through Sanqu and went to an old man named Liu's divination shop to seek a reading. The divination showed that I would be like a turtle in the Battle of Bo, always looking forward, my studies not yet showing my talent and reputation, but I could still be widely respected. If Xi Fang comes to study with you, you will be the same year, and there will be no doubt about it. This year, the three scholars from Ge, Zheng, and Su served as officials in the academy. I have taken the imperial examinations three times, but all have been unsuccessful. Chen also faced obstacles due to misfortune and was unable to attend the examination. This is the sign that we will be the same year." Indeed, in the Xinchou year of the Chongxi era (1181), they both passed the examination together. Xi Fang, whose courtesy name was Ziyu, came in fourth place.

Xiang Ming's Wife In the village of Hongya in Yudan, there lived a man named Xiang Ming from the mountain of Seifang. He married a courtesan named Hu, and after more than ten years of marriage, she gave birth to a daughter who then died. In the first year of the Longxing era (1163 CE), a witch came from another place and claimed she could summon the soul of the deceased. Xiang ordered his wife to be called, and upon command, she immediately arrived. Xiang saw nothing, but the daughter was already twenty years old. He only saw her, and she was indeed her mother. She stayed and did not leave, living at night with her husband on the same bed but with different quilts, and sharing the same room with the daughter. Whatever was needed, all was relied on the daughter to inform. After two months, she suddenly said her parents came, still with attendants. They wanted to eat, and Xiang immediately prepared food. At first, they shared the same table and seating. The wife said, "The master and servant should not be treated equally," and then placed them separately. Then she said, "My father's house is damaged, and we plan to build a new house. We need money for expenses. Quickly burned hundreds of bundles of paper money." She also said there was a lot of money, but no one to carry and deliver it, so she called two laborers. After completion, she found them weak and replaced them. Soon, she announced to leave, saying she wanted to build a house with her two parents. After more than a month, she came back. A relative, Old Lady Li, visited her husband, who did not speak. When asked, the daughter said, "She had already been hit by her mother on the waist with a stick." Old Lady Li returned with severe back pain and could not walk. A diviner divined that Hu was causing trouble. After praying to her, she began to recover. They lived together for a year, and she gradually shrank, almost like a baby, and then disappeared.

The Wife of Yan the Bucket Maker A resident of Raozhou, Yan the bucket maker lived outside the city in the neighborhood of Hezhong. His wife bore him three sons, all of whom had married. After Yan died, his wife passed away several years later in the fourth month of the year 1180 during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song. The three sons, filled with filial piety, kept their mother's coffin at home for seven days before holding a ritual to honor her. Relatives and in-laws gathered for the ceremony. Suddenly, a venomous snake, commonly known as "bi bei" (tortoise nose), which was about five or six feet long, emerged from beneath the coffin. It moved slowly and gracefully, showing no intention to harm anyone. The family was surprised by its appearance. The snake raised its body towards the sons, raising its head three or four times, with tears in its eyes as if it wanted to express sorrow. Someone was about to raise a stick to hit it, but the sons stopped them. An elderly woman neighbor then asked, "Are you the spirit of Yan's wife?" The snake answered directly. She asked, "Where is your spirit seat?" The snake immediately went to the place she pointed out. After a long time, it came out again. She asked where the rooms of the three wives were, and the snake led them to those rooms. The same happened when she asked about the three sons. After the ceremony, the snake slowly entered the threshold of the house, and no one knew where it went. Liu Shi Liu Shi was a commoner in Poyang City, living in the east of Huaihua Lane. He earned a living by copying books and was known for his stubborn nature. One summer night, he sat outside under the moonlight with his wife and children in the small courtyard. Suddenly, an object entered from the outer gate, resembling a human but with a head as large as a measuring vessel, without limbs or feet. It had two eyes that looked around arrogantly, with glowing eyes, ears standing up, and a wide-open mouth. It suddenly appeared at the edge of the courtyard. The Liu family fled in terror, but the object immediately disappeared. The next night, the same thing happened. From then on, it came several times, and the family became accustomed to seeing it. Gradually, they became less afraid and followed to see where it went. It went to the back of the house, under a large tree, and disappeared into the ground. Early in the morning, Liu Shi ordered a spade to dig the spot. At a depth of three or four feet, they found an ancient stone millstone, many of whose teeth were missing, but only two eyes remained. He took an axe and hammer to break it, and blood flowed out. He picked it up and threw it into the river. The strange occurrence did not happen again.

### Final Translation #### Zhang Nanchong A citizen of Poyang named Li Shiwu bought a house in a lane called Di Alley. He had not lived there for long when, every morning and evening, he often observed strange phenomena in the room. Sometimes there were bright stars clustered like sand being poured, accompanied by a loud crackling sound. Li's family was afraid of some strange disaster and moved to a rented house, selling the property to someone else. Zhang Nanchong, a high-ranking official, bought the house for a hundred thousand coins and personally oversaw the renovation. Upon seeing a clear and smooth large stone in the main hall, he sent a servant to lift it and found several hundred taels of silverware, or perhaps twenty ingots. Everyone believed that the treasure was naturally linked to something and not something that Li could enjoy or possess. #### Li Rou Li Rou, a courtesan from Quzhou, was praised by scholars and officials who visited because she was intelligent and skilled in singing and dancing. She was only in her twenties when she died of illness. Wang Xian, a soldier who lived in the same neighborhood, was ordered to go to Qiantang and returned to Shouchang County. On the road, he met Li Rou and was surprised that she was traveling alone. He asked her, "Where do you want to go?" She replied, "I want to go to Lin'an to see the suburban rites." Wang Xian said, "Why didn't you bring maidservants and servants? Nor did you ride in a sedan chair. But a single woman traveling alone on a long journey, how could that be convenient?" Li Rou smiled but did not answer. After they parted ways, Li Rou said, "When you come to my home, please convey a message to my parents, saying that I am safe on the road." Wang Xian agreed. Upon returning, he first visited the Li family and found out that what he had seen was a ghost.

### Translation Zhan Yu and His Brother In the spring of Bingwu during the Chunxi era, a scholar from Linchuan named Zhan Yu had a dream in which someone told him, "If you wish to gain the status of a provincial candidate, you must meet a worthy person of the present age." Upon waking, Zhan Yu pondered this and realized that the officials in charge of the autumn examination in various prefectures were merely minor officials with minimal administrative authority. Who among them could truly identify a worthy person of the present age? He shared this with others, and his brother Xie playfully responded, "If the examiner selects my essay, then he can be considered a worthy person." They both laughed heartily. When Zhan Yu entered the examination, he wrote an essay based on the theme of Emperor Wen of Han's respect for the wise and virtuous. Zhan Yu understood his previous dream and decided to use this interpretation as the opening statement of his essay. It was accurate and neither stale nor repetitive. In the first couplet, he wrote, "All the worthy persons of the present age are treated like honored guests and given proper respect." When the results were announced, Zhan Yu was the top scorer, and his brother was also recommended. That year, out of the three thousand candidates who sought to pass the examination through their essays, only the two Zhan brothers excelled with their two lines. Indeed, this was not by chance. The Prefect of Qichun and His Concubines The prefect of Qichun did not wish to record his name. His wife, Zhao, was extremely jealous and treated her maidservants harshly, as if they were wet straw. Whenever a maid displeased her, she would inflict severe beating and then use an iron clamp to pull out the maid's tongue, cutting it off with scissors. The maid would suffer pain while scraping the seat, unable to speak, and eventually die after more than a month of not being able to eat or drink. After her death, the family set up a water and land feast, and the monks were about to summon the spirits of the deceased. Zhao peered through the screen and saw the ghost of her former maid holding the iron clamp and scissors, covered in blood. Frightened, she ran back to her room and told the people around her, deeply feeling regret and fear. Soon, she fell ill and, while moaning, said, "The ghost demands retribution for grievances, and necessity cannot be avoided." The prefect offered to burn paper money and recite Buddhist scriptures to appease the spirit, but Zhao said, "It cannot be done." She died a few days later. ---

South City Inn During the tenth year of the Chongxi era, Meng Bixian Zikai, a professor of Jianchang, was about to take over a new position and temporarily stayed at the South City Inn. His concubine lit a lamp in the bedroom to teach their son to read. Meng's room was separated from another by just one window. Suddenly, he saw an object shaped like a cat with white fur as white as frost. It darted out of the room, and his mother was alerted. They held the lamp and followed the trail to see where it led. Meng heard the commotion and quickly went to investigate. As he stepped down from the corridor, both rooms were crushed, and the utensils leaning on the table were all smashed. It was not the white cat that showed a transformation, but the entire Meng family nearly perished; how perilous it was! Now, Meng has been appointed as the governor of Guiyang. This inn is usually filled with strange occurrences, and those who stayed before were all uneasy. Yet, it is now able to bring good fortune to people, cleansing accumulated sins. An Old Monk from Qiantang In the sixth year of the Zhenghe era, the people of Qiantang, Shen Quan and Shi Yong, both made a living by catching frogs. They went to the Lingzhi Township of their county to stay at the home of the local resident Li An. The place was abundant with frogs, and no one had previously caught them. Shen and Shi worked tirelessly to catch them, and their children carried the catch into the city to sell. The profit was ten times greater than usual. One day, Shi returned home first and met the old monk at Li An's house. The monk knocked on the door and said to him, "The group of frogs being caught started with you. The polluted pond's countless offspring are all empty. To recklessly waste natural resources in this way will incur swift retribution. From now on, change your profession; you can still atone for your sins. Otherwise, it is beyond my knowledge." He repeatedly warned him.

The monk who showed no remorse left, and Shen came and reported everything to Shen, saying, "How dare the wild monk interfere in my business affairs? If I see him, I will beat him severely, but then let him go." Shen replied, "It is still possible to catch up with him." They then followed him, walking about a mile, but found nothing. They blamed him for lying and scolded him continuously, which Shen could not endure, leading to a fight. Shen became even angrier and took a knife used for skinning frogs, stabbing the monk in the side, killing him immediately. The village headman arrested him and sent him to the county of Dongping, where Gong Tingyuan was the magistrate. He investigated the case, and all the evidence matched the description. However, the old monk was nowhere to be found. Shen was ultimately sentenced to death by hanging in the market. --- **Nine Li Pine Eels** Gong Tingyuan, the magistrate of Qiantang, was traveling with Zhang Xianzheng, a local official in Hangzhou, and Qian Shaopeng, the county magistrate, on a tour of Tianzhu Temple. They passed by the Nine Li Pine and saw small eels swimming in the water, chasing each other in a playful manner. The eels were about two inches long and were very charming. The attendants tried to catch them with a container. Gong invited the two guests to dismount and walk along the water's edge. After walking more than a hundred steps, they found a cave where water was gushing out, and a large number of eels emerged like clouds, too many to count. Nearby was a wine shop. They asked about the reason for this, and the owner replied, "There was once a Mr. Chen who specialized in selling eels. Anyone entering through the Yuhang Gate had to go through his hands before selling. In his old age, he moved to this place and sold grilled eels. His husband and wife both died recently, leaving no children to handle the funeral arrangements. The neighborhood buried them in one grave. Now, because the accumulated water has seeped into the tomb soil, it has collapsed, and often eels are seen coming out from it. Usually, they have never seen them there. They do not know what for." Gong and his guests were amazed and sighed, saying, "This old man and woman fell into the category of eels to repay their karmic debt." He then ordered the village headman to take their remains and bury them in the public cemetery and send the eels to West Lake. The eel seller I recorded was also like this.

Deng RuChuan The official of the rank of Jiangshi Lang, Deng Zeng, whose courtesy name was Ruchuan, was a member of the Deng family from Jianchang. He obtained his official position due to his father's title as the governor of Wugang and the imperial favor (昌国恩). He was open-minded and talented but somewhat lacking in restraint, and he was skilled in composing short poems and writing large characters. He married the youngest daughter of a member of the imperial family who held the title of Zongshi Chaoyi Daifu. She was exceptionally beautiful, but she died before her husband could start his official career. The family was poor, and Zhao had no means to maintain her virtue. After the mourning period, she took her two children to marry a wealthy man from Nanfu named Huang. Just one month later, Huang dreamed that Deng came to her and scolded her, saying, "Who are you to dare marry my wife? I am now appointed as the judge of the epidemic department. You should quickly annul the marriage. Otherwise, I will bring plague to your family. Do not regret it when it happens." Huang was shocked and afraid, although she greatly loved Zhao. She had no choice but to quickly sever ties. After more than a year, Zhao became even poorer, and sometimes there was no smoke from cooking fires in the morning. She remarried a man named Tong from Nancheng. After several months, she also dreamed that Deng came to scold her and said, "I will transfer my terminal illness to you." Tong was deeply in love with her and did not believe it. He indeed contracted a chronic illness, as Deng had foretold, and he died two years later.

Huang Wenming and Wu Ruhong, two scholars from Chongren, were good friends and both passed the local recommendation for the imperial examinations during the reign of Emperor Ningzong of Song in the year 1191. Huang Wenming fell ill and stayed at home, while Wu Ruhong went to another place to seek medical treatment. After several months, Huang felt slightly better than usual. One night, he dreamed that he met Wu Ruhong at a certain place. They encountered a messenger in yellow robes holding official documents to apprehend them. When they looked at the document, it had the names of Huang Wenming and Wu Ruhong on it. They looked at each other angrily, saying that the state and county officials should not call upon candidates for the imperial examinations without reason, and they were being insulted in such a manner. At first, they resisted, but eventually, they followed the messenger. They arrived at a large, magnificent mansion that resembled a government office. They peeked into the door and found no one there; the messenger was also not seen. They entered the main hall and went into a room. They saw a bed where two people were sleeping under the same blanket. Upon closer inspection, they saw that they had dog heads and human bodies. They were terrified, broke out in a sweat, and ran out to another place. They encountered a Taoist who bowed to them and asked what place this was. The Taoist said, "This is the place of rebirth." Before they could say more, they woke up. They told their father and informed their wife and children about the dream, deeply regretting the strange omen. Soon after, Huang's illness worsened, and he died. Wu Ruhong also died soon after. The villagers discussed that Huang and Wu, as scholars, had no obvious faults, yet their afterlife seemed to involve a strange transformation. If it were said that they had hidden vices, that would not be known by outsiders. *From the *Yijian Zhi* (Record of Strange Things), Volume 4, Section A*

Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries Record of Strange Events, Volume 5, Section A Song Dynasty, Hong Mai Tang Si Nang's Servant Girl Right Attendant Officer Yang Zhonggong practiced the method of observing a person's complexion to determine if they were possessed by a spirit or not. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (1163-1189), he served as the Recorder of the Judicial Department of Dao Prefecture. Before leaving office, he went out into the market and encountered a minor clerk. He called out to him and asked, "You must be possessed by an evil spirit. If not treated, it will lead to death." The clerk replied that there was nothing wrong. For three consecutive days, Yang met him and refused to speak. Yang said, "Are you not afraid of death? Tell me, what harm is there? This possession can only be treated by me." The clerk, upon hearing these words, began to be terrified and said, "In fact, I was having an affair with a neighbor's woman. We have been together for a long time. Although I do not know her family, her actions, habits, and tastes are no different from those of ordinary people. There is nothing to doubt. What the official said, could it be true?" Yang replied, "Yes, that is it. Keep this secret and do not reveal it. You should prepare a long thread and needle. When she comes tonight, secretly sew her clothes. Also hide some of her hats and shoes. If she is indeed a person, it will certainly be a joke. Otherwise, we can investigate." The clerk respectfully followed the instructions. The woman came as instructed. At dawn, she got up and lost her jade hairpin and one shoe. She looked confused and distressed and searched but could not find them. The clerk pretended to sleep and secretly observed what she did. As dawn approached, she left angrily. The clerk found the two items made of clay and immediately took them to show Yang. Yang performed his ritual and interrogated. He sent officials to visit all the temples. It turned out to be a servant girl from the Temple of Tang Si Nang in the north of the city. The clerk went to verify and found that she was the real person he had met. She had no hairpin on her head and one foot was bare. The thread was found on the back of the statue. Yang recited a spell and burned her body. The clerk was unharmed. The Temple of Tang Si Nang was a place of improper worship. Yang eventually died in Chenzhou, and the magistrate of Yingdao, the deputy magistrate, and the Taoist priest Li Daoding all witnessed the event.

**舒嫩四** In the first year of Chúnxī (1174 AD), the people of Ningyuán County in Tōngzhōu, led by Xīao Chúnlǐ and former official Ōuyáng Xuān, among others, collectively raised funds to build a bell tower and sacrificial altar at the Jiǔyí Temple to perform a *Huánglù Jìào* ritual. They agreed to use the remaining funds for other purposes, but they often embezzled the money. No one could investigate or verify this. Five years later, many of the people involved in the project died. Shu Nensì was taken to the underworld and saw people from his hometown, including his deceased relatives. He encountered an official who was chained to a pillar, and upon closer inspection, it was Chún Lǐ. Shu Nensì asked why Chún Lǐ was being punished, and Chún Lǐ replied that he was being punished in the underworld because he had concealed and embezzled money during the *Jiǔyí Jìào* ritual. Shu Nensì realized that he too had died and felt great sorrow. He then met an old acquaintance, a fortune-teller named Xiāo Zhūn, who told him not to be afraid and that he would return to life. Shortly after, Shu Nensì woke up, having been dead for three days. Another person from the same village, Huang Zhōnglì, was also taken to the underworld due to issues related to the *Jiǔyí Jìào* ritual. He was suddenly taken ill and died shortly after speaking about being pursued by officials in the underworld for his involvement in the ritual. **龚舆梦** In the fourth year of Qiándào (1168 AD), a scholar named Gōng Yú from Tánzhōu, along with six or seven other people from his hometown, traveled to the capital to take the provincial examination. They passed through Yíchūn to visit the Yǎngshān Temple and pray for a good dream. Gōng Yú dreamed of entering an official's office where he saw a piece of paper posted on a pillar that read "Gōng Yú cannot four" (with the number "four" broken and incomplete). Upon waking, he was very unhappy and thought he would fail the exam. When the spring examination results were announced, Gōng Yú passed, while the others failed. He then told others about his dream, saying that it had not come true. Some people who were interested in such matters said that the broken "four" should be read as "one," and the god was saying that Gōng Yú would pass the exam. This interpretation was indeed correct.

The Chaste and Virtuous Woman You A villager named Pang Liu, who lived near the southern outskirts of Nancheng, Jianchang, was simple and uneducated, devoted solely to farming. His sister-in-law, You, was somewhat beautiful but also violent and promiscuous, engaging in adultery with young men from the same village, which led to Pang Liu being constantly insulted and spat upon, yet he was powerless to stop it. Once, You stole a chicken and prepared to cook it. Pang Liu discovered this and searched her room, finding the chicken. He then attacked her with a knife, causing her to injure herself. She fled to a neighbor's house and falsely accused Pang Liu of trying to rape her, claiming he used a chicken as bait. The neighbors believed her and took her to the village head, who sent her to the county jail. The county magistrate found that You needed money to buy a plot of land, and Pang Liu, being poor and stingy, refused to give it to her. The magistrate, unable to see the truth, accepted the false accusation. Scribe Judge Zhao Jing supported the magistrate's decision, and Pang Liu was sentenced to death. However, You received ten thousand coins as a reward, and the magistrate ordered officials to visit her annually to honor her virtue. This led to the title "Chaste and Virtuous Woman," which became widely known in the county. However, Pang Liu was later found innocent, and the officials who wronged him were punished. The Buddhist monk at Lin Tian Temple, who was accused of having an affair with You, was beaten and later died from illness. All these events occurred in June of the fourth year of the Chongxi era, during the Southern Song Dynasty. Later, Dai Yi was removed from his position as provincial governor by Yan Xi, who exposed Zhao Jing's corruption. The county magistrate and other officials involved in the case were either executed or branded.

### Wednesday's Frogs South City farmer Zhou San, during the off-season, specialized in catching fish, turtles, eels, and loaches, and he killed the most frogs. He continued this until old age. In the tenth year of Chunxi (1183), he fell ill and could not name his symptoms. He first felt something moving in his abdomen near his chest, and the pain gradually became severe. He could no longer eat or drink. His family members reached out to massage him, and he felt like several frogs wriggling inside. After a long time, the condition worsened. He twisted and turned on the bed, jumping and tossing, moaning and crying in pain, suffering as if the frogs were suffering. After a year, he died. ### The Fields of Miaozhi Temple In Xincheng County, Jianchang, the Miaozhi Temple had high-quality land. The temple's monks had all died. A guest official named Lü came to the county seat and built a house, coveting the property. He entrusted the county magistrate Zhang to lease it. Lü's daughter had married the brother of Kong Jin, a military governor. Zhang feared Lü's influence and gave the land to him. The temple was thus abandoned, and the buildings were also demolished, leaving nothing behind. Zhang was about to be replaced and dreamed of a guest paying a visit. The guest said he was the land god of Miaozhi Temple and asked for the return of the land. He said, "Can make Lü return the land and appoint another monk to manage the temple and repair the temple buildings. Otherwise, disaster will come quickly." For three consecutive nights, the dream was the same. When he was dismissed, he dreamed again of the guest coming to pay a visit and pleading more earnestly. Zhang, feeling remorseful and fearful, told Lü. Lü paid no attention. Zhang was transferred to the position of安抚干官 in Hubei but died before he could take up the position. Lü dreamed of Zhang wearing a green robe as usual, accompanied by the temple land god, who came to scold him for his greed and warned him of the consequences. He repeatedly stated this. Lü still did not listen, and his eldest son supported him. He was soon appointed to govern a county in Sichuan. When he arrived at Wuchang, he died. His eldest son died shortly after.

**Liu Yang's Tang Shu Junbao** When Tang Shu Junbao, a scholar from Liu Yang, was studying and traveling in Qingjiang, he always passed through Yichun. In the year Chunxi Jia Chen (1174 AD), he visited the Two Kings of Yanshan to pray for a dream. That night, he dreamed of walking on a major road and meeting two scholars traveling together. He bowed and asked their names. The person in front said his name was Wang, and the one behind said his name was Ouyang. Soon, an attendant, like a receptionist, invited him to a public banquet. They arrived at a place with magnificent and grand houses. Two young men dressed in splendid attire, like sons of the nobility, sat with him. They drank and feasted, and after the banquet, they got up. A monk was nearby and greeted them. He then led Tang Shu to a separate building where there was a huge bell hanging on a frame. Tang Shu touched the bell and said to the monk, "Try striking it. What will happen?" The monk replied, "Even though the bell is complete, it needs to be cast in a great furnace before it can be struck. It cannot be struck now." Tang Shu asked why, and the monk explained, "If you strike it now, its sound will only be heard in one direction. But if it is cast in the great furnace and then struck with a bell, its sound will be heard far and wide, not just in this place." Tang Shu woke up in shock. In the year Bingwu (1176 AD), during the autumn examination in Tanzhou, he won the third place in the election. The first place was taken by Wang Yan, and the second by Ouyang. In the year Dingwei (1177 AD), in the southern province, Tang Shu was the best among many scholars, and I was actually in charge of the selection. Thus, I understood the omen of the great furnace, which was hidden in the dream with names. The two young men were likely the sons of Wang, and the monk was probably referred to as "little Buddha." Tang Shu wrote a record and had it carved on a stone, but only a summary of it remains.

**The Thunder God of Leizhou** - In the year 1176 AD, during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, Guilin experienced a prolonged drought with no rain for months. As the transition between autumn and winter approached, the fields and gardens were suffering. The governor of Guilin, Zhang Jingfu, and Shi sent a messenger to the temple of the Thunder God in Leizhou to inquire about when the rain would come. Upon arriving, the messenger presented the official document and stayed at the temple official's house. That night, the messenger and the temple official had a dream in which the Thunder God instructed them to report that there would be light rain three days before the Lantern Festival. The temple official also instructed the messenger to give 2,000 coins to the Left Department and to present them in the official document. The temple official further instructed the messenger to give the coins to the Left Department and to thank them. The next morning, the messenger returned with the document and the coins, but used them along the way. When the governor saw the document and asked where the coins were, only a few were left. The messenger took the coins from his bosom and presented them, but they turned into ashes. On January 12th, light rain fell, but it was insufficient to help the crops. **Liu Huasheng** - Liu Huasheng went to a nearby village and visited the Temple of the White Source. On his way back, he encountered rain and took shelter under a tree by the side of the road. He heard a woman's voice and saw two women walking in the rain, one old and one young. They called out to Liu Huasheng, saying, "We have been seeking revenge from you for 50 years. We have searched everywhere, and here we are." Liu Huasheng replied, "I have never killed anyone in my life, and I am not yet thirty. How can you say you have been seeking revenge for 50 years? You must be mistaken." The two women insisted, "We were indeed killed by you. How can there be any mistake? We will not let you go." Liu Huasheng was very afraid and ran to an inn, where he told the innkeeper about the incident. The innkeeper, who had not seen anything, believed Liu Huasheng was insane and led him to another place.

Liu briefly rested in the room, then went to the toilet, loosened his belt, and attempted to hang himself, but was rescued in time. To the left of the inn, there was a temple dedicated to the guardian deity, and the owner instructed Liu to pray for blessings. Two women followed and mockingly said, "If you want to pay homage to the great deity, why not spend ten coins on incense for a response?" Liu directly entered to earnestly request, and as soon as he stepped out, he fainted and lay unconscious for a while. Then, he suddenly woke up, saying that it was like a dream but not a dream; he saw a deity in a red robe with an ivory tablet, seated behind a desk handling affairs and ordering officials to check records. The deity then said, "Since Liu has observed ten precepts with sincerity, I specially grant him an extension of one year before releasing him." The two women bowed and waited outside, looking at each other and crying, as if they were not going to comply. The deity scolded them, and they stopped. Afterward, Liu was safe and sound as usual, and from then on, he never ate meat. It was during the early years of the Chongning era, and it is unknown if he still exists today. **Sour Filling from Jingde Temple** The young grandson of Zhang Qianshan, the county magistrate, and Yao Zhuo, a servant, was a monk at Jingde Temple. One night, during the full moon of the third month in the year Ding You of the Chunxi era, the boy dreamed of playing as usual and wandering to the front of the Buddha hall, where he met a tall monk who gave him a sour filling, which he placed in his sleeve. When he woke up, the filling was still in his hand. He told his mother he wanted to eat it, but she suspected it was unusual and told Yao Zhuo, who was alarmed. He quickly took the boy to the temple to inquire about the matter. It turned out that the previous night, Zhang the Inspector had set up a vegetarian feast at the pagoda yard, and the leftover sour filling was given to the boy. The next night, the boy dreamed again and was scolded by the monk for not eating the filling. The boy could not answer, and by morning, the filling was missing from the incense box.

He briefly rested in the room, then soon went out to the latrine, unfastened his belt, and attempted to hang himself. A person rushed to save him, and he was prevented from dying. To the left of the inn stood a shrine dedicated to Guangyou Wang. The innkeeper had him bow and pray for blessings. Two women followed, laughing without ceasing, and said, "You wish to pay homage to the great deity, yet you bought no incense—would ten coins be enough to make an offering?" Liu entered the shrine and earnestly made his request. No sooner had he stepped outside than he collapsed to the ground, unconscious. After a while, he awoke, saying it was as if he had been in a dream yet not quite a dream. He saw a deity in a crimson robe and a ceremonial tablet, seated behind a desk handling affairs. The deity ordered an official to check the records. Then he said, "Liu, having observed ten fasts with utmost sincerity, I shall grant you an extension of one decade of life. Immediately, you shall be restored." The two women stood in the courtyard, bowing in wait. They looked at each other, wiping their tears, as if reluctant. The deity scolded them, and they ceased. Liu then recovered and returned to normal health. From that day onward, he absolutely refused to eat meat. This was during the first year of the Chunchi era. It is unknown whether it still exists today. --- **Jingde Temple's Sour Filling** A servant of Zhang Qianshan, the magistrate of Nancheng, named Yao Zhuo, had a second son who became a monk at Jingde Temple. One of Yao Zhuo’s grandchildren, about ten years old, would occasionally visit his uncle. On the fifteenth day of the third month in the year Dingyou of the Chunchi era, the boy dreamed as usual, playing and wandering, until he arrived at the Buddha hall, where he met a tall monk who gave him a sour filling, placing it in his sleeve. When he woke up, the filling was clearly in his hand. He told his mother, who wanted to eat it, but she suspected something strange and told Yao Zhuo. Yao Zhuo was greatly startled and immediately took the boy to clarify the matter. It turned out that the previous evening, Zhang Tixing had come to the pagoda courtyard to set up a water and land offering, and the other monks had taken the leftover food. When compared, there was no difference at all. Yao Zhuo then took the filling home and placed it inside the incense box in the Buddha hall. The next night, the boy dreamed again of the monk, who scolded him, saying, "I gave you this sour filling, why did you not eat it? Since you have no use for it, I must take it back." The boy could not answer. By dawn, when he looked inside the incense box, it was gone.

### Final Translation **Liu Chengyi** Liu Dan, courtesy name De Yuan, was from Xin Cheng. In the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1144), he was nominated for the imperial examination and had a dream, but he did not tell anyone about it. Thirty years later, he passed the imperial examination and after more than two decades, he finally became an official in the capital. He was appointed as a Fengyi Lang, but he was not evaluated for promotion. His wife and children pressured him, and his friends and relatives encouraged him, saying, "If you persist in your principles like this, how can you extend rewards to your descendants?" Thus, he voluntarily requested a promotion and was appointed as a Chengyi Lang. Soon after, he encountered the emperor's birthday celebration and was granted a review of his service, which he believed would lead to his promotion to the rank of Lang. He thought this was a significant event and was very pleased. However, shortly after, while returning from serving as a Tongpan in Chen Zhou, he died on a boat in Jiujiang. At the time, he was still in good health. He took his imperial edicts and seals, sealed them, and wrote on them, "Open this after I have closed my eyes." After his death, his family opened the sealed documents and found that they described his past dream, in which he saw a strange person who warned him, "Your official career will end at the rank of Chengyi Lang. This is likely a divine warning. Therefore, I have always deeply wished not to advance to this rank. However, fate has been determined, and I fear it cannot be changed by human effort. My descendants should use this as a lesson." Those who heard this story believed that Liu understood his destiny but regretted that he did not fulfill his original intention. **Two Concubines of Liu** Liu Kong, a Fengshi Lang, was from Anfu, Jizhou, and was the son of Liu Ziang, the governor of Liyang. After the death of his wife, he had two concubines manage household affairs. One of them had already given birth to a son and then married into the Gao family, bringing four female servants with her. In the early years of the Chongxi era (1174), Liu Kong served as a Tongpan in Tongzhou, and the Gao woman was pregnant at that time. The concubine's son was around eleven or twelve years old. The concubine was cunning and feared that if the Gao woman gave birth to a son, in the future, the property would be divided, and her own favor would diminish. She secretly used immoral teachings to deceive and manipulate the Gao woman, but the Gao woman was pure and virtuous, and she did not feel jealous or have any flaws. The concubine's plans failed, so she began to teach her son to watch for her father to leave and attend to official duties or to cry and shout when guests were present, out of fear that her father loved this son very much. Every time her father returned, she would be worried.

The child, without speaking, complained in a secluded place that he was beaten by his mother and was already suspicious. One day, when food was served, the concubine prepared a soup and asked a maid to carry it to the child. There was a needle stuck in a vegetable stem, and the child was slightly pricked and vomited it out, crying, "Someone wants to kill me!" Afraid to ask, he saw the needle and questioned where it came from. The two concubines jointly testified that the maid had followed the mistress's intention to harm the child's life. He believed it to be true and all four maids were taken and sent to jail for interrogation, but no truth was found. The county magistrate, thinking the situation in the inner chambers was unclear and difficult to punish according to law, only flogged them and expelled them. The Gao family ultimately suffered a final separation; everyone outside knew it was a lie, but he alone did not realize it. Soon dismissed due to other matters, he just returned home and died. Zhang Diao, courtesy name Hezhong, was the grandson of Xuanwei Envoy Yao Zuo, appointed to an official position through family connections. When first appointed, he dreamed of climbing a three-story pavilion, and on the upper floor, there were hundreds of painted boards listing people's official titles and names, with one board inscribed with "Right Court Officer, Prefect of a certain state, Zhang Qian, etc.," where the "Qian" character was obscured by smoke and could not be fully read. At that time, the rank system did not include the prefixes "Left" or "Right." He found it strange. One person beside him said, "This is the new system of the Yuanyou era." He sighed and awoke. Another three years, the system of prefixes "Left" and "Right" began to be implemented. Zhang Hezhong later served as the magistrate of Chongren County in Fuzhou, holding the rank of Chief Officer, then as Deputy Prefect of Nanjian State, but did not take up the post due to mourning his mother. Later transferred to the position of Military Governor of Longyang, from then on, he had no desire for official advancement and did not seek promotion for ten years. An old friend, who happened to be a clerk in the Ministry of Works, submitted a petition to the Ministry of Personnel on his behalf, and he was promoted again to Right Attendant, but thinking his rank and position were already sufficient, he was silent and unhappy, and eventually died in this position. Chongren Deng Zhou wrote a biography for Zhang, recording his life story.

Garden Keeper Wu Liu A citizen of Linchuan named Wang Ming lived in a place called Fangjian, where he engaged in trade and accumulated a modest fortune. He bought an empty plot of land in the west of the city to cultivate as a vegetable garden and hired a strong servant named Wu Liu to plant and cultivate. He also allowed Wu Liu to sell the surplus for profit. In the year 1161 during the Southern Song Dynasty, Wu Liu strongly requested to leave and could not be persuaded to stay. Wang Ming was very resentful. Shortly after, he dreamed that Wu Liu came and worked as usual and reprimanded him by saying, "You have already requested to leave, why are you back?" Wu Liu replied, "I have been here since September 6th." Wang Ming woke up and became suspicious. Soon he heard that Wu Liu had died. On another day, he went to the garden and saw a hired farmer who said that during the past few nights, a dog gave birth to two puppies, one of which did not survive. Wang Ming finally realized that it was not Wu Liu. He asked when, and the farmer said the sixth day of the first month. Wang Ming told him about the dream, and the wife of a nearby gardener also had the same dream that night. They went together to see the new puppy, which opened its eyes and seemed to respond when called "Wu Liu." Wang Ming believed that because Wu Liu had hidden the profits from selling vegetables in his lifetime, he was receiving this punishment. Shi Shuxian Shi Shuxian was a wealthy man from Nancheng who married a granddaughter of the Prince of Puc. The official servant Wu Rong was entrusted with managing finances and expenditures, accumulating fraud and corruption, which was reported to the military. The military governor Zhao, who was not impartial, and his wife, being relatives, thoroughly investigated the crime, and Wu Rong was sentenced to flogging and exiled to Quanzhou. In the sixth year of the Chunxi era (1179), Shi Shuxian died at home. Wu Rong did not know this and was traveling when he met a merchant in white robes who resembled the master. He bowed and asked when he arrived, and the man replied that he had come urgently for business and had no time to chat. They parted ways, and Wu Rong went to an inn to look for him but found him nowhere in the city. Later, Wu Rong was pardoned and returned home to visit Shi Shuxian, only to learn that he had died long ago in Quanzhou. Shortly after, Wu Rong also died.

The Legend of the West Lake Woman During the Dading era of Emperor Xiaozong's reign, a certain official from Jiangxi traveled to the capital for an appointment and, while touring West Lake, walked alone until he was tired and took a short rest by the roadside. He saw a woman with two buns on her head inside a nearby house, who was brightly beautiful and captivating. He could not take his eyes off her, and she also looked at him with emotion in her eyes. The man was deeply infatuated and seemed lost in thought. From then on, he often went to see her, and the woman always came out to meet him. They laughed and interacted closely, and she teased him with subtle words, showing no sign of shyness or refusal. She hoped for a brief moment of pleasure but found it impossible to achieve. After he was appointed to an official position, he said goodbye, and the woman took the opportunity to speak privately. She said, "Since we met each other, we both fell in love with each other. I wanted to follow you to the west, but my parents would definitely not allow it. To run away and fulfill my wishes would be too much for me to bear. It made me restless day and night. What can be done?" The man sought his parents' approval and bribed them with a large sum of money, but they firmly refused. After returning home, there was no further communication. Five years later, he went to the capital again for an official appointment and hurriedly sought out his old haunts, but found nothing. He was filled with disappointment.

Certainly, here is the final translation of the source text: --- Suddenly, he met her halfway on the road. Although she had aged, her appearance was even more graceful and beautiful. He called out and bowed, asking after her. She said, "It has been so long since we last met, have you forgotten me?" The man was very happy and asked her why she had moved. She replied, "I have been married for a long time and live in a certain alley in the city. My husband was temporarily imprisoned for financial matters, so I came out to seek help. I did not expect to meet an old friend. Can you come over for tea?" The man happily walked with her for about two miles, passing his inn, and she pointed it out. She suggested they stay there comfortably, so they went in and spent the night. The man's inn was secluded, and there were no other guests. She said, "This place is suitable for staying; there is no need to go to my house." They then took her hand and entered her room, where she stayed for half a year. During this time, she no longer cared about her family and occasionally went out, making no demands or requests. The man did not remember that she was married and never asked about bringing her along. At the beginning, she put on a formal robe and frowned, saying, "Since you left, I have been suffering greatly from missing you. I have been listless and ill, and I died within a year. Now, this body is no longer a living person. Due to past-life karma, my spirit is with you. The time of happiness is limited, and there will be no more reunion. There is no way to accompany you after my death. I truly fear suspicion and surprise, so I will explain in detail. The negative energy has deeply affected you, and you are likely to fall ill. Only suitable to take Pingwei San to restore and stabilize your qi and blood." The man was shocked and saddened for a long time and then said, "I once read in *Imaginary Tales* about Sun Jiudong encountering a ghost and taking this medicine. I thought the medicine was all mild herbs, how could it have such effects?" She said, "It uses Atractylodes to dispel evil qi, which is the best quality. Just follow my words, and then I cried." That night, they slept together as usual. At dawn, she cried bitterly and parted. He suddenly fell ill and took the medicine as advised. Later, he often told others about it, still feeling very sad. My cousin, Gui Zixi, knew this story. --- This translation aims to be both faithful to the original text and natural in English, addressing the idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as planned.

Jiang Liangfu A man from Nan Cheng named Jiang Liangfu failed to become a scholar and spent his life in a village school. In the tenth year of the Chongxi era, he died of illness but revived after half a day. He told his family, "I was just now being chased to the underworld and stood in the courtyard, waiting for the King of Hell, who had not yet come out. Thus, I was allowed to return temporarily, but could not stay long." After two periods of time, he finally passed away. Far-An Elderly Veteran A household in Yuan'an, Xiangzhou, firmly believed in immortals and Buddhas and once practiced the Taoist ritual of Master Lu Pu Yuan. Many people participated in the ceremony, and after the vegetarian offerings were completed, an elderly veteran came from outside, wearing a tattered blue cotton robe, carrying a pair of broken straw shoes, and two bamboo baskets. He dropped his burden and sat cross-legged, calling out for food. They refused him but he persisted. The household still had leftover food, which they gave to him. He then asked for wine, and when given a small jar, he drank it all in one go, doing this up to three times. The host was amazed by his capacity and asked, "Still able to drink alcohol?" He replied, "It is indeed my wish, but for your household, the expenses have been great, so I dare not ask." When the wine arrived, it was empty upon reaching his hand, not leaving a single drop. The host slowly asked what the purpose of today's vegetarian offering was, and he told the reason for the guest. The host said, "If the True Person Lu himself came, he would certainly not recognize the host." He pointed to a painting on the wall.

A guest who presented an image stared and smiled, saying, "I have indeed seen his appearance and attire, which are completely different from this. I have five feet of silk, which I will use to create a copy." The host was pleased and handed it over. The guest took the silk, did not apply any pigment, but placed it in his hand and rubbed it. Suddenly, he spat out something and used it to wipe the remaining dirt. The host then became displeased. Realizing he was drunk and unable to do anything, the bystanders cursed and left. After a long time, the guest put the silk into an empty bottle, bowed and smiled, and left. A child looked inside the bottle and saw that the image of a celestial being had been completed, with clothes and shoes clearly visible, almost identical to the previous guest. The family then realized that a true immortal had descended, regretting not having met him. They adorned the image and placed it in a clean room, treating it with reverence. At that time, in the seventh year of the Chongxi era, in the Xinchang district of Junzhou, the county magistrate Zou Jianshan recorded this event. --- **巴东太守** Yú Shàozǔ, a native of Fèngxin, passed the imperial examinations in the year Dingchou of the Shaoxing era and was appointed as the deputy governor of Jingzhou at the end of the Chongxi era. When he was to take up his post, because the journey was long, he did not bring his family. Only two of his sons and a guest traveled together. On the second day, they arrived at the boundary of Fenning County, at Maoshan. In the evening, they stayed at a temple in a pine grove. As soon as they entered the path, the abbot and his disciples greeted them with great respect. There was a Taoist living in the temple as well. After they had alighted from their carriage and sat down in the Dharma hall, Yú greeted the abbot with the courtesy due to a monk. He asked, "Just now, I did not send a messenger ahead. How did you know we were coming?" The abbot replied, "This mountain village is quiet, and I do not usually hear news of outsiders. But this morning, I saw a Taoist talking about a dream he had last night, in which a high official was to arrive here. So we have been sweeping and tidying all day, waiting respectfully. And indeed, your official has arrived. One day, you will surely be a great person." Yú was secretly pleased. After the abbot left, he ordered the Taoist to be invited and offered him wine. He then asked about the dream, saying, "It is indeed so, but I don’t have time to explain it to the monk in detail. Please tell me the whole story. Last night, I dreamed that a noble guest came to the temple and went straight into the ancient wood behind the temple, where there was a rotten hole in a large tree, and the tree was very lush. I was surprised by this, and..."

One person claimed to be the governor of a certain prefecture. I heard this and thought he was actually a deputy official, only one rank below the governor, and living in a wooden cave, which is not a good omen. The Taoist used the story of Ding Gu and the plum tree to comfort me, and I felt somewhat relieved. I gave the Taoist one garment and one thousand coins as a gift and sent him away. When he arrived at his post and was about to be replaced, there was a vacancy in the governorship of Ba Dong. The governor of Jing Province, Zhao, recommended his name, and the imperial court agreed to his request, and the order was issued. Zhao sent officials to escort him west, but before he could leave, he fell ill. Zhao instructed his advisor Zhang Zhen to bring a doctor to treat him. The doctor said his pulse had already stopped, but Zhao gave him gold pills, and he seemed to improve slightly. One day, while in his main bedroom, he called his son and said, "The sky has turned dark, why aren't you lighting the lamps?" His son replied, "It should be daytime," and he scolded him. His servant lit two candles, but he still said he saw no light. In a short while, the number of candles increased to over ten, but he still saw no light. His son suspected he had lost his sight, but when he looked closely, his eyes were clear. A short while later, he died. It seemed that at the moment of death, his spirit had dissipated, and this is what they meant by "the light of life has gone out."

Lin Xuezheng Lin Jun, an elderly scholar from Fuzhou, devoted himself to diligent study from a young age but remained unrecognized throughout his life, teaching students as his profession. He lived a frugal and simple life, residing in the southern part of the state. Chen Wei Gong from Putian, who had a long-standing relationship with Lin Jun, sent a letter to invite him to the imperial court when he became prime minister, hoping to achieve something significant for him. Lin Jun refused to go. Wang Zhan Shu, the vice-premier and governor of Fujian, upon learning of Lin Jun's virtuous conduct, recommended him to Chen Wei Gong, who ordered greater respect and courtesy shown to Lin Jun. Wang Zhan Shu visited Lin Jun's residence, sent five soldiers and one carriage with 30,000 coins to hire him as a scholar. Lin Jun was very pleased, hosted the envoy in a side room, prepared a banquet, and arranged to leave immediately at dawn. That night, without any illness, he passed away. The villagers mourned his poverty, saying that he would not have received such unexpected wealth if he had not died, and thus would not have entered the registry of the dead. They realized that exceeding one's share and hasty pursuit indeed have a predetermined fate. Li Shanxue, the prefect of Junzhou, who was once under Lin Jun's tutelage, recounted the entire story in detail.

**The Two Women of the Zhou Family** During the reign of Emperor Ling, a man named Deng Zha from the southern city had a son who employed a fieldhand named Zhou Pu. Zhou Pu’s wife, Gao, served as a wet nurse. At the time, her husband had already died. Gao had an affair with a wicked young man, which led to pregnancy. Fearing that she would be accused, she drowned the child. Several years later, she contracted a mysterious illness, her abdomen swelled to an extreme degree, and her complexion turned pale and waxy. She screamed in pain day and night, feeling as if the weather was burning her body like fire. She lamented, "Why do you bring a baby to disturb me?" She then extended her foot as if to kick or stomp. She said to those around her, "You people do not remove it for me, so I will step down from the bed myself." A short while later, she again made a gesture of pushing or brushing away, and she looked into the void, arguing in vain. All knew of her grievance of killing her child and her suffering this retribution in life. The following month, she died. Her daughter, who was present, witnessed it with her own eyes. After her daughter married, she gave birth to another daughter and, already poor, could not provide for her. She immediately immersed the child in a water basin. The following year, she became pregnant again. She saw a strange snake enter the room, was shocked, and fell ill. She became extremely ill and spoke incoherently at the moment of death, for several days slightly similar to the time of her mother's death.

Saozhou The Gentleman of the Fourth Rank, Zhao Shanzai, styled Yanping, lived in Jianchang. In the year Dingwei of Chunxi (1187), he was appointed as the governor of Yuezhou, but he did not take up the post and died at home in November. The following year, his son, Ruchang, dreamed that he arrived at the government office. Walking slowly, he reached the courtyard and saw his father in official robes sitting at the desk, deciding cases. He saw Ruchang arrive and was very happy. Before they could speak, he looked around the four corners of the room and saw the building filled with documents. His father said, "Just after I died, I was immediately appointed as an official in the underworld. My power is no less than when I was serving as a governor in life, but I especially suffer from the complexity of reviewing documents. I have no moment of leisure, and I am not happy living here." Ruchang said, "Father, if you are not happy, why don't you seek to leave?" His father replied, "Two successors have already been appointed, and I will be relieved soon." Ruchang asked, "Who are the successors? Can their names be known?" His father said, "They are Zhou Zhaoqing and Tong Bishu, two local people." After he finished speaking, Ruchang woke up. The next morning, he told his mother, Dai, and his younger brothers and sisters, who all cried. The news spread joyfully through the city, and those who heard it said...

It is inappropriate to appoint three officials from the same county to the same position. Those who believe in and follow Zhao Qian, such as Zhou Xi, a Cao Feng Lang, were recently transferred to oversee mining and smelting in Ganzhou, where he was to serve but died the following winter. Bo Yu, a Cao Qing Lang, heard this and deeply disapproved. At the time, he was from Wan'an County in Jizhou, and he immediately resigned his post to report to the imperial court, hoping to be appointed to this position in order to appease Zhao Mengyun. If someone had already taken over from Zhou, he would have accepted the position. In the year Renzi of Shaoxi (1192 AD), after his term was completed, the Ministry of Personnel appointed him as the governor of Leizhou. He stayed in the capital city, waiting for an audience with the emperor, during which he bought two young concubines. He was delayed for a long time, and as the year came to an end, he died in the guesthouse. Three gentlemen in Jianchang were praised for their intelligence and integrity, and they were considered almost divine. They passed away within two or three years of each other, much like the way one might accumulate merit and experience in the world. The record in Jia Zhi mentions that Sun Fang, Shi Ni, Xu Ke, and others followed one after another to become Tai Shan Fu Jun, all appointed by the same edict, but this was a minor difference compared to the events described here, as they were all local officials. --- Fengcheng Lower Ferry In Fengcheng County, there is a new river at the intersection of three ports, and a ferry crossing named Xia Du. In the eighth month of the year Geng Xu of Shaoxing (1170 AD), a woman named Wang Ao, who lived on the riverbank, dreamed of a guest who was poorly dressed and emaciated, as if she had known him all her life. He came to thank her, saying, "I have stayed here for a year, causing much trouble. In a few days, I will leave, so I come to bid farewell." When Wang Ao woke up, she told her son about the dream, but he did not know what it meant. Five days later, two sons of Yan Kecheng from the village of Ku Zhu in the same county went to the West Mountain to visit the Yu Long Palace to pay homage to Xu Shenjun. As they crossed the river on a boat, it capsized before they could reach the other side, and they drowned. The local people, who worshiped Xu Shenjun with great reverence, suspected that the two Yan brothers had been punished for drinking and eating meat along the way.

### Final Translation **Monks of Tushita Temple** In Fenning County, the Tushita Temple had a copy of the *Yuanjue Sutra* written by Zhang Tianjue, which was destroyed by fire. Afterward, a local resident named Huang obtained it. The monks of the temple requested it back, but Huang refused. Huang was a foolish person who did not know the value of the sutra. His son, Jianjing, then destroyed it to make a bed. After a long time, Jianjing contracted leprosy, suffered pain and decay for several years, and eventually died. **Wu Shen and the Two Dragons** Wu Yi, a Taoist from Ying, passed the local examination in 1176 (丙午) during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong but failed the imperial examination in 1177 (丁未). He dreamed of two dragons embracing his body and dreamed of someone telling him to change his name to Shen, saying that he would be recommended again. By the spring of 1189 (己酉), when the emperor ascended the throne, Wu believed he would personally secure the top rank. He often told others, "The two dragons are embracing my body. In the coming year, when the dragon soars, I will be among the scholars. I will surely be the top scholar in the world." He changed his name to Shen and indeed participated in the selection again, but ended up at the bottom of the list. His uncle, Yinglong, was waiting to be listed on a smaller list, and there was a person named Shi Yinglong. The omen was fulfilled. By 1190 (庚戌), when the provincial examination took place, Wu did not succeed. He became ill with disappointment and returned home. Suddenly, he put on a ceremonial robe, boots, and a hat, and went to bow to his grandmother and mother. His family members were surprised and asked him. He replied, "The underworld has summoned me to be a judge. I am now going." He bowed twice to say goodbye and then went straight to the well, fell into it, and died.

The Land of Zīshèng Temple Fàn Xún, a minor official in Jiànchāng, borrowed ten thousand coins from the elder monk of Zīshèng Temple to marry off his son. After twenty years, the monk died, and Fàn Xún returned to take charge. Due to neglect, he failed to repay the debt. Later, Fàn Xún fell gravely ill and called his son Guangguan, saying, "Do you remember when you married? I once borrowed money from Zīshèng Temple. Now the local temple deity has sent someone to escort the elder monk to demand repayment. You must quickly buy paper money to burn for him." He then pointed to the family members and said, "The land deity's messenger along with the elder monk are standing here respectfully. Don't you see them?" After burning the paper money, he said, "The two have left. They want to go to Bàoēn Temple to find Xu Shěnggān to handle some matters." That night, Fàn Xún died. Xu's name was Yǐníng, a person from Láizhōu, who had just returned from serving as the supervisor of the military wine warehouse in Jízhōu. Not long after, he also died. At the time of the seventh year of the Chúnxī era, before this, Xu's father, a Fèngzhí Dàfū, lived in that temple. The temple people used temple property under his name to seek profit, and Fèngzhí therefore concealed much of his wealth. Yǐníng heard about it and thus it happened. The monk Zǔ Jué said.

Grand Secretary Zhang Grand Secretary Zhang Yanwen, a man of great virtue, was once a commoner when he formed a close friendship with a monk named Shaoguang from Jingde Temple in Jianchang. Later, Shaoguang became a Censorial Adviser. When Shaoguang died in his hometown, Grand Secretary Zhang was unaware of it. In a dream, Shaoguang appeared before him, shackled and standing in the courtyard, weeping and pleading, "Shaoguang died on a certain day of a certain month due to the heavy weight of my past sins, sinking into hell and unable to escape. I hope you will not forget our lifelong friendship and will specially save and release me. I ask you to perform Buddhist rituals to help me in the afterlife. I remember having one tael of gold with my disciple Master Yao and twenty thousand coins with a certain family in the market. If you seek and use them, perhaps you can help." Grand Secretary Zhang agreed. Later, he sent a servant back to inquire about the matter, and everything matched what Shaoguang had said. He then instructed his son Yuanjin to retrieve the gold and money, and performed Buddhist rituals, chanting scriptures and turning the wheel. He also sculpted an image of Guanyin at Taiping Xingguo Temple, lit a perpetual lamp to honor it, and engraved the story on stone to show others. In the early Chunxi era, when Grand Secretary Zhang was inspecting the prefectures under his jurisdiction and arrived twenty li north of Daozhou, he stayed at Xingyuan Temple. That night, he dreamed of a woman asking to be buried. Her words were very earnest. The next morning, he told the temple’s abbot, who found her coffin and handed it over to the magistrate of Yingdao, who buried her in the fields. Such was the depth of his compassionate heart. As for the "Seven Nieces," their meaning is unclear.

According to the *Yi Zhi*, in Tingzhou there were seven female spirits, and Ganzhou also had them. These are mountain spirits, widespread in cities, towns, and villages, and many temples were built for them. Their appearance was that of seven women, and they could bring about misfortune or blessings. In the tenth year of the Chunxi era, Wang Daguang, a person from Lin'an, was an official in charge of mining and smelting. The gatekeeper reported, "Early this morning, shortly after opening the door, a seller of soy milk came. Several women came out of the house and said to him, 'You will return here shortly with money.' He did not come out after a long time, and no one knew who to tell about this." Wang was startled and said, "My family members should not be buying things so early in the morning; it must be false. If it is true, it is likely that the spirits are pretending. But with the bright light of day, how could it be like this?" The gatekeeper mentioned the seven female spirits, and Wang then went to see the temple. The soy milk was on the incense table, and Wang immediately ordered it to be cooked and offered, and he compensated for its value. The ritual at Zhen Qi's home: Zhen Qi, the governor of Nankang, fell ill and became seriously ill. The doctors had given up. His son, Zhi, said, "If we invite a Taoist priest from the Tianqing Temple, we will set up a ritual feast at the military headquarters. We will make a sincere request and prepare with utmost respect. All the utensils, bowls, and chopsticks will be replaced with new ones." After the ritual was completed, Zhen Qi, his two sons, and the Taoist priest all dreamed of entering a grand government office. They saw a god who called out, "Zhen Qi's lifespan is nearly over. The Heavenly Emperor, seeing that your two sons are filial and sincere, has decided to grant them an extension of their lives. And the ritual feast was prepared with utmost purity and according to the rules. He has granted you an extension of twelve years." The next day, they all wanted to tell others. Zhi said, "If we truly receive a response, we should write it down instead of speaking it, so it can be believed." So they followed his request, and all the dreams were the same. Everyone congratulated the Zhen family, believing that the elder's lifespan would be extended by the number of years granted. However, he died on that day the following year, calculating the time, it was exactly twelve months. Those who understood said that 'one jī' means twelve years. The underworld does not want to state it clearly, so they use one month as one year. The limit set by heaven cannot be escaped in this way.

**Records of the Strange and the Marvelous, Volume 6, Part A** <Novelists' Collection, Category of Strange Tales, *Records of the Strange and the Marvelous*> Imperially Approved Four Treasuries **Records of the Strange and the Marvelous, Volume 7, Part A** By Song Dynasty, Hong Mai **Lady Cai Zhen** Chen Daoguang, courtesy name Bu Jin, was from Nancheng. After resigning from his post in Guilin, he returned home and passed through Dongting Lake. In a dream, he saw a child dressed in ceremonial robes who said he was the son of a dragon from the cave and had been commanded to inform him not to eat garlic, leeks, or dog meat. Three years later, he would meet someone. When the time came, in June, he was summoned by the military office in Hedong to serve in Shangzhou. On his journey, he passed through Lantian and stayed at Lanqiao Post Station. In another dream, he saw the child holding a jade staff and said, "The fairy awaits your arrival." The child then led him along. They reached a steep cliff and a sheer wall. The child struck the stone wall with the jade staff, and they heard a loud clanging sound as the lock was released. They entered the cave, where the halls and rooms were magnificent and splendid, adorned with gold and precious stones, and filled with beautiful flowers and trees that were rarely seen in the world. As they advanced, they arrived at the main hall, where they saw a beautiful young woman, about the age of marriage, with an ethereal and delicate appearance, as if she were a being from the heavens. She was sitting quietly, writing Buddhist scriptures. Upon seeing Chen, she was very pleased and invited him to sit opposite her. Their conversation flowed like clouds, and Chen took the opportunity to flirt with her, asking, "Do you feel bored living alone?" She smiled and said, "Divine beings are not troubled by boredom." Later, they prepared wine and drank together, consuming ten cups. She invited him to sit in the room, which was decorated with brocade and embroidery, and candles were lit.

A woman as large as a beam said, "It is the hottest time of summer in the human world, but here there is no heat. I feel cool as if it were early autumn." She spoke calmly and said, "I am the daughter of Cai, the True Person, currently residing in Ji. Because my worldly ties are not yet complete, I should protect people. My family is recorded in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*. My given name is Nai, my courtesy name is Jingniang, and my pet name is Cixi. As a child, I was skilled in playing the Qin zither. My parents changed my name to Zhen娘 because it was similar to the name of a girl from the Peng family. It is fortunate to meet you. You have the talent of a celestial being, but the complexities of the world do not allow me to stay here for long." She then said that the deity of fate did not want you to hold a high office, fearing that you might fall again. Therefore, she gave you a jade tablet and said, "Since you have transcended the material world, you should have a record." She wrote ten quatrains in one go. The first one said: "Her jade-like beauty returns from the green cave, A fairy in the cave has a letter urging her, The letter asks me why I have so many affairs, Why don't I ride the phoenix and come early?" The second one said: "I always worry that ordinary talents are not fit for immortality, It is fortunate to meet the divine maiden who holds the marriage ties, In the clouds, the golden lock opens faintly, The path leads into the small heaven of Ma Gu." The third one said: "Sea stones,

Pomegranate flowers reflected in ornate windows, green lotus blooms in a silver pond, a blue phoenix does not dance, and a green snake lies still. The courtyard is filled with spring breeze, and the day seems endless. In the fourth stanza, a deep, fragrant mist reflects in the windows and doors, with a gentle breeze blowing from the eaves all day. Sweat and rain suddenly become scarce, and worldly concerns fade away; one realizes they are in the celestial palace of the divine. In the fifth stanza, Laozi passed westward and became the Buddha; do not be surprised by the scriptures written on palm leaves. One often criticizes Yang Guifei's status as a celestial being, yet even a parrot can recite the truth. In the sixth stanza, one often wonders about Bai Juyi's "Song of Everlasting Sorrow," with jewelry and personal items sent as messages, too emotionally heavy. Now, one believes that in the celestial realm, there is a longing for human voices and messages. In the seventh stanza, upon arriving at the white jade hall of the divine, the fragrance fills one's clothes, neither dragon nor musk, nor incense, but rather a scent from the heavens of a foreign land. In the eighth stanza, a beautiful woman leans against the sky, smiling, and the pale, ethereal woman embodies the spirit of the moon. Even if one is not allowed to stay for long, it is still better than not meeting anyone in the human world. In the ninth stanza, after drinking the divine elixir, the sun sets, and a moment of joy is worth more than gold. How can one stay when worldly concerns fill the mind? The phoenix flute should not evoke a sound of parting. In the tenth stanza, the jade woman leans against the sky, smiling, and the pale, ethereal woman embodies the spirit of the moon.

The water flows around three islands, and peaches of immortality grow among five clouds. If not for those places being mundane and coarse, Liu An and Ruan Zhou would have been lost in confusion and error. After writing, the woman recited a poem and instructed a servant to play a flute tune called "Farewell to the Phoenix." When the tune ended, she woke up, and the flute sound lingered in her ears. Two nights later, she dreamed again of a child carrying a poem plaque, which said: "The Immortal thanks you, Lady Jade, who is actually a celestial maiden." The moon maiden, a celestial being, pointed out that it was absurd for the woman to belittle the immortals, comparing her to Liu An and Ruan Zhou, who often interacted with them. The woman asked why she would insult them so much. Laozi, the most revered figure in the nine heavens, is now replaced with "Daoist Deity of Lesser Rank," and "light and unrestrained, all mundane" is used instead. The woman followed the child's instructions, and the child left, saying, "Scholars in the human world are frivolous and fond of mocking and destroying others. They turn around and smile, then leave. From then on, she did not meet him again. Chen wrote about her, and the woman drank with Chen, spending the entire night in conversation, without any disorder. This is not like the celestial maidens recorded in Tang dynasty legends. --- Zhang Cheng Married a Wife Huang Ze, a scholar from Linchuan, passed the imperial examinations in the fifth year of Qian Dao and was appointed to oversee the wine tax in Anyuan County, Hengzhou. He lived in his hometown while waiting for his position. A fellow county scholar, Huang Zaoqing, dreamed that his friend Zhang Cheng married Huang Ze's wife Zhu. The next day, he told Zhang Cheng about the dream. Zhang Cheng laughed and was angry, saying, "Huang Zongde is still in his prime, and my wife is fine. How could such a thing happen? Don't tease me!" Soon after, Huang Ze was appointed to his official position and died after more than a year. Then Zhang Cheng also lost his wife, and later, he married Huang Ze's wife as his second wife.
