**Yao Diguang** During the implementation of the San She Fa (Three-House Law) in the Zhehe era, Yao Diguang was a military student in Jianchang Xincheng. He once dreamed of a divine encounter to predict his future success or failure. In the dream, he was already dressed in official attire and set incense outside his residence to express gratitude. Upon waking, he did not understand the meaning of the dream. Some said that the old student would receive a special examination and be granted an imperial edict, which would make him an assistant teacher in various states. However, he felt disappointed and decided to pack up his books and retire from official duties, giving up on the path to honor and fame. **Zongshi Shan Zhi** Zongshi Shan Zhi, a member of the imperial clan, lived in Mazhou, the southern part of Jianchang. Both he and his son Ru Tai were once recommended and summoned for an examination. During the year 1159, they received abundant grace and were supposed to be promoted to a high-ranking position. They both traveled to the capital to handle their affairs but found themselves stranded in a guesthouse where their traveling bags were nearly empty. They decided to return west and asked a local person named Fu Yong for help. After returning home, his father dreamed that his son's edict had arrived, but it was already too late. Upon waking, he was puzzled and thought that there might have been some misfortune on the road. A few days later, he had another dream where he walked to the Lintian Temple, two miles from their home, and found himself alone there. Only his son's wife, Zheng, was with him in the dream, and she also felt uneasy. The next morning, he told his family about the dream, and they all laughed it off. Two days later, he suddenly fell ill with severe throat pain and could not swallow food, medicine, or soup. He died after a few days. When his son's edict finally arrived, he had already passed away and was unable to receive it. Soon after, Zheng, his son's wife, also died. Their two coffins were temporarily stored at the Lintian Temple.

### Translation #### Deng Xing Shi Deng Xitang, from Jianchang, married the daughter of Li Jingzhi, who held the title of Cao Feng Lang. They had three sons and one daughter. The daughter married Shen Zongzhen, who held the title of Chengyi Lang. Their eldest son was named Da Fang, and their second son was named Xing Shi, who was the brother-in-law of the daughter. Xing Shi was studious and had a good reputation. He dreamed of being summoned to a place with a grand and magnificent building. Three handsome men sat on the dais, arranging wine and music. Several female attendants, wearing special headgear and red wide-sleeved robes, were arranged on both sides, singing or dancing. Xing Shi observed them intently without looking away. After a long time, he recognized one of the courtesans as his sister. His sister also frequently looked at him with her eyes. Soon, a man called Xing Shi forward and ordered him to strike a drum. Xing Shi declined, saying he was a scholar and knew nothing about this art. The man insisted, saying just hit it as you like, even if it doesn't follow the rhythm, it won't hurt. Upon waking, he felt uneasy and told his parents. The brothers and sisters, without planning, had the same dream and were amazed. Soon after, Shen Zongzhen went to the Ministry of Public Health in the imperial court. Deng's daughter followed her husband and died in Lin'an. Xing Shi died in the countryside. The three young men were known as the Three Young Men of the Wood. There were many temples dedicated to them in Lianchang. Xitang lived near one of these temples and therefore was affected by their misfortune. #### Tong Hanchen Tong Hanchen, a scholar from Nancheng, died at the age of twenty. His old friend Cai Yan dreamed of him coming to ask for news. The interaction was as if it were a normal day. Then for several nights, or every other night, he would see him. Cai Yan was very afraid and moved his sleeping quarters to another room. He dreamed of him again and he cried, saying, "Everyone dies, but my injustice is unspeakable." Try to look at my form. When he looked, it had turned into a large centipede with red feet, eight feet long, crawling along the wall. Cai Yan was shocked awake and never dreamed of him again.

Wang Fu of Jianchang In the Qian Dao period, Wang Fu, a military officer from Jianchang Prefecture, was on night patrol in the garden. After midnight, he encountered a young and beautiful woman at the main gate of the house. She smiled and said to Wang Fu, "I am the daughter of the old lady who lives in the military compound. I have long admired you, so I came out at night to spend the night with you." Wang Fu was extremely surprised and happy, took her to his sleeping quarters, and she left at dawn. From then on, he became deeply attached and could not let go. Even when he was supposed to be on duty, he would still take her, and this went on for several months until he became as thin as a ghost. During the day, he would sleep deeply, and his parents were worried. His father followed him wherever he went, mingling with the crowd to spy on him, and saw the woman coming to meet Wang Fu, they were intimately happy together. The next day, his father called him and scolded him, asking about his illness, but he refused to say anything, only saying he had never been sick. His father became angry and wanted to beat him, but Wang Fu finally told the truth. His father arranged for him to stay home for treatment and secretly asked the old soldiers in the prefecture if there was indeed an old woman's daughter, and they confirmed it. They were surprised that she could often go out, and they were both surprised and doubtful. One day, when Wang Fu was on duty again, his father went there to investigate, and the woman came again. His father lit a lamp and chased her, and she ran in a panic into the Temple of the Heavenly King and disappeared. The next morning, they checked and found that she was a servant girl holding a bridal chest. They brought Wang Fu forward, and he lowered his head and said nothing. His father then smashed the image of the Heavenly King. Wang Fu covered his face, sighed, and cried, and died after more than ten days.

Xu Defense Officer A man from Jishui, Jizhou, Luo Qinruo, along with Yang Zhubu, the county magistrate, and the eye doctor Xu Yuan, were strolling in the outskirts of the county when they encountered a guest who stared intently and did not stop. The three of them asked him, "Can you tell fortunes?" He replied, "Yes." They then tested him by asking, "What about you, Luo? You will one day become a high-ranking official." To Yang, he said, "Your destiny is modest, but you will gain status through your children. If you retire early, you can be ennobled as a junior official." Finally, he said to Xu, "You are truly a man of high status." The three looked at each other in astonishment, and even the children and servants laughed at his absurdity. They ridiculed him, saying, "Luo and Yang are both officials who have passed the imperial examinations, but Xu is only a doctor. He carries a medicine bag and practices medicine, earning a hundred coins per day. He has no means of supporting himself. How can he be of high status?" The guest said, "It is beyond your understanding. The one who approaches the moon is called a man of high status. This gentleman is about to meet his fate." They all laughed and dispersed. Later, Luo and Yang achieved the status predicted, while Xu, after wandering to Lin'an, was unable to find success. Just then, Empress Dowager Xianzong fell ill with an eye disease and sought a doctor from the common people. Xu was able to demonstrate his effectiveness, was appointed to an official position, and received numerous rewards, including a residence. He was known as Xu Defense Officer and had a son who passed the imperial examinations.

Huang Zuozi Huang Zuozi, a student from Fuzhou, was a scholar at the Imperial Academy and participated in the recommendation book of the seventh year of the Chongxi era. That same winter, a scholar named Wang from Chiyang also went to take the provincial examination. Wang's family was very wealthy, and they gave Huang Zuozi a sum of one hundred thousand coins to conclude their course or agreement. Wang was devoted to a certain deity, which was extremely effective and powerful. Huang offered prayers, and in his dream, the deity told him, "You will definitely pass the examination next spring." The deity then pointed to a woman and said, "This is your wife." Huang looked at the woman's appearance, which was not very tall, and saw a hair ornament made of silk and flowers on her head. In a moment of confusion, the deity scratched Huang's hand. Upon waking, he still felt a slight numbness in his hand. He thought to himself, "If I marry a woman after passing the examination, won't she have precious jewelry and ornaments? But why would she have a hair ornament made of silk and flowers?" Wang and Huang had been friends for a long time and enjoyed each other's company. They made an agreement: if Huang passed the examination, Wang would give his daughter in marriage. The following year, Huang indeed passed the examination and became Wang's son-in-law. He received a dowry worth five million coins. On the night of the wedding, everything was just as he had seen in his dream, including the hair ornament. Huang was initially appointed as a magistrate of Nan Cheng, and he often told people about this story.

A Child Delivers Pens During the imperial examinations of the Chunxi period in Shangrao, Yu Yichou, a teacher awaiting his appointment as a professor in Quanzhou, had a dream at the Jade Creek Gate. In the dream, a young boy in green clothes gave him five pens, saying, "Please deliver these to Professor Yu." When Yu Gao looked at the pens, he found that only three had ink, while the other two were empty. The next day, he informed Yu, who could not understand the meaning. After the examination results were announced, three of Yu's descendants were recommended, two were selected, and the following year, his nephew Yu Zhu passed the examination. Xu Dake Xu Dake, a low-ranking official from Hangzhou, was in charge of a customs office in the imperial capital and died in the fifth year of the Chunxi era. His brother Bai Lu, who was always kind and loving, deeply mourned him. He invited Taoist priest Tan Shi Yi from Mount Jiaosan in Linjiang to perform a Huanglu ritual at home. In the middle of the night, Xu Dake appeared through a small boy and asked for paper and ink, writing three sheets of paper. On the first sheet, he wrote, "Xu Dake has always loved wine and was deeply attached to his brother. During his period of abstinence, he received this merit and returned home, filled with deep gratitude and reverence for the divine favor, which is beyond comprehension." On the second sheet, he wrote, "I have received the divine favor." On the third sheet, he wrote, "Xu Dake's life's wishes have been fulfilled." The Taoists and Bai Lu and his family wept as they saw the handwriting, which was identical to Xu Dake's living handwriting. They engraved it on woodblocks for distribution to show the effectiveness of the ritual.

In the twenty-sixth year of the Shaoxing era, a scholar from Yichun County named Zhong Shierou visited Yingshan Temple to seek a dream to predict the outcome of the autumn imperial examination. That night, he dreamed of entering the temple from the outer gate and reaching the courtyard. He saw a person tied to a pillar in the corridor. The person looked back at Zhong Shierou with a joyful expression and spoke. Zhong Shierou woke up and told his friends, who did not understand the meaning of the dream. Upon entering the examination, he found the greatest joy in being sincere. When the topic was assigned, he realized that the dream was about sincerity and joy. The god had already indicated the topic, and Zhong Shierou used the fifth rhyme with the character "焉". He considered using a quote from Mencius, "The gentleman has three joys, but ruling the world is not among them." He worried about finding a suitable counterpoint and fell asleep. A yellow-clad official scolded him for sleeping during the examination. Zhong Shierou explained his situation, and the official suggested using a quote from Confucius, "Not blaming heaven or others, finding joy in simple food and drink." Inspired, Zhong Shierou wrote the couplet: "Confucius does not blame heaven or others; he finds joy in simple food and drink. Mencius is not ashamed to the sky or the earth; ruling the world is not among his joys." He was pleased with his performance and placed second in the examination.

In the first year of the Danyuan era, during the year of the Wood Rat (乙酉), Huang Kui Zhen passed through Jianchang. Many scholars and officials went to pay respects and would definitely give him poems and writings. Xiu Zhi Lang Deng Gui composed a poem that read: "Willow and peach blossoms in late spring, the servant woke him up while he was still half-asleep. By chance, a letter came from the capital, and looking back at the city by the river, the sun was setting." Deng Gui received the poem and then asked about Huang Kui Zhen's lifespan. Huang Kui Zhen stared for a long time and said, "Fifty-seven." Deng Gui was forty-four at the time, and he thought that his days were numbered, and he disliked the phrase "sunset." However, he feared that the letter from the capital might mean that he would be recommended for a post in the capital. Four years later, in the year of the Earth Tiger (乙丑) in the fourth month of the summer, on the seventeenth day of the fifth month, Deng Gui rose early and washed as usual. Near noon, he felt slightly unwell and lay down to rest, calling the small servant to massage him. Soon, his whole body became cold and his limbs stiff. The servant tried to wake him but could not, and when the family came out to see, he was already dead. He lived to be forty-eight, nine years more than what Huang Kui Zhen had predicted. The fifty-seven referred to the month and day of his death. Deng Gui's residence was near the city wall, and at the time of the funeral, the sun was setting, which was the sunset of the city by the river.

### Translation Lu Weifan, courtesy name Jiaqing, was from Jishui. In the fifth year of the Qian Dao era, he took the provincial examination. After the exam, he dreamed that his father told him, "You should not compete with Du Shen from Fu Tang in the examination hall. Your family has owed Du Shen thirty-eight years of merit." Both of them were famous for studying the Book of Poetry. When the results were announced, Du was the top scorer in the Confucian classics, while Lu was listed twenty-eighth below Du. In the imperial court, Du was ranked second in the second class, while Lu was ranked fourth in the fourth class, a significant difference. --- The seventh volume of the Yi Jian Zhi, Class A Authorized by the imperial decree, the Four Treasuries The eighth volume of the Yi Jian Zhi, Class A Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Dream of Dai Zhi Shao Dai Zhi Shao, courtesy name Cai Mei, was from Jizhou. He had a brief acquaintance with books and records but achieved no fame. Unable to support himself, he worked as a hired scribe for a wealthy family in his village. One night, he dreamed of carrying a hoe into their garden and digging the ground with a single swing. He found a copper seal with a square handle and a diameter of two inches, inscribed with intricate ancient characters resembling those on bronze vessels. The inscription read, "Day Fangbo Lian Shuai," five characters in total. He hung it on his elbow. With another swing of the hoe, he found a board similar to those used today for writing and painting, with two lines of poetry inscribed: "Chou jue Jiang Mei Kai Ling An, Bu Zhi Shi Jiao Dao Nan Tang." On the third swing, he found nine copper statues of the Heavenly Lord, which he held in his arms. On the fourth swing, he found eight or nine small seals, all of which he collected and took home. He saw his family performing a ritual sacrifice, and after the ceremony, they finished their meal. He woke up early the next morning, feeling quite happy, and carefully recorded the dream at the end of the master's book. From then on, he was moved to strive and expand his efforts, but he had no means to support himself. He wandered aimlessly, learning to write large characters to write shop signs for the common people. He traveled to Changsha, where he met Zhang Wei.

The official had no reason to see him, so he tidied up the official's door in hopes of a glance. On the official's birthday, he arrived early at the official's office and wrote the character for "longevity" on the ground with red powder, which was about two zhang wide and long. The official came out to see and asked who the person was. The person responded with the name "Dai Dao Ren." The official ordered him to be called forward and rewarded him with strings of coins and fine wine, but the person declined to accept, saying that "Zhi Shao" had wrong intentions, as they aspired to fame and success and certainly not here. He always showed off his talents to sell himself. The official admired his words and sent a letter to the military commander. The commander recruited him into the military, gave him much money, and assigned him to spy on the border and gather intelligence. After he left, he did not return on time, leading to suspicion that he had died or left. After several months, he returned, and the commander asked the reason for the delay. The person said that yesterday he took advantage of the opportunity to secretly enter the Central Plains, gradually reached Luoyang, and personally visited the Yong'an Mausoleum. The commander asked for proof, and the person said that there is a stele carved, took it out of his sleeve, and showed it to the commander. The commander reported to the court, did not conceal the truth, and still recommended him. Emperor Gaozu, who was thinking about the tombs, immediately ordered the person to be summoned. Dai Dao Ren presented in detail.

A Man with Unusual Dreams and Fates During the reign of Emperor Huizong in the year 1111, a man named Dai, who had been a scholar, was called to court. He spoke fluently, and the emperor was pleased, granting him the title of Baoyi Lang. However, Dai refused the position on the right side of the court, which was traditionally reserved for higher-ranking officials, and was instead given the lower-ranking position of You Chengwu Lang. He then realized that his dream of the first seal, which bore the character for "day," was a symbol of the emperor's face. He understood that the emperor was revered as an ancestor or a divine figure. Shortly after, he was promoted to govern Junzhou and was also appointed to oversee internal affairs, but he soon realized that this was a sign of his impending demotion. He returned to his hometown and visited his former employer, who gave him a generous gift of over a thousand strings of cash. He sought out books and documents to study, which led to his appointment as governor of Leizhou, a place in southern China. His career progression was verified by a smaller seal or sign. Later, someone criticized him for possessing texts from imperial tombs, which were said to have been brought by a righteous person from the north who intended to present them to the emperor. Dai was accused of killing this person and taking credit for his efforts, which led to his dismissal and eventual death. Dai was known for his bold and adventurous spirit, and some said that the person he killed was a monk who had died a year before, and whose spirit had been seen as a malevolent presence. County Magistrate of Qiantang During the reign of Emperor Huizong, Yang Tongguan, the Inspector of Educational Affairs for the Two Zhe Regions, traveled to Wuzhou to visit a strange man. While sitting, a clerk brought a letter from the County Magistrate of Qiantang. Yang did not open it immediately, and the strange man asked, "Is this not a request for nomination?" Yang replied, "Yes, I was about to recommend him." The strange man said, "It will be of no benefit." Yang was skeptical but sent a messenger with a recommendation the next day. By the time the messenger arrived, the County Magistrate had already died. Later, Yang arrived in Qiantang and called for the County Magistrate's clerk to ask about the circumstances. The clerk replied, "He died of illness." Yang asked, "Did he ever kill any geese?" The clerk said, "He never killed any living beings. However, several months ago, General Zhu of Pingjiang asked him to prepare goose paste, so he bought 500 geese to make it." Yang deeply mourned and was puzzled by the strange man's knowledge of the afterlife.

The Miscellany from Youyang, in the section called Zhi Nuogao, records that a man from Silla named Pang Fang sought silkworm eggs from his younger brother, who gave them to him by steaming them. Pang Fang did not know what he was receiving. When it came time to raise the silkworms, one hatched. It grew by more than an inch per day and, within ten days, became as large as a cow. Despite eating several leaves, it was still not satisfied. The younger brother found an opportunity and killed it. From a distance of one hundred li, silkworms flew and gathered at the younger brother's home, suggesting that the silkworm might have been a king. This story is highly unusual and possibly mythical. Near the northern border of Fuli in Suzhou, there lived a farmer named Wang Youwen in the village of Cai. He lived with his brother Wang Liang, and they both married local women. Wang Youwen's wife was the daughter of Qin Biao, a local resident. Wang Youwen was known for his fierce and cruel nature, constantly slandering his brother day and night. Eventually, he forced his brother out of the family, leading to a separation that lasted for years. Wang Liang once asked his brother for silkworm eggs. Qin Biao gave the silkworm eggs to Wang Liang by heating them, which was an unusual method of transferring silkworm eggs. Wang Liang's wife followed the usual method of warming the silkworm eggs to hatch them, but after the usual period, only one silkworm hatched. This silkworm grew to a weight of nearly a hundred pounds. Qin Biao began to suspect something unusual and waited for Wang Liang and his wife to leave the house to go to the east village, leaving a young girl to guard the house. She called her husband to join her, deceived the young girl to go to the kitchen, and entered the silkworm room. They saw the silkworm lying by the window, breathing heavily like a cow, and eating leaves with a sound like wind and rain. Qin Biao whipped the silkworm with a large stick, and with each hit, the silkworm spewed out several pounds of silk. Qin Biao was terrified and lost her spirit, rushing her husband back home. She fell ill and died after a few days. When Wang Liang's silkworm formed a cocoon, it was as large as a jar, and when unwound, it yielded exactly one hundred pounds of silk. In Poyang, there was a diviner named Wang Yi who rented a room in an inn. He kept two chickens, one male and one female. The female chicken hatched chicks and fed them in the nest. One day, Wang Yi had a guest who called a servant to take the male chicken to be killed and cooked. The male chicken called out, and the female chicken, hearing the sound, ran to the nest outside, looking intently, choking, and crying mournfully as if trying to save the male chicken. Wang Yi did not understand the situation and eventually killed the male chicken. The female chicken wandered around, crying sadly, and no longer cared for her chicks. She made small noises all night and did not eat in the morning. She stood still at the bottom of the wall, appearing as if drunk, and eventually died from gasping for air.

Xiao Zhang Er In Ezhou, a high-ranking official named Ding had a deceased wife who was thirty years old. She had a relationship with a butcher named Zhu Si, and their son, Er Lang, was too young to control the situation. As Er Lang grew up, Zhu Si became increasingly bold and openly engaged in sexual activities during the day. He was angry that Ding's son did not greet him, believing that Ding's son was being disrespectful, as he considered himself a stepfather. Ding was upset due to his mother's situation and feared that the scandal would become public, so he kept silent. There was another person named Xiao Zhang Er, from Zhucheng, Muzhou, who moved south due to turmoil and made a living as a butcher, known for his bravery and fierce demeanor. Ding thought he could persuade Xiao Zhang Er to help him deal with Zhu Si, and often invited him to drink in the market, where Xiao Zhang Er would buy meat and pay a generous price. One day, Ding invited Xiao Zhang Er to drink and asked why he didn't open a shop instead of carrying goods around. Xiao Zhang Er replied that he lacked capital. Ding then gave Xiao Zhang Er several hundred strings of cash, hoping he would help him by beating Zhu Si. Xiao Zhang Er was angry, saying that Ding lent him money to trap him in a fight, and left in a huff. They eventually stopped seeing each other, and others laughed at Ding for his poor judgment. Later, Xiao Zhang Er and Zhu Si went together to Hanyang to buy pigs and got into a fight on a boat. After returning, Xiao Zhang Er killed Zhu Si and three of his family members in the middle of the night and turned himself in to the authorities. Ding was not mentioned in the case. At that time, Yue Shao Bao, a high-ranking general, praised Xiao Zhang Er's loyalty and righteousness and took him into his army without questioning his past. Later, Xiao Zhang Er received an official position due to his military achievements.

### Final Translation **Wang Gong's Household Oddity** Wang Gong, a man from Poyang, lived near the Well of Wei. He enjoyed strange deities and sought their favor by worshipping five clay images of deities in his home with great care. One day, he suddenly heard unusual sounds coming from under the roof, which soon sounded like human voices. The morning meal was not yet cooked, and the food and utensils moved on their own, slowly entering the garden through a gap. When someone tried to retrieve them, they returned as before. The entire family felt uneasy and decided to move to a house in the Tea Market Alley. However, the oddities continued. Sometimes, a bandit-like figure with a hat, earrings, lost clothing, and rags would appear and carry away the bed and bedding, arranging them in the courtyard. Smoke and flames would burst out from the bedding, and by the time they rushed to extinguish the fire, a hole had already been made in the back of the house. They invited a Taoist priest to address the situation, performing rituals to ward off evil and pray. They moved the clay images to a city god temple, and the oddities finally ceased. **The Wandering Monk** In the Ningju Temple of Mingxi, a vegetarian meal was set up for a water and land offering ceremony. A wandering monk from Shan Tian Temple was invited from fifty miles away to write a protective talisman. He was housed in a small room in the temple, where the village shrine was desolate and there were no others to keep him company. It was the end of late spring, and as dusk approached, he felt a woman standing at the window. He thought it might be a neighbor who had been involved in illicit affairs with monks in the past. He went out to see her. It was a woman wearing a fish-scale crown, with a cunning and pleasant voice, and a demeanor unlike that of a commoner. They laughed together and said, "I live just a hundred steps below. I often come here, and I know the temple well." The monk, who lived in the village, had never dreamed of such a place and was eager to welcome her. He invited her into the room, closed the door, and lit the lamp. A young monk brought a jug of wine as a gift. The monk accepted it, and the woman hid under the bed. The monk told the young monk, "I have many documents to write, and it will take until after midnight to finish. I will only dare to drink then." He left the young monk and closed the door again. The woman came out and sat down to drink with him. She hung a small mirror on her chest, and the monk took it to look at. He asked what it was for, and she replied, "I have always loved this thing."

The monk often wore clothes that were clean and white, but the creases were not ironed, and the lines were clearly visible. He asked, "Why do my clothes have a musty smell?" The answer was, "Because they have been stored in boxes for a long time without being exposed to sunlight or air, they have developed a damp smell." Later, he went to bed, and the moonlight illuminated the room as brightly as day. The woman's appearance was even more beautiful, and they were deeply intimate and harmonious. However, he stayed awake all night, unable to sleep. The woman fell asleep and snored softly. As dawn approached, he left, and she showed him the way, saying, "This is my home. If you haven't left, come back." Soon after, the main monk repeatedly inquired and was surprised, saying, "Master, you have been writing by candlelight, which strains your eyes. Why do you appear so exhausted and listless?" The monk replied hesitantly without revealing the truth. The monk looked at the wall where a rose branch was inserted and was greatly surprised. He said, "There is an old tomb of Lady Zhao, a former magistrate's daughter, behind the temple. Roses were planted in front of the tomb, and anyone who picked a branch would encounter her ghost, sometimes leading to disaster. This has been going on for a long time. What you saw is her ghost. You should return home immediately and not stay here." The monk returned home in shame and fear, but he was ill for several days. Later, he returned to secular life and became a scholar, now living in the region of Huainan.

Land Spirit of Jianshi View Tán Yìzhōng, a man from Duchang, was upright in nature, fond of righteousness, generous with wealth, and free-spirited. Once, a gentleman named Dǎng, who came from Hebei, was living with him in Xichen Village. About to be summoned for service but lacking funds to travel, Dǎng was given a hundred thousand by Tán Yìzhōng and hosted his elderly and young ones in his home, treating him like family. Tán Yìzhōng was known for his charity and willingness to help others in urgent need. In the third month of the tenth year of the Qiandao era (1167), Tán Yìzhōng died of illness. The night before his death, he dreamed and told his father, "I cannot fulfill my filial duties, and now I have been appointed as the land spirit of Jianshi View." His father did not believe him. Later, his son also had the same dream. A year later, they saw him again in a dream, saying, "Since becoming the land spirit of Jianshi View, it has been a year, and the buildings have become dilapidated. When it rains, my face becomes soaked, and my body is almost entirely without intact skin. You should repair and maintain it for me." The next day, his father and son discussed it and went to see the Jianshi View. They believed him and renovated it completely. Wáng Ǎo of Èzhǔ

The Wang family of Ezhu had been in the business of selling food for three generations. After the old man died, the old woman continued to live alone without changing her occupation. She practiced Buddhism diligently and, when she had surplus wealth, would buy paper money and donate it to temples, following the Buddhist practice of depositing money for future use in the afterlife. She was illiterate and always had her cook servant Li Da write and sign the documents on her behalf. After the old woman's death, Li Da continued to work in the kitchen. One day, he suddenly fell ill and lost consciousness. After three days, he regained consciousness. Initially, he was taken to the underworld, where he saw a high-ranking official seated at a desk in a grand hall. The official asked about his hometown, name, and other details. A clerk then led him to the place where the documents were signed and asked him to identify the signature. Li Da replied, "I never signed the documents myself; they were signed by my mistress." The official then sent him back, and the high-ranking official also questioned him, receiving the same answer. The official said, "You are not guilty, but you must testify to this matter. You may return now." As Li Da was about to leave, he encountered the old woman and several others outside the gate. Upon seeing her, he bowed twice. The old woman was overjoyed and said, "Thank you for coming. My deposited money finally has a place to return. When you get home, please convey my message to my relatives and neighbors, asking them to take good care of themselves." Li Da then returned to life in the third month of the seventh year of the Qinding era. --- The Mother of Wei Liu Wei Liu was a poor commoner from Poyang who lived five miles north of the city. In the summer of the tenth year of the Chongxi era, he and a fellow villager named Shi Wu broke into the home of the Chi family to steal a cow. Wei Liu arrived first and stabbed the cow to death with a short spear. The Chi family discovered the theft and came out with a stick to chase them. Wei Liu fled immediately, while Shi Wu was caught and severely beaten. He died a few days later. Wei Liu's wife, fearing that her husband would be punished for the theft, did not report the incident to the authorities. She thought she had escaped punishment. A year later, Wei Liu's mother fell ill and died. After a night, she suddenly revived and spoke to her son, saying, "You once conspired with Shi Wu to steal a cow from the Chi family. Shi Wu died, but you escaped. Although you were not punished in the human world, the underworld requires you to testify to this matter. You cannot avoid it." After saying this, she died again. Three days later, Wei Liu also died.

A Crazy Monk of Shanyang During the Jianyan period, a monk named Xing Qin shaved his head and became a monk at the Longxing Temple in the state of Chu. During the military turmoil of the Shaoxing period, he left for another place and did not return for a long time. Eventually, he returned to his hometown. Among the people who had once known him, both monks and laypeople, none remained. He was in a state of confusion, as if drunk or in a trance, and even when speaking freely, his words were incomprehensible. He carried a scroll containing a Buddha image on his back, and he would go to the market to buy cooked meat, eat until he was full, and leave the leftovers. He would then take the Buddha image out, hang it near a household's toilet, and sprinkle the leftovers on it. This he did year after year, and the scroll's colors became more vivid, and it was well-preserved and clean. Many people suspected that he was strange, but no one knew where he stayed. When there were funerals in the county, he would always bring offerings for the deceased and participate in the rituals conducted by the monks. During the ceremonies, the crowd would blow conch shells and drums, and the chanting and singing would be loud and continuous. Xing Qin would lie with his head against the door, snoring loudly like thunder, and not utter a word. Then he would walk out directly. The host would invite and persuade him to eat, and he would eat reluctantly. As soon as he finished eating, he would carry rice, flour, salt, vinegar, chili peppers, and vegetables to his home and leave in a hurry. In the autumn of the Xinsi year, Jin Liang was about to invade the border. Before the troops withdrew, Xing Qin did not return. The following year, the people of Chu gradually returned, and Xing Qin had already arrived. When asked about his absence, his responses were strange, absurd, and without foundation. He would daily run around the streets, and when called, he would not respond, nor did he ever beg from others. The people of the county called him a crazy monk, and no one knows his final destination.

**Chao's Strange Toad** --- Chao, from the region of Jibei, was living temporarily in Wufu Temple in Fuzhou. One day, he was strolling leisurely by the pond behind the temple and saw a toad as large as a tray hiding in the grass. He found it strange and killed it. Just after returning to the room, he heard the noise of magpies, followed by a sound filling the sky like thunder that continued for some time without stopping. Upon going out to look, there was nothing to be seen, only the sound was as loud as before, and he also did not think it was strange. That night, his son was reading under the window. The lamp suddenly went out, and something stood beside him. The son hit it with a ruler in the dark, but was taken by it and ran out. Chao finally realized that the toad was causing mischief and sent his son to bed. While he was still half-asleep, he felt the bed move slightly. When he raised his hand to feel, it had already risen about a foot off the ground, almost touching the rafters of the roof. From then on, it played and danced on the beams and in the air, transforming in countless ways. They invoked priests and sorcerers to exorcise it, but none were effective, and they then moved to another place. The strange occurrences continued. They quickly painted an image of Xuanwu (the Black Warrior) and offered incense and sacrifices every day with great respect. After several months, the strange occurrences stopped.

**Zhu Feng Gets a Son** Zhu Feng from Xia Pi traveled to the capital to take the provincial examination. On the way, he reached Songcheng and encountered a fortune-teller who divined using the shadow of a carriage wheel. Zhu Feng asked for a reading, and the fortune-teller drew a hexagram of "Increase" and "Encounter." The image showed a monkey with the character "望" above it. A person dressed in purple, with a golden belt and holding a tablet, as if bowing respectfully, was depicted. A woman carried a baby in a basket in front of her. The fortune-teller said, "This time you will surely pass the examination, and in the future, your official career will also be prominent. However, the act of carrying a basket and feeding a child is not understandable." Then, he parted and left. Zhu Feng arrived in the capital and took the examination. The next day, two servants carried a box to deliver it to the examination hall and returned. They wandered through narrow alleys and heard a child crying on the ground. They looked and saw an old cloth wrapped around a newborn baby. They then said to each other, "It must be a child born out of wedlock. Our master has no son; we can adopt it." So, they carried it back to the inn. Just then, the wife of the inn had a wet nurse to feed and raise the child. By evening, Zhu Feng came, and his servants greeted him and told him about the baby. Zhu Feng was very happy and looked at the wet nurse, taking the child home. He asked where it was abandoned and found out it was Jisi Lane. Zhu Feng indeed passed the examination and named this child Shenglang. He never had any other children, so Shenglang became his heir.

The Drum Heap Spring of Jiangzhou In Jiangzhou, there is a temple dedicated to the Dragon Maiden beneath the Drum Heap Spring. The spring is a large pool, several zhang in diameter, capable of irrigating ten thousand mu of farmland. The local farmers rely on it for their livelihood, and they offer seasonal sacrifices and offerings with great reverence, never daring to be even slightly disrespectful. As a result, even during severe droughts, they never suffer from hunger. A Manchu official named Fuca served as the prefect of Jiangzhou. Given the high terrain and difficulties in managing water resources, he sought to lead the spring water into his garden. Despite extensive discussions, the significant difference in elevation made it impossible to achieve. He then respectfully visited the temple and earnestly prayed. That night, he dreamed that the god told him to quickly dredge the channel. Upon waking, Fuca mobilized the labor force to dig the channel. After the channel was completed, the water still did not flow into the garden. He dreamed again, and the god said, "I have three sons, all now grown but without any offerings. I have ordered them to guard the channel and transport the water to fulfill your good intentions." Fuca agreed to build a temple for the three sons, and the god was pleased and thanked him before departing. The next morning, the garden official reported that water had flowed into the garden through the new channel after midnight. Fuca immediately led his officials to the temple to offer sacrifices in gratitude for the god's kindness. He also requested that the god be ennobled as a marquis in the imperial court. The entire prefecture thus benefited from the god's favor.

### Translation The *Yijian Zhi* Collection, Volume Eight Imperially Approved Complete Library The *Yijian Zhi* Collection, Volume Nine By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Song Daoist Yang, a scholar from Yuzhang, had a slightly wealthy family and a fondness for alchemy, particularly the pursuit of turning base metals into gold and silver. Anyone who came with such skills was always welcomed. If they failed to achieve results over time, they were allowed to leave. As a result, alchemists and Daoist practitioners flocked to him. One day, a young servant reported that a guest identified himself as "Sangjin Song Daoist" and wished to pay a visit. Yang was delighted and put on formal attire to greet him. The man was thin, tall, dark-skinned, with a slight mustache, and his ears drooped forward like a hat. He wore a yellow linen robe, and his appearance was dignified and elegant. Yang immediately invited him to stay in the study, providing him with food and accommodation day and night. He began to treat minor ailments, and his treatments were effective. However, he never left to play or enjoy himself. Yang took the opportunity to ask about the essential techniques. After a month, he finally agreed to teach, using more than thirty ingredients. He listed all the missing ingredients, which Yang bought from the market. Yang asked to accompany him, but he refused, saying, "I am accustomed to quiet and dislike noise; how could I go to a noisy place?" As Yang was about to leave, he said again, "After you leave, the children will surely bother me; please lock the door so I can rest." Yang followed his advice and visited several pharmacies to buy the ingredients. Finally, he arrived at one pharmacy and saw someone sitting quietly inside whose clothing and appearance were exactly like those of Song Daoist; he was very surprised. Just as he was about to greet the person, the sitter waved his hand to stop him. Yang hurried back to the room, but the door was locked and did not move when he tried to open it.

Song closed his eyes and sat in a relaxed, meditative posture, just as he was at the beginning. Yang almost wanted to bow, believing that even if it were the legendary figures like Jizixun and Zuoyuanfang with their abilities to divide themselves, appear and disappear, and travel through the spirit world, they could not surpass this. From then on, he was treated with even greater respect, and whatever he needed was immediately provided. After a short while, he left without saying goodbye, taking the medicine he had traded to treat lead and mercury. He could not even calculate the exact amount, but the total cost of supplies, gifts, and the value of the medicine amounted to no less than a thousand strings of cash. He believed he had personally met a divine being and did not regret it at all. In Huizhou, Wuyuan, a native of Wuyuan named Wang was the wealthiest in his village. He was stubborn, rude, and detestable, and people called him "Wang Stinger" or "Wang Biter," a common term used to describe someone who causes trouble in the community. He was extremely stingy and especially hated monks who came to beg. He would always scold them and refuse to give them even a single coin. One day, a monk with shaggy hair and a fearsome appearance waited inside his house and suddenly appeared at the door, ringing a bell and chanting Buddha's name, the sound echoing loudly. Wang heard the noise, became angry, and beat him away with a stick. Just as he returned from outside, the monk was still in the corridor, ringing the bell and chanting as before. Wang felt ashamed and terrified, and respectfully treated the monk as a holy arhat. He slapped his own cheeks, repented, and immediately gave him twenty taels of silver. He remained in a state of fear for the rest of the day. People in the two states said that Song and the monk were twin brothers, very similar, and each was deceiving Yang and Wang with their tricks.

--- **Yidu Butcher Man Yi** Man Yi, the butcher of Yidu, was fierce and strong, capable of lifting a tripod, and absolutely fearless of ghosts and spirits. When drunk, he would visit clusters of shrines, cursing and insulting them, which had become a habit. The shaman Yanlong, who was deceitful, secretly conspired with his closest associates, saying, "The temple of Qingyuan Zhenjun has been dilapidated for a long time, and I am responsible for maintaining the incense and fire. I will renovate it, but the people of the county are unwilling to support me. Man Yi, the butcher, is known for his ferocity and is not afraid of the divine. If we use him to pretend to be a god, it will surely succeed." Thus, he invited Man Yi to a drinking session at his home. When they were drunk, he said to him, "I want to choose a certain day to pay homage at the temple of Qingyuan Zhenjun. On that day, there will be many spectators. You should take advantage of your drunkenness to shout and come, driving away the crowd and sitting in the main seat, acting as if you were a god." Man Yi ate the food and drank the wine, and then cursed the deity to the fullest, causing the crowd to be terrified. After a while, Man Yi suddenly went mad, with blood flowing from his mouth, nose, ears, and eyes, and he fell to the ground, dying. Everyone believed that Man Yi had provoked the anger of the gods and brought disaster upon himself. They were terrified by the divine power and competed in donating money to the temple, which was greatly expanded over several years. Later, Yanlong's followers distributed the bribes unfairly and reported the matter to the local government offices. They were all arrested, interrogated, and punished. --- ### Summary of Translation Choices and Challenges **Translation Choices:** 1. **Idiomatic Expressions**: Phrases like "絶不畏鬼神" and "以神自居" were translated to convey the intended meaning while maintaining the idiomatic nature of the original text. 2. **Cultural and Historical Allusions**: Terms such as "清元真君" and "叢祠" were translated to reflect their cultural significance, ensuring readers understand the context. 3. **Syntactic Ambiguities**: Long, complex sentences were broken down into shorter, more readable English sentences to maintain clarity and logical flow. 4. **Proper Nouns**: Place names and personal names were translated consistently to avoid confusion. **Challenges Encountered:** 1. **Cultural Nuances**: Some terms and phrases have deep cultural roots that are difficult to translate directly. Efforts were made to convey the cultural significance without losing the essence of the original text. 2. **Syntactic Complexity**: The original text contains long, complex sentences that required careful parsing and restructuring to ensure readability in English. 3. **Idiomatic Expressions**: Certain idiomatic expressions required creative translation to capture the intended meaning and tone of the original text. --- This final translation aims to be both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and natural in English, ensuring that the cultural and historical context is preserved while making the text accessible to modern readers.

From Si's Wife Yuan A person from Fulí named From Si lived near the Suī River. His family was always wealthy and prosperous, and he enjoyed food and drink, often brewing and selling alcohol. He cooked turtles and carp, eating to his heart's content in the morning and evening. His wife, Yuan, pleased his wishes and often personally wielded the knife, harming too many lives to count. Yuan died due to childbirth, and From Si never forgot her. In spring, the villagers went to Mount Tai, and From Si wanted to offer sacrifices to his wife, carrying offerings to present. After arriving in Taian for three days, before entering the temple, he wandered around the market. During the day, he met Yuan, and several people followed him. He asked in a dazed state, both sad and happy, exchanging greetings after a long time apart. Yuan spoke with tears in her eyes, saying, "Because of my cutting up fish and turtles, my accumulated karma is very deep, and I suffer daily torment. If you pity me, you should think about how to save me." From Si wept and said, "What I have done is not good, which led to your suffering. My life is no better than death." As for practicing Daoism and Buddhism, even if he exhausted his family's wealth, he would not hesitate. Yuan said, "There's no need to say more. At the time of the Shen and You hours the next day, you can go alone to the temple and find a place in the west corridor. I will be there, you can trust me." From Si then returned to his lodging, fasted, and went as scheduled. He saw Yuan with shackles and manacles, with straw and ropes, a group of spirits pushed her forward, removing the shackles and clothes, and tying her with straw. Using sickles to cut like threads, blood flowed all over the ground. Soon, a spirit held a cup of water, called her name, and sprayed it. She immediately returned to her original form, and soon it happened again, six or seven times. Then, the shackles were applied, and she was taken away. From Si was filled with fear and left the temple, not sleeping all night. The next day, he met Yuan again, and she asked, "Did you believe me?" He replied, "Yes, I believed you." She said, "Do not forget our promise," and they both wept bitterly before parting. From Si returned home and devoted himself to good deeds, and he never killed again throughout his life.

### Translation: **Shi Shenggan** Shi Shenggan, originally from Shandong, later settled in Xingjiao Temple in Guangde Jun. There was an empty house beside the temple, spacious but troubled by ghosts, making it impossible for previous occupants to live peacefully. The owner wanted to sell it but no one dared to buy it. Shi Shenggan, greedy for its low price, bought it alone. His relatives and friends repeatedly advised him not to, but he did not listen. He chose a date and ordered craftsmen to renovate and decorate the house, personally supervising the work. Sitting in the main hall, he saw an old man in black headgear and white robe bowing in the courtyard. Shi Shenggan had never met him before and hurriedly bowed and thanked him, asking, "Who are you, and how did you come here?" The old man replied, "I am the land spirit of this house. I hear that you are renovating the house and wish to offer sincere advice." Shi Shenggan asked, "What do you mean?" The old man said, "This place is actually inhabited by strange spirits, and there are many kinds of them. But they cannot compete with humans. Those who have lived here before were not upright and virtuous people, so they could not overcome the evil. Since you are an upright person living here, what harm can it do? You should move the rooms to the southeast corner and use the old rooms as a kitchen and storage area. You will surely sleep soundly." After he finished speaking, he disappeared. Shi Shenggan followed his advice and renovated the land temple. Thereafter, everything was peaceful. **Guan Yu's Headgear**

The Temple of Guan Yu in Tongzhou is located in the northwest corner of the state's administrative center. The local people treat it with great respect, and there are numerous statues in the temple. One of these statues, dressed in a yellow robe, has a fierce expression, a beard, and holds a banner, appearing awe-inspiring. Wang Yun, a fast runner from Chengdu, arrived at the temple, and the shaman Yu Tianyou saw him and thought he looked extremely similar to the statue in the temple. He invited Wang Yun to his home, offered him wine, gave him a silver cup as a gift, and also provided a model for a large headscarf. He told Wang Yun, "Geng Qian runs a shop in the market. Ask him to make a hat for me. It should be wide and ready within a few days. I hope for something exquisite." Wang Yun did not understand the meaning but followed the instructions and went to Geng Qian's shop. Geng Qian wondered how someone could have a head so large and also saw the profit in the five thousand coins. He agreed to the commission but noted that Wang Yun held a public official's document and could not stay long. Geng Qian did not inform Yu Tianyou of the commission and waited for Wang Yun to come back to collect the hat, but he never returned. Several months later, Wang Yun went out to the outskirts and entered the Temple of Guan Yu. Seeing the yellow-robed statue, he was greatly startled and exclaimed, "This must be the person I commissioned last year to make the large headscarf for five thousand coins!" He then measured the width and length of the statue's head with a small rope, went home to check the measurements, and found them to be exactly the same. Startled, he believed it to be a divine sign and immediately offered the statue as a gift. The matter gradually spread throughout the entire prefecture, and people rushed to show respect. Yu Tianyou, who was the temple official, took advantage of this to encourage and promote local customs, using money and silk to renovate the temple. Wealthy people were happy to contribute, and he collected a total of ten thousand strings of coins. However, Yu Tianyou embezzled nearly half of it. After ten years, Wang Yun returned to Tongzhou, and the people who saw him often pointed and laughed, asking him why he had come back. Some told him about Yu Tianyou's fraud. Wang Yun was furious and reported Yu Tianyou to the authorities. Yu Tianyou was punished with tattooing and exile, and all his assets were seized.

Yin Er's House Fire In the eastern part of the new river north of Shanyan, in Shanyang County, Chuzhou, there lived a fisherman named Yin Er whose family had been fishing for generations. They could barely make ends meet, having a modest home. One day, a fire burned everything to the ground. They rebuilt it, only to suffer again from the ashes. Year after year, up to three times, they were unable to do anything. They could only bind reeds together and use mud to patch up the house, only to shelter from wind and rain. Every night, they heard the sound of people moving outside, worried it might be bandits, they went out to look and saw more than ten people dressed in white holding torches. Yin Er shouted, and they suddenly dispersed, with no trace of them for a long time, but then it happened again. Fearing for his safety, Yin Er temporarily stayed with relatives and friends, but the disaster happened again as before. Once, while sleeping in the main room, they saw six or seven people moving in the air. They tied red charcoal with a string and placed it on the roof, quickly tried to extinguish it, but soon flames started on the side. Like this, for half a year, Yin Er's livelihood was completely ruined. His wife and children became sick and sad, and the strange occurrences continued. The villagers, fearing the fire god, moved the disaster to Yin Er's place and performed rituals to appease him. Later, although it calmed down, eight out of ten of his family members died. Cai Yi's Ominous Report

A Man from Chen Prefecture Pian Yi, a man from Chen Prefecture, was originally poor, and both his parents had died. He worked for Tuan San, a jailer, and thus learned his trade. By nature, he was cruel and ruthless, and what he saw with his eyes were all bad deeds. After a while, he was pushed by the wicked, and all prisoners who fell into his hands, even if their crimes were minor, were treated cruelly. His appearance was extremely ugly, and the people of the county even called him a "life-taking ghost." By the time he was over forty, one night while he was guarding prisoners in the jail, after midnight, the others heard a loud shout as if calling Pian Yi, very angry. They got up to see that he was hanging upside down on the wall. His companions, many of whom despised him for his character, pretended to sleep without asking. The next morning, they found that he was bleeding from all his limbs and orifices, and they helped him back to his cell. That night, the same thing happened again. He moaned and cried out in pain, as if he were being whipped, or he would stand up, supporting himself, as if he were tied up, begging for death but unable to feel pain. For a year, as he was dying, he made a sound like a cow, vomited blood in buckets, and then cried out loudly before dying. Zhang Xie's Loyal Servant Zhang Xie, a man from the eastern part of Chu Prefecture, was a wealthy person who was generous and helped people without expecting anything in return. When he was young and strong, he suffered during times of chaos, leading to the scattering of his family. He was left alone with a servant, living in the Sheyang Lake area, begging for food to survive. He was captured by bandits who intended to eat him alive. They cut off several pieces of flesh, but the servant escaped and saw him, crying bitterly and leaving. The servant stood up to shield and protect him, and bowed, saying, "He is my master." Though originally wealthy, now he is fleeing in poverty and has no food to eat, how could he possibly have any more wealth? If you want to satisfy your appetite, his flesh is already thin. The bandits, though they love killing, were moved by the servant's righteousness. Hearing this, they exclaimed in surprise and quickly untied Zhang Xie and the servant, letting them go, and gave them some money and silk. By the time of the Song Dynasty's reign, Zhang Xie returned to his hometown and restored his business, and his wealth was still worth millions. He treated his servant as a brother, and all his nephews and cousins treated him like their father.

梁小二 解州安邑池西乡的梁小二家世代贫寒，但家人都以耕种为生，性格朴实。唯有梁小二凶狠残暴。他的母亲是寡居，对他特别不服从。他的妻子王氏性格温顺安静，因此对婆婆非常恭敬。在皇统年间，河东地区遭遇了严重的饥荒、瘟疫和各种灾难，人们纷纷逃离，道路挤满了逃难的人。梁小二带着母亲、妻子和四个年幼的孩子一起逃难，到达了孤山的东陵，向村民乞讨食物来喂养他的孩子。王氏想到婆婆已经很久没有吃东西了，便把食物分成两份，给婆婆一半。梁小二看到后非常生气，假装让妻子抱着孩子先走，自己则留在后面，与母亲相距大约一百步远，突然将母亲推倒在地，拖到路边，用泥沙塞住她的喉咙，然后离开。不久后，他遇到妻子，妻子问他婆婆在哪里，他说：“老人走路慢，你先去找有钱人家乞讨食物，等她到了再回来。”然而，过了很久，妻子发现婆婆已经不见踪影，怀疑是被丈夫害了，于是回去寻找，发现婆婆的尸体已经僵硬。她悲痛欲绝，急忙取水为婆婆灌水，但婆婆最终还是没有苏醒。她于是跑去告诉村里的长老，长老们将梁小二逮捕并送到了县衙。当他们刚走到半路时，突然下起了暴雨，风雨交加，天色昏暗，几乎看不清人影。雷电交加，仿佛鬼神在空中飞舞，火焰四起，人们都吓得四散奔逃，没有人顾及梁小二。雨停后，梁小二回到土洞中，他的脸上被闪电烧得面目全非，只有头骨完好无损，看起来像骷髅一样。他的四肢虽然完好，但眼睛暗淡无光，却依然活着。他能够认出人，吃东西，说话，但行为混乱无章。他常常对人说有三个鬼守护着他，每次得到食物，他都会先祭祀这些鬼，然后才敢吃。官府怜悯他的妻子和孩子，给他们提供了粮食。梁小二这样活了几年，依然存在。

Zhu Biao, a man from Xu, took office in Cui Town, Suqian, and had been in his post for several months. A guest named Lu Jinqing came to visit him. Lu Jinqing had a graceful and elegant demeanor, which was very appealing. Zhu Biao treated him with special hospitality and kept him in an outer guesthouse, giving him preferential treatment. Whenever people visited, Lu Jinqing would put on small plays and skits to provide amusement, and he sought nothing in return. Those who saw him were all pleased and happy. One day, while Zhu Biao's relatives and friends were drinking in the garden, Lu Jinqing suggested transforming fresh carp into sashimi to share with everyone. Zhu Biao was surprised and asked, "How can this be done?" Lu Jinqing smiled and said, "Why didn't you tell me earlier? This is no big deal. I just need a piece of fish scale to serve as a catalyst." Zhu Biao had his servant fetch some fish scales, which Lu Jinqing placed in a large jar filled with water, covering it with a green cloth. He occasionally lifted the cloth to check, and after a while, several fish scales leaped out of the jar. Everyone present was amazed. The cook prepared the carp, and the sashimi was incredibly fresh and delicious, far superior to what could be bought in the market. However, everyone still thought it was a magic trick and did not fully believe it. Later, the governor of the region issued a new order, instructing the town officials to make hundreds of pounds of iron hooks, hinges, and nails. The deadline was very tight, and Zhu Biao could not manage to prepare everything in a hurry. He felt distressed and troubled. Lu Jinqing laughed and said, "Why didn't you tell me earlier? What's there to worry about? Let's drink some wine." When the wine arrived, Lu Jinqing drank several cups in a row. He then sent someone to cart yellow soil, fetch water, and mix it into clay. He molded various objects into shapes and left them to dry in the sun. He preheated the charcoal and placed the clay objects in the kiln when they were slightly dry. He called the blacksmiths to fan the fire, and after a while, he clamped the objects out of the kiln. They were all as strong and good as if they were made of high-quality iron, requiring no further polishing. The workers looked at each other in astonishment. Zhu Biao was finally convinced and, in his drunken state, begged for the method. Lu Jinqing remained silent. The next day, he was nowhere to be found.

Dong Xiaoseven, a native of Linchuan, wandered west of the Huai River to avoid famine and managed a boat for the Chen family of Hailing. He often slept alone in it. The weather was extremely cold, so Dong sealed the cracks, set a dim fire, and drank a cup of village wine before going to bed. His pent-up energy not released, he suffocated. No one knew about it, and he then appeared in a dream to Chen, saying, "I am about to suffocate to death in the ship's hold. Rescue me urgently, and I can still be saved." Chen woke up and told his wife. His wife said, "He already said he wasn't dead. How can we understand appearing in a dream? It's not worth believing." Therefore, she slept again. The dream was as before but even more urgent or severe. He said, "If the master arrives late, definitely will not be saved. If you come, please do not panic. Still do not use a lantern or candle to see the soul. Encounter it, definitely go away and not return. Must first remove the light. Wait for me to awake a little, then gradually help me up, and it will be okay." Chen woke up in shock and quickly called for a servant to join him in boarding the boat. Dong was in a state of apparent death, with only his mouth and nose breathing faintly. They followed his instructions and he indeed revived. Dong, a living person who could enter a dream to escape life, found this strange. It is said that those who are魇ed at night cannot hold a lamp; if misused, they will die. The saying is truly so.

Xia Yicheng Xia Yicheng, a resident of North Village in Leiping, had a sufficient livelihood and was single-mindedly inclined towards goodness. He did not engage in petty disputes with his neighbors and never involved himself in litigation that reached the court. He had four sons and built two houses, living separately from each other. He lived a leisurely life, enjoying the care of his family, until he reached eighty-eight years old, healthy and without illness. His eating and drinking were just enough to satisfy his palate, and he only drank three or four cups of wine a day. He enjoyed drinking alone with a sense of contentment. In the summer of the fourth year of Shaoxi, he felt slightly unwell and told his family to prepare a feast. All his descendants, nephews, and nieces gathered for a meal, and they drank three rounds of wine. Afterward, he went for a bath and had one son massage his back, finding it very pleasurable to be relaxed. After bathing, he changed clothes and sat on a trundle bed, sitting upright with his hands clasped, and then passed away. Two days before the funeral, his hands and feet were soft and pliable, and they could be bent and stretched by others. His breathing had stopped, but there was no sign of decay. Buddhists and laypeople came to observe, considering this as something beyond the capabilities of great virtuous teachers. It was a reward for his good deeds. Record of Strange Things, Volume Nine of the First Compilation Imperially Approved Four Treasuries Record of Strange Things, Volume Ten of the First Compilation Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty

Hai Wang San The *Jia Zhi* records the story of a merchant from Quanzhou who encountered a woman on an island. Today, the story of Hai Wang San from Shanyang is similar. Hai Wang San's father traveled as a merchant to the southern part of Quanzhou and sailed across the vast sea. He was caught in a storm that broke his ship, and he and several dozen others were drowned. Hai Wang San managed to grab a piece of wood and let himself drift. He eventually reached an island and climbed ashore. The mountain streams were filled with exotic flowers and trees, rare and strange birds and beasts, many of which were unknown in the central lands. The climate was mild and gentle, unlike the remote and uncivilized regions. The island was sparsely populated with no inhabitants. Hai Wang San rested under a large tree, not knowing why he had been left there. Suddenly, he saw a woman approach and ask, "Where are you from? How did you get here?" Hai Wang San told her about the shipwreck. The woman said, "Then follow me." She was quite beautiful, with long hair that hung down without being combed. Her language was understandable, and she had no clothing to cover her body. Hai Wang San could not determine whether she was a human or an otherworldly creature. He silently thought that he had already fallen into this foreign land, with nowhere to return and facing the imminent threat of being killed by wild animals. It would be better to follow her for now. So he followed her down the mountain to a deep, clean, and bright cave that was always illuminated as if it were daytime. The place was clean and well-lit, but there was no cooking stove. The woman stayed with him and shared her home. She fed him fruits in the morning and evening and warned him not to wander out unnecessarily. Although Hai Wang San had no clothes to change into, he was fortunate that the climate was not too cold or hot, so he could survive. After a year, they had a son. When the child was one year old, the woman went to gather fruits and did not return. Hai Wang San walked casually to the water's edge and saw a guest ship anchored to avoid the wind in a sheltered bay. He recognized the people on board as old acquaintances. He hurried into the cave, took the child, and boarded the ship. The woman came after him but could not catch up. She called out Hai Wang San's name and scolded him, crying bitterly and collapsing to the ground, nearly fainting. Hai Wang San raised his hand from under the sail to apologize, and he too shed tears. The ship had already set sail and returned to Chu. As the child grew up, the people of Chu called him Hai Wang San. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, he was still alive.

Shan Mingyuan Shan Mingyuan was a person from Cangzhou with the courtesy name Yan De. His ancestors were also of the scholarly or official class. By the time of Mingyuan, he had become increasingly poor and had no family to rely on. He then traveled around Bin and Di, making it a habit to seek audiences with influential figures. Sometimes he would go an entire day without food. His feet were so exhausted that he had to bend over by the side of the road, hoping to find something to eat, but could not. And again, driven by thirst, he went to drink water by the riverbank, then sat under a large tree for a long time. A rabbit passed by, and he quickly caught it. He stripped and ate it, not leaving a single piece of meat, and found it exceptionally delicious. He then thought to himself that in the forests and hills, bears, tigers, wolves, and leopards specialize in attacking and devouring foxes, deer, pigs, and rabbits, raising their bodies. At the top of the mountain and the entrance to the valley, the joy is boundless. As a human, he is yet less fortunate than them, pitiable indeed. After he had eaten, he went out and it was already evening. He visited the house of the old man Huang Ruoxu to ask for lodging. Huang had once entered the Dao and was always hospitable. He was happy to see him, set out wine, and kept him around for a long, unending conversation. After drinking to the point of intoxication, he said that eating fresh raw meat is pleasant. Huang heard this and was suspicious and afraid. He carefully examined his face and found it quite different from that of a human, thinking that an animal had transformed into a monster and quietly rose, calling the dogs to bark and chase him. Mingyuan hurried down the steps, called out a few times, and transformed into a black wolf, seizing something large and leaving, leaving no trace that could be followed. Huang later went to Cangzhou to inquire about his family background and found that he was completely unaware of where he was.

Jiang Jian, the Fortune-Teller and the Cattle In the first year of the Gande era, Jiang Jian, a diviner from Jinling, traveled to the western bank of the Yangtze River and arrived in Poyang, where he set up a divination shop. His expertise in the Liu Ren method of divination earned him praise from scholars and officials, and he stayed there. He had a mother whom he cared for diligently. In the fourth month of the Chunxi era, Jiang Jian fell ill and was in a drowsy state when he saw several people holding torches enter his room, making loud and chaotic noises. They shouted his name and presented a document, saying they had been sent to summon him. Jiang Jian tried to resist but could not, so he followed them. They walked together for about two hours until they reached the residence of a ruler. A messenger led him through the western wing, which was dark and gloomy. They entered and stood in the courtyard below the throne. The ruler sat upright on the throne, and an official loudly announced, "Summon Jiang Jian." Jiang Jian was led forward, and a red-robed official presented and reviewed the documents. Everyone was directed to the west, while the eastern wing was bright and empty. The ruler alone remained and asked Jiang Jian, "You have a deep-seated karmic debt for enjoying beef and veal. You must now suffer for it." Jiang Jian was terrified and replied, "Although I enjoy this taste, I never personally killed any cattle. I only bought meat from butchers." The ruler said, "Because..."

You love this, so people slaughter animals to serve you. How can you be without sin, and yet dare to fabricate excuses and deny the truth? Why is that? Jian said, "Though I am guilty, death is not to be regretted. But my old mother is seventy-six years old, and from now on, there will be no one to feed her. What will become of her?" The king laughed and said, "I also know that you are filial to your mother, so I am letting you go. From now on, you shall not eat beef again." Jian bowed twice to thank the king, who ordered a soldier to escort him back. Jian suddenly woke up, and his mother and wife were weeping together. Four years later, he died. In the year he first came to Poyang, he used three pieces of coarse cloth to write "Jiang Jian of Jinling" on a street. On December 4th, I went to the east garden and called him "Wen Hui Gong" to discuss his destiny when he was in charge of government affairs. Jian said, "This destiny will rise greatly; if you are a scholar, you will pass the exams; if you are selected, you will change your position; if you are a common official, you will become a servant; if you are a high-ranking official, you will enter the two courts; if you are a political leader, you will become a chancellor; still, you will hear good news today." I told him the truth, and he replied, "If so, then you will do great things without doubt, but I regret that my fate cannot last long; in March next year, you should bravely retire." I said, "Since it is said that you will be officially appointed, you should not retire too quickly."

As a result of knowing the Military Council, Wang Mingyuan was appointed as a signatory, and Ye was also given a position. I consulted Jian and he said that all should be promoted, and it was also very urgent. However, Ye would leave in no more than four months, and Wang in no more than five months, and all would leave. I did not believe it. Later, it turned out that on that very day, Wen Hui was appointed as the Right Chancellor, and Wang was promoted to the Military Council, and Ye was involved in major policies. In the following February, Wen Hui resigned from his position. In March, Ye left; in April, Wang left. All left in the order predicted, each differing by one month, as it was said. In June of that year, I was appointed as the governor of Jizhou and presented a report. Jian and other guests saw me off to a small ferry. Everyone thought I would definitely stay in the capital. Jian said, "Not yet; it would be better at the end of autumn." Indeed, after the audience, I was entrusted with the affairs of the prefecture. Then, I instructed the official to send a message to the prefecture to dispatch officials to meet me. After returning home, I chose September 20th to travel west to take up my post. Ten days before, I left my residence and stayed in a garden. I called Jian to consult the divination. There was a favorable omen for meeting the emperor, and there was no need to go. Just a few days later, the imperial summons arrived, and I set out on the day I had chosen. The two predictions were verified. In the Wu Zi year, scholars flocked to his door for the imperial examinations, and he almost earned 500,000 coins. From then on, he lived a comfortable life. Later, his reputation declined significantly, leading to his downfall.

A Taoist Priest and the Money Vault In the area beneath Yuhuatai in Jinling, a resident named Zhen was herding cattle when she encountered two men dressed like Taoist priests. They greeted each other formally, raising their fists and bowing. One of them said, "The back door of the money vault has been damaged for a long time and needs to be fixed urgently." The other replied, "Yes," and they then parted ways. After a while, Zhen walked alone to a steep cliff and saw a large hole in the rock, about the size of a winnowing basket, from which money was spilling out. She immediately wrapped the money in her clothes and planned to return home to tell her father and brothers, intending to transport it together. However, she was concerned that others might find it, so she sealed the hole with mud. On her way back, she met the two men again, and one asked, "Has the back door been fixed?" The other replied, "We have hired a herder to fix it with 300 strings of cash." Zhen, who was around seventeen or eighteen years old, understood their words and returned home to tell her father. When they counted the money, they found exactly 231 coins. By the time the family gathered at the original spot, the hole was no longer visible. The Dragon and Phoenix Egg In the northern part of Ronghe County, within the Hezhong Prefecture, there is a solitary peak named Phoenix Mountain, known for its steep and dramatic cliffs. To the west of the mountain, there is a temple called Phoenix Temple and a pagoda called Phoenix Pagoda, named after the mythical phoenix that once perched there. During the time when the Qin region was conquered by the Jurchens, a monk named Shao Hong served as the abbot for many years. After his passing, the temple community decided to build a pagoda for his burial. When they dug into the ground, they found an object about six or seven feet long, neither stone nor wood, but shaped like an egg. The people who saw it were astonished and threw it into a vast river, but it did not sink and was carried away by the waves. They left quickly, and when they returned to the original spot, the object seemed to have been supported by someone and was still there. Unable to hide it, they reported it to the local authorities, who then sent it to Yanjing (modern Beijing), where it became known as the dragon and phoenix egg.

Jianfu Temple, located at the foot of the East Lake in Raozhou, was the largest among the six Chan monasteries in the area. The abbot, Ruben from Guixi, Xinzhou, was highly favored by the monastic community. In the eighth month of the Chunxi era, he fell ill and did not recover for several days. On the twenty-eighth day, he called his attendant and said, "The old monk will part ways tonight." The attendant wept and said, "Master, you are returning to the Western Paradise. With my shallow karmic connection, I do not know when I will meet you again." The abbot replied, "We can meet again tomorrow." The attendant asked, "Where will we meet?" The abbot said, "At the home of Doctor Wang outside the Gate of Chongfu Temple in the city." The attendant went out and beat the drum to call the assembly. Many monks crowded into the bedroom, asking questions, and his words were as before. None could understand his intention. Just as dusk fell, he passed away. The night before, Doctor Wang Dabian's wife, Jiang, had a dream where she was accompanied by the wife of Doctor Xiong Yancheng to the temple. They saw the old master lying in a niche under the eaves. She asked, "Where is the elder abbot not in the abbot's quarters?" The abbot smiled but did not answer. She only felt someone crying softly by her ear. Upon waking, her son Tan's wife, Wang, was crying out loudly because of the difficulties of childbirth. The next day, at the hour of You (evening), a baby was born. By dawn, several monks came to see Wang and reported the old master's last words. Doctor Wang also told them about his wife's dream. Finding it matched, they led the monks to see the baby, who immediately opened his mouth and laughed. From then on, he never ate meat, and his family called him the monk old man.

**Fuzhou Vegetable Garden** After the ravages of war, the major cities of Hubei were turned into overgrown grass, and Fuzhou was particularly affected. Inside the inner city, there was a piece of abandoned land that was cleared of debris and cultivated into a vegetable garden. The people tilled and planted vegetables to provide for their food needs. The official in charge of the garden was located to the east, and the registrar was located to the west. In the fourth year of the Shaoxing era (1134 AD), my nephew, Chen Tichan, was appointed as the deputy governor and attended a gathering hosted by the registrar. During the gathering, a young boy saw two women wearing crowns and red robes, holding hands and laughing as they entered the garden. He thought they were official entertainers. He was surprised that they did not change into their evening clothes at dusk and told the registrar's boy about it. It turned out that officials never used such entertainers to accompany them at formal gatherings.
