It was later discovered that this was the same location where another ghost story was recorded, where Fu Wang saw a female ghost at night. **Chen Tichan** Chen Tichan, from the Chen family of Nan Cheng, was originally a scholar but later became a monk, taking the name Tichan. He was unrestrained and fond of alcohol and women, and his old habits did not change even after becoming a monk. Although he lived in the Baoren Guangxiao Temple, he often stayed at home and secretly defiled neighboring women. Everyone outside knew about his behavior, but he treated it calmly. Getting drunk and eating meat were just minor issues for him. In the late Qian Dao era (1173-1174 AD), he was infected with an illness but not yet severely debilitated. He dreamed of being summoned by an official in formal attire who rebuked him, saying, "You recite the *Prajnaparamita* but violate the precepts, and there is still sin in death." The official ordered the guards to bind him. Chen Tichan prostrated himself in apology, but it was not accepted. He woke up in shock and trembling, and told others about his dream, saying, "It's too late to regret." He died after ten days.

Baozhong Temple In the first year of the Qian Dao era, in the sixth month, the bandit Li Jin Huanggu from Chenzhou attacked Daozhou, captured Ningyuan County, and burned down all government and civilian houses completely. The Governor of Hunan issued an edict to Wang Zheng, the circuit inspector of Heng, Dao, Chenzhou, and Guilin, to gather other troops to suppress the bandits. Upon arriving at the town, they had not yet established a camp. Wang Zheng acted boldly and underestimated the enemy from within the military. He rode alone into battle, challenged the enemy, and was captured. Initially, they wanted to spare him, but he cursed and refused to submit. They then beheaded him and left his body by the roadside. It was the height of summer, and the bodies of those who died together were in a state of disarray, stinking and decaying. Wang Zheng's body did not decay, and even insects did not gather on it. Despite the swarming insects, they seemed to want to approach but could not move forward. The place of his death was two hundred li away from his official residence. His horse ran back home, and his family suspected something had happened and went to check. They collected his remains, which were still recognizable. The commander reported his loyalty and righteousness to the court. The emperor decreed that he be posthumously awarded the title of Guangzhou Observer and granted official positions to five of his relatives. A temple was built at the site of the battle to honor him, and it was named "Baozhong Temple." Two Lives: Gan and Lin If people are born in the same year, month, day, and hour, their lifespans, fortunes, and misfortunes are generally similar. Gan Tongshu from Fengcheng and Lin Zhiqing from Putian were both born on the same day and time in the same year and month during the Shaoxing era. Both were scholars who passed the imperial examination in the same year. Lin did not take the imperial examination due to mourning for his mother, while Gan was promoted and appointed to the position of household magistrate in Jingjiang Prefecture, awaiting an opening. Lin returned to take the examination in the year Xinchou and obtained the position of tax supervisor. Gan died after serving for over a year, and Lin served for only one year and left due to other reasons, and soon after, he also died. Both enjoyed similar salaries, but Gan wore the official robe for three years longer than Lin.

Wang Zhonggong During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong in the Southern Song Dynasty, Wang Zhonggong, holding the rank of Chaofenglang, served as the military governor of Wugang Army in Hunan Province. The governor of Hunan, Wang Xuanzi, recommended him for his talents, and he was given a position as a prefect. After accepting the position, he took a detour to visit the governor's office in Hunan to pay respects. On his way to Hengyang, his son Wang Wanshi had a dream in which someone told him, "Your father has been demoted to serve in a temple." When Wang Wanshi woke up, he told his father, believing that any demotion mentioned in a dream was actually a promotion. Wang Zhonggong laughed and said, "I am about to be promoted to a lower-ranking official due to my years of service, and I have no fault, so why would I be demoted? Moreover, I have just received a promotion to a prefectural position. I am not seeking a quiet life, so why would I be serving in a temple? Your dream is not credible." Upon arriving at the capital, he presented his views on the affairs of the remote regions inhabited by non-Han ethnic groups, and his advice was well-received. He was appointed as the governor of Yongzhou. However, he soon fell into disfavor after recommending a local leader, Yang, who was capable of subduing the remote regions. The emperor mistakenly wrote Yang's name in the imperial edict, and the court criticized him for carelessness, resulting in a demotion. Wang Zhonggong was deeply unhappy and requested to serve in a temple, where he was assigned to the Wuyi Chongyou Temple, just as his son had dreamed. Wang Zhonggong had once dreamed of traveling on a long road with a signboard carried by a messenger that read "Yong." He told others that he would be appointed as the county magistrate of Yongzhou, a small but prestigious position. When he was indeed appointed to that position, he vaguely recalled his previous dream and began to doubt that his official career would end there. He intensely disliked the idea and returned to Nancheng. Soon after, he died from a boil on his back. Wang Wanshi, his son, never received any benefits from his father's legacy.

### Translation: The Case of Ai Dazhong In the thirty-first year of the Shaoxing era, Ye Boyi served as the governor of Linchuan. He was known for his strict and righteous governance, and he dealt harshly with the powerful clans and prominent families who committed crimes. Those who were found guilty had their family assets confiscated, and in severe cases, their homes were destroyed. In Chongren County, a wealthy man named Ai Dazhong provided financial support to bandits, thereby becoming wealthy. After he was brought to justice, the governor ordered the use of ox carts to drain his fish ponds, and they discovered hundreds of human bones and skulls. Ai Dazhong had also once called two craftsmen to assemble large pieces of wood into a massive bench, which he filled with thousands of taels of gold and silver. Upon completion of the work, he killed the craftsmen to cover up his actions. His deeds were so heinous that they could have warranted the extermination of his entire family. At the time, everyone was pleased with his punishment. Thirty years later, in the Shaoxing era of the Xin Hai year, a man from Wu named Yang Qianshen was on his way to take up his post as magistrate. During the day, he fell asleep and had a dream in which a clerk stood in the courtyard and said they were searching for the records of Ai Dazhong's confiscation from Chongren County, a certain village and lane. They needed the case files for evidence. Yang Qianshen did not have time to respond before he woke up and did not know what to make of the dream. He decided to check the recent cases he had handled, but none of them matched the details of the dream. Since the dream was so vivid and clear, he could not shake it off. He visited many experienced officials and eventually learned about the details of the case. He ordered the records to be checked, and after a day, they were found. He selected a clerk to copy the documents, which amounted to several hundred pages. He prepared incense and paper and burned them together. The "Yi Zhi" records the case of a village in Xuanchou, which was an unjust death. If Ai Dazhong's crime was commensurate with the punishment, why would there still be litigation in the afterlife? There must be some reason, but it is something the people of the world do not know.

A Monk at the Ice-Striking Hall Cheng Yuqing, a native of Jian'an, had served as a retainer in another prefecture. He was bold and strong-natured, forming many local alliances and leading a carefree life of feasting and hunting. In the second month of the third year of the Qian Dao era, he attended a banquet at the home of a noble family. After drinking to the point of intoxication, he entered the Great Middle Temple with his companions and reached the Ice-Striking Hall. There, a painter was just finishing a mural of a Buddha, with a servant inside the mural having a strange appearance. Cheng pointed and mocked the figure. That night, he returned home and went to sleep. In his dream, he walked with his friend Ding Zihé to a different guesthouse, where they encountered two monks brandishing staves who shouted and attacked them aggressively. Cheng rushed to avoid them but was forced against the walls, unable to move forward. Feeling trapped, he knelt and begged for forgiveness. The monks' anger slightly subsided, and one of them asked, "Have you eaten beef again?" Cheng replied, "I vow to never eat beef again." The monk said, "If you eat beef again in my temple, I will cut off your foot and let you go." Cheng kowtowed twice and left. Upon waking, he was drenched in sweat, his heart was restless, and he waited for dawn to visit Ding's door. Ding was startled and stopped him from talking, first explaining the cause without any words differing. They then went together to the Ice-Striking Hall, where they saw the two figures from the dream, causing even greater fear. Cheng then decided to abstain from beef and other indulgences, and he also restrained himself from other excessive behaviors.

*The Records of the Unusual, Volume Ten, Section A* *<Miscellaneous, Novelists, Strange Tales>* *Imperially Approved Complete Library* *The Records of the Unusual, Volume One, Section B* *By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty* **The Family of Wang Yantai** Wang Yantai, a man from Lin'an, was very wealthy, living in luxurious houses where his wishes were easily fulfilled. Suddenly, he decided to sail to the South China Sea to engage in maritime trade. The boats and ships were all prepared, but he was reluctant to leave behind his young and beautiful wife, Fang. After a long time, the decision to depart was finally made. For many years, he did not return, and communication ceased. During the spring, people from Hangzhou often visited the lakes and mountains. However, Fang, who was usually frugal and quiet, did not go out. Instead, she walked around the small garden behind the house to relieve her inner distress. In the shade of red flowers, she encountered a young man wearing a red silk robe and a golden brocade hat. His skin was as smooth as if powdered, and his demeanor was scholarly and relaxed. He secretly observed her from a hidden spot and drew his bow to shoot at her. Fang scolded him, saying, "I am a respectable woman, and my husband has been away for many years, staying indoors and avoiding social interactions. What kind of person are you to enter my garden without permission and shoot at me with your bow? How utterly disrespectful!" The young man felt ashamed and fearful, lowered his bow, bowed, and apologized. Fang scolded him sternly, and he suddenly disappeared. Fang ran back to her house and called out to her maids, feeling her mind and spirit confused and physically exhausted and unable to stand. At midnight, the young man entered the house. Fang tried to run away, but he grabbed her by the long arm, which was several meters long. The maids tried to pull her away but were unable to do so.

Then, he climbed up onto the couch and warmly entertained the guest. From morning till night, he was present, and whatever he desired, he never had to speak before it came. He had committed a crime as great as the sky, and he said to his relatives and friends, "I will perform a Taoist ritual to exorcise the evil spirit. I will also perform a religious ceremony and hold a Buddhist meditation session." He chose twenty monks to perform the meditation session and strike with long arms, but they could not exhaust their skills. After several months, the young man was in distress and said to Fang, "Your husband is returning from the sea. When you see him, do not reveal what I have told you. If you violate my commandments, I will harm you. Do you know my supernatural powers? Even natural and man-made disasters cannot harm me in the slightest." Shortly after, Wang returned. Fang wept and said, "I have committed a great crime. You must cut me into pieces to atone to your relatives." Wang was surprised and asked why. She told him everything. Wang said, "This is a mountain spirit and tree demon. I must kill it." He hid a sharp sword and waited for it to come. One night, it appeared solemnly. Wang drew his sword and attacked it, hitting its back. The sound was like metal and jade, and it shone for several zhang, vanishing into the void. After that, the sound disappeared, and Wang and his wife treated each other as before.

**The Wife of Zhang Si** In Huizhou, Duyuan, there was a man named Zhang Si who made a living by carrying goods. His wife was young and attractive. One day, a white-clad guest passed by their home. The guest spoke flirtatiously and quickly, seeing no one around, he attempted to seduce Zhang Si's wife and offered her several taels of silver as a bribe. She accepted and engaged in the affair. A long time passed before Zhang Si returned. He secretly learned of the affair and pretended to tell his wife, "I want to go to another state for ten days and then return." The wife was delighted and agreed. That night, just before the second watch, Zhang Si saw the white-clad guest jump over the window sill and enter the room. Zhang Si, holding a short spear, hid by the door. The guest made a sound like "yo-yo" and ran away. Zhang Si saw blood and some white and fine hairs on his spear. Zhang Si thought, "How can a person have hair? This must be a supernatural being!" He did not further investigate. The wife eventually admitted to what she had seen. Zhang Si then prepared a document detailing the entire incident in the form of a deposition and went to the Daoist Master Hunyuan, Master Jin Zhongfu. The master performed a ritual, standing respectfully, and waited. Soon, a large eagle circled the roof, about five or six feet high, and flew over the crowd. It then flew down into a stone ditch and caught a large white rat, which it threw in front of the master. The master boiled oil to cook the rat, and the supernatural being was destroyed.

**The Wife of Zhang Si** Zhang Si was a man from Wuyuan in Huizhou Prefecture who made a living carrying goods on a pole. His wife was young and somewhat attractive. While Zhang was away, having taken a commission to travel a thousand li away, a white-robed guest passed by his house. The guest spoke eloquently and, seeing that Zhang was not present, flirted with the wife and attempted to seduce her. He took out several taels of silver as a bribe, and the wife, pleased, accepted his advances. After a long while, Zhang returned home and secretly learned of what had happened. He then deceived his wife, saying, "I wish to go to another province and will return in ten days." The wife was even more pleased, thinking that this meant he would be away for a long time and that the opportunity was now hers. That evening, Zhang secretly returned to the room, took a short spear, and lay in wait by the door. Just before the second watch of the night, he saw the white-robed man climb through the window and jump into the room. Zhang rushed forward and struck him with the spear. The man let out a cry, ran away, and fled. Zhang looked at the spear's blade and saw blood, along with some white hair and dozens of fine hairs. Zhang thought, "How can a human have such hair? This must be a supernatural creature." He did not press further, and the wife finally confessed what she had seen. She then prepared a formal document, detailing the entire incident in the style of a legal statement, and went to the Daoist Master Jin Zhongfu of the Mixed Primordial Sect to report the matter. Master Jin conducted the ritual according to the proper procedures, performing a binding rite. He arrived at Zhang's house, drew a talisman, and stood in a respectful posture, waiting. Soon after, a great eagle circled overhead, about five to six feet in the air, circling the house. The onlookers filled the area. Suddenly, the eagle flew down into a stone ditch and attacked a huge white rat, seizing it and throwing it before Jin. Jin ordered the rat to be boiled in hot oil, and the supernatural entity was destroyed.

The Aquatic Creatures of Dingtao In the north of Dingtao County in Caozhou, there is a marsh where the residents living nearby often collect snails, clams, fish, and turtles to sell for a living. During the middle of spring in the second year of Zhenglong (1157 AD) of the Jin dynasty, a Jurchen man named Asar, who served as the magistrate of the county, had a dream in which a guest dressed in a green robe, black boots, and a leather belt entered to pay respects, holding a tablet. The guest said, "Our clan has lived under your rule for generations, and our descendants have thrived, all benefiting from your benevolence. Unfortunately, they are caught and killed by common people to be eaten, and even sold to others, which will lead to their extinction. We hope that the kind magistrate will show compassion and slightly restrict this practice, so that your benevolence will flow endlessly, and we will think of how to repay you." Asar agreed in his dream but did not have time to ask what the guest was.

From where did he arise, deeply pondering, unable to comprehend? The next night he dreamed again, thoroughly questioning officials and soldiers, and consulting those skilled in the Dao and the arts, yet none could understand the meaning. By the end of spring, the weather was clear and warm, and people along the water’s edge gathered in groups, stripping off their clothes to fish, using nets and baskets, catching several times the usual amount. Suddenly, a thick fog obscured the sky, waves surged like mountains, and thunder rumbled. A huge creature, six or seven zhang long, resembling a dragon or sea serpent, spewed clouds and smoke, destroying the banks and emitting a chilling cold. People abandoned their catch and rushed to the shore, with about twelve or thirteen being seized by the creature, and nearly half drowning. The crowd then realized the meaning of the magistrate’s dream, and no one dares to fish again. Zhu Qi’s Son Zhu Qi, a man from Xiapi, was skilled in martial arts and served in the army of General Han Qi, where he was a commander of the intelligence unit. He fought against enemy cavalry on the Shu Water and died in battle. The court honored his loyalty and granted his son, Zhu Qi, an official position. At that time, Xiapi was already under guard, and Zhu Qi was in Suyu, where he gathered his companions and returned to the Jianghuai Military Governorate, where he was appointed as a subordinate general of the loyal and righteous army. He once took the opportunity to enter Haizhou, but later lost his way and was demoted to a low-ranking position. One of Zhu Qi’s concubines, named Xinnu, was pregnant for six or seven months when the baby cried in her womb, causing great alarm in the household. A few days later, the baby could speak, sometimes laughing, sometimes crying, or loudly calling out to its parents. When the baby’s teeth and hair were fully developed, its appearance was striking and unsettling, leading some to doubt its auspiciousness. The following year, Zhu Qi joined Yang Shunshao’s naval expedition, but the mission failed, and he, along with his comrades Kai De and Guo Shi, died. He was granted posthumous honors for his loyalty and was named "Zhong," and given an official position. It is unknown whether he survived or not.

Con Duanren Con Duanren, a scholar from Hebei, moved south with his parents and settled in the Xiuwen Lane of Qiantang. He had not yet married. One day, while dining in the kitchen, he saw a beautiful young girl in a state of confusion. She entered from outside and approached him directly, raising her hand to cover the food vessel, trying to break or eat something but failing. His parents were suspicious and asked him about it, but he attributed it to something else and did not speak, as he was deeply infatuated with her beauty. Since then, he became depressed and unhappy, almost like a fool. The girl appeared every night. Once, he was walking alone by the West Lake when the girl approached him, took his sleeve, and smiled, saying, "Do you miss me?" Con became angry and scolded her, saying, "You are a wicked ghost! How can you miss me?" The girl replied, "How do you know I am a ghost? You just saw me walking in broad daylight with no shadow. Aren't you a demon?" Con said, "If you have doubts, follow me to the Four Saints Temple." She took his arm, and they went together. As they entered the temple gate, she suddenly disappeared. They lingered for a long time and then came out, finding her standing by the roadside. Con mocked her, saying, "You are afraid of the Four Saints. How can you be a ghost?" The girl said, "Do you not understand this principle? Even the true saints are women." Con asked, "What do you mean?" The girl replied, "The *Dao Jing* does not say so? The *Tai Yin* transforms and gives birth to the essence of water." Con laughed and they continued on their way. People were surprised to see him walking alone and talking to himself, but no one dared to ask. Soon, he met a friend named Zhang Zhongqing, and the girl hid again. Con then told him about the incident. Zhang said, "Let's put aside the ghostly matter and drink together." They went to a tavern and drank. Zhang sang the song "Scholarly Rain Over Apricot Blossoms," and when he finished, the girl appeared, sitting to Con's right. Con was startled and put down his cup, adding more wine. Zhang saw nothing and kept spitting, still cursing Con for bringing a ghost along. They left abruptly. Con reported this to his father, who was terrified. He waited for Con to return home and took him to the closed door of the Yellow Taoist Master. The master said, "This is possessed by an evil spirit. It must be a cat spirit. I will eliminate it tomorrow."

First, he wrote two talismans and gave them to her. That night, she did not come. By dawn, Huang gave her three more talismans, instructing her to wear one, burn one, and post one on the door. Then he never came back. After several months, because he was sending a funeral carriage out to Guiren Temple at the outer gate of the vegetable market, the woman entered gracefully and exclaimed, "You are too heartless, making Huang the priest harm me. Now all three talismans are in my hands." She showed them and said, "It was not my idea in the first place, but I was forced by my father's command." She said, "If you didn't tell your father, how would he know about me? I don't blame you either. Come with me." Just as they reached the market bridge, she suddenly jumped over the railing into the water. Luckily, there was a reed boat below, and they quickly rescued her and saved her. They asked where she was going, but they only saw several beautiful women leading her to a palace, majestic like a royal residence. Just as they were about to freely wander, the other guests called them back. It was very distressing; I didn't expect to almost fall into an abyss. Being saved was a debt of gratitude. But soon after, she developed a mysterious illness and died after a short time. Nie Gongfu Nie Gongfu was from a family in Gao Tang County, Bo Prefecture. Originally from South Liu Town, they moved to the northern outskirts. They were rich with abundant stores. He believed in spirits. For anything he went to do, no matter how far or important, he would consult the gods. If the gods approved, he would proceed; if not, he would stop. He also strongly believed in witch doctors and consulted superstitious shrines with great reverence. After a long time, many prayers were not answered. Then he became lax and disrespectful. Once, on a midsummer noon, he was sitting in the hall with his servants nearby. Suddenly, he heard a scolding sound. He focused his gaze and saw several young men in yellow robes, small hats, jade belts, and green boots. They waved their sleeves as they passed through the courtyard. They were only a foot tall.

许而厯厶可观，聂震骇呼家众悉至，所覩俨然惊走出外。少年者冉冉腾空驾云，顾从吏一线一缕系通红炭一挺，长三尺，置于屋上。其去稍逺，聂遣仆升梯取之，炭洞赫不可向迩，而一线自若。继此后，百恠继作，中夜车马憧憧，蜡炬旁罗，照耀夺昼，邻里聚观如织，殊不能止。虽邀善法者考治，莫能絶扰，扰扰累月，聂不胜愁挠，遂得疾，竟至不起。 董成二郎 董成二郎者，居楚州北堰虾疃巷，以商贩斛斗自业，赋情险僻，而面状冷峭，有不可犯之色，里巷无不恶之。绍兴庚午嵗夏五月，阴雨大作，董正坐中庭，方具饭，天气暗，霹一声，火光赫然，觉有巨物堕地，视之乃一大雹，髙三四尺，上有二窍，空洞形如耕犁之偃土者，在坐侧尽室褫怖，亟邀道士建醮以禳之。自是得气疾，不能食，奄奄半嵗。一夕月下，见一白鹅，其大比常一倍，从砌间飞入房中，妻执炬访求，无所覩，而董以此时殂。既敛，家人用俚俗法筛细灰于灶前，覆以甑，欲验死者所趋，旦而举之，二鹅足迹俨立于灰上，皆疑董堕畜，其家日以沦败，妻女至为娼云。

In the village of Guan in Yongfeng County, Xin Prefecture, a strange phenomenon arose in the home of a certain scholar during the seventh year of the Chongning era. The phenomenon changed unpredictably, sometimes appearing as a man and sometimes as a woman, throwing tiles and bricks, occupying the main hall, soiling the beds and mats, and destroying household items. The family was greatly disturbed and called in sorcerers to drive the spirits away, but the efforts were not effective. They then ordered Taoist priests to perform rituals, and also invited those skilled in magic to use all their skills. Despite these measures, the spirits seemed to leave temporarily after investigation, but when they left, it returned to its original state. This happened many times before. Guan became increasingly troubled by it and gathered many Taoist priests, setting up altars and prisons, imposing severe punishments. The spirits were not moved by this and shouted loudly and cursed in the room, saying, "You few bald-headed men, you got enough money, I'm not afraid of you, knowing it can't be done, we leave together." After a long time, the phenomenon transformed into a beautiful woman who visited the servant's sleeping place at night, trying to seduce and bewitch him. The servant knew it was a demon, but the lazy servant was greedy for pleasure and ended up staying and having intercourse with her for several nights, extremely intimate and affectionate. He eventually worried it would bring disaster and secretly sharpened a knife to wait for her. When she came again, he tried his best to strike her head, took her out, and called out to everyone, saying, "I have killed the ghost." People from the Guan family came to see, and it was revealed to be a large cat.

马军将田俊 临安步军司钱粮官公淳熙中为祟孽所扰不可居遂废为马院第二将下田俊常隶宿其间一日羣辈尽出俊独留系所乘马于庑下且取随身衣服贮于小箧挂梁上以防草窃方解衣将寝忽一朱髪青躯髙七八尺自外入解其马绊俊大声叱之舍马趋寝所俊怖甚拟趋避而无路可投捽俊髻至寨门呼阍者启闗阍者曰统制约束军门不许夜开门已下锁了曰汝不开门我自从门上过即挟俊腾空出至西湖畔方家谷龙母池边大木下自坐盘石而置俊股上沃池水濯洗又掬泥塞其口若欲啖食俄一老叟白袍方防杖防来咄曰汝阴下小辄欲恣食生人岂不累我纷争不已叟举杖击之搦杖与相拒良久叟力不能胜之撑拄未决复见一长僧貌古恠顶僧伽防持锡杖击始弃而窜俊时裸袒无衣叟命取其所服者须臾而至皆箧中物也俊未暇致谢叟与僧俱不见矣明日院中失俊遍寻访之得于昨夕水次扶以归病十余日乃愈寨内由此建立僧伽塔相而奉事焉 Translation: 马军将田俊 During the reign of Emperor Gaozong in the Southern Song Dynasty, the money and grain official of the infantry command in Lin'an was disturbed by evil spirits and could no longer reside there, so it was abandoned and became the second camp of the cavalry unit. Tian Jun, a subordinate of this camp, often stayed there. One day, all his comrades went out, and Tian Jun alone remained, tying his horse under the veranda and storing his clothes in a small chest hanging from the beam to prevent theft. As he was about to undress and go to sleep, a ghost with red hair and a blue-green body, about seven or eight feet tall, entered from outside, untied the horse's tether, and Tian Jun shouted at it loudly. The ghost left the horse and rushed to the sleeping area, and Tian Jun was very frightened, wanting to escape but found no way. The ghost grabbed Tian Jun's hair and dragged him to the gate of the camp, calling out to the gatekeeper to open the gate. The gatekeeper said, "The commander has ordered that the gate must not be opened at night; it is already locked." The ghost said, "If you do not open the door, I will pass through the door myself." Immediately, it carried Tian Jun and flew out into the air, taking him to the side of West Lake near Fangjia Valley and Longmu Pond, where there was a large tree. The ghost sat on a large stone and placed Tian Jun on its thigh, pouring water from the pond to wash him and scooping mud to block his mouth, as if wanting to eat him. Suddenly, an old man appeared, wearing a white robe and holding a staff with a square end, and scolded the ghost, saying, "You, a small ghost from the underworld, dare to eat a living person? This will not stop the endless disputes!" The old man raised his staff and struck the ghost, which grabbed the staff and resisted for a long time. The old man could not overcome it, and just as he was about to give up, another old monk with an ancient and strange appearance, wearing a Buddhist hat and holding a staff, appeared and hit the ghost. The ghost finally fled, and Tian Jun was naked and had no clothes. The old man ordered his clothes to be brought, and they arrived soon, all from the small chest. Tian Jun did not have time to thank the old man, and both the old man and the monk disappeared. The next day, the camp was missing Tian Jun, and they searched everywhere for him, finding him by the water's edge from the previous evening, carrying him back. He fell ill for over ten days before recovering. As a result, the camp built a statue of the Bodhisattva Guanyin and began to worship it.

Zhai Baji In the regions of Jiang, Huai, Min, and Chu, merchants who travel long distances for months often take women with them to perform tasks such as cooking, fetching firewood, and carrying water. At night, they share a bed and sleep together, like concubines, and are called "aunt." These women are mostly local courtesans. Wang Sanke from Shangrao has been trading in the areas of Lu and Shou his whole life, and every year, he travels back and forth, obtaining a concubine named Zhai Baji. Although Zhai is a woman, she is strong and robust, with superhuman strength. While on the road, she carries loads and pushes carts, her shoulders red and her feet calloused, but she does not consider it tiring, not even strong men can match her. She is also shrewd and profit-oriented, skilled at managing and making a profit of ten percent, buying cheap and selling expensive. Wang gains more profit and becomes increasingly wealthy, saving every small amount of money for her private savings, which also exceed a thousand strings of coins. She secretly buys gold and silver but has no marriage or family ties. She is nearly forty years old and wants to plan for her future. Wang, a cunning and large merchant, although he finds her appearance unattractive, is moved by her wealth and persuades her to become his wife. Zhai empties all the contents of her bag, to give it to him in the future, as he was about to cross the river. The night before, they stayed in a guesthouse together, but the story does not continue beyond this point.

In the morning, he arose first with his luggage, boarded the boat, and hurried to untie the ropes. When Lady Zhai arrived at the water's edge, he was already far away, and he drowned himself in grief for a long time, thinking he had no way forward or back. The man looked back and saw it, thinking he had escaped, and then returned to his hometown. He managed his livelihood, built a house, and lived there, supporting himself more luxuriously than before. He had two young children who were very intelligent and quick to understand. Just as they were playing beside the house, a servant with a long-standing grudge against their father took a knife and killed them all. From then on, strange things began to happen in the family, and a woman with a tall and strong body, resembling Lady Zhai, led a group of ghosts and performed witchcraft. Sometimes at midnight, they would beat drums and clang metal, and a myriad of forms would appear, with tables, shelves, and utensils arranged in the courtyard. The eldest son was quite angry and ordered a magician to deal with it, but the magician did not stop, and the eldest son spoke freely, calling to heaven, and was slandered and insulted. Because he was drunk and beat someone to death, he was barely spared from execution and was tattooed and exiled to the south of the Five Ridges. Only the second son was there, and he became close friends with those who were not content, drinking wine together, and quarreled, and was also harmed. The man grieved and looked back, dying in sorrow, and his wife, poor and starving, died of starvation, left exposed and could not be buried. The house went into the family of a clan member.

Famous General Wu Chao Observation Commissioner Wu Chao, a native of Hebei, served as a general under General Han Qi Wang. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, he served as the governor of Chuzhou and led the military forces in the eastern part of the Huai region. He was known for his straightforward and honest nature, and he did not make enemies with others. In the year of the Earth Tiger (1180 CE), he sent a messenger named Li Wen from Zhenjiang to Hangzhou. Upon returning to the western border of Changzhou, Li Wen saw banners and flags blocking the road, resembling the dignified appearance of a military commander. He hurriedly stepped aside and suddenly heard someone calling his name. Li Wen prostrated himself and bowed twice, looking up to see that it was the official in charge of the Taifu. The Taifu smiled and asked about his well-being, showing great care. Li Wen said, "I wonder where Taifu is going. Are you not being summoned by imperial edict to the capital?" The Taifu replied, "I have been commanded by the Heavenly Emperor to serve as the land administrator of Kunshan in Pingjiang Prefecture. I will depart immediately. Return home quickly and tell my family that I am safe and well, and that everyone is in good health. Instruct the crowd to praise me, and step forward. I have also ordered a subordinate to carry official vouchers worth two thousand as travel expenses for you." Li Wen thanked him and withdrew, traveling at double speed. Upon returning home, he learned that Wu Chao had passed away several days earlier. He then realized that what he had seen were supernatural beings.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 2, Section 1 Imperially Approved Complete Library *Yijian Zhi* Volume 2, Section 2 Song Dynasty, compiled by Hong Mai The Sacred Language A guest from Changshu, the former Secretary of State Zeng, had passed away for a long time and had four sons. In the spring of the first year of Chunxi, he dreamed of telling his eldest son, the county magistrate, "I have been given a heavenly mandate to serve as the first official of the Fushan Mountain Temple. All officials in the temple should offer a private gift as a sign of respect, and this tradition should not be abandoned. There are thousands of fine sheets of paper in the black kitchen of my eastern study hall. You may take them all, dye them yellow, and stamp them with a seal to create the Sacred Language, which should be respectfully burned. Do not neglect my instructions." The magistrate woke up but did not take it seriously. He then dreamed of his second son, who asked a known Taoist priest, Zheng, "What is the Sacred Language? I do not recognize it." Zheng replied, "It is the final chapter of the *Duhuo Jing* (Sutra for Deliverance)." He showed it to the second son, who laughed and said, "It is not very important. How can it be useful for protection or assistance?" He also refused to heed his father's words. Soon after, the third and fourth sons had the same dream. The two younger sons were fond of wine and neglected their duties, paying little attention to the matter. A scholar from the county, Chen, who had always been in the company of Mr. Zeng, dreamed that the Secretary of State came to him and angrily scolded the sons for their filial impiety, saying he would report them to the Heavenly Lord and have them severely punished. Chen hurried to inform them without waiting until dawn, still believing and doubting. By the 26th day of the third month, the situation had not changed.

People from the county gathered at the temple, and Zeng's youngest son, accompanied by a few young men, watched over the proceedings. As they passed by the west wing, they encountered a woman of extraordinary beauty. He fixed his gaze intently, realizing it was the Minister of Works. She stood motionless in the courtyard when two ghosts grabbed him and threw him to the ground, stripping his clothes and scolding, "Unfilial son, how dare you come here!" The people around saw Ji groan and cry in pain, as if he could not bear it. The temple official burned incense and offered prayers, then ordered the attendants to escort him back home. Upon returning to his room, he was in a daze and unaware. All the objects in the room began to strike each other without reason, breaking until they were destroyed. The next day, the county magistrate invited the priest Chen Guoqian to his home. Chen performed rituals to banish and drive away the spirits. He arranged the table in the hall with wine and food, offered prayers, and performed the sacrifice. The family members all saw the spirits appear and sit opposite Chen. Chen repeatedly pleaded earnestly, and thus they were released. After a long time, Ji awoke, sweating profusely. He told everything he had seen to his three brothers and Chen. That very day, he printed 500 copies of this scripture and burned them as offerings to atone for his mistakes.

Tea Shop Servant Cui San In the spring night of the eighth year of the Chongxi era, Mr. Li Shi Liu, a citizen of Huangzhou, had already closed his tea shop under Guanfeng Bridge. His servant, Cui San, had not yet slept when he heard someone knocking on the door and asked who it was. The person replied, "It's me." Cui San thought it was the master and quickly opened the door. It was a young woman with a beautiful appearance. Cui San was startled and asked, "How did you come here, Madam? This is Mr. Li's tea shop. Could it be a mistake?" She replied, "I am the new wife from the house to the left. Because I angered my mother-in-law and was driven out, at midnight, having nowhere to go, I wished to stay for one night." Cui San, being employed by someone, said, "How dare I act on my own?" The woman asked with the threat of death and did not leave. Cui San reluctantly led her to a corner beside the shop, gave her a mat to sleep on, and after she had slept for a long time, he approached her bed and whispered, "I am not used to sleeping alone. Do you have any intention?" Cui San was overjoyed beyond expectation and immediately kept her for the night. She left at dawn. After that, she came often. Cui San, as a servant, obtained a good wife and was very happy, no longer inquiring about the details. One night, the woman pulled out a government bond worth ten thousand coins and gave it to him. After that, she often provided small assistance, and Cui San was even more pleased. The elder brother, Cui Er, who was familiar with hunting, often traveled to other states. He suddenly visited his younger brother and asked about each other, staying for more than ten days. The woman was present.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Example 2 **Source:** 兩頭四臂女 靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。 **Translation:** A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Example 3 **Source:** 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。 **Translation:** In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. --- ### Example 4 **Source:** 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。 **Translation:** From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you. --- ### Example 5 **Source:** 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。 **Translation:** As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the *Shujing* (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the *Liji* (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them. --- Source text: 杳不至崔思恋笃切殆见梦寐乃吐情实告兄兄曰此地多魅虑害汝命宜速为之图崔曰弟与之相从年余且赖渠拯恤义均伉俪难诬以也兄曰然则知我至则絶迹何邪崔曰正以兄弟妨嫌于礼不可兄曰彼每至从何处出入曰入自外门由楼梯而下兄是晩舍去取猎具卷网数枚散布之抵暮再来伏于所三更后戛然有声急篝火照视得一斑狸长三尺死焉兄曰是物盖惑吾弟者焉剥其皮而烹其肉崔惨沮凄泪不能胜情异日独处室中觉异香馥烈一女立灯下大骂曰我与汝恩意如此兼数济汝窘乏何为轻信狂兄之言幸我是时未离家仅杀了一婢壊衫子一领而已崔逊谢女笑曰固知非汝所为吾不恨汝驻留如初至今犹在 Provide only the final translation with no additional commentary.

Luo Chunbo Luo Chunbo, a native of Chongren in Fuzhou Prefecture, stayed in the home of a fellow townsman named Wu Deshou in the year Xianchun (1184) during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song. There were several students receiving instruction from him. One night, Wu Deshou had a dream in which he saw a creature resembling a dog emerge from beneath a cluster of banana plants. Soon after, the creature had two prominent horns and leapt into the air, crossing over to the east side of the house and ascending to the sky, its radiant light illuminating far and wide. Scales and armor fell to the ground, and it was then realized that it was a dragon. Upon waking, Wu Deshou was delighted and told everyone about it, but he did not know who it was or what the auspicious sign meant. When the imperial examination was held that year, only Luo Chunbo passed the test, and his success was attributed to this event. Before the examination, Luo Chunbo walked with a friend, making predictions and agreements, calling the first person a friend and the second a student, and setting the terms of their relationship before encountering the path.

A man looked back and said, "My friend cannot come," and immediately turned pale. Another person said, "The fragrance of osmanthus, it seems, is calling their companions to return together." That year, his friend was dismissed from office and named Luo. The following year, he passed the imperial examination as the second-place candidate. His original name was Weiyue, and his courtesy name was Bo Gao. He studied at a villa in the southwest of the county town. In a dream, someone reported the results, and his name was Fang. He then changed his name to Fang in the year Gengzi of the Qian Dao era. He obtained a local recommendation and had another dream in which he went to the government office and read a golden notice. Among the eight characters in his birth chart, he had two successful exams and four recommendations. He passed the provincial examination twice, nearly topping the list, and was forty-five years old when he was appointed as an assistant to the imperial court. However, he died after only two months in office, which was said to be a sign of his four attainments, though this is not fully understood. As for Yang Zheng, he was known for his ability to foretell destiny. Yang Hanqing of Linchuan studied for the imperial examination in his youth but did not achieve success. When he was about to reach adulthood, he dreamed that someone called out to him from the door, saying, "Yang Zheng, you will become an official." He woke up and was pleased. In the year Gengzi of the Chunxi era, he took the provincial examination and wrote down a number of homophones, randomly selecting one and getting the character "Zheng." He then changed his name to Zheng and his courtesy name to Zijian. He passed the examination and was admitted to the imperial academy. The following year, he passed the imperial examination. Zheng was skilled in divination, and his insights surpassed those of other fortune-tellers. At the time, he traveled east to take the provincial examination and met a fellow candidate named Le Shaoxian, who said, "I will definitely pass the exam, but I fear I will die soon. If I fail, perhaps I can live a little longer." However, driven by his ambition for fame and success, he finally entered the examination hall. After the exam, he returned home and dreamed that he was packing to go east again. He was angry and said, "I have traveled thousands of miles and cannot rest. What will I do next?" His mother comforted him, saying, "I thought you would prepare a carriage to go. Do not worry." When he woke up, he heard the sound of a carriage. Zheng's thoughts flowed like a river, and he could write ten thousand words in a day. Before the imperial examination, he suddenly became ill with red eyes and pain, and could only complete one essay. The good-hearted people in his hometown were sorry for him. When the names were announced, he fell to the bottom of the list, just one name ahead of those waiting for assignment, and was eventually appointed as the magistrate of Shanyue in Tanzhou. He died after several months in office.

Huang Minzhong, originally from Sinwu, temporarily resided in Linchuan. In the winter of the thirteenth year of the Chongxi era (1186 CE), he traveled to the capital to take the provincial examination. On his journey, he hurried through the day and arrived at a lodging place late in the evening. The inn was filled with merchants and foot soldiers, and there was no place for him to rest. Huang told the innkeeper, "It is so dark here that I cannot proceed further. If you do not accommodate me, how can I find peace?" The innkeeper had no choice but to provide him with an empty room. The room was not far from the innkeeper's home. That night, the innkeeper had a dream: a yellow dragon came from outside, used its claws to pry open the door, and coiled itself in the main hall, with brilliant flames that made the innkeeper wake up in alarm. Suspecting that there must be a distinguished guest in the inn, he got up before dawn to visit. He visited several guests, but none were worthy. Only Huang, a scholar, seemed to match the omen. The innkeeper asked and learned that the guest's surname was Huang. He became more confident, ordered the servant to wait until Huang finished washing, and brought tea and soup to him to discuss the dream and ask him to write it on the wall. Huang was secretly pleased but dared not act rashly. The innkeeper sent his two sons again, all dressed in scholar's attire, thinking it could not be avoided, and Huang reluctantly wrote the dream on the wall.

After a few words, he left, and when the results were announced the following spring, he was demoted. Someone said that although he was of high status, he still remained depressed because he had not yet achieved immediate success. Disappointed, he stayed at the residence of Zhang Dingsou, a government official. At that time, there was a man named Nan Ju, who was skilled in fortune-telling and often accurate. The person went to consult him, and after the divination was complete, Nan said, "Your good fortune is merely delayed. It will be better three years later, and you will eventually hold an official position. But there is one concern: your father fears something unforeseen. You should return immediately." At that time, the person's father was at home and just received a message that he was sick. Hearing Nan's words, the person felt anxious and uneasy. He returned westward immediately, and upon arriving at the middle point, his father died as expected. The person should strive to take the exam and can pass it. In the year of Geng Xu, he took the exam in January during the mourning period. There was no way to reach the deadline, but precautions were taken due to the cold snow in early spring, fearing that those from afar would be late. The lock-up period was especially extended by half a month. Therefore, he traveled at full speed to the main hall, but the deadline had already passed. The villagers made arrangements for him to take the exam in a separate place to avoid suspicion, and he passed the exam. The auspicious sign of the yellow dragon is unknown when it will be proven.

Fú Hǔchén's Dream and Divination Fú Hǔchén, known for his broad knowledge and excellent memory, was a renowned scholar in Jiangxi. As a candidate for the imperial examinations, he sought divine guidance by visiting a dream to Mount Yang to predict the timing and success of his exams. In his dream, a red-clad woman held a tablet and sang, but he could not fully understand the meaning. He only remembered one line: "Seeking spring is not being a top scholar." Later, he did not pass the exams but gained an official position by presenting a book to the emperor. Fú Hǔchén worshipped the Zigu deity very respectfully. He often said that the deity's predictions were often accurate. A fellow townsman, Wú Zhòngquán, was about to be transferred to a new position and consulted the deity about his future. When the divination was performed, the deity drew several dragons and sheep. Fú Hǔchén interpreted this as a sign that Wú Zhòngquán would meet the emperor and enter the court. Wú Zhòngquán, who was currently a low-ranking official, asked how this could be possible. Fú Hǔchén replied that even as a commoner, Wú Zhòngquán had been summoned to the emperor's presence, and now that he was already in official robes, his prospects were even better. The deity's words were supported by evidence and should not be mistaken. Wú Zhòngquán privately thought that his fortunes might improve in the years of Chen or Wei. However, when he took up his new position, he was appointed as the magistrate of Longyang. This passage highlights the role of divine guidance and the historical practice of gaining official positions through scholarly work, emphasizing the importance of both personal effort and divine favor in achieving success.

During the year 1131 of the Shaoxing era, a farmer in Fengcheng County dreamed of a Taoist priest who gave him a coin and asked him to deliver it to the fifth official of the Huang family. The farmer replied that there were multiple candidates named Huang who were the fifth in their family, so he did not know which one to deliver it to. The priest then specified the correct candidate as the one living in a certain alley. Upon waking, the farmer was confused and did not tell anyone about the dream. The next day, while collecting firewood in the mountains, he found the coin as described in the dream. The coin was intricately inscribed with symbols. Suddenly, he remembered the dream, but he was surprised by the discovery and did not have time to deliver the coin. The following year, the scholar was preparing for the imperial examination and dreamed again. The same person appeared and asked why the coin was lost. The farmer was advised to deliver the coin immediately or face consequences. He woke up in fear, feeling cold and shivering, indicating he was ill. However, he realized the coin was causing the illness. When the scholar received the coin, he was very happy, believing it was a sign of divine favor. Soon after, he went to the prefecture to take the examination. Huang Hong had studied the *Spring and Autumn Annals* but was dismissed by the examiners. The professor from Jianchang, who obtained Huang Hong's essay, wanted to place it on a supplementary list. He wanted to see the drafts of the essay but found the first draft unsatisfactory. He then took the essay to the main examiner, and they decided to have the essay retyped. After reviewing the dismissed essay, the scholar regretted his earlier mistakes. He thanked the professor for the opportunity to correct his mistakes. The professor corrected the essay and placed it at the top of the supplementary list. Huang Hong was ranked third and passed the examination in the year 1133. ---

The Examination Hall in Shaowu In the eighth month of autumn in the thirteenth year of the Chongxi era, during the local examination in Shaowu, on the fifteenth night, a fire broke out in the copying office, and many of the candidates' examination papers were also burned. The next day, as the candidates entered the examination hall, they all worked together to deal with the officials, who were ordered to capture the culprits. The officials fled to various places, some hiding themselves on the beams, and did not dare to breathe until nightfall. Suddenly, a black object descended from the sky, its appearance like a ghost. It went through the cases more than twenty times, placing three coins each time, and when it saw a case with a happy expression, it placed one coin on the desk and left. Afterward, it held a stick and struck those who did not receive coins, some falling to the ground or dropping their pens, but the candidates did not feel it. The officials thought to themselves, "Isn't it that those who received coins were preselected for recommendation, while those who were struck should be disqualified?" They silently noted ten or so people they knew and used this as a test. When the results were posted, they matched their predictions. Thus, the gains and losses in the examination, at the moment of writing the essay, were indeed under the control of a divine entity in the midst of the examination, and it could not be judged or discussed in terms of skill or clumsiness.

### Translation **Tu Wenbo** Tu Siyou, courtesy name Wenbo, from Yihuang, lost his mother at a young age, and his father Guang did not remarry. He and his brother Siyou, as well as his eldest son Siyue, lived in a humble alley with thatched roofs, content with their simple life. In the spring of the Gengwu year of Shaoxing, Siyou napped during the day and dreamed of a woman with a beautiful appearance. She showed him her right hand, which had golden characters inscribed with two lines of poetry: "In the autumn examination, you must pass the difficult test, and your son will surely ascend." She then showed him her left hand, which had golden characters inscribed with six characters: "Wenbo's wife, Du Shi." After waking up, he told his father and brothers. That year, he was indeed selected in the autumn examination, and the county official Du Xueyu sent a matchmaker to propose marriage. The dowry was not very substantial. Realizing the dream was a sign, he accepted the proposal. He then passed the examination in Xinwei and eventually became the governor of Liuzhou, living happily with Du Shi until old age. **Wang Maosheng** Wang Yi, courtesy name Maosheng, was a scholar from Chongren. In the year Gengshen of Shaoxing, he and his brother Maogeng went to the Yanshan Temple to pray for a dream. He dreamed that someone said to him, "Your name will not be below Zhang Jiucheng." Upon waking, he was very pleased, thinking that in the future his rank in the imperial examinations would be as high as that of Zhang Jiucheng. After being recommended, he went to the provincial examination in the spring of Renxu. At that time, the examination hall was at the Tianzhu Temple. When he entered the examination hall, the place where he set up his table had the name "Zhang Jiucheng" in large characters on the right side. He thought this must match his previous dream and became even more delighted. However, that year was not favorable, as the deity had only indicated the seating arrangement. Initially, Maosheng's father Guoguang, who was still a guest scholar, once dreamed of a large banner hanging in the air, with someone saying from the banner, "This is the assignment topic when your family's descendants pass the examination." The dream was vague and indistinct, not fully clear.

After only recognizing the end of a beautiful character, he instructed his family that all beautiful characters that can be used as titles should be encompassed. He composed an essay on the principles of Yao, Shun, and Wen Wang, and revised it several times because the intention was not fully realized. Zhou Mao Sheng struggled to make progress, and on the first day of the lunar new year in the year of Bingzi, he found a single coin from the Tang Dynasty on the road, which was bright and clean, lovely to look at. He thought to himself, "I believe I will not pass the imperial exams this year, but finding money on New Year's Day might be a sign of divine intervention." Thus, he meticulously studied the origins and development of coins and made extensive preparations. In the year Dingchou, he arrived at the provincial examination hall and composed a poem or essay for the exam titled "Listening to All the Beauty of the World." The topic was the same as his previous essay, and the first question asked about currency. He finally passed the exam. Zhou Guo Guang and Zhou Mao Qian had already passed the exams before this. The father and son succeeded each other in passing the exams, which was envied by Confucian scholars, but they regretted that their official careers were not very successful. --- ### Zhou Family's Three Generations of Success in the Imperial Exams Huai Ren is a small town in Fuzhou. Only three scholars in the area were recommended for the imperial exams, all from the Zhou family. The first was Zhou Zhao, with the courtesy name Yan Bao. His son was Zhou Long Zhang, with the courtesy name Guan Qing. His grandson was Zhou Xiao Ji, with the courtesy name Jun Ju. Zhou Xiao Ruo originally had a different name. In the spring of the Bingwu year of the Chongning era, he dreamed that a person who reported the exam results came to him, carrying a bamboo basket on his back and bowing respectfully at the right side of the steps. After the results were posted, he was not selected. A friend named Yuan Gong Fu dreamed that two solution papers arrived, one for Yuan Shi Cheng and one for Zhou Xiao Ruo, and he was asked to deliver them to Zhou. Yuan delivered them to Zhou in the autumn of the Jiyou year, and they both changed their names and entered the exam. They were both recommended, and the person holding the results was a young man with a bad reputation, whose attire and behavior were similar to those in the dream. There was no significant difference between the dream and reality.

### Final Translation **Huang Pu's Dream Concerning His Name** In Chongren, Huang Pu was initially named something else and had been taking the examinations for a long time. In the autumn of the seventh month of the ninth year of the Chongning era (1182), he dreamed that a person who announced the examination results arrived. He hurried out to see it, and only saw someone wearing a scholar's robe, not like a messenger. Both of his brothers were standing in a line wearing white robes. Huang asked his middle brother, "Who is the one who passed?" The middle brother replied, "It is you." Huang asked, "What is your name?" The middle brother replied, "Pu." In the dream, Huang did not ask how to write the character. In the morning, he wrote it on the wall, thinking that there were multiple characters with the same pronunciation and unable to decide which one to use. He intended to use the character "普" but had not yet decided. That night, his friend dreamed of someone bringing the announcement of the examination results. The eldest son recommended a book, and in Chongren, only one person with the surname Huang passed. Huang privately congratulated himself, saying, "Li's dream was intended to support my name 'Pu.'" The following year, he changed his name and took the examination, eventually being selected. Li's eldest son indeed passed along with him. **Yijian Zhi Volume II** **The Complete Library in Four Branches** **Yijian Zhi Volume III** **By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty** **The Monk of Anan Temple** The monks at Anan Temple in Raozhou claimed the land and water resources, using them for their own benefit, which was better than other temples. Although called a temple, the monks were all local people who passed down the tradition of seeking profit for themselves. The monk named Miao Bian was especially known for his bad behavior and lack of adherence to the precepts. His robes and bowl were somewhat valuable, and he treasured them as if they were his head and eyes. He died of illness, and at the moment of death, he had something stuck in his throat, showing his reluctance to let go due to attachment. The temple community observed him and realized that his confusion was due to his attachment to material possessions and mental disorder. After he died, the process of preparing the body for burial began, and the monk Fa Zhen guarded the coffin until it was lifted and burned.

On the evening of the first day of June, as dusk fell, Fa Zhen lit candles and incense. She heard footsteps creeping around her, but upon turning to look, she saw nothing. Feeling quite afraid, she was still half-asleep when she noticed the ghost, Miao Bian, slowly emerging from the wall. It looked just as it had in life, tapping the offering table, clicking its fingers, and sighing deeply. The ghost then opened a chest, examined its contents, and closed it again. Next, it opened and closed the door before angrily pushing two walls, causing them to collapse with a loud sound. Fa Zhen was terrified and called for help, after which the ghost disappeared. She fell ill and nearly died as a result. The head monk ordered the monks to carry her coffin out of the city and burn it, and all her belongings were sold to fund the memorial services. The strange occurrences finally ceased. Liu, the wife of the loyal official Wang Cheng, who had served as the governor of Raozhou and later passed away, moved from her old residence near the city god temple in the west alley to a rented house in the south of the archway, owned by the Zhong family. Before Liu moved in, a local official named Li had lived there, died of a severe illness, and jumped into the well. Ghosts frequently appeared in the empty house, causing strange events, and the people of Poyang were well aware of these occurrences. Liu rented the house because the rent was low and she was unaware of the previous events. After moving in, she heard knocking and sighing sounds. Upon investigation, she learned the cause and placed the *Beidou Jing* on the well cover. The entire family stayed awake all night, keeping the lights on to ward off the ghosts. After the second drumbeat, they saw the well cover rise on its own. A ghost emerged from the well, holding onto the edge, naked, and playing with the *Beidou Jing*, laughing loudly. The ghost mocked the *Beidou Jing*, saying it could not harm it, and returned the text to its original place, walking slowly to the front door, coughing a few times, and then disappearing. Liu and her family were terrified, hugging each other and sitting for a long time. The ghost pulled on the person's clothing, clicked its fingers, sighed, and circled around until midnight, then disappeared into the well. The next evening, the same thing happened. Liu quickly moved away, and no one dares to live there after her.

The Ghost Fight in Jingdezhen In the first year of the Chongxi era, during the early summer, fishermen from Jingdezhen in Fuliang set their nets in the Poyang Lake. At midday, the scenery was clear and harmonious, with no ripples on the water. They were busy casting bait when suddenly a strong wind arose, and the cold air felt like deep autumn. The fishermen quickly rowed their boats to the shore. Suddenly, they saw more than a hundred tall and imposing men, all with tattoos and conical hairstyles, with dignified and imposing appearances, circling around a sandbar. Among them was a giant wearing a blue cap, green robe, brown boots, and a jade belt, holding a gold cup, sitting on a rope bed, directing the crowd. He divided them into two groups, the east and the west, each armed with spears, shields, and swords, fighting each other. Their bravery was like that of tigers, and the sound of the fight was loud. After a while, the eastern group won, retreated, and rested briefly. The western group, defeated, turned and fled, transforming into cattle. The cattle swam across the water using their noses. The eastern group shouted and pursued them, shaking the sky. After the cattle crossed, they spread out and disappeared, their whereabouts unknown. That year, an epidemic broke out near the county and town, with houses filled with sick people in the west, while the eastern village remained peaceful. They then realized that what the fishermen had seen was significant. The victorious group in the fight was the village gods, and the retreating group that transformed into cattle were plague demons.

In the autumn of the year 1180, during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Song Dynasty, I, Yu Yongzhong, set out to take the provincial examinations in Raoyang. Before the examination hall was opened, my servant Cheng Xin had a dream in which he saw several people holding small notes saying they would come to the residence of the governor. As soon as we entered the governor's residence, Cheng Xin exclaimed, "Wrong, wrong," and it turned out that the person in question was known as Qianyilang. The next day, Cheng Xin told me about the dream, and I was not pleased. Qianyilang was a nickname for one of my relatives, Zhizhuan, who could write exam essays but was less skilled than me. The nickname "Qianyilang" was a local custom of playful teasing, indicating someone who was foolish or naive. On September 10th, the official announcement of the results arrived. Indeed, the first to visit my home was Zhizhuan. Initially, there was a mistaken belief, but he was eventually recognized. He brought a bottle of wine, which we drank, and then the official list of successful candidates arrived, which included my recommendation. The process of creating the list of successful candidates was often hurried, with names written on small slips of paper. Sometimes, pre-selection would occur, and these pre-selected names would be marked as evidence, leading to many errors. On this occasion, Zhizhuan came to congratulate me, and I consoled him and compensated him with the wine.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, Yu Ronggu, from Leiping, drifted and moored on the Huai River due to famine. He偶然 obtained the Five Thunder Method and gradually practiced it. At that time, many oxen in the village were sick, and he went to treat them, always curing them and receiving considerable rewards, which allowed him to make a living and settle there. In the year 1179, he temporarily returned to his hometown. His cousin Zhiquan's wife, Zhan, whose parents happened to be in the Huai region, sent her back. However, she became ill and spoke deliriously, acting like a madman or a fool, and no longer recognized people or understood things. They then invited Ronggu to examine her condition and perform the ritual to summon and question the spirits, revealing that Zhan's deceased ancestors were involved. Ronggu looked and said that he could prevent the illness from worsening. At that time in the bedroom, he immediately raised his hand forward as if accepting protection. Then he used a whip to interrogate, and she cried out and confessed guilt. He gently explained to her, saying, "You are Zhan's ancestors and should naturally follow the descendants to their place and receive incense. How dare you擅自 enter someone else's home and cause harm and disaster?" I consider you to be related by marriage and do not wish to apply punishment. You should leave quickly. She immediately apologized and requested release, and he agreed to it. In a short while, the patient returned to normal.

During the reign of Emperor Shao of Song, in the year 1135, the magistrate Yu Jiage of Yeping, a place in Jiangxi, remarried a woman surnamed Xu. Her former husband, a man surnamed Cheng, had come to marry her. Xu was a woman of a severe and harsh temperament, and she was scolded and blamed day and night by her new husband. She could not endure it and hanged herself. Another maid, while drying shoes for a young child, accidentally burned a piece of silk. She was severely beaten for this mistake and died shortly after. Several years later, in June of 1135, Yu Jiage was about to take up his post as magistrate of Yuan'an County in Xia Prefecture. One of his kitchen maids placed a ladder at the end of the house and overturned a jar of sauce while climbing it. Just as she reached the first step, she lost her footing and fell. She made no sound, but she held onto the ladder with both hands, raising her neck and hanging on the rungs. An old woman saw this and called out to her, but she did not respond. The old woman approached to look at her and found that her face was either blue or red, her breathing was like a saw being dragged, and she remained motionless. They carried her back home and gave her soup to drink, but she remained slightly unconscious until dawn. My cousin Taiheng, who was quite skilled in magical spells, was called to investigate and treat her. Although her breathing slightly stabilized, she remained dazed as before. Taiheng said, "This is because she is possessed by a ghost; it is not something I can exorcise." He recommended that we call Master Peng, who lived twenty miles away and was skilled in the Three Pure Land rituals. Therefore, a servant was sent all night to invite him. When Master Peng arrived, he drew magical symbols, spat water, walked the ritual circle, and recited incantations for some time. The kitchen maid then revived. She said she remembered that when she was just climbing the ladder, she saw two women come and grab her throat, throwing her to the ground. She felt her mind was confused and followed them, arriving at a grand mansion with a high and bright hall, where tables and wine vessels were arranged, all made of gold and silver. They led her into the kitchen, where they put her in a pot with soup, and she could smell the guests drinking and laughing. It felt like one night passed, and suddenly people ran in and shouted, "The Master has called!" and she was able to return. She realized that the two ghosts were the deceased wife and the maid.

**Dong Zai Brothers** Yu Yi's daughter married Dong Zai from Yudian. Dong Zai traveled to take the provincial examination and stopped at Donghu Lake. He visited the author's sister's husband, Xu Dasheng. A servant of Dong Zai excused himself by saying he had to go out. Xu Dasheng saw Dong Zai and his brother still relaxed and unconcerned on the boat and assumed they were part of the Yu family and treated them disrespectfully, angrily cursing loudly. Dong Zai's brother appeared as if he did not hear the cursing. Xu Dasheng returned to the city gate and met Dong Zai, greeting him and engaging in conversation. Xu Dasheng still felt angry and asked why Dong Zai could come so quickly from the boat. Dong Zai explained that he and his brother were twins with identical appearances, and the one who appeared relaxed was his brother. Xu Dasheng felt ashamed and laughed loudly. **Zhou Gou Shi** In Chongyang County, Yuezhou, there was a village witch named Zhou Gou Shi. She could perform minor rituals and spells and was fond of eating dog meat. She was most skilled at summoning rain. Her method involved using paper money, offerings, and a bamboo stick inserted into water at a spring or water source. For those who came to request rain due to drought, she would list their names and fields in a petition, and the rain would fall within a day. Only those whose names were listed in the petition would receive rain. Sometimes, even if an area was close to the source, it might not receive rain. When villagers sought rain, they would first offer money and grain, and if they could not afford it, they would even sell their oxen as collateral. If the rain did not come, they would feel disappointed and frustrated.

Then he would carry money to redeem it. What Zhou obtained was not insignificant, but he would only use it to buy alcohol and meat for drinking and eating. His dwelling was just a single thatched cottage. The springs and wells he pierced or the sources of water would dry up immediately. He feared that the local people would suppress him, so he would definitely go at night. The magistrate often suffered from drought and would visit many shrines, but he would not respond. He called Zhou to pray. Zhou said, "Please request that the county magistrate and his assistants all go to a certain place. He needs rain gear to go, or he will be suddenly soaked and have no way to protect himself." The magistrate reluctantly followed. After the ritual was just completed, heavy rain came. Zheng Zongshuo, the eye doctor from Linchuan, once traveled to the mountains and recognized the person. He regretted not donating his bag and teaching his technique. The method of piercing springs is not recorded elsewhere. Zheng Zongshuo, the eye doctor from Linchuan, once traveled to the mountains and recognized the person. He regretted not donating his bag and teaching his technique. Daoist nun Miaojing Yu Zhongyong initially had an eye disease and summoned Zheng Zongshuo, the eye doctor from Linchuan, to scrape away the cataract. He stayed at the Xu family's hermitage near his residence. Because the method requires avoiding noise and dust to prevent damage, it was the eleventh month, and he had just settled down. He stood at the door and met a Daoist nun named Yueqin who came blindly. She could only distinguish the road, bowed forward, and would not leave. He asked, "Who are you and where are you from?" She replied, "I am Miaojing, just from Yeguan. I usually go to wealthy families to beg. Unfortunately, I have an eye disease. I heard that officials have invited a good doctor to this place, so I wish to see him. But Miaojing is a beggar, barely surviving, with no money in my bag. I beg you to help me establish a connection so that I can see the light of day again." Yu felt compassion and ordered the young monk to lead her to the kitchen to stay for the night. It was already dusk, and he would wait until dawn to treat her. The young monk saw this.

**The Daoist Nun** In the Dragon Swirl Cave, there was a woman who enjoyed boiling water and washing her feet. She often used subtle words to tease and, at night, placed the bed together to share the same room. The condition was extremely easy to treat, but it still required several days to complete. I told her, "You are a woman and staying here would be inappropriate. You only have concerns about sleep and food. I will send you to the servant's house for a temporary rest and provide you with food." She laughed and said, "That's a good idea. But I am actually a man, not a woman." I observed her movements, mannerisms, and speech, and found no doubt that she was a woman. I did not want to be deceived by her pretense, so I called the servant to guide her to the servant's house. I visited slowly and carefully, and indeed, she was a man. He usually called himself a Daoist nun and visited wealthy households extensively. Sometimes he stayed for over ten nights, engaging in various forms of immorality. Only now are people beginning to know about it. **The Dragon Swirl Cave** In LePing County, thirty-five li west, there is a place called Dragon Swirl Cave. It is said that in ancient times, a dragon emerged from the ground and formed a pool, the exact year of which is unknown. In the first year of Shaoxi (1191), on the eighteenth day of the seventh month, there was a great thunderstorm, and during the day, it was as dark as night. People sitting opposite each other could not see each other, and everyone thought it was evening. After a while, the sky cleared, and the sun appeared, shining on the mountains. Looking at the side of the official road, there was a hole in a mulberry garden, from which a smoke-like vapor was rising. Soon after, someone came from two li away and said they had just seen a huge dragon, hundreds of feet long, twisting and rising into the sky. The spectators were terrified and remained in shock for several days. They tried to measure the depth of the hole with a rope and stones, but they could not reach the bottom. In the eleventh month of the same year, I, Zhong Yong, passed by and heard the elders talk about it. I went to the place and found that the hole was about two chi wide, with clear water inside. The deeper it went, the wider it became, and white vapor still rose from the outside. It was only about a hundred steps away from the old pool. This place is far from rivers and lakes, yet a divine dragon lives here, which is hard to understand.

The Garden of Xiaosheng and the Snake Wen An Gong's Xiaosheng Garden is located to the south of Miaojing Temple, with its western side being secluded and overgrown, making it hard to see the end of the wild vegetation. In the year 1194 of the Shaoxi era, on the 20th day of the seventh month, the gardener Xu San poured water into a jar at noon and saw two dogs capturing a snake as large as a pillar, which was about five or six feet long. The snake turned its head to bite the neck of one of the dogs. One dog bit the snake's head, causing it to become stuck in its throat, unable to move. The snake then bit the dog's tongue, causing it to spit out the snake. Soon after, both dogs died from the snake's venom. The snake had black flowers with square patterns and fine hair covering its body, and it was identified as a five-step viper (铁甲五步盖蝮). Zhu Wushizhi, the Fifty Scholar Zhu Zhongshan, a native of Poyang, was originally a minor official in the imperial administration but later quit his job to study and became known as the Fifty Scholar. He lived in the Upper Lane. On the evening of August 4, 1194, a storm suddenly broke out, with thunder and lightning surrounding his house for a long time. After the first watch, he heard voices in the air saying they were going to the fields to harvest. Someone asked, "Where are you?" They replied, "In the fields of Zhang." After the voices stopped, a black mist suddenly appeared, moving northwest and fading away. The next morning, his family found that the roof tiles were all broken. At that time, Zhu was at his estate for several days overseeing the rice harvest. His wife feared divine retribution and invited the Taoist priest Zhang Tianqing to come.

Zhu Pingsheng went to make amends to Zhang, who said it was the wrath of heaven, and he dared not proceed. Later in the afternoon, a servant came and said that the scholar had been struck by lightning and died the previous night. Zhu Pingsheng was known for being greedy and stingy with his wealth, unwilling to help others, yet no one had heard of him doing any overt evil.
