Having suffered a severe calamity, some suspected that there must be some hidden treachery. His son-in-law Tao Sheng, who was in the city, heard of it and rushed to see him. He returned home two days later and also died. On the day Zhu was to be buried, the mourners were walking along the field path when suddenly they were struck by lightning, and all of them crouched down and hid. On October 6th, lightning struck again, and the coffin was exhumed. His wife ordered the monks to recite sutras, perform penance, and pray for the cessation of all anomalies. The monks of the Zuhu Temple in Poyang dreamed that someone told them they needed to boil something through with three bowls of water. They repeated the message twice but could not understand it. The next day, a boy brought a large white mushroom, as big as a bucket, saying he found it under a tree in the back mountain. The monks were delighted and immediately ordered it to be boiled. Initially, they used about one bowl of water, but after some time, the water dried up, and the mushroom lay flat like a living thing. They added more water three times, but it did not cook. The monk suddenly remembered the dream and suspected it was an unusual object. He called the boy with a hoe to the place where it was found. They dug just two feet and saw a dead snake with its mouth still open, exhaling. The heat from the steam had cooked the mushroom. There were many small branches nearby. Villagers who ate them died within a day. The monks of the temple were all saved from this disaster, showing the power of the dream.

Hong Jili In the year 1100 of the Yuanfu era, Hong Xuan, known as Hong Jili, was born. By the year 1146 of the Shaoxing era, he had reached the age of fifty-eight. On a certain day in June, he got up early and invited guests, including his cousin Qiao, to talk with him. He said, "I had a good dream last night; please congratulate me. Last night, while I was sleeping deeply, I saw a deity patting my back and laughing, saying, 'Your lifespan was originally limited to sixty-eight years, but because you have recently accumulated hidden virtue, which is highly valued by the unseen forces, it has been increased by ten years.' There was no time to inquire about what it was, and I woke up suddenly. Thus, my age is approaching eighty. From now on, I still have twenty years to enjoy a leisurely life in the countryside, which can be called an unexpected blessing." Qiao and I recited and praised it together, and we were just about to invite relatives and friends to celebrate with wine. That night, just before dawn, Hong Jili suddenly fell ill and had severe diarrhea. By the next day at noon, he did not get up. The divine message said that the number of years was reduced by ten. Evil spirits can treat people so cruelly. Peng's Coffin The wife of a cousin named Cai, whose name is Peng, gave birth to a child in late August of the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, but the child did not survive and died. Peng fell seriously ill, and Cai's father stayed in Jinling (Nanjing) for some reason. My uncle placed a coffin for her and prepared it with lacquer and decoration, costing a total of one hundred thousand coins. After ten days, the illness slightly improved. My uncle thought it was unlucky to keep the funeral equipment at home and discussed selling it to provide money for medicine and food. There was already a person named Wang Sanlang who had agreed to pay. On September 7th at noon, my uncle took a nap and dreamed of an old man seven feet tall who came from outside carrying a staff. The old man said, "This container is ready to use. Do not move it recklessly. Why are you so eager? If it is not as desired, how will you find it in a hurry?" My uncle woke up in a state of shock and quickly sent a child to inquire. The patient's condition had already changed, and she died three days later.

### Translation The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Three Imperial Library Collection *Yijian Zhi* Volume Four By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Young Woman of Quzhou Li Wushi, a resident of Quzhou, originally came from a wealthy family but later fell on hard times and now manages households for others. In the year 1158, during the third month of the night, the weather was clear and moist, with a slight cloud covering the moon. He was alone in a small pavilion when someone seemed to touch his face. Startled, he got up, thinking it was almost dawn. He was just about to go to the prefecture to submit a petition, so he put on his formal clothes and hurried down to the city gate. Hearing the night watch bell ring three times, he felt uneasy in his mind and wandered aimlessly in front of the Ban Chun Hall. Suddenly, he smelled a strange fragrance. He looked up and down, peering around, and saw lights inside the hall, which made him even more curious. He wondered how there could be such things at midnight. Climbing the steps to take a closer look, he saw a young woman, about eighteen or nineteen years old, standing alone, leaning against a pillar with a small lantern. Her posture was extremely graceful, and she greeted him with a smile and a bow, saying, "May you be well, sir." Li humbly thanked her and nodded. The woman asked, "Are you here to submit a petition to the prefect?" Li said yes. He then asked, "I don't know who you are or what your family does."

And arrived saying, "I am the wife of Qiu Miao, a scholar from the eastern part of the city. I was married for only a few months when, unfortunately, my husband died. I lived in a small house and suffered bullying and abuse from my neighbors. They bullied me terribly, and I could not bear it or complain, so I had no choice but to report it to the authorities. If not for coming at night, they would have certainly intercepted me; it was inevitable." Li Zheng was pleased with her appearance and found her words charming and pleasing to listen to. Looking around, there was no one else, and his emotions could not be restrained. He tried to use subtle words to flirt with her, and she happily complied. They became intimate and tender, and soon the east began to turn white (dawn). The county magistrate had already taken charge of the case, and the two of them waited until the judgment was made and then parted ways. The woman agreed to meet again tonight, but before the agreed time, they met again, and gradually, more than thirty days passed. Li's family was surprised that he was out at night for several consecutive days and suspected he must have some immoral behavior. They told the ruffians to capture him, and they caught him behind the Ban Chun Hall, just as he was sleeping with the woman. The crowd shouted and rushed forward, and both were captured. The woman pulled her arm and made a sound, transforming into a green fox and running out. The crowd was shocked and chased after her, but they could not see her anymore.

"People encounter strange misfortunes that are not within the scope of foresight or precaution. During the Shaoxing period, when the main armies were stationed in Huai and Zhejiang, an unnamed officer of the Zhenjiang military post was using an iron awl to pierce a hole. A colleague standing beside him playfully scratched his armpit. The officer raised his hand to avoid, and the awl then entered his left eye, piercing the eyeball and causing it to protrude. He fainted for a while and gradually regained consciousness but ultimately lost his sight and was removed from military records. Recently, I saw him in Jiankang and personally heard his account. The man named Yan Si from Raocun, who managed a garden south of Danting Lake, was squatting and planting leeks. His trousers were torn, and a dog suddenly attacked from behind, biting off one of his testicles. He collapsed in a pool of blood. Neighbors rushed to help, and it took two days before he could feel pain. A child from Minqing County in Fuzhou, aged eight or nine, was defecating in a field when a pig came and ate part of his intestines, also biting off both kidneys, but he survived. After the wound healed, a hole like a pig's mouth remained, through which he urinated and defecated. Huang Yong's father raised him as a servant, and he came to Poyang."

**Little Red Qín** **TianTai Wang Qing Shu, whose courtesy name was Qing Shu, was born during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song. He was quick-witted and skilled in various arts, including playing the zither, chess, divination, music theory, archery, and medicine. He possessed a zither of exceptional quality, which he treasured deeply. After serving as an Attendant in the Imperial Residence, he was appointed to govern Huai West. By the time of the Chunxi era, he was forty-three years old. In his hometown, he dreamed of a Taoist clad in a cloud-like robe and wearing a celestial garment, whose aura reached the heavens. Upon seeing him, the Taoist gave him the zither. Wang was overjoyed and played with it, realizing it was already his own. The Taoist smiled and said, "This zither is from my family, why did you take it?" Wang replied, "I merely have this zither but do not understand its meaning." The Taoist then turned the zither over to reveal a dragon pool on the underside, inscribed with eight characters: "One year of one decade, the matter lies with Little Red." Wang was about to ask more when he woke up. He casually wrote the characters on a note and did not bother to record them again. In the Shaoxi era, he was summoned back from Luzhou and appointed as the Junior Minister of the Imperial Clan. He bought a young girl skilled in chess from a broker, asking her name, which turned out to be "Little Red." He suddenly remembered his dream, and it had been exactly one decade since then. The girl was not only skilled in her craft but could also play the seven-stringed zither. He bought her at double the usual price and gave her the zither he cherished, treating her with great favor. Just two days later, he was ordered to serve in the Jin Dynasty. Wang was skilled in the Five Elements, and that year's astrological influences were unfavorable. He did not wish to go and declined repeatedly, but he could not refuse. On the seventh day of the seventh lunar month, he left the country and arrived in Suzhou, where he developed a boil on his back. By the time he reached Jingkou, the condition had worsened, and he died before reaching Yangzhou. On his deathbed, he left a message to inform his family to send the girl to her parents in Huai. The people of Huai said, "The ten miles of Yangzhou are the Little Red Bridge," which coincided with his dream. Initially, while serving in Luzhou, he was fond of rewarding soldiers excessively, leading to a lack of moderation. When he left Luzhou, military supplies were depleted. His successor, Zhang Kaofang, could not continue, and frequent reductions were made, leading to an unexpected disaster. In the vast regions of Huai and Shu, spanning thousands of miles, two people died on the same date, the twelfth day of the seventh month. Indeed, it is strange!

A Playful Warning for Performers Jesters and dwarfs, though considered the lowest and most insignificant performers, are also capable of using playful language to offer advice or criticism on current politics, aligning with the ancient practice of reciting and advising. What the world calls "miscellaneous plays" is this. In the early years of the Chongning era, loyal and virtuous officials of the Yuanfu period were expelled, and academic pursuits were restricted. Any performance involving events or people from the Yuanfu period, regardless of size, was prohibited from being performed by entertainers. However, when performing in the presence of the emperor, one soldier played the role of chancellor, sitting and proclaiming the beauty of court politics. A monk requested an eight-year period of wandering, and upon checking his ordination certificate, it was found to be issued in the third year of the Yuanfu period. The certificate was immediately torn up, and he was made to wear a hat and a headband, symbolizing his return to secular life. A Taoist priest lost his ordination certificate, and upon asking when he became a priest, it was revealed that he became a priest during the Yuanfu period. He was stripped of his priestly garments and made a commoner. A scholar who, in the fifth year of the Yuanfu period, was recommended and thus exempt from the imperial examination, was not appointed to a position. He was sent away by order. Later, the manager of the palace treasury whispered in his ear, saying, "Today, from the left treasury, I asked for one thousand strings of cash as the chancellor's allowance, and all the money is from the Yuanfu period. It should be taken as a precaution." The person bowed his head for a long time and said, "Bring it in through the back door." The assistant lifted the staff to strike him.

The response was: "You, as a prime minister, only want money." At that time, the emperor also smiled. Cai Jing served as prime minister, and his brother Cai Bian served as chancellor of military affairs. Cai Bian was the son-in-law of Wang Anshi, and they honored Wang Anshi as their father-in-law. When the ceremony of offering sacrifices to Confucius at the Confucian temple was held, Wang Anshi was elevated to the rank of co-offering and enfeoffed as the Prince of Shu. Court jesters set up a performance where Confucius was seated centrally, and Yan Hui, Mencius, and Wang Anshi were on either side. Confucius commanded them to sit. Wang Anshi invited Mencius to sit above. Mencius declined, saying, "Among the highest ranks of nobility, the one who holds the most esteemed position is the Duke of Zhou. Mencius was only granted the rank of duke. Why be humble? So, Wang Anshi then invited Yan Hui to sit above. Yan Hui said, "I am a poor commoner from a narrow alley, having achieved nothing significant in my life. You, as a true sage of the enlightened age, hold a higher status. To decline is excessive." Wang Anshi then sat above Yan Hui. Confucius could not remain seated and also stood up. Wang Anshi became fearful and bowed, saying he dared not. The situation remained unresolved. Zilu, outside, was furious and could not bear it. He rushed to the hall of secondary offerings and dragged Gong Yechang out by the arm. Gong Yechang appeared distressed and asked, "What crime have I committed?" Zilu rebuked him, saying, "You completely failed to save your father-in-law. Look at others' sons-in-law, who are better." This was meant to ridicule Cai Bian. At that time, the incident occurred.

The intention was to elevate Wang Anshi above Mencius for this reason, and that was all. They once set up three groups representing Confucianism, Taoism, and Buddhism, each reciting their teachings. The Confucian said, "What I have learned is benevolence, righteousness, propriety, wisdom, and faithfulness, which are called the Five Constants. Thus, they elaborated on their defenses, all citing classical texts without mixing in vulgar language." Next, the Taoist said, "What I have learned is metal, wood, water, fire, and earth, which are called the Five Elements. Also speaking the general idea, when it came to the monk, he clapped his hands and said, 'You two are just talking about stale, common knowledge.' What I have learned is birth, old age, illness, death, and suffering, which are called the Five Transformations. The scriptures are profound and not something you can understand. I will explain the wonderful principles of the Buddha and Bodhisattvas to you. Why don't you ask me in order?" I dare to ask about birth, saying that from the inner university to the outer provinces, all scholars who read are all three-shed students. They live in beautiful houses, read monthly books, and are tested quarterly. After three years, they are compared, and if they pass, they can become high officials. The state treats scholars like this. I dare to ask about old age, saying that if one is lonely, poor, and in need, they will end up in the ditches. Now, there are orphan and elderly homes where they are cared for their entire lives. The state treats the elderly like this. I dare to ask about illness, saying that now there are hospitals where they are treated. The state treats the sick like this. I dare to ask about death, saying that now there are public cemeteries where they are buried. The state treats the dead like this. I dare to ask about suffering, saying that now there are relief homes where they are taken care of. The state treats the suffering like this. Nothing is unhelpful in matters.

**Translation:** When asked about illness, it was said: "Unfortunate to have an illness, poor and unable to receive treatment, there are charitable institutions called Anji Fang that provide shelter, assign doctors, and give medicine, holding them accountable for complete effectiveness. Thus is how it is done for the sick." When asked about death, it was said: "Death is something no one can avoid. Only the poor have nowhere to go, so they choose vacant land to build a public cemetery. If there are no means to bury them, they are given coffins so they can be buried. Offer sacrifices in spring and autumn, extending grace to the netherworld. Thus is how it is done for the dead." When asked about suffering, the person closed their eyes and did not respond, suddenly feeling a sense of compassion and fear. After urging them repeatedly, they frowned and answered: "It is just that the common people suffer immensely." Emperor Huizong felt compassion and pondered long without considering it a sin. During the Shaoxing period, Li Chunnian implemented the land measurement law. At the beginning of the implementation, the counties and prefectures strictly followed orders, causing much hardship for those in charge. The best officials sat together like the three great sages. A disciple from the last seat asked about doubts. Mencius exclaimed: "Benevolent governance must start with land boundaries. After 1500 years, my words are being used in a holy era. All 3000 disciples are not as good as me." Yan Hui remained silent.

When asked about illness, one says: "Unfortunate is the one who falls ill, yet poor and unable to receive treatment. Thus, there are the Anji Fang (public medical institutions), which provide shelter, assign physicians, and dispense medicine, demanding perfect efficacy. Thus is the treatment of illness." When asked about death, one says: "Death is unavoidable for all people, yet the poor have nowhere to return. Thus, they select vacant land to establish the Louze Yuan (public cemetery). If they lack means for burial, they are given a coffin, allowing them to be buried and honored with seasonal sacrifices, their benevolence reaching even the underworld. Thus is the treatment of death." When asked about suffering, the person closes their eyes and does not respond. With compassion and a start of alarm, one urges them repeatedly, and finally they furrow their brow and reply: "It is simply that common people endure boundless suffering." Emperor Huizong, moved by compassion, long reflected upon this and did not hold it as a crime. During the Shaoxing era, Li Chunnian implemented the Land Boundary and Measurement Law. At the beginning of its enforcement, the counties and towns, having received strict orders, placed great hardship upon the people in their roles. Among them, the most capable sat among the Sages and Teachers in a triumvirate. A disciple rose from the last seat and inquired about his doubts. Mencius then spoke up, saying: "Benevolent governance must begin with land boundaries. I have been dead for 1,500 years, yet my words are now being put into practice in this sacred age. All three thousand disciples are not as good as my Yan Hui, the silent one."

A silent person or someone standing by laughed and said, "If you weren't destined to die prematurely, you would surely cause some scandalous trouble." At the time, Qin Hui was advocating for peace negotiations, and those who heard this were afraid of being punished. Without waiting for the segment to finish, they accused him of slandering the sages and had him arrested and sent to prison. The next day, he was beaten and expelled from the country. In the year Ren Xu, during the provincial examination, Qin Hui's son, Xizi, and others were all nominated, but public opinion was hesitant and no one dared to speak up. By the spring of the year Yi Chou, the top performer at the theater set up a scenario for scholars to gather and discuss who the chief examiner might be. Someone pointed to a high-ranking official, a Shangshu or Shilang, and said they would be in charge of the examination. The performer replied, "No, this year it will definitely be Yue." The questioner asked, "I have never heard of such an official in the court." The performer said, "He is the King of Chu, Han Xin, and Peng Yue." The questioner laughed and said, "If it's not Han Xin, how could he have taken the Three Qins?" The audience was too afraid to understand and left in a commotion. Qin Hui also dared not openly punish them. As for the kitchen maid of the Ye family, Ye Zhengze from Yongjia was an official responsible for peace negotiations and pacification in Hubei. One of his kitchen maids suddenly became pregnant, and he suspected she had an illicit relationship with a servant. However, this maid was rural and simple-minded, and she was very cautious in her behavior, so he ignored the matter. Later, after ten months, she gave birth to a child. She remained silent and did not make a sound. When she touched the child's body, it was cold and lifeless. She lit a fire and looked at it, and it turned out to be a clay figurine. She held it and threw it away. An old man followed her and called out, "My child! Don't throw him away." He picked it up and took it away. It was later revealed that the child was a spirit or ghost from a local shrine. The Ye family did not investigate further.

Three Blooms --- According to the collection of Su Shi (Su Dongpo), Xu Wangshi, the Deputy Magistrate of Fangzhou, wrote to me saying, "In our county, there is a strange person who always wears three blooms and no one knows his name. He can compose poems filled with otherworldly language and can write true people. He can also write his own portrait, and those who have obtained it wish to give me one. I have written a poem for him: ``` Learning the Dao has not been successful, my hair has already turned white. There is no need to labor over the thousand swords, let them steam the sand. Come and see if you can stay awake for a night. Not yet having time to search for the Three Blooms. With two hands, I want to cover the bottle's birds. Four long ropes fear the well's snake. Painting the portrait to recognize the master. Ask the good-hearted people of Fangling. The people of the county still call him the Three Blooms. Some say his name is Li. He lives in the Fukexi Rock. Whenever he wears paper blooms, people gather around and call out to him, but he only smiles and says, 'Don't hit me, don't hit me.' There are also two or three old men who often accompany him. Sometimes they invite him into the mountains and say, 'Go into the city to drink wine, we will come soon.' When the old men return to the city, Li is already there. When they go together to the wine shop, the money runs out, but the mood is still high. Li reaches into a small bamboo tube at his waist and takes out money to buy more wine. He drinks until he is drunk. The three old men secretly watch his tube, which is empty, but when Li takes it out again, it is full. This happens many times. Suddenly, he says goodbye to everyone and disappears. His statue still exists in the county. The people of the county worship him. His poem says: Wearing three blooms, forever young. Who understands the mystery of the single path? In drunkenness, he naturally captures the spirit and brings it to life. Eventually, he will never age, seeing the universe. The last line sometimes says, 'Unconsciously, it's evening.' For he always writes his portrait when he is drunk. Even after the chaos of war, common people still keep them. In the early years of the Xuanhe era, the notorious bandit leader Zhang Qi in the Jianghuai region also claimed the name 'Three Blooms' to deceive the public. ```

Jiao Old Man's Tomb Field Thirty li west of the western gate of Fangzhou lies a stone cliff of great height and steepness, with a stone chamber below it. A Daoist temple called the Nine Chambers Palace stands beside it. The local people have passed down that Chen Xiying, who lived on Mount Hua, also often stayed in this place. The stone chamber was a sleeping room. An old man named Jiao lived at the foot of the mountain, and Chen visited him every day. When Chen arrived, two birds would fly in the air and then descend, and the Jiao family would wait for them in this way. They would welcome him by emptying their home of all possessions, preparing tea and fruit to entertain him, and this has been the regular practice for many years. One day, Chen announced that he was leaving. Jiao asked, "Where are you going, Master?" Chen replied, "I am returning to the Three Peaks." Jiao's father and son tried to persuade him to stay, but Chen refused. Jiao then asked, "What does your family desire? Do you want to be officials or rich?" Jiao replied, "We are people living in remote, backward areas; we do not wish to serve in government. But if we could get a thousand cattle, our wishes would be fulfilled." Chen laughed and said, "It is an easy matter." He then left with them and pointed to a cave on a hill, saying, "In the future, if you bury your ancestors here, it will be as you wish." He then left. When Old Man Jiao died, his son carried his coffin to the grave site indicated by Chen. Within several years, the family's wealth became abundant, and they indeed had a thousand cattle. Up until now, two hundred years later, the descendants still maintain their old occupation, although the number of cattle has decreased, they are still powerful and influential in their village. The villagers named the place the Jiao Old Man's Tomb Field.

**Jiao Lao's Burial Field** Thirty li west of Fangzhou there is a very high and steep cliff, beneath which lies a stone chamber. A Daoist temple, called the Nine-Chambered Palace, stands nearby. The local people say that when Chen Xiyan was residing on Mount Hua, he often stayed in this place; the stone chamber was his sleeping quarters. A man named Jiao Lao lived at the foot of the mountain. Chen visited him every day, and whenever he arrived, two crows would fly from the sky, dance, and descend. Jiao Lao would always anticipate this sign and spend all his wealth to welcome him, preparing tea and fruits to entertain him for an entire year. This always happened until one day Chen announced that he was leaving. Jiao Lao asked, "Master, where are you going?" Chen replied, "I wish to return to the Three Peaks." Jiao Lao and his son tried to persuade him to stay, but he would not be swayed. Then they asked, "What does your family desire? Do you want official rank or wealth?" Jiao Lao replied, "We are poor mountain dwellers who do not wish to serve as officials. If we can obtain a thousand oxen, our wishes will be fulfilled." Chen smiled and said, "That is an easy matter." He then went with him to a mountain behind and pointed to a cave, saying, "In the future, when you are buried here, your wishes will be fulfilled." Then he departed. When Jiao Lao died, his son carried his coffin and buried him in the location Chen had pointed out. Within a few years, his family's wealth and property flourished, and the number of oxen indeed reached a thousand. To this day, over two hundred years later, his descendants still maintain the old family business. Although the number of oxen has decreased from the original count, they remain prosperous and respected in their village. The locals have named the place "Jiao Lao's Burial Field."

A Second Record of Mistakes Made by Doctor Xu - According to the records, Doctor Xu made two mistakes: in the reception room of the government office, he encountered a censor, and in Wuzhou, he referred to an elderly judge as "vigorous" and often used this as a joke. While reading Wang Yanfu's "Dust History," he came across two errors at the end. The first one says: In a case involving a censor in western Beijing, he traveled to a county and insulted the county magistrate, Zhang Bohao, ordering him to walk instead of ride, while walking and counting his actions. After entering the inn, there was a low-ranking official named Yu Hou, a sly and unscrupulous person, who reported before saying: "The provincial censor just scolded the official, who is the son-in-law of Chancellor Wang Tao." The provincial censor nodded and said, "Why didn't you tell me earlier?" He immediately summoned the magistrate and sat with him after tea, then said, "I heard you are talented; I just came to discourage you a bit." His words and expression remained unchanged, and he asked, "Is it easy to predict the future?" He immediately ordered a scribe to send a recommendation letter.

One of the stories is as follows: An inspector from a certain road arrived at a prefecture to inspect the troops and encountered a military commander who was advanced in age. He said to the local official and his deputy, "This old military commander cannot handle his duties anymore. How can he be tolerated?" The official and deputy remained silent. The military commander spoke loudly, "I originally did not want to come here, but I was forced by the young generation. Now I am indeed being humiliated." The inspector asked, "Who are the young ones?" The military commander replied, "My nephew." The inspector then asked, "Who is your nephew?" The military commander answered, "Zhang Dexiang." It was said that at that time, he was just the prime minister. The inspector then said, "What a healthy old man! I will offer you some wine and leave." These two incidents are entirely similar to those of Doctor Xu. It is indeed known that overseers and superiors are light and frivolous in their treatment of local officials, speaking in a mocking manner. Such cases have existed since ancient times. Therefore, I have fully recorded their words to provide conversation topics for those who are interested. --- A Woman from the Jizhou Scholar Liu Boshan's Younger Sister The younger sister of the scholar Liu Boshan from Jizhou was about to marry the day before. Her family held a banquet to say goodbye. The sky was clear and vast, but suddenly it poured with rain, and the thunder rumbled. She felt something touch her clothes and was startled, falling to the seat. A short while later, she woke up immediately. Her entire body and clothes were unharmed, except that her left foot lost its shoe. Everyone searched for it and found it outside the door. The sole of the shoe was already torn, and a piece of paper was pulled out, about three inches wide, with the character "天" (heaven) filling a line. It is said that this girl used paper for children to learn writing as a backing. The thunder god took it because of her carelessness and showed it to express respect and warning. The girl fell ill because of this and missed the wedding date. Another month later, the ceremony was finally completed.

Li Shanglao of Mount Lu encountered soil during construction, and as a result, the entire family, regardless of age or gender, fell ill with swelling. Despite seeking medical treatment, the illness did not improve. He then cleaned the rooms and instructed his family to fast, burn incense, and recite the Chishengguang Zou to ward off the offending cause. Within less than seven days, Li Shanglao had a dream in which he saw a white-clad old man riding a cow in his courtyard. Suddenly, the old man sank into the ground. The next day, all the sick people recovered. It was then known that this old man was the one causing the mischief, and it was suspected to be a minor deity of the earth. --- ### Translation of the Second Passage **Source:** 夷坚志乙卷四 钦定四库全书 夷坚志乙卷五 宋 洪迈 撰 张小娘子 秀川外科张生本郡中虞其妻遇神人自称皮场大王授以痈疽异方一册且诲以手法大概遂用医著名俗呼为张小娘子又转以教厥夫吴人张县丞祖母章子厚侍妾也年七十疽发于背邀治之张先溃其病而以盏贮所泄脓澄滓而视之其凝处红如丹砂出谓丞曰此服丹药毒发所致势难疗也丞怒曰老人平生尚不吃一服暖药况于丹乎何妄言如是母在房闻之亟 **Translation:** *Yijian Zhi Yi Bian Volume Four* *Imperially Approved Four Treasuries Collection* *Yijian Zhi Yi Bian Volume Five* *Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty* *Madam Zhang, the Surgeon* Zhang Sheng, an external medicine practitioner from Xiu Chuan, was originally a military officer in his county. His wife met a divine being who introduced himself as the King of the Skin Field and bestowed upon her a special formula for treating boils and abscesses, along with instructions on the techniques. She then became famous for her medical skills and was commonly known as Madam Zhang. She also taught these methods to her husband, Zhang Xiancheng, a county magistrate from Wu. Zhang's grandmother, Chang Zihou, was a concubine aged seventy who had a boil on her back. She invited Zhang to treat her. Zhang first lanced the boil and collected the pus in a cup, then filtered it to examine it. The solidified part was red like cinnabar. He told the magistrate, "This is due to a reaction from taking dan (a type of elixir), and it is difficult to cure." The magistrate was angry and said, "My grandmother never took even a single dose of warm medicine, let alone dan. How can you speak such nonsense?" The grandmother, hearing this in her room, rushed out in a hurry.

He called out, saying it was correct. When I was young, living in your house, every time the master took the *Fuhuo Dan* (伏火丹), he would make us accompany him and take a pill. Over time, the accumulation became large, thus storing up poison roots, and now it cannot be regretted. Zhang thanked and left, and Lady Zhang soon died because of this. Liu Xia's concubine, Xiang, developed a very ugly sore. The village doctor burned mugwort, and soon the flesh swelled up like a roll, somewhat resembling a flower bud. Sometimes it was mistakenly touched, causing intense pain throughout the body. Zhang said, "This is called a 'flipping flower brain hemorrhoid.' Very few people in the world suffer from it. My medical books do not record a treatment method, so I left it and went away." The village doctor then applied medicinal thread and bandaged it for about half a day. The roll fell off with the thread, but it reformed in the hand, and it recurred several times until it did not heal. After several weeks, the concubine eventually died. **Zhang Huaxiang** During the transition between the Jianyan and Shaoxing periods, there were many bandits in the rivers and lakes. Zhang Huaxiang and Qi Fang were particularly fierce and cruel. Zhang broke into Chizhou, stationed his army at the school, and took countless women. They were chased away by the soldiers, but unable to abandon them, he selected those who could not walk and found eight hundred people. He told his followers, "Each of you will contribute a pair of feet." In a moment, the eight hundred women's feet were piled up in the courtyard. Then he left, but those who had their feet cut off did not die immediately; they called out in pain for days before dying. Qi, in Xuancheng and Guangde, killed all the officials without leaving any. Zhang, the King of Xun, fought them fiercely; the two bandits could not withstand the strength and were captured. The King of Xun blamed them for their crimes. Qi said, "I am the leader of these people."

He called out, "This is indeed correct!" When I was young and living in your household, whenever the magistrate took the *fu huo dan* (a fire-subduing pill), he would have us accompany him and take one grain each. Over time, having taken many of these, we accumulated toxic roots within us. Now, it is too late to regret it. Zhang thanked and left, and Zhang’s mother soon died from this. Liu Xiasheng’s concubine, Xiang, developed a very foul sore. A village physician burned mugwort on it, and soon the flesh swelled up like a scroll, then rolled into a shape resembling a flower bud. If touched, it caused unbearable pain throughout the body. Zhang said, "This is called *fan hua nao zhi* (flower-turning brain abscess). Very few people in the world suffer from it, and my medical texts do not record a method of treatment. I shall leave it be." The village physician then applied medicine, threaded a line through it, and left it for about half a day. The growth then fell off with the line, but soon reattached itself, repeating this process four or five times. In the end, it did not heal. After several weeks, the concubine died. Zhang Hua Xiang During the transition between the Jianyan and Shaoxing periods, the rivers and lakes were filled with bandits. Among them, Zhang Hua Xiang and Qi Fang were particularly cruel and violent. Zhang attacked Chizhou, stationed his army at a school, and seized countless women. When pursued by government soldiers, he could not bear to abandon them, so he sorted out those who could not walk and selected eight hundred women. He instructed his followers, "Each of you must offer a pair of feet." In an instant, the feet of the eight hundred women were stacked in the courtyard, and then he left. Those who had their feet cut off but did not die immediately cried out in agony for days before dying. Qi Fang, in Xuancheng and Guangde, slaughtered all the officials without leaving a single one alive. Zhang Xunwang fought him fiercely, but the two bandits could not withstand the resistance and were finally captured. Zhang Xunwang reproached him for his crimes. Qi Fang said, "I am the leader of these people."

### Final Translation Zhang, the military commander, is currently a deputy general under his command. The prince ignored him and then asked Huaxiang. Huaxiang smiled and replied, "Fate has made it so. Now I should present my humiliation to the nobleman." The prince was angry at his refusal to yield, shouted, beheaded him, the head was cut off, and the body did not fall. Qi escaped punishment, seized power, and became a military governor. In his old age, suffering from illness and in dire straits, he only responded to Huaxiang. What Huaxiang said was mainly that he resented him for betraying himself. Qi said, "Since then, the military commander once made a mistake, leading to his death, what does it matter now?" In the midst of chaos, he did not rise up, this event was recorded by Zhang Jizhong in the commentary of Jing Pei. --- Con Sixi To the east of Xiu Prefecture, about thirty to forty miles away, there is a settlement called Jiangjing. Beside it is a Great Holy Temple, where the temple usually entrusts a monk to manage the lamp oil for the Buddha hall, giving him a certain amount each month. After a long time, the monk would often fail to deliver the oil on time, and it would run out. The head monk blamed him for misappropriating the funds and subjected him to repeated beatings, which he found extremely painful. It was not that he had initially been given no deadline, but he had no way to prove his innocence. He could only watch carefully at night. One night, he heard the door open and rushed in to see a tall man removing his golden armor and applying oil to his body. He was startled and asked what was going on. The man replied, "Do not blame me. I am the guardian deity of the nearby village, known as the Seven-Killings Spirit. This old man, relying on his wealth and lacking in righteousness, has built extensive buildings and dug wells, causing me constant disturbance. He has offended me many times, and I have been injured all over my body. I need the oil to heal my wounds."

The pain was indescribable, yet I knew you were unjustly punished. Now that you have fortunately recovered, I will no longer come here, but I will secretly protect you, enabling you to frequently serve and provide for others as repayment. The traveler thanked him, saying, "Old Gou has already offended the clear gods, why is no calamity or retribution added?" He replied, "He is still enjoying stubborn good fortune and cannot be questioned." Then he disappeared and was not seen again. As a result, there was no trouble with losing oil. Old Gou was rough and fierce, a bully in the village, and he disciplined his children very strictly. He called them and said, "I have heard that after people die, their sacrifices often fail to reach them. Why not take advantage of my not yet closed eyes to perform the funeral rites? You all should dress in mourning clothes according to the proper rites until the spirit table is set up, and people cry and worship in the morning and evening, offering sacrifices for a hundred days and then stop." His son, unable to bear the foreboding of the funeral, wept and advised, but he scolded and was angry, not listening, and finally, he followed his instructions. It was another ten years before he died. The Bee King of Nanling In the old days, there was a Bee King Temple in Nanling County, Anhui Province, but no one knows its origin. The shamans used it to rally the crowd, claiming it was extremely powerful. The locals revered it very much. After the temple was built, they also decorated the shrine hall and kept the bee inside. During festive seasons, they would always bring it out for play. In the early years of the Song Dynasty, Qian Dang of Lin'an served as the county magistrate. He had not been in office long when there was a drought. The officials and people requested him to pray to the god. He said, "This god can be relied upon," and then prepared the ceremonial procession and led it into the county seat. As soon as he ascended the platform, Qian burned incense and showed respect. Looking inside, there was only one bee as large as a fist, flying and moving freely. Qian was accustomed to practicing the True Way of Heaven's Heart and knew it was a strange and false thing. He then loudly said to it, "..."

A Stupid Little Insect You, a stupid little insect, should find your proper place. How can you rely on evil spirits to prey on others? I now make an agreement with you: what happens today cannot have two outcomes. If you are truly a spirit, you should immediately sting me, even if I die, I will not fear. If not, I will burn you to ashes to cleanse the foolish customs. After the words were spoken, the bee as if did not hear. The money was already hidden in dry twigs, and the order was accumulated in the courtyard. Close the shrine doors and carry out and add more. The bee inside was roaring and colliding, the sound was mournful and resentful. Soon, the ashes were completely gone. Then he burned down the temple. The townspeople from then on dared not speak again. Dang, the daughter of Yuanzhi, married Fang Zhang's son, Pei's brother-in-law, and talked about the matter. Yang Jian in His Prime When Yang Jian was in his prime, he once went to Zhengzhou to visit the tomb, taking his family westward. The concubines he left in the capital numbered in the dozens. The middle and main gates were all locked, and food was passed through a wheel mechanism in the wall. The guards were very strict. There was a guest staying in an outer room. One of the concubines admired his appearance, set up a ladder to climb over the roof and enter, and indulged in intimacy. She sent her away just before dawn, and the next night, she repeated the previous plan. Her fellow concubines gradually heard about it and began to take turns inviting him in until several days and nights. They bribed the school servant to keep silent. The guest, exhausted and worn out, reported that Yang Jian was about to return. He hurriedly climbed up the roof but was too weak to come back down. When Yang Jian returned home and saw him, he was surprised that he was in an unusual place, thinking he might be possessed by a spirit. He had him carried down and called a Taoist priest to exorcise him. He falsely claimed that he had been bewitched by a ghost and was unaware of it. After ten days, he recovered well. Yang Jian, knowing of his deceit, held a banquet to celebrate and comforted him greatly. The guest thought that the matter was kept secret and would not be revealed. One day, he invited him to eat together and then sent him to a private room. Then there were several strong men.

A physician tied the patient to the bed and cut open the abdomen to remove the kidneys. The patient fainted from the extreme pain. The physician ordered the usual method to administer medicine. These methods are usually used in castration. After more than ten days, the patient could only sit up. He was given soup and water to wash his face. But he noticed falling hair in the basin, which increased day by day. Eventually, he became a eunuch in appearance. From then on, the owner treated him more kindly. He often invited him into the bedroom to dine with his wife and daughters. Because he knew there was no need to be cautious and considered him a toy. The guest was always on good terms with Fang Wude. Every time he took a holiday, he would go out to visit him. At that time, half a year had passed without any trace. Everyone believed he was dead. Once, while touring the Xiangguo Temple, he met him at the Dabai Hall. Seeing his appearance and expression, he suspected he was a ghost. The guest called out, "Fang Wude, why have you suddenly become so strange?" and bowed to him. The guest held his hand and wept, telling him about the changes and deeply regretting his actions. He said, "How can I face my friends again? I am clinging to my remaining life and unwilling to die." After that, he disappeared and was never heard from again.

True Person Tan When Daoist Master Zhao Zujian first began practicing the Tianxin method in Hengzhou, he worked with villagers to suppress evil spirits. After the spirits were initially subdued, they reappeared, and Zhao could no longer bear his anger. He captured the possessed and questioned them. They replied, "We did not dare to come without permission; it is the divine general of the court who received a bribe from someone, and thus we dared to act. Now we are gone." Zhao silently reflected, "The reason I uphold the true law and subdue demons is because I rely on the divine generals. Now this is public corruption, relying on me, what can I depend on?" He intended to accuse the divine general of its crime and report to Dongyue. That night, he dreamed of an armored warrior with a very fierce appearance, who stood with hands clasped in front. The warrior said, "I am the divine general under the master's command. In life, I was a soldier with great strength, and the crowd called me Chen Tiebian. After death, I became a god and truly served the altar. Unable to be cautious, I led to accepting bribes from ghosts. Hearing that the master intends to report to Daiyue, I will fall into the North Feng (a hell) and never escape. I hope for your mercy and forgiveness, allowing me to cleanse my heart and renew myself." Zhao said, "I do not wish to speak of your crimes, only saying that I do not want to practice this method, so you may return." The divine general bowed and thanked him before departing. Zhao finally submitted a document to abandon the method, discussed changing to the Wu Lei (Five Thunder) technique, but had no teacher and only burned incense.

Before the statue of Tán Zhēnrén, Sào offered incense, hoping for enlightenment. After several years, he was summoned by someone for an examination. When the boy was asked to watch over him, Sào suddenly leaped up, his hair disheveled, his feet bare, and holding a sword, he spoke loudly, "I am Tán Zhēnrén. I pity your diligence and will teach you the method. Have you gained anything from it?" Sào replied, "I have only received four talismans, which are said to be passed down from Zhenwu." Tán Zhēnrén said, "There should be seventy-two talismans of the Five Thunder. This is only one eighteenth of the total. How can you control evil spirits with so few? You should take a hundred sheets of paper and place them on a table for me to teach you. I will insert my sword into the ground and say, 'Look up, officials of the Five Thunder, quickly transmit the seventy-two talismans, all to be completed now.' At first, there was no visible action, but after a meal's time, he said, 'The talismans are complete.' He commanded Zhao to retrieve them. Zhao revealed the paper, which contained sixty-eight talismans, each drawn on a separate sheet. The characters were clearly visible and not of this world. Zhao was surprised and reverently received them, bowing twice before standing up. The boy also awakened, and from that point on, the techniques became effective and the boy became spiritually adept. The record in the *Gui Zhi* about a ruler who managed the Heng Temple is this Tán Zhēnrén. Zhao kept the matter secret and did not tell outsiders, but his disciple, Yu Chūn Pǔ Dào Rén, fully described the events. --- Fu Xuan studied the method. When Fu Xuan was the Deputy Governor of Jiangxi, he invited Wang Shì Chén from Líncuān to Yùzhāng to study thunder techniques. Wang disliked the person but, due to his fearsome reputation, could not refuse to teach him, though he only taught a general overview. Among his peers, Fu Xuan was considered highly skilled. When Fu Xuan's skills were perfected, he decided to test them by affecting a tall tower at a Buddhist monastery. He burned a talisman, and soon fire rose from the center, reducing the tower to ashes, yet the tower remained undamaged. Fu Xuan then became angry that Wang had not taught him everything and planned to hire an assassin to harm Wang. Wang had already foreseen this and was angry, saying, "How can a disciple plot against his master, like Yi shooting the suns? It would be inappropriate to let this spread and succeed." He then used a magical method to summon all his spiritual officials and officers. From that point on, Fu Xuan's methods no longer worked as effectively.

**Translation:** **The Wife of Zhao Bu Yi** Zhao Bu Yi served as the military secretary in Jiangyin. His wife contracted a strange illness; she did not eat cooked food or fire and only consumed raw meat. Her clothing and daily routines were the same as usual, but she slept and lived in a separate room from her husband. After his term ended, Zhao was transferred to serve as the supervisor of Guiyang, and his wife's illness worsened. One maid served her, and she became emaciated, short of breath, and her face turned as pale as wax. She died within half a year. When another maid was sent to serve her, she too died in a similar manner. In total, three people died in her presence. Whenever an old soldier brought meat in through the guard gate, she would become angry and say, "How could it pass through the gate of the drum and horn tower and be discarded without being accepted?" If the meat came from the back garden, she would accept it. After this, Zhao, awaiting his appointment as the supervisor of Fenzhou, lived in Changning, Hengzhou, until he took office. One day, his wife transformed into a tiger and roared out during the day. Qian Yuzhi, who was Zhao's successor in Jiangyin, heard the details of the matter. When Zhao served as the supervisor of Guiyang, the officials there could still speak of it. The judge Hou Xiaoyou, who also lived in Changning, said that Zhao's wife was a daughter of a eunuch family, but her origins were unknown. She was beautiful and had never been pregnant. She was very clean and, whenever her husband entered her room and sat on a chair, she would order it to be cleaned immediately after he left. All three maids who died did so because she sucked their blood at night, causing them to slowly die.

Donghu Lake Lotus and Water Lilies In the Donghu Lake of Poyang, lotus roots, water lilies, and water caltrops were very abundant, and the Jiansu Temple relied on them to support its monks. During the Chunxi period, they suddenly withered as if sick, and only one-third of the flowers and fruits were produced. Jing Pei's younger brother traveled to Fuzhou and, while cooling off under a willow tree with the monk Facong, asked about the reason. The monk said, "It was burned by a village witch, that is why it is like this." Jing Pei found the words strange and unbelievable, and spoke with disdain and criticized, "Earlier, in a certain village, a witch had completed a ritual, and her disciples who followed her wanted to see it. They placed a live fish to be sacrificed to the deity they served. The fish suddenly struggled and died." The witch showed her anger and said, "There is a dead tree on the north side of the stream, possessed by a spirit. I haven't had time to exorcise it because I have done enough good deeds and don't care. The true law is here, so this disrespect cannot go unpunished. I immediately wrote a spell, threw it, and soon saw the tree on fire from top to bottom, completely burned. The fish came back to life, and the disciples wanted to go to another place. Thus, the people in the temple only saw a slight smoke rising and swirling over the lake, which soon disappeared. The next morning, they saw that the lotus and water lilies were all withered and could not be saved. The disaster was severe. The gods and spirits above should have seen this, but they did not punish it. Why is that?"

### Translation of the Source Text **Zigu's Poem on Her Hands** In the homes of Jizhou, Zigu was invited to compose poetry. Just then, a beautiful woman was beside the winnowing basket, so they asked her to write a poem about her hands. She wrote: Laughing while picking cherries, unable to bear the effort, At times climbing willows and playing in the spring shade. Slow, melodic singing in a bamboo flute tune, Deeply bowing before the starry sky and moonlit tower. Stealing a glance at the embroidered curtains and the double sleeves of the dancer, The green-clad one leisurely adjusts the small eyebrows. How many times has she picked up her silk socks in autumn, To recall longing, she sets aside her needle. Writing with confidence, without much thought, Quite elegant. **The Chess Monk of Xiuzhou** In the Doushui Temple of Xiuzhou, there was a monk named Shiyu who could practice medicine and greatly loved chess. His chess skills were of very low rank, but he spent day and night without tiring. In the ninth year of Qinding, he contracted an epidemic illness, died, and then revived. He said he was summoned to the government office and stood in the courtyard. There was a large well in front of him. The king said, "Mistakenly summoned you. You are a monk. Have you often recited the scriptures of merit and virtue?" He replied, "Naturally foolish, I did not know about this scripture." The king said, "It is the Diamond Sutra." He replied, "Then I can recite it." The master looked to the right, took it from the well, drew it up, and gave it to him. He recited several sections. The master and all the officials in the office bowed deeply in reverence. Afterward, he ordered an official to send him back. The official who passed by the corridor told me, "This also has a place worth viewing. Should follow along." They then arrived at a room. On the table was a chessboard. Two boxes contained black and white pieces, extremely different in size. The official asked if the monk could do this. I replied that I greatly love it, because it is too low, it is a burden. The official said he would make a plan for me. "But if you swallow one piece, your skill will improve." I wanted to take the large white piece, but the official said no. He took a small black piece and made me swallow it. Immediately, I became shocked and woke up. The next day, the illness was cured. The usual chess players came to see me, asked for the chessboard to compare skills, and indeed increased several ranks.

### Translation **Huang Chao Temple** In Liuzhou's Yizhang County, there is a valley called Huangsha Dong, with mountains that are rugged and winding for over a hundred miles, forming eighteen valleys known as the strong and fierce abodes of rebels. Near the exit of the valley, the land is slightly flat, and on the mountain, there stands a temple dedicated to Huang Chao, the famous rebel leader. It is unknown when and by whom this temple was built. In front of the temple, there is a large pine tree that embraces the mountain. People living at the foot of the mountain often hear the sound of commands and acknowledgments inside the temple, as if hundreds of people are receiving orders and responding respectfully. Whenever this happens, the villagers in the valley inevitably rise up in rebellion. During the Chunxi period, Wang Xuanzi, the Minister of Works and Governor of Hunan, paid close attention to suppressing bandits. When there was a rebellion, he ordered Commander-in-Chief Yang Qin to lead troops to suppress it. They launched flaming arrows to burn the temple and cut down the tree. As the tree was about to fall, a large black snake with hair on its head leapt out in an aggressive posture, as if it were attacking. The soldiers surrounded it and shot it with bows and arrows, killing it. They then cleared and fortified the area, establishing a camp to station garrison soldiers. The sound of drums and gongs resounded day and night, and from then on, the region became peaceful. General Zhang, who participated in this campaign, described these strange events in detail. **The Best Time of the Year** Scholars in Wu often planted oranges and tangerines in their gardens. They liked to quote Su Dongpo's poem, "Remember, it is a good thing to do once a year. It is the time when oranges are yellow and tangerines are green." They called this period "the best time of the year." Only Chen Yancun, who built a garden in front of his residence in Weitang, uniquely named it "the best time of the year," which was quite unique in spring. Chen Yancun, who was a censor in the central government, was granted a position as the deputy transport commissioner of Jiangdong. He died exactly one year after taking office, as if it were fate. Han Yu's poem says, "The small rain on the imperial street is soft like butter, the grass looks green from a distance but disappears up close. The best time of the year is spring, far surpassing the willows and smoke-filled capital." Thus, the term "good thing" cannot be exclusively attributed to oranges and tangerines.

*Yijian Zhi, Volume Five of Section B* *Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries* *Yijian Zhi, Volume Six of Section B* *Song Dynasty, Hong Mai, compiled* *Three Unusual Births in Jiankang* A doctor from Jiankang named Yang Youcheng reported three cases he had witnessed, all involving unusual births among women. The wife of the owner of a guesthouse in Tonglin Bay was twenty-nine years old. In the eighth month of the third year of the Shaoxi era (1192 AD), she gave birth to several large snakes on the grass. Both the midwife and her husband were so frightened that they fled. The snakes immediately ran to the Qinhuai River, where they remained for the night before returning to her home to nurse from her breast. They left only at dawn. For seven days, the residents of the inn moved to other accommodations, and the snakes eventually disappeared. The wife of a salt merchant named Liu Yi was about to give birth but could not deliver. She was extremely weak and close to death. Yang Youcheng examined her pulse and said, "There must be something unusual in her womb. We should save her mother." He gave her medicine, which she drank until midnight. She then gave birth to a child with a very large head. The child's hair was growing and about five inches long. It also had two prominent horns and over ten white, serrated teeth. Her family killed the child and threw it into the river. Another woman, the wife of a man named Zhang Da near the Doudmen Bridge, gave birth to a chicken. Her husband held a knife and began to cut it up, but before he finished, she complained of abdominal pain again and gave birth to a monkey. They also killed the monkey, wrapped it in cloth, sewed it shut, and threw it into the deep waters with a large stone. All three mothers survived, but the cause of these unusual births remains unexplained.

Rob Gu had a brain tumor. The *Spring Churn Chronicles* records that Rob Gu developed a tumor on his nose, which grew larger each day. He met a Taoist priest in Xiangyang, who gave him medicine the size of a grain of millet. That night, he used a needle to pierce a small hole and placed the medicine there. Soon after, he felt the medicine rotating inside, as if wandering around. By dawn, the tumor had disappeared. In my hometown, Rob Gu was a scholar when a tumor grew on the back of his head. After several months, it grew as large as half a winnowing basket, making it impossible to comb his hair. He heard that there was a highly skilled surgeon in Wuyuan and sent a servant to invite him to his home. The surgeon applied medicine and tied the tumor with a thread. He cut it off and rested in an outer room. After more than two hours, the pain became so severe that he bit and gnawed at his sleeves, unable to bear it. He called his son to remove the thread and said, "Rather than suffer daily, this pain almost penetrates the marrow." His son was about to follow the instructions but found no more space to cut the thread and could not proceed. In the winter month, he fell asleep on a heated mat and woke up to find the pillow beside him as wet as water, with a piece of skin nearby. When he touched the tumor, it was gone. His son lit a candle to examine it, and there were no scars on the outside of his head. It was attached and formed a growth, but initially it was not related to anything.

阎义方家雷 A Thunderstorm at the Home of Yan Yifang Yan Yifang, a native of Bianzhou, followed his father to Poyang for official duties and bought a house to live there. After Yan Yifang's death, his son, Fu, practiced the Five Thunder Techniques but was frivolous and lacked sincerity and reverence. He first married a woman from the Yanling Yu family but divorced her after several years and kept concubines. Every few months, if there was no pregnancy, he would dismiss her. He did this with several concubines over time. At night, he indulged in wine and women and no longer cared about the ritual texts and talismans. During the middle period of the Song Dynasty, in the fifth year of the sixth month, a heavy rainstorm with thick fog enveloped his home. Fu's daughter, who was twelve or thirteen years old, saw a tall old man dressed in white enter the courtyard, rolling a large fireball. The girl was terrified and trembled, almost fainting. The old man took her back to her room. A thunderclap struck the house, damaging a pillar and a beam, leaving deep cracks. The small statues of the Four Saints that Fu had painted and set up earlier were shattered. The horses in the stable, which had missing teeth, were also affected. When the lightning struck the pillar, it damaged the statues' eyes. Fu then ordered the painter to redo the statues as seated figures and covered the old items with a stone carving of ink bamboo by Su Dongpo. On that day, everything was torn apart without a trace, and one sedan chair was shattered like a powder chair.

All items in the category of tables were affected; a lamp stand with two arms, one long and one short, had the short arm pulled while the long arm remained stationary. A kitchen maid suffered facial burns from a fire, and a neighbor's maid, Guo, was also burned by the remaining flames. Ash and dust accumulated on the ground, about an inch thick, as if the place had been abandoned for years. Nearby houses began to shake, and Fu was worried that the punishment had not yet ended. He invited a Taoist priest to perform a ritual at the Wang Shrine to appease the god Wang. The priest, who usually practiced purity, said, "The gods in this ritual are powerful and fierce. Even I, who am a member of the feather stream, am always cautious. Is it appropriate for someone like you, who is not a Taoist, to be so concerned? Any documents related to this ritual should be handed over to me; otherwise, you will face great misfortune." Fu, out of fear, agreed and vowed not to perform the ritual again. Initially, Fu's older sister, whom their mother loved, married into a family in LePing and became a son-in-law. That autumn, she died of illness. On her deathbed, she told her mother, "This house has been cursed for a long time and will surely bring misfortune. We should move quickly to avoid it." The mother did not have the time to act on her advice, and soon after, a lightning disaster occurred.

During the Yuanfeng period, there was a great flood in Jinling, causing ravines and gorges to overflow suddenly. A tree, about the length of a standard unit of measurement, floated out of Mao Jun Mountain, entangled with vines and lichens and covered with green moss. Passersby did not notice it as unusual. A fisherman encountered it at a winding shore and found it to have a pleasant, fragrant smell. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was a person. He quickly ran to inform Liu Hunkang, the head of the Moshan observatory. Liu led his disciples to verify it and found it to be true. They moved it to the side of a stream, cleared the vines and moss, and bathed it. His ragged clothes were worn out, like a fishing net; his purple hair and jade-like complexion, round pupils, bright and clear, suddenly rose up. He looked at the crowd and said, "Drink water to me." After drinking, he asked about the current year, and they replied with the current year. He sighed and said, "It has been twenty years." In the past year, I was a guest in Zhenzhou and met two monks who told me, "Your appearance is clear and upright, with an interest in the outside world. You can learn the Dao, but the bustling capital is not a place to live. You should travel to Moshan Mountain to cultivate yourself at Lianzhen Pavilion." I said I had no funds to travel, and they said, "Just follow us." So we traveled together and stayed in a cave, where they taught me the technique of inner vision and inner hearing. Before parting, they taught me a secret technique and warned me, "Blend your spirit and quiet contemplation, do not disturb, do not."

Fearing that one day you will see me again, I am confined here, unaware of the changes of day and night, cold and warmth, wind, thunder, frost, and snow. Unaware of the burdens of human life, including the ability to choose and the emotional ups and downs (joy and sorrow), I am free and unrestrained in vast, open spaces, like lying in a large room without feeling anything. Now awake and interacting with the mundane world, how can one see my teacher now? Daoist practitioners are surprised and invite me to a meditation room, where I offer incense and fire daily, wash and be cautious. The news gradually spreads from the local level to the county. The military and local authorities in Nanjing respectfully receive me and provide accommodation. High-ranking officials and scholars come daily to pay respects and seek advice on health and longevity. The news spreads to other regions, and those who hear about me feel regret for not being able to meet me. This person originally had no enlightenment or insight. After a year, their arrogance and indulgence increase daily. Occasionally accepting fruits or food that are given, they would be pleased but not refuse simple food. Clothing, bedding, money, and silk are accepted without hesitation. Indulging in luxurious clothing, rich food, and strong alcohol, unrestrained in desires without discrimination or consideration, after a short time, they die without any significant changes or symptoms. Shangguan Yanheng writes a biography of the event, expressing regret over the person's abandonment of years of cultivation, departure from the teachings of their teacher, and loss of their original intentions in a short period of time.

**Translation:** **The Venerable One Revealed in a Dream** In the eleventh month of the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1144 CE), a monk named Shiman from Longxing Temple in Guangce County, Shaowu, had a dream. In the dream, he saw an old monk sitting cross-legged, holding a staff, and wearing patched robes. The old monk said he had come from Nanyue (Mount Heng) and was about to go to the Shangguan family for a ritual offering. The next day, Shiman heard that a wealthy man named Fujun had prepared offerings for sixteen arhats. Soon after, a wandering monk arrived and entered Shiman’s room. The monk said he was traveling far and lacked funds, and wanted to sell his collection of Wu Dongqing’s paintings of arhats and the central figure stele to someone. Shiman examined the paintings and steles, and when he reached the thirteenth arhat, he recognized it as the same person who had appeared in his dream. Shiman was greatly amazed and exclaimed, "The Buddhas are compassionate and have sent this to strengthen my faith." He then sold all his belongings to purchase the paintings and steles, which he placed on the two walls of the Ying Hall in Dingguang Garden. He wished to add some embellishments, such as offering bowls, towels, and alms bowls, but lacked the means. Therefore, he explained the dream to the donors, and the first to respond was the local scholar Shangguan Zhi. Shangguan Zhi also had a dream about the same arhat and said to Shiman, "You are poor and have no funds; I will perform a Buddhist ritual for you. You should diligently maintain the temple and its rituals." Shangguan Zhi then paid the price Shiman had initially paid for the paintings and steles, and added his own offerings, including statues of Huayan and Ruyi Bodhisattvas. He also renovated the hall. Li Duguangzu wrote this record.

The Wild Bridge of Yingzhou My late father was exiled to Yingzhou and lived there without official salary or grain, so he had to buy his daily food from the market. Some people in the county said that seventy miles east of the city was a place called Dongxiang with good land. So, he immediately prepared an empty vehicle and bought a hundred acres of land, sending his younger brother Jing Xu to inspect and manage it. Just as he was harvesting rice in winter, my late father suddenly fell ill and sent a servant to report to Jing Xu. When Jing Xu received the message, it was already dusk. He hurriedly rode back on horseback. Halfway, the horse suddenly curled up and shivered, as if it was afraid of something. The driver said there was a tiger nearby. At that moment, the moon was dimly lit, and from a distance of several hundred steps, a tiger was seen passing by with its ears lowered. The horse must have already seen it. Jing Xu was also frightened and thought of his father with great concern. He urged the horse forward with a strong whip. When they were about five miles from the city, they encountered a broken waterway with no boat to cross. They had to go up and down the cliff and found a horizontal log across the water, which they called a wild bridge. They stepped on it, but it was very slippery and they could not move their feet. They had to cross it and proceed. The place where they placed their hands was sticky and fishy, like candy. By the time they arrived home, the east had already become bright. The next day, when they passed through that place again, there was originally no bridge. It was because the water dragons sleep soundly in the morning and evening, so people tread on its back without realizing it. Some say it is due to sincere filial piety that they managed to cross and return safely, avoiding the danger of being attacked and devoured. The danger was like this.

**Translation:** **The Chanyu's Inquiry About Family Background** Su Shi sent his brother Su Zhe to serve as an envoy to the Khitan people. In the last line of his poem, he wrote: "If the Chanyu asks about your family background, do not say that you are the first person in the Central Court." This was a reference to the story of Li Kui from the Tang Dynasty. During the Song Dynasty, Cao Xun was sent as an envoy to the Jin Dynasty. Someone with a penchant for fun composed a short poem, the second half of which read: "If the Chanyu asks about your family background, tell him that it is the innermost part of a red window." This referred to the fact that Cao Xun's father, Yuan Chong, was once famous for this phrase. Later, Zhang Qiwu's son, Zhang Anshi, who served as a high-ranking official, changed the phrase to: "Tell him that you are a Han person in the Central Court." The people of the capital city referred to non-eunuch males as "Han people." Finally, Meng Sigong, who also served as an envoy to the north, changed the phrase to: "Tell him that you are the son of a wealthy salt merchant," referring to the fact that Meng Sigong's father was a wealthy salt merchant.

True Yang's Clever Prostitute In the customs of Jiangsu, Anhui, Fujian, and Zhejiang, there are various public taboos. For example, in Hangzhou, "Fú ér" is taboo; in Suzhou, "dāi zǐ" is taboo; and in Changzhou, "ōu diē" is taboo. Common people may sometimes violate these taboos, leading to conflicts. During the Xuanhe period, a prostitute from Zhenzhou welcomed the new governor in Yangzhou. The Yangzhou governor held a grand banquet with two rows of musicians and entertainers. In Yangzhou, the taboo is having a missing ear, while in Zhenzhou, the taboo is being referred to as a "match head." The Yangzhou prostitute, feeling superior due to her association with the governor's household, looked down on those from subordinate cities. She ordered a servant to light the fire, but the servant produced smoke and flames. She sent a young female servant to warn him, but he continued. She called him out and scolded, "With a high-ranking official present, how could you be so careless in burning ash and instead produce smoke and flames? Why don't you listen to me?" The prostitute laughed and said to the servant, "As the head of the prostitutes, I have commanded you several times. Why don't you listen? Do you have an earlobe or not?" The Yangzhou prostitute was greatly embarrassed. During the Qian Dao period, a teacher from Chuzhou was examined in Yangzhou. After the examination, he attended a banquet where prostitutes served wine. The teacher, a young and handsome man, called out a prostitute to sing beside him. He was angry that she did not meet his expectations and scolded her, "How could someone from the official register of entertainers be so rustic?" The prostitute replied calmly, "All around Chuzhou are mountains." The guest was startled and did not dare to speak a single word for the rest of the banquet.

**合生诗词** In the Jiang-Zhe region, there were wandering female entertainers known for their intelligence and knowledge of literature. They could improvise poetry on the spot, pointing to objects and composing verses immediately upon command. This style of performance was called "合生" (Héshēng). When their performances included humor and playful banter, it was referred to as "乔合生" (Qiáohéshēng), a remnant of the capital's tradition. At that time, Zhang Anguo was serving as the governor of Línchuān, while Wang Xuānzǐ, after resigning his post as governor of Lúlíng, was returning to his hometown in Fǔzhōu. Zhang Anguo hosted a banquet at his official residence, inviting local scholars and officials, including Chen Hànqīng, a senior official. As the entertainment began, a wandering female entertainer claimed to have learned how to compose poetry. Chen Hànqīng remarked, "The governor is called the 'Five Horses' (a metaphor for the governor). Today, with two governors seated together, it makes ten horses. You should capture this meaning in eight lines of poetry." The entertainer stood in deep thought for a long time before reciting her poem: "Both of us are ministers at the imperial court, Meeting by the river, our bond grows close. Like a flawless jade from Lín, And the warmth of spring in Lúlíng. The five horses of today unite as ten, Two men of yesteryear, their influence boundless."

People immediately saw the flying edict urging him to return. Together with the Prime Minister holding the balance of power, An Guo was so moved by it that he admired and praised it all day long. He rewarded him with ten thousand coins and appointed him governor of Fuyang. There was a girl named Hong Huiying who was singing a variety of palace tunes; just as she was about to sing a line, she suddenly stopped the drum and said, "Huiying has a short song expressing her feelings; I would like to share it with you." She then sang: The plum blossoms, like snow, are just being bent by the snow. In the snow, the plum blossoms display infinite spirit, all belonging to them. The plum blossoms, silent, have only the east wind to take charge. Pass this on to the East Lord and let him be the master of the plum blossoms. After finishing the song, she bowed twice and said, "The plum represents myself, Huiying; I dare not compare myself to famous flowers, but I use this metaphor to express my meaning. The snow refers to those rascals and young scoundrels, the official slaves; because he said that in the month he was in the government office, he was troubled by these young scoundrels several times, hence the emotion is expressed in the words, which is truly hard to find among his peers." Liao Shui's Dream A monk named Liao Shui from Yongning in Poyang initially traveled to Mount Lu to seek out the elder Tianchi, who was a senior monk. When he entered the hall, the elder Tianchi asked him, "Are you not a certain person?" Liao Shui replied, "The elder you are asking about is my ancestor, who has been deceased for a long time. How could you know about him?" The elder Tianchi said, "I dreamed last night that this person came to pay respects. Now that you have arrived, are you not the reincarnation of my ancestor?" It turned out that Liao Shui's ancestor had been a monk who passed the imperial examination, and Liao Shui was also a monk who passed the imperial examination. Those who heard this confirmed it as a sign of his reincarnation. The elder Tianchi called himself Jiantang, a famous monastery that was renowned in the forest. He did not accept disciples lightly, but Liao Shui became his top disciple. After inheriting the Dharma and becoming a monk, he took charge of the Yuantong Temple. On the night he first entered the temple, he dreamed of inviting many monks to have tea together, and he was in the sixth position. Upon waking, he did not understand the meaning. After six months, he moved to another temple and realized the significance of the sixth position. After a long time, he returned to the Jianshou Temple and passed away. Eternal Enlightenment Attendant

Monk Yongwu from Fuzhou frequently resided in temples, and whenever he stayed in one, he would bring his nephew as an attendant. Yongwu was strict and orderly in his conduct, and if others violated the rules, he would definitely scold them. However, with his nephew, even if he made mistakes, Yongwu would always show leniency and never reprimand him lightly. No one dared to speak up about this. Later, when Yongwu stayed at Guqing Temple in Baizhou, his nephew became even more unrestrained and lawless. All the senior monks rose up against him and went to the abbot's quarters to say, "If this person does not leave, we will all disperse." Yongwu then sadly said, "This matter has been long unspoken, but now it cannot be hidden anymore. This monk is my mother." The monks bowed and asked for an explanation.
