Yongwu said, "Although I have been a monk for a long time, I often stayed in my old home, meditating in one room. My mother had already passed away, but one night, I suddenly saw her covering her face and entering my brother's room. I was startled and wanted to get up, but then I heard that my sister-in-law had given birth to a boy. After he grew up, I bought him a certificate and made him a monk, always keeping him by my side. Even though I knew he was violating the monastic rules, I couldn't bring myself to ask." Everyone was sorry and apologized. Yongwu said, "Since the matter has become known, it is not appropriate for him to stay, so we sent him out of the temple." Not long after, Yongwu also left his position. On his way through Nankang, he met Elder Fahuan, the abbot of Doulue Temple, and told him. **Records of Guangfu Temple** Forty li north of Pengze County in Jiangzhou, Guangfu Temple had a revolving sutra cabinet that was extremely magnificent, clean, and well-maintained. At the beginning of the Shaoxing era, the great bandit Li Cheng invaded Jiangxi and stayed overnight at the temple, planning to burn it down and move south at dawn, intending to kill all the people of the county. That night, Li Cheng set up a bed in the sutra cabinet hall and was sleeping soundly. The sutra cabinet kept turning without stopping, and he suspected that there were hidden servants of the temple planning to harm him. He got up and called his bodyguards, who held torches and swords and followed him inside, but found no one there. However, the sound of the sutra cabinet became louder and it spun faster. Li Cheng was very afraid and immediately put on his formal clothes, went to the Buddha hall, and burned incense to apologize. Immediately, it became quiet. By dawn, he led his people through the county without harming a single person, and the entire region was saved.

The Guarding Dogs of Fuzhou Prefecture In the Fuzhou Prefecture, the official in charge of guarding the treasury kept dogs to alert against thieves, and these dogs were called "fángkù" (guarding the treasury). A yellow dog lived there for over ten years. Wu Xing's Zhou Li, while in office, was particularly attentive to the care of the dog, as if it were grateful. The dog would often sit and lie beside him. When Zhou Li's term of office ended, his nephew Gao Zhi took over the care of the dog due to concerns about it startling and biting children. The dog was taken to the Lotus Temple, twenty miles south of the floating bridge. The next day, the dog returned and was taken back to Gao Zhi. Zhou Li had not left yet, and during a visit, the dog recognized the carriage and identified its former owner. It greeted him warmly, followed him, and wagged its tail affectionately. Zhou Li understood the dog's intention and told it to stay on the boat. A few days later, the boat arrived at Ba River, and the dog jumped onto the boat. The boatman untied the rope and headed east, but the dog saw Zhou Li and jumped, barking and running after the boat for thirty to forty miles. Zhou Li looked back and saw the dog, so he ordered a small boat to pick it up. Once the dog was on board, Zhou Li was overjoyed and they proceeded to Lake City. In Yiling County, located in Xiazhou Prefecture, a child was born in the first year of the Shaoxing era. At one year old, the child suddenly grew several whiskers. By the time he was three, his beard was fully grown, despite his head being like a gourd and his speech still that of a baby. The child was black in color and was taken to the city. My second son, who was a judge, saw the child and intended to buy him from his father, but the child died before reaching five years old. Volume Six of the "Yijian Zhi" (Records of the Strange) From the "Qinding Siku Quanshu" (Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries)

*Etsi Venerabilis Liber Yijianzhi, Tomus VII, Part II Sui Tempore Song, Compositus a Hung Mai Lü Weng de Yueyang In Anno Chunxi XVI, Zhang Zou fuit Praefectus Yueyang. Audita est de templo Lü Gong in latere vetustae salicis in meridiano urbem, itaque profectus est ad reverentiam et adorandum, et vidit effigiem eius. Vestimenta eius erant obscura et non lucida, itaque mandavit artifices ut emendarent. Non erat tempus ut inquireret utrum opus esset finitum aut non. In nocte una, familia eius viderunt spiritum qui vestiebatur novo pallio flavo, stantem in viis civitatis, et evitabant eum. In aliis locis, iterum se invenerunt. Interrogavit eos de nomen et cognomen, et dixit: "Ego sum spiritus." Familiaris dixit: "Si tu es spiritus, cur non ambulas in caelo et non in terra?" Respondit: "Ego sum spiritus terrae." Sequenti die, narravit Zhang Zou, et egressus est ad officium, ante servos dixit: "Hodie mandatum est ut effigiem veritatis mutem, et opus est finitum." Zhang Zou miratus est et cogitavit ut emendaret vestimenta eius, et forma eius apparuit in somnio, sicut imaginatio. Itaque mandavit scriptum de secretis Dan Lü Gong, quod tenebat, in studiis civitatis, ut amplificaretur transmissio eius. Lü ipse scripsit praefationem, quae dicit: "Caelum et terra, duo instrumenta, et spiritus et vita, radices unius aeris. The Eighteen Verses Integrating the Methods of Proven Techniques, Respecting the Teacher, and the Law of Success, Secretly Revealed to the Younger Generation Seven Principles for Achieving Minor Success with Alchemical Elixirs - Eternal Youth Method (First) - Concentrating Fire to Boil the Sea Method (Second) - Matching Yin and Yang Method (Third) - Concentrating Fire to Return to the Original State Method (Fourth) - Dispersing Fire to Refine the Body Method (Fifth) - Dragon and Tiger Alchemical Elixir Method (Sixth) - Celestial Cycle Timing Method (Seventh) Four Principles for Achieving Intermediate Success with Alchemical Elixirs - River Carriage Method (First) - Flying Gold Method (Second) - Jade Liquid Elixir Method (Third) - Golden Liquid Refining the Body Method (Fourth) Five Principles for Achieving Major Success with Alchemical Elixirs - Integrated Return to the Origin Method (First) - Refining the Essence into Form Method (Second) - Inner Observation and Exchange Method (Third) - Immortals' Entry and Exit Method (Fourth) - Dividing the Form and Transcending Method (Fifth) The book contains three thousand words, with each verse consisting of four lines of four characters each, making it clear and easy to understand, revealing the true essence of cultivation. Zhang Er, the Second Doctor Zhang Er, a doctor from the capital, later moved to Lin'an and served as a high-ranking official in the imperial medical service. After retiring, he settled in Jizhou and opened a pharmacy. Although his skills were average, he focused on making a profit and accumulated wealth in the tens of thousands. Behind his house, there was a small garden measuring less than ten zhang. He went for walks every day. One day, the wall suddenly collapsed, falling on his right foot and causing a severe fracture with unbearable pain. A famous surgeon named Fan, known for his expertise in bone setting, was called urgently. Fan examined the bone and found it black and dry. He said, "All humans from head to toe are supported by marrow, which allows us to live long. When we are young and strong, if we suffer an injury, as long as the body is healthy, we can use splints and external medicines to heal. The severity and depth of the injury will determine how quickly we recover. But now, the marrow in this doctor's leg is dry and dead, and there is nothing I can do. I must leave." Zhang lay on the bed, moaning in pain for several months before dying.

**Translation:** The Fish of the Peng Family Pond Peng Zhongguang of Poyang had a fish pond thirty miles outside the county. In the late autumn, when the water receded, he went to the pond with his younger son Da Ban to observe. The fishermen set their nets all day but caught not a single fish. He was very surprised by this and stayed overnight in a village house by the shore. Da Ban dreamed of several yellow-clad soldiers with sticks driving dozens of criminals, all bound and lined up, men and women mixed together, thin and emaciated in appearance. One by one they entered the water, all covering their chests and weeping bitterly as if unwillingly falling in. Da Ban woke up in fright and told his father. The next day, when they lifted the nets, they found them full of fish. Peng Zhongguang was moved by this and could not bring himself to eat the fish. Grassroots Official Yao Jiangshi During the time when Wen Huigong was in charge of Huai East, he took his younger brother Xun with him while on duty. Unexpectedly, his biological mother fell ill with tuberculosis in Run, where there were no good doctors. He then took her to Changzhou. At that time, his elder brother Gao, who served as the magistrate of Jinling, feared that the disease would spread and sent them to the household of a certain officer named Fan An. They sought medical and magical treatment but ultimately she died, and she was buried in a temple. The following year, on the night of the Lantern Festival, Gao's eldest son Fang, who was ten years old, went to watch the lanterns at Dongyue Temple with two acquaintances. Fan An's residence was outside the temple, and he invited them to drink tea and eat fruit. After returning home, Fang fell ill and began to speak nonsense, no longer recognizing anyone. A man from the county named Yao Jiangshi, who had bought an official position by contributing grain, could perform the "Heavenly Heart Method" and was ordered to treat him.

A Divine Child and a Judge's Fate Before, a divine general was commanded to summon the land deity and interrogate him. A child who had descended as a spirit saw the general arrive and muttered to himself, "The official government is strict and orderly. How could there be evil spirits?" He suspected that it might be due to the actions of servants or concubines from the household. "How can I be blamed? There are no ghosts to capture," the general said, frustrated. Yao left and flew talismans and sprayed water from his own home. For more than ten days, he captured a woman, who turned out to be his mother. She would not speak about why she came. Yao issued a document to the City God to hold her in custody. Another day, when he called her again to ask, she finally said, "Because the young official came to the Fan family, I followed him into the county office." With wine, food, incense, and paper money, Yao sent her away and submitted a request to the Tian Shan Temple to register for rebirth. He burned her coffin, and the illness immediately improved. In Li Prefecture, there was a judge named Lin, the son of the late Governor De of Shaozhou, with the style name Bocong. In the second year of the Shaoxi era, he came from Poyang to become a judge in Li Prefecture and served for several months. One night, a night watch soldier saw the temple of the land deity in the courthouse, where the judge was sitting arrogantly on the altar. Someone told Lin, who thought the soldiers were joking and did not find it strange. The next night, the same thing happened again. Lin secretly observed but found no trace. He only beat the gatekeeper and personally went to close the temple door. He also selected a trustworthy person to stay beside the temple. This person, fearing punishment, did not dare sleep all night. Just before dawn, he saw a person with a large body and a beard like a horse's mane, who came from outside and stepped on his bedding. By morning, the figure was already sitting in the main position, arrogantly. Lin quickly checked the seals and locks, and the door was still closed as before. Finally, greatly frightened and surprised, he said, "It must be because the worship was not respectful, causing this strange phenomenon." He then prepared meat and wine, worshiped, and apologized for the mistake. Soon, he received a handwritten imperial edict. Lin rode out of the city to meet the emperor. In the middle of the journey, his whip fell, as if he had been struck by a wind. The driver picked up the whip and continued, but Lin had already fallen off the horse's back. He was helped down and could only bow to the edict. As soon as he returned home, he died. The guards said that a few days ago, they dreamed that the official and the temple judge had switched places. Lin was originally a virtuous and auspicious person, but met with this fate. Even after death, he became a minor deity.

**Two Strange Events in Linjiang** It is said in Linjiang that there were two strange occurrences. The first involved a wild cat within the military administration with eyes as red as cinnabar. When it appeared, it would hold its head with its front paws and look around with a glaring expression, standing upright like a human. The second involved a white dog in the provincial granary, whose presence was unknown. Anyone who saw it would surely suffer misfortune. In the second year of the Shaoxing era, my son-in-law Qian Mi served as the military governor and often saw the cat. Soon after, an old soldier reported that the cat had given birth to six kittens, but their location was unknown. Shortly thereafter, the cat carried two of its kittens to the magistrate's office and began to eat them. At that time, Jing Wei was in office, but not long after, Qian was dismissed on the same day due to accusations made by Wei, who was a censor. My nephew Zhu, who was the magistrate of Qingjiang, temporarily took charge and entered the granary to retrieve the grain for the horses. Suddenly, the soldiers knelt and bowed repeatedly, and I was startled and asked what was happening. They said that the white dog was standing outside the pile of grain. Upon seeing it, I confirmed that it was indeed there, and they also showed respect to it. Zhu died in office a few months later. **The Concubine of Zhu Cong** Zhu Cong, a judicial official from Lishui, Zhejiang, obtained his position through the favor of his ancestor, a high-ranking official. In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, he served as the judicial assistant in Linjiang and had a concubine. His wife, Wang, could not tolerate her presence and subjected her to cruel and relentless abuse, eventually leading the concubine to commit suicide. Zhu Cong saw her ghost frequently, and it troubled him greatly. He called upon a Taoist priest from Ge Sha Mountain to perform an exorcism and sent a decree to the Temple of the City God to bind the spirit. The priest instructed the ghost, saying, "From today onward, you must not go to the Yue Temple." Afterward, the ghost was no longer seen. One day, when the officials went out together to pray for clear weather, it started raining unexpectedly. They arrived at the Yue Temple, and all the carriages entered to take shelter. Zhu followed them, and soon the rain stopped. As they exited, Zhu suddenly encountered the ghost of his former concubine approaching without any sign of respect. Her face was filled with anger and resentment. Zhu said to her, "Since your death, I have felt deep sorrow and pity for you. Now you should know that this is not my doing." The ghost replied, "If it were not for you, I would not have come here." ---

A servant of an official once acquired something unusual. Returning home, he told his wife about it. Not long after, he fell ill and died. Wang's wife died shortly after. They had three sons, the eldest being only six years old, and no one to manage the funeral. Relatives and neighbors who were officials, upon hearing this, quickly sent a message to arrange the funeral affairs. They also summoned a Daoist to examine the situation. The Daoist saw Zhu dressed in a robe holding a tablet, standing with two female prisoners wearing shackles and being questioned. When the coffin was taken out, a neighbor's daughter saw two coffins followed by a woman with disheveled hair and ragged clothes clapping and laughing, then leaving. The girl was shocked and told her parents, who immediately fell ill and became mute, never speaking again. Zhu's neighbor, Huang Xiang, a local official, said to me that he thought the death of one concubine and the master and mistress paying for her life, the strictness of the temple and not capturing a ghost, allowing it to move freely, and a young girl telling of the strange events immediately falling ill and becoming mute, all of which are suspicious. A Woman from Yiyang A scholar from Yiyang named Chen and his friend went to watch lanterns on the Lantern Festival night. A girl from a household, about 13 or 14 years old, was sitting on the shoulder of a servant. A hairpin fell from her hair and landed on Chen's hat. Chen was very happy and took her home to show his wife. His wife suspected that he had obtained her from a brothel and took the hairpin away, throwing it on the ground. Chen invited his friend to verify the reason. His wife looked sad and unhappy, saying, "If what you say is true, it must be an unusual event, a sign of good fortune, and I will not live long." After several months, she died. Chen remained single for ten years before remarrying. He again went to watch lanterns on the Lantern Festival night and mentioned the previous incident. It was the girl who dropped the hairpin.

### Master Who Encourages Good Deeds The Dongyue Palace in Raozhou was completely destroyed by fire. Later, it was roughly repaired and restored, leaving only about twenty rooms. In the twelfth month of the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, Judge Meng Zhi and his wife went to pay their respects. Upon reaching the outer hall, they entered a small room where they saw a statue of a monk whose colors and decorations were all faded. They asked what deity it was, and the attendant replied, "It is the Master Who Encourages Good Deeds." Judge Meng glanced at his wife, who had the intention to renovate the statue but had not yet spoken about it. That night, he dreamed of a tall monk coming to beg for clothes to protect against the winter cold, saying, "It will not cost you more than three thousand of your family's money." Upon waking, he was delighted and found the dream strange. The next day, he immediately ordered the workers to renovate and repaint the statue, using exactly three thousand pieces of paper money. ### Monkeys and Rats in Jingnan In a certain year during the Chunxi era, along the official road in Jingnan, there were suddenly rats numbering in the millions, blocking the thoroughfare. They came in various colors—black, white, yellow, and blue—and some even resembled chickens or birds. People walking through the area were barely aware of their presence, and those who were trampled by carriages and horses could not be counted. This continued for two or three months before it subsided. Then, a monkey about two feet tall, hidden in a tall tree, began to harm people. It was summer, and many women were sitting exposed to the open air, and the monkey often played pranks on them, making them too afraid to leave. The brave residents tried every means to catch it, but after a long time, it fell into a net. A villager put it in a bag and carried it to Baqiu Mountain, intending to throw it into the river current. Before dawn, they reached the western part of the city and met an old man with a beard and hair as white as snow. He smiled and asked, "What is in your bag? Isn't it a monkey?" The villager replied, "Yes." The old man said, "It truly has committed a crime, but I wish to spare its life. I just happened to have thirty official vouchers. Thank you, and let it go. It will be released, and it will not return. Let us both go to Baqiu Mountain and release it in the deep forest. That will be enough." He then took out the vouchers from his sleeve and gave them to the villager. The villager, overjoyed by this unexpected gain, followed the old man. From then on, the monkey indeed disappeared and did not return. Could this old man have been the monkey's grandfather or ancestor?

In the third year of the Shaoxing era, Ji, the nephew and grandson of Lu Jiuyuan, served as the signatory judge in Jingmen. Lu Jiuyuan, a renowned Neo-Confucian philosopher from Lichuan, traveled with the physician Zhou Li from Guixi. In November of that year, Ji had a dream where he saw himself wearing a fur coat, sitting in the convenience hall, and those around him calling him "Governor." In the morning, he told Ji about the dream. Ten days later, he had another dream where he heard people saying that he would soon hand over the seal. Before the end of the year, on the tenth day, Lu Jiuyuan fell ill and did not recover. Ji was wearing a fur coat in the study, temporarily taking over the seal, and soon received a command from the commander to take charge of military affairs. Prior to this, Lu Jiuyuan had read the "Jingmen Gazetteer" and came across Wang Zhan's "Huiquan Pavilion Record," written in October of the Gengwu year (1130 CE) of the Shaoxing era. The record stated that perhaps the spirit of Jiuyuan could find a place to rely on to benefit the people. Lu Jiuyuan strongly disliked this statement and quickly closed the book. Soon after, he passed away.

A Fierce Beast in Guiyan My cousin Zhongkan, who lived in Hongyuan, returned from visiting his uncle's residence at Guiyan to join his elder brother for a drink. It was already midnight when they began their journey back. One servant held a torch and walked ahead, feeling that something was following behind. After three miles, as they approached home, another person from the household emerged with a torch. They then saw a wild boar, which had likely arrived by riding the firelight. Both sides thrust at each other with spears. The boar jumped from the slope onto the shore and happened to be at a dry well, where it fell and was killed by the crowd. The boar was covered with arrows all over its body, like small tortoise-shell tubes on the surface, sharp enough to treat itchy heads. Usually, when encountering people, it would attack and provoke them, causing sores when it bit, and if it bit in a vital area, it took a long time to heal. The villager Cheng Shiba, who could shoot tigers, was known by the villagers as Cheng Dachong. His method was to boil the juice of Aconite and soak the arrowheads in it, setting traps and waiting on the path where tigers came and went. Once, a tiger was wounded by an arrow and could not move, leaning against a tree and squatting. Cheng said, "A dying tiger does not fall to the ground; this one is already dead, there is no need to fear it." Passing by and wanting to take it, the tiger was still alive and roaring in pain, raising its two paws to attack. Cheng raised his arm to resist, his strength nearly exhausted. His companions saw and rushed to help, and he barely managed to escape. His back of the head, arm, and shoulder were grabbed by the tiger's claws, and he lay ill for several months before recovering.

### Final Translation **Pan Zhan and the Monk** Pan Zhan, a physician from LePing, lived in the county market. One day, a merchant visited him and said, "This morning, I encountered a monk who bought two bolts of purple silk from me. He paid the full price and put them in his sleeves, instructing me to follow him to get the money. We arrived at your residence and went straight in. Now it has been a while, and he wants me to speak and hurry him." Pan Zhan replied, "Why do we need a monk to buy silk when we already have it? Moreover, we have never had such people come and go. You should leave quickly." The guest refused to leave and spoke forcefully, still describing the monk's appearance and the worn-out state of his sleeve. Pan Zhan finally realized and rushed to the temple where he worshipped the Bodhisattva in Suzhou to check. Indeed, the two bolts of silk were on the left side. Because he usually revered and respected it, dressing it in a true robe, prayers were answered, and over the years, it accumulated dust and was gnawed by mice. Not having time to replace it, on that day, he was startled. He burned incense and apologized to the whole family, took the money to compensate the guest, and called a craftsman to make new clothes for it. **Yu Invites a Doctor** Wang Chun, a scholar from Poyang, lived dozens of miles outside the city. His wife, Yu, fell ill in the early autumn of the fifth year of Shaoxi and lay on the bed for two weeks, treated by several doctors without success. The family discussed wanting to invite Liu Chang, a doctor who served in the imperial court as a medical officer and had once been a detective officer in the Jiangdong Criminal Court, because he lived in the city. That night, Chun dreamed of a strange man giving him a golden knife, about a foot long, with brilliant light. Awakening, he silently rejoiced, thinking the golden knife was Liu, and concluded it must be a sign for Chang, without any doubt. Early in the morning, he ordered his servant to go. Just as he was opening the door, his friend Song Zhen happened to arrive and asked about her illness, saying that last night someone from the city came and said the newly stationed physician Liu Shunchen had very high skills, why not have him examine her. Chun realized that the dream was a divine sign and immediately changed the message, sent his servant and horse to invite him, arriving when it was already dusk. Yu was already in a critical condition. Shunchen gave her two medicines, and by dawn, she was suddenly and completely revived, and fully recovered after a few days.

The Middleman Wang San In the seventh year of the Qian Dao era (1171), a villager named Zheng Wu from Poyang was afflicted with an epidemic disease that affected his entire family. He was extremely poor and could not even provide basic sustenance like porridge. He wanted to call a doctor or a shaman and buy medicine but had nothing at all. He only managed to get a felt hat and asked the middleman Wang San to sell it for a thousand coins. Wang San secretly kept half of the money. As soon as Zheng Wu returned home, he fell ill and became unconscious and unaware. After six or seven days, neighbors thought he would surely die. Suddenly, he shouted loudly, as if he was being beaten with a stick, in great pain. His wife asked him what he saw, and he could describe it. He said he was just chased by a yellow-clad official near the Hu family's house. He saw a large ship docked at the shore, with a high-ranking official sitting there, surrounded by attendants in red and purple, with a dazzling retinue. The official led him to the shore and asked, "Why did you dare to hide and keep the money from Zheng Xiaowu?" He did not dare to conceal it and was beaten on the buttocks a hundred times, thrown into the grass, the pain was unbearable. The official ordered someone to give him cooling medicine immediately, and the ship moved away from the shore. The attendants told him to drink from two jars of water. He then woke up trembling and broke out in a sweat, feeling better. The pain in his buttocks became more severe, and when he looked, there were red pustules, about a hundred of them, and he was bedridden for several months before recovering. Later, a major epidemic broke out, and it was said that what he saw was a plague deity.

### Final Translation #### Cao Hongmai's Account of Tang Xianzu Tang Xianzu, a native of Shidai, Chizhou, was the grandson of Vice Minister of War Yu Gong. In the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, he became the magistrate of Jing County. Upon taking office, the chief clerk informed him that he should pay homage to the temple three days after assuming his duties. Tang rebuked him, saying, "I practice the Five Thunder Method, where the deities are under my control. Why should I bow to a clay idol?" Instead, he ordered the preparation of two tables with food and drink, set up in the temple. He then commanded the ceremonial guards to clear the way, and he went directly to the temple, where he sat as an equal with the deity, drinking and conversing, explaining his intention to seek protection while in office. Officials and common people who saw this were secretly surprised and worried. That night, a sudden storm arose, and the waters flooded the county seat, submerging it up to seven or eight feet deep, even reaching the beds. Many documents and chests were submerged, but no one was injured. Everyone believed this was due to disrespecting the gods. Tang had the damaged houses and buildings repaired by cutting wood from the forest. He also commissioned the painter Wang to create seventy-two large statues of divine generals, and he treated them with great respect, offering incense and performing rituals. The following spring, he was dismissed by the high-ranking official Li Tangqing, whose name was Zimian. #### The Two Zhao Brothers of Shuiyang In the town of Shuiyang in Xuancheng, there were over forty members of the imperial clan living there. Shigong and Shibān were cousins. Their residences were located near the Kōngxiàng Temple, about several hundred steps apart. During the mid-Chuanyi period, the two brothers together beat and killed a monk. Shigong managed to escape, while Shibān took the blame and was imprisoned in Quannan, an external branch of the imperial clan's administration. Later, he was released due to an unexpected favor from a superior. His father, Boling, was a military officer stationed in Pingjiang Prefecture. Shibān stayed home to manage his mother's grave. One evening, as he returned to his residence, he heard someone calling out "Zhao San" repeatedly from outside, which was very frightening. He hurriedly entered the house, and several relatives who were present also heard the calls. The next day, when he went to the gravesite, he accidentally stepped on a fallen tree, which caused him to fall and be injured. He fell ill and was unable to move or rise from bed, and eventually died. This occurred in the autumn of the Shaoxi era, the year Jiazi. The community was greatly affected by this event.

Knowing that the monk's wrong was avenged, the monk was confident he had escaped calamity, his name was not entangled in official documents, so he had nothing to fear. By the autumn of the year 1613, because of some matter, he was summoned to the government office. The boat was ready, he instructed the servants to untie the ropes first, and waited a moment before walking alone to the boat. People were surprised at his long delay, and his family, watching by the riverbank, did not see the boat start. But they did not suspect any other reason. At that time, two travelers passed by the road, finding it strange that he stood there dazed in the grass, they shook him repeatedly, and he finally responded. He said he had gone to drink with his third brother and felt quite drunk. The two travelers spat on his face and said, "He has been dead for a year. You must have seen a ghost." He was startled and realized, then went to the temple and instructed the monks to set up a meal and perform a ritual of repentance all night, hoping to atone for past sins. He died within a few days. **Chen Li Yuan's Response** There were disputes between the Li and Chen families in Shuiyang. The Li family, who were officials in the Qin government, used their power to blame the Chen family. Chen Weng died in prison after several years. One of his younger sons, while working in the fields, was harassed by a white dog and chased it away with a stick. The dog ran into the Li family's hall and suddenly disappeared. That night, Chen Weng appeared in a dream to his wife, saying, "I have harbored resentment for many years. Now that I have received justice, I come to inform you. Tomorrow, you can set up a memorial for me to celebrate." The wife woke up and cried, following his instructions. Soon after, his eldest son entered Li Mountain to dig firewood. Li happened to be at the foot of the mountain and heard the sound of chopping. He rushed to see and found him. Li took a large pole and struck him, dragging him to the ground. He then had his servants beat him severely, leaving no part of his body unscarred. The man died, and Li quickly chopped several bundles of bamboo and piled them in the thicket, shouting, "Chen held a knife and was a thief. I fought with him." The local magistrate, Wang, examined the body and reported the truth to the county. Li was imprisoned and soon died, at the place where Chen Weng had died years ago.

### Translation **严州女子** A young unmarried woman from the scholar's family in Yanzhou woke up one night to find a fruit resembling a peach beside her pillow. She ate it, but upon waking the next day, she began to cover her nose whenever she saw food and drink, and refused to eat anything. Her parents were astonished and sought help from doctors and shamans, but none could alleviate her condition. This continued for over ten years, during which her body did not feel weak or emaciated, and she appeared normal in every other way, except that she dared not consider marriage matters. Suddenly, she asked for wine, and her family gave her a cup, which she immediately drank. She then claimed to be very hungry and asked for food. From that day on, she returned to normal and was eventually married to a scholar named Hong from the same region. Hong's two younger brothers, Yan and Pu, both passed the imperial examination in the year Geng Xu of the Shaoxi era, and Yan became the magistrate of Nanling County. **徐南陵请大仙** In the first year of the Shaoxi era, Xu Daren, a native of Wuxing, was the magistrate of Nanling County. In the early autumn of the following year, an old friend visited him who could recite a spell to invite a great immortal. They lit incense and poured wine to test the spell. Soon, the divination tool moved and wrote, "Zhang Zifang." From then on, whenever they invited Zhang, he would appear and answer their prayers. Xu would always set aside an empty seat for Zhang during his gatherings, and Zhang would write down whether the wine was good or bad, and how quickly the cups were passed. Once, Xu offered a golden cup, and Zhang wrote, "This is an old family heirloom, and no one can understand it." Later, Xu suddenly remembered that he had once served under Zhang Gong, the governor of Huai Prefecture, who had invited them to a banquet and used this cup to toast. Xu wanted to give the cup to Zhang, but Zhang declined and left early the next morning. Xu then realized that Zhang's words were true, and he wrote several poems in one go, filling dozens of scrolls. Xu set up a convenient seat and displayed the two large characters "Ice Vessel" written by Zhang Cunshi. He asked Zhang to write a record, and Zhang wrote several hundred words with a vigorous and powerful brush, unlike his usual handwriting. Xu treasured the writing and could not bear to part with it. He had craftsmen decorate it and placed it on the wall. He also named the southern pavilion "Song Yu" and the northern pavilion "Qin Ji." Xu's wife, Zhou, died in the winter, and her death was very tragic. Zhang wrote a "Drum and Pot Song" to mourn her.

Inquiry: Where is Xu Kouzhou now? It was said that he was reborn in the family of General Wang in Jiangzhou, and in the third announcement of the imperial court, he was reborn as a man. Further questioning about the date and name revealed that he would be informed tomorrow. The next day, it was said: "I will travel by riding the wind for you." Upon investigation, it was found that this year in April on a certain day, he was born, and his small name was Ronglang. On the night of his birth, his mother dreamed of a woman pulling the curtain and entering, and upon waking, she gave birth. You may pass through Jiujiang and try to visit him; we will exchange a glance and smile. Xu inquired about the fortunes and misfortunes of his fellow officials, and it was said that the magistrate of Shao had the fault of frivolous speech. Soon after, Shao died without illness, as he was always a skilled speaker. Xu thus became even more devout and believed. It was then said: "You have the bone of the Dao; in the future, you will accompany me on a journey to the three islands of Penglai. You should clean a room, seal it tightly, arrange incense and fruits, use a small tray with a brush on top, and hang them from the beams. Wait until the day of Jiazi in September; I will be summoned and come upon hearing the command. All prayers and requests should be written down, but no outsiders should be allowed in." Xu followed these instructions carefully. When the time came, the so-called old friend had already left, and he forgot the request for a farewell, so he did not go.

A Beautiful Woman of Nanling In the Han dynasty, Nanling was an ancient county under the jurisdiction of Xuan Prefecture. A commoner named Seng opened a tavern inside the gate of the county office. One night, he went out of the door on a moonlit night and encountered a beautiful woman who seemed to be coming from her own home hall. She saw Seng and immediately spoke to him with a smile. Seng, being a mere butcher and seller in the market, was too shy to respond. The woman took his hand and directly went to the tavern. Seng became infatuated with her beauty and kept her there for the night, leaving at dawn. On other nights, she returned again, and this continued for several months. Every time she came, she would give gifts. Initially, she received money, but later she carried silver cups and gradually accumulated to pots and jars. The amount received was too much to count, and Seng gradually suspected it was stolen from the master's house. However, he was greedy for wealth and deeply in love, and did not consider it a worry. Therefore, he went to the outskirts to perform some task and met a Taoist priest asking for money. The priest looked dry and haggard and told him, "Your face is full of evil qi; you will die by the hand of a ghost." Seng was shocked and realized it too late. He told the Taoist priest everything. The priest asked for a piece of paper about three inches long from a nearby house, wrote a talisman, and instructed him to stick it on the door. That night, Seng heard a woman.

He angrily shouted, "I treated you with utmost sincerity, and you received my gifts generously. I entrusted you with my entire life, yet you suddenly developed a deluded heart. If you act thus, then I will not act thus." He feared the talisman and dared not enter, saying, "You betrayed kindness and forgotten righteousness. I swear to abandon you and leave angrily." After several nights, he knocked on the door again and said, "You are extremely unrighteous, making it unbearable for me. Tomorrow night, I will punish you." He then left. The person began to be greatly terrified and sat waiting until dawn before moving to another neighborhood, thereby severing all ties. Three years later, the county magistrate Xu Dalun's wife Zhou died. Her younger brother came from Wu to pay condolences and stayed in the rear hall. At night, as he sat by the lamp, he suddenly leaned forward and fell, with saliva flowing uncontrollably. A servant carrying a lamp below quickly carried him back to the room. Xu carefully examined him and administered soup and medicine. After some time, he woke up and said, "I just finished using the toilet and saw a woman leading me to a different pavilion. The furnishings were luxurious, and there were singing and music, but we did not have time for intimate affection when people called me awake. The county official said that the house had always been haunted by evil spirits, and many strange occurrences had happened before. Therefore, the place was made empty, and the western side was evacuated. Xu Zizi, a person from Huzhou, was also present."

Trance of Officer Hu Officer Hu of Huating, during the summer night, sought to cool off and sat on a trapeze bed to sleep. In his dream, a tall and strong man dressed in white robes shook him awake, saying, "There is an unsuitable matter in your family that needs urgent attention." Officer Hu was startled and woke up, quickly going out the door. He indeed saw a person hanging himself under the eaves. He went to look and found that the person was a maid whom his son's wife had used in the room. The wife was a woman from the same county, surnamed Zhang, who was cruel and jealous. This maid had been beaten with a hundred strokes even for a slight mistake, could not bear the pain, and thus died. Officer Hu called for his wife to come close and look at the scene. His wife showed no emotion, calmly saying, "Others do not need to worry. If it is not possible to save her, I will take the responsibility myself." She then fetched a stool, climbed up, untied the hanging rope, and after a while, the maid regained consciousness. Officer Hu's family was very strict in their worship of the gods of Guangde, and they abstained from eating pork. Therefore, it is said that they received divine power. The wife of Officer Hu was still not reformed in her evil ways. Eligible Concubines of Yang Zheng Yang Zheng, during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, was a famous general in Qin, with a reputation comparable to that of the two Wu generals. He rose to the rank of Grand Marshal, but his nature was cruel and he enjoyed killing people. One day, when he was commanding in Xingyuan, he invited his military advisors to a banquet. During the meal, Li Shuyong, one of his advisors, got up to change clothes. A soldier held a candle to guide him to the latrine, which was a long, narrow passageway. Looking at the walls, there were faint human-like shadows, which were thought to be paintings. Upon closer inspection, there were no brushstrokes, and the figures had no faces or features. There were about twenty or thirty of them. Li Shuyong was puzzled and asked the soldier, who glanced around and whispered, "The general has dozens of concubines, all of whom have musicians. But if they are slightly displeasing to him, he will beat them to death and skin them, from head to toe, and nail their skins to this wall. They are left until they dry and harden, and then thrown into the water. These are the traces of their skins." Li Shuyong was terrified and left. Yang Zheng's favorite concubine, whom he loved exclusively, was the one he was most attached to, especially in his later years when he was ill and bedridden, unable to attend to any affairs. He cared for nothing else but this concubine.

Having someone attend to him on the side, he suddenly spoke to them: "My illness is so severe and lingering; I will not survive. I speak with complete honesty and sincerity, only on you. What shall I do now?" At that time, his breath was barely present, and most of what he said was incomprehensible. The woman wept and said, "Sir, please take the medicine; if I do not recover, I wish to follow you to the underworld." Yang was greatly pleased and asked for wine, drinking one cup each with her. The woman withdrew, pondering deeply and regretting her previous words greatly. Secretly planning to hide and escape, Yang was on the verge of death, frequently closing his eyes. His close general scolded him: "Sir, you have killed people as easily as picking lice; a true man of great courage. Today, at the end of your life, to linger and be attached to something is truly lacking in courage and decisiveness." Yang called the woman's name and said, "I will wait until he dies first, then I will leave." The general understood his intention and deceived the woman, saying, "Sir, he called; calling ahead a strong man to hold a thin rope hidden behind the bed." When the woman arrived, she stood and put the rope around his neck. After a short time, he died; his body was laid out on the ground; Yang immediately lost consciousness. --- A Man from Yixing A man from Yixing, Wu Guan, in the fifth year of the Shaoxing era during the Southern Song Dynasty, suffered from a severe eye disease. He took his wife and children to rest in Yuhang. For over a month, he stayed there. One day, he suddenly felt a strong desire to return home. His wife sent someone to buy things in Lin'an, but before they returned, Wu insisted on leaving, as if he was being urged to go. He only left one servant to guard the house and set off on the road without interacting with anyone. The next morning, they set out from Tianmu Mountain, but a sudden flood came, and the water was several feet deep. Wu's residence was submerged in the water, and the servant drowned. At that time, more than ten thousand people were affected by the disaster.

Zhang Yuan Gan's Dream Zhang Nan, courtesy name Yuan Gan, was a renowned scholar from Fuzhou. He entered the Imperial Academy and served as a scholar there. After achieving high honors and deregistering from his place of residence to take the provincial examination, he failed and stopped. He offered prayers at the local shrine, saying, "Though I am unworthy, I feel that my level of scholarship is not inferior to my peers. Now that I have failed, there must be a reason. I dare to ask the god for an explanation." That night, he dreamed that a god came to visit and said, "You will pass the examination because someone recently received a bribe of four hundred and thirty or so strings of cash and several hundred and several dozen coins, which caused your failure." Upon waking, Zhang Nan silently thought, "As a poor scholar, how could I have such a thing?" At the time, the students who had studied under him heard that their teacher was going to take the examination and each contributed according to their ability, but he estimated that the amount was still not enough. He suspected that this was the reason and took a small notebook to count the contributions, which matched exactly with what the god had said, including the strings of cash, hundreds, tens, and individual coins. Only then did he realize the truth and informed everyone, making them aware that one should not obtain wealth unwarrantedly. He continued to pass the higher examinations and was granted a government position. The Family of Luo Jiangshi In the year Chunxi Guiyou (1173), Zhang Jinmei, a professor from the Western Outer Academy, was appointed as an official in charge of revising imperial edicts. He moved his family to the capital but did not yet have an official residence, so they rented a house in Lengshui Lane, where Luo Jiangshi lived temporarily. Luo himself lived nearby. After several days, Luo's wife visited Zhang's family frequently, asking if they were well. She continued to do so each time she met them, thinking it was a common courtesy. The area was particularly damp and low-lying. After a few months, they moved away, and Huang Jingxiang, who came from Chuzhou, took up residence as a scholar at the Imperial Academy. After a few days, the maidservants in the house cried out at night, saying there was a thief. Soon after, the front door was wide open, and the whole night was disturbed. The next morning, they checked carefully and found nothing missing except for four new sets of laundry that were originally in the kitchen, which were nowhere to be seen.

Two of them were on the wall, and it was still thought they were taken away by a thief and accidentally dropped. The other two were under a pile of firewood. Yellow was suspicious and strange but did not want to tell others. After several days, he saw something strange again. Originally placed four porcelain bowls on the table, they were all stacked under the wall, and he also did not ask about it. Yellow's mother, the lady, was ill and he served her medicine until the third watch of the night. Carrying a lamp to the toilet, he found dozens of kitchen utensils arranged on the toilet boards. Fearing, he quickly left, and so he also removed them. Later, he investigated the matter. It turned out that a year ago, the eldest son of Luo was severely reprimanded by his parents for being reckless and careless, and he hanged himself in the kitchen. Since then, strange occurrences have been frequent. Jin Ying heard about it and realized that what Luo's wife had asked was indeed this. Yellow's mother soon passed away. The wife of Chen Er, a farmer from the filial piety town of Jinhua County, was pregnant and about to give birth. She went to the Great Peace Temple in the town and asked a monk to recite the *Kokāla Vidyārāja Sūtra* in front of the Buddha to pray for protection. After giving birth to a boy, she did not fulfill her initial wish for a long time. Her wife then became blind in both eyes. All her sewing and mending were done by her neighbors. The boy could take steps and crawl. The wife dreamed of a monk who said, "Give me a thousand coins, and I will treat your eye disease." In the morning, she told her husband about it. Her husband said, "Is it not because the sutra you promised has not been returned?" The wife said, "Our family is also poor, and we barely have enough to eat. How could we have time for this? The Buddha should not be so petty as to argue with me." That night, Chen Er also had the same dream. By chance, he went to the Great Peace Temple and read the merit book in the monk's room, suddenly remembering the matter. He then burned incense and prayed, asking for his wife's eyesight to be restored, promising to repay the debt when the autumn harvest was successful. This was during the year of Xichu, the 20th year of the Shaoxi era. By the end of autumn, he finally fulfilled his promise, spending exactly one thousand coins. By the beginning of spring, his wife's eyesight was as good as ever, and she had no more suffering.

In Wuyuan, there were deceitful people who made a living by slaughtering cattle. Some of them used poison to kill the cattle, boiling it slowly and mixing in other medicines to create a toxic solution. They would then insert a three-inch-long iron needle into the cow's flank skin. The cow would die within a day, and they would call others to slaughter and skin the meat. Since the meat was not diseased, eating it was harmless, and it was called "good slaughter," with a slightly higher price. Several years ago, butchers in Poyang village widely practiced this method. There was a man named Jiang Liu San who lived fifteen li east of the city and often practiced this technique. Farmers saw the cows die without any illness and could not understand it. They sold the meat, but even neighbors and townspeople did not know about it. On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month of the fifth year of Shaoxing, Jiang Liu San went out and did not return until dusk. His wife and children searched everywhere he usually went, but no one had seen him. The next day, after noon, his wife saw a flock of crows and eagles circling and making noise near a dirty pond a hundred steps away from their home. She went to see and found that Jiang had drowned in the pond, which was only three feet deep. When they were preparing the coffin, the coffin-maker found two poisoned needles in his waist bag. It was then known that he had been deceiving for a long time, and he was killed by a ghost when he went to someone's house.

### Record of the Immovable Rock, Volume Eight, Section B **Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries** **Record of the Immovable Rock, Volume Nine** **By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty** **The Old Man of Yihuang** During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Song Dynasty, the magistrate of Yihuang County in Fuzhou Prefecture, named Xu, was presiding over a lawsuit. An elderly man, named Hou Lin, wept bitterly and presented a petition, stating that he lived near the altar of the earth god. He claimed that a bowman named Xia Sheng had set fire to his home, destroying everything and reducing his three sons to ashes. Upon reading the petition, Xu was greatly shocked and immediately arrested Xia Sheng, sending him to jail for interrogation. The questioning was very rigorous, but Xia Sheng did not know how to respond and wept without eating. The county officials all spoke, saying that Xia Sheng was usually law-abiding and how could he possibly commit such a crime as setting fire and killing people during the day? They wished to follow the lead of the petition and investigate the end, and entrusted an assistant official to go to the scene and verify the facts, hoping to find out the truth. Xu followed their advice and went to the altar to look for traces, but found no trace of Mr. Hou's residence or signs of burning. It turned out that Xia Sheng was there to worship the earth god.

The magistrate, following the order of the county magistrate, swept and cleaned the altar, cleared away thorny weeds and rubbish, and carried away dry grass and straw. He placed it in an empty hole and burned it, for it was a den of foxes. Three cubs died, and the old fox took advantage of public power to exact revenge, saying that summer began to be released. Another female fox transformed into a beautiful woman and came and went, seen by all officials and commoners. She had a black mole on her left cheek, as big as a bean, and many died because of her enchantment. Many officials and clerks in the Blue Wave Hall often saw her engaged in needlework, and when she heard human voices, she would disappear. In the year of Gengchen, the county magistrate of Qu County, Mr. Zhu, came to this hall alone to sleep and met her, delighted. He took her into his bed, and soon after, his son also died. The snake in Yihuang County Magistrate's Office is connected to the county government. There is a large snake two zhang long, with green-black scales, making a sound when walking on the ground. The elders say that whenever it appeared in one place, the owner would surely suffer misfortune. In the spring of Gengchen year during the Shaoxing era, it appeared at the east wall of the magistrate's house, beside the lotus pond. It hid half its body in the wall, with its tail hanging in the pond. Just as the midday arrived, he came to the pond and called out to anyone who could catch it. He dug a hole in the wall to catch it. The snake coiled and did not move. He ordered strong men to carry it out of the city, passing the Hundred Zhang Bridge and releasing it into the wilderness. Thinking it had gone far and could not return, the next day, Mr. Zhu still came at noon to the same place, but the snake was still in its original den. He wanted to kill it, but none of the officials dared to accept the order, saying this disaster is too great, and they would surely be beaten. Unable to do otherwise, he only ordered them to carry it away, doing so four or five times. Until Mr. Zhu died, it was never seen again.

Administrative Center of Yihuang County In the rear of Yihuang County, there are scenic spots called the Moon-Watching Terrace, the Tame Pheasant Hall, and the Hundred-Step Pavilion, all built according to the terrain. During the early period of the Shaoxing era, a great bandit entered the county, and the people rushed to flee for their lives, all dying in the process. Therefore, spirits and supernatural beings caused disturbances, and dozens of officials were too afraid to stay in the main bedroom, mostly living in the western wing or boat-like study. In the year 1158, Li Yuanzuo from Nanchang arrived as the new official. He first opened the doors, swept the dust, removed the rooms, and repaired the windows before living there. He lived there day and night in peace, seeing nothing unusual. Only the servants lived in the western part of the boat-like study, not far from the Tame Pheasant Hall. Someone said they heard the sound of a bell shaking every day, and they hurried to see it. It was the night patrol soldier's bell, ringing by itself in the air, coming and going suddenly, with no object being carried or controlled. They hit it with a stick, and it fell. One day, a bundle of bamboo emerged from the hall, twisting and turning like a snake. The servant cut it with a knife and threw it into the fire, to report to Li. Li dismissed it as unbelievable. He remained in office for three years, living as if it were just one day. When he was about to be replaced, he moved to the inn and planned to repair the old administrative center to welcome the new magistrate. The household items had not yet been fully moved. The next morning when he went to fetch them, they were all piled up by ghosts, from small to large, until they reached the roof. It took a lot of effort to clean up. Later officials heard about this and also lived in the western wing.

Zhang Bao Yi In the county of Jing'an, there was a butcher named Zhang Bao Yi from the village of Chaoshan. Due to his military service during the Jianyan era, he was awarded an official rank and became very wealthy. His story was recorded in the "Wu Zhi," a local chronicle, and he was known to the chief monk of the Baofeng Temple, Master Jingxiang. After the temple was destroyed by fire, Zhang Bao Yi rebuilt it with great effort and acquired several thousand acres of land to support the temple. He had an enormous amount of money and built dozens of earthen warehouses as places for storage. For thirty years, he carefully accounted for his lifetime expenses. In his later years, he suddenly heard a faint, continuous sound of money inside the warehouse. He listened carefully from outside the door and struck the door with a staff, saying, "You must wait until I am dead to leave. What are you making such a fuss about?" After that, there was silence and no further effect. Several years later, he died. Neighbors saw his money flying out of the warehouse day and night, like butterflies. Soon after, all hundred rooms were burned to ashes in one go, and the Baofeng Temple, located several miles away, was also completely reduced to ashes within a few days. Although Zhang's descendants still existed, their livelihood was reduced to less than one percent of what it once was.

The Nine Beams Star Taboo The School of Yin-Yang has a taboo regarding the Nine Beams Star, which states that one should not violate its position, as doing so would immediately bring disaster. The common people feared this. At that time, the monks' hall at Baofeng Temple in Jing'an County was severely damaged and could no longer be used. Elder Jingxiang, a senior monk, wanted to renovate it. The monks collectively said that this year was the time when the Nine Beams Star was in residence, and they advised against acting without cause. However, Elder Jingxiang disagreed, saying, "Humans and spirits are one; both desire a place to settle. How can there be a place where humans cannot live but spirits can?" He then led the monks to recite scriptures and ask the gods to temporarily move to the Dharma Hall while the construction was underway. After the work was completed, they followed the previous procedure of reciting scriptures and welcoming the gods back to their original place. There were no other issues. This was in the year 1156 AD. By the year 1190 AD, the neighboring temple, Daziyuan, discussed renovating their monks' hall. The monks there also cited the Nine Beams Star taboo. The chief monk used the same method as Elder Jingxiang, praying and reciting scriptures to welcome the gods to move. However, within a year, several monks died one after another, and the hall was never completed. The local people, who were frivolous, began to imitate this behavior and criticized the two temples, using them as subjects of ridicule.

The General Administration of Ezhou and the Snake The General Administration of Ezhou was the former state capital. Later, it was close to the city. There was a garden at the foot of the city, and the garden had a large snake, several zhang long. In the middle of the Qian Dao era, Han, the General Administrator, wanted to build the Chu Lookout Pavilion in the northeast corner, but the foundation was not completed after several attempts. Someone said this place is where the snake lives. If a temple is built for it, it will be successful. Han followed this suggestion and built a small temple within dozens of steps, and the foundation was completed. The snake moved back and forth east and west. Or like a large well in the training ground. Or from the air well in the granary, hanging its head down to eat rice. It once shed its skin in the bamboo grove. A soldier found it and stored it in a cloth bag. He showed it to people at times. The shed skin was as wide and long as its body. There was one foot on the left side of its rib. The county resident Yang Ba rented the moat under the city to grow water chestnuts and lotus seeds. He tied reeds on the embankment to build a hut for his mother and himself to watch for thieves who steal. In the middle of the summer night, he heard a sound and his mother thought it was a thief. She went out to see it and saw the snake on the parapet wall with its head in the moat, raising its head to look at her. His mother was terrified and screamed. Yang Ba came and could only raise his hand to point at it before falling dead. Yang Ba was afraid and left. Afterward, the snake did not come out again. People suspected it had entered the Yangtze River.

### Translation There is a temple in the prison of Yihuang County, said to have been established to serve Xiao Xiangguo, though its origins are unknown. The people of the county claim that the deity often transforms into green toads, and the smaller ones are considered more valuable. If a toad is no larger than an inch, the county magistrate must recommend and summon it, and it may be promoted for excellent governance, ensuring that officials remain in their positions without being punished or dismissed. However, if a toad is as large as a foot, the opposite is true. During the Shaoxing era, toads appeared frequently, sometimes as large as a fan, a plate, or a large turtle. The county magistrate was dismissed within a year for reasons not to his liking, or he resigned due to personal loss. The next year, they suddenly appeared everywhere in the prison, halls, and gardens, numbering in the thousands. They were only about the size of a small coin, resembling frogs but with narrow, flat feet, slightly longer than usual, white feet, and a body color like green feathers. Each foot had five claws, and they could climb walls and trees up to several meters without falling. The entire city rejoiced, considering them auspicious signs. People competed to set up banquets and offerings at the temple to thank the gods, sharing sacrificial meat to celebrate. They reported to County Magistrate Li Yuanzuo, requesting a grand feast. Li Yuanzuo alone did not believe, scolding them away. By the next day, they had all disappeared. For three years, the entire region remained peaceful. Li Yuanzuo was recommended and eventually became a court attendant.

Ezhou Corpses In Ezhou, the land is narrow and the population dense, so there are few places for burial. Near the city walls, gaps are filled with overlapping corpses, and many coffins are placed on top of them, with soil from elsewhere brought to cover them. The poor, lacking the means, are left exposed for a time before being buried, which moves passersby to pity. In the eighth year of the Qindao era, someone reported this matter to the various offices. They collectively donated funds from the treasury to the Shengyuan Temple monks to prepare for cremation and burial. First, a notice was posted in the main streets, allowing relatives to identify the bodies and have them reburied in proper tombs. The names were written on the outside; if there were no known relatives, the remains were cremated and the bones thrown into the river. The number was too great to count. Among the corpses, one skeleton was unique in that its bones were still connected, and its appearance was unlike the others. The monk mentioned the Southern Jinsha Lock Case and considered it a segment of a great result. Soon, another body was found among the accumulated corpses, a young girl of about 14 or 15 years old. She was wearing a blue dress and a red robe, her face painted, and she appeared as if alive. She was carried to the temple gate, and for three days, people from far and wide came to closely examine her, but no one knew her. At that time, it was the hot autumn, but her appearance did not change at all. The crowd was amazed and respectful, so they prepared two coffins, changed her clothes, and reburied her, building a stupa over her grave and marking it. But there was no record in the maps or annals, fearing that it would be forgotten over time, which is a pity.

During the middle of the Song Dynasty, Hui Yancheng Anxing, a local official from Yuleping, was the governor of Quanjiao County, which was part of Xuzhou Prefecture. He handled a case involving the trial and execution of a condemned prisoner in Quanjiao County. Twenty miles outside the county, there was a secluded mountain hermitage where only woodcutters and farmers would visit. A monk lived there alone, hiring a villager to provide firewood and guard the hermitage. The monk kept a very tame cat that stayed close to him during the day and slept under his bed at night. He also had a particularly lovable dog, a breed known as a "lion dog," which was highly prized for its loyalty. One day, the monk sent the villager to buy salt, but he did not return before dusk. Taking advantage of the monk's absence, a bandit attacked the hermitage, killed the monk, and stole his begging bowl and bag of belongings. The bandit then stayed outside the hermitage overnight. The next day, as the bandit entered the county, the dog secretly followed him. Whenever the bandit encountered groups of people, the dog would rush forward and bark at him, continuing to chase and bark as they entered the county market. Many people in the market recognized the dog from the hermitage and were surprised by its unusual behavior. They all questioned the bandit, saying, "The dog seems to hate you. Did you commit a crime at the hermitage?" The bandit, though he tried to defend himself, lowered his head in fear and admitted to the crime. He was then taken back to the hermitage, where the monk had already died. It was the height of summer, and the cat was sleeping beside the monk's body, so the mice had not disturbed him. The bandit was arrested and brought to trial, but he could not deny the charges and was eventually executed. The dog's loyalty was similar to that of a dog recorded in earlier accounts from the household of Li, a high official in Wuxi. All living beings, no matter how small, have a Buddha-nature, and this story is another example of the truth of this belief.

**Translation:** Wang Yu, the Marshal of Jiangdong, was the son of the late Qingjun Circuit Governor Wei Ding Gong De. By nature, he was severe and harsh, completely unaware of moral principles. He lived in Jiankang and once invited the Taoist priest Yan Zhen to his home to refine gold. Wang Yu became angry at Yan Zhen's unrestrained and irrational behavior and made many demands. He ordered his attendants to get Yan Zhen drunk until he was completely drunk, then struck his head with an iron mallet and killed him. Maidservants and concubines who failed to please him were stripped of their clothes, tied to trees, and whipped with willow branches from back to heel, often numbering in the hundreds. Sometimes he placed thin boards on both cheeks and beat them, or beat his toes until blood dripped down. He often placed them in chicken coops and pressed heavy stones on them. In summer, he would heat charcoal beside them; in winter, he would pour cold water on them. All of them were buried in the garden. His wife Zheng died, and his concubine He Yan Yan continued his evil deeds, pretending to be the principal wife and receiving a title. In September of the fifth year of Shaoxi, his concubine Li was beaten and lay exhausted. Wang Yu dragged her to the back courtyard and locked the door. Li's breath was barely perceptible. Thinking that this family had killed many people, why would there be no consequences, she suddenly felt as if she met someone. The door suddenly opened wide, and before dawn, she left in pain, thinking that the master would see her and kill her. By the time she passed through the inner hall and the three outer gates, there was no one there. She then ran back to her home. Wang Yu just realized and quickly sent soldiers to chase and capture her. Li's father brought his daughter to the government office. At that time, the acting governor was Zhao Congshan, who listened to her story and called the local officials to investigate. They found out that two nights ago, He Yan Yan had killed a maid by hand, and it was not yet concealed. So they ordered the officials to guard Wang Yu and arrested He Yan Yan to be interrogated. They found all the bodies and arrested two people, then reported the case. In April of the first year of Qianyuan, the emperor decreed to strip Wang Yu of his rank and exile him to Zhucheng. He Yan Yan was beaten to death in the market. Wang Yu died in Wangan Army.

Xu Qiansan, the son of Xu Chaofeng from the north of Huzhou city, was a man of sincere and simple nature from his childhood. As he grew older, he noticed that some of his relatives were afflicted by mischievous spirits. He then dedicated himself to Daoism and practiced the "Heavenly Heart Summoning Method" to treat and save people, achieving miraculous results that were beyond the ordinary. He did not seek any requests or demands and handled all rituals himself, using incense, candles, and paper money without regard to the wealth or status of those he helped. He responded promptly to resolve matters and never went to the homes of the afflicted to confront them directly. Instead, he instructed them to prepare written complaints, summoned the gods, and waited for the spirits to appear. After interrogating and making them confess, he would return the resolution and implement it. His efforts to help others accumulated over many years. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (1174-1189), a commoner named Zhang, who was an old man, had a daughter who was possessed by a malevolent spirit. He invited several Daoists to drive it away, using chains, handcuffs, and whips, but the spirit showed no fear and would not be controlled. The spirit only insulted and scolded them, and the old man went to Xu Qiansan and expressed his earnest request. Xu deceived him and said, "Old man, go and do not tell others about this." The old man did not understand, but his neighbor, who was also afflicted by a spirit, came to seek help. Xu knew that the neighbor lived next to Zhang and directly captured the spirit and brought it to the altar. He interrogated it and said, "You appeared as a ghost because of lack of food. You must know the beginning and end of the spirits in the Zhang family. Why not tell me? I can record your merit to atone for your sins and let you go." The spirit replied, "I am a spirit of a drill. That is why I am not afraid of being beaten." Xu then called the old man Zhang and instructed him to prepare shovels, picks, charcoal, and vinegar. He gave him a talisman and told him to go to his room and dig the ground. As soon as he found the object, he was to burn it with a blazing fire and pour vinegar over it. The old man had not yet reached the door when his daughter began to cry and beg for mercy, her tears flowing profusely. Indeed, they found a drill, burned and melted it as Xu had said, and the girl soon recovered.

### Pujing Jingshan Three Wonders The Pujing Temple in Wuzhen, Huzhou, was originally the burial site of the Shen family during the Liang Dynasty. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang, Shen Rongwen, a prominent figure, was highly esteemed. Each spring, he would come to pay respects at the burial site. Upon his return, the emperor would always send Crown Prince Xiao Tong to welcome him. At the outskirts of the city, a pavilion was built for him. Feeling uneasy, Shen Rongwen decided to relocate his burial to Jinling (modern-day Nanjing) and converted the burial grounds into a temple. Crown Prince Xiao Tong also built a pavilion there, which became known as the Mìyìn Temple. Afterward, both temples venerated Shen Rongwen as the local deity. During the Xuanhe period, the Pujing Temple suffered a fire. The abbot, Shang Kui, decided to remake the statue of the deity and sought a skilled artisan. A Taoist monk from Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing) arrived, claiming to be highly skilled in this art. He requested that no one observe the work, and Shang Kui agreed. Within a few days, the work was completed, and the monk bowed and left, leaving everyone amazed. When they entered the temple to inspect the work, they found a poem on the wall that read: "Once served as a chancellor of the Liang Dynasty, Now is the deity of Pujing Temple. A thousand years of noble spirit, Protected by monks generation after generation. All agreed it was Shen Rongwen without doubt." Shang Kui passed on the teachings to Huibin, and during the Jianyan period, the monk Dao Fa from the Lüjiao Temple in Chenliu brought the tooth relic of the Buddha to the town, which was said to date back to the Three Dynasties.

The relic was placed in a jade box, then in containers of gold and silver, and finally sealed in a wooden box for protection. Master Bin, accompanied by monks and laypeople, prayed fervently for relics, but after seven days, there was no response, and everyone grew disheartened. Master Bin, however, continued to pray with greater intensity, raising his voice and declaring, "If there is no sign tonight, I shall offer my life." That night, at the third watch, a clear and resonant sound was heard in a silver basin, and relics began to emerge, numbering over thirty. They shone in five radiant colors, half visible and half hidden within the box, seemingly infinite. They were quickly collected and stored in a foreign glass bottle. Previously, a fisherman from Taihu Lake had discovered a seven-story sandalwood pagoda in a pond, two feet tall, intricately carved with Buddha statues. A monk purchased it. That night, the monk dreamed of a divine being who said, "A fragment of the Buddha's body is now in Wuzhen. You should offer this pagoda to enshrine it properly." The next morning, the monk brought the pagoda, and Master Bin placed the bottle of relics inside. The size and depth of the pagoda matched perfectly, and thus he built a memorial tower, which he named "Fangguanzi" (Guardian of the Relics). The eastern part of the tower was inscribed with a record of the event. Master Bin then passed on the Dharma to Master Miaoxin of Jing Mountain Temple, a disciple of the Zen master Tang Juewen. In the year Renwu of the Shaoxing era, Master Bin was staying at the guesthouse of the temple, intending to travel south. He dreamt of two imposing men in ancient attire, who arranged themselves in a procession.

A respectful figure entered, performing elaborate rituals with great reverence, and said, "In the past, the Zen Master Jue Wen came here. The guardian spirits of the seventy-two villages heard the Dharma, took great vows, and abstained from meat and alcohol to protect the orthodox teachings. The Zen Master prophesied that he would return in five hundred years to revive this mountain. Now, the time has come. Where does the Zen Master wish to go?" The Zen Master was aware of the prophecy but still doubted it. For several nights, he saw dreams urging him to stay, pleading earnestly. He said to them, "This temple is in ruins, and the monastic community is poorly supplied. How can I possibly revive it? Who are you two figures, and why are you so serious?" They replied, "The guardian of the temple and the benefactor will support the teacher; this is not something to worry about." The Zen Master still did not believe it. The senior monk, who was the abbot of the temple, also dreamed of a spirit urging him to stay. Reluctantly, the Zen Master decided to stay temporarily for the summer. In June, the senior monk passed away. Coincidentally, Zhang Gongzhuang, a high-ranking official, invited the temple to become a merit-making institute and invited the Zen Master to preside over it. For thirty years, the temple was revived, and everything that had been neglected was restored, looking entirely new. It was exactly five hundred years since the Zen Master Jue Wen first established the temple. Huibin, of the surname Shen, was the grandson of Zhaoyao, a former official. The Zen Master was his nephew. Across three generations, they all felt a rare and profound virtuous connection.

**The Strange Tales of the Yi Family, Volume Nine** **The Four Treasuries, Officially Approved** **The Concubine of Magistrate Zhao** A poor man from潭州 died, and his wife, taking her two daughters, remarried. When the daughters grew up, she sold them both as concubines. The younger one was taken into the household of Zhao, a county magistrate in湘隂. After a year, she was not allowed to return home. Her stepfather died, and her mother, through the court, reclaimed her. While she was still in Zhao's household, she was privately involved with Zhao's son. Even after she was dismissed, he continued to pursue her. Her mother rented a room in a guesthouse in the city but could not pay the rental fee. The owner of the guesthouse drove her out, and she moved to a store on East Street. Zhao's son did not come to see her again. The daughter became pregnant, and Zhao's wife asked her, "You have no husband, how can you be pregnant?" She replied, "I was sold by my stepfather, and I became a witch, so my belly is swollen, not from pregnancy." After several months, her belly grew even larger. Her mother, unable to pay the rental fee, went to the court to accuse her and sue her.

### Translation This man committed incest with his daughter. The government apprehended her and detained her in the Right Judicial Office, where she endured beatings for more than a month but refused to admit it. However, because she was pregnant, she could not be interrogated. One day at noon, the prisoners were asleep, and she was also asleep. Suddenly, she woke up to find a large white snake wrapped around her abdomen three times, with its head and tail raised and facing each other. She only cried out for help. The people in the room looked at each other in terror. The attendant old woman went out and told the official, who examined it and believed her. He then ran to report to the judicial official, who put on formal attire, burned incense and paper, and worshiped and prayed. The snake loosened its grip, climbed the wall and roof, and then disappeared. Soon after, she gave birth to a boy named Dao Ben Shi, the son of Zhao Sheng. Yi was released. The mother was beaten, but the daughter was exempted because of childbirth. This occurred during the sixth month of the leap year in the sixteenth year of the Chunxi era. --- Wang, the Minister of Works, sent a name paper to visit. Chen Yuan from Nancheng, style name Changqing, was recommended again in the tenth year of the Chunxi era and took the provincial examination, staying at a lodging near the examination hall. He dreamed of an official calling out, "Wang, the Minister of Works, has sent a name paper to visit." When he woke up, he found it very suspicious. Three days later, Wang was appointed as the chief examiner, and Chen Yuan then passed the examination. --- Chen Yuan, a scholar from Jinxi, caught fish from the pond to make fish paste. He cut open the belly and found eggs filling the basin. He placed the basin in the room under the eaves. At midnight, he heard a sound like chirping inside the basin. He thought it was mice gnawing and got up to look, but the noise became even louder. When he knocked on the basin, it stopped, but soon started again.
