The next day, he told people, and some advised him to throw it into the stream. His wife did not listen and cooked and ate it all. The following spring, one night, Chen Yuan dreamed of a woman in black clothes asking for mercy. When he woke up, his wife's family sent a servant with a bamboo basket containing a large turtle as a gift. He immediately remembered the dream and went to the riverbank to release it, regretting that his wife could not do the same. This occurred during the sixth month of the leap year in the sixteenth year of the Chunxi era.

Guilin Soldiers In the sixteenth year of the Chunxi era, the governor of Guilin, Ying Meng Ming Zhong Shi, sent two soldiers and carpenters to cut wood in the mountains of Guilin. They stayed overnight at a civilian's house. One of the soldiers had a dream in which a deity held a knife and cut out his kidneys. In his dream, he called out in pain, and the other soldiers also woke up, asking what had happened. They quickly lit a torch and looked, and they saw that both of his kidneys had fallen to the floor, with blood flowing like a stream. It was unclear what had caused this. The civilian and the nearby soldiers helped him back to the city and reported the incident to the governor. Ying Meng Ming Zhong Shi doubted and did not believe it, so he sent the soldier to jail for investigation. The temporary judge, Liang Zhou, who was the magistrate of Xiu Ren, was sent to investigate the soldiers. The injured soldier described the appearance of the wound, which looked exactly like that of a eunuch. Liang Zhou's Academy Liang Zhou, a temporary judge from Tanzhou, lived in the suburbs near the Polaris Gate, renting a vacant house in front of the General's Temple where the Lu family lived. He established an academy for his thirty students. One night, Liang Zhou returned home, and seven of his students were sitting in the courtyard. They had not yet lit the candles when they saw a figure with long hair coming out of the bathhouse. They initially thought that Xu Guo Liang, a fellow student, was playing a prank, but it turned out not to be him. Xu Guo Liang ran to the top of the building, and the other students followed. They were not hit by the ghost, and a scholar from Shaoyang named Liu Kai Dao began to explain the situation. He said that the ghost of the Wooden Boat King would not cause trouble and that the ghost continued to strike them for a while. The students reached the top of the building and saw a dish of fatty meat and chicken heads.

A certain object was on the table, and everyone was confused but unaware of it. They all ate two things, and soon after, they vomited all over the board. Only Guo Liang, who was inside the bed canopy, did not suffer from the attack. Faintly, he saw the others eating and vomiting out chicken heads. Each breaking one, they each rubbed and stretched it until it was more than a foot long, still amazed, saying, "This dried meat is fragrant." A ghost hand as large as an umbrella suddenly disappeared into a crack in the wall. When the matter was settled, they only then went to bed. In the morning, when they looked at what they had vomited, there was no trace left. They competed to sharpen their white blades in preparation. A few days later, while reading under the lamp, he again saw a large hand entering through the window lattice, stabbing with a knife, missing, and fleeing. Therefore, all sorts of strange things gathered, but they all agreed not to speak to the teacher. He stayed there for seven months. Until the official position was abolished, only then did he report it. One Bright Master Duan Fang, courtesy name Xianqing, from Jianning, took the provincial examination during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong in the year Bingxu. Although elderly, he ranked first in the main text but lost one point for a citation error in the *Mencius* and was thus disqualified. On his second attempt, he met Huang Rixin of Linchuan by chance on the road to Xinzhou. Huang said that he had once sought divine assistance at the Guangyue Wang Temple in Shaowu and dreamed of a divine being writing characters. The characters said, "One month as a Master." Wasn't this the rank he would hold in the future? Huang replied that passing the exams would certainly not lead to such a low rank. That year, he failed the examination. Six years later, he ranked thirteenth in the provincial examination but could not become a teacher. He was instead appointed as a local official in a neighboring state and returned home. By then, he was already old, and among his attendants, those who pitied his poverty sent a letter to Lisi, allowing him to act as the Master of Songxi. Upon arriving at the county, he stayed there for one month and just wanted to submit a memorial criticizing the flaws of the government bureau. Both the county magistrate and the military officer refused, and on the same day, he resigned and returned home. Before he could, he died.

**Huang the Scholar and His Prayers for a Son** Huang, a scholar from Xinghua, had no son in Xin Zhou. He brought his wife, Shi, and his concubine to a Buddhist temple to pray in the Hall of Arhats. That night, he dreamed that he and his wife and concubine were counting the seats of the arhats together, smiling at each other. Suddenly, the arhats spoke and said, "Three years before and three years after." When he woke up, he could still remember the dream vividly. His wife and concubine also felt the same dream but did not understand its meaning. They only thought that it meant they would each have a child in three years. As predicted, they all had sons in the same year. After Huang's death, Shi taught her son, who was named Tao, to read diligently. Tao became a prominent scholar. **Dong Yaofu** Dong Yaofu, whose name was originally Dan, was going to the prefecture to take the spring examination. He dreamed that someone showed him a scroll of writing with the title "Tai Xue Rao Yaofu" (Dong Yaofu of the Imperial Academy). When he woke up, he thought that his name was not similar to the one in the dream, so he changed his name. Later, he passed the examination and was admitted to the Imperial Academy. On the day before the autumn examination, he suddenly suffered from a severe headache and could not bear it. He sighed, "I must die." The night before, a fellow student in the same dormitory dreamed that someone came to tell him, "Dong Yaofu will win first place." The fellow student saw that Dong's exam paper had more than 500 annotations and reported it to Dong. Dong thanked him, saying, "I am grateful for your kindness, but I don't know if I will die today. How can I care about my success or failure?" The fellow student secretly submitted the paper for him. The next day, Dong forced himself to write with a handkerchief covering his head, and he passed the examination. ---

By noon, he suddenly woke up refreshed, read the text, laughed, and completely erased it, rewriting hundreds of words before finally achieving first place. After being promoted to the upper class, a neighbor student dreamed of the school gate wide open, and servants came shouting, saying, "Liu recites poetry to be released from civil service." Liu, hearing this, said, "Mr. Shao's poem has the line 'Yao Fu is not fond of reciting poetry.' This must be for me." Thus, he was granted two honors for his degree. When Liu was a child, he dreamed of a sign on a board saying, "Liu's house for the Censorate, inscribed by his family on the pillar." Therefore, he did not set high expectations for himself, progressing from being a doctoral student at the Imperial Academy to an assistant in Yuzhang. Later, he suddenly fell gravely ill, and family members gathered and wept. Liu said, "I have long known this would be the end. Does this not match what was inscribed on the pillar?" On his deathbed, he wrote several papers about family matters and composed a verse saying, "Not reaching the clear spring and white stones, instead, coming to the city and walking in noisy confusion. Last night, a stream of silver Milky Way water flowed towards the silver tablet and jade mirror." For he had never engaged in Zen practice in his life.

Liu Wei Held Office Liu Wei and Huang Ze were both scholars from the Huang family. During the year Gengzi of the Qian Dao era (1168), Huang Ze passed the local examination and Liu Wei, along with Huang Ze, took the provincial examination in 1169. They traveled to the provincial capital together. A man named Xie Ji from Shaowu, who was an expert in geography and feng shui, once visited Linchuan and met the two Huang brothers. At the time, he was staying at the home of Zhang De Yong. One night, Zhang had a dream in which someone told him, "Huang Ze will pass the imperial examinations, and Liu Wei will hold office." Upon waking, Zhang shared this dream with the Huang brothers. Liu Wei asked, "Will Liu Wei be granted a special favor in the imperial examinations?" Zhang replied, "Yes, but that means he will not be ranked among the top candidates. That's why the dream only mentions holding office." Later, Huang Ze indeed passed the imperial examinations, and Liu Wei was granted a special favor to enter the third rank and was appointed to the Imperial Academy. Xie Ji was delighted that his dream had come true and spoke of it when he later visited Fuzhou, much to everyone's amusement. Liu Wei was then appointed as the magistrate of Fenning County. Just before he was to take up his post, he encountered the death of his mother and had to return home. He was subsequently reassigned to the wine office in Anren, Hengzhou. While waiting for his appointment, one morning he opened the door to find an owl flying into the room and perching on a screen behind his main seat. He quickly tried to drive it away, but it had already disappeared. At that time, Liu Wei was ill, and he was worried about this omen. However, he soon recovered, and the wine office sent a messenger to inform him that the current official had died. Liu Wei thought, "The owl's flight must have been a bad omen for that person." He immediately set out for Anren and arrived after one year. Suddenly, he recited a formal resignation poem and died that very night without any illness. Meanwhile, Liu Wei was appointed as the household registrar of Shaoguan, where he had to wait six years for a vacancy. During this time, he was staying in the same inn as the new governor of Lin'an. The innkeeper told him that the household registrar position was originally vacant, and the previous official had left due to illness. After the new official took over, the previous one had died. Liu Wei accepted the appointment and served for five years before being reassigned to the county of Wuxian in Qianling. He served there for another five years. Once, he acted as the deputy county magistrate and later as the county magistrate. Upon returning home, he bought land and retired to the capital, where he lived until he was nearly eighty years old. Discussions about Xie Ji's dream suggest that Huang Ze's success was limited to passing the imperial examinations, while Liu Wei's career began with holding office. Huang Wei, a relative of Liu Wei, provided these details in great detail.

Jade Sister Seeks Wine A man from the Ye family in Jianchang had many concubines, including one named Wáng Jie, who died without a son. Although she was buried in the family tomb, she was not included in the annual rituals of offering food and drink. In the year 1179 of the Chunxi era, Ye traveled from Shaoguan to the imperial court, accompanied by two concubines. All of them dreamed that Wáng Jie came to them, seeking wine, and said in a solemn voice, "After my death, my spirit has nowhere to return. I wish to be remembered by the official, but he is far away. You two, please speak on my behalf." Upon waking, they told their master, who was also deeply moved. When they returned home, they ordered a ritual to be performed at her tomb and set up an altar on the 15th day of the 7th lunar month. Dream of a Name Zhang Xu, the governor of the new city of Jianchang, had a dream in early summer of 1186 during the Chunxi era. He dreamed of visiting the garden of the late Ye Zhaoshou, a respected ancestor, and encountered an elderly man with a long white beard. The man said, "This autumn, you should take the provincial examination. Just use the name of Ye Zhaoshou, and you will succeed." Zhang woke up and found the dream strange. He thought, "As a junior, how could I dare to use the name of an elder?" But secretly, he was delighted and feared he might forget. He decided to use only the surname Ye. That year, he ranked second in the provincial examination and paid his respects to Ye Zhaoshou, telling him about his dream as a way of expressing gratitude. These two events were reported to Ye by Wang Kui, a local scholar.

### Translation A Small Essay on the Five Classics in Wu In the Southern Song Dynasty, during the year 1155, Wu Zhen, a man from the Wu region, went to take the local examination. His brother, who lived in the same town, had a dream on the night of the first venue of the examination. In the dream, Kemu returned and said that the fourth sentence of his small essay on the Five Classics mentioned "the sacred ones have created," which he found very pleasing. He believed that this would ensure his success in the examination. Upon waking, he wrote this down on the wall. When Kemu returned from the examination, his brother hurriedly asked, "Did you use the phrase 'the sacred ones have created' in your small essay?" Kemu was surprised because he had not shown his essay to anyone. His brother knew about the phrase because he had seen it written on the wall. Soon after, Kemu was pre-selected and passed the examination ten years later. A House Rented by Chen Chen Jie, a man from Jianchang, had a very prosperous livelihood. He decided to renovate the entrance of his house, but he was deceived by the carpenter, who gradually made the renovation more extravagant. The renovation extended from the entrance to the main hall, and everything was completely rebuilt, becoming increasingly elaborate, even down to household items. Over several years, the house became beautifully and neatly renovated. However, the fertile and high-quality land was completely exhausted, leaving nothing behind. He began working with the carpenter in the spring of 1166. His wife, Lady Cai, had a dream in which a man told her, "Nie will pass the exam." She asked, "What does it have to do with me if someone else passes the exam?" The man did not respond further but placed two thousand coins on the ground and left. Lady Cai woke up and told Chen Jie about the dream. Nie was a fellow townsman who was then going to take the provincial examination. Ten years after passing the exam, Chen Jie's family became even poorer, and Lady Cai died, leaving them with not a single coin to sustain them. They then sold the house to Nie and received exactly two thousand and some coins. Chen Jie regretted it too late and later told others about this story.

Yang Shouzi As recorded in Tang dynasty stories, a fisherman named Zhang Huizi from Wu Prefecture caught a giant fish in Taihu Lake. On the fish's belly was a red-inscribed message: "Nine ascents of the Dragon Gate Mountain, three drinks of Taihu Lake water, ultimately fails to become a dragon, destiny entrusted to Zhang Huizi." A similar event occurred recently in Jianchang. In the eighth year of the Chongxi era (1181 CE), during the spring, Nancheng County experienced prolonged rain, causing river overflow. Fishermen set nets by the riverbanks. Before this, the prefecture had no large fish; the largest caught in the river did not exceed one or two catties, and all others were pond-raised. This year, a commoner named Yang Shouzi set nets in the tributary of Zhang Mountain. When he lifted the net, he felt it was too heavy, and alone could not pull it in. He was nearly drowned in the struggle and called out for help. Three fellow fishermen assisted him, and they found a fish of enormous size leaping in the net. They slowly dragged it to the shore and, after many attempts, killed it. The fish weighed a hundred catties. Upon close inspection, there were faint red letters on its forehead. The fishermen, all rural peasants, could not recognize them. A scholar came and interpreted it: "Three times entered the tidal gate, four times encountered a flood, finally met Yang Shouzi." The village has many Yangs, and the one who caught this fish was indeed Yang Shouzi.

Tchen Ruixun Tchen Ruixun, courtesy name Bohe, was from Nancheng. His father, Su Yi, had once exchanged poems with Huang Tingjian, and his name appeared in Huang's collection. Although Tchen Ruixun did not read books, he was known for his strong and fiery temperament, often drinking and using his temper without fear. He had a younger sister who married into a distant family, the grandson of He Tuntian. One day, Tchen Ruixun visited his sister's home and saw a spacious building. He wanted to stay overnight, but his sister warned him, saying, "There are many ghosts in this place. Even the people of the He family dare not enter. Brother, do not bring disaster upon yourself." She then cleaned the study room and invited him to stay. However, Tchen Ruixun refused to listen and sent a servant to keep watch. His sister was very worried but could not dissuade him. At midnight, a woman dressed elegantly and smiling came slowly from another part of the house. Tchen Ruixun knew she was a ghost and pretended to be deeply asleep. The ghost approached the bed, but Tchen Ruixun, who had not been talking, pushed her thigh and moved his foot downward. When she turned around quickly and stood up, Tchen Ruixun shouted at her. The ghost disappeared immediately. Tchen Ruixun then returned to his bed, tidied his bedding, and settled his pillow. By morning, everything was silent. His sister asked him what happened, and he explained in detail. Those who heard the story admired his courage. After this incident, the place remained undisturbed for ten days, and no one dared to sleep there again.

During the seventeenth year of the Shaoxing era in July, in the Jianchang Army's jurisdiction in Ruoling, a scholar named Fu Zongdao was hosting a banquet and inviting guests. Just as he was about to sit down, he heard the sound of gongs resounding. Looking afar, he saw a group of bandits, numbering dozens. He then urgently called for strong servants to prepare for defense, while the women climbed mountains. The bandits entered the house, saw the wine and food, and indulged in eating and drinking. They stole forty strings of cash and then left. The village guards, hearing of the bandits' arrival, all came with spears. The bandits were already drunk, and half of what they had stolen was taken by others. In the nearby village of Houping, there was a servant of the two fields of Fu Quanmei who fled and was killed by the bandits. His spirit every night came to the master's door, calling out in anger and resentment. Fu Quanmei's father was very angry, opening the door and loudly scolding him, saying, "You took advantage of the bandits' wealth to the point of losing your life. Why did you interfere with the master's affairs and frighten people like this?" Remembering the servant's years of hard work, he did not want to punish him. Instead, he decided to draw his image in the nearby temple to receive incense and wait for the time to be reborn. He advised the servant's spirit to leave quickly. Afterward, the sound of the ghostly calls gradually became distant, and after the painting was completed, it became silent.

**Translation:** **The Strange Tales of the Isles, Volume Ten, Part B** <Subclass: Novels, Category: Strange Tales, Title: The Strange Tales of the Isles> Approved by the Imperial Library The Strange Tales of the Isles, Volume One, Part C By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty **The Tiger Spirit at Yangtai** From Ezhou to Xiangyang, a distance of seven hundred li, the road was desolate and devoid of human habitation after the turmoil. Only the stationed troops were present, with a relay station for fast horse communication every twenty li, and a lodging place for soldiers and officials every seventy to eighty li. Scholars and officials also stayed there when passing through. In the sixth year of the Qinding era, Jiang Tongzu, an official of the Huguang General Administration, traveled from Ezhou to Xiangyang via Hanchuan, reaching Yangtai Relay Station. At night, he was disturbed by mosquitoes and could not sleep. He instructed his attendants to rise at the first crow of the rooster. The station official informed him, "This place is extremely quiet and dangerous, with many fierce tigers. Some of these tigers are known to harm people. Recently, a military officer was attacked while riding his horse before dawn, and both the officer and his horse were devoured. It is best to wait until it is light enough to see clearly before continuing your journey." Jiang followed this advice. On his return journey, he passed through Jingzhou and planned to stay overnight there. He traveled with three attendants and two soldiers, starting early in the morning. As they rode, both men and horses seemed to retreat in fear. Suddenly, they saw a yellow object rushing through the grass, which caused them to be extremely frightened. As they approached, it turned out to be a large deer, as big as a water buffalo, which was still frightening.

Suddenly, halfway through the journey, a woman appeared in front of my horse. She was around forty or fifty years old, wearing a single-tail bun, with a pale green face and no makeup. Her eyes were extremely red and looked very threatening. She wore a brown shirt, a blue skirt, and dragged a straw shoe, holding a small wild cat, moving back and forth, following and not stopping. When I relaxed my burden, I no longer saw the river in the middle. I wondered if this could not be what is called a tiger spirit. I kept it secret and did not tell anyone. At dawn, I wanted to go east, and the innkeeper said, "Yesterday, I found two cubs on the side of the road that could not yet move. Do you want them, sir? I am willing to offer them." I laughed and said, "Should I keep tigers and leave myself with trouble?" I refused to take them. After staying for two more nights, I followed the Han River to cross the Yangtze River. Several people were traveling together, and that woman was among them. Her appearance and clothing were exactly the same as before. I became even more afraid, sitting in a sedan chair, lowering the curtain, and not daring to look directly at her. I returned to my home after about a month. I heard the sound of gongs and drums outside, and thousands of common people gathered around. It seemed like a procession escorting a dangerous criminal. When I asked why, they all said she was from the South Market.

One after another, many pigs and children were lost from the household. Despite searching for thieves, no evidence was found. A woman alone stayed at a small inn for three weeks. She had never cooked and had no money in her purse, but she carefully took care of a cat. Sometimes, the cat's mouth would be stained with fur and blood, leading the woman to suspect that it must be a monster. Therefore, she sued the authorities. Now, the woman was arrested and sent to the prefecture. She appeared calm and composed, showing no fear. Once in the prefecture, Prefect Li Shouqing, a high-ranking official, was sent to take her statement. The woman could write with a brush and said, "My surname is Tu, and I am the daughter of a scholar-official." Her father once served as the magistrate of Yuan'an County. Her marriage was not to her liking, and he had since died, leaving her without heirs. She lived alone and childless, barely surviving as a guest in the market, where she was bullied by bad young men. This turned into a strange case of injustice and suffering, and she wished the official to take charge. Prefect Li Shouqing could not bring himself to investigate thoroughly and temporarily ordered a warning and a statement of responsibility. The woman was expelled from the prefecture and then entered the tea mountains of Xianning, where she lived among the tea-picking households for a long time. She also got into fights over food with a dog, which led to her being seen, beaten, and driven away. Later, she disappeared without a trace.

Deer Enclosure in Jing Shan Jiang Tongzu passed through Jingzhou and arrived at Jing Shan in the evening, reaching a village post station. The station attendant said that there was a deer enclosure ahead. They immediately went out to see it. Stretching as far as the eye could see, it covered several miles, with countless large deer forming a circle around, with their horns facing outward, forming several layers. Two fawns and a doe were in the center, leaping and playing energetically. The nearby fields were all trampled, and the crops were destroyed, leaving everything barren. Hunters gathered around, saying it was dangerous to approach. Those who approached were often trampled and killed. They only left the next morning. Hunters with bows, arrows, spears, and halberds followed them, waiting until the larger ones moved ahead slightly before daring to shoot. The younger and weaker ones were also captured by others. Musicians Under Wang Xuan's Command In the early years of the Shaoxing era, Yue Shaobao opened a military district in Jingzhou and Xiangyang. At that time, the villages were particularly desolate, and tigers and wolves were rampant. Even when troops were organized, they were still attacked. A scholar said that wild animals are afraid of music sounds like flutes and drums and will retreat. He adopted this idea. In the middle years of the Qian Dao era, Wang Xuan served as a deputy commander, traveling from Xiangyang to Ezhou. On the way through Jingzhou and Ying Prefectures, he was accompanied by hundreds of horses, and the sun was still near the horizon. Various musical instruments were played. A messenger reported that a musician playing the bili was taken by a tiger from among the crowd. Another messenger reported that a musician playing the flute was also taken. The place was not far from the previous night's inn. They hurried to ride their horses to the scene and dismounted, waiting for a long time. The bili player was pale and could only speak after a short while. He said he was first caught by the tiger and placed in a den. The tiger returned to take another flute player and ate him. It seemed the tiger was full and did not cause further harm, but it played with the two young ones. He suddenly remembered the instrument in his belt and quickly took it out, blowing it loudly. The large tiger was startled and did not have time to carry its young, running away without looking back. They watched until it was out of sight before returning safely. He narrowly escaped being eaten by the tiger. At that time, Lü Yansheng was governing Xiangyang.

The Inscriptions on the Towers of Yudu County South of the county administration in Yudu County are two towers: the front one is called the Drum Tower, and the back one is called the Imperial Edict Tower. The inscription on the back tower was written by the county magistrate Du Shidan during the early years of the Qian Dao era. On the left side of the character for "edict," there was a high bee nest, which the locals considered a good omen. They believed that the county would produce a noble person or a scholar who would achieve high ranks in the imperial examinations. At that time, Zhao Zhi was living in the county and was preparing to take the provincial examination. Soon after, he was announced as the top-ranked scholar in the grand court. Thirty years later, during the year 1194 of the Shao Xi era, another bee nest appeared on the character for "strength." The people were even more delighted, and Zhao Zhi eventually became a chancellor. The following spring, the bee nest was accidentally knocked down, and within a month, Zhao Zhi resigned and returned home. Were these three occurrences coincidental? They were indeed strange in this manner.

### Final Translation **Tomb of Zhū Zhōngjìng Gōng** Zhū Zhōngjìng Gōng, a native of Cai, settled in Huzhou after crossing the river and was buried at the foot of Miao Xi Mountain. Several years later, a fortune teller passed by and sighed, saying that the mountain's terrain was very auspicious, but it was too far from water, and the auspicious energy was not concentrated. Although the descendants were numerous and prosperous, they might not be able to achieve success through the imperial examinations. All of Zhū Zhōngjìng Gōng's sons knew this, but they had no time to move. Later, those who died were buried according to the ancestral order. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (Qián Dào era), Zhū Zhōngjìng Gōng's second son, the official Xià Qīng, and his eldest son Yì, following the will of the deceased, abandoned the ancestral tomb and sought a new place, choosing only water as the primary criterion. Despite the crowd's advice to stop, they did not accept it and ultimately followed their own wishes, finding a grave site in front of a clear stream. Twenty years after the burial, Xià Qīng's youngest son Qiāo and Yì's son Chái both achieved the rank of Jinshi in the year Ding Wei. Soon after, Chái's younger brother Yǎn and Jiǎ followed suit, and the achievements continued unabated. **The Village Merchant of Jiangling** A family in Jiangling had been engaged in the business of raising pigs for generations. A village merchant lived fifty miles away and frequently traded with them. The family's eldest son once carried a silver note worth a thousand coins and a servant to trade in the countryside. After a night away, he did not return, and the situation gradually worsened over several months. Many young men from the market and defense areas, carrying money, indulged in drinking, gambling, and wandering, squandering their fathers' money and unable to return home. Some slowly drifted to other places. The family suspected their son but did not deeply worry about it. One winter, when the village merchant finished his farming, he led a pig past the family's door and stayed for a short rest. Another servant saw the rope he was using and was surprised, saying, "This is the five-foot rope that my eldest son made himself. How did it end up in your hand?" The five-foot rope was a specific length used for binding pigs.

The locals call the ropes and lines used for pulling livestock by a certain name. When the peddler heard this, his face turned pale, and he claimed that he had picked it up yesterday on a certain road. Who doesn't have this thing? Why be mistaken? I reported it to the master, who forcibly took the peddler along. On the way, the peddler pointed to something and drew pictures. We heard an unusual stench of rotting bodies in the dense forest and covered our noses to look. There, we saw the bodies of his son and servant lying in a collapsed ditch. Even though exposed to the heat of summer, the dry bones were undamaged and slightly not eaten by wolves or foxes, clearly recognizable. We then took the peddler back, and he finally admitted that he saw him carrying something, got drunk, and killed him. It has been a long time since then, and he thought there was no way to be discovered. Who would have thought that using a rope led him to fall into the web of injustice? Now there is no escape, and he was finally executed in the market. The Temple of Mount Tai in Xiangzhou To the east of Xiangzhou lies the Temple of Mount Tai, which is similar to other Eastern Emperor shrines but has fallen into disrepair after many years. The locals planned to rebuild it, but despite the abundance of timber in the area, most of it was far from the river, making it difficult to transport. In the winter of the year 1153 during the Song Dynasty, a violent storm swept through, and thousands of large pieces of timber floated downstream from deep valleys, forming rafts that were carried all the way to the outskirts of the city. The people collectively reported this to the local government, claiming it was a divine gift and requesting to use the timber to build a new temple. They gathered the community by ringing bells and placed the timber in a wide, open area. Skilled craftsmen examined the materials, and everything from the large beams to the small rafters was ready. All the pieces that could be used as beams were laid flat with a central arch, and all the pieces that could be used as columns were straight and of various lengths, each suitable for its purpose. The bark had already been stripped off, making the work much easier. Everyone marveled at the efficiency and felt blessed, as no one dared to be deceitful. The project began immediately, and within a few months, the temple was completed, becoming a grand sight in Yiling. At that time, a certain official was serving as a signatory in the local government and witnessed the entire process.

A Man Named Yuan Yi and His Horse In Changlin County, Jingmen Prefecture, there lived a man named Jian Da, who lived about seventy to eighty miles north of the city. He had a daughter whom he married to a fellow villager named Liu Ya, who came to live with him as a son-in-law. Liu Ya was foolish and ignorant, and he had only a modest amount of wealth and property. He often engaged in long-distance trade on behalf of others and spent little time at home. Jian Da occupied his house and farmed his land, eventually leading him to his death and seizing his property. A young man named Li San frequently visited the Jian household and gradually became intimate with the daughter. Jian Da often advised him, saying, "If you can kill Liu Ya, I will marry my daughter to you." Li San eagerly accepted the task but did not yet find an opportunity. In the fourth year of the Shaoxi era (1193 CE), a neighbor named Yuan Yi was trading cattle to Xiangyang and hired Liu Ya to accompany him. Upon their return south, Jian Da saw Yuan Yi riding a horse from a distance, with Liu Ya carrying a load behind him. He urgently called out to Li San to bring a weapon and meet them. As soon as they met, Li San attacked and slashed Yuan Yi's back, causing him to fall off the horse and die. Then, Li San killed Liu Ya. They quickly carried their bodies to the side of the road. At first, there was no one around, but later people gathered. In the rush, no one knew what had happened.

The relative of Yuan by marriage lived near a group of militia soldiers. Yuan's horse, having lost its master, ran directly to the door of the militia, where it began to kick and bite a young horse in the stable. The militia came out in surprise and exclaimed, 'This is Yuan's horse! I just saw him riding it and returning home. How did it get here? There must be a reason.' They then went forward to investigate and found two corpses. One was identified as Yuan, and the other, recognized by the crowd, was Zou Gu. It was already known that they had been involved in a secret plan. They immediately went to intimidate Jian, saying, 'How could you kill a woman in broad daylight?' Jian became red-faced and hoarse-voiced, unable to answer. Li Zheng was present, and all were arrested. The following spring, the case was resolved. Jian and Li were found guilty of murdering a woman due to an affair that led to her husband's death and were sentenced to execution. Since there was no evidence at the time of the killing, the case was reported to the court. My nephew, Ji, who was the signatory judge, knew the truth and resigned from his post to mourn his mother. By July, a general amnesty was issued, and these three were executed. Those who escaped punishment were unaware of the events. The case was discovered because of the horse, and the principles of justice were clearly evident. This was not just a matter of chance.

Zhang Ten-Thousand's Daughter In Jing Shan, Jingzhou, there was a wealthy and influential local man named Zhang Xiangxiong. He was famous for owning vast tracts of land and storing large amounts of wealth, all amounting to tens of millions. His wealth included gold, silver, and silk/cloth. In the early years of the Shaoxi era, a notorious bandit named Sang Sheng was causing havoc in the region between the Han River and the Han River basin, leaving the land desolate. Zhang heard that Sang was coming and that his wealth and dependents could not be easily moved. Therefore, he prepared the guesthouses, cooked oxen, slaughtered pigs, and brewed a lot of wine to greet him on the road ahead. Sang was very pleased and stayed for several months. The bandits followed him, enjoying daily feasts and receiving gifts of silk and silver. Sang promised to spare Zhang's family, but Zhang's daughter, who was of marriageable age, saw Sang through the curtains and found him attractive. Zhang was afraid and quickly married her off. After staying for a long time, the bandits gathered in numbers, and with nothing to eat, they killed the entire family, still.

Due to the complexity and length of the source text, I will provide a translation in two parts. Here is the first part: --- Due to his wife, he gathered up her remains and buried them in the hall, building a large tomb and covering it up before leaving, taking his wife with him alone. Another daughter ran out and escaped, but no further news of her survival or death was heard. After forty or fifty years, villagers who went down the mountain still sometimes saw her in the rock caves, with an appearance still like that of a twenty-year-old. Occasionally, she returned to the old home, accompanied by cries of sorrow. To this day, she remains a ghost or a demon, or it was said she met a fairy and attained enlightenment—all are unknown. The foundation of the old house still exists, with a strange stone over ten feet tall, and the rocky ravines and holes are naturally formed. During the Xuanhe period, the flower and stone convoy wanted to take them to the capital, but they were too heavy to move, and the chaos of war stopped the attempt. Today, scholars and officials who pass by all look at it with admiration and regret. The stone inscriptions of the hall still exist, written by Fan Qianshu, and there is also the record of the orchid pavilion, written by Zhu Zifa. The old house only has the gate tower left, which the people moved to be the small hall of the East Peak temple. Its size can be imagined. ---

On March 27, 1195, a severe thunderstorm struck Ganzhou. Zhang Lixin, the magistrate of Ganzhou, had just been relieved of his duties and was attending a farewell banquet hosted by his colleagues at the Jinghui Hall in the county administration. Around 3 PM, several fireballs suddenly appeared in the courtyard, rotating up and down in a terrifying display. Everyone present stood up and said, "A sudden storm and strong wind are signs of change. This is not a fitting occasion for our gathering." They all dispersed and returned home. By evening, the storm had cleared, and they learned that Shi Beisan, a clerk in the provincial administration, had been struck dead with three red characters written on his left side. At the same time, Liu Zhao, another clerk, was having dinner at home and also saw fireballs rolling across the hall, entering his younger brother's room. His younger brother, who was a minor clerk, was just climbing onto the bed to check the leak. His sister, who was in the room, saw more than ten red-robed deities, each about ten feet tall, looking around menacingly. She was so frightened that she screamed for help. One deity grabbed her hair and pulled her out, saying, "This is not your concern." The deity was about ten feet away from her. A lightning axe then descended from the roof, breaking the rafters and tiles, and grabbed the younger brother, pushing him to the threshold of the door before striking his head. Soon after, a servant girl also died in the kitchen. It turned out that three people had died in total.

The Wife of the Signatory Judge The son of Zhang Tao, a man from Wuchang who held the title of De Wen Si Lang, married a woman from Yongjia surnamed Lu. They had one son, but after several years, she suddenly stopped eating. Initially, it was thought that she might have something she disliked or was avoiding, or perhaps a minor illness was causing trouble. Her daily activities and demeanor remained normal, but the next day, she again refused to eat. Zhang asked her about it, but she remained silent. His mother-in-law, Lady Zheng, came out and questioned her, but she only remained silent and submissive. As a result, they began to suspect that she was possessed by evil spirits. They questioned all the maids and concubines, and it was discovered that she had recently gone to the back garden to observe. She told the concubine that there was a fruit on a peach branch as big as a cup, which must be sweet and delicious to eat, and asked her to pick it for her. The concubine looked and saw that the fruit was abundant and numerous, but all were ordinary kinds, with no exceptionally large one. Lu made a gesture as if to pick it up with a bamboo stick, in the act of eating. Her female companions, who were present together, were all surprised. From then on, she stopped eating every day, but still occasionally drank a little wine and ate some fruit. Although she managed household affairs as usual, she slept separately from her husband. After fifteen years, she neither drank wine nor ate fruit, only drinking cold water. After another seven years, she also stopped. In the fifth year of Shaoxi (1194), Zhang, who was the signatory judge of Gan Prefecture, brought his wife along. His brother Yue, who was appointed as the Deputy Governor of Jiangxi, had his official residence in Gan. Yue once fell ill and saw several doctors but did not recover. Lu took care of him by sitting on his bed and massaging the painful areas. The next day, his chronic illness was completely gone. It was then verified that she had a supernatural encounter. Zhang Lixin invited his colleagues and their families to a banquet, but only Lu refused to come. At the time, she was seventy years old.

**Translation:** **The Sutra Written in Blood by Lady Yuhuan** Chang Tao, on his journey to Shu from his maternal grandfather Zheng Hengzhong, a statesman, passed through the western part of Jing and entered a Buddhist temple. The temple buildings were extremely dilapidated, and beside them was a hall dedicated to Guanyin, with her statue placed in a niche. On either side of the hall were several volumes of the *Avatamsaka Sutra*, many of which were scattered and incomplete. Beneath the niche was a small drawer, which he opened to find a small scroll. It was a *Diamond Sutra* written in red ink, though the scroll was somewhat damaged and the characters were faint. However, the strokes were bold and elegant, making it still quite readable. When he examined the end of the scroll, it read, "Lady Yuhuan wrote this with her own blood for the emperor." It was said to be the remains of Yang Guifei, and the blood color was still vivid, not just red ink. Zheng's son took it and treasured it as a relic. **The Giant Camphor Trees of Xinfeng** In the southern part of Xinfeng County, Ganzhou, there is a pavilion called Duan Yin. In front of the pavilion stand two massive camphor trees, separated by more than a hundred steps. Their branches reach high into the clouds, and their trunks are lush and sprawling, like objects from the heavens. The pavilion is named after these trees. In the autumn of the year 1193 during the Shaoxi era, a great flood hit the Ganzhou region, spreading between the county drum tower and the two camphor trees. The floodwaters washed away the soil, revealing a branch that had grown intertwined with another. The branch stretched from east to west, measuring 14-15 zhang in length, and was about 1 zhang above the ground. It had grown over many years, and only because of the flood was it revealed, becoming a rare sight in the county.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 1 Approved by the Imperial Library *Yijian Zhi* Volume 2 By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Peacock Chasing Away Leprosy Demons Zou Zhiming of Yihuang, Fuzhou, was wealthy. He suddenly contracted leprosy and became dazed and unconscious. After a while, he spoke to his wife, asking his uncle (who was also his father's younger brother) to come. This uncle was a monk who resided and presided over Linjiang Temple and could recite the *Kuanger Mingwang Jing*. Upon arriving, he said, "You can set up a Buddha statue outside the room and then recite the sutra at the bedside." The wife followed his instructions. The monk finished reciting two scrolls of the sutra and went out to eat. Zhiming saw a peacock chasing away the leprosy demon with its tail where the Buddha statue was hung. The monk completed the sutra reading and left. By evening, a small demon came and said, "We are wearing the Buddha's command and should leave this place, but there is still a nail in your head that has not been removed. I wish you would burn more protective money for me, and I will help remove it." They then called the servant to scatter and buy paper money, which they burned together in the courtyard. Various demons, in strange and unusual forms, danced and thanked them happily. The one who had spoken first climbed directly onto the bed, pulled out the nail, and said, "I will go to the county market tomorrow to visit the silver shop and will be sick." Prior to this, Zhiming had suffered greatly from severe headaches. Suddenly, he felt as if something had been lifted off him and could get up immediately at dawn. To verify the demon's prediction, he went to the house of the craftsman Zeng and found that Zeng had indeed suddenly contracted a severe cold.

**Translation:** **The Ghost of Yunmen Monastery** In Jianchang, the third valley of Mayuan is renowned for its clear and profound waters, making it a scenic spot in Jiangxi. There is a Buddhist monastery and a Taoist temple in the area. A monk named Chen, who was of the Chen family, hanged himself. His disciple, Yiyan, a son of the surname Zou, temporarily took charge of the monastery's affairs. He once went to a household three miles away to hold a gathering for religious or charitable purposes, and all his disciples left. He then recruited a village farmer named Chen San to guard the house. Just after the second watch, he sat alone with a bright lamp when he heard a sharp sound outside the door. Just as he was beginning to feel afraid, a monk lifted the curtain and entered, sticking out his tongue to the ground. Chen was so shocked that he ran into the kitchen, grabbed a kitchen knife, and slashed the air wildly, almost as if he were insane. Halfway through the night, the monks returned and heard shouting, disturbance, and noise inside the house. They rushed to see Chen, who was confused and disoriented. After a long time, he finally came to. Luchuan lacquerer Lu Sheng, who often served the monks, would stay temporarily in the temple at night. At that time, Wang Sansi from Nancheng was studying in the temple. He said to Lu, "Why don't you choose a room to rest in when you work there?" Lu replied with concern, "I have been coming and going between the two places for a long time. Before, I didn't know there were any strange occurrences. Yesterday, I brought my son to stay in the temple during the hot summer month because of the annoyance of mosquitoes and gnats. We hid inside the screen in the Dharma Hall. At that time, the night was halfway through, and the moonlight filled the courtyard. We saw a monk wearing black trousers and dragging sandals, walking from the east corridor to the west corridor, calling out the name 'Yiyan' repeatedly. My son and I were very afraid and worried that he would come. We held our breath and didn't dare to breathe. Fortunately, he left on his own. From then on, we no longer went there at night."

Pompadour Ghost At Yunmen Temple, there was a strange and deceptive spirit known as a "pompadour ghost." Once, a traveler arrived and the head monk, Xian, housed him in a small room beside the temple gate. On a winter night, the traveler warmed himself by the fire and fell into a deep sleep. Upon waking, he saw a figure three feet tall with disheveled hair sitting directly opposite him. The traveler was terrified and shouted, running out of the room. Xian, hearing the commotion, calmly approached and said, "What harm is there? It's just a pompadour ghost." Later, Marshal Deng Chun from Mayuan came to build a small pavilion for his quarters in a guesthouse. At the time, his cousin Wang Sansi was at a Taoist temple, and they often met. Wang told him, "The deep mountains are full of strange and eerie things. You should add more servants to guard against unexpected events. Otherwise, you should move into a monk's room to sleep safely." Deng Chun did not believe him. One day, he sent a note inviting Wang to a meal. Wang suddenly said he wanted to return home. Deng Chun was surprised by his sudden change of heart. Deng Chun said, "I did not listen to my brother's advice. Last night, only a small clerk was with me in the large bed. During the night, I heard heavy objects being thrown and knocked against the left couch several times. In panic, I called the clerk, who was already frightened, and we opened the door and ran out. We climbed down the stairs, rolling and tumbling through more than ten levels, without time to step carefully. Our spirits were still shaken, and we decided not to stay there anymore. That evening, he left.

**Deng Fu's Wife** In Shaowu, Guangze County, there was a village named Niutian Wupai. Deng Fu, a wealthy man, bought a concubine who was greatly favored. His wife could not bear it and hanged herself. Immediately, strange phenomena occurred in various ways. Deng was troubled by it and had no way to stop it. He summoned two grave diggers to set up a funeral hall in the study. Deng dared not lie directly on the bed and instead slept against the wall, ordering the guest to stay outside. At night, under the bright moon, he heard the sound of wind blowing through the banana trees outside the window, startling him and saying, "It has come again!" The deceased wife stood by the tent with her hair loose, gradually approaching the bed. The three people shouted in terror and ran away, never sleeping there again. The concubine, during the day, entered the wine cellar and saw the deceased wife standing beside her with her hair down, and she immediately fainted on the ground, eventually dying. This continued until the wife was buried.

### 会稽独脚 In the Huiji region, there was a ghost known as the One-Footed Ghost. The story goes that a servant accidentally spilled food in the kitchen, and the same thing happened with the dishes. The mistress suspected the servant of stealing and punished her repeatedly, but she could not find any evidence of theft. One day, an elderly woman visited the house and encountered a strange creature with one foot. She was too frightened to see its appearance clearly and fled, telling Zhang about it. Zhang was surprised and decided to move to avoid further misfortune. The county magistrate, who was skilled in dealing with ghosts, identified the ghost as a One-Footed Wutong, a type of demon. Zhang said, "It is not a great offense; there is no need for severe punishment. Just make it disappear and do not let it cause trouble." The magistrate used magical symbols to banish the ghost, and it never appeared again. ### 孙判官 Judge Sun was a man of noble demeanor who enjoyed discussing self-cultivation and health. Every early summer, he would place a table in front of his house in the intense sunlight and lie down on it. He would cover himself with another table and only get up to eat, then lie down again. This continued from morning till night, and he would do this for three consecutive days. Despite the intense heat, he never felt thirsty and did not take any cooling medicines. He often boasted that he could live forever, and many people believed he had achieved immortality. In the fifth year of the Qian Dao era, he served as an official in Pizhong. He relied on his robust health and engaged in excessive indulgence with courtesans without restraint, ultimately losing his life at the age of sixty.

Sun Chou, a man from Dengzhou, served as a thousand-household commander in the Jin Dynasty and later returned to the Song Dynasty during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, where he was appointed as a deputy military supervisor in the Western Capital region. He bought land and built a house in Gucheng, a county in Hubei Province. He was a very brave man. Once, his kitchen maid reported that at night, when she entered the kitchen, she saw an object squatting by the stove with disheveled hair and an unrecognizable face. When called, it did not respond, and when chased, it would not retreat. It must have been a ghost. Sun Chou said, "If it comes back, I will inform you." The next night, it came again. Sun Chou went to see it and found it to be true. He laughed and said, "This is the soul of someone who was eaten by the army of Sang Shen." He then punched it vigorously in the head, and it immediately disappeared into the ground. By morning, he ordered his servant to dig up the earth and found a single set of bones. He then carried them and abandoned them in the wilderness. Sun Chou's Precious Sword Sun Chou owned a precious sword that was extremely unusual. When placed in a dark place at night, the stars and constellations were clearly visible above it. Zhao Yan, the commander of the vanguard troops of Xiangyang, also came from the north and told my younger brother Jing Pei about it. During the reign of Emperor Tianzuo of Liao, a star fell in Yan and its light was so bright that it penetrated the inner palace. After it fell to the ground, it still glowed faintly. The emperor summoned the court astronomer to interpret the omen, who said, "There must be something unusual below." The emperor immediately ordered a search and, after digging several feet deep, found a large piece of iron ore weighing over a hundred pounds. He ordered it to be delivered to the imperial workshop to be cast into ten swords. He wanted to test their sharpness and dullness, so he called out a dead prisoner from the prison and covered him with three layers of thick armor. He said, "I will now pardon you." The prisoner was delighted and bowed in thanks. Sun Chou then lifted the sword and struck the prisoner's waist, cutting through all three layers of armor. The sword was so strong and sharp. He once gave one of the swords to Sun Jun Gai, a high-ranking military official. Jing Pei's younger brother repeatedly asked to see it, but Sun Chou resisted with great effort, saying, "I never had the intention of giving it to anyone. I fear that others might take it." At that time, Jing Pei was serving in the military headquarters of Xiangyang.

In the fifth month of the first year of the Qianyuan era (1195 AD), a woman with fair skin and wearing black bow-shaped shoes was walking alone and slowly through the southern gate of Huzhou. She called for a small boat to rent and asked to go to Yicun by what mountain path. After boarding the boat, she immediately lay down and covered herself with a reed mat. The boat was just one leaf, and as it moved back and forth, the sound of her breathing could be heard, but there was no other noise. The boatman was surprised and lifted the mat to see. He saw thousands of small snakes, each about a foot long, coiling together in a cluster. Terrified and sweating, he covered her again. They traveled sixty miles before finally reaching the shore. The boatman stepped and tapped the side of the boat to wake her. She stood up and looked like a normal person, no different from when she had first arrived. She took out 200 coins to pay the fare, but the boatman dared not accept. The woman asked why, and he replied, "I just saw you like that; how dare I accept money?" She smiled and said, "Don't tell anyone. I came from the city. This journey was to spread snake plague for a month and then return." She walked slowly into a bamboo forest and disappeared. There were 700 households in the village, and in that summer, almost half of them died. From spring to summer, a major epidemic broke out, and Huzhou was particularly severe. Only in May was there a bit of calm, but in June, it happened again. When the snake woman returned again, how terrifying! Shen Qingchen's daughter married the military governor of Fujian, Tang Yuanshan, and an old woman came to Fuzhou to tell this story.

The Elixir of the Immortal Pan In the county of Maoming, originally known as Panzhou during the Tang dynasty, there is a mountain called Huangjian. The elders of the area say that in ancient times, an immortal named Pan lived there and refined elixirs. In recent decades, workers entering the mountain to quarry stone found a wooden screen embedded in the rock, which was said to be the elixir left behind by the immortal. The elixir had fallen to the ground and melted, forming layer upon layer, almost as if it could be lifted. However, there were barely any with straight and proper branches and leaves. The Huang family owned this mountain, and outsiders wanting to cut wood would first offer salt as a precaution. After worshipping the earth god, they would proceed slowly with the work. Zhu Ziyuan, as the governor of Guangxi, sent two screens to me. The old trunk was lush, holding clouds above and overlooking the vast wilderness below, naturally formed with no trace of axe or chisel. Unlike those produced in Qiyang, which are refined by human effort, losing their natural form. It is said that the sea contains all kinds of things, and Fan Shih-lu's *Shiyang Zhi* has recorded stone pines. I have just obtained a branch from the sea, rooted in a stone block at the bottom of the sea, with intertwined roots and painted-like branches and leaves. Its color is purple, its roots are white, and its substance is all stone. When it is cloudy and rainy, there are always water droplets on the leaves and branches, and there are also pine seeds, all formed by the condensation of stone oil. Zhu Ziyuan wrote a poem saying that the wonders of the sea are inexhaustible, and only those who endure the cold know the truth. Who carved the moon with a golden axe, creating branches of purple jade reaching the clouds? The straight trunk cannot be soiled by dust, and the spiritual root is especially suited to stone. Naturally formed without the need for cultivation, it does not follow the rise and fall of spring. I responded with a poem saying that the sea tree, rooted in stone, is a wonder that has never been recorded in mountain books or geographical records. The refined elixir has created thousands of pearls, dyed purple and formed into a jade branch. The dragon-like posture of the bones, seen according to the substance, is suitable for the roosting of cranes and phoenixes, and is fragrant. The general's high singing is truly difficult to match, I am ashamed of my declining pen skills in recent years. There are also stone plum and stone pine, which I have not seen.

The Snake Monster of the Yu Family During the reign of the Yu family in Le Ping, Madam Cheng, the wife of Yu Sixi, was only one year old when she once, during the day, wanted to climb onto the bed and saw a snake winding its way on top. The snake was only about a foot long but broad and flat, about three inches wide. Madam Cheng was terrified and called the family to take weapons and chase it away. The snake jumped down and went straight out the door, and was no longer seen. Local folklore says that in the mountains and woods, there is something called a "Wangshen," a kind of demon, which is quite capable of seeing ghosts and spirits and demanding sacrifices. However, its power and spirit are not particularly impressive, and even village shamans and sorcerers can drive it away. In some cases, it can transform into a person or a snake and cause mischief with women. The Yu family suspected this. Madam Cheng's condition was confused and slightly different from her usual state, and no one knew if it was a sign of a curse or not. After a month, she died. When her body was being prepared for burial, the snake returned and sat on her abdomen. Its shape was exactly like the one seen before. When people took weapons to chase it, it quickly ran out, and its whereabouts were unknown. This snake is said to be called "Zhutun." This was during the second month of the first year of the Qinyuan era.

蜀中道人 During the Xuanhe era, Zhang Jun served as the prefect of Chengdu, and his mother, Lady Ji, practiced Taoism. She offered a vegetarian meal to a Taoist practitioner every day, regardless of where they came from, and sometimes personally burned incense and added extra respect. Once, a guest arrived and remarked, "Your lady should have a distinguished son. Where is he now?" Zhang Jun replied, "He is currently the prefect of Chengdu, overseeing grain storage in the state granary." The guest then went to meet Zhang Jun. After they met, the guest looked at Zhang Jun closely but did not speak. Zhang Jun called a minor official to speak to. Then, the guest bowed, rose, and walked a few steps before calling Zhang Jun "Chancellor" and bowing again. He said, "Your noble appearance is evident in your voice and gait. Within ten years, there will be great turmoil in the country. You will hold military power and achieve great deeds for the nation. Take care of yourself." Zhang Jun was startled and politely declined the prediction. The guest quickly left without having time to ask about his origin or name. The predictions came true, and Zhang Jun often tried to secretly find the guest, but failed. Later, Zhang Jun was demoted to live in Hezhou, where he was disliked by Qin Hui, the chancellor, who was determined to have him executed.

Lord Earth had been in extreme distress, not knowing how to extricate himself. Suddenly, a guest burst in through the door. Looking at his appearance, he was just as dignified as he was thirty years ago. He greeted him and said, "I once sought you but could not find you. You are just as desolate. Would you kindly look after me? Why?" The guest replied, "Knowing that you are worried about your situation, I have come to inform you. He is just a dead person. What is there to worry about? In the future, you will regain your former possessions. Your good fortune is not yet exhausted." The eldest son, Jingfu Shi, came out and bowed to the guest. He looked at him for a long time and said, "His eyes were about to burst from looking at things. The good fortune is right here." In the future, he will become a military governor in the southwest, famous throughout the world. The second son, Dingsou Shao, was just a few years old and was still asleep in bed. Lord Earth said, "There is still a young child whom I wish to ask for your opinion. But he is sleeping soundly and cannot be woken up." The guest took a candle and went to look at him, laughing and saying, "He has great fortune, even surpassing his older brother. It is not easy to measure." After a short while, he left and did not return again. The槐 of Yingshan Half a li south of the county seat of Yingshan, in De'an Prefecture, after crossing a small stream, there is a wild temple. A槐 tree stands between the temple and the surrounding area, about two zhang tall and equally wide. Its roots and trunk are similar to those of ordinary槐 trees, but its entire body is covered with soft, intertwined branches like vines. No one can see where it originated. Each branch splits into two, and each leaf grows on the back side, none opposite each other. Even the smallest branches are the same. It is graceful and lush, and the local people love and protect it. Therefore, it is planted in the open, uncultivated fields, and has never been pruned or cut down. Scholars and officials always stop to admire it. During the Huashi Campaign in the Zhenghe era, there were those who wanted to move it to the imperial garden, but it was so firmly rooted that it could not be dug up. So they gave up. Zhang Xun of Poyang was the county magistrate and ordered a painter to sketch it to send to people far and wide. But he could not put it on paper, so he gave up.

**Translation:** The Toothed Fish Outside Yingshan County, there stands the Great Turtle Mountain, towering for about twenty miles. At its summit lies a small temple, and outside the temple is a pool whose spring never dries up. This pool produces a unique kind of fish, unlike any other, known as the Toothed Fish. It has four legs, can climb ashore and ascend trees, and makes sounds akin to a baby's cry. Larger specimens can weigh up to a pound. It is said that this fish cannot be caught with nets or hooks. Once, a monk at the temple attempted to catch one and prepare it for cooking, but others tried to persuade him to stop. He did not listen, and shortly thereafter, the fish died. Since then, no one has dared to harm the Toothed Fish. Zhang Yunshou, in his *Records of the Southward Relocation*, mentions that while passing through Wuchang, he met Su Shi, who told him of a fish he had recently caught that resembled a catfish but had four legs, capable of walking on land. Fearing it might be dangerous, he did not kill it and released it back into the river. Some say this was a type of mermaid, likely similar to the Toothed Fish. Wang Wenli, while passing through Yazhou, received a specimen of this kind. Three Caves of Qizhou Within Qizhou (Qichang) county, there are three notable caves. The first is the Dragon Cave, located at the base of Santianshan Mountain in Qishui County, where it is said a divine dragon resides. Prayers for rain are always answered, yet the dragon's presence is never revealed. The second is the Turtle Cave, situated near the Guangjiao Temple in the outskirts of Qizhou, where various turtles live, with green ones being particularly numerous. The third is the Snake Cave, found on the side of Qizhou's town gate, believed to be the habitat of white-flowered snakes. Today, this cave is empty, but local people still catch these snakes in the mountains of Luotian. Shi Shaolu Wenshu, while serving as the governor of Xinyang, once mentioned that he had received a specimen of such a snake.

**Translation:** In the *Yijian Zhi* (Strange Tales of the World), Volume II and Volume III, compiled by Hong Mai of the Song dynasty, there is a story titled "Wang Jiabin's Dream of a Son." Wang Jiabin, the son of Wang Yanguang, a censor from Wu, was once traveling with his father to Sichuan and stayed in Han Prefecture. In his dream, he came to a place with towering pavilions and towers that reached up to the clouds, with pearls hanging from the curtains. There were three or four people dressed in fine robes and jade belts, with an elegant and extraordinary demeanor. Wang Jiabin asked those around him, "Who are these people?" They replied, "They are the descendants of the imperial family." He then came to a grand mansion with willows lining the path, where twenty or so officials in red and purple robes were seated, handling affairs. They said, "This is the home of a noble and wealthy family." As he walked through the wilderness, he saw countless thousands of children. A clerk said, "These are the descendants of the poor and weak." Wang Jiabin, who was deeply concerned about not having an heir, was very pleased to hear this and quickly took one of the children. When he woke up, he found it very strange. The following year, he had a son. However, as the child grew up, he was dull and unintelligent, unable to understand things, likely because he came from a humble background. Wang Jiabin, whose courtesy name was Zhongxian, was in charge of the Left Treasury and the Sealed Storage in the twelfth year of the Chongxi era. He told this story to his fellow officials.

Ghost of the Eternal Day Pavilion In the second year of the Qian Dao era, Lü Yansheng, who served as the magistrate of Zhenjiang Prefecture, was visited by his close friend Li Boyu. They were hosted in the west wing of the Jinbo Hall within the prefectural administration. During the early summer of the fourth month, while sitting up at night with the windows open, they noticed that the cherries on both sides of the Eternal Day Pavilion were ripe. Under the bright moonlight, they saw something stealing and eating the fruit. Assuming it was the night watchmen, they shouted and questioned them. To their surprise, two white creatures came down from the tree tops, each about a foot long. It turned out to be ghosts. They called the crowd to shout and chase after them, and the ghosts disappeared to the southwest after about ten steps. They then informed Lü Yansheng, who ordered the Taoist priest Wang Dongxian to write an iron charm and bury it at the place where the ghosts had disappeared. Since then, the strange occurrences did not recur. Two Elderly Men of Wu Kang In the mountains and valleys of Wu Kang, in Huzhou Prefecture, there were two wild old men. One was named Yang, and the other was named Xu. Both were in their eighties or nineties, with impressive and robust appearances, long gray beards, and white hair. They walked as swiftly as if they were flying. They built their homes at the top of the mountain, with dwellings located on opposite sides of the mountain, about a hundred steps apart. They constructed stone walls that encircled half of the valley, surrounded by pines and bamboo that reached to the sky. On a small pavilion and an ancient altar, each with a single plant of fragrant wood, the flowers were in full bloom on the eleventh day of the second month. Lü Yiqing came from Jiacheng to the county just in time for his birthday. A guest whom he had never met before introduced himself as Shen Daoist and visited the inn. He wore a blue cap, a cotton robe, and had a well-groomed appearance. His eyes were bright, and he looked like a celestial being. His speech and laughter were quite extraordinary. Because of a lively conversation about the beauty of the old men's dwellings, they immediately led him to visit. When they arrived at the flower stand, they saw that the area was wide and measured more than a yard. Although there are many such plants in Wu, they had never seen them before. The Daoist said, "Do you want it? You can pay respect to the two old men to obtain it." But he hesitated and said, "I..."

This flower has been protected and cultivated by divine beings for a long time; it has not been allowed to be seen by common people, how could it be casually sought? Even if high-ranking officials and wealthy people bring money and silk, I will not give it to them. Then I treated him with courtesy and showed a lot of kindness, and he seemed very pleased. The Daoist said, "Mr. Lv is a virtuous person, not one who pursues power and profit. What harm is there in giving it to him?" The two old men looked at each other and suddenly had a pleasant expression. Slowly, he said, "It seems you have a predestined connection. If the flower finds a good home, what would I care?" But I fear that if it is planted in the human world without proper care, it will cause me worry. I hope you will take good care of it. Then he called the servants and woodcutters to move it in bamboo baskets from the mountain top down to the village market, without saying a word, and left it there. The Daoist named Shen also bowed and took his leave without spending a single coin. Then he bought a boat to take it home, and the flower became even more lush, with branches and buds twice as abundant as other varieties. Yi Qian wrote this record himself, suspecting that those three men were all extraordinary people. It was the third year of the Chongxi era. In the first year of the Chongxi era, an elderly woman named Zheng from the Long Bridge side of Wujiang, over eighty years old, lived alone under a thatched roof, begging in the market every day, and was pitied by many. Besides her ragged clothes and coarse food, she stored her savings in a vase to draw an image of Guanyin. In the fourth month of summer, a neighbor's fire spread and burned everything she had saved to nothing. The next day, she cried as she sorted through the ashes and found the old vase, which was slightly damaged but the money inside had melted into a treasure image of Guanyin, about a foot tall, with a crown, clothing, jewelry, and a willow branch and a clean vase, all exquisitely crafted. The sculptor was amazed and said he could not match it. A wealthy family named Wang took it away, built a room to worship it, and left the old woman to manage the incense and candles. She lived to be over a hundred years old, and the treasure image still existed during the Shaoxi era. Recently, Wu Dounan wrote about a similar story of a common woman in Mingzhou, which is fully recorded in the *Geng Zhi*. The power of the Buddha is beyond imagination, and it does not hesitate to show its transformation, even if the stories are similar.

He Baijiu In the Fengyi Li village of Shicheng County, Ganzhou Prefecture, there was a commoner named He Baijiu. He was strong and fierce, without any means of support, and made a living by slaughtering cattle. Once, he was involved in a crime and was imprisoned in Nan'an, where he encountered a general amnesty and returned home, but he did not change his old habits. In the spring of the fourth year of the Shaoxi era, the magistrate Zheng Wan, while investigating official matters, came to that village and happened to see He Baijiu wielding a knife to slaughter a cow. He cut a small piece of rough skin, about three inches in size, and threw it aside. His son, Wu Zi, who happened to pass by from the side, was struck directly on his right shoulder. The skin could not be peeled off and immediately grew together, becoming inseparable. Black hair stood up, and even a slight touch caused extreme pain. People believed that this son seemed to share his father's wickedness and that it was a karmic retribution. Water Crystal Ring The *Shao Shi Wen Jian Lu* records that the Chu family of Luoyang possessed a crystal pillow. Within it was a branch of half-opened apricot blossoms, which they brought from the north to the south during the Jianyan era. When the Jin people invaded the Huai region, they left the pillow behind in the mountains and marshes, and its whereabouts are now unknown. The grandson of Guan Zidong, a scholar in Xiuzhou, once brought out a water crystal ring that his grandfather had kept. The ring was transparent inside and out, with a piece of living bamboo leaf inside. Both the pillow and the ring were extraordinary objects.

### 应梦寳塔 Lu Chunyan Deqing had long desired to find a fine copy of the Lotus Sutra but had not succeeded. In the early autumn of the ninth year of Qian Dao, he rested in the central courtyard on a cool evening, as if waking up. He dreamed of a young man in white clothes presenting a golden tower, three feet high, radiating a bright light. He placed it in the middle, and Lu Chunyan looked around in awe, admiring the tower and the young man, but they both disappeared. He awoke with a cry. The next morning, after finishing his meal, a person carrying the scripture for sale appeared, and the person looked no different from the one he had seen in his dream. He tried a page and a board, and indeed, it was the golden tower. Lu Chunyan then understood the earlier mystery and bought it at double the price. The scripture was a handwritten copy with elegant and strong characters, and the titles were gilded. He stored it in a black box and placed it in the Buddhist hall, treating it with great reverence. ### 大钱村 Eighteen miles outside of Huzhou City is a village called Daqian Village. In the spring of the tenth year of Qian Dao, a farmer named Zhu Qi, who worked as a hired laborer, saw a green object flying past in the wind from the northeast, falling like a winnowing basket and scattering coins like rain. He picked up over seven hundred coins, suspecting that this was the so-called money dragon. ### 吕氏画扇 One summer day, Lu Chunyan took a nap and dreamed of a grand palace with bright moonlight at midnight. He saw two thousand-petaled white lotuses planted near the steps, with several fragrant flowers nearby. On the screen, there were several lines of cursive writing that could not be read. A concubine held a candle and led out a young woman, who was very beautiful. While looking intently, he suddenly heard the sound of chess pieces being turned over in the hall and woke up, not understanding what had happened, thinking he had spiritually traveled to a fairyland. In a blink of an eye, he found a painted fan beside his pillow, and everything on it was what he had seen in his dream. He told the family about it, and it turned out that it was the young daughter who had placed the fan there; Lu originally did not know.

**A Woman from a Wine Shop in Shaowu** - **谢用光源明邵武人所居在城内尝以夏夜独步门首见邻家门外灯烛荧煌賔従杂沓拥一舆而进疑此家防下不应有重客来趋前视之无有也乃审为物遽还舍迨旦闻邻妇正以此时生女焉后十二年一夕所见复尔但肩舆却従其中出谢缓行踵其后过十四五家径入一舍而寂认彼处葢酒家也明日询之则邻女昨夕已死而酒家诞一女云** - **Translation**: Xie Yongguang, a man from Shaowu, lived in the city. One summer night, he was walking alone near his door and saw that the neighbor's house had bright, flickering lights and guests and attendants bustling about, carrying a sedan chair into the house. He thought it must be a funeral, but there should not be an important guest. He approached to see, but there was no one. He then determined it was a ghost and hurriedly returned home. By dawn, he heard that the neighbor's wife had given birth to a daughter at that time. Twelve years later, one night, he saw the same thing again, but the sedan chair emerged from within. Xie Yongguang slowly followed behind, passing through fifteen or sixteen houses, and directly entered a house, quietly recognizing it as a wine shop. The next day, he inquired about it and found that the neighbor's daughter had died the previous night, and a daughter was born at the wine shop. **A Man Dressed in Red Robes** - **绍熙癸丑吕椿年梦四皂衣吏逾墙而入自称乡司惊而寤犹髣髴在目也三鼓后又梦一绯袍道人秉笏称通判直前设拜吕巽谢致答傍若有賛者曰君是他主人宜端受坚握其臂不容屈膝遂四拜而退觉而异焉念之悚恻达旦不能寐天甫明守园人来告五更时后圃犬生五子扣其毛四黒一赤悉与梦契为之怃然者终日** - **Translation**: In the year Xihui of Shaoxi, Lu Chunian dreamed of four officials in black robes climbing over the wall and entering, calling themselves local officials. He woke up startled, and the image was still faintly in his mind. After the third drumbeat, he dreamed again of a man dressed in red robes, holding a tablet and claiming to be a deputy magistrate. He stepped forward to perform a bow, and Lu Chunian humbly thanked and responded. There seemed to be someone beside him saying, "You are his master; you should take it seriously." He firmly held his arm and would not allow him to kneel. Lu Chunian then performed four bows and withdrew. He woke up and found it strange, feeling a sense of unease. He could not sleep until dawn. Just as dawn broke, the garden keeper came to report that at the fifth watch of the night, the dog in the backyard had given birth to five puppies. He examined the puppies' fur, four were black and one was red, all matching the dream. He was pensive all day.

### Translation **Three Mountains Lu Cang** Fu Chang, courtesy name Cizhang, a scholar from Weizhou, was traveling through Wujiang during the 20th year of the Shaoxing era (1150 AD).
