He stopped at a temple and saw a room in the temple decorated with bamboo, which was clean and elegant. He rested there and noticed a burial chamber in the east room. He asked the monk who it was, and the monk replied that it was a guest of the county magistrate who had died several years ago. The family was from Fujian but could not afford to bring the body back, so they had temporarily buried him there. Fu Chang felt compassion and regret for the situation. After returning to his boat, he dreamed of a man wearing a scholar's cap who handed him a name card and said, "I am Lu Cang from the Three Mountains. My story matches what the monk told you." The man then bowed and said, "My soul is homeless and without a place to rest. Please remember me." The next morning, Fu Chang informed the local magistrate, who also knew the story from an old student. They decided to move the body to a public cemetery and built a Buddhist altar to honor him. By July, Fu Chang was preparing to take the imperial examination and stayed at a small temple by West Lake. He dreamed again of Lu Cang, who came to thank him and said, "The exam questions for the next three days are all known to me. I will reveal them to you, but please do not tell anyone. If you do, both of us will suffer." Fu Chang woke up and carefully thought about the questions, and when he took the exam, he answered them all correctly. He was then highly recommended for the top position. **Wang Wugong's Wife** A man from the capital city named Wang Wugong lived in a lane called Weiaoxiang. His wife was very beautiful. A monk who relied on alms for sustenance passed by their door and saw her, becoming enamored with her. He plotted to seduce her but failed to do so. Coincidentally, Wang Wugong was about to leave for an official post in the Huai region. While he was away, his wife sat inside the house behind a curtain. An outsider placed a box in front of her and said, "This is a message from Master Cong, who wishes to convey his regards. The county magistrate and I will be parting soon, and I have nothing to give as a token of our farewell." The couple quickly opened the box and found it contained a hundred meat seals, each containing a small golden medal worth one coin. They thought it was a mistake and opened the other seals, finding the same inside. Wang Wugong became angry and scolded his wife, saying, "I suspected this monk would come here every day. Now that it has happened, I will report him to the local authorities." The monk had no name or residence, making it difficult to find him.

A woman who had hidden herself in a place where she could not be caught, only the king's wife was imprisoned and interrogated, but she could only cry and call out to heaven, unable to answer. One time the king abandoned her and left alone on his mission. The wife was imprisoned for several months. The magistrate, due to the ambiguous nature of the situation, could not resolve it and recorded the case, sending it outside. She was poor and without clothes or food. A monk heard about the situation and secretly returned, secretly sending a sewing woman to persuade her, saying, "You now, what are you doing? You will soon die of hunger!" I will lead you to the temple, where you can sew for the community and pass the days, waiting for the king to change his mind. What should be done? The king's wife reluctantly followed her advice. After going, she entered the room of the previous monk, hidden in a pit, committing adultery freely. After a while, she gradually allowed the monk to come and go. Then she waited for an opportunity to inform the patrol officer, who arrested the monk and brought him to the authorities. The wife also died of regret and anger. The Nun of the West Lake Temple The wife of a certain official in Lin'an was extremely beautiful and was admired by a young man. Every day, he sat at the teahouse across the street, looking at her with contempt, stretching his neck, and acting as if in a trance. Once, he saw a nun come out from her house and followed her directly. The nun reached West Lake, entered the temple quarters, and immediately sought to meet someone. The young man drank tea and went many times from then on. The young man indeed had much wealth. He donated money and silk for the sake of building temples and halls, the amount reaching a thousand strings of cash. The nun was surprised by the lack of reason and approached to ask the reason. Then, with the usual feelings, he told her. The nun happily understood and appreciated the situation, agreeing to come in three days. Then, he made a list of a banquet, listing the wives of high officials, sealed with more than ten names, and went to the official's residence to invite her, saying, "Since the temple halls have been newly built, there should be a grand gathering. All the guests are already at the temple. Please climb into the carriage and dress up. Take two maidservants with you."

By the time of the Yuan Dynasty, there was no guest nun who held money to reward the sedan chair servants and sent them back. Instead, she set up wine and drank with two maids, and the woman also got drunk. She led them to rest in a secluded room, where they lay down on the pillows for a while before waking up. When they woke up, they found a young man lying beside them, and they were startled to ask who he was. It turned out that he was dead. This young man, who had pleased himself, had hidden in this place. Once he got what he wished for, he died suddenly from extreme joy. The woman had no time to wait, called the maids, and returned on foot. Her husband was outside and dared not speak. The two maids could not hold back and leaked a little. The nun feared the matter would be exposed and buried the dead under the bed. After ten days, the young man's family searched for his trace in a roundabout way and complained to the nun in Qiantang. Both the nun and the woman were shackled and interrogated, involving more than ten people. After a year, the truth was found out, the nun received a sentence of hard labor, and the woman was exempted from punishment. --- At the Jianyang post station, there were strange events involving children. Wang Songshou and his brother Qi, along with Li Yi, entered Fujian in the 15th year of the Shaoxing era, passing through Jianyang. They stayed at the post station and, after the fourth watch, saw several children entering through the window, jumping and dancing in front and behind, and not retreating even when shouted at. This caused them to be disturbed and unable to sleep. One servant slept at the door and hit them with a broom, causing them to run out together. In the morning, they investigated where they came from and found that there was a small temple to the east of the post station, where clay figurines were arranged in front of the deity, not in neat rows, and were the ones who caused the disturbance the previous night. They did not dare to destroy or throw them away but only admired them and left.

### Translation **Two Hymns to Guanyin** The Left Chancellor of Linchuan, Wang Guanying, whose courtesy name was He Fu, was a descendant of the Left Chancellor. Throughout his life, he did not concern himself with officialdom but excelled in literary writing. In his later years, he studied Zen Buddhism and gained sudden insight, composing the *Two Hymns to Guanyin*: *The Hymn to Guanyin in Meditation* and *The Hymn to Guanyin in the Water Moon*. *The Hymn to Guanyin in Meditation* states: The bodhisattva's dharmakaya is like space; is there any motion or stillness that can be described? It responds to the minds of sentient beings according to their capacity to understand. Seeing this meditative state with a robe covering the head, the meditative practitioner, in the secret and perfect samadhi, directs the tip of the hair. A wondrous sight, I praise with a single word: like wind over water, both are without emotion, accidentally forming a principle. *The Hymn to Guanyin in the Water Moon* states: One moon in the sky reflects in all waters; the enlightened person responds to things in the same way. Billions of beings have different desires; I do not have your mind, yet I feel and understand. What is the feeling? It is your clarity. Not deep, not swaying, everything is accomplished. You, a high person in the dark, attain great freedom. At the tip of the hair, high and clear, pervading the universe, a hundred thousand wonderful meanings without the need for words. Saluting the wonderful forms appearing from all directions, all are passed down and recited by people. At the age of sixty, he died peacefully without illness, sitting cross-legged, at the time residing in Zhuji, Zhejiang. **The Ant in the Monastery of the Mo Family** The Mo family of Yuehe, Huzhou, is a prominent clan with a long history. Their ancestral tomb is located southwest of Bian Peak, called Qi Mountain, where thousands of ancient trees and ten thousand tall bamboo shoots create a beauty unparalleled in the entire county. In the first month of the second year of the Qindao era, a large ant suddenly gathered in the temple hall, moving back and forth busily without seeing where it stops, not yet realizing it was strange. The next morning, seeing it had dug a large hole in the brick, with a strong trunk and branches, and lush foliage, all who saw it could not help but admire, saying that the skill of painting could not match it. The monk decorated it with red railings and protected it very carefully. Up until now, after thirty years, it is still there.

Ice Flowers on Tiles In both *Bi Tan* and *Yijian Zhi*, there are accounts of ice flowers. Nowadays, they are occasionally seen as well. In Jinan, there was a man named Yanlong who lived inside the western gate of Xiuzhou. In the first year of Chongxi, he was appointed as the governor of Hezhou and came to see the tiles on the side of his house, which had been placed there originally and were now covered by snow. By the time the snow melted, the ice had formed intricate patterns resembling pavilions, railings, carriages, horses, people, and even lotus flowers, multi-tiered peonies, long-lived daylilies, and thousand-year vines. These patterns were ingeniously and delicately crafted and remained intact for days. Yanlong ordered his son Shuqing to make ink prints from these tiles, creating more than ten copies to be kept as keepsakes and admired. The Sea God Zhenwu A scholar from Wuzhou named Ye Fang passed the imperial examination in the year Gouchou of Qian Dao. He went to Mingzhou to visit relatives and old acquaintances and undertook a sea voyage. Just as he was boarding the ship, he saw something floating along with the current toward the ship. He tried to hook it with a fishing line and found it to be a piece of old paper. On the paper was a depiction of Zhenwu sitting on a rock with a sword, with a fierce and powerful divine figure holding an axe and standing respectfully by the side. At the bottom of the paper, the words "Daozi" were written. Ye suspected that it was the work of Wu Daozi. The paper was slightly dry and not damp, as if it had never been wet. Ye moved to Jiahe, and this image was obtained by his nephew Yi. It was worshiped in the shrine and was extremely effective and auspicious. Ye was later appointed as the magistrate of Huaining in Shuzhou and died in office. *Yijian Zhi* Volume Three <Subclass, Novels, Strange Tales, *Yijian Zhi*>

The Complete Library in Four Branches Yijian Zhi, Volume 4 of the Bing Series Song Dynasty, by Hong Mai Jiang Chugong At the age of twenty-four, Jiang Chugong from Zizhou, accompanied by his wife Gongsu's two brothers who had been exiled to Nan'an and Guiyang, traveled to visit them. Taking a shortcut from Nan'an to Shangyou County, they headed towards Guiyang, traversing steep mountains and vast valleys, completely devoid of human habitation. After a day, they finally found a village, but there was no lodging. Crossing a large stream, two miles further, they came upon three houses marked as "Like Home Inn." The innkeeper lived on the other side of the stream and heard there were guests, so he opened the door. Then he returned, and there was no one else to provide firewood and water. Jiang sent his servant to the village to buy wine and meat. Soon, they met three warriors who arrived, two holding long spears and one holding a large knife. Their complexion was as white as silver, and the one holding the knife was the most powerful and robust. His name was Xue Zhong, and they were stationed at a certain place. It was during the hot summer days. Jiang fetched water to prepare cooling medicine, and they shared food and wine and drank together. He wanted to take the knife to cut the meat, but Xue Zhong drew a dagger from his left ankle and gave it to the servant, then unsheathed his knife and sharpened it on a stone in the stream. As night fell, they sat together to cool off. Xue Zhong said, "We have met by chance, having eaten your medicine and drunk your wine; we have no other way to enjoy ourselves. Please watch us practice our martial arts." They stripped naked and leaped, and after a long time, they stopped. They sighed and said, "Tomorrow we will have no money. What shall we do?" Jiang firmly suspected they were not good people. After going to bed, he sat upright on the earthen bed without sleeping. Just after midnight, he heard the inn door suddenly open, and his heart trembled even more, and he called out to ask. They replied, "We couldn't sleep and got up. Don't be surprised." Before dawn, they left, still sharpening their knives against the window frames several times. Jiang left at dawn and, after five miles, saw three bodies lying on the road, looking like merchants, which were the victims of the warriors. After another leg of the journey, he lost his way, and as evening approached, there was no one around. He often saw paper money and straw offerings. Soon, a mist appeared.

During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, an imperial eunuch named Chen Yuan was exiled to Chenzhou due to his crimes. The imperial decree ordered the confiscation and sale of his property, and Lü Cheng Yiqing acquired two items: a musical platform made from purple stone from Guozhou, finely carved and depicting a scene of lush forests, tall bamboo, and a small village by the river, with four legs each tied with green silk and adorned with a pair of shoes made of agarwood; and a chess table measuring one foot five inches in height and two feet five inches in width, with a hollow center for storing chess pieces, surrounded by a border of agarwood and a grid of ivory bars, and decorated with several layers of ebony, huanghuali, and white sandalwood, edged with agarwood and carved with waves inlaid with gold. In the dense fog, it was impossible to distinguish who or what was in front, and the travelers had to be cautious as they passed through the night. As the dust and mist gradually cleared, the moon became visible. Suddenly, a loud roar like that of a tiger was heard, and the travelers quickly placed their burdens and climbed a high tree, tying themselves up for safety. After a while, they descended and continued on, eventually reaching a small thatched house. An elderly couple greeted them, expressing surprise and joy, saying, "Sir, you must have good fortune ahead. Do you remember passing a place where paper money was hung?" The travelers recounted their encounter with spirits and tigers, noting that they had managed to avoid any harm. The elderly couple predicted that one day, when the travelers returned to Nan'an, the criminals like Xue Zhong would be captured and executed in the city. Chu Gong, whose courtesy name was Anli, was skilled in poetry. After the events described in my previous records, a scholar from Xishan named Chen Shan documented these events, stating that Jiang Ping, throughout his life, was known for his righteousness and filial piety, which allowed him to survive such dangerous situations. As for the musical platform and chess table, they were exquisite works of art, reflecting the refined tastes and cultural achievements of the time.

The waves are adorned with silver fragrances and aromatic perfumes, and all other objects are in proportion. The residence is located east of the Ministry of Rites, with a wall connecting it to a building. Below the building, a platform is constructed. Whenever guests are invited for a banquet and music is played, it is always done in this place. A scholar from Fujian presented a book as a warning, saying: "The west of the residence is where the Three Halls are located, where many poor and cold officials envy the wealth and power of others. They often feel unable to bear it, and if they are promoted, they may become high-ranking officials who can harm you." After this, the book was no longer seen, and it was mixed with old papers, so it was seen by outsiders. The officials' extreme luxury and extravagance were beyond reproach, yet they still escaped punishment. Emperor Gaozong read about the inlaid bed and water crystal dish, saying that such items were not found in the imperial palace. The Green-Scaled Turtle of the Lu Family Lu Deqing kept a green-scaled turtle in a pond in his home. Over time, the turtle became very tame. Every noon, he would strike the water with a small bamboo stick, and the turtle would always come out in response. He would then place small pieces of raw pork on the stick to feed it. After eating, the turtle would return to the pond. This routine continued for two years without any deviation. A young child wanted to play a prank and used this time to strike the water, causing the turtle to come out. He then placed the turtle in a fixed bowl and returned it the next day. At noon, the turtle did not come out again. After six or seven days, a child was sent to search for it, but it had died. This small creature was resentful of the betrayal and preferred to die rather than eat. How strange! Another white turtle was raised in the study of Lu Zili in a stone pot. It was about the size of a small coin, with golden script on its back and a glossy appearance. Its eyes were black like lacquer. Later, it was fed shrimp sauce, and the golden script fell off, leading to its death. In the city of Xiuzhou, there was a turtle that resembled a medicine ladle. When asked about it, people said that it was bound with an iron scroll around its belly and back when it was first obtained, which is why it looked that way. A Taoist priest kept two turtles in a basin. They both resembled tortoises, with turtle patterns on their bodies, beaks like parrots, and tails as long as their bodies. Their scales were spotted. These four turtles were all different from the norm.

Wu the Master --- Lü Chun's young son, aged three, fell ill with a respiratory disease in the summer of the Gengchen year of the Shaoxi era (1193). His parents were very worried and sought help from doctors and prayers. They heard that there was a Wu the Master who was extremely skilled in talisman water and should be used to treat the child. They quickly went to invite him and hired a child to deliver a message to Wu the Master, commanding the spirits to reveal themselves. Wu the Master scolded and questioned, commanding the spirits to bind the child's hands and feet. He slowly raised the child's hands in a ritualistic manner, binding them, and then did the same with the child's feet. He asked, "Are you a certain spirit?" The child bowed his head and said, "Yes." The child answered the questions whisperedly. Wu the Master then said, "I cannot treat you. Do you want to perform a good deed?" The child nodded and thanked him, saying, "Thank you very much." Wu the Master then sent the child away, and the child collapsed and fell to the ground. After a short while, the child got up. Wu the Master left, and the Lü family asked the child if he could still remember what happened. The child replied, "I was greedy for the money, so I agreed to everything. The binding and answering were all done by me. I was exhausted from standing for so long, so I answered carefully and wanted to go out and sleep after the matter was settled." The family laughed and dismissed the situation. The young son also recovered on his own.

A Person with a Tail In Lin'an, near the Mi Shi Bridge outside the Jie Bridge Gate, there was a seller of a certain snack who had a tail growing from his waist, over four feet long. He would tie it with a rope several times before he could come out. He was often chased and harassed by children in the market, and only stopped when someone asked to see it. Another beggar also had a tail, but it was only a few inches long. Mr. Lu Si Jun's Dog Mr. Lu Si Jun of the Lu family in Xiu Zhou kept a very tame dog at home for several to eight years. After Mr. Lu's wife and himself died in succession, the house became empty and was locked up. The dog still lay at the door every day, looking sad and lonely, occasionally going to other places and then returning. It often made sounds like 'wuwu' as if searching for something, sounding sad and choked. Neighbors couldn't bear to see it and sometimes called out to feed it with scraps, but it wouldn't eat. After a long time, it suddenly let out several long cries and died.

Wang Anshi's Poems Wang Haoshi, an official from Jiankang who had returned to his proper status, was from Jinan and could recite the short poems of Wang Anshi. One of his poems, titled "Zhaohun Yuan," describes snow: Yesterday, a fishing village and a rustic pond, Today, a pristine river and a small island. The mountain scenery, like a curtain, reveals the aged peaks. In a luxurious tent, a beautiful woman sleeps, unaware of the falling snow, which is as fine as cut water. She asks in surprise, "Is it willow catkins or reed flowers?" In another poem, titled "Qiaoxian," he describes the Mid-Autumn Festival, noting the absence of the moon: Holding a cup but not drinking, pausing a song, Sitting and watching the silver crab rise from the sea. A sudden cloud appears, creating a barrier between earth and heaven. Frustrated by the slow blade, the poet angrily cuts through the darkness, Comparing it to the graceful form of the moon goddess Chang'e. Reading the latter part of the poem, one can sense the poet's fierce and commanding presence. The Baogong Practitioner Baogong Temple in Xianju County, Taizhou, Zhejiang Province, though a small temple, has always maintained strict discipline and order. During the Song Dynasty, Master Yuanwu was the abbot of the temple. A gardener named Chen Jia often brought vegetables to sell in the temple. One day, a monk named Qinxiu secretly stole a plot of Chen Jia's vegetables. Chen Jia's wife, Wang, knew about the theft but could not confront the monk. She cursed him by looking up to the sky and speaking: "I lost a hundred and twenty coins' worth of vegetables. You are an animal, return what you took!" Later, Qinxiu fell ill with a severe fever. He sweated profusely, lost his voice, and was on the verge of death. The monks tried to feed him with soup, forcing it down his throat. He lay ill for a year, but suddenly woke up and spoke to others: "I was wrong to take Chen Jia's vegetables. I remember when I was mute and ill, I saw a figure in a yellow robe."

A man came and said that Master Wang called you, and immediately put on black clothes on my body. Master Wang was a brother of the Wang family. I was then driven into the back of his house, where there were already seven or eight people. After about a month, he took me alone to Chen Garden. When I looked at myself, I had become a pig. I was in a desperate state, wanting to return home, but was bound. He fed me with fermented rice for several months, and my skin and muscles became full and plump. He specially sold me to Zhang the butcher and got 1200 coins. The butcher applied the knife and stabbed fiercely under my throat, the pain was unbearable. I screamed once and suddenly woke up like from a dream, from then on, I quickly recovered. Gradually, I asked people about the matter, and what they said was all the same. The records say that many have died to repay their debts. If someone like Qingsheng was born as a pig, it has never happened before. The elder Qing, who lived in Ziqiao, personally witnessed it. He the Left Censor, He Wan Yi, Chen E, and Jian Shu, all from Fuzhou, were famous scholars. They were the closest and most virtuous friends in life, although they were friends from the same hometown, their affection was no different from that of siblings. In the thirteenth year of Chongxi, they were both officials. After a few months, Chen became a Left Censor. His wife dreamed that He came and said that he is no longer the governor of Pingjiang and has received a good appointment. The situation is much better than before, and he will be in the same office with your husband. I am very happy, so please tell him first. Upon waking, she told Chen, but he did not understand what she meant. Soon, news came that He had passed away. The wife dreamed again that He was wearing a gold seal and purple sash, riding a white horse with a large entourage. He told the wife, "I am going to take up my new position first. Tell the Left Censor to pack his luggage and come early." Lü Deqing, who was then overseeing the treasury, and Chen, who was the head of the treasury, both spoke to each other.

### Translation #### Clear Pond Stone Buddha The Zhou family, who were officials in Huzhou, had a servant named Tao Zhong who collected debts by force or threat. Every evening, he would pass through the Qingtang gate and often see a faint light emerging from the water's edge. This happened for several dozen nights in a row. He casually noted the location and went there the next morning. There, he found a square stone with four sides, each engraved with an image of the Buddha Shakyamuni. He took it back and served it with incense and candles very carefully. The stone was fine and smooth, and he found it very beautiful. When it rained, water droplets emerged the night before, and this method of predicting weather had always been accurate. This was during the early years of Emperor Gaozong's reign (1165-1173). The Tao family gradually accumulated wealth and became prosperous. By the year 1184, Tao Zhong's son, Tao Xian, placed a Huangtou birdcage near the Buddha room. He left excrement to soil the image without cleaning it. Suddenly, a violent thunderstorm occurred during the night, and by dawn, the Buddha had disappeared. Despite searching frantically, they could not find it. Soon after, Tao Xian fell ill with malaria and dreamed that he was caught by a low-ranking official dressed in yellow and taken to the Temple of Mount Hua. The god there scolded him for his sins and ordered him to be whipped twenty times. When he woke up, he found that his buttocks had no skin left. A doctor applied ointment for whip wounds, and it took over a month for him to recover. The following summer, he suddenly suffered from severe depression and drowned himself. His family returned to poverty, and everything was as it had been at the beginning. #### Zhao Hulü Imperial Clan Gongheng lived in Xiu Prefecture. He had a gentle and easygoing nature and was good at interacting with people. However, he had a naturally humorous disposition. At the sight of something amusing, laughter burst forth. From relatives and neighbors to performers and actors, he knew no bounds in his teasing and mocking. No one dared not to respect him. Due to his sparse hair, people called him Zhao Hulü. Someone then wrote a satirical poem about him: A door with few differences, Raised one who follows a pattern to draw. Incompetent in everything, Leaning against a fence, the vine doesn't fall, On the water, it doesn't flip over. Truly strong, there's no place to accommodate him, Only to bake under the scorching sun. Readers couldn't help but laugh. This is also a humorous retribution.

### Translation **Hu Xu Zan** After being demoted from his position as the Commander of the Zhenjiang Garrison and exiled to Changsha, Qi Fang settled in Huzhou and lived there. In the seventh year of Qian Dao, he suffered from a severe and painful disease in his thighs and buttocks. Medicines, acupuncture, and moxibustion all failed to work. Later, he experienced an unbearable itching sensation and placed a pot with burning coals across his thighs to allow the steam to rise and warm the area, finding some relief. This continued for several months until he died. During his most critical illness, his concubine held a candle to administer medicine when she saw dozens of human heads appear on the lamp flames, filling the room with the same phenomenon, almost a thousand in number. One of them was slightly larger, and Qi Fang pointed to it and said, "This is the spirit of Hu Xu Zan." It is said that when Qi Fang was a notorious bandit, he destroyed Guangde Commandery, slaughtering all officials from the governor down. Each time he beheaded someone, he would cut open their belly, break their thighs, and fill them with coins. Only the family of a professor was spared. Hu Xu Zan, who served as a military official, suffered an especially cruel fate. His wife, Bian Shi, was beautiful, and Qi Fang took her as his wife. At the moment of her death, it was known that she had a tragic fate. Bian Shi also died shortly after. Her son, Shi Xian, was sentenced to death for killing someone in the city and robbing their paper money, receiving the harshest punishment. **The Tomb of Rong Shilang** Rong Maoshi, a Shilang, was buried southwest of Bian Mountain in Huzhou. Ten years later, a Daoist named Xu Cunzhen passed by his tomb and told a temple monk, "This place was originally auspicious, but the auspicious energy has already departed. The family will decline within two or three years. Urgently seek a new burial site and relocate the grave; you may still save half of the situation. If you delay or ignore it, disaster will come without delay." The monk informed Rong's son, Jian, who was indifferent, saying that the matter was very significant and could not be resolved in a short time. Soon after, Jian died, followed by his grandson, the County Magistrate of Qianshan, Fu Chen, the Judicial Officer of Nankang, Ying Chen, and the County Magistrate of Renhe, Shou Chen, one after another. Seventeen women and wives also died. Only Tao Yuan County Magistrate Gongchen survived. The fate of the tomb is like this; is this by chance? Xu Cunzhen was also an extraordinary person.

**Wang Shuangqi** Wang Chao, a Zhongyi Lang (a mid-level military officer), was from Taiyuan. He was strong, brave, and skilled in archery and horseback riding. His face was marked with two flags, which is how he got his name. He once served in Liu Wuzhong's army as a small squad leader. Later, he was discharged from military service and became a patrol inspector in Hunan. Due to a crime, he was demoted and reassigned to Dingzhou. He then became a bandit in the marshes, killing people and stealing goods, even to the point of being branded and exiled. However, he refused to reform his bad habits. Once, he met a Taoist who taught him the art of longevity and alchemy. By the time of the Gengyin and Xinmao years of the Qinding era, he was eighty years old. At that time, several people in Yueyang were robbed and killed, and their women were also raped. The authorities had been unable to catch the bandits for years. Huang Shiming, a magistrate from Lianjiang in Fujian, was from a neighboring region and was determined to track them down. He captured ten bandits, with Wang Chao being the leader. Once captured, he was imprisoned. The magistrate saw that Wang Chao was advanced in age, but his lower abdomen was warm and solid like iron. He called out to him and asked, "Do you have some special skill?" Wang Chao replied, "I have no other skill, only the ability to apply heat." He said that every summer and autumn, he would burn thousands of moxa cones. The more he did this, the less he feared cold and heat. He could go days without eating or eat a day's worth of food in one sitting, and he never felt extreme hunger or fullness. He said, "Isn't it known that earth turns to brick, wood to coal, and they last a thousand years without decay, all due to fire?" When questioned about his crimes, he confessed without much reservation. He was sentenced to death and sent to the prefecture for execution. When the executioner cut open his belly, he found a block that was neither bone nor flesh, solid like stone. This was the result of his heat application. It is a pity that he did not restrain himself, leading to a great crime and ultimately turning into a burden for longevity. How ignorant he was.

The Golden Chicken Elder Zhaoshi Fang, residing at the Yuanjue Temple on the upper part of Wuyuan in Huzhou, in the spring of the ninth year of the Qian Dao era, was planning the burial ground for his father but had not succeeded for a long time. In the fifth month of the summer, he dreamed of an old man dressed in white, holding a golden chicken in his right hand, who spoke to him and said, "The auspicious prediction is only within thirty li. You can obtain it tomorrow." At that time, he was still unsure about the dream and did not dare to believe it. The next day at noon, while sleeping, the temple's head monk came to visit and said, "There is a Daoist who holds a sutra account and is selling land for a certain family. You should let him come in to inquire." By evening, they went together to the place and asked for the name of the mountain, which turned out to be Jinji Peak. He suddenly realized that the dream was a message from the old man, calling the master to come. They negotiated the price and agreed to pay a hundred thousand coins. On the day they completed the contract, it happened to be the day of Xin You. The tomb was dug with the north as the main direction and the south as the facing direction, and according to the geomancers, it was an excellent site. His father was buried there, and the following year, Zhaoshi Fang passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi. The Cat's Retribution In the household of Lü Deqing's relative, there was a kitchen maid named Qingxi. She placed rabbit meat in the kitchen, but it was stolen by a cat. The mistress scolded her, and Qingxi, unable to bear the anger, caught the cat and threw it onto a pile of firewood. Coincidentally, there was a sharp bamboo piece in the pile, which pierced through the cat's abdomen, causing its intestines to spill out. The cat cried out for a full day and night before dying. A year later, Qingxi, while drying clothes, lost her footing and fell to the ground, being injured by a sharp bamboo piece. Her lower abdomen was pierced, and blood spilled all over her body. She died the next day, and the scene was very similar to the cat's death. This was the cat's retribution.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume Four *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Yijian Zhi* Volume Five Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Clerk Gao of Lin'an During the early years of Emperor Xiaozong's reign, Zhu Si Yan was the governor of Lin'an County. While auditing official assets, it was discovered that Gao Sheng, a low-ranking official, had exceeded the limit in his embezzlement. His wife was especially greedy and reckless, often deceiving local villagers by having them pay official money and then falsely issuing receipts while keeping the money for herself. At that time, Gao was sent to the provincial government for some matter, and his wife was arrested first and sent to prison. When Gao returned and questioned her, she answered in a very disrespectful manner. They were both interrogated and detained for more than a month, with daily torture. One night, the county magistrate visited the prison, and the two prisoners wept, saying they had been tortured to the extreme and feared they would not survive. The county magistrate felt pity for them and, coincidentally, Zhu was hosting a banquet for guests. He broke their chains and let them escape secretly. The next day, the county magistrate went to the county office and spoke to Zhu about Gao, who was a clerk, and his wife, who had embezzled people's money and shouted at the superior officer. They should indeed be severely punished, but they had been in prison for a long time. Last night, when they were called by name, they were already too weak to respond and were released. Soon after, they both died at home, fortunately not in prison. There is no need to publicize this; their son also wishes to bury them. I have followed his request, but I feel shocked by this unauthorized action. Zhu was pleased that the county magistrate shared his disdain for evil and handled the matter discreetly, with no trace of his involvement. He expressed his gratitude and returned to his room. However, he then thought that their crimes did not warrant death, and if they were both killed, it might bring trouble upon him in the future. If not, he would be even more troubled.

A ghostly retribution began to trouble him, causing him to lose his appetite and sleep. Soon, he saw two ghosts, one of whom appeared naked and disheveled, with scourge marks all over his body. The ghost approached and pulled at his clothes, saying, "My crime was no more than being a common laborer, yet I fell into a muddy road. The county magistrate then removed my bones in a cruel and inhumane manner. Unless you come to testify from the grave, there is no reason to abandon me." Zhu could not respond and became ill. The ghost was present day and night. When the magistrate came to inquire about his illness, Zhu told him the cause and said, "I could not bear my anger at the time, but now it is too late to regret. What can I say now?" The magistrate laughed and said, "These two were not actually dead. I pitied their plight and released them, hiding them in the homes of relatives in the county and deceiving them by telling them they were dead." Zhu said, "If that is the case, why do they appear every day? Who was the other one?" The magistrate replied, "This is caused by excessive worry and suspicion. You should call him to come." After just one night, the other ghost appeared, kneeling and bowing in the courtyard. Zhu's mind and will suddenly became enlightened, and his illness suddenly healed. He ordered Gao to return to his original duties. Some also said that the laborer Zhu had dealt with was truly dead but often appeared as a ghost. When Zhu served as the governor of Hanyang and Fuzhou, he was often haunted by visions of Gao entering the government office, still angry that no one had held anyone accountable, and that the soldiers and officials had failed to take action. They were all sentenced to flogging and exile. The people of the county knew of these past events but no one dared to report them, and they have not yet found peace and tranquility.

Xu Liu Lang Xu Liu Lang, a citizen of the southern part of Huzhou, originally made a living by selling baked goods, spices, and sugar-coated pastries. People called him "Xu Tangbing" because of his trade. He earned daily profits sufficient to meet his needs and had some extra income. As a result, he lent money to neighbors in need and charged interest, which was more profitable than other places. His business prospered, and his family's wealth increased, earning him the nickname "Liu Lang." He died in the sixth year of the Qindao era (1170 CE). His niece married into the Chen family in Weitang, Xiuzhou. A neighbor's female dog gave birth to three puppies. One of them was white with brown fur on its belly, bearing the characters "湖州许六郎" (Xu Liu Lang of Huzhou). The niece went to see and confirmed that it was true. She had her husband pay 1,000 coins to buy the puppy but did not dare to inform Xu Liu Lang of the puppy's existence. The Xu family held a ritual for the dead every three days for 21 days. The entire family dreamed of the deceased, who wept and said, "I had no major faults in life. I only lent money at high interest, which led to poor people being unable to repay their debts. Some were forced to sell their wives and children. This is why I was reborn as a dog. I am fortunate to be born near my niece's husband. My niece has already redeemed me and can bring me home. However, I will only have 100 days left before my karmic connections are exhausted." The next day, his son sent a servant to the Chen family. Upon arrival, the puppy greeted the servant at the door with a wagging tail. The servant carried the puppy back home in a nest of straw. When they arrived home, the puppy sat at the head of the table and shed tears. The family was deeply moved by the puppy's tears and cared for it with all their heart. Exactly 100 days later, the puppy died.

Tong Qi the Butcher In a village near Taizhou, thirty miles from the city, there is a small temple also known as Jing Shan Road. There lived a butcher named Tong Qi, whose family had been in the pig-slaughtering trade for generations. Each year, he slaughtered not fewer than a thousand pigs, selling them in the city to enrich his family. On New Year's Eve of the first year of the Chongning era during the Song Dynasty, Tong Qi's family members dreamed that all their deceased ancestors gathered and said, "Because Xiao Qi does not change, he has caused more than ten pigs to die by his hand. The pigs in the pen are all of our kind. The debt is paid, do not kill any more." By morning, they went to see the pigs, and the heads of the pigs all turned into human form, resembling their grandfather, father, uncles, aunts, and sisters. They spoke with sorrow, roughly as told in yesterday's dream, but their lower bodies remained those of animals. Soon after, they returned to their original form, and others did not see this. Tong Qi immediately left the pig pen, went to the temple, and offered food to the monks while repenting. He deeply repented and changed his livelihood to selling silk and cloth, and he still exists to this day.

Chun'an Pan Old Man In the twenty-fifth year of the Shaoxing era (1155 CE), Zhongyi Lang Diao Duanli, following his relative, the Transport Commissioner Shao, traveled to Jiangxi via the road through Yan Prefecture and Chun'an. They stopped late at a traveler's inn when the day was not yet dark, and Diao took a stroll along the village paths for two or three miles. He entered a village house and rested briefly, seeing the host couple pounding rice. He asked their names, and they said they were surnamed Pan. The wife brewed tea and served it. Diao heard a rustling sound from a nearby house and peered in, seeing a headless man weaving straw sandals with swift hands. Diao was greatly surprised and startled. He asked the couple, who said, "This is our father, Pan Old Man." During the uprising by the Fang bandits in Bangyuan in the year Gengzi of the Xuanhe era (1120 CE), he was beheaded. I happened to escape and was spared. I returned to find his body among the accumulated corpses, which was still recognizable. I carried him back home, and his limbs could still move, and his body was warm. I could not bear to bury him, so I made a small coffin, placed his head in it, and buried it behind the house. I applied medicine to the severed area, and later the wound healed. A new opening formed, and when he wanted to eat or drink, he made a whistling sound, and I slowly fed him porridge and soup. Thus, he survived, and now, thirty-six years later, he is seventy years old. Diao hurriedly returned to his rented inn, and for several days, his mind was in a state of confusion and unease. Later, whenever he thought about it, his hair stood on end. This is what I have recorded, and the people of Guang are also like this. Madam of Master Tang (End of translation)

Tang Heng Pingfu, a native of Lin'an, passed the imperial examination to become a Jinshi and was waiting for a vacancy as a professor in a certain state. He then moved to the guesthouse of Wang Shilang in Shangrao, where he lived with his family beside the gatehouse. During the Qian Dao period, the Wang family's house was burned down by fire, and everything was destroyed. Tang's wife contracted a severe illness and died, and she was buried in that place. Later, Tang went to the capital and bought a concubine who had both beauty and talent. He gave all the jewelry and accessories from his late wife's boxes to her. Once, on Tomb Sweeping Day, he planned to go with her to sweep the tomb and then visit the mountains, but they did not get to do so. During the day, he saw his late wife raise her hand and strike his concubine, breaking her hairpins, clothes, and other ornaments, and beating her for a long time until she disappeared. The entire household was terrified and dared not invite a witch to exorcise the spirit. Tang then collected all the valuables worth a thousand strings of cash and donated them to temples and monasteries to perform rituals to help his late wife. The concubine was ill for over a month before recovering, and the supernatural influence also ceased. Gao Zirun, a Wenlinlang, served as a judge in Zhenzhou during the Gengzi year of the Chongxi era. Because of an illness, he dreamed of a divine being who said, "In your past life, you mistakenly sentenced a case, leading to the death of an innocent person. Now, even though we are separated by this life, they still seek an opportunity every day to avenge the wrong. I have protected you, so they cannot move forward. I fear that I will not be able to protect you in the end. If you can quickly resign from your position, not only will you extend your life, but this ghost will no longer cause trouble." Tang understood and told his wife and children to prepare to return home. He reported to the governor and requested retirement, but the governor tried very hard to keep him. Gao explained his dream in detail, saying, "If you know but do not leave, you will not escape death." The governor was moved and immediately agreed to his request. He reported to the court, and the chancellor praised his peaceful retirement. He was promoted and received an additional rank. He returned to Xiu Prefecture and lived outside the eastern gate, focusing on making a living and eventually becoming a wealthy household. Moreover, he had a bold and generous personality. The Gao family is a large clan with many relatives and friends, and those who came to him in poverty were always helped. Some say that at the time of the dream, the divine being had other instructions, which were not revealed to others, so it is unclear.

Zheng Si, a man from Xianju, Taizhou, was a tenant of Lin Tongpan's family. Over time, he had accumulated some savings and sometimes went out to trade silk, cotton, and sea products. In the second year of Chunxi (1175 AD), accompanied by his servant Chen Er, carrying loads, they arrived at Zhaixing Ridge. The day was already late when they suddenly encountered heavy rain, which made it impossible to proceed. They found an empty house and rushed in to rest. Quickly, they struck a stone to get fire and broke reed fences to cook and light the fire. Suddenly, they felt something moving under the pile of straw. Upon looking, they saw three tiger cubs. Zheng Si quickly stabbed and killed them with his spear and used other wood to prop and close the doors. Soon, they heard a loud banging on the door. Peering through the cracks, they saw two large tigers. One was carrying something several feet long and dropped it on the ground. The other scratched through the threshold, intending to rush in. Zheng Si cut off its three claws with a sharp blade, and both tigers left. After a long time, he listened carefully and heard human-like moaning. No one dared to open the door. Until dawn, when he went out to see, it was Lin Tongpan's daughter who had been attacked by the tiger, fortunately, she was unharmed. Zheng Si carried her down the mountain and slowly returned to the county, sending her back to the Lin family. They were just gathered in tears, but were overjoyed and thanked Zheng Si generously. Due to his small fortune, Zheng Si also punished the tigers and no longer engaged in trade.

**Translation:** **Saint Qi Niang** At the beginning of the Jianyan era, the imperial carriage stayed in Yangzhou. Many scholars and officials from the Central Plains fled to the south but had no time to bring their families. From Zizhou, Jiang Tingyan arrived at the imperial court to participate in the selection process. His mother and younger brother, Fu Yan, had already left their hometown and were on the road for a long time without receiving any letters from home. This prolonged absence caused him great worry and distress. A local woman named Saint Qi Niang was highly regarded for her abilities as a female shaman. She practiced the method of extinguishing traces and was believed to be able to communicate with spirits and foretell future events. Jiang Tingyan went to her house, burned incense, and silently prayed. As soon as he entered, he saw the shaman, a young woman, already standing barefoot on a red-hot brick. On her head, she wore a hat resembling a frying pan, and the spirit was just descending. She immediately said, "Di Gonglang, the supervisor of the Nanyue Temple in Tanzhou, Jiang Shen, asks about the news of your mother and younger brother. You will know in ten days, and you will meet them in three more days." Jiang Tingyan heard this and bowed respectfully, and his accumulated worries began to dissipate. Exactly ten days later, he received a letter, and three days after that, his family arrived. Jiang Tingyan was overwhelmed with joy and sorrow, and he offered a generous amount of money to thank her. She refused everything except for the incense, candles, and banners, which she kept. Later, Jiang Tingyan became a Shilang of the Ministry of Works and often recounted this story to his guests. **Lü Deqing's Dream** Lü Deqing, who had served as the magistrate of Shicheng in Ganzhou, was dismissed from his post and went to the capital for a new assignment. In his dream, a person holding a notice came to visit him and said, "This is the former Tongpan of Xinzhou, Hong Chaofeng. His name is written in large characters about two inches long. He is my eldest son. We have never met before, but I wish to introduce myself to you. Now, we can also interact with each other." Upon waking, Lü Deqing pondered deeply and could not understand the meaning. At that time, his eldest son had already been appointed as the deputy governor of Fuzhou. After returning to his hometown, several months later, he received a special imperial pardon in the year Jia Yin, which included a promotion in rank. The official announcement stated, "Hong Zi and five others are recommended for the following positions." Lü Deqing was then promoted to Zhaosanlang. Only then did he recall his earlier dream.

**Sheng Qiniang** In the early years of the Jianyan era, the imperial procession halted in Yangzhou. Many scholars and officials from the Central Plains, fleeing due to the turmoil, came south but had no time to bring their families. Jiang Tingyan from Zizhou arrived at the imperial court to participate in the selection process. His mother, Lady Fu, and his younger brother, Fu Yan, had already left their hometown and had been on the road for a long time without receiving any letters from home. After a long period of worry and distress, people in the area greatly praised a female shaman known as Sheng Qiniang, who practiced the "extinguishing traces" method, was spiritually connected, and could foresee future events. Jiang went to her home, burned incense, and silently prayed. As he had just entered, he saw the shaman, a woman in her prime, already standing barefoot on red-hot bricks, her head adorned with a mysterious pan-shaped headdress, as a deity had just descended. She immediately said, “Di Gong Lang, supervisor of the Nanyue Temple in Tanzhou, Jiang Shen, inquire about the news of your mother and younger brother. In ten more days you will know, and in three more days you will see them.” Hearing these words, Jiang bowed respectfully, and his accumulated worries eased somewhat. Exactly ten days later, he indeed received a letter, and three days later, his family arrived. Jiang was overcome with a mixture of sorrow and joy. He gave her a generous amount of money to express his gratitude, but she refused everything, accepting only incense, candles, banners, and flowers. Later, Jiang became a Shilang of the Ministry of Works, and often told this story to his guests. **Lü Deqing’s Dream** Lü Deqing, after completing his term as magistrate of Shicheng in Ganzhou, was transferred to another post. In a dream, a person came to visit him holding a notice and said, “This is Mr. Hong, former Tongpan of Xinzhou, whose name is written in large characters, about two inches long. He is my eldest son. We have never met before, but I wish to make your acquaintance, as my father had a close relationship with you.” After this, the dreamer merely said, “Now we will also get to know each other.” Awakening, Lü thought about it carefully but could not understand its meaning. At that time, his eldest son had already been appointed as the deputy governor of Fuzhou. After returning to his hometown, several months later, Lü was granted an imperial decree under the year Jia Yin, granting promotions. The decree stated, “Hong Zi and four others are to be recommended for official posts as follows.” Lü then realized this was a reference to his earlier dream.

Fan E's Wife Fan E, styled Changyan, was from Yiling. He was fond of learning and skilled in writing. He married the daughter of Mr. Guo Yizheng from Baiyun. The couple were extremely fond of each other. When Fan E was over thirty, his wife De passed away. Fan E dreamed of her as if she were alive. She pulled his sleeve and said, "Let us go together." Fan E replied, "You do not forget the deep kindness we shared. How could I refuse?" But my father is old and weak, and it is difficult to fulfill my wishes." De said, "Since we cannot go together, you must not attend the provincial examination." From then on, whenever Fan E attended the provincial examination, he was always anxious and feared some unforeseen misfortune. Although he had no particular misfortune, he was always unsuccessful and died without achieving his goals. Perhaps the advice not to attend the provincial examination was a sign of things to come. Teachings of the Five Prime Ministers Li Chaozeng from Jianchang, styled Jianmei, had always been very respectful in his family's worship of the spirit of Wu Zixu. The local custom was to call him "Prime Minister Wang," and all prayers were answered. Li was in the Imperial Academy and was able to avoid capture when the enemy arrived and the city was defended. In a dream, the spirit sent a messenger to show him a赋 (rhapsody) with the title "Emperor Guangwu is the same as Emperor Gaozu." When he woke up, he could not remember it, but in the next night's dream, the spirit told him to read it thoroughly, and he then remembered it completely. In the year Xinhai of Shaoxing, during the provincial examination in Raozhou, the candidates used the previous sentence as the title for their赋 (rhapsody), and Li was successful and later became a Left Attendant of the Imperial Guard.

Dong Xing's Mother In the village of Changfeng in Dexing County, Raozhou Prefecture, there lived a scholar named Dong Xing, whose mother, Lady Li, fell gravely ill with an abdominal disease in the fifth month of the twelfth year of the Chongning era (1185). At that time, many people in the village and countryside were suffering from this ailment. Lady Li's condition worsened daily, and on the ninth day of the seventh month, at midnight, her breathing became labored, and she seemed on the verge of death. Her family gathered around her, weeping, and had already prepared the coffin, shroud, and bedding. The weather was extremely hot, and it was expected that she would pass away before dawn. The next day, at the hour of the Rabbit, Lady Li seemed to yawn and stretch, and when she was helped to sit up, she spoke, saying, "I must be on the brink of death. I have just begun to walk on flat land and open wilderness for a few miles, entering a great city. I hear people's voices, but everything is pitch dark, and I see nothing. I know I will die. I thought to myself, 'I have recited the *Guanyin Pumen Jing* (The Sutra of the Universal Gate of Avalokiteshvara) for thirty years without ever relaxing. If there is any divine power, I dare to hope for salvation.' So, I called out loudly to the Bodhisattva of Compassion, 'Save me from suffering.'" After calling out a hundred times, it seemed as if a hand grasped her left arm, and they walked together for about thirty steps. Gradually, she felt a bright light, as if it were daytime. She saw a woman adorned with precious jewels, radiating a brilliant and fragrant aura, with a dignified and serene appearance. She knew this was the Bodhisattva manifesting herself and asked for help, saying, "Your fate is nearly finished, but because of your long-standing good roots, I have come to assist you. You should return quickly and do not stay here for another half-century. We will meet again." Lady Li bowed in gratitude and saw the Bodhisattva taking steps higher and higher, moving farther and farther away, pointing westward. She saw banners and canopies leading the way, with golden and jade-like brilliance that dazzled her eyes and heart. Suddenly, she woke up from her dream, unaware that she was lying on her bed. From then on, she recovered and remained healthy and peaceful. Five years later, on the seventeenth day of the eighth month in the first year of the Shaoxi era (1190), she passed away peacefully without illness. Dong Xing wrote an account of his mother's experience.

The Political Figure Dong Teyuan Dong Teyuan, the political figure from Luzhou, repeatedly failed the imperial examinations but was appointed as a teacher in a prefectural school by special imperial favor. He was so poor that he could not support himself, so he worked as a tutor in a wealthy family's household. In the year Dingmao of the Shaoxing era, he took the provincial examination. Some of the candidates who went to the transport office to request an examination wished to have him join them in the examination hall. Dong, being old and no longer having any ambition for fame or success, refused to go. They forced him to accompany them as a guest, and he passed through Linjiang Prefecture, where the governor Peng Zicong, a fellow townsman, examined his name card and said, "Just an old examination official, what's worth mentioning?" He paid no attention to him at all. That year, Dong was pre-selected for recommendation, and the following year, his name was submitted to the Ministry of Personnel. He placed first in the palace examination, but due to his previous official position, the emperor decreed that Wang Xuanzi was to be ranked first and Dong second. Despite this, Dong received the same benefits as the top-ranked candidate and was appointed as Left Censorate Officer and signed as the military judge of the Southern Army. On his return journey, Peng Zicong sent an envoy to bring a public letter to greet him. Dong wrote a quatrain at the bottom of the paper: "The imperial edict was just issued and the ink was still wet. The emperor's favor allows me to bow before the Golden Hall. Friends from my hometown come to greet me; I am still the old examination official." Peng, upon hearing this, felt ashamed and regretful for the next six or seven years. Dong was quickly promoted to the position of Censor. Peng did not dare to seek an official position. Just when the position of Grand Councilor was vacant, Dong took on the role, and Peng was appointed as an envoy to Guangdong. People praised his ability to forgive and let go of grudges.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (1174–1189), a fire broke out at the Nan Yue Temple. An imperial edict was issued to the governor of Tan Prefecture to rebuild it, and the magistrate of Xiangtan, Xue Dazhu, was appointed to oversee the construction. The materials required were of extraordinary size, and almost every deep mountain and remote valley was searched. However, the main hall was missing one beam, five zhang long and five or six chi in diameter. After a thorough search, it could not be found. Someone said there was a giant cedar tree in front of the Temple of the Great King of Yellow Ridge and White Horse in Xiangtan, whose height reached the clouds, and none of the trees produced elsewhere could match it. Now that the project was underway, this tree was indispensable. But the temple's divine spirit was terrifying to people, and who would dare approach it? Xue verified the information and found it to be true. He immediately prepared a document and sent the county magistrate Wang Yining to burn it at the temple. Then he led a hundred craftsmen with axes to chop down the tree. They saw a long snake coiled around the roots, and the craftsmen trembled, their legs shaking, and stood in awe. The magistrate sent a messenger to report to Xue. Xue rode a horse and personally went to the temple to offer sacrifices and wine, praying respectfully: "This land is under the jurisdiction of the Lord of the Southern Peak. Now the construction of the palace is ordered by imperial decree. This small tree should be offered as a beam for the palace. Why should we be so attached to it?" He threw the divination cups and got a favorable result. Then a hundred axes were swung, and the sound of the tree's top falling was like the clashing of swords. The craftsmen were afraid and wanted to stop, but Xue did not listen. The next day, the tree's sap flowed like blood, covering the ground. A loud sound like thunder, and the tree trunk, once cut, spun in the air, wanting to fall but not falling. Xue waved the crowd to move away and then reported to the spirit: "Since you have already allowed us, please do not use light, sound, or supernatural phenomena to frighten us." After a while, the tree finally fell. He returned to the temple to offer sacrifices and thank the spirit, whose image was completely split apart. The onlookers were terrified. The tree snake was its spirit.

The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries Yijian Zhi, Volume Six, Section Three Song Dynasty, Hong Mai, compiled The True Immortal Zhu of Fuling An Chuhou, a military man from Guang'an, served as a professor in Chengdu. He once visited the Great Mercy Temple, where the head monk treated him with great respect. The temple is located in a strategic and important location, and every spring, it is visited by people without fail. The monk was tired of receiving guests, but only when high-ranking officials such as governors and military commanders arrived would he prepare a full ceremonial reception. He treated other officials with indifference. An was curious about this and asked the monk why. The monk replied, "Last night, at the third watch, people outside shouted that the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat was about to arrive. In the morning, the Chancellor came, and I knew he would be great in the future, so I treated him with respect." An was summoned to serve as an Imperial Censor after submitting a memorial on the system of learning, and later became the Transportation Commissioner of Hunan. Dun Wen, a poet, was demoted to serve as the Tax Inspector of Tan Prefecture. He dreamed of meeting the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat on the riverside, and he recognized his face very well. That night, An entered the prefecture, and the next day, he saw the Chancellor, who looked just as he had in the dream. An also said that when he was a student, he dreamed of being guided to a grand palace, where he saw a true official dressed in ceremonial robes sitting on the throne. At that time, the River God was already present below the hall, and he was ranked lower than other gods. After a long while, the true official ordered a servant to lead the River God to stand aside and then invited An to sit on the throne. An then asked, "The River God is a revered deity among the people of Shu. Why is he ranked below?" The true official replied, "How can a ghostly official be ranked above a divine one? In your previous life, you were the Daoist immortal Zhu of Fuling. In this life, you will be a Premier, but alas, your nose is not well-formed." Upon waking, An silently rejoiced and composed a couplet to record what he had seen: "In the dream, I met a true companion in the Immortal Hall, proving that in my previous life and this one, I was Zhu of Fuling, a temporary visitor to the human world, holding the cauldron of creation." Sun Zongjian wrote in *Donggao Zalu* about this event, noting that An's highest position was Vice Minister of Military Affairs, but he was posthumously granted the title of Distinguished Rank, which was a ceremonial title in the imperial bureaucracy.

During the reign of Emperor Shenzong, I served as a high-ranking official and found the prevailing views to be incorrect. At that time, I held the position of Censor-in-Chief and was strongly aligned with Zhang Zifeng and Cai Jing. Consequently, there were discussions about the destruction of families through grand and minor offenses. Some even accused Sima Guang, Lu Ji, Liu Xun, and Liang Kuangzhi of grave offenses against the state. As a result, hundreds of officials and scholars were punished for their writings, and they could be considered the most wicked and evil individuals. The grandiose ambitions of becoming a "divine chancellor" came from their mouths, expressing excessive self-importance and hoping that others would fear to criticize them. Even celestial beings, if they were to fall to the mortal world, would not behave in such a manner. Historical accounts from the Tang Dynasty describe Li Yifu, Li Linfu, and Lu Qi as "immortals," and they were remarkably similar to the individuals I am discussing. One of their sons, who was executed for criticizing his father, and another who was exiled to Fuzhou, saw their ancestral worship cease. Divine justice is clear and unforgiving. --- Ye Zuyi, also known as Ziyu, was from Wuzhou. He studied at the Imperial Academy and gained a reputation for his sharp wit and playful nature. His tendency to joke often led to resentment wherever he went. He once remarked, "There are people in the world who lack discernment and understanding." He would often express this with a couplet: "Drunk, I write the poem 'Moon Eclipsed' by Lu Tong on a black lacquer screen." After passing the imperial examinations, he became a professor in Hangzhou, where he treated his students and colleagues with disdain, causing widespread resentment. Eventually, he was dismissed from his post, and no one came to bid him farewell. Only a few monks from West Lake, who were somewhat friendly, accompanied him out of the city. Ye and the monks drank wine, shared stories, and sang half-drunk: "As if in a dream, as if in a dream, the monk leaves the city gates." Those who heard this reaction with great amusement.

### Final Translation --- ### Liling County Magistrate The Liling County Magistrate, whose name is unknown, had previously suffered from a wind-related illness. After recovering, he took up his post and received an official summons to go to Hengyang to measure land. He entered a deep valley where there was no meat to be bought. He saw one of his attendants catch a pangolin, which was then cooked and eaten. He tasted a few pieces, and his illness flared up again, leaving him with paralysis in both hands and feet. He then requested to return home. On his way to Yongzhou, he met a man named Sun Shaowei, who felt pity for his suffering and gave him one or two types of medicine from his chest. After ten days, Sun heard that the man had suddenly become strong and healthy again, believing the medicine to have been effective. Upon arriving in Yongzhou, Sun read a geographical record that stated pangolins should not be killed near dikes, as their blood, once it enters the soil, will cause the dike to collapse and cannot be repaired. This is because the pangolin has the ability to penetrate the earth's veins. Sun then realized that the magistrate's long-standing illness, which had temporarily flared up and then healed, was due to the smooth flow of qi and blood. In his hometown, there were many pangolins, but no mention of their blood causing dike damage. ### Daozhou Dwarfs In Daozhou, there were dwarfs mentioned in the satirical poems of Bai Juyi. Today, such dwarfs are rare in the state capital, but the counties of Jianghua and Ningyuan have the most. Sun Shaowei passed through these areas and asked local people, who said that these dwarfs were born due to the influence of the qi of monkeys. Monkeys are afraid of the sound of bamboo fans, so wealthy women, during pregnancy and sleep, would always order their servants to beat their abdomen with a fan, preventing monkeys from approaching. Poor women, however, could not afford to do this. Since they slept deeply, they would often dream of monkeys interacting with them. Their children would then be born short and stunted, becoming dwarfs. The two counties are adjacent to Zhaohe, fifty miles from Jiuyi Mountain, and are both prone to malarial diseases. On the mountain ridges, monkeys gather in large numbers, pulling each other and showing no fear of people. Their spirits can also harm people. ---

**The County Magistrate of Liling** The county magistrate of Liling had lost his name; it is unknown. He once suffered from a wind disease, but later recovered. After taking office, he received an official summons to go to Hengyang to measure land. Venturing deep into the mountain valleys, where there was no meat to buy, he saw one of his attendants catch a pangolin and cook it. He tasted a few slices, and soon after, the illness returned, causing his left and right hands and feet to become paralyzed. He then resigned and returned home. On his way to Yongzhou, Sun Shaowei met him along the road and, moved by his suffering, searched through his medicine chest and gave him one or two kinds of medicine. After only ten days, he heard that the man had suddenly regained his strength, and his long-standing illness had vanished. He believed this to be the effect of the medicine. But when he arrived in Yongzhou and consulted the geographical records, he read that pangolins must not be killed near dikes, for once their blood enters the soil, the dikes will inevitably collapse and cannot be sealed again. This is because the nature of this creature is to penetrate the earth's veins. At this point, he realized that the magistrate's previous illness had temporarily flared up and then subsided because his blood and qi had become unblocked. Although our village is full of these creatures, we have never heard the saying that their blood can ruin dikes. **Dwarfs of Daozhou** Dwarfs in Daozhou were mentioned in Bai Letian’s satirical writings. Today, such people are rare in the city itself, but they are most commonly found in Jianghua and Ningyuan counties. When Sun Shaowei passed through that area, he asked the local people, and they said that these dwarfs were all born under the influence of the spirit of monkeys. Monkeys are afraid of the sound of bamboo fans. Wealthy women, when pregnant and lying down to sleep, would always command their maids to tap their bellies with a fan, so that monkeys would not dare to approach. Poor women, lacking the means to do this, would often fall asleep and dream that monkeys came and had intercourse with them. When they gave birth, their children would be short and become dwarfs. These two counties are adjacent to Zhaohe, fifty li from the Jiuyi Mountains, an area known for its miasma and disease. On the mountain ridges, monkeys gather in hundreds, moving together and showing no fear of people. Their spirits can even harm humans in this way.

**The Snake of Wen Di's Family** In the county of Yuduan, at Runbei Patrol Camp, Wen Di, a military officer skilled in capturing bandits, rose through the ranks to become a Du Yu Hou. After his death, over a decade passed before his wife also passed away. His son, Bai Yi, was mourning at home when he suddenly saw a huge snake, about the length of a person's arm, measuring about ten feet, wrapped around his father's coffin. No one knew where it came from. The whole family was terrified and dared not approach. After two days, the snake left and immediately entered the house of a neighboring soldier named Zhang Jin, killing him. Just a few days later, Zhang died, and then Bai Yi followed. This occurred in the fifth month of the fourth year of the Shaoyi era. **The Deity of Xiangyi Fang** In the first month of the first year of the Qinyuan era, a man named Zhou翁 from Pingjiang City suffered from malaria that did not stop. He once heard people say that malaria ghosts can be avoided by going to another place. So, at dusk, he sneaked into the city god temple and lay down under the god's seat. The temple officials did not know this. At midnight, he saw a procession of lights, soldiers, and officials attending the city god, who sat on a high platform. A yellow-robed soldier led several people to the courtyard, where they were dressed formally and attended the city god. The city god asked, "I have been ordered by God to spread illness in this area; each of you is a deity of a district, how can you delay?" They all kowtowed and wished to obey the order. One of the deities spoke up, saying, "I am in charge of Xiangyi Fang."

The residents of this neighborhood are all virtuous and without fault, and it would be difficult to use illness and suffering to trouble them. The king became angry and said, "This is a divine protection for you. Your small duty is only fit to be carried out." The deity again said, "Since it cannot be avoided, how about using children to make up the numbers?" The king pondered for a long time and said, "If it must be so, then it is excused." They each agreed and withdrew. The next day, Mr. Zhou returned to his home and told everyone about it. All of them laughed and thought it was a delusion, not believing a word of it. By February, a great epidemic broke out in the city, but only the Xiangyi neighborhood had young children suffering from the illness, proving that Mr. Zhou's words were not false. When the sick recovered, the neighborhood residents collectively collected money to build a grand temple to repay the land deity's benevolence. In Xi'an, there was the Purple Lady. Wuxing Zhou Quan, known as Xunbo, served as the magistrate of Xiushan County in Quzhou during the fifth year of the Qindao era. He invited the local scholar Shen Yanian to be his guest. Shen was able to summon the Purple Lady deity, who would always accurately predict future events and was particularly skilled in writing, producing fresh and agile works that surpassed expectations. Whenever Zhou had spare time, he would visit to observe her. Once, while Zhou was listening to the chatter outside the window, he heard crows cawing loudly. He tried to ask, "The crow's cawing seems quite joyful. I wonder what news it brings?" The deity immediately wrote a quatrain, with the last couplet reading, "Before the door, who is connecting? What is the reason for looking ten thousand miles away to see you ascend the leopard pass?" Zhou laughed and said, "I am only a humble county magistrate. How excessive is your reverence for me, great deity?" That day, Shen was with a small official holding a winnowing basket. Suddenly, the basket leaped up, and the deity quickly wrote on the official's cheek, "Not clean." Zhou's nephew, Hu Chaoju, was standing by and took his place. The deity then raised its head, held a brush toward Zhou, and for a moment seemed to be concentrating. Everyone was startled. She slowly approached the table and wrote several dozen characters, roughly meaning, "I usually see..."

The provincial governor appeared lethargic, with a dull complexion. Today, upon observing his forehead and temples, I noticed that his brow was bright and clear. Within twenty-one days, he will surely receive a joyful summons; you must take this seriously, do not dismiss it as a jest. It is the end of October. On November 13th, a high-ranking official from Lin'an arrived to announce the summons. Two days later, the official document arrived. Zhou was promoted to a higher rank for capturing counterfeiters, and the case was further reviewed by the central government. It has been only eighteen days since the previous report. After three years, Zhou was promoted to govern Wuchang, and Shen accompanied him. Zhou invited the local monk Zhi Yong to serve as the head of a small temple. Bai Xian said, "This monk is extremely skilled in playing the qin and chess." Bai Xian asked if he could write a letter of recommendation for him. Bai Xian took up the pen and wrote immediately, including a striking sentence: "With five strings, he plays through ancient melodies; at the end of the game, one move reveals the deeper principles." The words were both elegant and profound, touching on the essence of Zen. The vice-governor Fang Jie hosted a banquet for guests, borrowing a courtesan from the prefecture. Zhou invited Bai Xian to join.

A Poem Requested for a Gathering During a leisurely gathering, a poem was requested to entertain the guests. The host asked for a composition about a red peony, with the word "捻" (pinch) as a key term. Another request followed, for a poem titled "瑞鹤仙" with "捻" as the rhyme. The task was completed effortlessly, and the poem went as follows: "Beholding the delicate red peony, one sees the fine texture of its petals. It is like Xi Shi, who once took care of the thousand-petaled peonies of the Western Zhou capital, which were vied to be planted and admired in gardens and pavilions. Why not daily stroll among the light and flowing fabrics, expending much effort in harmonizing the warmth and balance? In the morning, the dew moistens the fragrant buds, and the redness of the petals, like a blush on the cheeks, is captivating. The '姚黄' (Yao Huang) and '魏紫' (Wei Zi) varieties, with their national beauty and heavenly fragrance, lean against the wind, shy and hesitant, as if trying on cloud-like hairpins, stirring the wild bees and butterflies. As the spring god opens the banquet, the joy of the occasion is such that one should not hesitate to offer and receive in abundance. Watching the '红药' (red medicine) peonies bloom, the servant girl completes the poem without further revision. Other poems, though numerous, can all be enjoyed. It is said that during the reign of Emperor Shaoxi in the year Jia Yin, Zhou served as the Deputy Governor of Fujian, and his eldest son, Wu Er, obtained this account while in Fuzhou." This translation aims to capture the poetic and descriptive nature of the original text while maintaining its historical and cultural context.

The County Magistrate of Huangpi In the county of Huangpi, Huangzhou, there was a certain official who was exceptionally pure and cautious in his conduct, and he never committed any mistakes while in office. One day, as he sat in his study during the daytime, reviewing historical texts and literature, he suddenly saw a woman wearing a hat standing beside him. She appeared angry and said, "Officer, I have been searching for you for a long time, and now I finally found you."
