Another woman, resembling a wet nurse, came with a baby and tried to mediate, saying, "Many things have happened, but let's rest for now." The woman with the hat replied, "We will meet again tomorrow." As promised, she returned the next day, even angrier, and struck the official several times on the right cheek. The other woman tried to stop her, but soon the woman with the hat handed the baby to her and also struck the official ten times on the cheek. From then on, she came daily and always hit the cheeks until they were swollen and painful, unbearable to endure. The county magistrate, Li Desou from Jianchang, led the county clerk and the county magistrate to view the situation. The two women did not hide at all. Li Desou blessed them, saying, "The official has an elderly mother. Why not be a bit more lenient? I suspect it is a grudge from a past life. Should be made to perform great deeds, which will help in redemption. I hope the lady will have compassion." Both replied, saying, "The official is very intelligent. Immediately understood the beginning and end of the matter. However, this matter cannot be easily resolved. Now, let's go." Then, there was no further effect. The county applied to the prefecture to request the official's resignation and searched for a doctor for over a month. The Ministry of Personnel issued an order, and he arranged boats to return east to Wu. Colleagues held a farewell banquet by the river pavilion. At the moment of parting, the official suddenly exclaimed, "She came again," but others did not see anything. The official shouted loudly to rebuke, "Cut out the eyes, immediately raised his hand to cut out both eyes. Then, cut out the lungs and intestines, filling the ground, and died." This was the matter during the Xuanhe period. Li Desou's name was Bing.

**The Deputy Magistrate of Huangpi** A certain deputy magistrate of Huangpi in Huangzhou was a man of great refinement and caution, and during his tenure in office, he committed no faults. Once, while sitting in his study during the day, reviewing historical texts, he suddenly saw a woman wearing a cap standing beside him. Her expression was filled with anger, and she said, "Sir, I have been searching for you for a long time, yet now I finally see you." Another woman, resembling a nursemaid, came with an infant and tried to pacify her, saying, "After so much time, how can you still be so upset? Let us leave this for now." The woman in the cap replied, "We shall meet again tomorrow." As promised, she returned the next day, and her anger was even fiercer. She struck the deputy magistrate's right cheek more than ten times. The nursemaid tried to stop her, but soon after, she handed the infant to the woman in the cap, who then struck the deputy magistrate's cheek ten times with her palm. From then on, she came every day, striking his cheek until it swelled and became unbearable to endure. The county magistrate of Jianchang, Li Desou, accompanied by the chief clerk and county constable, came to inspect the situation. The two women showed no fear or hesitation. Li Desou prayed to them, saying, "The deputy magistrate has an elderly mother. How can you not slow down a little? I suspect this is a grudge from a past life, and I will offer you great merit and fruit, hoping you will be recommended and thus be released. I beg you, Madam, to have compassion." Both women replied, "Your honor is wise; with one glance, you have understood the beginning and the end. However, this matter cannot be pursued further. We shall leave now." And so they vanished, leaving no further trace. The county submitted a report to the prefecture requesting that the deputy magistrate be relieved of his post to seek medical treatment. After a month, a decree from the Ministry of Personnel arrived, and he then prepared a boat and returned east to Wu Zhong. His colleagues saw him off at the riverside pavilion. As they were parting, the deputy magistrate suddenly exclaimed, "She is coming again!" But no one else saw her. He shouted loudly, "Pluck out my eyes!" and immediately raised his hand and plucked out both of his eyes. Then he pulled out his lungs and intestines, which lay all over the ground, and died. This event took place during the Xuanhe era. Li Desou's name was Bing.

**Water Grand Marshal** Li Dai, courtesy name Yi Kuang, posthumously titled Jianchang Gong Ze, was the son of a high-ranking official and served as a deputy director of the Transport Bureau. Once, he held an official document and traveled to Hebei, arriving late at an inn. The innkeeper arrived first and saw a sign that read "Water Grand Marshal summons." Li asked the innkeeper where this was, but the official could not provide an answer. He listened carefully but heard no sounds inside. The innkeeper then hung the sign on a pillar. When Li arrived, he heard someone cursing, saying, "Both of us are officials, how can we press each other?" The person claimed to be the grandson of one official, the son of another, and related to yet another by marriage, all of whom were high-ranking officials, and their names were those of prominent figures from the Tang Dynasty. Li did not respond and did not engage in competition, instead resting under the eaves. Soon after, he heard someone say, "Wait until we move out and return to our original positions." Shortly, a child with braided hair, naked and barefoot, wearing only short trousers, appeared. The child's body was golden, and he was about fifteen or sixteen years old, carrying two empty buckets. Following him was a woman with a bun, wearing a black skirt, leading a white horse as white as snow. Last came an elderly man wearing a hat with a circular brim and a yellow robe, carrying various items, like a stable hand. He shouted, "Grand Marshal," bowed deeply to the child, and then quickly left. They all went to the inn gate, entered a large pool of water, and disappeared. I recently heard Wang Jiashou recount this story.

Mr. Hu of Meipu in Le Ping was a man of good character, diligent in his studies, and respected by his fellow villagers. He lived harmoniously with his family and neighbors, without any minor quarrels, and delegated household affairs to his children, never concerning himself with them. Suddenly, he seemed unhappy and said to his wife, Lady Yu, "I want to hang myself." Upon hearing this, she was shocked in many ways and tried to dissuade him, but he remained discontented and did not understand her efforts. Secretly, she considered this a worry and hid all ropes, belts, and other potential means of harm. At night, she made him sleep inside the bed and ordered a maid to sleep on a stool, in order to prevent him from doing anything. These precautions continued for more than a year, but he became increasingly tired and lazy. One night, he was lost, and she quickly blew out the lamp and searched the house thoroughly. He was found in the weaving room, using a large handful of silk to tie a knot around his neck on the loom. His body was cold, and his tongue protruded, indicating that he was dead. The Dragon Cave of Chu Field Mr. Zheng Beiyong of Wu Prefecture oversaw the salt field in Chu State, which was very close to the sea. He often saw dragons hanging, sometimes golden, sometimes blue, sometimes white, sometimes red, sometimes black, winding and twisting, rising and falling with the clouds, but he could not see their heads and horns. Locals said they were most afraid of the Dragon Cave, as every time it appeared, there was a flood that greatly damaged the salt production. One day, suddenly, he saw a large hole appearing on the ground, with deep passages branching off, which was where the dragon entered and exited. The salt field workers went to look, but there were no traces left. The place was full of turtle, crab, snail, and clam shells. Sometimes inside the clams, there were statues of Guanyin, with dignified appearance, adorned with pearls and necklaces, holding a willow branch and a pure vase. Also inside the crab claws, there was a carved demon with hair standing upright, looking strange and terrifying. Such things were not one. Mr. Zheng took several items and kept them, now serving as the magistrate of Fuliang, occasionally showing them to guests.

Zhu Xian Encounters a Ghost Zhu Xian, a soldier from Raozhou, served my family as a horseman for twenty years, being diligent and cautious without fault. In the first year of the Qingyuan era, in June, he went to the Cheng family in LePing for some business and returned with other soldiers to White Deer Ridge on the 26th day of the month, during the daytime. Suddenly, he bowed his hands three times to the left. His companions asked him why he did that, and he said he saw three or four officials sitting together but did not greet them, and there was nothing to be seen there. Just then, they suspected it was a vague and mysterious state, and shortly after, he lowered his head, cried out in pain, and could barely be supported to the inn. By then, he could not speak and died that night. The locals said that where Zhu Xian bowed his hands, there was an old small temple, and they suspected that the ghost was causing harm. --- The *Yiji* (Strange Tales) Volume 6 of the *Yijianzhi* (Record of Unusual Events) <Subcategory: Fiction, Oddities, *Yijianzhi*> Imperial Library Catalogue *Yijianzhi* Volume 7 By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Horses from Ezhou Horses purchased from Qin and Shu to be sent eastward are typically organized into groups of fifty, led by military officers. Many horses often go missing along the way, so a supervisor was placed in Hanyang to rest the horses for five days before waiting for the three military divisions on the Yangtze River to collect and dispatch them. They would first go to the Lake-Guang General Command for verification of the horses' colors and ages, which were no different from those managed by the Sichuan Horse Office. Only then would they continue their journey. In the ninth year of the Qiandao era, the deputy commander of the palace guard, Cheng, returned to Hanyang again. On that day, they were about to cross the river, and the squad leader said that according to the old custom, one must prepare offerings of meat and wine to pay homage at the city god temple before proceeding. This would ensure a smooth journey. Cheng did not respond and was asked again, but suddenly he became angry and scolded, "What does fetching official horses have to do with the city god?"

A deity or spirit commanded them to depart. That evening, they crossed the river and camped at the city gates. The city gate was opened at the fifth drumbeat, and all the horses were guided to the headquarters. They had to open the gate to enter. Suddenly, they heard the sound of hooves coming from the west. The soldiers thought another convoy had arrived and stood up, warning each other to hold their horses tightly to prevent any collisions. Soon, the other horses had arrived. In the darkness, it was impossible to tell how many there were. The horses began to collide, kick, and bite, and the situation became uncontrollable. After about two hours, as dawn approached, they looked around and saw no other horses. The entire convoy of horses had died, half of them with broken ribs and bloody intestines, as if they had been attacked by spears. Everyone believed that the deity had punished them for neglecting its will. At that time, Li Yuanzuo was the overall leader. --- Two Dogs of E Zhu Li Yuanzuo, while in Ezhu, obtained two dogs from the region between the Xiang and Han rivers. These dogs had a fierce and intimidating appearance, quite different from ordinary dogs. He ordered them to be kept in the backyard, fearing they might harm someone, and usually restrained them. One day, the guards temporarily released them, allowing them to roam freely, but still tied their necks. The backyard was adjacent to the military camp, and the wall was not solid. A group of ten or so dogs from the camp rushed over, bullying and biting the two dogs. The two dogs were tied with neck ropes and could not resist, and both were attacked and bitten. The guards chased away the group of dogs and dragged the two dogs back to the original place, tying them up again. As a result, the two dogs did not eat for ten days, as if they were filled with anger. The soldiers sometimes discussed among themselves, saying these two dogs were not comparable to ordinary dogs, yet they had suffered harm. Now that they were not eating, could they also be harboring a desire for revenge? Why not release their bindings and let them satisfy their resentment?

To determine victory or defeat, all agreed, and they released them into the pen of dogs. The dogs saw them as before and could be deceived, barking collectively and rushing forward. Two dogs met them with a vigorous attack, like a tiger facing sheep and pigs; some choked them, others broke their ribs, and all died immediately. A total of four or five dogs were killed, and the rest all fled. The soldiers cooked and ate the meat of the dead dogs, and after being satisfied, they returned home to give the leftovers to their wives and children. After ten days, they recalled the benefit of the meat, and they released them again as before. They wandered until dusk without any coming. Soon, two large and fierce tigers unexpectedly arrived. Even though they held sticks and chased them, the tigers did not retreat. After a while, both the guest and the host lost one of their tigers. The surviving tigers bled and cried out, then scattered. They did not survive the night and both died. It seems that the four dogs fought fiercely and none escaped, and the animals harbored resentment that could not be resolved. --- Xinglin Temple Heron Thirty li east of Fuxin County in Hongfu Prefecture, there is a temple called Xinglin Temple. The temple has a pine hill with tall pine trees reaching the sky, and birds gather in flocks on top of it, with herons being the most numerous. Each year, they raise their young, and by autumn, they leave. A villager named Wang Liu from the nearby town of Jiangan, who could climb trees, often climbed high to take the chicks to feed his family. Over ten years, thousands of people suffered from his cruelty. In the summer of Shaoxi Jia Yin, he led his followers to the pine trees below, tied a small cage to a branch, climbed up, and reached the top where an old heron was, which cried bitterly. Soon, the herons flew together and surrounded Wang Liu, pecking his legs and grabbing his eyes. Wang Liu tried his best to hold onto the pine tree, but his hands could not let go. His followers looked up and shouted, "Don't take the chicks, and hurry down." But before he reached halfway, the herons still wouldn't let go, and he fell and died, his body looking like an axe.

### The Ox and Tiger of Ru Ridge In Jianchang County, there is a village called Kungze, and on the western side of the village lies Ru Ridge, which rises to a great height. The distance from the peak to the base is nearly ten li. A man who lived near the foot of the ridge owned a very large water buffalo. Every morning, he would send a child to graze the buffalo at the base of the ridge, allowing it to graze on grass until dusk, after which he would bring it back. During the winter of the year 1189 (the first year of the Chunxi era of Emperor Xiaozong), the buffalo suddenly disappeared. The entire family searched the mountains but found no trace of it. They suspected that it had been stolen by thieves and reported the matter to the village security system. Three days later, a woodcutter reported that he had seen the buffalo cross the ridge and was seen leaning against a stone cliff, facing a tiger. The tiger made a threatening gesture, and everyone was afraid and dared not approach. The man's son, being very brave, drew his knife and charged forward to stab the tiger. Then, both the ox and the tiger were standing dead. It was very cold at the time, so they remained stiff and did not fall. The man and his two sons returned to butcher and skin them. They examined the meat and found no other abnormalities, except that the ox's heart and gallbladder were broken. It seems that although the ox had the strength to fight the tiger, it was so frightened that it died. ### Wang Xuan's Two Dogs During the winter of the year 1159 (the twenty-ninth year of the Shaoxing era of Emperor Xiaozong), a notorious bandit named Xie Junjiu from Yihuang County, Fuzhou, gathered a group of a hundred people, committing robbery and murder. They even killed the local wealthy man, the county magistrate's family. The county magistrate Li Yuanzuo happened to be in the city, and he sent archers to go out and capture them. The squad leader Liu Chao led dozens of people forward. The next day, Wang Xuan followed. When they met the bandits, Liu Chao fled. Wang Xuan's men were less than half the number of the bandits. He shouted for battle and fought fiercely at the foot of Huang Mountain. Wang Xuan always kept two dogs that followed him on outings, and at this time, they also barked and bit, killing about twenty bandits. The bandits then fled in panic, and Wang Xuan withdrew to the mountain. Later, the bandits returned and cut off the heads of the dead and carried them out. They were afraid that the soldiers would capture them and recognize their appearance and names.

**Translation:** Xuan was greatly angered when he heard that the name of the person involved had brought disgrace to their relatives. He said, "If I kill him and allow them to take his head away, then who will I use as an instrument to achieve my goal? I must take it." Thus, he led the troops down, and they fought again. After a long time, they were unexpectedly defeated. Xuan and two soldiers captured three levels and rushed to take the shortcut, turning westward. They penetrated through the dried reeds and fell into the mud. The bandits caught up with them, and all were slaughtered. Only two dogs remained alive, guarding their bodies. The neighborhood gathered around to report the matter to the county. The county official returned to the town and generously comforted the three families, ordering the preparation of coffins and going to collect the bodies. He still set up a small temple at that place. The dogs stood still for days without eating, and they saw the family members coming, wagging their tails to meet them at the place of the bodies. Xuan then buried them, and the dogs also died. The townspeople were amazed and sighed, making statues of them inside the temple to honor their loyalty. The Silkworms of the Hu Family in Nanchang In the 14th year of the Chongxi era, the silkworms in Yuzhang suddenly flourished, and the price of mulberry leaves was dozens of times higher than usual. The people were greatly worried, and they even cried in the silkworm room, ordering a monk to recite sutras and send them to the river. Rich families sometimes used large boards to float wicker baskets on top, placing paper money beside and writing a sign saying, "Those who are good friends and have plenty of mulberry trees in the lower reaches wish to offer this money to compensate and ask to raise these silkworms to avoid being ashamed before heaven and earth." Those who could not do so and had to discard them were all frowning and reluctant. Only the people of Zhongxiao Township in Nanchang County, Hu Er, had more than enough mulberry and Chinese parasol trees, enough to feed them. He was determined to sell the leaves to make a substantial profit. He discussed with his wife to bury the silkworms, but his wife disagreed. Hu Er paid no heed and called his son to bring a hoe, digging holes in the mulberry trees to bury all the silkworms, and he agreed to wait until dawn to collect the leaves and go to market, thinking he would be safe.

After drinking and sleeping past the third watch of the night, he heard a zipping sound from the wall and thought there was a thief. He lit a fire to investigate and found it was a silkworm. He swept it away with a broom, but it would appear again as soon as it was swept away, disturbing the whole family all night and causing fear. His wife especially blamed him, saying that his past mistakes were worse, and he became even more angry. He decided again to completely eliminate the silkworms. Only at dusk the next day did it settle down, and he did not regret it at all, but he regretted losing the profit from selling silkworms for a day. Soon after, he heard the zipping sound again. He called out, "It might be a monster coming again!" and rushed to light a lamp. As soon as he put his foot down, he felt that he was being bitten by insects and cried out in pain. His son also got up and felt the same way. His wife hurriedly ran to see and found countless centipedes all over the bed. They suffered from pain for several days. Tragically, the father died and the centipedes disappeared, but fortunately, the son was unharmed. Meanwhile, outside, other people's silkworms had already spun cocoons, and his mulberry leaves were abundant in the garden, but he couldn't make any money. The Ancient Tomb of the Tienwang Temple About three miles north of the city gate of Longxing Prefecture, there is the Tienwang Temple, which has a stupa containing relics. It is said that during the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui, relics were distributed to fifty states, and this stupa was one of them. On the day he first arrived at the temple, he saw a white-necked bird in front of the path, so the temple was also named after the bird. During the Jianyan period, bandits destroyed the stupa, but the base remained intact. The monks only built a few small houses to live in, and the temple could not be restored to its former glory. In the seventh year of the Chongxi era, a man from Hangzhou named Yu Sheng came to serve as the prefectural governor. His wife, Xu, had a dream of a strange monk leading her to an abandoned temple with the site of an old stupa and a gathering of birds. Xu, who was devout in her worship of Buddhism, told Yu about her dream and asked him to investigate the auspicious signs. Someone suggested the Tienwang Temple, and he went there, finding it exactly as he had dreamed. When Xu was young, she was the concubine of General Han Qi, and later married Yu, who was wealthy. She donated all the treasures in her purse to build the temple. The temple had been in ruins for a long time and was occupied by several families. Yu reported to the prefectural governor, who ordered them all to be evicted. One of the families had lived there for a long time and had built a deep and solid foundation.

A stone monument was found, with small stone figures arranged outside it, resembling modern funerary objects. These figures were a few inches tall, each with a different shape, and their craftsmanship was exquisite. People competed to take them. Another small stone tablet was also found, less than a foot tall and half as wide. Upon closer inspection, it appeared to be a mold made of clay and earth. The top of the tablet was decorated with lotus leaves, and the bottom with lotus flowers. There was a true script inscription on the tablet, which read "Temple of the Divine Martial Sage Emperor." On the sides, it read "21st Year of Zhenyuan." The observer,绅, felt fear upon seeing the inscription. He left one stone figure and one tablet, then covered the grave and built a high structure over it. The place is now a Dharma Hall.绅 did not dare to speak about this to others. After a long time, he secretly told Li Zhongshi to keep the discovery confidential. The era name "Zhenyuan" refers to the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang. In the 21st year of Zhenyuan, Emperor Dezong was buried in the tomb of Chongling in October. During his reign, he was called "Divine Martial Sage Emperor," and posthumously honored with the title "Divine Martial Filial Emperor." While the four characters are similar, the tomb was originally in Chang'an, and the inscription on the stone tablet is unclear. Later,绅 was appointed to a military position, and his wife died in office. In 1146, Li Yuanzuo bought Chen Shi, a commoner's wife, as a wet nurse for his daughter. As the daughter grew up, she no longer wanted to return to her mother. Chen Shi was fierce and often quarreled with others. In 1168, the daughter married Wang, and because Chen Shi was known for her cursing, she was not allowed to accompany her. Three years later, Li became a minister of the Ministry of Household, and Chen died in Lin'an. Her body was temporarily placed in a temple, and then cremated. The next day, when the servant went to collect the ashes, he heard a loud cry and saw a group of dozens of old and young people gathered around the fire, saying that the burning body smelled like lotus flowers, which was unusual, so they came to see. The servant stayed overnight and in the early morning, he called a monk to recite sutras before the group had gathered.

People from far and near had already gathered to look at the ashes, which were filled with relics. Everyone competed to take the relics. I was so focused on collecting the bones that I didn't have time to search for more relics, and I only managed to collect a few to take home. The color was like mud mixed with gold, and it shone brightly enough to be seen clearly. The shape was like a small child, not more than an inch tall. Li began to marvel at the strange phenomenon and asked the old women and maidservants. This old woman was usually harsh and unkind, and he wondered what kind of power or merit led to this. They all said there was no other good deed, but every morning she would recite the Lotus Sutra more than ten times. She could not remember the entire text, only a few lines. There was a lotus flower in a pot with a jade immortal sitting in it, and every day, she would hear the clear bell and clean water, and that was all. The old woman in her past life must have accumulated bad karma from cursing others, but because she recited the sutra for twenty-six years, she received this reward. It is also due to her strong good intentions, they said. Fan was an official. In the year Ximao of the Qian Dao era, Jiang-Zhe suffered a great drought, especially in Yuzhang. Long Shi, the governor, ordered the counties to register wealthy people who stored grain and to demand they sell it. They were to transport the grain themselves to a designated place according to their distance. Each village chose one capable person as an official to oversee the distribution. Fan, a resident of Xianmen Village in Jing'an County, was selected. His neighbor Zhang had to sell two thousand dou of grain. He told Fan, "Based on the situation, at the official price, it's less than two-thirds of the market value. I lose a lot more than I gain. We have been together for a long time, and you should protect me. Why don't you give me a false number to report to the authorities? One day, I will repay you." Fan was happy with his words and hoped for future thanks, so he agreed to help Zhang. Zhang no longer gave any grain. Many in the neighborhood knew about it but were afraid of the two families' power and dared not reveal it. In the autumn of the year Renchen.

Before the great harvest, the previous matters had ceased, and Fan and Zhang became even more friendly as a result. In the spring of the year of Gui Si, Fan died of a minor illness. As they were preparing to bury him, he revived and called to his younger brother, saying, "I just arrived at a public office. The palace buildings were severe, and the officials were arrayed. They made me bear responsibility to reduce my lifespan by two decades. I recall that throughout my life, I had no wrongdoing and refused to comply. The officials said that last year, you were a local official and falsely declared selling two thousand stones of rice to the Zhang family, leading to the starvation of several people. Is this not a grave offense? I remember that at the time, the Zhang family only recognized one thousand stones. Now, what they say is double that amount. I pleaded with these officials to release me to retrieve the documents. I was able to return temporarily. The official seals and documents were all stored in a certain chest. You should search for them for me, as I fear I might be able to help. My younger brother quickly went to retrieve them and found that it was indeed two thousand stones. Fan then closed his eyes. He was thirty-eight years old at the time. After a year, Zhang also died. Duan Fangming Duan Fangming, as recorded in the first chronicle, was the son of Prince of the Open Source Palace, Yu. At the time of his birth, Yu dreamed of someone reciting two lines of poetry: "Flags wave over the three thousand miles of Chu territory, clothes attract a band of clouds over the Qin region." Afterward, Fangming was born. He later served up to the position of Grand Master of Ceremonies, serving as a general in the states of Tan and Kui, fulfilling his father's dream.

### Translation **September Plum Blossom Poem** In the twenty-eighth year of the Shaoxing era (1158 AD), during the month of September in Chaozhou, Jieyang County, plum blossoms bloomed profusely in the east study of the county administration. Plum blossoms in the southern regions (Lingnan) bloom earlier than in Jiangsu and Zhejiang, but they still require the winter season to bloom. The local people found it very strange, and many scholars wrote poems, most of which were flattering the magistrate. At that time, Liang Zhenggong was a guest scholar staying in this study and also composed a poem: "Old chrysanthemums and withered phoenix trees in September frost, Who brought the warmth of spring into the east hall? Not because nature is particularly kind to people, Would it allow the southern branch to emit its special fragrance? The Nine Tripods require proper governance, The other flowers would be ashamed to claim their fragrance. When seen, the ice and jade are perfectly reflected, It would be good to take the dragon's roar and broadcast it in a musical composition. The meaning is extraordinary and similar to Wang Yigong. Although it has not yet been used to make a harmonious broth, It still blooms above the other flowers. The following year, he returned to Quanzhou and ranked first in the provincial examination. The next year, he became the top scholar in the national examination and eventually reached the position of Prime Minister." **The Cheng Family Camphor Tree** The Cheng family from Songziyuan, Poyang, had a large camphor tree in their ancestral home. It had been passed down for over twenty or thirty generations. The clan had grown so large that no one could claim ownership, so it was not pruned or cut down. It grew so tall that it reached the clouds. In the first year of the Qingyuan era (1195 AD), the clan leader Zhizhan and the people gathered to decide to give the tree to Jianshi Temple for them to cut it down. The abbot of the temple, Shaoxi, went to oversee the matter. The tree had a large growth (burl) that rose several feet. A carpenter climbed up to remove the burl. Once removed, there was clear water inside. A snake, dark brown in color, seemed surprised and jumped into the air. The carpenter told Shaoxi about this. Shaoxi was afraid and wanted to abandon the tree. Zhizhan said he would not allow it, saying, "We have gathered as a family to make this decision."

**Translation:** The tree by the Buddha channel was cut down, its trunk and branches completely removed, leaving only the main trunk standing upright like a rainbow dam. At the time, farmers were planting rice in the fields, and they beat drums to gather people, offering them cups of wine. They pulled and dragged the tree for less than ten steps, but it rolled back and forth, crushing five people. Zhi Wan's nephew also died, and several others were injured, with some having broken legs and faces. The dead family wanted to sue the official, but Zhi Wan said, "Unfortunately, this has happened to us. I myself bear the pain of my nephew; how could I bear to further trouble them?" In the end, he loaded the wood onto a cart and returned it to the temple. Shao Xi was imprisoned for other matters by the deputy county magistrate, shackled in the prison, about to be beaten but allowed to redeem himself with the wood, which is now a pillar of the Great Buddha Hall. --- The Flood of Qingtang During the middle of the fifth month in the summer of the year Bingmao in the Xingyuan era, heavy rain fell for seven days and nights in Raozhou. The rivers and lakes overflowed, with water entering the city over six feet deep, especially severe in Poyang and Fuliang. Qingtang Village, 90 miles from the prefectural capital, was home to the Liu family. At the time, several places in the courtyard had small holes through which water splashed like jumping pearls. The family thought it was just accumulated water and did not worry about it. On the 22nd day of the month, around noon, two large holes suddenly formed, and water gushed out from them. They then realized that a water disaster was inevitable. They gathered all their chests and boxes, tying them to the beams, and led their family onto a boat, heading towards the back mountain. Just halfway up the mountain, the water came rushing, and looking back, the old house had already been swept away by the current, not a single beam or tile remaining. When the water receded, it was seen that it had turned into a swamp, completely devoid of the previous form of the house. All the tools and utensils of daily life were swept away. Those who understood the situation said that dragons and water spirits took advantage of the water to harm people, but there was nothing to be done about it.

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume 7 The *Yijian Zhi* Volume 8 By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Wife of Lu Sheng of Zhujì In the administrative center of Zhujì County, there was a lake that surrounded the area. Lu Sheng, a resident of the county, lived on the shore of the lake and made paper money for burning as part of funeral rites. One day, after finishing his work at dusk, he was tired and went to bed. His wife was pregnant but overdue by two months. As his wife was not yet asleep, she felt abdominal pain and lay down beside him. After a while, Lu Sheng woke up and found his wife missing, though the door had never been opened. He realized she had fallen into a strange or supernatural realm and quickly lit a lantern to go outside and search for her, inviting neighbors to help. In the middle of the night, they heard a response from the lake. The moon was bright, and they saw that it was his wife. They took a young man with them to retrieve her. The wife grabbed the young man's sleeve and said, "Bring my child back to me." They reached the shore, and Lu Sheng brought her back. The fetus had already been lost. She could finally speak and said she had seen several people come into the room and call her out. They brought her to a place and led her into a small room where they arranged bedding, like a birthing room. She gave birth without realizing it. After washing, they wrapped her in swaddling clothes. People around her said it was a boy, truly delightful. Before she could see it, she suddenly saw nothing. Now thinking about it, it was almost as if she were dead. Fortunately, she survived. The next day, she acted normally and had no suffering. The lake, though not large, was connected to the river. It is suspected that the woman must have dreamed of encountering dragons or other mythical creatures during conception but was unwilling to speak about it. This occurred in the first year of the Qingyuan era, after the Zhongyuan Festival.

**A Taoist Priest from Mount Mao** Yangzhou's famous physician, Yang Jilao, was renowned for his medical skills. One day, a scholar from the city seemed to be suffering from an illness, feeling disgusted and unable to bear it, and he could not name what kind of illness it was. He went to consult Yang Jilao, who said, "Your condition is very severe, and your blood and qi are almost completely depleted. You will die from a back abscess in three years, and there is nothing I can do for you." The scholar was unhappy and left. He heard from his friends and relatives that there was a Taoist priest in the temple on Mount Mao who was a master of medicine but did not seek fame or fortune and might not be willing to help others. The scholar decided to dress as a servant and went to the mountain to pay homage to the priest, wishing to serve as a menial laborer under his guidance. The priest was pleased and took him as a disciple, teaching him to read the scriptures. Day and night, the scholar served the priest diligently and obediently, always pleasing him. After two months, the priest noticed that he was different from other servants and asked him about his background. The scholar apologized and told the truth. The priest smiled and said, "There is no illness that cannot be cured. Show me your pulse." After examining it, the priest laughed and said, "You can go down the mountain. I have no medicine for you. Just buy a good pear every day, eat it raw until it's finished, then use dried pears to make a decoction and drink it, still eating the residue. This illness will naturally resolve itself." The scholar followed the priest's advice carefully and after a year, he returned to Yangzhou. When Yang Jilao saw him, he was amazed by his healthy appearance and peaceful pulse and said, "You must have met a wise person. Otherwise, how could you have recovered so well?" The scholar told him everything, and Yang Jilao immediately put on his ceremonial robes, lit incense, and bowed towards Mount Mao, feeling that he had not reached the same level of skill. The *North Dream Trivial Talk* records that a scholar once fell gravely ill, and the physician Zhao E said, "There is only one method: please eat good pears without limit. If you can't chew and swallow them, squeeze the juice and drink it. There is a slight chance of recovery." The scholar followed this advice and recovered. The meaning is the same.

Huang Yan and His Brothers In the Wu region, the brothers Huang Yan and his siblings were involved in the imperial examinations. The eldest brother, Yan Yuan, had a name given to him by his father in a dream. The father dreamed that someone told him, "If you name your son 'Yan,' his wishes will surely be fulfilled." He followed the dream and named his son Yan. That year, Yan was recommended by the local authorities and passed the examination the following spring. For the next examination, his younger brothers were also to take the test. The father had another dream in which someone instructed him to name his two younger sons "Yan" as well. Upon waking, he discussed this with his wife, wondering how it could be that three brothers would have the same name. He had the dream again, and in it, he saw the character "Yan" split into two parts. He then named his second son "Yan" and his youngest son "Ye." All three brothers passed the examination together, and Wang Shunbo and Li Zhongshi, who were related to Huang Yan's descendants, had long-standing ties with the family. Wang Kui of Pingyang In Yongjia, a scholar had a dream in which he found himself at the foot of a great mountain. He saw a cave with a vast opening, and a grand temple stood before him. In the center of the temple sat a deity, and a green-robed official stood before him, holding a document and saying, "Present the list of this year's successful candidates." The scholar pressed closer to see the list and only saw the name "Wang Kui." The official pointed to the name and said, "This is Wang Tingyong of Pingyang." The scholar, who was acquainted with Wang Tingyong, replied, "He is not named Kui." The official insisted, "You must change his name." The scholar awoke from the dream and was overjoyed. He wrote a letter to Wang Tingyong, advising him to change his name to Kui, saying, "A scholar's success or failure is ultimately determined by fate. Why should you not believe in this one dream?" Wang Tingyong did not agree to change his name. A few days later, he had the same dream again. He told the scholar of his resolve, which remained unchanged. The official in the dream became angry and said, "Wang Xiucai, you are so stubborn and obstinate. I will force you to change your name!" The scholar, discouraged by the repeated dreams, stopped talking about it. Not long after, the provincial examinations were approaching, and the candidates submitted their examination papers, attaching their family registers. Wang Kui wrote ten papers, all of which were riddled with errors and were unusable. He suddenly realized the meaning of the dream and changed his name to Kui. He submitted one paper, which was selected, and he subsequently passed the examination in the year Ding-Chou, alongside Wang Shipeng on the list of successful candidates.

**Yangchun County** The southern regions are generally plagued by miasma and poison, and the state of Chenzhou is the worst. Since the Tang Dynasty, northerners who were exiled rarely returned alive. In our dynasty, it was abolished and became Yangchun County, subordinate to Nanling Prefecture. Once it became a county, officials and criminals could avoid punishment. This is a suggestion made by those with a kind heart. Those appointed as county magistrates, if they serve for three years, may be exempt from having to provide recommendations. This is because they are selected through the imperial examination system, and those who leave their capital posts cannot return. However, those who crave honor and advancement often take on these positions rashly. Only during the Chongxi era, Ye Yuanbian from Putian completed his term without harm to his family, but all his children died. One servant girl was ill and lying in a separate room. At night, she was heard moaning, and it sounded like she had phlegm and was suffocating. In the morning, she was found hanged from a beam. The beam was about ten feet away from the bed, and there was no ladder to reach it. It is said that those who did not die a good death became vengeful spirits, not just due to miasma. I, Qian Zhonghong, was the patrol inspector of Guishan in Huizhou when I witnessed these events; Ye was named Zi'ang, the nephew of a chancellor. **Wang Family Temple Monk** Outside the city of Huizhou, three miles away, is the Wang family temple built over the tomb of Wang Chaoyi's grandfather. During the Shaoxing period, the temple was inhabited by a monk named Huigong. The monk simply ate and sat, never reciting scriptures or performing rituals. The offerings and incense were very minimal, and he could only maintain himself without causing trouble. The Wang family was content with this. He served for twenty years before dying in 1166. There was a large Chinese parasol tree nearby, lush and thriving. A few months after his death, the tree suddenly withered and died. Mushrooms then grew on it. One of Wang Chaoyi's servants who tended the sheep saw the mushrooms and found them to be white and shiny. He picked them and presented them to his master. They were cooked and tasted very fragrant and sweet, almost as good as meat. They were picked every night and grew again the next day.

The inexhaustible source lasted until the third autumn, gradually becoming known outside. Some people came with money to buy it, but it was refused and not given. Fearing theft, a short wall was set up to protect it. The neighbors, envious and resentful, climbed over the wall at night to strip the roots bare. Suddenly, the fungus spoke like a person, saying, "This is not what you should eat. If you force it, you will surely suffer disaster. I am the former master of the temple. I received offerings without merit and did not feel ashamed to withdraw. After death, I was punished by the underworld to become a fungus to repay. The reason for its richness is that it is transformed from my essence and blood. Now my punishment is complete, and I will leave from here." The neighbors were frightened and retreated, and they told Wang Wang. Wang Wang still did not believe and went to check it himself. There was no fungus left, and he cut it down to use as firewood. Xi Han, a model of correctness, Liang Qidao served as the governor of Quzhou. There was a Wang Zongyu, who was transferred from the position of Grand Censor due to an official matter, and was appointed as the magistrate of Yudu. Upon arriving, he paid respects to the local officials, and after the ceremony, he was supposed to leave by the corridor. Liang considered him a fellow official and had known him for a long time, so he asked him to climb into the carriage. Wang refused to climb, saying he was afraid. Liang called him by his courtesy name, Xi Han, and said, "Don't be like that." He said it several times. The attendants thought it was his official title and passed the message, asking Xi Han to get into the carriage. Both the guest and the host laughed. Although Liang had a strict and stern nature, seeing that he was naive and unsophisticated, he did not question him further. Li Zheng, who was on a tour of inspection of mines and smelting in Guangxi, passed through Changsha, and the local officials prepared a document to greet him, calling him "Tidian Dazheng." Not knowing his name and mistakenly thinking it was his official title, similar to the title of a temple official, Li Zheng was angry and sent a letter to the governor of Tanfu to punish the officials and clerks. Zhang Jingfu, who lived in Tan, also wrote in the same manner. The governor, Liu Gongfu, laughed and said, "Others might not understand, but why did you do it?" Zhang felt embarrassed and laughed. When Li arrived, he apologized and said he was not capable.

小楼烛花词 In the fifteenth year of the Shaoxing era, on the fifteenth day of the third month, I completed the third examination in the ci competition in Lin'an. It was still early when I left the examination hall, so I went out with those who had taken the exam together—He Shan, Ming Xu, Bo, and Sheng. We explored the market together. At that time, my uncle Zhi, also known as Yingxian, and Xu Liangzuo, a fellow townsman and friend of Shun, had finished the provincial examination and joined us. We then went to Baodian Street. Bo had long-standing ties with the courtesan Sun Xiaojiu, so he invited her to visit her home. We set up a banquet on the small pavilion, and the night was as bright as day under the moonlight. We were standing by the railing, and inside the inner room, two candles formed beautiful patterns, resembling pearls. Sun was intelligent and understanding, and she spoke to the guests, saying, "Tonight, the moon is bright and the candle flowers are auspicious. All five of you are comparing your literary skills. This is a sign that you will all achieve high honors in the exams. Let us each compose a ci to record this event and make it a good story for the future." She took out five sheets of paper and placed them on the desk. Sheng, Yingxian, and Shun all declined to write, but Bo was quick-witted and agile. He immediately took up his brush and composed a ci titled "浣溪沙," which read: 草草杯盘访玉人，灯花呈喜坐添春。邀郎覓句要竒新。黛浅波娇情脉脉，云轻柳弱意真真。从今风月属闲人。 The guests admired and praised it, but they were dissatisfied with the last line. I then completed a ci titled "临江水仙," which read: 绮席留欢欢正洽，高楼佳气重重。钗头小烛花红。直须将喜事来报，主人公。桂月十分春正半，广寒宫殿葱葱。姮娥相对曲栏东。云梯知不逺，平步蹑东风。 Sun filled a cup and encouraged me, saying, "Scholar, you will surely pass this auspicious sign was set for you." Later, I indeed passed the exam and received a title, while the other four did not succeed.

### 南康戏语 Wang Zhongjia, exiled to Nankang and staying at a Buddhist monastery, once invited local officials for a banquet. Entertainers were present, including two named Yang and Li, who were slightly better in skill or appearance compared to others. The official in charge of managing Li was called "适理掾," and the official in charge of managing Yang was called "户掾." During the banquet, they often teased each other. The "适理掾" looked at the "户掾" and said, "You love your sheep, I love your rituals." The "户掾" replied, "It's useless to laugh at each other; this is recorded in the *Analects*." The guests laughed at them, but sought a fitting response. Ao Mi, using his chess skills, was playing a game with Wang Gong. While competing for a critical move, he thought of a solution. Shen Ling whispered from the side, and Wang said, "I already have an answer!" The bystanders were clear, but the players were confused. Everyone clapped and praised. ### 泗州邸怪 Zhao Delin, the Duke of Anding, during the early Jianyan era, traveled from the capital with his family eastward and arrived at the northern city gate of Sizhou. They stayed in an inn and, in the late afternoon, called for hot water. A concubine responded by holding a cup and bringing it forward, using a purple cap to cover her head. Zhao said, "You people are already in the room; why do you need to do this yourself?" It was just a skeleton. Gradually, it disappeared.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Song Dynasty, a member of the imperial family, whose name is now forgotten, was appointed as the temporary governor of Tingzhou following the rebellion led by Fan Ruiwei. There was no official residence available for him, so he temporarily stayed in the room of the magistrate and the watchmen. Many officials spoke of strange occurrences. Coincidentally, there was a vacancy in a regular position, so he stayed there for a while. This room was already haunted by seven ghosts, causing disturbances. In the early winter, with a mild cold, Zhao Sheng sat alone in a warm room. Suddenly, a guard appeared with more than ten calling cards, presenting himself for an audience. Zhao Sheng saw that the names on the cards all belonged to former governors of Tingzhou. He realized that these were ghosts and questioned the guard, asking why they had all gathered there. The guard replied that they were all former governors who had died in their official residences and had no place to rest or live, spending their days aimlessly. Hearing that Zhao Sheng had arrived, they came together to seek an audience. Zhao Sheng asked, "Who are you?" The guard replied, "I am also a former guest." Zhao Sheng said, "Since there are many guests, if we have one meeting, there will be no time for intimate conversation. Invite them one by one according to their order of arrival." The guard bowed and said, "Yes." Then he led one guest to Zhao Sheng, who received them with the courtesy due to guests. Zhao Sheng asked about their hometown, previous positions, and current family members. The guests answered as requested, without engaging in other topics, and left shortly after. Another guest was treated similarly. After all ten guests had been invited and thanked, Zhao Sheng said, "It is late, and tea and wine are not available. I cannot serve tea and soup, and there is no way to repay the visit. Please do not blame or criticize." They all nodded and bowed goodbye. Then silence fell. People said that Zhao Sheng, as a living person, faced ten ghosts, and his courage and bravery were admirable. His career should not be over yet. However, after only five years, he only became a governor of a certain state and died.

Zhang San娘 In the county of DeXing, Ye JiFu, a physician, had a wife named Zhang, who was the third daughter. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (Jianan era), she suddenly fell gravely ill and died. She had not yet been buried when she revived and reported that she was followed by a public official in official attire, walking through the outskirts and countryside. The place was remote and mysterious, and she did not know where she was. After a long while, she arrived at an official building and rushed to the courtyard below. There stood a nobleman sitting in the center. A servant presented a document, and the nobleman became furious, ordering him to be thrown to the ground. A jailer then beat his feet with a willow stick and shouted, "I was originally ordered to follow Zhang San娘 of Xumén in Jinshan Village, LePing County. How could there be a mistake now? This woman should have another twelve years of life and give birth to two sons. Why did you bring her here so soon? Immediately send someone else to return me." She then woke up. Ye JiFu was glad that she was reborn and did not have time to inquire about the Xumén family. Later, Zhang San娘 indeed gave birth to two sons and lived for exactly twelve more years before dying. Ye JiFu is still alive today. The Commoner of LePing In LePing County, an official named Liang Jie, who was responsible for recording and managing documents, committed a crime and was captured and sent to the state court by a criminal justice official. He died in prison. A commoner who had been a witness also died of an epidemic. After two inspections, the town guards wrapped his body in reed mats and buried it next to a random grave in Zhishan Temple. Three days later, a temple monk arrived there and saw that the earth covering the burial site had one hand protruding and reported it to the crowd. The monk ordered his servant to open it, and the man was already alive. They helped him out and gave him soup and porridge. The man looked dazed and could barely describe what he saw, only saying it was like a dream. Even though he became a person again, his face was completely pale like wax, lacking blood to nourish, and all his limbs were black. When he returned home, his wife had been taken by another man and no longer wanted to return to him. This man wandered and begged, still alive until now.

The Wife of the Servant at Meishou Temple During the reign of Emperor Ling, a man from LePing, He Heng Cheng, built his own burial ground and constructed a small temple, naming it Meishou. He invited a monk to manage it. Later, the monk left, and he only left a servant to manage the temple. One day, the servant went out to work in the fields and returned at dusk. He did not see his wife upon his return. He called out to others to help find her, searching everywhere, even in the mountains and dense underbrush, but found no trace of her. Exhausted, he returned home and tried to sleep, but could not rest deeply. He heard a sound inside the wall, like someone moving around and making small noises. He listened carefully and realized it was his wife. He quickly lit a candle, climbed a ladder, and broke open the wall. He found her lying on a mat inside, with no gaps around her, not even allowing birds or mice to enter. He asked her what she had seen, but she could not speak, appearing dazed and confused. There was no further incident after that. The Physician Shangguan Shangguan Yan Cheng, a physician from the same region, was a former soldier from Beijing. He claimed to be a resident of Beijing and said that during the reign of Emperor Huizong in the Song Dynasty, he passed the imperial examination in acupuncture and was appointed as a medical officer in the imperial court, eventually rising to the position of deputy official. All of this was false. In the early years of Emperor Xiaozong's reign, he came to Poyang. His medical skills were average, but he was known for exaggerating his abilities. A witty and humorous man from the imperial family, Gongyi, often joked with others. One day, while sitting with a group of guests, someone mentioned that a certain person's illness was concerning. Another guest suggested calling Shangguan to treat the illness. Gongyi frowned and said, "If Shangguan comes, then the lower-ranking official will have to leave." The guests all laughed heartily. This was because, in the state and county, military officers changed shifts daily at the hour of Shen. The officer on duty would loudly announce, "The first shift arrives," and the officer leaving would say, "The second shift leaves." Thus, they used this as a joke. Shangguan heard this and was very offended by the words, and his reputation gradually declined.

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Eight The *Siku Quanshu* The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Nine By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Small Box of the County Magistrate's Subordinate Lü Shuzhao served as the temporary county magistrate of Taiping. Wang Sheng, his colleague, was leaving due to personal matters. On his departure, he handed over a black wooden box and several other items to be stored in Lü's residence, emphasizing the importance of carefully guarding the box and preventing any damage. He repeated this instruction several times, but Lü did not fully grasp its significance. After accepting the responsibility, Lü placed the box on a wooden partition in his bed. Initially, he heard faint sounds coming from the box, which he mistakenly attributed to mice. He tried to scare them away by tapping on the floor, and the sounds ceased. The box was locked, facing south, but after some time, it seemed to turn its face to the wall, as if someone had moved it. Lü's wife, thinking it was misplaced, straightened it again. The next day, the box turned to face north-south, and Lü began to feel uneasy. Several months later, Wang Sheng returned to retrieve the box. He also requested a boat to take him to Jiangjin, where he slowly inquired about its contents. It turned out that the box had contained a courtesan with whom Wang had been closely associated. She had died, and Wang had personally gone to her burial site, cremated her coffin, and wrapped her remains in a small container, placing them in the box. That day, he had thrown the box into the river. It was now clear that the strange occurrences were caused by the courtesan's spirit. Lü, reflecting on the events, felt a sense of fear and unease. He removed the wooden partition and felt as if he was being watched.

**Jiankang Temple of the Three Saints** In Jiankang, the local customs involve many activities related to the three saints, and a temple has been built to worship them. The statue is a single body, and it is unknown which specific deities it represents. The temple's influence is quite prominent, and both officials and common people show great respect and reverence to it. In Jurong County, there is a temple located inside the east side of the main gate of the county administration building. Every spring, many people come to offer sacrifices, and the scene is noisy and crowded from morning until evening. In the year 1191, a man named Yan Jingyan from Wu served as the county magistrate. He wanted to block the entrance of the temple, and the officials and soldiers advised against it, fearing it would bring bad luck. Yan reluctantly agreed to stop. The following year, the noise became unbearable, so a short wall was built to limit access. From then on, visitors had to take a detour through the back of the official quarters to reach the temple, and the number of visitors decreased compared to before. Yan was about to complete his term and requested permission to return home. In June 1194, the people of the county went to the temple to burn incense and make an announcement about the previous events.

The obstruction of the sacrificial rites was all due to the will of Yan Xiancheng. Do not blame our people; Yan arrived at his home and suddenly fell ill with diarrhea and died that night. Yan's name was Shuping, the son of Lü Zi, the vice minister, who was promoted to the imperial examination at a young age, bright and lively, but suddenly died, causing grief among his friends and colleagues. Was it that he was destined by heaven to be taken by one god, or was it not a mere coincidence? The Jiangning Magistrate's temple was also like this. All officials who came must pay respects. The magistrate of Danyang, Liu Pingguo, alone refused to show respect. Whenever his sedan chair passed by the temple, he would raise his sleeve to cover his face. His younger brother, who was accompanying him, died of a minor illness. His wife, Tao, was pregnant and fell ill, dreaming that a god said, "Your husband was disrespectful to me, and I have already taken your younger brother. If he does not repent, I will take you too." Tao told Liu about the dream, but he did not believe her. Tao eventually died, and Liu began to regret and fear, going himself to pray and apologize, and then the matter was resolved. Fan Chengji was the younger brother of the Shihu Censor. He had a stubborn and violent nature. Whenever he followed his elder brother to reside in a remote place, he was always troubled and obstructed. In the year Shaoshi Jia Yin, he was appointed as the Deputy Magistrate of Jiankang. On the evening of the Winter Solstice, the cook's maid reported that the steamer was making strange noises. The whole family was afraid and wanted to seek help from monks and sorcerers to ward off the evil, but Fan ignored it. The strange noises became more intense, sometimes roaring like chickens, dogs, and other animals. The sounds were loud and could be heard outside. Fan personally broke the steamer with a knife. That day, the sounds came from the pot, and the pot and stove were broken. Various strange phenomena occurred, and the table and chairs stood upright by themselves. The heating stove moved and jumped, but it did not fall. It danced and leaped as if nothing was wrong. The disturbances lasted for several months, and the family gradually got used to it. The next year on the 8th day of the 4th month, while he was with his colleagues in the capital, he suddenly felt a severe cough and could no longer bear it. His attendants helped him get on the carriage, and he barely reached home. There was a stream of saliva in his throat, and he made a sound like 'yiyi.' His family said it was exactly like before. Many doctors took his pulse and prescribed medicine, but none could be taken. Soon, he let out a loud cry and died.

Chen Yuancheng, the Attendant Scholar, was from Fujian. He was naturally inclined toward the Dao. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, he often served in the background of General Yue Fei's military campaigns but was later persecuted by Qin Hui, the chancellor, and forced into seclusion for several years. He then abandoned his ambitions for official rank and built a thatched hut to the south of the Great Peak of Damao Mountain in Jurong. He set aside his wife and concubines and constructed an octagonal platform, where he worshiped the stars morning and evening. In his spare time, he focused his mind inward, practicing Taoist meditation and cultivating contentment with little desire. He purchased several acres of farmland at the foot of the mountain to support Taoist recluses. Every spring, in the second month, the birthday of Damao Jun was celebrated, and people from all walks of life, including commoners, officials, and Taoist practitioners, gathered to offer sacrifices and perform rituals. The venue was always fully occupied, but the temple of Yuan Daiwang, located to the north, was the most popular. One day, Chen went to pay his respects and encountered a tall man with blue eyes and large pupils. The man wore a garment made of oak leaves and held an eight-sided fan to block the path and beg for alms. Chen had a large coin from the reign of Emperor Huizong in his pouch, which he gave to the man. The man happily accepted it and left. Soon after, the man returned and said, "You gave me money, but I cannot accept it without returning something." He then gave Chen the coin he had received, and Chen was astonished to find that it had transformed into two coins. When he looked back, the man had disappeared. Chen returned to his hut and found that the coins were now made of gold. He was greatly surprised and amazed by this event.

At night, I dreamed that the person came to me and said, "I am Lü Dongbin. Because you have the appearance of a sage, I have tested you. Your aspirations to learn the Dao are strong, but your achievements in benefiting others are not yet evident. Why not strive further?" He then taught me the methods of breath control and refining the true self, flying talismans to cure diseases, and the practices of the Tianxin Zhengfa. He also promised that we would meet again in thirty years. Upon waking, I abstained from meat and alcohol, and diligently practiced the teachings of the Tianxin Zhengfa. Strange and ominous calamities were cured by the talisman water, and I continued to help others with great success. Living in the mountains for many years, I walked lightly and swiftly like flying, and both the Daoist and laypeople unanimously respected me. After the fall of the Qin dynasty, those in power, who were my old acquaintances, encouraged me to return to public life. At first, I held firm to my resolve and refused, but eventually, I yielded to the requests of my nephews and nieces, and established a shrine at home. Liu Xinsu, who was in charge of Jianghuai, appointed me as a counselor, and I soon returned to my original position in the state. However, my Daoist heart became increasingly lax, and the magical arts no longer worked. In my old age, I still maintained concubines, and my back bent and weakened. My vision and hearing dimmed, and I was no longer the same as before. One day, while bathing with hot coals nearby, I vaguely saw a divine being rebuking me, and I fell into the furnace, half of my body burned and died. My son-in-law, Li Xun, who was the county magistrate of Jurong in the late Shaoxi era, spoke to people about this.

The Temple of Lady Lin In the jurisdiction of Xinghua Army, there was an old Temple of Lady Lin in a place called Haikou. No one knows when it was established. The temple was not very large, but it was famous for its miraculous phenomena. All merchants going to sea must offer prayers at the shrine, asking for the divination of the cup and jade, seeking divine protection before they dare to set sail. It is said that someone once encountered a storm in the open sea and, looking far away, bowed a hundred times, begging for mercy, when they saw the deity appear on the mast. A wealthy man named Wu in the village had a very lush forest, deep and vast, filling the valley. One day, a guest came and pointed to a certain place, wanting Wu to agree but needed 3,000 strings of cash. The guest paid only 300, and Wu laughed and said, "You came to buy but only paid one-tenth, treating me like a fool. There is no way to reach an agreement." The guest left immediately. That night, a violent storm came in the morning. When Wu opened the door, he saw 300 strings of cash neatly stacked on the ground. Just when he was in doubt and fear, someone from outside came to report that the trees the guest had wanted were mostly felled. Wu went to see and found that on each remaining tree, the words "Lady Lin" were written. He finally understood that it was the work of a divine being and quickly took incense and paper money to the temple to offer thanks. He saw that all the trees had been transported to the temple grounds, realizing that the deity wanted them. He then offered all the trees from his forest to the temple and transported the original value back. People from far and near came in a hurry. An old man with the richest household was very stingy and only donated 30,000 strings of cash. Everyone thought this was too little and asked him to increase it, but he refused. When he sent his servant to carry the money out, it felt like a heavy burden, and the servant could not move. Filled with fear and regret, the old man immediately increased his donation to one million. The new temple was completed in no time, with hundreds of rooms, magnificent halls, and lofty towers, now the best in Fujian.

**Xie Shumian's Dream** Xie Zisù, from Linhai, Taizhou, was originally named "Mou." As a candidate for the imperial examination, he dreamed that someone told him, "If you change your name to 'Mou,' you will have a minor good fortune; if you change it to 'Shenfu,' you will have a major good fortune." In the year Ji Mao of the Shaoxing era (1159), he first used the name "Mou" to attend the state school's spring exam. The Jin Dynasty's Li Xiang appreciated his writing, and he was selected. From that month onwards, he consistently ranked at the top in his quarterly exams. When he took the imperial examination, he changed his name to Shenfu, but he was unsuccessful. In the year Renwu of the Qinding era (1175), he failed again and privately laughed, thinking, "The spirits are playing a trick on me like this. Could it be that I am destined to remain a state student?" In the year Yi You of the Qinding era (1175), he decided to change his name again. As the deadline approached, he dreamed of the previous figure who told him, "It will not work this time either." He woke up startled and still submitted his application with the name Shenfu. He expected to pass together with others the following year and indeed passed the examination. Later, he dreamed of a soldier like a royal guard carrying a tablet inscribed with "Grand Censor." In the early years of the Shaoxi era (1190), Xie, as the Left Historian, governed Lin'an. A fellow townsman heard about his dream and told his friend, "This time, he will not sit alone for long." The friend replied, "I know a scholar who dreamed of obtaining a list of successful candidates. It turned out to be a print sold by street vendors, filled with names, and Xie Gong's name was on it, with the word 'Prime Minister' written in white ink next to it, as if carved in black ink." From this, it can be inferred that becoming a Grand Censor was not a significant step, but Xie eventually rose to the position of Chancellor, advancing step by step.

**Final Translation** Ding Feng and His Success A scholar from Changzhou named Ding Feng (Ding Fu), in the year 1193 (Shaoxi 29th year), had a dream in which someone told him, "If you meet someone named Ding Ke, you will pass the exam." Upon waking, he changed his name to Feng. That autumn, he took the provincial examination but failed. He sighed, "How can there be someone with the surname Ding and the given name Ke? I will surely not pass." This happened again four times. By the year 1163 (Longxing 1st year), the list of successful candidates was posted, and indeed, there was a person from Tian Tai named Ding Ke. Although the name matched, Ding Feng was delighted but knew it would take several more years for him to be selected for an official position, and he might not be chosen before Ding Ke. In the year 1165 (Qian Dao 1st year), Ding Ke was waiting for an official position at home. The漕使, considering his poverty, appointed him as the chief examiner for the Changzhou provincial examination. Ding Feng was very happy and knew he would definitely pass. Ding Ke, however, fell ill at the examination hall and had to leave early. Ding Feng was greatly disappointed. When the list of successful candidates was posted, he found himself among the selected ones. He then went to pay respects and express gratitude to the chief examiner and his colleagues. They all said, "Ding Ke, the chief clerk, handed us a scroll of model essays before he left. He said that since he got this scroll, he had intended to place it at the top of the list, but due to illness, he was unable to fulfill his wish. He asked us to ensure that this person is not overlooked. Even if he dies, he will have no regrets." Just as they were checking the scrolls, they found one that violated a taboo and should have been disqualified. It was difficult to find a replacement in a hurry, so they included Ding Feng by default. It was your son. Ding Ke then told him about the dream he had.

**Final Translation:** Li San's Wife Li San, a man from Raozhou, had a wife named Yang Shi, who was the daughter of a county official. In the spring of the fifth year of the Shaoxi era (1194 AD), she contracted an epidemic illness and summoned the local physician Zheng Zhuang for treatment. After several days of treatment, she did not improve. Suddenly, she sat up, startled, and her speech and behavior were like those of a man. She called out to Li San, saying, "I am the god of the main hall, and your family has always been strict in its worship. I have felt your sincerity and came to save you from this illness. You may summon the physician." A short while later, the physician arrived, and Yang Shi scolded him by name. Zheng Zhuang angrily said, "How dare you speak to me so disrespectfully?" Yang replied, "As a deity, I cannot be addressed by your name. You have previously used aconite in your medicine, nearly endangering people's lives. How dare you do that again?" Zheng Zhuang denied it, but Yang said, "The medicine you gave me yesterday contained aconite, and you are lying."

I am terrified, Zhang began to be frightened. He said, "Prepare and drink the bamboo leaf and gypsum decoction to give me a little more time, but it is already too late to save me." Zhang replied, "I do not know the use of this medicine, and the official named Fang Zhong Yang angrily shouted, 'A physician does not recognize this formula; it is truly laughable!' He then listed the names, qualities, and dosages without error. Zhang then prepared one dose and gave it to me. I took it immediately, drank it, and then fell asleep. After waking up, I drank another cup, and the next day I became calm. The following spring, I fell ill again, and a spirit claimed to be Zhang the Great King, but his words were ineffective. However, I often looked at a specific spot on the pillow screen. Li suspected something unusual, lifted the paper to see, and found an unfinished painting of a deity. He quickly burned it, and my illness improved immediately. Qiu Ding entered a protective state. Qiu Ding, a person from Yihuang, was ill and in a difficult situation, and two officials with warrants took him away. They arrived at the government office, where many officials were lined up in the courtyard, waiting for the main official to come out and lead them forward. Then, they called a woman who was holding a cool shirt and dragging a long cloth, seeming to have something to prove to Qiu Ding. Before she could speak, someone beside her argued with her, and the woman's argument was refuted, and the official ordered her to leave. Qiu Ding silently understood that the young man had lived with this woman day and night, and they had a relationship, and she later married a wealthy man from a certain family. She used her wealth to donate to Daoist and Buddhist causes, and she died within a few years. The celestial officials assessed her deeds and she should be reborn as a man, but there was an old affair with Qiu Ding. The truth needed to be revealed, so Qiu Ding was brought as evidence. Before Qiu Ding could speak, the side argument was already clear. It turned out that the private intention was actually from the woman, and because of this, she was unable to be reborn as a man. The officials then ordered Qiu Ding to leave. As Qiu Ding was leaving, he saw the officials presenting documents and peeked at them. Everything was as it was in the world.

In the local county, officials listed the names of the people at the beginning, but I could not recognize them; I only saw the names of two individuals with the surname Guo. The officials quickly covered it up. The guard led me out to the main gate, where a notice was posted stating "such and such person" and "such and such person." His brother was there. Under the name, it was noted that the 17th day was the last day, and the official title was attached. The newly deceased official in the village was a supervisor, and his title was very different from the usual. On the way, the guard privately prayed and said, "Others who come here will definitely offer bribes; how can you not have any?" I replied, "I am indeed a poor scholar, and I did not bring a single coin when I came; how can I thank you?" The guard said, "Wait until you return home, and I will ask a Taoist priest to recite the *Sutra of Transforming and Delivering People* a hundred times, which will be enough." I agreed. Suddenly, I woke up and realized that I had already died. One day, my family surrounded me and cried, preparing the coffin and shroud; the sound of the death gong from the temple was clearly audible. I told my mother and wife that I had returned to life; they were happy, and both my mother and wife were surnamed Guo. I felt sad and unhappy. After several days, both of us fell ill and could not get up. My brother indeed died on the 17th day.

Yao Song, a native of Binzhou, passed the imperial examinations in the eighth year of the Qian Dao era. He was skilled in poetry and prose, and had a good reputation in his hometown. However, his actions were often too casual or careless. Once, he attended a banquet at the Office of Military Affairs. The guests were served drinks around the table. The host, Yao, noticed the wine was black. The servant holding the wine vessel was not the one who had been serving before. He suspected that the wine had been colored with purple perilla water and exclaimed, "Why is this so?" The host replied, "This is also wine. How can there be two kinds?" Yao ultimately did not believe it and served another drink to him. He took the drink himself and soon suffered from severe abdominal pain. He hurried back home and soon vomited, followed by blood. He died the next day. The entire city said that Professor Yao was poisoned by the officer of the Office of Military Affairs. The governor deeply suspected this. The officer was in the process of requesting a higher rank in the capital and resented Yao for being promoted ahead of him. The governor immediately impeached and dismissed him. Later, he investigated the truth. It turned out that the servant holding the wine vessel had been favored by the hostess, but her favor had waned. She intended to harm the host and placed poison in the wine. Yao took the blame for the disaster. The governor arrested and flogged the servant to death. The officer of the Office of Military Affairs soon fell ill and became incapacitated. Xiong, a person from Leizhou, was mentioned in the context.

A Man from Chongren and His Encounter with the Thunder God Xiong, a man from Chongren, served as acting governor of Leizhou Prefecture while concurrently holding the position of Tongpan in Guang Prefecture. On his arrival, an official informed him that he should pay homage at the Thunder Temple. Xiong replied, "I only know of the gods of the state, mountains, rivers, and the rituals of the scholars. How can there be any veneration at the Thunder Temple?" Before he could finish speaking, a sudden fierce wind, torrential rain, thunder, and lightning struck, and a horizontal board fell from the sky. He picked it up and saw that it was a board from his home, used to mark the boundaries of his granary, and bore his own handwriting. Overcome with fear, he hastily offered incense and coins to pray and express gratitude. Subsequently, he sent a letter to inquire of his family, and indeed, on that day, the board was lost and eventually disappeared in the prefecture. When I was serving in the Western Office, I once drafted an imperial edict to add a title to the Thunder God, naming the temple Xianling (Manifestly Divine), the deity Wēidé Zhāoxiǎn Wáng (Mighty Virtue Manifestly Divine King), and the stone deity and land god Xiéyìng Hóu (Harmonious Response Lord). Thus, such names, recorded in the ritual annals, how can they be neglected? Qiu, the Scholar Zhang Sheng, a wealthy man from Fuzhou, always won lawsuits.
