He lived in Ciguiling, where his fields bordered those of the Ai family. During a drought, the ponds and reservoirs dried up, and he seized water from the Ai family for irrigation. This led to a dispute, resulting in the injury of an Ai family servant, and mutual complaints to the prefectural and county authorities. After many years, the case remained unresolved. When a canal commissioner arrived, the Ai family went to plead their case and requested that the matter be handed over to a capable official. They also requested that Zhang and they jointly decide on punishment and reward, with the current determination as the basis, without questioning the correctness of the decision, and neither party should speak further. The commissioner entrusted the case to Chen, the magistrate of Yihuang County, who was assisted by Qiu, a local scholar. Qiu, being skilled, accepted the bribe from the Ai family and went to pray at the temple. The magistrate, hearing the case first from the officials, ignored it. Qiu then pleaded the case, saying, "This family has been virtuous for generations, and the matter is as I have stated. I wish you would handle it fairly, so that the unjustly aggrieved may be righted, and this can also provide a small benefit to your calculations, benefiting both parties." The magistrate was moved and decided as Qiu requested, reporting back. Zhang's three servants were arrested and imprisoned. One, surnamed Li, died from illness, and two others were flogged. Zhang was very angry and refused to reconcile, only setting up a monk's alms table each year and listing the matter as if under a curse. In the Dingwei year of the Chunxi era, Zhang finally died in a sullen state.

A scholar named Zhongqiu took up residence in the household of a village strongman. While sitting opposite the host, he suddenly became confused and disoriented, as if in a state of delirium. After a long time, he began to speak, saying, "I am about to die. Just now, I was chased by officials to the residence of a king. There, I saw the old man Zhang and the servant Li demanding my life. The officials called me by my old name, and I bowed and said, 'My name is such and such.' They claimed it was different from what was pointed out, and I insisted that it was a mistake. The king ordered the questioning of Zhang and Li. They cried out, 'Indeed, this is the person without error.' I still insisted on my previous statement, citing the fact that I was a candidate for the imperial examination last year as evidence. Before I finished speaking, a clerk carried a large register forward, titled 'The register of candidates from all states and armies for the year Bǐngwǔ.' He checked up to the entry for Fuzhou, where my current name was listed. Zhang and Li said, 'You are the one we are after. How could you have changed your name? You are deceiving the king!' The king ordered the release of the two and led me to hear the judgment. The clerk read aloud, 'The local land deity is responsible for verifying the case within fifteen days.' I bowed and withdrew, and thus I regained consciousness. I thought, though I managed to delay the judgment, I cannot escape it. I sought to leave the household and bid farewell to my wife and children. The host strongly urged me to stay, but I noticed that I was gasping for breath. It was not until the fourteenth day that I finally returned, but I died before reaching home."

The *Yiji Zhi* Volume Nine 钦定四库全书 The *Yiji Zhi* Volume Ten By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Gracious Official Yao Gracious Official Yao You, courtesy name Bo Shou, was a poor scholar from Anji County, Huzhou. He lived with his brother in the mansion of a wealthy family. The wealthy man chose a burial site and invited a guest, a skilled practitioner, to stay in the study. The guest became friends with Yao. The wealthy man had previously inspected a certain mountain with the guest and deemed it unsuitable. After re-selecting another site, he went back to the mountain one day and found auspicious signs clearly indicating a good burial site. Reflecting on the mistake of his previous advice, he dared not speak out and privately informed Yao, saying, "You should ask the owner for it. Once obtained, you should point out the specific site, which is the location of the old man's ancestral tomb." At the time, Yao was in mourning for his father and politely requested the wealthy man. The wealthy man said, "My initial intention was also to find a place for you. Now that it is suitable, what more can I say?" The guest then told Yao, "If this old man regrets it, he will definitely argue. It is necessary to have a written contract." After the contract was made, the guest led Yao to view the site and said, "There are two burial sites in this area. If you choose the upper site, both of you will pass the imperial examinations after completing mourning and gradually reach important positions. The only concern is the length of life."

If one can endure the lower depths and strive with slight delay, it will become prosperous thirty years later, and one can emerge as a ruler. The choice is yours. Yao said, "I am currently poor; in ten years, I cannot make ends meet. If I can obtain a salary early enough, that is sufficient. Why bother hoping for something thirty years from now? I wish to be in a higher position." The guest said, "Then tentatively pursue it. At some point, it will slightly decline as I said, but it can move downward. That is acceptable, but there will no longer be any ruling power." So it was done. Afterward, the brothers joined forces and received recognition; the elder brother became a treasure officer. The elder brother died as a state judge and thought of the guest's previous words, fearing and often dreaming of his father coming, whose clothes were all stained by water. Thus he requested leave to relocate the burial. When the tomb was opened, water filled it, and its temperature was like boiling water. The elder brother received the position of Minister of Rites, was sent to govern Taiyuan after the death of his mother, and because the local minor official built a tomb that was close to his ancestral grave, he suspected it was meant to harm him. He requested to resign and observe mourning, and the emperor decreed him to be in charge of the Upper Clear Treasure Palace. In all three attempts to remove him from his position as chancellor, it failed, and soon he died.

侍其如罔 豫章寓士侍其如罔就馆于临安监打套局门朱徳初家，庆元九年，当赴秋试。中元后，左手大指甲上忽见一道人侧面立身，洁白而眉目具，如绘画状，惊而示于人，皆谓耳目闻见所未有，或以为祥，或以为怪。越二日，又变作正面，而形貌益明。于是以为祥者亦怪焉。如罔始惧，以书刀刮去之，心切自念：“吾逼试期，而有此异，岂非佳兆乎？”正荧惑不自定，而其叔父讣音来，限期亲百日制，不获入试，一时之应乃若此。 赵积智 赵再可思侍郎有子八人，皆好学，多预荐送。第六子积智尤好读书，独屡试弗效，居常郁不乐。绍熙三年，从其父在桂林，时年三十八矣。当就邻路湖南转运司试，家素奉张王神，默祷求助。梦夜如银网内，大书一中字号，觉志之于策，朝夕思念其意，忽若有省。告其兄弟曰：“月中有中字，唯用字实应之。”即白于父，改名用是嵗，遂贡名春闱，虽不利而中太学补选，以覃霈免乙卯举，时未可量云。

### Translation #### Poxi and the Sound Divination He Zhan, from Kuacang, was the grandson of Wang Qingsuan from Qingyuan. In the year Dingwei (1177) of the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Song Dynasty, he went to the provincial examination and stayed at an inn in Sanqiao. On the eve of the announcement of the examination results, he sent a servant to inquire, but the servant did not return, causing him to look worried. He then led his companions to climb a bridge to listen to the sound divination. While standing still, he heard a woman on the riverbank calling out, "Poxi, you've got it." The people of Wu became angry and started to scold her, which is a sign of bad luck according to folk belief. However, He Zhan was delighted and quickly returned, saying, "Congratulations to me!" His companions asked, "Did you deliberately listen to the sound divination and get the same word four times? What else could there be to doubt?" He Zhan replied, "Not only this, but my childhood nickname was Poxi. Everyone was happy and they were about to buy wine to drink when the servant finally arrived, and he indeed got the same result." #### The Two Boys from the Li Family Li Yuanzuo, the magistrate of Nan Cheng, once took his family on a trip to Mount Magu. His children were still young and accompanied him. Two of the boys climbed up to the Qi Yun Pavilion and saw flowers and butterflies fluttering in the mountain stream, which they found very charming. They were ordered to catch the butterflies. One of the boys entered the deep part of the forest and did not return for a long time. When they called out to him, there was no response. They reported this to their father, who sent officials to search the mountain for a long time before finding him. The boy only said that he had lost his way but did not explain why. After a month, the children saw the boy wearing new clothes and often buying wine and meat to eat together, which made them suspicious. They feared he might be a thief and tied him up to question him. He finally said, "When I was chasing butterflies, I met a Taoist priest who called me to his dwelling. The house was splendid and the surroundings were clear and beautiful, unlike any human place. The priest ordered a boy to make tea, but before I could drink, I heard a loud call and rushed back along the path."

A scholar threw an object at me, which I picked up and found to be silver. Looking back, I realized that people's houses had all been robbed, and I was overjoyed but dared not speak of it. I held the silver and sold it at a market shop, where it weighed ten taels and fetched twenty-two thousand cash. I then deposited the money in the shop and took it out from time to time to support my living expenses. The other scholars did not believe me and inquired at the shop, which confirmed the truth. Yuanzuo said that the silver must be a divine object, different from ordinary items, and wanted to buy it back at its original price. The shopkeeper said that a passing guest had already bought it. Later, I visited the Daoist monks in Xiandu, and they all said that the man carrying a towel was the Purple Yang Daoist, Master Deng. It was then that I realized the two boys had met someone. In the years of Xining Gengwu and Jiwei, the seals of Li, the county magistrate, were lost one after another, and no one investigated where they went. One of the boys, named Futo, was surnamed Dai, and the other, named Shoutong, was surnamed Fu, both from Nankang. Gong'an Wood Hand In Jiangling Gong'an County, there was a very magnificent temple. The deities worshipped there were commonly known as the Two Saints, the Green Leaf Tied Buddha and the Lou Zhide Buddha. Their miraculous effects were renowned. In the early years of Xining, a monk from Sichuan named Lantou was the abbot of this temple. The temple's main pillar was rotten and needed to be replaced. Hearing that a distant village had a large Yuzhang tree, he sent his disciples to ask the owner for it, who demanded a high price and refused to sell it. Lantou intended to go himself but had not yet left when a guest monk led the owner to the temple, saying that they had come to offer the wood for alms, and the price had already been returned. The next day, a sprout grew on the tree, resembling the hand of a Buddha. How could we dare to withhold it now? We vowed to burn incense and make offerings before the two Buddhas to clarify our initial intentions. As promised, we felled the tree and placed the sprout in a niche on the altar table in the incense hall. The sprout looked just like the statue, with no small differences.

Zheng Er Kills His Son In the region of Wuling, Zhang Er, a local resident, held a wedding feast for his daughter, inviting neighbors to partake. Zheng Er and his wife were also invited. Zheng's wife had a reputation for being intimate with Wang, a monk, and many people knew about this. After drinking heavily, Zheng's wife accidentally dropped her chopsticks on the ground. Zhang's wife jokingly said that there would be good news. Zheng's wife laughed and asked why, to which Zhang's wife replied that there was no good news, just a bald-headed man. Everyone at the table was shocked or surprised. Zheng could no longer bear it and was called out by someone else who whispered something in his ear before leaving. When asked who it was, Zheng said it was Wang, who had pawned his robe at Zheng's place and was asking for a price to redeem it. Zheng and his wife were angry and left. Both Zheng and Zhang were volunteer militia members. Zheng returned home, took his sword, and went back to Zhang's door, calling out to Zhang's son and expressing his anger, saying that Zhang's family had done such a thing and invited him to a meal, but then he drew his sword and went to Zhang's house to scold him, and Zhang's son also drew his sword and chased Zheng. Zheng became even more angry, and his son, who was eight or nine years old, was lying on a stool, strangling himself, breaking his arm and ribs, and died, shouting for help. He reported to the village head that Zhang Er had killed his son, and the village head arrested Zhang. Zhang reported to the county magistrate, Li Da Dong, who took over the case, issued a decree for an inspection, and Zhang claimed he knew nothing about Zheng's son's death. Zheng's wife guarded the body, beating her chest and crying, saying that they had only one son, who was killed by his father, and blamed Zhang Er. As a result, both families were brought to court. Zheng was found guilty of killing his son and falsely accusing others, and was exiled to a distant region. Zhang and his wife were beaten, and Zheng's wife, Wang, was found guilty of adultery and received a punishment of flogging on the back. The incident was attributed to the joking remarks made during the wedding feast.

**Zheng Er Kills His Son** A man from Wuling named Zhang Er held a wedding feast for his daughter, inviting neighbors to partake in food and drink. Zheng Er and his wife also attended. Zheng’s wife had long been in an affair with a monk named Wang Hanshan, and many people knew about it. After drinking heavily, Zheng accidentally dropped his chopsticks on the ground. Zhang’s wife teased him, saying, “There must be something good happening.” Zheng’s wife laughed and asked why he said that, to which he replied, “There’s no good thing—it’s just a bald-headed man.” The whole table erupted in laughter. Zheng was already embarrassed and humiliated. Soon after, someone called him outside, and he went with them, whispering something in his ear before leaving. The others asked who it was, and he said, “Wang Hanshan. His robe is in my possession. Bring the price to redeem it.” Everyone laughed. Zheng and his wife were both angry and left. Zhang and Zheng were both members of the volunteer militia. Zheng returned home, retrieved the knife he was wearing, and went back to Zhang’s house. He called out to Zhang’s servant and, overcome with anger, said, “Your family has done such a thing. I invited you to my meal, yet you came to my door and insulted me. I also drew my knife to chase you.” Zheng became even more enraged. His son, who was only eight or nine years old, was lying on a stool. In a fit of rage, Zheng grabbed his son by the head, breaking his arm and ribs, killing him, and then shouted. He ran to the village head, declaring, “Zhang Er killed my son.” The village head arrested Zhang and immediately sent word to County Clerk Li Dading, who was acting as magistrate. Li issued an order for an inspector to investigate and verify the facts. Zhang claimed he knew nothing of what had caused Zheng’s son to die. Zheng’s wife, mourning at the body, struck her chest and cried, saying, “I have only one son, and he was killed by my husband. I will hold Zhang Er especially responsible.” Thus, both families were brought before the court. Zheng was found guilty of killing his son and falsely accusing another, and was exiled to a distant province. Zhang and his wife were beaten with the rod. Zheng’s wife was found guilty of adultery with Wang Hanshan and received a punishment of flogging on the back. All of this was the result of the joking words spoken at the banquet, which led to such a tragic outcome.

A Man Named Changsan In the region of Wuling, there lived a young orphan named Zhang Daozeng, who lost his parents at an early age and lived with his grandmother, Huo, who was known for her filial piety. When he was around thirteen or fourteen years old, during the winter of the year Xinhai in the Shaoxi era (1191 CE), after a long period of rain and snow had cleared up, Zhang saw fish swimming near the Zhang Riverbank. He went home to get a fishing hook and started fishing, catching a fish which made him very happy. He threaded the fish onto a bamboo stick and was about to return home when Tian Changsan, a blacksmith living west of the river, saw Zhang and asked him to give him the fish. Zhang refused, saying he wanted to take it back to his grandmother. Tian insisted, promising to pay him a hundred coins if he followed him to his forge. Zhang still refused, and Tian took the fish away directly. Zhang also crossed the river to chase after him to the shore, and they tugged at each other’s clothes, struggling fiercely. Both fell into the water. Tian was tall and strong, and he straddled Zhang’s chest, pressing him down and hitting his head and neck. People on the bank heard the cries and rushed to help. Zhang was in great pain and tried to escape, but Tian stepped on his foot, causing him to fall. Tian died, and Zhang was taken to the county prison. The case was brought to the prefectural court, where Prefect Liu Liyi of Yongjia read the case file and said, "This man is innocent." He then cited the case of Wang Anshi protecting a fighting quail and wrote the judgment as follows: "Taking something by force is theft. Zhang caught the fish, but Tian forcibly took it, making him a thief. He lured Zhang across the river, took the fish, and pressed him underwater, not letting go. It was a cold winter, and Zhang was weak and nearly died. Zhang kicked him with his foot, accidentally hitting a vital spot, causing Tian to die. This is a case of a thief fighting his victim and being killed by the victim." Zhang was found innocent, and his guarantor was called to testify. The case was reported to the higher courts, and the final judgment was in favor of Zhang. The people of the Tian family had no further words. Some said that Tian’s strength was no match for Zhang’s, and his reliance on brute force led to his downfall.

Shang Dazheng and the Sheep In the winter of the 13th year of the Chongning era, a retired official named Shu Zhi Zheng from Jinxian County in Longxing Prefecture presented a live sheep to the officials. Shang Dazheng, one of the officials, stayed at home for ten days before taking the sheep to the pagoda garden for breeding. The following summer, he sold the sheep to a butcher named Kong Sheng, who pastured it near East Lake. By early winter, the sheep was about to be slaughtered when it suddenly disappeared without a trace. On that day at noon, Shang Dazheng was at home and saw a sheep run in from outside. The sheep went to the hall and knelt down, making a pitiful cry as if seeking help. It then panted and lay down in the kitchen. Shang recognized it as the original sheep and called Kong Sheng to ask about the matter. Kong Sheng told him the story of the sheep's disappearance. Shang Dazheng was amazed and said, "The sheep came here for a short time and left. Now, after a year, I live in Shengping Lane, which is five miles from the pagoda garden. The roads are winding and hard to recognize, yet the sheep was able to escape death and find life. Could this be the work of some divine force, warning the world? Immediately, Shang Dazheng returned the original price and kept the sheep alive. A local person named Lu Guoying wrote a memorial called "The Memorial to the Sheep." Three years later, the sheep died and was buried in the pagoda garden. Because of this, Shang Dazheng talked with the lodger Tian Yingshu about the event. That night, Shang Dazheng dreamed of arriving at Zhuqiao Village on Xishan Mountain, where he met a woman carrying an infant in a swaddling cloth who came to pay respects. The woman said, "The child was saved by your kindness and is now a son of the Ding family. How dare I not respectfully thank you?" The next day, Shang Dazheng went to verify this, and indeed, on the day the sheep died, a boy was born to the farmer's servant Ding. Tian Yingshu also wrote a record about this event.

### Translation **A Large Mushroom in Jianfang Market** In the market of Jianfang in Jinxian County, there lived a farmer's servant named Zhao San. Every day, he would go into the mountains to gather firewood. In the seventh month of the first year of the Jingyuan era (1195 CE), after a long period of rain, the sky suddenly cleared. Holding his axe, Zhao San climbed to the top of a mountain and saw a huge mushroom growing under a large pine tree. Its diameter was eighteen inches. He picked it and proudly brought it back to boast to his neighbors, claiming it was something he had never seen before. An old man named Wang, who ran a wine shop, was particularly amazed and said, "I will give you money if you let me hang it outside my shop to attract customers." Zhao agreed but felt the payment was too low. He told his wife that the mushroom was so large, yet Wang only offered fifty coins. He thought it was better for his family to enjoy it themselves. The neighbors also praised the idea. So, he divided the mushroom, washed it, mixed it with rice and added flavors to make a dish. He called his wife, daughter, and grandson to eat it. After they finished eating, they went to bed. Before they could even close their eyes, they all felt severe abdominal pain, like thunderclaps. By the next morning, they were all dead. Only the grandson, who was a few years old, survived because he vomited. The Zhao family took care of him. It is known that mushrooms can be toxic, and this tragedy was truly heartbreaking. The Wang family and the wine shop customers were also in danger. **A Copper Bell from Fuzhou** In the four corners of the Danming Temple in Fuzhou, there were lakes. In the year Xinhai of the Shaoshi era (1191 CE), a fisherman caught an ancient copper bell in his net. As the mud gradually eroded, he brought it out of the water to wash it. After washing it for a few inches, its light shone brightly, illuminating the intestines and continuing to rotate. When placed on the roof of the boat, the same phenomenon occurred, and the waves suddenly became turbulent. They thought it was a strange occurrence and threw it onto the shore. They struck it with oars, and the sound reached the heavens, but it continued to rotate more vigorously. They then filled it with rotten fish, but it still did not stop rotating. Finally, they threw it into the water. The following spring, the head monk of the temple, Qingxian, heard about it and recruited many fishermen to search for it. They found it in a different stream, but the part where it had been contaminated by fish was no longer bright. Only the edges were still bright enough to illuminate beards and eyebrows. Although the sound was still clear and resonant, it was not as good as before.

Xiang Zhongkan Xiang Zhongkan, a scholar from LePing, had the courtesy name YuanShen. In the 11th year of the Shaoxing era, he served as the deputy governor of Hongzhou. The governor Liang Yangzu was strict in dealing with bandits and once intended to execute two individuals, entrusting Xiang Zhongkan with the task of conducting the interrogation. An official presented the case file, asking where the bandits were located. Xiang Zhongkan replied that they had already confessed to their crimes and that according to the law, it was not necessary to personally confront them, as this could lead to them changing their minds. He suggested that it was sufficient to record their names and positions. Liang Yangzu asked, "How can we interrogate someone without seeing them and confirm their guilt?" The official Zhao Bushi slandered Xiang Zhongkan to Liang Yangzu, who then summoned Xiang Zhongkan and accused him of overcomplicating the matter. Xiang Zhongkan responded, "If the governor's office were to execute them immediately, there would be no need for further investigation. However, once they are in prison, we must follow the proper procedures and conduct a thorough interrogation. If they are innocent and face death, I, as a humane person, would not allow such a thing to happen." Liang Yangzu was moved by Xiang Zhongkan's words and decided to conduct a thorough investigation. It turned out that the accused were innocent, and they were released. Later, Xiang Zhongkan went to another state for a new appointment and stayed at a Jin hotel. He fell ill and was on the brink of death. In a dream, he found himself in a grand hall and heard the announcer say, "Xiang Zhongkan has performed good deeds in secret and has been granted an additional six years of life." Upon waking, he gradually recovered. He went to the Tianqing Temple to offer a ritual feast and wrote a blue ink self-narrative to thank his second chance at life. In the self-narrative, he recounted, "Recently, I fell seriously ill and had a dream in which I was told that I had performed good deeds in secret and had been granted an additional six years of life. After waking up, I gradually recovered. I offered a ritual feast at the Tianqing Temple to thank my second chance at life. This is my self-narrative." Six years later, he died in his official position.

The Prescription from Gong'an Xiang Youzheng, the son of Yuanzhong, served as a deputy in Jiangling and temporarily in charge of the magistrate of Gong'an in the eighth year of Chongxi (1179). A severe boil erupted on his chest, and after half a year of treatment, he did not recover. Once, after bathing, he was in severe pain and lay down, entering a dream-like state where he saw a tall, imposing man with large eyes, wearing a dust broom and a loose robe. The man sat and gave him a prescription, saying, "Use myrrh, *gouxi* (a plant), and frankincense, boiled and drunk with wine." He also mentioned that Xu Zhen, the magistrate of Taoyuan, had recorded this prescription but without using *gouxi*. "If you want quick results, take this," the man said. Xiang Youzheng respectfully thanked him and followed the instructions, recovering before finishing the entire prescription. Later, he went to Yuquan to pray for rain and saw a statue of Guan Yu in a pavilion. He then painted the vision at home. In the fourth year of Shaoxi (1193), Xiang Youzheng served as a judicial official in Fuzhou. On the fifteenth night of the first lunar month in the fifth year (1194), the moon was not very bright. His son, Yan Zhang, saw a black object about ten feet long behind the house, thinking it was a shadow of a tree. Soon, it moved slightly, and he shouted at it, causing it to slowly disappear. The next day, he told his father, who said, "When your grandfather was alive, I was reading at night under the bright moonlight and saw a very large shadow peeping through a crack. It was a very tall black figure whose waist reached the eaves. I was terrified and went to bed. Afterward, your grandfather passed away. Could it be like this?" Nine days later, Xiang Youzheng died without any apparent illness. Previously, Ren Xun, a low-ranking official, had died more than ten years earlier. On that day, Ren Xun's wife saw her deceased husband's hat, shoes, robe, and笏 (a ceremonial tablet) rushing past the door, completely like his living form. She invited him back to the house, but he said, "I am now the judge of the Western Shrine. The god of the shrine summoned me because of the strictness of the judicial officer. Wait a little while; I will come back to knock on the door after two watches (of the night)." He then left, and soon after, Xiang Youzheng died.

D Liu Zihan Tian Shifu, the commander of Quanzhou, a man from Jingnan named Liu Han, was waiting for the vacancy of the Zhubu (a minor official) in Xiazhou's Yuan'an County. He composed a "Shuidiaogoutou" (a type of classical Chinese poetry) and presented it to him, saying: "The cool dew cleanses the golden well; a single leaf falls from the wutong tree. What does the demigod do, wandering aimlessly? With gray hair, he becomes a poet. Wearing a black hat, he sits facing clear springs and white stones, raising his head to touch the tall pine. Alone, he returns late, his voice echoing in the azure sky. In the abode of immortals, he passes through jade gates and gold-embroidered saddles. In the southern region of Qian, ten thousand tigers hold finely crafted bows, laughing as they break the green lotus, casting reflections. They sing new songs about picking mushrooms, their words carried by the autumn wind. Wearing swords and belts for eight thousand years, they enter the Great Ming Palace. Tian read it and was greatly pleased, writing a letter to invite him to Jin Cheng, intending to provide him with generous support. Liu Han died suddenly. The following year, when Tian went out to inspect the troops, he saw Liu Han standing by the roadside, weeping and saying, "Our paths have diverged, but you have cared for my family, which is enough to demonstrate the fulfillment of your promise." Suddenly, Liu Han disappeared. Tian was greatly surprised and immediately sent a thousand strings of coins to Liu Han's orphaned child.

The Strange Tales of Yi Jian, Volume Ten of the Bing Section <Subsection: Novels, Strange Stories> Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries The Strange Tales of Yi Jian, Volume One of the Ding Section Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Stele Inscription for the Temple of Shunji in Nankang In the autumn of the third year of the Daguan era, Lu Duanyun of Changle, originally a Deputy Minister of Ceremonies, was exiled to serve as the magistrate of Ruijin in Fangzhou due to his disagreement over the construction of the ancestral temple. His younger brother, Duanzao, who was also dismissed from his position in the imperial bureau in Beijing, accompanied him to the Wuyi Chongyou Guan. That year, on the night of the Wu Zi day in September, Duanzao dreamed that he had moored his boat at the riverbank and climbed the northern bank. He saw a tall and deep hall, one zhang in height, with a beautifully dressed figure on the dais who bowed forward and said, "I am King Shunji. Why are you here? However, my temple, you should write an inscription for me, and write it down." Duanzao hesitated and replied, "To write an inscription requires carving it in stone, but there is no stone for the stele. To write it would not be skillful, and my name is obscure, not worthy of much importance. Perhaps a high-ranking official in the central government could be entrusted with this task." King Shunji said, "Please entrust it to him." His expression became stern, and he spoke harshly, "Who says Nankang lacks stone for the stele? Who is a high-ranking official? I do not know. Just write the inscription and do not hesitate." Duanzao bowed his hands and said, "How dare I not respectfully obey the command." King Shunji's expression then calmed, and he invited Duanzao inside. The long corridors and curved railings were adorned with flowers and trees, reflecting as if in a painting. After a while, he said, "I am now on patrol, you should also return. I will send you with the favorable wind." He added, "The wind's direction is not something that can be privately controlled, but the world does not."

Wang Bai娘 A woman named Bai娘 from the Wang family of Mingzhou, who was young, widowed, and without support, was taken by her uncle, Chen Anxing, a court official, to various posts. She suffered from chronic illness for many years. In the summer of the third year of the Shaoxi era (1192 CE), she suddenly fell ill with deafness and muteness, unable to interact with others, and could only recognize characters. Whenever she wanted something, she would write it down on paper. Her uncle advised her to surrender to the Bodhisattva Guanyin, hoping for compassion. She worshiped morning and evening without tiring, and often fell into a meditative state while napping, always seeing a dignified and auspicious appearance. She was earnestly admonished and encouraged to pay respects to Amitabha Buddha in the West. He personally taught her four lines of verse: The Pure Land encompasses all realms. Why should one only pay respects to the West? If one can turn a single thought, everywhere is enlightenment. It can be widely encouraged to recite. Within a month, both illnesses suddenly healed, without the help of medicine. Chen said her faith became purer, and she responded as if by echo. He had it carved on a woodblock to spread it widely.

Xu Xizai Prays for a Son Xu Xizai of Yuleping had only one son who died on the 24th day of the eighth month in the year of Jia Wu during the Chunxi era. The next year, Xu stayed at the residence of Zhong Zhao, the magistrate of Susong in Shuzhou. When the son's initial period coincided with the ceremony of the senior monk Zongwu at Nantai Temple, it was raised as the story of Gu Kuang's son Feixiong being reborn as a son of the Gu family. Xu also said, "I have a sacred image of Guanyin, which is extremely rare. I will now give it to you. If you can carve it into woodblocks and print and distribute it, you will surely receive a reward according to the rules." Xu respectfully accepted it and took it back to his study. Zhong Zhao ordered a craftsman to be called from the city, and it took over a month for the craftsman to arrive. When the image case was opened and examined, there were already three hornet nests as large as dragon eyes. At the same time, two large hornets flew out, very strong, and one small, very weak, almost unable to follow each other. Zhong Zhao praised, saying, "The son of the insect died and encountered the wasp, which blessed them saying, 'Be like me, be like me.' If they are like me for a long time, they will resemble me. Shun Yu, in the future, will have three sons who are men." The next year, on the 24th day of the eighth month, a son was born, named Bo Ren. According to the Five Elements and Destiny book, this was indeed a reincarnation. Later, two more sons were born, but the youngest was excellent but not real, corresponding to the saying about the weak hornet. Zhong Zhao once wrote a preface for it.

Ancient Tomb of Taihe in Jiankang In Jiankang, the training ground for the central army was located outside the city walls. In one corner of this field, there was a prominent mound with uneven slopes, traditionally believed to be an ancient tomb. The commanding officer Cheng Yanxin, considering it an obstacle to the running of polo horses and recklessly assuming there were treasures inside the tomb, wanted to remove it. One night, he dreamed of a woman dressed in attire not of the current era, who appeared to be around thirty years old, with a beautiful face. She knelt and said, "I have been here for over eight hundred years. My bones are pitifully preserved, and I am fortunate to still exist. Now that you intend to excavate, if it does not cause great harm, I wish to stop this action so that the dead do not suffer exposure." Cheng woke up and told his fellow officers, who all advised him to abandon the plan. However, Cheng firmly refused to listen and ultimately ordered the soldiers to level the mound. Upon investigation, they discovered inscriptions on the bricks and tiles indicating the Taihe period of the Jin dynasty, confirming it as the burial place of concubines during the reign of Emperor Sima Rui (Emperor Yuan of Jin). Although the bones were not fully preserved, items such as makeup boxes, mirrors, and stands, all made of fine gold, were found, totaling several hundred pounds. Cheng believed this to be a good strategy. Not long after, a servant from Lin'an came to report.

A man who performed menial tasks under the supervision of Grand Viceroy Gan was sent by General Guo, who had been recommended by the Grand Viceroy but had now forgotten his kindness and betrayed him. The messenger was entrusted with the task of discreetly handling the matter and was to be dismissed if the facts were gathered and presented. The messenger was greatly pleased and believed the promise, so he was generously rewarded. However, he then fabricated several charges and told his close associates. Moreover, his nature was not cautious, and he leaked information to others. When General Guo heard this, he became furious and pointed out the crimes, leading to an impeachment. As a result, the messenger was demoted and reduced in rank, forced to serve without official status. This occurred in the eleventh year of the Chunxi era (1184 AD) during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song. Jing Pei's brother, who served as a deputy governor in Jiankang, also recognized him. After more than ten years, all the generals considered him a traitor and difficult to control, so they decided not to place him in military command again. Those who knew about it believed that divine retribution was at play. The servant of the Gan family, who was often sent to various military units to convey the master's intentions, was later dismissed due to an error but continued to pretend to follow the old ways, so Cheng Sheng did not consider it a deception. Cheng Sheng, the son of Guan, the former Grand Master of the Three Departments in Kaifeng, recorded the name, but it was not very clear or precise.

Three Zhao Lost Their Boat During the twelfth year of the Chongxi era, the governor of Jiangdu, Zhao Shuo, recounted that among the imperial family, there were three relatives—uncle and nephews—who returned from a selection process in Lin'an to their hometown in the same region. In the transition between April and May, they jointly purchased a small boat and passed through Wuxing, navigating a stream in a ravine. At noon, strong winds arose, and the sky became dark, as if something was gripping the tiller. The boat capsized immediately, but fortunately, the water was shallow, so they did not drown. Once they reached the shore, they were completely at a loss as to what to do. Soon, a basket floated to the front, and when they looked inside, it contained their uncle's official robes and shoes, though they were soaked, they were not very dirty. The uncle was very happy, but the two nephews wept and said, "Uncle has nothing to worry about, what about us?" Soon after, another basket floated over, containing the documents of the two nephews. The day had already grown dark, so they took lodging in a village house. Early in the morning, they walked out and saw a farmer carrying a hoe, tending to the land. They saw him in a state of panic and distress. They asked him, and he told them the reason. He suggested, "Why don't you go ask Zhao the monk? He might know the details," and he told them where he lived. So they went to visit him, and he was also a member of the imperial family.

Those who have always been close friends, Zhao Li, inquired into the details and said, "That place is only a small river or stream; how could it sink the boats? There must be something unusual. I will use my ghostly and divine techniques to summon and investigate. It will be visible immediately." After performing the ritual, the house was filled with ghostly entities, and the leader of these entities was identified as the old farmer Zhao had met before. Zhao rebuked him and searched for the items on the boat, finding that all of them were there, all belonging to the ghost family. If these were the ornaments and jewelry of the capital city, the women had taken them for themselves. Zhao scolded, "You sank people's boats and stole their belongings; how can you escape punishment? I will carry out the punishment." The ghost replied, "I am unworthy but the local land god. Several days ago, I was given an official document by the City God's office, which specifically listed the items on the boat. According to the document, I received and handed over all the items, except for the imperial edicts and commands of the three people, which were not listed. I immediately returned them. The document is still here and can be verified." Zhao took the document and read it, finding no basis to punish him. And so, he was released and allowed to leave.

De Xing County Pond Fish In De Xing County, a village whose name is now lost, there was a large pond. Forty years ago, a giant mandarin fish appeared in the pond. Its body was as large as a reed mat, long and broad. Every time it surfaced, dozens of smaller fish followed it, each about three or four feet long. The villagers saw this often and did not find it strange. A Taoist from Jiangxi Province came to the village and spoke with the villagers. He said that this fish could not be left in the pond, as it would bring great disaster to the village if it stayed longer. If they trusted him, he would ask for some money and then remove the fish. The villagers agreed and promised to pay him 3,000 units of currency. The Taoist then began to cut water lilies and collect them in worn-out bamboo baskets. After several days, the baskets were filled to the brim. The next day, a thick fog covered the pond, and light rain fell. A loud sound like thunder was heard, and then the fog cleared. A trench was cut from the pond, and the giant mandarin fish followed it out into the river. Dozens of smaller fish followed it, but they were exposed to mud and sand and could not move. The villagers gathered and caught the fish, and the entire village rejoiced. They paid the Taoist the agreed amount of money, and he left without revealing his name or hometown. A scholar from the county, Zhang Seng Shichuan, witnessed this event and recounted it for Jing Fei.

The Destruction of Temples by Yang Jian Since the reign of Chongning, Daoism has been highly esteemed, and many Buddhist temples in the capital have been destroyed. Initially, the site of the Chongxia Temple was converted into the Dianzhong Province. During the Zhenghe era, the Ganming Temple was transformed into the Five Temples and Three Supervisory Offices. Yang Jian then proposed to convert the Taiping Xingguo Temple into inns and private residences for rental purposes. At first, the main hall was dismantled, and the Buddha statues were buried beneath the foundation. Later, Yang Jian fell ill and died with his chest and abdomen torn apart. At that time, another attempt was made to destroy the Qishi Temple, but it was stopped due to these events. The Cool Hall in the Inner Palace During the Zhenghe era, an edict was issued to build a cool hall in the southwest of the inner palace. The hall consisted of four layers of grand buildings, each with several dozen rooms. Once completed, it was prepared for a royal visit, and the doors were tightly secured. Every night, two young eunuchs were ordered to guard the place. By then, the heat was intense, and they slept under the sprinkling of water. Before midnight, they heard strange noises, a mix of silent and loud sounds, inside and outside the hall, which were very fierce. The bamboo cooling devices moved and danced, making it impossible to sleep, and they shivered in fear all night. They reported this to Duke Lu of the Ministry of Works. Lu sent two people to investigate the strange occurrences, but he still did not fully believe. He personally went to verify it. As soon as he reached the main gate of the hall, it opened by itself. Lu hurriedly entered, and immediately, someone locked the door. This happened up to the second and third layers, and looking inside, he saw the light and cold wind.

### Translation Emperor Zhezong sat facing south, surrounded by imperial concubines and attendants, with several high-ranking eunuchs like Hao Sui and Liu Youduan standing respectfully. When Lu arrived, he shouted, "Why don't you pay your respects, Lu?" Lu, sweating profusely, bowed twice. He was then called forward and asked, "Why have you come?" Lu replied, "I was ordered to sweep the new palace and did not know the emperor was here. I have committed a grave offense by offending the imperial presence." The emperor, still angry, said, "Go back and tell the emperor that these rooms are not to be given to me." Lu replied, "I will obey the imperial command," bowed again, and left, locking each door behind him. The next day, Lu reported, "I dare not say everything, but I suggested that the emperor should not go there, so it was left empty." Lu's grandson lived by Donghu Lake in Yuzhang and told this story to guests. Han Zhuangmin, the Chancellor, loved eating donkey intestines. He always used them in banquets, sometimes even three times. He wanted them to be crisp and delicious, but if they were overcooked, they would become mushy, and if undercooked, they would be tough. The cook, fearing punishment, would only bind the donkey alive to a pillar, and as soon as the wine was poured, he would quickly stab the donkey's belly, pull out the intestines, wash and briefly cook them in boiling water, then serve them with various flavors. He would wait by the door with paper money, and after eating, when the guests put down their chopsticks and remained silent, he would burn the paper money. One day in Qin Prefecture, a guest got up to change clothes and passed by the imperial kitchen, where he saw several donkeys tied to pillars, already with their intestines removed but still alive, and he was moved by the scene.

Shenzong sat solemnly facing south on the throne, with concubines standing in attendance, and a dozen or so high-ranking eunuchs such as Hao Sui and Liu Youduan standing with clasped hands. When Lu arrived, the emperor shouted, "Lu, why don't you pay your respects?" Lu, sweating profusely, bowed twice in submission. The emperor then called him forward and asked, "Why have you come?" Lu replied, "I was ordered to clean the new palace and did not know that Your Majesty was here. I have dared to trespass upon Your Majesty's presence, and I deserve to die for my offense." The emperor was angry and did not forgive him, saying, "Go back and tell the emperor that these rooms are not to be given to me." Lu said, "I respectfully accept Your Majesty's decree," and bowed again before retreating. Each time he passed through a gate, he immediately locked it behind him. The next day, Lu reported to the emperor but dared not speak fully, only saying, "The imperial carriage should not go there." Thus, the place was left empty and unoccupied. Lu’s grandson later lived by Donghu Lake in Yuzhang, where he recounted this story to guests. Han Zhuangmin's Fondness for Donkey Intestines Chancellor Han Zhuangmin had a strong fondness for eating donkey intestines. At every banquet, he insisted on having them, sometimes even ordering them multiple times. He desired them to be crisp and delicious; if they were overcooked, they would become soft and mushy, and if they were undercooked, they would be tough and chewy. The cooks, fearing punishment, would only tie the donkeys alive to pillars. As soon as the wine was poured, they would immediately pierce the donkey's belly and pull out its intestines, wash and prepare them slightly in hot water, then take them, season them with five flavors, and serve them. After eating, they would hold paper money, wait by the door, and when the meal was finished and the chopsticks were set down in silence, they would burn the paper money into the air as an offering. When he was in Qin Prefecture, one guest, during the middle of a banquet, rose to change clothes and passed by the official kitchen. He saw several donkeys struggling under the pillars, all having had their intestines removed but not yet dead, and was deeply moved by the sight.

A Guest from Guanzhong This guest was born in Guanzhong and often ate a certain type of meat. From then on, he no longer hesitated to speak. Han Ping, who was strict and resolute, had his orders obeyed and prohibitions respected. After being dismissed as chancellor, he was sent to govern Chang'an. At that time, the ceremonial rites of the regional military governors had been long abandoned. Only on the first day he briefly mentioned it, and Han's order was that it should be done every five days. The officials were tired and annoyed. One day, he found a small poem on the screen, which said: "Every five days, the court assembly is held, just like a grand audience. The chancellor sits facing south, but it is missing the three calls." Han read it completely without showing any emotion and slowly said, "It was my mistake." He then changed the order, and every time there is a sitting guard, it is done. Those who criticized also stopped, and people admired his fearlessness and decisiveness in handling affairs. Deputy Magistrate Guo Deputy Magistrate Guo Da Ren cleared away the evil spirits in Huzhou, as recorded in the *Geng* annals. His son Yun lived in Pingjiang and was appointed as the magistrate of Yujian County in Hangzhou. Before taking office, he dreamed of entering a study room where someone gave him several hundred coins and several liters of rice. He said, "This is your lifetime salary." He woke up unhappy, thinking that the salary was so low, and the future was clear. From then on, he no longer went there. After a long time, he felt as if he had met someone. He wrote a letter to his wife Yang, who was the daughter of Wang. They had lived together for more than ten years and had three or four children. Holding the letter, he cried and did not know what to do. Yun left to live in a separate house. For several months, relatives and friends urged him to return. Yang's brother, Yu Jing, who was a military governor of Jingling, passed by his home and took the letter to burn it. However, he slept separately from his wife and no longer accepted salary; when the official period arrived, he did not go. From the time of the imperial examination, he had accumulated many years.

Official documents regarding promotions and appointments were sent to the Ministry of Personnel, where Zhao Zijing, the Minister of Personnel, held them back and did not report them to the emperor. He questioned, "How could there be a case of someone staying at home for fifteen years without holding an official position?" An edict was issued to Pingjiang to investigate his existence or absence. Chen Gongliang, a judicial official, temporarily took charge of the governor's duties and urged him to come to the capital. Finally, he received the imperial decree. The Gate Office questioned him again because he lacked recent salary records. After much negotiation and handling, he was finally allowed to register. Wang Jide was appointed as the magistrate of Jiande, Zhejiang, during the fourth year of the Shaoxi era. Although he was retired, his thoughts were often restless. On the 18th day of the fourth month, in the middle of the night, he suddenly called his son and said, "My mental state is very poor. I fear disaster is coming." His son was terrified and asked why. His voice gradually lowered to a point where it was almost inaudible. After six days, he did not rise. It was on the 25th of the month when he was supposed to receive his stipend, so he died the night before. He was only fifty-three years old. His son-in-law, Wang Lin, who was a magistrate in Raozhou, recounted this. Wang Jide, who was appointed as the governor of Pingjiang due to his former position as one of the Nine Excellencies, died just one month after taking office. The officials of the government gathered wood for the funeral arrangements, but when they prepared to bury him, his body suddenly swelled, making the coffin too small. His son wept and told everyone, "My father had previously made a coffin for himself, which was quite grand. When he was at home and felt slightly unwell, he would lie in it for one or two nights. When I saw him, he would smile and often pat me with his hand. Now it is stored at the house of a servant in Zhenze. I suspect that the spirit wants to use this coffin for his final journey, so it has shown this sign. If you send someone quickly to fetch it, it should arrive in a small boat within two days." They then used a divination method to determine if it was appropriate. After one throw, the incense smoke cleared, and the body returned to its original state. The next morning, the original coffin arrived, and they were able to proceed with the burial. Doctor Guo said that he had just passed away. His family spent fifty thousand coins to buy a new coffin and used it for the burial.

A Filial Woman from Yingdao In the county of Yingdao, which is part of Daozhou, there was a village woman who took great care of her mother-in-law with filial piety and diligence. The mother-in-law had lived alone for twenty years and died after eating meat provided by the woman. A neighbor, harboring a slight grudge, accused the meat of being poisoned. The county magistrate dispatched Officer Xue Daguai to investigate. The woman was unable to express herself clearly, her emotions were filled with sorrow, and she wished to die. Xue doubted that the accusation was true and repeatedly questioned and examined her. The woman explained that whenever she got fish or meat, she would store it in the holes of the kitchen pillars because it was high, dry, and close at hand, and this had been the case for many years. She asked, "Now, without knowing, what caused this change?" Xue rushed to the place and saw that the pillar had rotted in some places. He ordered it to be cut open and found countless centipedes breeding inside. He looked solemnly and said, "These are the ones causing harm." He reported the truth to the county, and the woman was released. I recorded this in my collection of stories, as there is a similar case. Xue's courtesy name was Yugu, from Hedong. I once wrote his epitaph. --- A Woman from Chenliu Inn Huang Yin, whose courtesy name was Qingzhi, was from Jian'an. In the second year of the Zhenghe era (1112 AD), he traveled to the capital to take the imperial examinations. When he was still sixty li away, he arrived at a small inn called Chenliu, where he stayed for the night. It was nearly two hours before midnight when he was reading a book and heard someone knocking on the door. The voice sounded delicate and soft. He went out to see and found a young girl with twin braids, dressed in fine clothes, smiling and saying, "I live only three or four houses to the west. I have a slight fondness for literature and know a bit about books. I regret being born into a woman's role, as my parents only taught me needlework, which did not fulfill my aspirations. Tonight, both my parents are out attending a wedding feast. I took the opportunity to come here and heard your voice reading. I am infinitely happy. Would you allow me to talk with you?" Huang Yin agreed.

A man sat down and casually handled his books, then asked for some wine. The host prepared wine and warmly entertained him, engaging in light-hearted conversation. The man showed no embarrassment and they soon retired to bed. He left at cockcrow and they made another appointment to meet again. They met several times over the course of a few months. The innkeeper was surprised that he stayed for such a long time without any apparent reason. One day, his relative, Liu Zhongong, passed by and met him by chance. Liu Zhongong brought him into the capital. The woman had already known about this, and suddenly came to bid farewell, holding hands and crying. The man opened a chest and took out five taels of silver to give as a gift. In the morning, they traveled about twenty li, to a place called Willow Forest. There, they saw a temple with a goddess or deity on the seat beside it. The goddess looked exactly like the one they had met before. Upon closer inspection, her face was flushed, as if feeling ashamed or guilty. The silver was in her hand, but she had never opened it to look at it. The Yan Taiwei Building Yan Da, the Taiwei, died in the capital at the beginning of the Yuanyou period, serving as the commander of the palace guards. His family had a very grand and beautiful building. His thirteen-year-old grandson, Gu, was rather resolute. One night, he saw dozens of officials and soldiers accompanying a man wearing a light yellow shirt, a green lotus cap, and holding a white feather fan. The man directly went up to the building, and singing boys and dancing girls followed. The man sat in the center, and many ladies performed their arts in turn. The wine was flowing, and the atmosphere was pleasant. Gu peeked at it from a crack and called the servants, who held utensils and tools and wanted to go up. Just halfway up the ladder, he heard a voice from the air shout loudly, "The prince of Han is here; there must be no disrespect." When he reached the building, it was empty, and nothing to see. But the wine, roast meat, and fruits were there. The wine vessels, bowls, and chopsticks used were all Yan family items. A high-spirited ghost looked down, and his words were indeed true. Afterward, he did not come again.

Wu Geng Passes the Imperial Examination Wu Geng's father, Wu Wu, was a government official from Chishui County in Hezhou. His family was quite wealthy, and for four generations, there was only one son each, all of whom devoted themselves to performing good deeds secretly. All efforts to help the many and the poor were fully utilized. By the time of Wu Geng's father, there were two sons. His son-in-law Zhang Pu, a government official, encouraged Wu Geng to study and invited a local scholar named Zhang Tan to teach at his home. When they took the local examination, Zhang Tan failed but Wu Geng was recommended. Before the provincial examination, Wu Geng donated a thousand coins to support his fellow examinee Gu Longhuan. When they entered the provincial examination hall, they sat in separate rooms. In the year 1146 (the third year of the Shaoxing era), the examination hall was very strict, and they could not communicate with each other. Wu Geng was short on talent and had a slow mind, feeling desperate and unable to think of a solution. He saw Zhang Tan nearby and took his exam paper, writing it in one stroke. This happened for all three days, and he was admitted to the higher rank. Zhang Tan asked him about the content of his essay, but he couldn't recall anything. Zhang Pu said, "Even if the entire essay was written by Gu Longhuan, wouldn't you remember some outline to answer inquiries from acquaintances?" Wu Geng said, "I sat far away from Gu Longhuan in the corridor, so I was very disappointed. I didn't just rely on Mr. Zhang's help, but I might have failed completely without it." Grateful for Zhang Tan's kindness, he quickly went to thank him. Zhang Tan was shocked and said, "If I didn't pass, how could I enter the provincial examination? You're not joking with me, are you?" Soon after, Wu Geng came to thank Zhang Tan, who was amazed and said, "There is no other explanation; it is because of the accumulated good deeds of your family over generations that the gods have rewarded you with good fortune." Wu Geng passed the examination and was appointed as the magistrate of Xiangru in Guozhou. According to the *Register of Successful Candidates*, there is no record of Wu Geng in the 1147 (the fourth year of the Shaoxing era) list, so it is doubtful or there might be an error in the year.

### Translation **顾百一** In a village in Wenzhou, there was a mountain far from the city that no one had bought or leased. It was simply known as an official mountain. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, in the year of Gandaowu, two elderly farmers, Chen and Li, each cleared land to plant millet. They heard a mournful cry deep in the area and stopped their work to listen. Soon, they heard someone nearby comforting the crying, saying, "Do not be worried. Tomorrow, a person named Gu Baiyi from Che Tou Dayang will come to replace you." The next day, Chen and Li returned and saw the person carrying hemp coming. They asked his name, and he replied, "Gu Baiyi." They asked where he lived, and he said, "Che Tou Dayang." He then explained the previous day's events, urging them to believe him. If they did not, he warned, they would surely die at the hands of ghosts. Chen and Li gratefully thanked him and went with him. They returned home just as they reached the gate, and Gu Baiyi suddenly fell down dead on the road. **黄衣妇人** Anzizhang, courtesy name Xinglao, was a high-ranking official in Jingnan during the year of Shaoxing twenty, serving as an advisor to the governor. His son, An, ordered his servant Wei Zhang to buy a yellow cow and its calf on the first day of the tenth month. After killing the calf and making it into jerky, only a few days later, they wanted to slaughter the mother cow. On the night before, An dreamed of a woman dressed in yellow, who knelt and pleaded repeatedly, saying, "The girl has already been cut up by the official. Please spare my life." An did not have time to respond to her, but she followed him, pleading incessantly until he woke up. He told his cousin, Sheng Lai, about the strange dream and questioned Wei Zhang, who informed him that the cow had already been tied up and was about to be slaughtered. An was greatly moved and realized his mistake. He threw the dead calf and the remaining meat into the river and took the mother cow to the Huasi Temple, vowing never to eat beef again.

### 安妾柔奴 蜀人安自牧防妻之后买妾曰柔奴付以阃政恃主人宠嬖恣横颇甚一婢安儿产子方满月用计杀之而逐其母乾道七年自牧以制置司干官卒于成都其家归常州寓居新兴寺柔奴感水虫疾岁余而死所亲盛子东及诸安族来吊以大殓命僧修设荐严方咒亡位食尸忽自起而坐众骇惧奔走惟一老僧独留不去曰此尸蹷尔何足畏伸脚蹴之倒亟舁入棺咸以为积恶之报 **Translation:** An Qie Rounu After An Zimeng's wife passed away, he bought a concubine named Rounu and entrusted her with household affairs. Relying on the master's favor and indulgence, she became extremely arrogant and domineering. One maid, An'er, had a child who was only a month old. Rounu plotted and killed the child and then expelled the mother. In the seventh year of Qian Dao, An Zimeng died in Chengdu as a dry officer of the Zhi Zhi Si. His family returned to Changzhou and lived in Xinxing Temple. Rounu contracted water insect disease and died after more than a year. Her relatives, Sheng Zidong and other An clans, came to mourn and wait for the big burial. They ordered a monk to perform rituals and offerings. As the monk chanted for the deceased, the corpse suddenly sat up. Everyone was terrified and ran away, but only one old monk stayed behind. He said, "This corpse is just fainting; what is there to be afraid of?" He stretched his foot and kicked the corpse over, which fell and was quickly placed in the coffin. All thought it was retribution for accumulated evil. ### 盛八总干 盛八总干者名挺字特夫开封尹章之族孙也寓居润之金坛小曲观生三子长曰木登进士第次曰粟季曰果绍兴十九年十一月十九日晡后有异怪起甲士铁马可百辈各长三寸分为两阵驰骤战鬭于庭除凡厯半月日増数十骑稍闻金皷之声閙喧特甚挺不胜骇愤抛大砖乱掷则散走门堂庖厠随处四集咸遗下弓矢刀矛皆絶小而形制悉具至日暮乃不见一家不知所为岁未尽挺卧疾而卒木待次州教官继死粟忽失所在罄地搜寻杳不得其踪迹妻徐氏愳祸之延亟挈孤幼往常州依表叔安自强同居始得息厉为妖若是所未之闻也 **Translation:** Sheng Ba Zonggan Sheng Ba Zonggan, whose name is Ting and courtesy name is Te Fu, was a descendant of the clan of the Prefect of Kaifeng, Zhang. He lived in a small curve view in Jin Tan, Run. He had three sons: the eldest, Mu, passed the imperial examination; the second, Su; and the third, Guo. In the nineteenth year of Shaoxing, on the nineteenth day of the eleventh month, after the evening meal, strange phenomena arose. Hundreds of armored soldiers and iron horses, each three inches long, divided into two armies and rushed and fought in the courtyard. This lasted for half a month, and the number of soldiers increased daily. Gradually, the sound of drums became particularly noisy. Ting could not bear the fear and anger, so he threw large bricks, and the soldiers scattered and ran away. They gathered everywhere in the hall, kitchen, and toilet, leaving behind bows, arrows, swords, and spears, all extremely small but complete in shape. Until dusk, they disappeared. The family did not know what to do. Before the year ended, Ting lay ill and died. Mu waited for his turn to become a teacher in Yangzhou but died subsequently. Su suddenly disappeared. They searched the entire ground but could not find his trace. His wife Xu Shi feared the disaster would spread and quickly took the orphaned and young ones to Changzhou to live with her cousin An Ziqiang. They finally found peace. Such demonic spirits causing mischief is something I have never heard of.

A Layperson from the Dashi Temple During the reign of the Prince of Enping, he married the daughter of Wang Yi, an official holding the position of Sina Cheng. Due to this marriage, Wang Yi's family temporarily resided in the Yue mansion at the Dashi Temple's Arhat Hall. The prince's second son, Yuanqing, frequently visited the princely mansion, where he supervised the construction and repair work. He built recklessly, disregarding all spatial and directional taboos. In the 13th year of the Chunxi era, in April, Yuanqing fell seriously ill and was in critical condition. He was moved to a room outside the main house. On the 26th day, in the afternoon, he had a dream-like experience where he saw a tall, imposing figure, about nine feet tall, wearing a Tang-style headscarf, white shirt, and black belt. The figure said, "You have offended the Nine Beam Star and should die, so I come to inform you. There is a method to dispel this. Tonight, at the third watch, two divine beings will come, and it will be harmful to you. Take a piece of wood, cover it with a quilt, and place it on the bed. Then lie down on the ground with something underneath you. You can escape this calamity."

Yuan Qīng, having received his orders, suddenly felt a presence at midnight. Two gods appeared, with strange and fierce appearances, terrifying and horrifying, bustling and distracted as if searching for something. Suddenly, there was a sound, and the one with a Tang hat came out, saying, "Congratulations! He has already been turned into flour!" He raised his hand and dragged the two gods away. Yuan Qīng thanked him, saying, "I don’t know who you are, but you have saved me with such kindness." The Tang-hatted figure replied, "You served me diligently in your past life, so I saved you. There’s no need to ask for details; I left quickly." By dawn, the wood in the quilt was crushed into powder. The illness was cured, and Yuan Qīng thought of repaying the debt but couldn’t. Recalling yesterday, when he saw him, his clothes were quite old and worn. He made a large robe with two pieces of silk, prepared a memorial, and burned paper money to offer to him. His elder brother, Zheng Qīng, once wrote a record of it. **The Round Pond and the Fallen Bell** In the jurisdiction of Changxi County in Fuzhou, there is a round pond with water so deep that it is impossible to measure. On both sides, there are paths that connect to roads where merchants travel, but the changes are unpredictable. Once, a man carrying a load passed by and saw a wide path with brick paving, neat and clean, and many gold and silver objects thrown on the ground. People rushed to grab them, but they were swallowed by the pond and could not be retrieved. In the third year of Shaoxing, a villager named Yao Lao, who lived nearby, heard the sound of a bell at night in autumn. The sound was loud and resonated through the mountains and valleys. He went with his two sons to see it, and they saw a bell fall into the pond. On both sides of the pond, there were three temples, and the monks knew it was unusual. The next morning, they each held scriptures, chanted spells, and submitted petitions, hoping to retrieve the bell. The temples on both sides did not respond, but the middle temple, as soon as they threw the petition, the water rose more than ten feet, and the bell floated up. The monks prepared with solemn ceremonies and chants, and the bell was brought back. It weighed three thousand pounds, with two dragons intertwined, exquisitely crafted. It was enshrined on the temple’s upper floor. After this, the pond remained calm and no longer showed any abnormalities. Several years later, the temple was burned down, leaving only the bell tower standing.

Zhang Cishan's Wife During the reign of Emperor Huizong, in the year Xianhe Jia Chen (1124 AD), Zhang Cishan, a Household Censor of Suo State, was traveling to the capital for the imperial examinations. His wife had entered the capital that year's winter to participate in the selection process. On the day of the winter solstice, during a holiday, they visited Xiangguo Temple. Amidst the crowd, Zhang unexpectedly met his deceased wife, Ying'er, whom he had not seen since her death. He was astonished and asked her, "You have been dead for a long time. How did you come here?" She replied, "I am serving my mother-in-law at an empty house five miles west of the city gate. You can come to visit me tomorrow after having a meal. Ying'er will wait for you on the road." Zhang followed her instructions and indeed met his wife. She wept and said, "I have been punished here for my lifelong jealousy and drinking excessively. Please stay a little longer so I can show you." By dusk, they heard the sound of horses and the shouts of attendants. A nobleman dressed in purple robes dismounted and entered the main hall. A group of soldiers transformed into fierce-looking ghosts, each holding a long spear. They stabbed Zhang's wife, cutting off her limbs and dividing her body into dozens of pieces. However, she soon came back to life, suffering from whipping and interrogation for some time. The nobleman then left, and everything returned to its original state. His wife said, "Every day I suffer greatly. I must ask the monk at Sizhou Dashi Temple to recite the Diamond Sutra to free me from this karma. Come back here tomorrow to see me again." As promised, the next day, Zhang saw her again, but this time she was only bound and not dismembered. The nobleman asked, "You must have made a vow, which is why your evil deeds are gradually diminishing. Please tell me the truth." Zhang's wife replied, "Good, good. Be strong." After the nobleman left, she bid farewell to her husband. Zhang was later transferred to a position in the east and traveled to Sizhou, where he held a ceremony to dedicate merit to the sutras and returned to the west. He could no longer see his wife, and Ying'er told him, "My mother-in-law has sent me to thank you for the sutra dedication, which has helped me become a man from a scholarly family." During the reign of Emperor Gaozong in the Song dynasty, Zhang served as the governor of Changzhou, and his nephew An advised him.

The Wife of Fan Zhigang A scholar from Kuaiji, Fan Zhigang, lived in the city and died in his prime. He left behind two sons who were cautious and respectful, and he provided them with enough to live comfortably. His wife burned incense day and night to pray to heaven, earth, and all the gods, reciting scriptures fifty times every morning. For over ten years, she never missed a single day. In the first year of the Chongxi era, she set up altars for the Three Officials and served them diligently. By the candlelight, a divine being appeared and spoke to her: "Grandma, your family's fortunes have improved greatly this year. Your two sons are paying attention to the family business, and you have five grandchildren, both male and female, as well as servants. You are healthy and strong with no illnesses, and living in the world is truly not easy to come by. What else could one ask for? Day after day, night after night, your incense and prayers are so sincere. I wish to know what you desire." The mother replied: "An old servant thanks heaven and earth for protecting the family. I have no other wishes; I only wish to have enough food to eat." The divine being said: "Food and clothing will certainly not be lacking. If you wish to obtain them peacefully, it might be difficult." After speaking, the divine being disappeared. I say that those in the world who do nothing and enjoy wealth and honor should hear the story of Fan's mother and know what to be warned about.

A Daughter of Zhang Chengshi A daughter of Zhang Chengshi from Huzhou, known for her beautiful appearance, was born in the year Jia Si of the Qian Dao era and was eighteen years old by the year Geng Xu of the Shao Xi era. She was filial and respectful to her parents and did not leave the house or courtyard unnecessarily. One spring, she suddenly fell ill with a mental disorder, often engaging in intimate conversations with others. Neither doctors nor shamans could cure her. At that time, there was a Daoist priest skilled in exorcising ghosts and spirits, who was highly respected and known as Master San. People from the same region invited him to treat the family. He arrived and, wielding a sword and spitting water, the daughter came out to mock and challenge him, but he was not intimidated at all. The Daoist priest deeply felt ashamed and depressed, and went out to walk around, taking a nap by the side of a bridge. His spirit rose and seemed almost supernatural, encountering the Celestial Envoy from the Nine Heavens, who was on a solemn tour. The Daoist priest approached the carriage and bowed twice, reporting that the daughter of the Zhang family was being possessed by an evil spirit and could not be expelled. He asked the Celestial Envoy for guidance. The envoy replied, "You can use the Golden Bridge Spell to treat her." The Daoist priest then dreamed and woke up, returning to the Zhang family's home. He performed the spell, and the daughter bowed her head and submitted. He asked her name, and she said, "I am the deity from the nearby temple. When the Zhang family was poor, they prayed to me and vowed to give this daughter as my wife. I have since taken special care of her. Now that they have become wealthy, they have broken their vow, so I am causing mischief. I did not intend to cause harm without reason. Please judge and decide what to do." The Daoist priest said, "I wish to have a craftsman create a statue of the daughter on the left side of your shrine, and you can calmly allow her spirit to return to its original place. What do you think?" The deity replied, "I agree." Afterward, she was calm and submissive. The daughter has since been married off.

**The Giant Crab of Longxi Well** --- In the east of the Changxi River in Fuzhou, about two hundred li away, there is a deep spring called Longxi Well, which borders Pingyang County in Wenzhou. Above it lies Longjing Mountain, and below it is a large well. It is said that a dragon resides there. During the early years of the Chongxi era, in the transition between July and August, there was no rain for fifty days. The people were suffering and could not sustain their lives, having exhausted all prayers and requests but receiving no response. A scholar named Liu Yingzhi, known throughout the village for his goodness, righteousness, and generosity, led Taoist priests, Buddhist monks, and shamans, equipped with flags, drums, banners, and musical instruments, and gathered 300 farmers. At the time when the rooster first crows, they went to the well and submitted a petition for water. When they arrived, the sky had already dawned, and the monks and Taoists were circling around to recite scriptures and incantations, preparing to draw water from the well. They saw a large crab swimming on the surface of the water with claws like pincers, a back with seven stars like a ladle, and large eyes like marbles, shining brightly. They then cleaned the containers and welcomed the crab, drawing water from it. The crab followed as the bearers just moved, and clouds and fog suddenly rose. Before reaching Longxi, the rain had already poured down clearly. The next day, they welcomed everyone to the villages to see, and the crowds were crowded. Suddenly, the crab was lost. The sweet rain was sufficient, and everyone agreed to send it back. They wandered and searched and found it in Liu's backyard pond. The next day, they prepared a ritual offering to thank and returned it to the well. From then on, all prayers were answered.

Zhu Juchuan Zhu Juchuan, a resident of Tuanku Lake in Yudan, focused entirely on making a living through immoral means, prioritizing wealth acquisition at the expense of moral considerations. Once, during the day, someone threw a brick into his room, which was suspected to be the work of thieves. Upon examination, nothing was seen, and he did not consider it unusual. Ten days later, a large millstone fell from the sky into his courtyard. Soon after, fires started inside his chests and boxes. He then called upon the village witch to deal with the strange occurrences, but the strange phenomena became more rampant. Both men and women in his household were choked and slapped, and they were subjected to various forms of physical discipline, causing the entire household to be restless day and night. He then ordered a Taoist priest to set up a ritual to ward off evil. Everyone saw a large ball spinning around the altar, but the ritual was not yet complete when a stone was thrown near the priest's seat, rendering the ritual ineffective. His residence was soon burned by fire, and he moved to another place, about ten or so miles away. A multitude of strange phenomena occurred simultaneously, disrupting his livelihood almost entirely. A man named Yu Zhengguan passed by his home and said, "This is not the work of evil spirits. Your household's use of scales and measures is unfair, and you are too fond of profit. This is not tolerated by the Creator, and heaven punishes you. It is not something human effort can save. You should repent and change your heart, and the disaster will stop on its own." Zhu Juchuan, fearing the consequences, admitted his fault and changed everything. Since then, he has been spared from further trouble.

"Record of the Strange" Volume Two of the "Yijian Zhi" (丁卷二) "钦定四库全书" (The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries) "Yijian Zhi" Volume Three of the "Yijian Zhi" (丁卷三) Compiled by Hong Mai during the Song Dynasty (宋 洪迈 撰) The Deer Mother of Ru Jiao In the ancient city of Linhai, Taizhou, a wealthy household named Li had a Buddhist monk named Ru Jiao. His mother, Ye Daxiao, lived with her cousin in a village to the north. Ru Jiao often visited to check on her. In the spring of the thirteenth year of the Chunxi era (淳熙十三年), his mother passed away. The following year, on the first anniversary of her death (小祥), Ru Jiao performed a ritual offering of food and incense to honor her. After the ceremony, he returned to his temple. That night, he dreamed that his mother came to him, weeping, and said, "Because I did not do good deeds in my life, I have been reborn as a deer. I am only in the nearby mountains. Tomorrow, I will be chased by eagles and dogs. You should go out and watch. If you see me, redeem me with money. Do not forget!" Ru Jiao woke up feeling sad. As soon as it was light, he took several servants and waited outside. In the morning, around 7-9 AM (辰巳间), hunters indeed chased a deer. The deer ran straight into the temple. Ru Jiao gave the hunters 5,000 coins and kept the deer, feeding it for three years. He then dreamed again of his mother, who said, "I have completed my karmic retribution and no longer need to be consumed by people. It is all due to your filial piety." The next morning, the deer died in the fence. Ru Jiao buried it next to his mother's grave, and the villagers called it the "Deer Mother's Tomb."

**Stone City Temple Deity** In the Stone City of Ganzhou, the temple of the local deity is located beside the county government. Prayers for rain or drought are often answered, and the local officials and common people worked together to renovate the temple.
