Originally, two horses were sculpted on either side of the temple gate. At the time, they forgot to set them up, and after a month, the county magistrate Lü Dayuan dreamed of a man in purple robes standing in the courtyard. The man said, "Your Majesty is now conveying a message to the official: there is a lack of transportation for going in and out, and I wish to temporarily borrow two fine horses." Lü Dayuan agreed, and upon waking, he thought about it. The mountainous region had never raised horses before, so how could they be sent or borrowed? Gradually, he realized the significance of the dream and used his own salary to immediately sculpt and decorate the horses on both sides, using four sturdy soldiers in fine brocade to control them. By July, after ten days without rain, they offered prayers at the temple. The incense and offerings were just beginning when dark clouds gathered in the middle of the day, and it soon rained, bringing much-needed moisture. The year was bountiful. Sometimes, the locals dreamed of the deity's grand procession, riding the horse sculpted on the east side of the temple. **Tomb of Chen Gonggong's Ancestors** In Shicheng, the Yongfu Temple had a county clerk who used it as an official residence. Opposite the temple, there was a mound about ten zhang tall, said to be the tomb of Chen Gonggong's ancestors. No one had ever swept or offered sacrifices at the tomb, and its authenticity or significance was uncertain. In the fourth year of the Shaoxing era, Zheng Guan, a native of Quzhou, was the county clerk. He thought the location was prominent and suitable for viewing from afar, and he wanted to level the top of the mound and build a small pavilion. The materials and labor were already prepared, but the monks were concerned that the project might bring punishment or retribution, and they collectively advised against it. Zheng Guan, believing he had the power to enforce his will, did not believe their concerns. One night, his clerk Ye Yuan stayed in the study and dreamed of a procession of attendants calling out for the Master. A man in purple robes arrived in a sedan chair and called out to Ye Yuan, saying, "I have lived on the western hill for two hundred years. How dare you consider infringing on my tomb?" Ye Yuan woke up in a state of fear and sweating, and he quickly informed Zheng Guan of the dream. Zheng Guan then abandoned the project.

Liao's Fish Ponds In the fourth month of the fourth year of the Shaoxi era, Master Lü Deqing, the magistrate of Shicheng, was on his way to the prefecture and stopped for the night at the inn of a resident named Liao Shaoda in Quyang Village, Yundu County, to the east. It was a hot summer, and he rested there. Liao Shaoda was someone with whom one could have a meaningful conversation. He spoke of his younger brother, Liao Si, who liked to drink and was high-spirited. In the past, Liao Si often belittled him, but he did not retaliate. They lived near two fish ponds, each 20 mu in size, which were the only source of their livelihood. Liao Si suddenly proposed dividing the property, and they reluctantly agreed. When it came time to catch fish, Liao Si asked a village shaman to write a talisman on a tile and throw it into the eastern pond. When they cast their nets, they caught no fish. Later, Liao Shaoda realized what had happened and called the same shaman, who followed the same method for the western pond, with the same result. Later, the shaman came to inform them, saying, "You two are brothers, yet you no longer get along. By asking me to use improper methods, you risk punishment. You should both repent and return to your original intentions, live together, and share your business. I will help resolve this, but do not have any further unreasonable thoughts." Both brothers followed his advice. The shaman then drew two new talismans and threw them into the ponds, and the fish returned to normal. Now, they earn an annual profit of at least several hundred strings of cash. The shaman was not swayed by the profit and was commendable for promoting harmony. It is regrettable that we do not know his name.

Li Shi and the Red Snake Pan Jia, a man from Changxi in Fuzhou, had a wife named Li who was beautiful in appearance. She was twenty-three years old when she married. Two years after her marriage, she went out to play in the garden with her sisters-in-law and other women. There, she saw a red snake coiled and motionless on the road. She focused her gaze on it and observed it closely. When she returned home, she immediately fell ill. At first, she talked and laughed without restraint, uttering strange and unverified words, but she still responded appropriately to others. Later, her condition worsened, and she completely changed her form. Sometimes she wore beautiful makeup and elegant new clothes, with fresh hats and shoes, and had flowers all over her head. Other times, she had loose hair and was completely naked, walking barefoot on the main street, avoiding mud, dirt, and thorns. Passersby gathered to watch her, but she remained unmoved. Pan Jia feared that she was stepping into a dangerous situation, so he locked her in an empty room and watched her day and night. He summoned the village shaman, Ma's son, to perform a ritual examination. The shaman wore a red robe and gathered several dozen neighbors and servants, dressed like a ritual team, circling the place where Li had been playing at the foot of the mountain. He rang bells, beat drums, and raised a large flag inscribed with four characters: "Qingyang Great Fear Exhibition Hall." He shouted loudly, calling it a "burning the mountain and catching ghosts" ritual. When he encountered snakes and insects, he captured them and brought them back, pouring wine and vinegar over them to honor the spirits. At night, he led Li out, singing and responding in time with her. The shaman picked up broken pottery, walked barefoot, and stepped on it. Li initially imitated him, but her feet broke and bled. The shaman then burned square bricks until they were completely red and stood on them. He boiled water vigorously in a large pot, picked it up with his hands, and threw it onto her head, walking around her in two rounds. Li could not move and then folded her arms and cowered. The shaman poured boiling water to wash her feet, made her sit on a small chair, forced her to provide her full name, and wrote a statement saying she would not commit more evil. He then sent one person to escort her out the door. After walking about a hundred steps, she fell to the ground. Pan Jia supported her up and carried her back to the room. She was completely unaware of what was happening, dazed and drunk, as if in a stupor. After a night and day, her spirit slightly recovered, but she was extremely ashamed. It took her two weeks to calm down and recall what had happened, but she could not speak of it, as she was also ashamed of it.

Strange Bamboo of the Sea Mountain A wealthy merchant from Wenzhou named Zhang Yuan had traveled as a sea trader for decades, never missing a season. In the seventh year of the Shaoxing era, he crossed the great ocean and encountered a storm that carried his ship away, leaving him with no idea where it had drifted to. After several days, he found a mountain, its tall bamboos reaching the clouds, stretching as far as the eye could see. He went ashore and cut down ten stalks, intending to use them as oars and paddles. Once the task was complete, he saw an old man in white clothes who said, "What era is this? It is not the place you should stay. You should quickly return; do not delay." The sailors bowed their hands and replied, "We have lost our way and are afraid of being buried in the fish's belly. Divine old man, please instruct us on how to reach our hometown." The old man pointed to the southeast direction, and indeed, they managed to return safely. Nine out of the ten bamboos were used, and as they approached the shore, there were Japanese guests and African slaves who looked at the mast and ship's side, clutched their chests, and kept crying out "what a pity!" Once the ship was anchored, the crowd stared into the ship and saw one bamboo still remaining. They competed to buy it, saying, "I don't care about the price. I suspect they want to test it and are willing to pay two thousand strings of cash." The crowd shouted in unison "good" and immediately took the money and paid me. I said, "This is a treasure. I was just joking. Unless it's five thousand strings of cash, don't even consider it." The African slave was particularly pleased and agreed to the amount. They brought the money and handed it over, and then we made an agreement. We agreed, and I asked them, "Since we have traded this bamboo, we cannot regret it. But I really don't know what kind of treasure this is, yet you all are so eager to buy it. Why don't you tell me about it?" They replied, "This is the treasure bamboo from the mountain of treasure. Every time a stalk is planted in the vast sea, treasures gather without being collected. I have been traveling by ship all my life, seeing the waves as vast as the sky as if they were flat ground. I only know the name of the bamboo but have never seen it. Even if it costs millions, I would not hesitate." I then sighed and handed it over.

Bao Hua Bell In the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era, Wang Dequan, also known as Shaoqing Jue, was a junior official in charge of grain supplies. He lived in a place called Hengjin, located in the southwest of Pingjiang Prefecture, in an area known as Ludou Bay. He owned several hundred acres of land and had intended to build a dwelling there, but he did not succeed. About half a mile away, on Bao Hua Mountain, there was a Buddhist temple called Ci Yan Zhi Xian, whose grand halls and spacious courtyards surpassed those of nearby temples. The locals called it "Bao Hua." One day, the abbot of the temple came to visit Wang and told him that the previous night he had dreamed of a divine being who said, "Wang, the judge of Hengjin, is a former benefactor of this temple. There is a good karmic connection here. You should encourage him to live in this place." The abbot found the meaning of the dream extremely mysterious. Wang, after much consideration, decided to move but could not quickly organize the preparations. Just then, his brother-in-law, Li Shu, a local official (tongpan), offered to sell his house. Wang bought it and dismantled it to use its materials for construction. Within two years, his new residence was completed. One month later, Wang visited the temple with his relatives and friends, climbed to the bell tower, and read the inscription on the bell, which stated: "In the first year of the Chonghe era, the junior official, former county magistrate of Kunshan, Wang Jue, donated one thousand units of pure wealth to cast this bell." The abbot and his disciples looked at each other in amazement, realizing that the divine being's words were indeed prophetic.

Mr. Ruan Gongming Mr. Ruan Gongming, a scholar from Yuzhen County in Lin'an, was known for his intelligence and lack of adherence to strict rules. He was a student at the Imperial Academy and was on his way to Linping Town to marry a woman. After drinking, he struck the boatman with a pole, causing him to fall into the water and drown. Local officials recorded the incident and transferred him to Renhe Prison, where the law deemed his crime as accidental injury, not warranting the death penalty. However, due to prolonged detention, he lost his life. Ten years later, a fellow student named Wang Zhi, who had arrived from a distant state to replace him in the central capital, sat in an inn late in the evening. A servant came in holding a business card and said, "Mr. Ruan pays respects." Looking at the card, it was indeed Mr. Ruan. They met, expressed joy and sorrow, and reminisced about old times. They then invited each other to drink at an inn. After a few drinks, Mr. Ruan sighed and said, "I have been in the realm of the dead for a long time, but because my life span has not yet ended, the underworld does not allow me to be reborn. I am only detained by the city god during the day, allowing me to come and go, but at night, I am confined to a dry well in Wu Mountain. There are many like me in the capital city. Every evening, those who are bound with yellow belts and walk with heads down, crawling, are all of us." Turning around, he pointed to his servant and said, "This person is also one of us, serving each other according to rank and status, just like in the world, not worth being strange." After the wine, they parted ways, shaking hands and entrusting a message. Mr. Ruan said, "I still have ten years to stay and cannot be reborn. Please write a letter to inform my father to hold a water and land temple fair, which can reduce the limit by six or seven years. Hope you do not forget." He bowed deeply and left. The next day, Wang Zhi sent a report to his father, who did not believe it. Wang Zhi himself donated money to arrange Buddhist rites. A few days later, when Wang Zhi returned to the inn, the innkeeper said that Mr. Ruan had left a thank-you note and gone away, using paper that was probably not from the human world. From then on, he was never seen again.

A Mermaid and a Sword-Spine Snake In the year 1167, during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, a snake handler in Huzhou kept a fish-like creature in a clay pot. The creature resembled a catfish, was black in color, and had two hands like a strong man, with ten fingers on each hand. This creature was known as a mermaid. In the mountainous area of Wuchang, there was a type of snake called the Sword-Spine Bird. It was large and agile, and when it encountered a person, it would raise its head and stand still, with its tail barely an inch above the ground. One had to quickly avoid it to prevent harm. Yousheng Palace on Bian Mountain At the beginning of the Shaoxing era, in the western part of Bian Mountain in Huzhou, there was a Taoist priest named Shen Chongzhen who obtained a statue of the deity Zhenwu. He built a hermitage there and worshipped the statue. He also used talismanic water to exorcise evil spirits and treat illnesses, with remarkable efficacy. The local people called him the True Man. Later, he built a hall and purchased a degree as a Taoist priest. Many of his followers, numbering in the dozens, joined him. Suddenly, four red lights appeared behind the hall. When they were closely observed, nothing could be seen. After ten days, someone dug at the spot where the lights had appeared and found a green stone, about three feet long and one foot wide. The stone was intricately carved with patterns of the heavens and earth intersecting, and it shimmered like water or sunlight. They carved it into an altar niche, and from then on, the number of believers and worshippers increased significantly. They moved the inscription from the original site to a new location and renamed it Yousheng Palace. Shen Shouyue, a high-ranking official, recommended the temple to the emperor, who granted it the title "Yousheng Palace." After Shen Chongzhen's death, his grandson now manages the temple.

### 圆贞僧粥 吕彦能从天台城出发进入山中，经过一个村落时路过一座小寺庙。当时寺庙的住持圆贞恰好外出守舍，一个童子熟睡在室内。吕彦能呼唤他，但他没有醒来。吕彦能行走了很久，感到非常疲倦，便暂时躺在榻上休息。他闻到厨房里散发出阵阵芬芳的香气，便起身前往厨房查看。只见炊烟微温，厨房里空无一物，只看到锅旁用线系着四个崇宁年间的铜钱，放在锅的四角，这让他感到困惑不解，于是他离开了。圆贞从外面回来迎接他，并笑着说：“手脚已经露出来了，不再需要掩饰了，希望你能留下来一起享用。”于是他们一起喝了数杯酒，圆贞准备了一碗粥，粥中撒上了白色的米粒，味道极其甜美，不知道是什么食材。圆贞说：“刚才看到佛前系着四个铜钱，所以才这样做。”原来他的做法是用四条大鱼，彻底清洗干净，去掉头尾和皮，用线系住鱼骨的两端，悬挂在锅中，然后加入水和米，所有的盐、酒、姜、椒等调料都有固定的用量。当鱼完全煮烂时，将四个铜钱聚在一起并拉出，鱼骨完全脱落，鱼肉变得非常嫩。因此，粥的味道如此美妙。山僧的生活虽然简朴，但不足以向贵公子讲述。吕彦能喝得饱饱的，便离开了。 ### 虞一杀螺 奉化海上的渔人虞一以捕捉呀螺为生。每当捕到时，他通常用生丝线制作一个圆形的套子，等待呀螺吐出肉时，便尽力将它紧紧绑住，然后迅速拉出，不留任何残留。几年后，虞一的右手生了一个严重的疮，五指和皮肤都脱落了，痛苦不堪，几乎无法动弹。他后悔之前的行业，发誓不再做，经过很长时间才康复。于是他抛弃了妻子和孩子，出家成为寺庙的奴仆。

Zheng Xingpo In the city of Hezhou, there was an old woman named Zheng Xingpo who, from a young age, abstained from drinking alcohol and eating meat, and silently recited the Diamond Sutra without ever stopping. In the spring of Xinhui, Shaoxing, she went to Baoren Guangxiao Temple to listen to Venerable Master Wu preach. On her way, she passed by a butcher's door and saw them cutting up beef. She jokingly told those accompanying her, "If this meat were cut raw and seasoned with salt and vinegar, it would surely be delicious." By the time she arrived at the temple, Venerable Master Wu asked her, "How is it that you can eat raw beef?" She replied, "Since birth, I have never tasted meat. How could the monk say such a thing?" Venerable Master Wu said, "You truly do not eat? I will give you medicine. I will take a small amount of medicine and mix it into a cup of tea for you to drink." After a short while, she spat out a bowl of raw beef. She then regretted her false thoughts and displayed a miraculous transformation, intensifying her self-discipline even more. She is now over ninety years old. A Poor Hermit from Jiaxing In the winter of the fifth year of Shaoxi, there was a poor hermit in Xiu City whose clothing was extremely ragged, but his complexion was full and lustrous. When people asked him about it, he would pick up the ground with his right hand as if taking something, several times. He never begged for money, and people only pointed at him as a subject of ridicule. Suddenly, he bowed to Lü Deqing and his brothers, saying, "When you have nothing to do, come to my hermitage for tea." He did not stay long and left, suggesting that he was not an ordinary person. The next day, when they visited his dwelling, he was already gone. The Broken Jade Dragon Hairpin Around the Pingshan Hall in Yangzhou, there were the imperial gardens of Emperor Yang of Sui, much of which had been cultivated into farmland. In the ninth year of Chunxi, a villager dug up the soil and found a half of a white jade hairpin with a warm and smooth texture. The upper part was carved with dragon scales in yellow chestnut color, and the luster seemed to be moving. The commander Zhu Zhen bought it for three thousand coins and showed it to his guests, including Lü Deqing, who had seen it before.

### Translation #### Ban Gu in a Dream In the sixth year of the Qian Dao era, during winter, Lu Deqing, accompanied by his friends Wang Li, Yi Yu, and Wei Zizheng, went to Lin'an to observe the Southern Suburbs ceremony and stayed at the Huang family's guesthouse. Both Wang and Wei dreamed of a person dressed in Han dynasty attire who introduced himself as Ban Gu. After meeting, they questioned him about difficult points in the history of the Western Han dynasty. As he was leaving, he said, "Tomorrow I will pass by my home briefly for a short visit. Would that be acceptable?" Upon waking, they could not understand the dream and recounted it to each other. The dreams were largely the same. On that very day, while reviewing the five chariots, the four of them sat together in a tea house outside Jiahuimen. They saw a note attached to the end of a piece of red paper that read, "Tonight, we will discuss the *Book of Han*." They laughed together and said, "Isn't Ban Gu here?" They returned to the office gate, feeling a bit hungry, and went into a food shop. Looking at the sign, they saw it was Ban's four-color buns. They laughed and sighed, and thus believing, they took a rest and had a light meal. Even in dreams, such things are evident. Isn't everything predetermined? #### Zhang Si and the Sea Snails In Lin'an, near Jieqiao Gate, north of Taiping Bridge, there lived a poor man named Zhang Si who made a living selling sea snails. Whenever boats from Zhejiang arrived, he would buy the snails and store them at home. Calculating the daily sales, he would cook them with salt. People in Hangzhou liked to eat them, and he accumulated the killing of countless lives. On the night of the sixteenth month of the Chunxi era, the snails in the pot climbed up the walls, onto the roof, and into the beds. They wrapped around clothes, were swept away, and gathered again, until they stuck to the skin and wouldn't come off. Zhang Si had a sudden realization and took an oath saying, "From today onward, I will no longer engage in this evil deed. I will find another way to make a living. I pray that the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas watch over me." When he finished speaking, the snails all fell to the ground. At dawn, there was nothing left. He threw them into the river and changed his business to frying tofu to support himself and his family.

### Translation of the Source Text **Yijian Zhi, Volume 3, Part D** <Subcategory: Strange Tales, Category: Novels, Class: Miscellaneous, Book: Yijian Zhi> **Qinding Siku Quanshu** **Yijian Zhi, Volume 4, Part D** Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty **Lin Ziyuan** Lin Ziyuan, a scholar from Minqing, Fuzhou, had taken the provincial examinations multiple times but had not been successful. In the thirteenth year of the Chunxi era (1186 AD), he dreamed that someone told him, "You should wait for your elder brother to become the governor of Fujian before being recommended for the imperial examinations." Upon waking, he wrote this down in a document. His elder brother, Lin Cheng, although he had once studied Confucianism, had long since abandoned his books and taken up the life of a merchant. Friends who knew about this would often tease him when they met, saying, "Has your elder brother opened up a fief yet?" Lin Ziyuan, however, gave up on his hopes and stopped longing for it. By the first year of the Qingyuan era (1195 AD), after the provincial examinations, he heard that Lin Yunzhi from Xuancheng had become the governor of Fujian. He was delighted and told his friends, "My elder brother, whom I was waiting for, has the same name as the new governor. The spirits have not deceived me. I am eagerly awaiting good news within a set period." On the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month, the results were announced, and Lin Ziyuan indeed ranked first in the literary category. This proved that the success or failure of candidates is not accidental. The local governor's appointments were also predetermined nine years in advance. Therefore, everything from morning to night, from sudden departures to unexpected arrivals, and the frequent changes in the world, when people blame fate, they do not realize that the gods have a plan and use people as tools. It is all a matter of effort and planning, and it is just laughable. His eldest son, who was a state affairs officer, found this explanation very accurate.

杨九巡 术士行山者或畏墓穴妨其身则必迂枉避就如予前志所书刘少保家保安军地者是已鄱阳人杨九巡习此技而絶贫困至无衣可出梁企道侍郎亡访地未得杨往见寓客张承事谓之曰我有一个好经纪恨衣裳破碎难谒达官非君不能成吾事张曰此易办耳吾职库役其中多有之即择衫裘之属数种衣之导往梁氏先以地图入梁之子宏夫视之喜便与偕诣彼处至即指一穴曰此是也宏 夫囬还四顾曰地势趋下恐有水患令移上三丈许杨惨然曰鸦鸦俗间叹声盖知其于已不利也及塟毕得犒钱二百千次日忽病风挛数月小愈扶杖诣张谢曰赖君之赐使我一家温饱愧无以报有土湖上一地可作花园切不可失张本邢州人北俗以豫凶事为讳不先卜夀蔵杨之意欲使他日作塟地故婉其辞张曰吾衰矣无用旋营一圃也然竟如其言及卒其子圮塟之十年后圮从白屋登第 Translation: El Jiu Xun The geomancer who travels through the mountains sometimes fears that certain grave sites might harm his body, so he will take detours and make concessions, as I previously recorded in my writings about the security forces' grounds belonging to Liu Shao Bao. Yang Jiu Xun, a man from Poyang, practices this skill but is extremely poor, to the point where he has no clothes to wear. When Liang Qi Dao, a magistrate, died and sought a burial site without success, Yang went to see the guest Ouyang Xiu, saying, "I have a good business opportunity, but my clothes are torn and I cannot visit high officials. Without you, I cannot succeed." Ouyang replied, "This is easy to arrange. In my job in the imperial treasury, there are many such things." He then selected several sets of clothes and dressed Yang, guiding him to Liang's son Hongfu. They first showed him a map, and Hongfu was pleased, so he went with them to the site. Upon arrival, Yang pointed to a grave and said, "This is the right place." Hongfu looked around and said, "The terrain slopes downward, and there might be water problems. Let's move it up by three zhang." Yang sighed, "Ya ya," a common expression of lament, indicating that he knew this site was unfavorable for him. After the burial, Yang received a gratuity of two hundred thousand coins. The next day, he suddenly fell ill with wind paralysis, which lasted for several months. He recovered slightly and, with a cane, went to thank Ouyang, saying, "Thanks to your help, my family has become well-fed and comfortable. I feel ashamed that I have nothing to repay you with. There is a piece of land by a lake that can be developed into a garden. Do not lose it." Ouyang, originally from Xingzhou, was from a northern region where it was taboo to discuss ominous matters. He did not want to predict a grave site in advance, as Yang intended to use it for his own burial in the future. Therefore, he spoke tactfully, saying, "I am too old to build a garden now." However, he eventually followed Yang's advice. After Yang's death, his son Pi arranged for his burial. Ten years later, Pi rose from humble origins to pass the imperial examinations.

Scribe Jiang Zuo Hong Yingxian, a scribe from Wuyuan County, returned home after serving in the countryside for many years. The following year, in autumn, he asked for leave to return home. After a month of tending to his fields, he returned again. His second son, Zhongkan, dreamed that Jiang Zuo came to pay respects, as if they were meeting for the first time. Zhongkan asked how much Jiang Zuo had earned. Jiang Zuo replied, "I only returned home and immediately fell ill. People thought it was an epidemic and did not come to help, so I died of hunger and poverty. Now I have come to inform you of one thing: My younger brother, Shēn, is a lay practitioner at Longquan Temple. If he becomes a monk in the future, our father will have no descendants to carry on the family line. I am now a servant." Zhongkan then asked, "If you died from an epidemic, do you think anyone will handle your burial arrangements?" Jiang Zuo replied, "Relied on the local community and clan to raise money to prepare for my burial. They used a thin garment to wrap the body and a white pine wood coffin for the burial. I was buried outside the eastern gate of the county." After finishing his words, he wept and left. Zhongkan then asked what he wanted. Jiang Zuo said, "I have no money with me. I wish to be given travel expenses." In his dream, Zhongkan bought two thousand paper money and called a shaman to make an outer guide for announcing and burning. After that, he asked again, "Was there any obstacle on your journey?" Jiang Zuo replied, "I was particularly troubled by the customs checkpoints." Zhongkan then made the shaman give him a guide, which Jiang Zuo took and accepted thanks. He then climbed onto the bed, covered himself with a blanket, and a short while later, when the blanket was lifted, he was gone. A few days later, the former county official Wang Ji came to inquire about the matter, and everything matched what Zhongkan had dreamed. He then called Jiang Shēn to stay in his study, where he served for a year before leaving. The narrator is currently a scribe in the office of the governor of Guangzhou.

A Witch Named Zhang In Huaijin Village, Wuyuan, there was a witch named Zhang Sheng who was skilled in witchcraft and could bring disaster to people. She often demanded money and grain from wealthy households. If they were dissatisfied with her demands, she would leap and jump vigorously, which she called "doing somersaults." The households would then fall ill with skin diseases or rashes, or even die. Because of this, no one dared to defy her. A scholar named Wang Yanrui admired her ability to gain unjust wealth and supported her. He posted a large sign that read "Tiantianmen" and hung it at her residence. The witch wore a tall hat, a loose-sleeved red robe, and a wide yellow belt. Every day, she would ascend to a high seat and freely discuss good and bad fortune. She had up to thirty followers. One day, a county bowman named Wang Zao passed by her door and was angry that she did not burn incense. He sent someone to take her back and interrogate her. Wang Zao said, "Wash your hands and show respect. After burning incense, I will command my attendants to bind you." Zhang Sheng replied, "If you have magical formulas, you can send invisible soldiers to seize me in the void and beat me. Otherwise, I will leave." Wang Zao reported this to the county magistrate, who then had Wang Zao prepare a complaint and go to the county office to report the incident. The county magistrate, Hong Yingxian, pursued and arrested Zhang Sheng to the court and interrogated her about her witchcraft. She responded, "What I do is the true method of the heart, best for curing people's illnesses. I never intended to create chaos." Her words and demeanor were arrogant. The magistrate said, "You can do somersaults. If you can jump over the drum tower, I will release you." Zhang Sheng became frightened and immediately knelt down, pleading pitifully. She was then imprisoned in jail. The next day, the magistrate personally went to interrogate her again, holding a talisman and a magical seal in his hand and saying to her, "You often say you can divine the future. Tell me, what is in my hand?" Zhang Sheng was startled and could not respond. She was beaten with twenty strokes of the stick and expelled from the territory. Initially, the witch had used the creation of a court to collect money from the people, amounting to several thousand strings of cash. All of this was seized and registered into the official treasury, filling the monthly salary. The townspeople celebrated together. Wang Yanrui, fearing punishment, also moved to another place.

Incantation for Treating Burns and Fire Injuries Local shamans are often skilled in chanting incantations and performing feats involving boiling water and fire. My younger brother Yuan Zhong learned a specific incantation that can effectively treat such injuries. The incantation goes as follows: "The Buddha of the Dragon Tree grants me the power to practice the Northern Water Fire Control Method. The Buddha of the Dragon Tree, I am the water of the Northern Water, capable of extinguishing all fire. Mars in the hours of Chen and Wu must be subdued. Quickly, as the law commands." After reciting the incantation, one holds the True Martial Seal and blows on the affected area. Then, a small amount of cold water is used to wash the burn. Even if the hands and feet are severely burned and blistered, this method can still provide relief. Courtesan Feng Yan Feng Yan, a courtesan from the state of Xiang, was fourteen years old. Her beauty surpassed that of her peers, and she was skilled in singing and dancing. She was originally from the Xie family. Her mother went to the prefecture to report that when this daughter was sold, she was only five years old, and an agreement was made to return her after seven years. Now, two years have passed beyond the agreed term, and she requests to take her back for raising, so that she does not have to spend her life in the world of debauchery. The prefect Zhang Ding Sou called Feng Yan and asked her, "When you left home, you were still young. Can you recognize your mother?" She replied, "Yes, I can recognize her." The prefect then brought the old woman to show her to Feng Yan. Feng Yan shook her head and said, "That is not my mother." The prefect ruled that since she was not the true mother, it would be difficult to force the return. He dismissed them, and the old woman cried bitterly with regret. Feng Yan returned to Feng's residence. As soon as she entered the door, she became lost and could not recognize the way. Her mother asked her why. She said, "Before my eyes, there is a dim and obstructive feeling as if hands were covering me, and I cannot understand it." Upon reaching her room, she felt increasingly confused and reported her condition to the prefect, requesting leave due to illness. The prefect did not believe her and, during a gathering, ordered her to perform as usual. She performed as if in a daze, and when given wine, she did not know where the cup was. The prefect still suspected her of being deceitful and said, "Go back, just go straight." Feng Yan walked aimlessly and fell into the courtyard. It was then evident that she was ill. She was removed from the registry and subsequently went blind. Judge Sun Dingchen, who was present, once saw her and noted that her eyes were still clear, but her blindness remained unchanged.

Xu Cheng Disobeyed His Mother A commoner from Jinxi named Xu Cheng, a farmer, returned home from the fields and asked his mother for food. His mother told him, "The rice is in the pot; you can go and take it yourself." When he reached the stove and looked into the pot, he found it empty. Angered, he cursed, saying, "You're not blind, why would you lie like this?" He immediately went outside and broke off a branch of firewood. Suddenly, the branch leapt up, directly entered his eye, pierced one eye, and came out. He immediately fainted and took several days to recover. Now he is a porter for Sun Dingchen and recounts his past misdeeds, but it's too late to regret them. Wu Erjiu In the spring of the second year of Shaoxi, a commoner from Jinling named Wu Erjiu was going to plant rice and asked his mother to lend him the black silk robe he was wearing, saying, "Tomorrow we need money for planting, hiring workers, and food expenses." His mother replied, "I'm afraid of the spring cold, and tomorrow might not work out. Your wife has her own coat; why don't you just take it?" Wu Erjiu angrily left. His family had over ten mulberry trees, which the wife and mother-in-law had divided equally. One day, the mother-in-law mistakenly picked the wife's mulberry leaves. The wife told Wu Erjiu, who immediately went into his mother's room and dragged her out, saying, "You didn't lend me the coat, and you picked my mulberry leaves. Don't live here anymore, go find food somewhere else." He then took an axe and chopped up the bed and the straw mat, destroying them too. It was already late in the evening, so the mother went to a neighbor's house to ask to stay for the night. The neighbor said, "Your son is very bad-tempered; if I let you stay, you'll cause a commotion," and refused.

A woman named "Bu Na Mu" stood outside under the eaves, hesitating and wandering, sitting and standing. By the fifth watch, she called out to the empty sky, hoping for the dawn to come early. Soon after, she heard the rooster crow, and the sky was overcast but suddenly cleared up. Wu was very happy and went to the fields with three farmers. He asked his mother to return to their home and found that the bed had been broken. She cried out in tears. Her wife said that her husband had beaten and scolded her all night because of the mulberry leaves. The grandmother left, and at noon, a cloud rose from the mountain, soon turning into a dense fog. The four people sought shelter under the mountain due to a sudden thunderstorm, which cleared up shortly after. The three farmers gathered as usual, but Wu was missing. They found him buried upside down in the rice, two feet deep, firmly stuck. The wife quickly invited a monk to recite scriptures and offer prayers of repentance, and by evening, Wu was freed, but his whole body was black like coal. Qiu Cen ate mushrooms. A scholar named Qiu Cen from Jinxi stayed in a wealthy family's garden, where a servant lived nearby and was tasked with delivering meals. The servant once prepared a large mushroom stew, and they all ate it at night. Qiu Cen, impressed, asked for wine to pair with the mushroom stew. After eating, the servant and his family all vomited blood and died. The host feared that Qiu Cen would also suffer and tried to get him drunk to protect him, but Qiu Cen remained unharmed, and it was believed that the wine neutralized the mushroom's poison. The host's house was thirty miles away from Qiu Cen's residence. On the way, he met a friend and told him about the incident. They stayed and drank together, finishing the bottle of wine. Qiu Cen fell asleep on the couch and woke up without any discomfort. Those who knew about it believed that the wine had neutralized the mushroom's poison.

### 缪夫人 In the fourth month of the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, when Luo Chunbo was the Minister of Justice, his mother, Lady Miao, was in Linchuan. She dreamed that Luo Chunbo was riding in a carriage back to their hometown, dressed in mourning attire. She was greatly alarmed, and one of his attendants brought a box, which he opened to reveal a document granting him a position in the Chancellery of Military Affairs. Luo Chunbo then changed into official robes, tied on a belt with round and square knots, and appeared before guests to greet them. Lady Miao was delighted and asked, "What new position has the Minister of Justice been appointed to?" The guests replied, "He has been appointed to the Chancellery of Military Affairs." Upon waking, she told the family, and they were all happy but also fearful. After some time, they deeply doubted the dream. On the sixth day, the will of Emperor Shizong arrived, confirming the omen of the mourning attire. Two months later, Luo Chunbo entered the Chancellery of Military Affairs, and he realized that what he had dreamed of was indeed this. ### 王监之 Wang Jianzhi, a student at the Imperial Academy and a native of Wuzhou, was on leave during the autumn examination of the ninth year of the Shaoxi era. He often invited a soothsayer to predict his fortunes. One day, he burned incense, wrote with a brush, and bowed to pray for the outcome of the coming year. The soothsayer then wrote fifteen characters: "Luo Chunbo is eager to meet you. He has been appointed to a position in Fangzhou." At that time, Luo Chunbo was serving as both the Minister of Justice and the Minister of Personnel. Wang was very pleased, thinking that Luo would surely be the examiner for the spring examination, and that he would be able to pass under his evaluation. He wanted to visit the academy to take leave, but as he was preparing to leave, he suddenly developed a severe hernia and had to rush home. Soon after, he died. They then prepared his funeral arrangements, using two pieces of pine wood and two pieces of cypress wood, with boards in front and behind. It was then that they verified the soothsayer's prediction: the three characters "Luo Chunbo" were indeed an omen of Fangzhou, how could it be considered auspicious?

Zhu Si, a man from Wuxing, had a daughter who was a concubine of Wu Jufu. Every year, he must go to visit her, always bringing one servant with him. On his way to Xiangyang, passing through the area around the Yangtze River, he encountered a strong and tall thief who stopped him and opened his box. Zhu Si was also strong, brave, and intelligent. Taking advantage of an opportunity from behind, he kicked the thief down and fell to the bottom of the cliff, and then took the thief's spear and continued on his way. In the evening, he arrived at an inn. The elderly woman in charge saw the spear and questioned him. Zhu Si then recounted the events, and the elderly woman was startled and seemed to have lost something. She was about to go to bed when the thief, limping and dragging, came from outside and sighed, saying, "Today, I went out and lost out, being trapped and humiliated by a guest." He wanted to tell the details, but the elderly woman shook her finger and said, "Don't talk about him. He is staying here." She then prepared food to feed her husband and patted her own heart. Zhu Si was greatly afraid and cut through the wall and fled, hiding in the grass and searching for the thief. He held a torch to search until midnight but did not find him. The thief's husband and wife followed him for several miles. Zhu Si thought they had gone far away and quickly left, burning his house. Not long after, the thief returned, panicked and carried water to extinguish the fire, and did not have time to look for Zhu Si again. Thus, Zhu Si escaped.

The Tiger at the Guesthouse in Wuchang During the winter of the fourth year of the Shaoxi era, the son of Censorial Commissioner Sun Dan, named Yu Gong, traveled from Lin'an with his family to serve as an official at the Military Command in Xiangyang. Passing through Ezhou, they left their boat and headed for land, staying overnight at an inn. They felt there were thieves moving around outside the door. The servants shouted at them, saying, "These are officials of the military command and should not cause trouble." After a while, the situation gradually calmed down. They cautiously opened the door to look outside and saw two tigers fighting each other. Upon arriving in Xiangyang, Yu Gong sent his servant Cai De back to check. That night, Cai De stayed at a guesthouse in Wuchang and saw the people in the guesthouse bustling about with fearful expressions. He asked slowly what had happened. They said that last night, the four of them (father and sons) were all awake, gathered around the stove, when suddenly a tiger rushed in and sat on the stove. They were in great fear, thinking that one of them would surely be eaten, so they dared not move at all, just added more wood to the fire and stayed with the tiger. The tiger remained calm and composed until dawn, then leaped out with vigor. A neighbor woman just came to borrow a light, and as soon as she reached the door, she was scared and fell dead. They were fortunate to escape, but their spirits are still unsettled, and they fear that the tiger might come again tonight, and there is no way to escape. Cai De also became pale with fear, spending the whole night in fear and unable to sleep. The next morning, at around 7 AM, he finally dared to leave.

The Yellow Top Scholar Huang Ying, a scholar from Poyang, had the courtesy name Ji Peng. He was skilled in writing and had a reputation for excellence during the Xuanhe era while studying at the Imperial Academy. He often dreamed of someone calling him "the Yellow Top Scholar." Huang Ying was quite confident in his abilities and frequently mentioned this dream to his friends. However, as soon as he entered the examination hall, he always failed. In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, he was exempt from the preliminary exams and went directly to the provincial exams in Lin'an. That year, Huang Gongdu topped the list of candidates, and there was no palace examination, so Huang Gongdu was named first. Huang Ying finally realized and sighed, saying, "After dreaming of being the Yellow Top Scholar for twenty years, it is now taken by someone else." He returned home with a gloomy expression. After a long time, he barely obtained a special appointment and became an acting official. His two essays on the Flying Peak and the Smiling Flower were remarkably impressive, and one of them was presented to Emperor Gaozong for imperial review. Huang Ying's residence was a hundred miles away from the city, and whenever he entered the city, he stayed at An Guo Temple because he disliked the bathroom being unclean. When he needed to use the toilet, he would go to the Miao Guo Temple Hall. He was an upright man. The Residence of Wang Zhuangmin Wang Zhuangmin built a residence in Fuliang County. The house was spacious and grand, with his children living in separate rooms, but he could not accommodate all of them. His nephew, Guo, was studying for the imperial exams and hired a local man named Zhu Guiwei to teach at the eastern wing of the house. There was also an outer room where two scholars studied. All four of them lived together. Guo once went out to study first and carried a paper lamp with him. As he approached his residence, he saw a man dressed neatly peeking through a hole in the window outside the scholars' room. He thought the two scholars were playing a trick on each other. When he entered the room, he found that both scholars were taking a short nap. He was startled and suspected it was not a person. He went out to look, but saw nothing. The paper was never broken, and it was a ghost. This translation aims to be both faithful to the original text and readable in English, preserving the cultural and historical context while addressing syntactic ambiguities and proper nouns.

### The Strange Tales of the World: Volume Four of the Yi Jian Zhi Imperially Approved Four Treasuries The Strange Tales of the World: Volume Five of the Yi Jian Zhi By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Master Pan Who Deals with Ghosts The story of the cat Qìngxǐ (庆喜) has already been recorded in Volume C of the "Yi Jian Zhi." After being dead for twenty-two years, during the summer of the year 1192 (Shaoxi Renzi), the master's wife contracted a water-induced illness and gradually became more dangerous and weak. The servant Wang Fu said he once heard from Qian Er Lang, who runs a tea shop in Tianjing Lane, that Master Pan, who lives outside the Gate of Mount Gen, is skilled at dealing with supernatural matters and is commonly known as Master Pan Who Deals with Ghosts. He suggested trying to pray to him. Wang then took Qian to Master Pan's residence. Master Pan burned incense and paper money, placed a handkerchief before the deity's statue, and as the lamp was lit, a woman and a cat were seen standing together. Master Pan said they both have grievances, but he did not understand the reason. The two men took the handkerchief back and explained to the master's wife what happened. The wife was greatly surprised and said, "Last year, I really scolded this maid, but her death was due to injury, not because I caused it. Why would she cause such disturbances?" She then sent someone to see Master Pan again. He then ordered a child to possess someone and summon the spirit. The child immediately spoke in a woman's voice and said, "My name is Qìng."

The woman died an unnatural death and has not yet been reborn. It was not the mistress who killed her, but the cause of her death originated from another source. Previously, her good fortune had not yet diminished, so she had been waiting for a long time. Pan refused to perform rituals and instead made a cat cry out several times. The child became dizzy and upon waking could not fully understand what happened. Pan sent a message to the city god to place the deceased in the underworld and used spells to treat water-related diseases and illness, with some improvement. However, after a few days, the condition worsened and the person did not recover. Qian went to mourn and had a dream where a woman came and said she was seeking justice, which was unrelated to Qian. The woman ordered Pan to imprison her in a dungeon, suggesting that she cannot be brought to the underworld for testimony unless forced, and then she fell ill and died shortly after. Alas, the path of karma is vast and unclear, making it difficult to question. The death of the cat led to misfortune for the mistress and Qian, implying that the consequences were excessive. Is this due to fate or mere coincidence? Pan practiced divination with spirits and opened a divination shop in the year of Wuxu during the Shaoxing era. His nephew, who was previously a judge in Wuling, should be appointed as a deputy official. Someone in Huating, a scholar named Hu, was waiting in Jiankang and fell ill, with the news being very unfavorable. If someone seeks a replacement, they can be appointed in two and a half years. Yu, who was unsure and planned to consult a fortune-teller, visited Pan and bought incense and paper, leaving a blank sheet of paper in the shrine for a prayer request, and agreeing to return the next day. When Yu returned, Pan took the ashes from the incense burner, examined the paper for a long time, wiped it clean, and saw a square character form. Yu asked about Hu and concluded that the matter would not go well. At that time, Zhou Yigong was in power.

**Translation:** When Yù went to visit him and made a request for Jiankang, Zhōu said, "Both deputy positions have been vacant for three to four years. Why not seek Ganzhou?" Yù replied, "I am not uninformed, but because there are many matters to attend to in Ráo, Jīn, Yǒu, Huáng, Yán, and Fāng, and the official period is far away." Zhōu said, "Ráo has already stopped serving. Huang should immediately take up the position, but he is tied up by personal considerations and is planning to request a temple post. Now, it is only to replace Fāng Shèng. Many people are seeking this position, and if you don't take it today, it will fall into someone else's hands, and it will be too late to regret." Yù gladly thanked him and immediately submitted a petition to request it, but still had doubts. The next day, Ráo's retirement order was issued, and Huang then requested a temple post. When he arrived at the hall, those with the same qualifications competed to seek it, about thirty people in total, but Yù's petition was submitted first, so he got it. Thus, the correctness of the character 'Fāng' was verified. **Xia Juyuan** Xia Juyuan was also skilled in divination and lived in Lin'an. He charged five hundred coins per卦 for any who came to consult him. In the winter of the third year of Shaoxi, Yù, after being relieved of his deputy position in Ganzhou, went to the capital with his son Jie as his companion. After arriving, they found that one of the imperial edicts was missing. Despite sending a servant back home to search for it, they could not find it and were still troubled by it. They then went together to Xia's shop. Xia wrote on the paper, "The matter is far away. Combine the characters '食' and '尧' to read it as '饶.'" Asked, "Is it correct?" Answered, "Yes." Said, "The document is being held by a person with many mouths, and the mandarin ducks are guarding it. There is no need to worry." The explanation was vague and without definite meaning, so it was considered false. Later, the servant came from Ráo with the forgotten document. It was forgotten when packing the luggage, and the concubine Fú'ān saw it. In Jie's room, there were ten boxes, and a clay mold of flying swallows and warm-sleeping mandarin ducks was used as a label. So, it was placed in the mandarin ducks' box, responding to the mandarin ducks' guarding words. The character '福' has a mouth and a field, and another four mouths, so the person with many mouths is like this. It is also miraculous!

In the thirtieth-first year of the Shaoxing era, Li Jun, a Chaosan Lang, supervised the salt warehouses along the coast. The sea produces many clams, and officials often used them to make vinegar and meat sauce, which they sent as gifts to relatives and old friends. Li Jun purchased a great deal of these clams. One night, he dreamed as if he were wandering elsewhere, and in the official office, he saw a divine person in ceremonial robes and a hat sitting upright. The divine person called out to him and said, "Why have you recently killed so many living beings? You should be reduced by one calculation. I think you are good, so I will forgive you. From now on, you should strongly refrain from it." The divine person then introduced himself, saying, "My surname is Yu." He continued, "I served up to the rank of Xuanjiao Lang. Due to years of service, I should have been promoted to the rank of Chaoyi and undergo evaluation, but the order for Tongzhi was issued, and I was unable to bow and receive it. I am about to be replaced and will manage all birds, beasts, and aquatic creatures. Before death, I was related to you by marriage, so I specially tell you this. Then he left. There was a brief statement that said, 'Reading scriptures and wearing the attire of a Taoist priest.' Li Jun woke up.

Thinking about it, Yu was the name of the wife’s clan, which followed the “立人” (standing person) character, and she was the wife of the ancestors of the Yu clan. But I did not understand the explanation after his departure. The next day, I asked the locals and learned that there are two types of clams: the small ones are called “child clams,” and the large ones are called “Taoist clams.” Yesterday’s preparation was selecting the larger ones. I immediately called a monk to recite sutras to offer merit for him. When my term was completed and I returned home, I told the Yu clan about it. The one I dreamed of was named Yi, who served as the magistrate of Deqing County with the title of Xuanjiao Lang. Just before he was to be promoted, he died. Soon after, he told his wife, “I am now a spirit and truly oversee all fish, birds, and beasts. And have them make a ceremonial cap and robe for me.” His wife followed his request, imitating the norms of the temple, decorating, setting up a ritual sacrifice, and burning offerings to present them. By the year Xin Si (1181 CE), it had been nearly forty years, and his spirit was so powerful. Yu’s great-nephew E carved a stone tablet to record the event. The Gecko Plague In the spring of the fourteenth year of the Chongxi era (1187 CE), the epidemic spread throughout Jianghuai and Zhejiang, but it was not very harmful. Only those who were affected felt headaches and body aches, but they recovered within three days. It was called the “gecko plague,” said to have come from the north of the Huai River. Zhao Shijin, Ming Shu, said that his ancestor Yanze, who was stationed in Yangzhou, was deciding a matter when a clerk fell ill, and soon after, many more fell ill, more than half of them. The next day, of the officials who went to court, only one in three remained. During the day, while entertaining guests, one of the entertainers was about to serve wine, but they also fell ill and left. By the end of the banquet, no one was left. Weng Lin, who was at the time the magistrate of Lizhui, suffered from the plague himself. Soon after, the entire county was affected. I was in the Hanlin Academy, and my eldest son came from the countryside to visit me. When we bought a boat in Qu, just as we were about to leave the shore, some of the people on the boat fell ill, and none were spared. But they recovered without medicine. It was said that this had never happened before.

### Translation: **Two Tigers of Shu and Liang** In the deep and remote valleys of Shu, fierce beasts gather in groups, and travelers dare not travel alone. Wanzhou is especially desolate, with almost no market stalls. The professor's official residence was isolated for a night. He once invited fellow officials to come at night and lit candles and lanterns to encourage drinking. Suddenly, a tiger leaped onto the steps, seeing the flickering firelight. All were startled and fled. One guest outside dared not enter and quickly hid behind a folding chair. The tiger gradually approached, and the guest had no way to defend himself. He lifted the chair to cover the tiger's head and pressed it down several times. The tiger struggled to resist, its head going deeper into the chair, like being shackled. Greatly embarrassed and frightened, the guest carried it out. The other guests dared not drink again and left. The next day, villagers entering the city said there was a folding chair shattered on the ground over thirty miles. The professor sent someone to fetch and examine it, finding it was the chair lost by the guest the previous night. The tiger had shaken it off along the way and escaped. The guest escaped being attacked but also suffered from a fear-induced illness, taking a month to recover. Near the outskirts of Xingyuan Prefecture, a farmer held a long knife to cut firewood, traveling a narrow path through a field. Below were muddy and swampy areas, about a dozen feet away. A tiger saw the farmer approaching and wanted to leap onto the shore. The farmer quickly jumped onto its back and slashed at it with the knife. The tiger also struggled and resisted. The villagers watched in awe and dared not help. They all went to the military commander to ask for help. The commander ordered a hundred mounted hunters to ride out with drums beating. When they arrived, both the man and the tiger were exhausted. The hunters stabbed the tiger and killed it, helping the farmer back. His body was torn and scarred because he had used the knife to the fullest and was also terrified. The next day, he also died. The commander generously gave the family money and grain to bury him. **Empty House in Jiankang** (Translation for the second part is not provided as it is not included in the source text.)

The Commander of Jiankang was hosting a gathering where guests were drinking and toasting. A close relative of his had traveled a long distance to visit, and he sent a messenger ahead to arrange accommodation. This person was usually proud and arrogant, particularly disrespectful of deities. The commander's children and followers wanted to put him in a difficult situation. There was a military camp that was considered extremely dangerous, so no one dared to live there. They led him to that place. Zhao entered and stayed in the main hall for the night. At midnight, he saw two torches inside the main gate, thinking they were servants who had not gone to bed. He called out to them. Soon the door opened, and an object two feet long and wide, holding a large fan, entered directly. Zhao intended to get out of bed to attack it. It was winter, and he had already taken off his clothes, fearing the cold and unable to get up. The object approached the bed, raised the fan, and with one sweep, Zhao felt a cold wind like a knife. He was terrified and trembling, worried it would do it again and did not have time to call out for help. The commander's banquet had ended, and he learned the reason, scolding Zhao's son, saying, "How could you treat human life as a game?" He immediately ordered Zhao to be brought back to the public camp, where there was a loud commotion. The ghost finally left. The next day, Zhao looked very weak and lost his former boldness. A year later, Zhao died. Li Yan, who knew about it, was in Wenzhou and told his nephew's son, Wing. --- Soldiers stationed at a relay station called Rao Fengpu in the area between Jin and Yang found the relay road desolate, with a relay station every ten miles. A soldier delivering a document was approaching the end of the day when a tiger came from the side with the intention to attack and devour. The soldier was extremely anxious and stood still, speaking to the tiger: "I have heard that you are also a spiritual being. The document I am delivering is a top-secret imperial decree for the Military Governor's Office. If you eat me, you cannot escape responsibility. How will you handle this imperial decree?" The tiger lowered its ears, bowed its head, and showed a listening posture before leaving. The soldier arrived at another relay station, handed over the document, and stayed overnight, telling the people there that he was glad to be reborn. The next day, when he returned to the previous place, he met the tiger again and was ultimately eaten. He realized that his fate was to die at the hands of the tiger, and his earlier escape was due to the imperial decree.

In the winter of the first year of the Chongxi era, my distant relative traveled from Poyang to Siming, passing through Wuzhou and Yiwu County. After traveling two stages, he stayed overnight in an inn. Suddenly, a man dressed in plain clothes sat beside me. When I asked who he was, he replied, "I am Mr. Sun Dao, a physician skilled in treating eye diseases." I spoke with him, and he said, "You must be a noble or wealthy family's son, who keeps good prescriptions; please give me one or two that might help others." I had always kept a secret prescription for treating gastric ailments and taught it to him orally. The method involves taking a large aconite root, removing the cap and hollowing it out, then placing 49 cloves of cassia inside and securing it with a string. Place the prepared aconite root in a silver or stone container and soak it in ginger juice until the cover is fully submerged. Cook it slowly over a low flame until dry, then roast a small amount and use it to rinse the mouth. If thirsty, eat porridge slowly and avoid greasy and cold foods. Mr. Sun was delighted and wrote it down on a bamboo slip. Not long after, the governor of the state was suffering from this illness and was in critical condition. Mr. Sun treated him using the same method, and after several doses, he recovered. The governor thanked him with 50,000 coins. Mr. Sun, being poor, received more than he had ever expected in his life. He did not mention the source but only spoke of the benefit, and he awaited my return for further requests. Suddenly, one night, he passed away. He was only fifty years old, full of vitality and strength, yet he suddenly passed away. Wealth cannot be obtained lightly.

### Translation of the Source Text **Haikou Town's Large Fish** One night, Wang Maotong, who supervised the tax collection at Haikou Town in Fuzhou's Fucheng County, was informed by a customs officer that a sea vessel had passed by the shore, carrying only one large fish. The fisherman was eager to sell the fish in the market and did not want to delay. He asked Wang to inspect and release the fish. Wang recognized it as a large fish and told the fisherman, "Wait until the taxes are collected, then buy the head for me." The fisherman replied, "I fear there might be too much meat to handle." Wang then instructed him to buy only the cheeks and gill meat, also mentioning that it was too much. Thus, only half of the fish was purchased. Soon after, four soldiers brought the fish, which weighed around seventy to eighty pounds. Wang and his household indulged in eating it, and the remaining part was made into dried meat and sent as a gift to the county officials. Estimating the total weight of the fish, it was roughly several thousand pounds. **A Woman on the Road in Qian County** Cheng Fa, a man from Fuliang, worked as an employee and frequently traveled between Jiangsu and Zhejiang. In the 14th year of the Chongxi era (1187 AD), he returned from Lin'an and passed through Qian County. Early in the morning, he met a woman on the road. Her appearance was sad and weary. She told Cheng, "I am unfortunate; my husband is cruel and abusive, often beating me. My mother-in-law is especially strict and violent, and I have never had a day of rest. Last night, she drove me out and left me with nowhere to stay. Can you take me along? I will marry you." Cheng replied, "I already have a wife who takes care of my mother. Why would I abandon her for another? Let's just walk together. People on the road will surely call us thieves or bandits, which is not safe." The woman said, "If that's the case, you can go ahead. I know this road well and will wait for you at your place of rest tonight." Cheng had no words to refuse and casually agreed, "Alright," and continued walking alone. By evening, he arrived at an inn, and the woman was already in the room. She strongly invited him to stay.

Cheng initially did not agree, but she became angry and said, "If you do not come with me, I will go to the city guard and tell them that you have kidnapped me and raped me." Cheng was very afraid and saw her appearance surpass his wife, so he agreed to her request. At dawn, she said again, "I will need to come to your house later. If you fear your wife won't allow it, find another place to live nearby, but you can still come over often." Before parting, she took out a patched coat from the handkerchief and gave it to Cheng. After returning home, Cheng did not tell his mother and wife. Five miles away from where he lived, there was a market called Guangping, fifteen miles from the city. Cheng went there every day. Suddenly, he fell ill and then told his mother what he saw. That woman just entered the room, inquired about each other, sat on the bed for a while, and finally left the house. His mother was shocked because she seemed almost without form or shadow. Until dawn, she died, and his mother dressed her in the patched coat and buried her. His wife later remarried and only had one daughter. When she married, Cheng's mother went to live with her, and the house was left empty. --- Cattle Trader from Huai West In the first year of Qianyuan in summer, in Taoshu Village in the northern part of Fuliang, many households bought a cow for a ritual and received one from a cattle trader from Huai West. The cow was extremely fat. After the offering was completed, they divided the meat and ate it. About seventy to eighty people, the meat had just gone down their throats, all suffered from abdominal pain and bloating, as if poisoned. It took three days to recover. Slowly investigating the cause, it turned out that the dishonest trader had killed a green bamboo snake, ground it into powder, mixed it with straw, and fed it to the cow. The cow chewed on it and found it delicious. Within ten days, its skin became twice as thick as before. The trader made more than half a profit and then led the remaining cows to sell in Zhe Dong. People were just discussing catching him and reporting his evil deeds, but heard that he had drowned at the previous ferry crossing, and so they let it go uninvestigated. This ghost got the chance to punish him.

Li Jinren, the Transport Commissioner from Hedong during the Zhenghe and Xuanhe periods of the Song Dynasty, was known for his upright and honest nature, though he had a very humorous personality. When he was in the capital, he reported and impeached officials who failed to report droughts in Tang, Deng, and Fang, as well as those who prohibited the people from making complaints. He would deliberately humiliate officials who were old, mediocre, and not respected by scholars. Once, Li Jinren served as the magistrate of Fuyang County in Cizhou. The governor of Cizhou was old and senile, and he was overly concerned with the formalities of his subordinates. When Li Jinren first paid his respects, he bowed deeply and spoke loudly, causing the governor to be surprised and step back. After Li Jinren left, the governor sent a messenger to scold him. The next day, Li Jinren returned, bowing humbly and remaining silent. The governor became even angrier and issued an official document to summon Li Jinren for questioning. Li Jinren wrote a detailed response, explaining that he considered himself a small and insignificant official who should treat superiors with propriety, so he performed a high bow. However, he was rebuked by the governor, so today he dare not speak. Unexpectedly, he received another reprimand, and he did not know whether to bow high or low. He humbly requested a single "yes, sir" to serve as a basis for action. The governor became even angrier but ultimately had no grounds to punish him. Wang Rusao, a junior official who served under Li Jinren, actually witnessed these events. The narrator heard Wang Rusao's account fifty years ago and noted that he could not remember the exact details of Li Jinren's time as an official.

### Translation of the Source Text **The Stone of the Drunken Fairy's Dancing Sleeves** Xu Xianzhi, a high-ranking official, was originally from Guixi, Xinzhou, and resided in Poyang. While serving as the governor of Dongping Prefecture, he acquired a peculiar stone that was three feet high and wide, resembling a painting of a fairy dancing and waving sleeves after being drunk, as depicted on a wall of a tavern. The stone raised its right foot, and he had it carried back and placed in the hall. The night watchmen often encountered a tall and handsome man who danced without stopping, and he was extremely strong and robust in appearance. Initially, they were afraid of him, but over time, they grew accustomed to his form and began to enjoy watching him. They would sometimes chase and strike him with sticks, but when they held a candle and examined the stone, they realized it was the stone. Xu Xianzhi then ordered the stone's leg to be broken off, and from then on, it could no longer be supernatural. At the beginning of the Shaoxing era, the house became the property of Chancellor Wang, who knew that the object was a monster and discarded it in the corner of the wall. I obtained it and brought it into the thatched hall for enjoyment, and it was very interesting to look at. **Yijian Zhi Volume Five** **Qinding Siku Quanshu** **Yijian Zhi Volume Six** **By Hong Mai** **The Daughter of the Xu Family from Shangrao** The Xu family from Shangrao had two daughters. The elder daughter married a scholar named Wang, who had a rather licentious nature. Because her husband was away, she would secretly have affairs with a young servant. She fell ill and the condition worsened day by day without recovery. She used a mirror. Her younger sister, who married the Yang family, often asked for it but was not given. At this point, she told her family, "I am ill and have no hope of survival; how can I be attached to a mirror? Auntie wants this object; you can take it to her to express my wish." After thinking about it for a long time, she indeed died. Her sister, who lived thirty miles away, came to mourn her and together they arranged the funeral and made preparations for Buddhist offerings. They stayed for several days. When leaving the sister's house, she recounted the deceased's words and handed over the mirror. Her sister cried bitterly and sobbed, and then took it back. When returning to the house, she took it.

When the sun had set and it was late in the evening, she suddenly applied makeup and ointment, opened the box, and changed into new clothes. Her appearance was pleasant and cheerful. People asked the reason, and she said her sister was calling her in the mirror. She had to follow her sister. Everyone was surprised and came to see, but initially they saw nothing. She then laughed and spoke to her sister, seemingly unaware of the situation, as if she were mad or crazy. She was dressed up as if she were mad or crazy, and shortly after, she felt sleepy and went to bed, dying soon after. It was in the first month of the fourth year of the Qingyuan era. After the funeral, a servant went to the tomb, as if someone was calling him. He circled the tomb many times, saying angrily, "Lady called me." He rushed to the front of the tomb, knelt down, and bowed twice, unable to stand up. Another servant helped him up, and he died. The scholar Zheng Zhe from the county said. --- ### Learning in the Zhengguo Temple A physician from Mingzhou named Yu Zhengchen told a story about a scholar from his hometown, Wang Mou, who, during the year of the imperial examinations, wanted to go to the mountains to study. He found the Zhengguo Temple, which was extremely secluded and free from the noise of carriages and horses. He went to the temple and borrowed a room from the monks to stay. The temple had only three or four monks. He often went to a house ten miles away during the day to recite scriptures and collect the dead. He stayed alone in the temple until midnight, when he extinguished the candle and was about to go to bed. He heard someone knocking on the door and invited him in. It turned out to be an old friend. Wang was very happy to see him and said, "It's fortunate to meet an old friend while I am alone in bed." He regretted that the monks had all gone out and there was no oil to light the lamp or boil tea, which was a great loss of hospitality. The guest thanked him and said, "There is no need for that. I should not have come out at night, and I have nowhere to stay. You can accommodate me." Wang kept him in the same bed and they talked for a long time. The guest smiled and said, "There is one thing I cannot avoid telling you the truth. Please don't be afraid. What is it?" Wang was startled and said, "If so, then I am talking to a ghost. How can I serve you?" The guest replied, "I have been dead for many years. I came tonight to ask for a favor."

No harm done, I am not doing anything strange or confusing, but I have a request for you. After my death, my wife will remarry, and my young son is weak and may not be able to support himself. During my lifetime, I earned 200 taels of silver, which I buried under the house at a certain place. I wish to tell my son to dig it up and use it to support himself, and do not let my former wife know. In the desolate wilderness, he should think of the reward. He bowed and left, and the king was relieved that he had gone. In the darkness, the king saw that the person was still in bed, tossing and turning, unable to sleep. Soon after, the king got up quickly and went out, just as the temple monk and the mourners arrived. They said that last night, they had just finished reciting the sutras and were about to wrap the body when they found only an empty quilt on the ground, and after searching everywhere, they could not find it. The king led them into the room and saw that the person on the bed was the dead man. The king was still anxious and hurriedly returned home to visit his friend's house and called his son. As expected, he found the silver. Recently, I heard Zhang Dingsou talk about the story of a mountain temple in Sheng County, which is somewhat similar. Is it not possible that the storyteller made a mistake about the county? However, the way it happened was completely different, so I am writing this again to broaden the account of strange events.

"It is harmless; I am not being strange or confusing, but I have a request of you. After my death, I shall remarry. My young child is timid and will have no means to survive. During my life, I earned two hundred taels of silver from the lodging house, which I buried beneath the house at a certain location. I wish you to tell my son to dig it up and use it to support himself. Do not let my former wife know. In the vast emptiness, he should remember to repay me." With these words, he bowed deeply and took his leave. The king was glad to see him go, but in the darkness, he secretly saw the man still lying on the bed, restless and unable to sleep. Soon after dawn, he hurriedly got up and went out. Just as the temple monks and the mourning family arrived, saying they had only just finished chanting the sutras the previous night and were now preparing to lift the body for burial, they found only an empty quilt on the ground. They searched everywhere but could not find the body. The king led them into the room and saw that the person on the bed was indeed the dead one. The king was still filled with anxiety and hurriedly took him back, then went to a friend’s house and called his son, who indeed found the silver as he had said. Recently I heard Zhang Dingshou speak of an incident at a mountain monastery in Sheng County, which is roughly similar. Could it not be that the storyteller made a mistake regarding the county or city? However, the way it came about was completely different, so I am writing this again to expand upon the account of strange occurrences."

Chen Liu, a local official from the eastern part of Weitang Town in Huzhou Prefecture, married a daughter of Zhu Jianshu, a fellow villager. He followed his father-in-law to Wenzhou, then returned home. He had a falling out with his brother and bought a house from the Sun family to live separately. There were many ominous and strange occurrences in that house, so the Sun family did not live there. When Chen entered the house, a hundred demons arose. Magic spells and taboos could not cure it. He called upon shamans and prepared sacrificial animals and wine to pray and offer thanks. Only then did the situation calm down. Chen favored a young concubine named An'an, who was just sixteen years old. Because of this, he often quarreled with his wife. In the season of summer in the year 1195, his wife died of illness. Two days later, the ironworker Shen Ershi, a neighbor, also died. Chen could not settle down and temporarily took refuge in the nearby village of Xu Village, which was thirty li away. An'an's mother, Qian Er's wife, did not hear the news of Zhu's death. She saw Shen Ershi arrive and said, "Lady Chen has called you to come and take An'an. If you do not, she will entrust her to the pawnbroker. I am now going to the state capital to handle affairs. You should go immediately." Qian Er's wife prepared food for Shen and fed him. She rented a small boat and went to Chen's former home, only to find that he had already moved away. Lady Chen and Shen Ershi were both dead. She returned in great fear. Chen then moved to Zhangjing Village and lived in the Sun family's house again, which was empty, and An'an remained unharmed. Now she is only one year old, and it is not yet certain whether she will live.

A Record of Amu's Revival In the early summer of the first year of the Gande era, Amu, the wife of Wang San from the Fengxian Township of Jiaxing, died and revived after a night. She said that while she was gravely ill, she saw two officials with documents pursuing her, saying, "We call you to account for something." I said, "Ordinary household matters are naturally handled by the husband and son; I have no involvement. Why are you pursuing me?" The officials said, "You must come with us." Without realizing it, she followed them out the door and walked down a path that was dark and gloomy, with no sunlight visible. They traveled about ten or so miles to an official residence where people were bustling and noisy. The two officials stopped her outside while they entered and wrote down a document. Then they drove her to the courtyard, where there were curtains hanging on all sides. Another official came forward and asked, "Why did you forcibly take property from the younger generation?" I replied, "I have never interfered in family matters. I have been eating vegetarian and reciting Buddha's name for over thirty years. All I remember is that my uncle Wang Da and my husband Wang San divided the ancestral property equally. After that, my uncle went to the Great Sage Temple and became a monk. One part of the farmland was sold by my uncle's son, Wang Ba, to the Sun Family of Weitang Town."

A Man Who Gambled Away His Fortune He gambled away his money unjustly and used it up, leaving only the foundation of his house. He then hired a tenant, and after many years, his elder brother died, and the younger brother, Wang Ba, went out and did not return. The husband, Wang San, burned and scattered the bones of his elder brother. By the time the house was sold back to Wang Ba, there were no other claims. The villagers heard rumors and forced Wang Da to appear in court. There stood a man wearing a wooden collar at the steps, who was the elder brother. He told me that he had been in the underworld for a long time and did not know the details of the world above, which is why he had two legal defenses. Now that it is clear, he no longer wants to fight. I asked him why he suffered so much. He said that when he was a monk, he begged alms from donors, took the money to build a bell tower, and secretly kept it for himself. He also took back the food offered as alms and returned home, which is why he still suffers punishment. After the judgment, I begged him to be released and said that it was impossible to return empty-handed. He was then beaten with a stick on his back three times, and upon the third strike, he woke up as if from a dream. His family members saw the marks on his back, which were clearly visible and extremely painful, and he took several days to recover. The people of the county, under the new magistrate of Guangde, Sun Han, recorded this event.

The Prince of Shao Prefecture in Chengdu The grandson of the Tibetan leader Gai, named Long Zha, returned to the capital during the Xining period and was given the surname Zhao and the given name Huai De.
