He was appointed as a military governor and enfeoffed as the Prince of Anhua. His grandson inherited the title and later entered Sichuan, serving as the military commissioner of the Chengdu region. Zhao Huai De was naturally outstanding and occupied the buildings of the four lecture halls of the Daci Temple, causing significant burden to the area. The monk Zhi Ze, who had a high level of moral and spiritual attainment, was the abbot of the Xi Tang hall at Baofu Temple and had a particularly close and good relationship with Zhao Huai De, meeting every day without fail. No one knew why they were so compatible. A wandering scholar skilled in divination discussed people's fortunes and misfortunes as if by divine insight and predicted that Mr. Zhao would die after his birthday in September. At that time, it was early spring, and Zhao was very worried. He invited Zhi Ze to discuss the matter and prayed, "Master, as an experienced monk, you view death as a dream. Can you take my place in death?" Zhi Ze smiled and said, "What's so difficult about that? But I have a request of my own. If you can listen to me, then it will be fine." Zhao asked what he wanted. Zhi Ze said, "The Prince has been occupying the temple for a long time, causing the halls and statues to fall into disrepair and the lectures to nearly cease. I really can't bear it. If you can find a way to move out and return the temple to the monks, I don't mind even if I die." Zhao agreed.

On the same day, Zhao built a new residence outside the city at Xiaodonglang while abandoning his old home. Seven days later, monks from four monasteries had all gathered, and all the monks in the temple prepared incense and candles to welcome the master. The master then had a meal at the Shuiluyuan Temple, after which he ascended to sit and expounded the teachings of Prajna, explaining the intention of substituting death. Zhao then took charge of the funeral arrangements, wearing mourning clothes and carrying the coffin to burn at the foot of the Great Wisdom Temple tower. That year, Zhao was unharmed. The following year, Cao Tingjian, the Military Governor of Shu, arrived in Shu. Zhao, who was arrogant and behaved as usual, rode in a sedan chair with attendants shouting, directly entering the military governor's office. Cao Tingjian was angry and said, "The role of the military governor is higher than that of the military officer; how can one violate military etiquette?" He immediately ordered the military governor to arrest and bring Zhao to the court. Zhao was then imprisoned in jail, and a notice was posted demanding the confession of past sins. Within a few days, officials and civilians began to argue, and it was discovered that Zhao had fraudulently requested grain and silk, and the amount of embezzlement was incalculable. Zhao and his two sons were all executed in prison. Only a year had passed since Zhao's death, when the elder monk Leixiang of An Guo Monastery, who was from Jia Prefecture, discussed this strange event. I said, "If death can be substituted, then subjects to their rulers and children to their fathers have been doing so for a long time. How could Zhao and Zhizhe's matters be so?"

Liu Guo, the Professor Liu Guo, courtesy name Gai Zhi, was from Xiangyang. Although he was a scholar, he was well-off financially and had a concubine whom he loved very much. In the year 1174 (Chunxi Jia Wu), he prepared to take the provincial examination. At the parting, he was deeply attached and reluctant to leave. On the journey, he composed a poem called "Water仙子." Every night after drinking at the inn, he would have a minor servant sing it. The words were as follows: Still drunk from the previous night's wine, my heart is already intoxicated. Looking back, thirty miles away. The horse just goes on flying. Riding one guard post after another, the mountains and rivers are killing me by their beauty. Indeed, the deep green mountains are delightful, but the separation of love is not yet resolved, and the path ahead is still unclear. The small bridge stops, and it is unclear whether to go or stay. Thinking of me and thinking of you. The poem is simple and shallow, but it conveys the meaning. When he reached Jianchang and the Mount Magu at dusk, he drank alone and sang this poem repeatedly, thinking deeply until he shed tears. After two watches, a beautiful woman suddenly appeared, holding a clapper, and said she wished to sing a song to encourage drinking. She then sang, saying: The wine has not yet been poured, but my heart is already drunk. How can I bear to listen to the words of the Yangguan, bidding farewell to my hometown. Whip the horse and urge it to the imperial city.

All three questions are appropriate, and I am here to assist you with them. Fate has caused me to bring you good news first, but I am unsure if you have heard about it yet. Pao-yang, widely knowledgeable in the sound of burning firewood, has a hidden meaning in his obscure phrase, "this is the only thing." Pour me a drink of wine, and I will continue in the style of the original rhyme. Liu was very happy with the Dragon Gate verse and immediately ordered it to be sung again, writing it down on paper and meeting with joy. However, he did not understand the meaning behind Pao-yang's obscure phrase, so he stayed with him and began to ask who he was. The response was, "I am the younger sister of the immortal Ma Gu. Because of Wáng Fāngpíng and Cài Jīng, I was exiled to live in this mountain and have not been able to return to the capital for a long time. I just heard that you have newly composed something elegant and have tried to fit the rhyme to promote yourself. From now on, I wish to accompany you in the future." Liu still refused with words, but deeply moved by emotion, the distant guest could not control himself and therefore accompanied him eastward, ordered to ride in a small carriage and looked at each other from a distance of a hundred steps. Upon entering the capital, they rented a secluded alley and lived together in a private room. Indeed, he passed the examination and was assigned to Jingmen as a teacher, returning home. Passing through Linjiang, he paid a visit to the Taoist of Geshan Mountain, Xióng Ruòshuǐ, and spoke to him.

A Man with a Lute Liu asked, "May I speak?" The man replied, "What could possibly be wrong with that?" Xiong said, "I am skilled in talismans, and I suspect that Miss Suichewangzi might not be human. I am unsure where she was obtained." Liu explained everything and said, "Yes, yes. Wait until tonight when we share a bed. I will perform rituals outside the door. Call the teacher to hold the person in the same bedding and prevent them from escaping." Liu followed the initial instructions and called a servant to enter with a lit candle. As he entered, he saw a person holding a lute. He suddenly realized the words of Cai Yong from the past. He tightly bound the person and placed them aside. By dawn, Liu personally carried the person for sleeping and eating. After visiting Magu and other Daoist practitioners, they said, "Recently, a military officer named Zhao passed by with an ancient lute. He valued it greatly. While playing it, it was accidentally dropped onto a stone and damaged beyond repair. It was then buried in the western part of the official enclosure. This is that lute." They quickly dug it up and found the box empty. Liu placed the lute in the box, ordered the Daoist practitioners to burn incense, recite scriptures, and weep while burning the lute, and wrote a short poem to express his feelings. I noted that Liu should be listed in the Zhan Sao list but is not recorded in the list of successful candidates. Ujiang Wei Zhai

### Final Translation Behind the administrative office of Wujian County, there was a fox den from which a minor demon often appeared. In the fourth year of the Chongning era, Wei Changxian served as the magistrate. One day, while sitting in the main office, his family members, who were in the curtain, saw a beautiful woman standing beside him and reported it to Wei's wife. At that time, the official servant Wang Daonu was known for his beauty and attracted the attention of the local officials. Wei's wife did not verify the truth of the matter and called Wang Daonu into the house, severely beating him with a stick. The next day, Wei went himself to the curtain and saw the same thing as before, but could not recognize her appearance. When they called Wang Daonu, he was indeed sick with sores and lying down. Wei then suspected that the fox from the den was causing harm and told Wei about it. Wei went to the backyard and found a hole in the bamboo forest. The entrance was only about a foot wide, and it was dark, deep, and could not be measured. He gathered Daoist priests to perform a ritual to drive away the evil and carried tiles and stones to carefully fill and seal it. It took a whole day of effort to seal it, and afterward, everything was peaceful and no more disturbances occurred. --- A Fairy Guest from Nanling Lin Sen, a scholar from Haojiang with the courtesy name Xiushi, studied hard and endured hardships, eagerly pursuing fame and success. He disliked the noise and bustle of the city and set up a room in the countryside. Every night, he studied until the third watch. One night, suddenly, someone called from below the window, and the voice was that of a woman. She called out, "True wealth and honor are truly hard to achieve. Studying at midnight, when have you ever slept?" The moonlight was bright and clean, with no servants around. Knowing it was unusual, he shouted, "Who are you, a ghost? Daring to come at midnight to play tricks on me." She laughed and said, "I am a fairy guest from Nanling. My father has ordered me to be your wife. To serve you, and my appearance is very beautiful." He opened the door to let her in and then scolded her. The woman said, "There's no need to doubt. I am the daughter of the magistrate from the south side of the village. My father has left no written records here. He admired your hard work and desire to succeed, so he ordered me to marry you. There is no one else in my family. To the point of a young lady offering herself, for this reason, I do not wish to come during the day. Therefore, I have brought a letter to show you, and it is indeed my father's handwriting."

Sen, a young man, met a woman and was pleased to have a match. He stayed with her overnight and left at dawn. From then on, he didn't care about day or night and didn't look out of the courtyard for months. Sen was like in a drunken or dream-like state and no longer cared about the reasons behind it. After a year, they had a son. Sen held the baby and said to the woman, "I am your husband, but I have never been to your home. I always feel uneasy. Why don't we go together?" The woman refused, and Sen began to suspect something. He remembered the story of Lu Chong's ghostly marriage and wondered if it was similar. He asked the neighbors about the magistrate's house, and they said it wasn't there. Instead, there was a woman buried on the southern hill for twenty years. Sen took the neighbor to the grave and saw a hole like a tunnel that went through the middle. He was afraid and returned home. The woman was still lying in bed. He shook her awake and asked about what he had seen. She remained silent and had a guilty look. She took the child and left. Sen bought wine to offer at the grave and sealed the hole with a stone. He cried and left, returning to the city the next day. From then on, he disappeared.

The Governor of Yongkang The Chongde Temple of Yongkang, originally a shrine to the deity of Gankou, was enfeoffed as the "Ba Zi Wang." Officials were appointed to oversee the temple, their status equated with that of the Five Great Mountains. The people of Shu treated the temple with great reverence. During festivals and seasonal events, as well as when people prayed due to specific needs, regardless of their wealth, they were required to slaughter a sheep. In a year, up to 40,000 sheep were slaughtered. For each sheep passing through the city, 500 coins were taxed, and the annual tax revenue could reach 400,000 strings of coins, providing infinite benefit to the state. On the deity's birthday, the people of the prefecture collectively raised funds to welcome the deity, showing utmost respect to officials, from high-ranking officials down to ordinary officials and commoners, all paying homage. In the first year of the Jingyuan era, Yang Guang of Hanjia was the governor of Yongkang. He alone refused to participate, as he was always stern and upright and did not believe in heterodox beliefs. The military office advised him to join, but he refused to listen and instead held a banquet for guests. That night, a fire broke out in the hall, spreading and being uncontrollable, completely destroying the military administration. Several days later, his family sent a servant to report that his residence also suffered a similar calamity, just as the same time. Yang Guang began to regret and fear, realizing that the deity was angry and punishing him, but it was too late.

**Translation:** **Ghost Crying in Chengdu** In the third year of the Shaoxi era (1192 AD), during the early night of April, there was a ghost crying outside the noon gate of the Chengdu Prefecture. The sound of sorrow and suppressed emotions could be heard far and wide, affecting several dozen people. This deeply alarmed everyone. By June, there was a sudden incident involving soldiers from Luzhou. The rebels were captured and executed at the place where the ghosts were crying. This was a sign of good or bad fortune, auspicious or ominous, as the gods or spirits had foreknowledge of it. Even if one wanted to escape, it was impossible. **The Dream of Shi Deyuan** Shi Deyuan, a person from Huzhou, was the great-great-grandson of Shi Daren, who served as a chancellor. In the fifth year of the Qindao era (1169 AD), he went to take the provincial examination. On the first day of the first month, he had a dream in which his father, the chancellor, came to him and said, "You will be the same age as me this year." Upon waking, he felt confused. His father died in the year Ren Shen of the Yuanyou era (1092 AD) at the age of seventy-eight. He did not understand the meaning of the dream. After the exam, he entered the Imperial Academy. He met a fellow student and asked, "How old is your father now?" The fellow student replied, "Seventy years old." Shi Deyuan casually mentioned his dream. The fellow student was pleased and said, "You will surely pass the imperial examination. Being seventy-eight means passing the provincial examination." Soon after, Shi Deyuan was indeed listed in the imperial register and ranked third in the third class of the imperial examination, under Zheng Qiao.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 6, Section 6 *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Yijian Zhi* Volume 7 Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Water Calamity of Zhang Yuanshan In the coastal area of Chuzhou, even the smaller tributaries and branches are deep and wide, and there is always the danger of turbulent waves for passing ships. One day, an old man living by the village shore had a dream in which a man in the water was holding a green dragon, about ten feet long, and seemed very frightened. The man called out to the old man near the door, saying, "I will repay you greatly." The old man replied, "That is not difficult." He reached out and pushed the dragon away, and the man was able to climb ashore. The old man woke up feeling strange and told his son about the dream. The next day at noon, a strong wind arose, and a violent tide surged like a mountain. A small boat broke apart in the waves, and someone was drowning. The person held a large green bamboo pole and called out for help. The old man went out to see, and it was exactly like what he had seen in his dream. He quickly rowed his fishing boat to rescue the person, who was unconscious and could not speak. The old man called his children to bring the person back to their home, where they lit a fire to dry his clothes and prepared food and drink. After a long time, the person regained consciousness and said, "I am Zhang Tiyun from Pucheng, Jianzhou. I was passing through Yongjia and unexpectedly encountered this disaster. Without the old man's kindness, I would have already been buried in the sea." He stayed for ten days before he could return to normal, and then he bowed and thanked the old man before leaving. The old man did not tell Zhang about his dream. Later, Zhang passed the imperial examinations and became a high-ranking official, sending someone to visit the old man's home with money and silk as a gift. Zhang then changed his surname back to Zhan and took the courtesy name Yuanshan, holding the position of a high official and governing Fujian. His future prospects were certainly not yet exhausted.

Four Ancestor Pagoda In Qizhou, the Four Ancestor Pagoda Mountain suffered from war and fire, and the temple was reduced to ruins. The monks rebuilt only three small rooms to house the Buddha statue. Scholars and officials who came to Huangmei had to take a detour to visit the site, but often the clouds and mist gathered all around, obscuring the view and preventing them from seeing the statue. Zhang Shou's son, Zhang Shoupeng, who served as a deputy magistrate, happened to be in the countryside inspecting the fields and stayed overnight at the side of the temple. The next morning, he accompanied the elder monk Zong Shao to climb the base of the pagoda. At first, the sky was still covered in dense clouds and fog, but soon it cleared up, allowing them to see the pagoda in a hazy outline. Elder Zong Shao mentioned that he had once dug down at the base and found a stone tablet, which was a land record by Guo Pu. It stated that when the pagoda would be destroyed, someone with the surname Zhang would come and rebuild it. Today, this prophecy has been fulfilled. Zhang Shoupeng exclaimed in surprise, "If so, we should try to draw it." Upon returning to the city, he informed others of the matter, and everyone was eager to contribute. Within a few days, they collected several million coins, and the construction was completed in half a year. At the beginning of the work, the workers were digging and uncovering the ground to a depth of several feet when they saw a monk with closed eyes sitting cross-legged, his nails protruding from his back. The abbot informed Master Zong Shao, who scolded him, saying, "Why do you monks often speak of ghosts and spirits? Do not talk about such things again." They then covered the monk with earth. Once the pagoda was completed, the monks admired Master Zong Shao's wisdom and capacity, considering him unmatched. Zhang Shoupeng's son, Zhang Zhen, personally witnessed these events. I believe that when Guo Jingchun was in Jiangnan, Buddhist teachings had not yet entered China, and it was unlikely that he would have written a land record for the Fourth Ancestor. Perhaps someone who knew the numbers (i.e., a fortune-teller) used his name.

**Guo Jieshi (郭节士)** In Fuliang County, the magistrate's residence had a pavilion built on the hall's columned corridor, consisting of three rooms, which was quite bright and clean, serving as a place for rest and relaxation. During the year 1137 of the Southern Song Dynasty, when Xue Jiyi, a magistrate from Yongjia, was in office, he was resting on a folding chair in the pavilion, feeling drowsy. In a dream-like state, he suddenly saw a man in red clothing standing before him. Startled, he asked, "Your attire is strange. Are you human or a ghost? Please tell me." The man replied, "I was once human, but now I am a ghost. Why do you ask?" The magistrate then asked, "Then why are you revealing your name? What do you want? What is it that you wish to complain about?" The man responded, "My name is Guo. Thirty years ago, I served as a magistrate in this county. Unfortunately, bandits invaded the area. I defended it steadfastly, refusing to leave, and fought with all my might against the enemy. My entire family was killed. After my death, the officials recorded my loyalty and bravery, and I was posthumously honored as a deity. However, my bones still lie buried in the garden behind the residence. I wish the magistrate would show compassion and bury me properly. This would be a great kindness." The magistrate said, "I am the magistrate of this county. How can I be addressed as a high-ranking official?" The man reassured him, "I heard that you would hold this position. I am not flattering you." The man then disappeared, and the sun had already set. The next day, the magistrate ordered the search for the man's remains and found them on the side of a flower trellis. They were then placed in a coffin and buried in the original location. The next magistrate also had a dream and did not speak of it to anyone. Instead, he requested that the previous magistrate's deeds be recorded, inscribed on a stele, and a statue be made in his honor at the Chongsheng Temple to commemorate his loyalty and sacrifice. This person is known as Guo Jieshi (郭节士). Later, Xue Jiyi indeed became a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.

**Translation:** **Jin Langzhong** The Jin family's genealogy records the story of Jin Junqing's marriage. When Jin had not yet passed the imperial examinations, he was studying in the mountains of Fuliang. One night, he did not sleep and heard a crowd of people moving outside, their voices loud and noisy. He went out to look, but the moonlight filled the courtyard, and he saw nothing. After a while, he heard the crowd return, and one person whispered, "Langzhong, you are not asleep. Please do not speak loudly." Then everything became quiet. The next day, he asked about it and learned that it was a neighboring household preparing a lavish banquet. At that time, the country was at peace, and official titles were highly esteemed. Jin, who had been appointed as a Zhenglang (a fifth-rank official), was very happy and said, "The spirits have informed me! My career in office is not yet complete, and passing the exams is not enough." Soon after, he passed the high-ranking examination, served as a county governor, and later as a regional official. He eventually reached the position of Duzhi Langzhong (a finance minister), with many friends in high places. He thought he had already climbed to the highest ranks, but he never progressed further and remained a Duzhi Langzhong. Today, he holds the title of Chaosandafu (a high-ranking civil servant). His fate was determined forty years ago, so how could he rashly seek further advancement? **Three Generals** In the western hills of Fuliang, there was a temple called Xin'an Si. A monk named Huizhao left his master, Haiming, and traveled to Jiang and Xiang provinces. He stayed for several years in Dazhong Mountain in Suizhou before returning home in the sixth year of Qian Dao (1167 CE). He brought several stone carvings and left one paper with the image of the Three Generals to the abbot, Rong Ji. The abbot intended to use this to seek donations and thus hired a woodcarver to carve the three divine figures, making their forms match the stone carving exactly. He then built a hall to house the statues and planned to apply colors and decorations. However, he could not afford the cost. A nearby villager named Liu Jiu's wife had suffered from a leg ailment for a long time and could hardly move. One night, she dreamed of a tall, white figure from head to toe, who said to her, "I know you have a problem. If you dedicate yourself to me, I will help you treat it."

The wife, upon waking, told her husband about her dream, suspecting it to be a supernatural phenomenon. They both went to the temple to invite monks to recite scriptures for spiritual protection. While passing by a new hall, they saw three statues. She pointed to one and said, "This is the one I dreamed of; it looks exactly like him. Could it be a sign of reverence?" They immediately took ten thousand coins to the temple to help with the painting. They did not mention what they had seen. Within ten days, the wife suddenly put down her walking stick and started walking again. After another ten days, she was completely recovered as if she had never been ill. She then went to the temple to burn incense and pay respects. The monks asked her why, and she finally told them the story. The news spread, and many people came to offer help. Allow Ji, who was skilled in medicine, could diagnose a person's fate by taking their pulse for three years. All the arrangements for this event were made by Ji, who received the offerings and alms. He is now deceased. His name was Lian Hui, and his grandson also has some of his talents. --- In the autumn of the third year of the Shaoxi era (1192 CE), after the results of the preliminary examination in Xinzhou were announced, the Deer Cry Banquet was to be held to celebrate the scholars who had passed. The prefectural governor, Wang Daofu, chose the 29th day of the ninth month to hold the banquet. Scholars from various counties arrived early. Yu, a scholar from Guixi, dreamed on the 26th night that someone told him, "I heard you are coming to attend the Deer Cry Banquet, but this event is no longer happening. Everyone else has left. You should return home early." Yu woke up feeling unhappy, thinking that the gathering for fame and fortune would surely not be beneficial and fearing that it might bring disaster to his family. The next day, a fire broke out in the market, burning hundreds of houses from noon until midnight. In the aftermath of the fire, both public and private affairs were in disarray. It took several days to clean up the ashes, and the banquet was ultimately canceled. Instead, money and wine were given as a farewell gift. At the time, Yu's eldest son, Zhang Zhenzhi, was the assistant governor of the state and was in charge of the military wine warehouse.

When the wife awoke, she told her husband, who suspected it was a supernatural occurrence. They then went together to a temple, intending to invite monks to recite scriptures in order to perform rites to ward off calamity. On the way, they passed a new hall and saw three statues. Pointing to one, she said, "This is the one who appeared in my dream—every feature is exactly like him. Could it be that he had a desire for spiritual cultivation?" Immediately, she took ten thousand coins and gave them to the temple to assist in the preparation of the ritual, though she did not mention what she had seen. Within ten days, the wife suddenly dropped her staff and began to walk. After another ten days, she was fully recovered, as though she had never been ill. She then went before the deity, burned incense, and offered thanks. The monk asked her why, and she finally told him what had happened. The story spread, and those who heard it competed to make offerings. Yun Ji was a skilled physician who could determine a person’s fate—life or death—by reading their pulse for three years. This matter was arranged by Yun Ji, and all the rewards and offerings were given in return for his services. He is now deceased. His name was Lian Hui, and his grandson, also named Lian Hui, is quite like his ancestor in talent and reputation. The Lu Ming Banquet In the third year of the Shaoxi era, during the autumn, after the local examination results were posted in Xinzhou, a Lu Ming banquet was to be held in honor of the candidates taking the examination. The prefect, Wang Daofu, chose the 29th day of the ninth month to hold the feast. Scholars from various districts had arrived in advance. On the 26th night, Mr. Yu from Guixi had a dream in which someone told him, "I hear you are coming to attend the Lu Ming banquet. However, this matter has already been canceled—everyone else has gone. You should return home early." Upon waking, Mr. Yu was unhappy and thought that this gathering for advancement would not be of any benefit, or perhaps it was a sign of misfortune for his family. He was deeply troubled. The next day, a fire broke out in the marketplace, burning hundreds of homes. It raged from noon until midnight before finally being extinguished. In the aftermath of the burning, both public and private affairs were in a state of distress. Clearing the ashes and debris took an entire day, and the banquet was ultimately canceled. Instead, only money and wine were given as a farewell gift. At that time, his eldest son, Zhang Zhenzhi, who was the deputy governor of the state and in charge of the military wine warehouse, was present.

Zhang Fang Two Wine Shops A man from Fuliang named Zhang Shining brewed five dou of white wine in the late winter month of Chunxi's Gui Mao year, intending to sell it during the New Year. On New Year's Eve, the wine was made and immediately drawn out. He then drew water and mixed it with the lees in the jar, planning to feed the pigs with it. Two days later, he entered his room and smelled a fragrant aroma. Upon inspecting the jar, he found that it had turned into another batch of fine wine, clear and spicy, even better than before. He also drew out this wine and filled the jar with water again. On the third day, he found yet another batch of wine as before. The neighbors were amazed and some believed he possessed magical powers. They debated with him, but he did not believe them. Eventually, they invited him to the brewing place, where they verified that it was not a lie. They spoke words of congratulations, saying that the Zhang family was blessed by the gods and would prosper. However, by late afternoon, when he went to check again, the jar was empty. In the western village of Cold Water, a commoner named Fang Jiu made one dou of wine and placed the jar beside his bed. He took it out whenever he needed it, and it never ran out for more than ten years. He earned money daily with no labor involved, relying on it to support his family. He did not know why this was happening. Later, he married off his eldest son. After ten days, a guest came and asked his wife to bring wine. She carried the jar out from the dark place and saw a small snake coiled beside it. When the snake saw people coming, it fled away. From then on, the jar was empty. Now, Fang Jiu has passed away, leaving only his descendants, who are now extremely poor and unable to recover.

A Silkworm Transformed into a Horse In the village of Runcheng, Yeguan, two families, Tan and Zeng, raised a hundred silkworms each year. In the first year of the Shaoxi era, during the month of April, the wife of one of the families got up at night to feed the leaves. She suddenly saw a silkworm in the cocoon that was much larger than the others, almost several times larger, and each section was a different color: green, red, black, and white, clearly distinct and not mixed. In the middle, it was like a piece of gold, transparent from front to back. His wife knew it was a good omen and took a fragrant box, held it up, and placed finely cut leaves on it, putting it in the Buddha hall. The next morning when she lifted the lid to look, it had already grown two ears. The next day it grew a tail, and soon all its feet appeared. Gradually, four feet appeared, and it could stand, completely resembling a horse. It would often jump and play for seventy days. The horse disappeared, but a small Buddha statue appeared, resembling Guanyin in meditation, with her head bowed and sitting cross-legged. People from outside came to see it, crowding in rows. Fearing something else might happen, the Tan family buried it together under a mulberry tree. That year, the amount of silk and cotton they got was double what they usually got. Even the small and cold silkworms were all successful. The same happened in the second and third years. But in the fourth year, the spring and summer of 1203, what they raised was still the same, and not a single cocoon was formed. The same happened in the years 1204 and 1205. A neighbor in the village, a girl who was a servant to Zhang Sishun, told this story, saying she had seen it herself.

The Water Essence of Ling Mountain The water essence, known for its beauty, is found in the mountains beneath Xinzhou. It is valued primarily for its size, and those with patterns resembling flowers and bamboo are especially prized. Zhu, a scholar from that area, once had a prosperous family, but over the past decade, their fortunes have gradually declined. During the Qingming Festival, he visited his ancestral graves, and while a group of common people searched the mountains for water essence and played a game of comparing different types of grass, Zhu walked alone through the hilly terrain. Suddenly, he saw a stone block radiating light that dazzled his eyes. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was a precious stone, as large and wide as a large jar. Overjoyed, he feared that others might see and take it, so he hid it among the leaves. After returning home, he called together twenty of his servants and, under the cover of night, carried the stone back. Soon after, merchants heard of it and came to view it, offering a price of six thousand guan. Zhu was still not satisfied. Craftsmen from the imperial gardens of Lin'an, upon hearing of it, requested permission from the eunuchs in the palace to travel to Xinzhou to inspect it. They set a price and increased it by another three thousand guan, which Zhu paid, allowing him to regain some financial stability. Sheng Shu, a man from Lishui, obtained two pieces of similar stones, each about four inches high and wide, with rows of green leaves that resembled the initial sprouting of daylily buds. He treasured them and only showed them to those who were interested and knowledgeable.

**Translation:** The Professor Guo A man named Guo from Chengdu, whose name is unknown, served as the overseer of the Xingzhou Grand Granary and was closely associated with Wu Ting, the military commander and Shao Bao. Once, a military courier offended Guo by requesting his monthly rations in a disrespectful manner. Guo reported this to Wu, who subsequently killed the courier. Several years later, Guo passed the imperial examination in the year 1163 (Gui Chou) and was appointed as a professor in Xingyuan Prefecture, but he had not yet taken up the post. Wang Yi, a fellow examinee from the same year and a magistrate in the same prefecture, had a dream in which he was pursued by several officials who urged him to leave very urgently. Wang Yi recognized that it was the defense office and refused to move forward. He prayed, saying, "I have an elderly mother; I do not know what crime I have committed. I hope you will clearly inform me, and then I will comply." One of the officials replied, "We are investigating the matter of the military officer in Xingzhou." Wang Yi responded, "I, a poor scholar, have obtained an official position, and I have never been outside the border. The person known as the military officer is someone I have never met. What exactly is the matter?" The others looked at each other in surprise and said, "Be careful, be careful." One of them said, "We almost made a mistake," and they immediately left. Wang Yi woke up feeling confused and unable to understand. A few days later, he heard that Mr. Guo had died. He finally learned the details of the incident in Xingzhou and spoke to others about it.

Feng Zizhou's Son-in-Law A man from Shu named Feng Zichun was the governor of Zizhou, and his son-in-law accompanied him to the post. Once, Feng Zichun needed a silver basin and sent an old soldier to carry it in. The son-in-law hid the basin and claimed it was lost, telling Feng Zichun that he had never used it. Feng Zichun thought the soldier had stolen it and put him in prison. The soldier, being old and unable to withstand the interrogation, confessed falsely. When they searched for the basin, the soldier said he had sold it long ago and spent the money. Feng Zichun then judged and sentenced him to corporal punishment and ordered him to pay the original value. The soldier, overwhelmed with grievance and anger, submitted a detailed statement to the East Peak Shrine, wept and bowed, then burned it. He tied a copy to his waist and hanged himself outside the temple gate. After Feng Zichun was replaced and took over the administration of Guozhou again, he suddenly saw this soldier standing beside him in broad daylight. Startled, he said, "You died a year ago, how can you be here?" The soldier replied, "The silver basin incident, I have appealed to the god of Mount Tai, who now comes to pursue the case with the governor's son-in-law." Feng Zichun was shocked and terrified. Soon after, the soldier disappeared. His son-in-law immediately fell ill as if struck by a sudden illness and died on that day. Seven days later, Feng Zichun also died. The magistrate of Fengzhou, De Guo, then in the year of Xingyuan Bimao (1195 AD), led the silk convoy from Tongchuan through Ezhou and met with Sun Ji, who told him about this incident.

"龙祠" means the Dragon Temple, a place of worship for the dragon deity. | | 绍熈五年春江西安抚司将官林应趾部豫章米纲往金陵抵芜湖内一舟最大所载千斛中夜忽漏作水如涌舟中之人惶窘无计 | In the spring of the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, Lin Yingzhi, an official of the Jiangxi Pacification Commission, led a convoy of rice from Nanchang to Nanjing. Upon arriving at Wuhu, one of the largest ships carrying a thousand hu of rice suddenly developed a leak in the middle of the night, with water surging like a fountain. The people on the boat were in a state of panic and had no solution. | "绍熈五年春" refers to the fifth year of the Shaoxi era (1194 AD). "江西安抚司" is the Jiangxi Pacification Commission. "豫章" is an ancient name for Nanchang. "金陵" is an ancient name for Nanjing. "千斛" is a unit of volume, equivalent to about 1,000 liters or 264 gallons. | | 林具衣冠谒龙祠拜祷曰应趾以贫为此役今若是将大有损失何力以偿势须徙出又非仓卒可办 | Lin prepared his ceremonial attire and went to the Dragon Temple to pray, saying, "Yingzhi, out of poverty, undertook this task. Now, if this continues, there will be a great loss. What power can repay this? It is necessary to move the cargo out, but it cannot be done hastily." | "具衣冠" means to prepare one's ceremonial attire. "谒龙祠" means to visit the Dragon Temple. "拜祷曰" means to pray, saying... | | 舟有七仓辄用甲乙次叙书七阄以卜所向愿大神威灵曲垂昭告遂得第三阄水及舟运而漏自止 | The ship has seven holds, and he used the order of the days to write seven lots to determine the direction. He wished the great spirit's divine power to show mercy and reveal the way. He drew the third lot, and the water level reached the ship, and the leak stopped. | "七仓" means seven holds. "甲乙次叙" refers to the order of the days. "阄" is a small slip of paper used in divination. | | 于是安寝至旦后三日晚至采石 | And so he slept peacefully until dawn. Three days later, he arrived at Caishi. | "安寝至旦" means to sleep peacefully until dawn. "采石" is a place in Anhui Province. | | 舟复漏乃集纲众如神告之证空第三仓见底板正脱一节一小鱼当漏处帖帖如遮防然已腐矣 | The ship leaked again, and he gathered the convoy to follow the divine instructions. They found that the third hold was empty, and the bottom board had a small piece of wood blocking the leak, which was rotten. | "集纲众" means to gather the convoy. "证空第三仓" means to find that the third hold was empty. "底板正脱一节一小鱼" means the bottom board had a small piece of wood blocking the leak. | | 葡者漏止正以鱼故神之赐祐大矣哉 | It was because of the fish that the leak stopped earlier. The divine blessing was truly great. | "盖前者漏止正以鱼故" means it was because of the fish that the leak stopped earlier. "神之赐祐大矣哉" means the divine blessing was truly great. |

**The Dragon Shrine of Wuhu** In the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, during spring, Lin Yingzhi, a military officer under the Jiangxi Regional Pacification Office, led a grain convoy from Yuzhang bound for Nanjing. The convoy reached Wuhu, and one of the boats—by far the largest—was carrying a thousand *hú* of grain. In the middle of the night, the boat suddenly began to leak as though water were gushing in. The people on board were filled with alarm and helplessness, having no idea what to do. Lin dressed in formal attire and went to the Dragon Shrine, where he prayed and said: "I, Yingzhi, have undertaken this mission out of poverty. If things continue as they are, there will be great loss. How can I make it up? It is necessary to move the grain out, but this cannot be done in haste. The boat has seven compartments; I have therefore written seven slips of paper, numbered in order from A to B, and drawn lots to decide which direction to move. I pray that the great deity will show mercy and reveal the way." He then drew the third slip. As the water reached the boat, the leak stopped. He then slept peacefully until dawn. Three days later, they arrived at Cai Shi, but the boat leaked again. He then gathered the convoy members and told them as if the deity had revealed it: the third compartment was empty, and the bottom planks had come loose at one section. A small fish lay flat at the location of the leak, as though blocking it. But the fish was already rotten. It was then realized that the earlier stoppage of the leak had been due precisely to the presence of the fish. How great was the divine blessing!

丁湜科名 丁晋公原本是吴地人，他的孙子迁居到建安，家产十分丰厚。家族中有个名叫湜的年轻人，少年时英俊洒脱，才华横溢。酷暑时节，他喜欢赌博，虽然有时能赢，但很快就输光了。他在闲暇时四处游玩，父亲多次教训责备他，但他丝毫没有悔改之心。父亲一怒之下将他囚禁在空房间里，断绝了他的饮食，使他饥饿困顿，几乎饿死。一位老妇人同情他，打破墙壁让他逃走。父亲见他逃走，也不再追究，只是认为他一定会自寻死路。丁湜向族人和亲戚借了路费，径直前往京城，在太学参加补试，被预选为贡生。熙宁九年，南方乡试上报了他的名字。在相国寺有一位以技艺闻名的相士，他的店铺像集市一样热闹，主要是举子们来询问考试的得失。丁湜去拜访这位相士，相士说：“你的气色极佳，我见过很多人，没有像你这样相貌出众的。你一定会在科举考试中取得优异成绩。”相士随即在纸上写下“今年状元是丁湜”，并贴在墙上。丁湜变得更加自负，依然保持着过去的爱好。同年榜上有两位蜀地的士子，他们也很富有，同样喜欢赌博。丁湜巧妙地应对了这一切。

**Ding Shi's Academic Achievement** Ding Jinguang was originally from Wu, but his grandson relocated to Jian'an, where he became wealthy and prosperous. Among his sons, there was one named Shi, who, as a young man, was handsome and sharp-witted, possessing great talent and ambition. During the intense summer heat, he indulged in gambling; although he sometimes won, he would immediately squander his winnings on pleasure and indulgence. His father repeatedly reprimanded him, but Shi showed no sign of reform. Enraged, his father locked him in an empty room, cutting off his food and drink, leaving him starving and nearly dying. An old woman, moved with pity, broke through the wall to let him escape. His father, glad that he had gone, did not inquire further, but remarked that he would surely end up dying in destitution. Shi borrowed money from his relatives, secured travel funds, and went directly to the capital to take the supplementary examination at the Imperial Academy, thereby being enrolled in the list of candidates for the imperial examination. In the ninth year of the Xining era, the names of successful candidates were announced at Xiangguo Temple. A fortune teller, famous for his skill, had a shop as busy as a market, where mostly candidates for the imperial examination came to consult about their prospects. Shi went to visit him. The fortune teller said, "Your appearance is excellent. I have examined many people, but none like you. You will surely be the top scorer." He immediately wrote on a piece of paper and stuck it on the wall, stating: "This year’s top scorer is Ding Shi." Shi became even more self-confident, yet his habits remained the same as before. At the same time, there were two scholars from Shu who were also wealthy and enjoyed gambling. Shi was evasive.

The Luring of Shen to the Wine Shop Shen was lured to a wine shop, where he ordered his servant to carry gambling tools and stand by the side. The people of Shu saw this and were pleased, so they played in a small pavilion. They agreed to a rate of ten thousand coins, and the game began with fierce determination. Each player could not stop, piling up their winnings as they advanced. Shen obtained a strange method in this art, and on that day, he won six million coins, taking the amount as promised and returning to his inn. Two days later, he returned to the fortune-teller, who was surprised and said, "Your appearance today is greatly different from before. How can you still hope for the top selection, which would mislead my art?" Shen asked for an explanation. The fortune-teller said, "In fortune-telling, one first observes the forehead. If the hair is yellow, bright, moist, and lustrous, it is auspicious. But now it is dry and black. Is it not because you have harbored evil intentions for the sake of profit, offending the gods and spirits?" Shen was startled and told the truth, saying, "Then, can I give it all back?" The fortune-teller replied, "Having already formed an intention, one can predict it. If you truly repent, you can still achieve the first rank below five others." Shen urgently sought the people of Shu to return most of what they had gained. When the court announced the names, Yu Diao was first, and Shen was sixth. His nephew's grandson, De Xing, and Wei Guangmin said.

**Translation:** Volume Seven of Yijian Zhi (Strange Tales) <Subcategory: Fiction, Strange Tales> The Complete Library of the Four Treasuries Volume Eight of Yijian Zhi By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Zhao San翁 Zhao San翁, whose given name was Jin and courtesy name Congxian, was from Baisha Town, Zhongmou County. Originally a soldier responsible for maintaining the Yellow River embankments, he fled his duties and went into hiding. He met Sun Simiao in a forest and was taught the essential principles of Taoism. After a long time, Sun Simiao left, instructing him not to leave the county limits and to stay in Chunze Village, saying, "Do not leave here unless summoned by the emperor. Wait for me to return, and we will go together." By the year Renyin of the Xuanhe era (1122 CE), Zhao San翁 was 108 years old and was indeed summoned to the court, where he was hosted at Baozhen Palace. Soon after, he requested to return home. The emperor asked what he desired, and Zhao replied, "I am originally a soldier and have no official document of release. I wish only for a grant from the emperor; I desire nothing else." On the same day, the emperor issued an order for the Prefect of Kaifeng, Sheng Zhang, to grant him what he asked for. Zhao then lived freely and unrestrainedly, possessing skills in all arts and being able to command spirits and know future events. He could heal diseases through breathing and massage, curing pains instantly. Bao Yi Lang Dun Gong Ru had suffered from a cold disease for two years, becoming emaciated. One day, while undergoing moxibustion, Zhao San翁 came and asked about the source of his illness. Dun Gong Ru told him the truth. Zhao instructed him to remove everything and, in the midst of the scorching summer, ordered him to lie on his back in a room with three windows opened to allow sunlight to shine down. He then placed moxa on his abdomen, covering it with about ten pieces, and heated it with the sunlight. After a while, the moxa penetrated his navel and abdomen, and he could no longer endure the heat.

*Strange Tales of Yijian*, Volume 7, Section Ding *<Philosophy and Miscellaneous, Fiction, Strange Accounts, Yijian Zhi>* The *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Strange Tales of Yijian*, Volume 8 By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Old Man Zhao San Old Man Zhao San, whose given name was Jin and courtesy name Congxian, was a native of Bashi Town, Zhongmu County. Originally a soldier in the Yellow River埽 force, he evaded his duties and fled. He encountered Sun Simiao in the forest of Hui, who taught him the essentials of Daoism. After some time, Sun departed, telling him not to leave the territory of Chunjie Village, saying, "Do not leave this place unless summoned by imperial decree. Wait for me to return, and I shall come with you." In the year Renyin of the Xuanhe era, he was one hundred and eight years old and was indeed summoned and housed at Baozhen Palace. Soon after, he requested to return home. When the emperor asked what he desired, he replied, "I was originally enlisted in the military register and have not received official clearance for discharge. I only wish to be granted such clearance; I desire nothing else." Immediately, an imperial decree was issued by Governor Sheng Zhang of Kaifeng, granting his request, and so he lived freely thereafter. He was skilled in every craft and could command ghosts and spirits, foreseeing future events. He would exhale and massage those afflicted with pain, and their ailments would be immediately cured. Officer Dun Ru of the Rank of Bao Yi had suffered from a cold illness for two years, becoming emaciated to the point of near starvation. One day, while undergoing moxibustion, Old Man Zhao San arrived and inquired about the source of his illness. Dun truthfully recounted his condition, and the old man ordered all treatments to be removed. At that time, it was the height of summer. He instructed Dun to go indoors, open three windows, and allow sunlight to shine down upon him. He had Dun lie on his back while he rubbed moxa over his entire abdomen, placing about a dozen pieces on top, and then exposed them to the blazing sunlight for some time. The heat penetrated his navel and abdomen, and the pain became unbearable.

A woman endured fasting, and her abdomen throbbed with a thunderous rumbling. Thick mugwort scent filled her mouth and nose, and then it ceased. The next day, she repeated the process, doing it like this for a month, and her illness was completely cured. She was instructed to continue for a full hundred and twenty days, and from then on, old illnesses were washed away, and she became strong and healthy like a young person. The old man said, "This is the secret of Master Sun." People only know about burning mugwort but do not know about preventing the root cause. They also do not consider the pain and the loss of strength. The sun's true fire, the warming effect of mugwort, covers the abdomen and slowly irradiates into it, which is a great benefit. However, June, July, and August are the best times; if it's autumn or winter, one should lay thick mugwort on the abdomen and use a hot iron wrapped in cotton to slowly warm it until the smell of thick mugwort is detected, which is the next best option. The method is strange but reasonable, all like this. A friend named Xi Dongyun from the mountain of Duanmen in Mian County went to the side of the waterfall at Duzong Ridge, admired its clear and serene beauty, and built a house to live there. Soon after, all kinds of misfortunes gathered, and within a year, disasters followed one after another. Xi visited the old man and told him the reason. The old man said, "Is it not because you live in a place called Five Arrows?" Xi said, "There are many theories about geography, but I have never heard of Five Arrows. May I ask what it means?" The old man said, "It refers to the peaks, ridges, and slopes of the mountains."

The entrance of a bag facing the wind, as fast as a dart, is called a wind arrow; steep rapids, swift currents, hanging springs, and waterfalls, the sound of sand hitting rocks like thunder, day and night without ceasing, is called a water arrow; hard and dry soil, barren banks, sandy deserts, no grass or trees, no moist springs, hard iron and smelly tin, toxic insects and ants gather and disperse like ruined land, is called an earth arrow; layered cliffs, overlapping peaks, steep walls, sharp rocks, pointed peaks, sharp blades, protruding teeth, exposed bones, resembling a pagoda, is called a stone arrow; tall trees, ancient woods, dense shade, thickets, blocking the sky and sun, hanging vines, dark and solemn like a graveyard, is called a wood arrow. The five types of arrows, places where they strike and injure residents, all are unusable. Winding and embracing, clear and deep atmosphere, wide and grand terrain, fertile soil, sweet springs, clear stones, these are the best lands. One does not need to rigidly follow the stars and divination. If you follow my advice, avoid evil and seek good, you will be safe. Xiji followed his teachings and lived in peace, but the old man's fate is unknown. On the peak, Zhang Shoupeng's father wrote an inscription, and Guo Baoxiang obtained the text and recorded it at the end of *Kui Che Zhi*. I wish to expand its transmission and record it here again.

A Scholar from Jianchang A scholar from Jianchang, whose name is unknown, went to Lin'an for the provincial examination. In his dream, he entered a shrine just as an official was holding a large register on the podium, about to ascend the hall. A jar containing water was accidentally knocked over and fell to the ground, making a clear and resonant sound. He immediately woke up, considering it an inauspicious omen and feeling very unhappy. Later, he was selected in the spring list for the highest rank and appointed as the chief clerk of Leping. Then he realized the meaning of the register and the fallen jar. A Military Official from Tanzhou A military official from Tanzhou, originally from the Tianwu imperial guard, left the military register and obtained an official position. After serving his full term, the governor sent him to escort ten thousand stones of rice to Ezhu. He took his family along the way, passing through the Qingcao and Dongting lakes. At the boat dragon temple, he should have prepared sacrificial offerings to pay respects. The person there was usually strong and stubborn, and afraid of the cost, offered meager sacrifices. The priest said, "The spirit is quite displeased. One should be cautious and respectful in every matter." He did not think so. On a bright moonlit summer night, he sat on the boat’s prow and looked at the large golden sand beach, the light was like scattered stars, shining brightly, gathered and dispersed, slightly forming five colors, dazzling and swirling across the river. He asked the boatman, "Are these spirits all out playing?" The man laughed and said, "These are just ghost fires. What spirit?"

### Final Translation A Man Who Shot Bullets at the Wind God Statue This person was skilled in using a slingshot, with every shot hitting the target. One day, he shot a bullet, and it suddenly disappeared. The boatman secretly worried about this. The next morning, he went to the temple to examine the wind god statue, which had moved from its original place and stood to one side, with cracks all over its body. The boatman reported to the supervisor the previous night, saying the bullet was still in the bag, and the supervisor apologized, bowed twice, and then left. By evening, the wind damaged one of the boats, causing the loss of several hundred dou of rice. The narrator barely had enough to cover the cost, having depleted two years' salary. Disrespecting the deity brought about these consequences. Despite being frightened, the narrator was still fortunate not to have drowned in the river. Generally speaking, spirits and ghosts are often frightened, especially by bullets. Jing Pei heard this story from Qian Bu Gu and forgot the name of the supervisor. ### Translation of the Second Passage Fan Dou Nan's Concubine Fan Dou Nan, whose courtesy name was Yi Qing, was from Ou Ning. In the second year of the Chunxi era, he passed the imperial examination and was appointed as a professor in a certain state. He bought a concubine and favored her, but his wife, You, did not allow it. He then lied to her, saying, "Next year, we will travel to our official post. The expenses for our journey are very limited. Now, the Zhao family from Pucheng has sent a servant with a letter, offering to give us thirty thousand coins a month in exchange for us staying as guests at their inn. We cannot miss this opportunity." Thus, he took his concubine with him. When they arrived at the county, they found a village near a mountain temple, where a temple room had been slightly repaired and used as a temporary residence. When they were about to move in, the concubine hesitated at the door and refused to enter. He asked her what she saw. She said, "There is someone in the room. I am afraid to enter." Fan said, "This is an empty room. How could there be anyone? Are you not seeing things due to some trickery?" He forced her to enter. Not long after, the concubine fell ill and died. After removing the bedding, they found that the wall behind had turned completely dark, and a woman dressed in a brocade quilt was lying in the room, just like the concubine. Fan realized that what he had seen earlier was indeed this woman. Fan was greatly saddened by this.

Fan Dounan was displeased and unhappy, and then he was afflicted by illness and vomiting, with the situation being critical and urgent. A friend named Zha Zhengtong came to inquire about his illness and said, "Three miles ahead in the village, there is a fisherman who keeps medicine that can revive the dead, but I worry that it might not be available. We must pray to the gods and heaven for it." So he lit incense and prayed aloud, saying, "If Fan Dounan's future prospects are not yet exhausted, I wish to obtain the fisherman's medicine. If that is not the case, then fate has its limits and it cannot be extended by human effort. I dare not refuse." So he went to visit him and happened to meet this old man, who told him the reason. The old man said, "The medicine is very difficult to prepare and not commonly used. This morning, there was still one pill left in the box, but someone had taken half of it. So I gave the remaining half to him and told him to take it immediately." Zha carried it back and prepared a decoction for Fan, and the phlegm was expelled as if swept away. The next day, he recovered. Zha is a talented and generous gentleman, thus he is so loyal to his friends in terms of righteousness. Po Yang Zhang Qi and Dong Nanyi are fellow jinshi with Fan, and they said they personally saw Fan telling others, "With my concubine traveling with me and my wife coming from the countryside, the story is different."

Chen Yaozi's Dream The deity worshipped at Liyue Temple in the east of Jianning is Tang dynasty governor Li Pin, known for his manifestly miraculous powers. Every year during the imperial examinations, scholars flock to the temple to pray, and some even stay overnight to seek dreams, all of which prove to be accurate. Pu County is about 300 li from the city, and a scholar named Chen Yaozi from the county was poor and reluctant to bear the expenses required to participate in the examinations. He said, "Only sincerity can move heaven and earth and affect the spirits. There must be a temple dedicated to divine learning here. I will bring incense and paper to pay my respects and surely receive a great response." That night, he stayed in the temple and had a dream. A ghost with one foot off the ground jumped around several times and said as it moved, "With an official position, you will have a wife; with a wife, you will have money; with money, you will have land." After waking up, Chen Yaozi told his friends and decided to go to the city. The story spread widely in the village, and some people laughed at it. Indeed, the results of the provincial examinations were posted, and Chen Yaozi was selected in the first round and passed the examination on his first attempt. Supernatural Disturbances in Longxi County Wen Deguang, courtesy name Zhongshi, was from Jian'an and passed the imperial examinations as a top-ranked candidate in the Qian Dao era of the Song dynasty. He later served as the governor of Zhangzhou and Longxi County. The official residence in Longxi was often haunted. In the following year, Wen Deguang's daughter was about to look in the mirror when she saw a woman in yellow standing beside her, seeming like a dream. She thought the woman was her father's concubine and called her three times without response. Soon after, another woman entered and stayed inside for a long time. The daughter did not realize anything was unusual. When Wen Deguang returned from outside and called for his concubine, she was nowhere to be seen. He only heard loud noises coming from inside the room. He rushed in and found his daughter had vomited blood and fallen, with mud blocking her ears and nose. Despite his efforts to lift her up, she could not be saved.

### Final Translation **Counselor Cao's Concubine** Counselor Cao, the superintendent of tea affairs in Fujian, had his residence in Jianning. One of his concubines saw a woman dressed in a dry red shirt in her bedroom, holding a cup of soup for her. Upon closer inspection, she realized it was the blood she had given birth to, which she spat out and refused to accept. For several days, she persistently pleaded, but the concubine thought she could not avoid it and accepted it to eat. She died within a few days. **Inspector Liu** Inspector Liu Daliang was appointed as a deputy governor of Jiankang Prefecture in the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, after serving as a deputy chief of the Imperial Works Department. He was assigned an additional post due to excess personnel without official quarters and lodged in the residence of Yang and Wang. Soon after, he was troubled by evil spirits. Although there were no visible ghosts, the room felt like a cage. One day, during the daytime, he walked out of the hall as if held by someone. Realizing it was strange, he reported to the prefecture and moved to another place. Soon after, his wife died. The following year, he was accused of supervising the autumn examination by the two sons of his colleague Wang Wanshu and was dismissed from office and returned home. **Wang the Pot Maker and the Strange Story of the Louse** Wang Liu, a resident of Songyang in Quzhou, made a living by binding and sealing pots and steamers. He went to Jinyun to repair pots for the Zhou family. While working, he felt very itchy in his waist and picked up a louse that was playfully drilling a hole in the pot. He put the louse inside, sealed it with a piece of wood, and left. After a year, he went back to the Zhou family in Jinyun. Suddenly remembering the previous play, he opened the hole and looked inside. The louse was still alive and wriggling. Wang the craftsman was surprised by this and picked it up and placed it in his palm, saying a prayer: "Thinking of how you have been starving for so long, now I will give you a full meal." The louse immediately bit into his palm, causing a small amount of blood to flow. The itching became unbearable, and a wound formed. Over time, the wound penetrated to the back of his hand. No medicine could heal it, and he eventually died. **Wang Qi, the Sixth Patriarch of the Shaolin Temple** This entry seems to be incomplete or missing context, so it cannot be fully translated.

A Merchant from Lishui named Wang Si Liu often traded cloth and silk between Quzhou and Jinhua. In the fourth year of the Shaoxi era (1193 AD), he arrived in Quzhou and visited the home of Zhao San, a market leader. Wang carried 300 strings of cash, but Zhao used up all the money. Wang stayed for a long time trying to get his money back, but Zhao evaded and delayed, using evasive language. One night, Zhao got Wang drunk with wine and then choked him to death. He put the body in a basket and hid it. Wang regularly worshipped the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara and, even when traveling, carried a painting of the deity. He worshipped daily with incense and offerings. Zhao feared that the painting and other items would incriminate him, so he bundled the painting, a clean vase, incense burner, and other items into the basket with the body. He waited until midnight when everyone was asleep and intended to throw the basket into a deep pool. As he was about to leave the house, several monks came one after another. Zhao feared they would see him and stopped for a moment. After a long while, he tried to leave again but met more monks. This happened several times, and by dawn, he had no time to think and carried the basket to the riverside. A neighboring butcher named Jiang Yi was surprised by the disturbance and took Zhao's hand to look. He couldn't prevent it and told the truth. Zhao bribed him with five paper notes, but Jiang didn't listen and said he would report Zhao to the authorities. Zhao pleaded and added ten more notes. Jiang was pleased and let Zhao go. That day, Zhao didn't buy pigs and returned home, exchanging the notes for money. His wife suspected him and said, "If you have nothing to do and return early, why do you have money? You must be a thief." Zhao explained the reason, but his wife said, "If this involves a human life, and it is exposed, we will face a serious lawsuit. If you go to jail, how will our family survive? We should report this to the authorities." Jiang hesitated but his wife shouted loudly, so he went to report to the local officials. They found Wang's body in the water, and the painting beside the body had writing: "A follower of Buddhism, Wang Ji from Lishui, Zhejiang, donated money to paint a picture of Avalokiteshvara. The officials of Xi'an County confirmed the truth." Zhao admitted guilt, and it was verified that the monks' appearances were due to the miraculous transformation of Avalokiteshvara. However, it would have been better to save his life. Judge of West Lake

**Translation:** Dì, a third-ranking general of the imperial guard, on the sixth day of the second month in the third year of the Shaoxi era, commanded the troops of various camps to receive their pay at the fifth watch. He sat on a trundle bed outside the front lake gate, waiting for the gate to open. He felt a hard object touch his foot and took a candle to look at it. It was a giant crab, three feet long, with an unusual and ugly appearance. He ordered the soldiers to capture and bind it and send it home. He sat again and took a brief nap, dreaming of a man with a long beard and an ancient, evil appearance, wearing a light green robe, soft hat, black boots, and a black rhinoceros belt. The man held a tablet and bowed, saying, "I am the official in charge of the West Lake. Because I was playing in the fields, I was caught by you and fear I will be boiled alive. I wish to send a messenger to inform you so that I can preserve my life and plan to repay you generously. If it cannot be avoided, my small life is not worth worrying about, but I am afraid it will bring disaster to your household." Dì woke up and found the gate already open. The crowd entered the city in order, and he did not have time to ask about the dream. After finishing his duties, he rode back home on horseback. His sons had already cooked and eaten the crab, surprised by its sweetness and freshness. His wife had not touched it. Dì told her about the dream and asked her not to eat it. Soon after, all five of his sons fell ill and died one after another, leaving only Dì and his wife. --- **周氏买花** In Lin'an, near Fengle Bridge, there was a flower shop run by the Wu family. Their daughter was very beautiful. One day, she heard the sound of flower sellers in the market and went out to look. The flowers were unusually fresh, beautiful, and attractive. She bought them all and placed them around the house, admiring them as she came and went. She could not take her eyes off them, as if she were in a trance. She slept during the day and did not wake up until evening, and stayed up all night, forgetting to sleep. Every evening, she would wash her face, put on makeup again, and change into a new outfit. At midnight, she whispered as if talking to someone. Her parents considered it a worry and secretly invited a practitioner of magic to come. The girl did not show any emotion and was not afraid at all. A man named Yu San, who sold flour, lived near the tide gate. Zhou met him by chance, and Yu San asked her, "Someone said that your family has a demon that cannot be treated."

Indeed, Zhou said, "I am greatly troubled by it and have no way to deal with it." He then told Wu the reason. Wu said, "This is a cat demon. Tomorrow I will perform a ritual to exorcise it." On the appointed day, Zhou prepared wine, food, incense, and paper offerings, and invited Wu. Wu invoked spirits through specific movements and breathing techniques. Soon, the girl was terrified. Wu swung his ritual sword and cut off its head. The girl did not realize what happened and went into her room to sleep. After sleeping for a few moments, she woke up feeling refreshed. When asked about what she had seen, she said, "Just after dusk, a young man with an extraordinary appearance came, wearing clothes and riding a horse. Two red candles led the way, followed by flutes and pipes. All food and drink required were prepared immediately upon request. Singing, reciting, laughing, and talking were no different from those of ordinary people. Now, all of that has disappeared." After several weeks, the girl fell ill as if she were pregnant. Zhou again summoned Wu to write a talisman for her to swallow. From then on, everything returned to normal. The Temple of Taifu Tao in Nankang was originally the temple of General Tao Kan of the Jin Dynasty, long known for its spiritual power. The local people treated it with great respect. Since the Song Dynasty, it has not been as well maintained as before. The incense and sacrificial offerings have gradually declined, and the buildings have fallen into disrepair. Passing travelers never paid their respects, and animal sacrifices and wine have almost disappeared. In the early years of the Chongning era of the Song Dynasty, a villager named Tong Ba, who was always bold and lawless, destroyed the temple to expand his home. But he built a small hall in the corner of his house to continue the worship. Soon his wife fell ill and went to a fortune-teller for divination. The fortune-teller said, "She offended the son of General Tao, which caused the disaster." Tong Ba said, "The spirit of General Tao is no longer active; how much more so his son?" The fortune-teller replied, "General Tao has been in a state of spiritual decline for three decades, so it has been silent for so long. Now it still has half a decade left, after which it will revive. You will not bring disaster to your family." Tong Ba stopped, and later, the spiritual response was indeed double what it used to be.

Chou Jun's Household On the twenty-sixth day of the sixth month in the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, the birthday of the city god of Poyang was celebrated. Many men and women gathered at the temple to offer sacrifices, and a Taoist priest was ordered to perform a ritual. The guest, Chou Jun, was appointed to oversee the event. Chou's wife was at home, and while using the toilet, she encountered a beautiful woman dressed elaborately who entered through the back door, jumped over the fence, and terrified her. She ran back to her room. The woman followed, folding her sleeves and sitting down facing Chou's wife. Chou's wife was already in a state of confusion but managed to send her son to call Chou back home. The next day, Chou went to the government office, and the woman appeared again, accompanied by three servants. They shouted, jumped, and took items from the hall, throwing and breaking them. Chou's wife became ill from this, showing signs of confusion and speaking incoherently, as if possessed by a ghost. She died three days later. After her burial, the strange occurrences continued. Chou fell ill suddenly and died within a few days. The *Yijian Zhi* (A Record of Unusual Events), Volume Eight, compiled by Hong Mai of the Song dynasty.

戚彦广之女 戚彦广本是霸州寨兵之家子，至彦广时，他粗略读书，尤其精通法律，舍弃了父祖的传统，致力于农耕和粮食种植，居住在文人聚集的东边，在野外探望在滨州蒲台丁河上的亲戚，停留了很长时间。他的长女苏娘因小病在家，戚彦广忽然看到几个人捧着一位美丽的女子进入家中，女子在前拜见，原来是苏娘。戚彦广问她为何而来，女子说收到父亲的信，说父亲病重，母亲忧心忡忡，无法言说，兄弟们都不肯来，让她去探望。戚彦广说：“我原没有病，何曾发信？”女子探怀取出信，果然是父亲的笔迹。戚彦广对此感到非常奇怪，将女子安置在房内，另设一榻，等到天亮，榻上空无一人，戚彦广更加惊恐，当天便兼程赶回家。苏娘正因病卧床不起，询问往事，她却一无所知。从此家中多怪事，女子似乎被妖物所凭，有时盛装打扮，有时高谈阔论，有时狂吟啸歌。戚彦广请来里巫范道钦，备酒设宴，祈求禳谢，女子欣然而出，与范道钦对席而坐，笑语自如。范道钦自度无法治，请求设醮筵以祷，道士到来，方执炉行道，青词简录皆遭窃去，众人惭愧恐惧而散。荏苒数年，戚彦广为人颇刚直，置之不理。一日，女子复涂泽扬袂，坐于堂上，召戚彦广告之曰：“君识我乎？我本海神侍妾，获罪于王妃，屡遭鞭挞，所以寄身于君家。近日怒已息，命我来归，混君家许时，从此话别，他日当致微报矣。”言讫，一揖如房，女子恍如梦初觉，故疾亦愈。后数年，戚彦广致仕，到海滨遇一妇人，自称神之妾，以银百两为赠。

During the Shaoxi Jiazi Year, in the regions of Henan, Pizhou, and Xuzhou, there were many people using heterodox teachings to deceive the masses, with Chen Jingbao being their leader. The Jin Dynasty set up a very strict reward to capture him. A woodcutter from Xianyang named Cai Wu was gathering firewood in the wild, feeling tired, hungry, and in need, unable to provide for his own clothing and food. He often sighed and lamented on the road, saying, "If I could catch Chen Jingbao, I would have an official position and money, and I would be a happy man. But now I cannot even survive, what can I do?" Continuously thinking without ceasing, he encountered a white-clothed person carrying a burden, who followed him and called out, "Cai Wu, do you recognize that person?" Cai Wu replied, "I do not recognize him." The white-clothed person said, "If you do not recognize him, how can you catch him? I do recognize him and know where he is, but I alone cannot defeat him." Cai Wu was greatly surprised and put down his burden to ask the white-clothed person. The person took a mat and laid it beside a ruined wall, sat down, and discussed together how to track and capture Chen Jingbao. After a short while, he turned to the east of the road, turned back, and shouted loudly at Cai Wu. Cai Wu was carried up and into the sky by the mat, flying upward through the sky, and flew eight hundred miles before falling in the courtyard of Yidu Prefecture. The prefect was shocked and called it a great monster, ordering the executioners to bind and imprison him. They thoroughly investigated the matter, but Cai Wu could not find words to explain, only saying that he was in Xianyang, chopping wood, and suddenly found himself flying away, which was indeed a great injustice. The prefect sent a document to Xianyang to investigate his neighbors, verifying that he was a commoner, and he was finally released. However, Chen Jingbao had fled, and it is suspected that the white-clothed person was him.

A Girl from the Zhou Family of Fangcheng In Fangcheng, there lived a man named Zhou Si who resided in a place called Menglong, to the north of Sheyang Lake. The area was surrounded by marshes and swamps, with no arable land for farming. Zhou Si was a poor and unambitious man, making a living by cutting and weaving reeds into mats. He had a daughter who was around seventeen or eighteen years old, who was unfamiliar with sewing or needlework but could assist her father in weaving reeds. There was a man named Liu Yuan from the Shenyu堰, who married her to his son. However, the girl was unable to sew clothes, and Liu Yuan sent her back to her parents. After her parents died, she had no means to support herself and began begging in the marketplace. Zhurong, who lived near the Shenyu堰, often called her into his home to do chores like fetching firewood and water. Over time, he wanted to arrange a marriage for her. Wu Gongzuo, a man from Chu, was once a wealthy and reckless youth who had left his family and lived as a wandering monk in a monastery. Later, he returned to his hometown, where he was tolerated by his relatives and classmates. They would often tease him and suggest that he take the Zhou girl as a wife. They borrowed clothes for her and prepared wine and roasted meat, renting a thatched hut and choosing a day to arrange the marriage. All their friends and peers gathered, but it was just for a laugh. They knew she was a beggar and would surely despise her, but to their surprise, they got along very well. One day, Wu Gongzuo met the son of Guo Ming, who was wealthy. The son invited Wu to gamble with him. Wu had only a thousand coins but continuously won by throwing dice, winning six hundred strings of cash in one night. Guo Ming could not bear it and fought Wu again the next day. Within twelve days, Wu won ten times more. From then on, Wu opened a pawn shop and took out loans, making profits in the tens of thousands within a few years. His father called him back home to study, and Wu participated twice in the imperial examination. The Zhou girl was intelligent and quick-witted, able to do women's work without learning it. Her skin was smooth and beautiful, and she suddenly became beautiful. In the village, there was an elderly man named Yan who was skilled in explaining the *Classic of Filial Piety*. He could recognize Zhou among the beggars and told others, "This girl is destined for greatness." Indeed, if she had been in Liu Yuan's home, she would have been like this, but instead, she suffered from hunger and cold all her life.

Dan Zhiyuan was a man from Hezhou, residing to the south of Tongguan, who had inherited and managed a family business. His family was somewhat wealthy, and Dan Zhiyuan was simple, ancient, and calm in demeanor, with a particular fondness for longevity techniques. Whenever Daoist masters came, regardless of their reputation, he welcomed them all. During the Zhenglong period of the Jin Dynasty, there was a man named Qiu Dechang, who claimed to be over seventy years old, though he appeared to be in his thirties or forties, with a dignified and spirited appearance. Qiu Dechang was skilled in discussing Daoist philosophy and practiced breath control techniques. Dan Zhiyuan was overjoyed beyond expectation and treated him with the respect due to a teacher, keeping every promise. One night, Qiu Dechang spoke calmly: "Who among us lacks a Daoist heart? It is generally corrupted by desires. Now, if you seek to prolong life and achieve longevity, if you do not cut off this desire, few can see it through to the end." Dan Zhiyuan burned incense, knelt twice, and earnestly requested the key to longevity. He persisted through the night, and Qiu Dechang eventually gave him a pill from his box. He instructed Dan Zhiyuan to fast, bathe, and purify his mind, and to choose a favorable day to take the pill. After only a month, Dan Zhiyuan became exhausted and weak, his scrotum shrunk and retracted, making him completely like a eunuch. His lustful desires were not yet extinguished, but he was unable to do anything, which suited his wishes. His faith became even more steadfast. Qiu Dechang then warned him to stay in a quiet room, not interacting with the outside world, and to sit upright all day, only leaving the house for urination and defecation. Qiu Dechang moved freely in and out, gradually using his techniques of sexual conquest to please his wives and concubines, who all engaged in sexual relations with him. The neighbors all knew about it, but Dan Zhiyuan did not realize at all. Eventually, Qiu Dechang seduced his wives and concubines and fled. The neighbors reported this to Dan Zhiyuan, who had been away from home for a long time and still did not believe it. However, when the report came three or four times, he finally began to pursue him. He caught Qiu Dechang in another village and took him to the county seat, where he was beaten to death. His wives and concubines were also punished. Dan Zhiyuan abandoned them and went on a journey into the mountains and forests. Later, no one knew where he ended up.

The Woman of Qingfeng Bridge Wang Geng, courtesy name Le Dao, was from Taoyuan in Suxu. He failed to make progress in his studies and instead became a dealer, becoming well-versed in worldly affairs and skilled in planning and strategy. When villagers had doubts, they would seek his guidance and follow his example, and he was highly trusted in the neighborhood. During the later years of the Song Dynasty, he left the Jin and followed the Southern Song, temporarily residing in Shanyang. In the autumn of 1124, the Jin again invaded the border, and he fled to the area behind Beigushan in Danyang.
