At that time, people from the Huai region who had crossed the Yangtze River were provided relief by the government. Wang Geng assumed the name of someone from the village and reported to the government. On the full moon of late winter, with snow and moonlight shining together, he heard the rooster crow and thought it was dawn. He quickly got dressed and went out. A servant came slowly, and Wang Geng went ahead, passing through the Lishemen gate, heading east along the river to the west, intending to go to Qingfeng Bridge. Just as he reached a hundred steps, he met a woman carrying a young servant, who asked, "Is it dawn yet?" He replied, "Yes." The woman said, "I and my sister-in-law are dealing with a dispute and are going to report it to the government, but I don’t know which road to take into the city." Wang Geng said, "I am just going that way; we can go together." Soon after, they were on the same path and entered the city. They met several more people, one of whom was dressed neatly, while the rest were holding torches, all of them servants. Seeing Wang Geng and the woman together, they shouted, "What kind of person are you? In the middle of the night, you are inciting and leading away other people's children!" Wang Geng tried to defend himself, but the man became even angrier and ordered his servants to bind and whip him a hundred times. Wang Geng cried out in pain and refused to let go, and there was a commotion and struggle. Just then, his servant arrived, calling out repeatedly as a scholar, and Wang Geng responded. The strange figures disappeared, and the ropes and bindings came off by themselves. He could not go to the government office and returned home, still feeling bewildered and fearful. The place where he was bound was unbearable, and it took over a month to return to normal. The Wife of Zhang Sheng from Huaiyin

A Man from Chu and a Woman of Integrity Zhang, a man from Chu, lived in the bay of the millstone near Huaiyin, where he ran a prosperous tavern. In the winter of 1161, during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, the Jin cavalry advanced southward, and the people of Huai fled to Jingkou for safety. Zhang, who had a foot ailment, was unable to walk and wandered in Yangzhou. Soon after, Emperor Wanyan Liang of the Jin arrived, and Zhang's wife, Zhuo, was captured by a Jin soldier. She immediately became intimate with the soldier and told him, "My husband is in the city, and he has five ingots of silver. If we don't retrieve it, it will fall into someone else's hands. It would be better if we went together to get it." The soldier was pleased and accompanied her to Zhang's place. There, he forced Zhang to hand over the silver. Zhang resisted and cursed the soldier, which only made the soldier more pleased, as he thought Zhuo was attracted to him. The soldier, who had a habit of seizing gold and jewels during raids, treated Zhuo as his wife. After a short time, Emperor Wanyan Liang died, and the Jin army retreated. Zhuo drank the soldier's wine to excess, and he fell asleep. She then stabbed him in the throat, took all his belongings, and rode back to Zhang's place. Zhang recounted the previous events and was angry, wanting to end the relationship. Zhuo offered the silver she had taken and said, "If I hadn't taken this silver, he would never have believed me and given me this silver today." Zhang's greed for silver was the cause of this, and those who heard about it praised Zhuo for her actions. The millstone is located in the county, at the confluence of the Huai and Si rivers, and is shaped like a rotating millstone, hence its name. It is the former residence of Han Xin, a famous general of the Han Dynasty. Over the generations, many heroes have emerged, and even women have shown strength and integrity. Xu Zhongche's collection tells the story of a woman from Huaiyin whose husband was killed by bandits. She did not know about her husband's death until the bandits proposed marriage through a matchmaker. They lived together for three years and had two sons. One day, while traveling by boat, she passed by her husband's burial site. The bandit thought that since they had been together for a long time and had children, she would not hate him. He laughed and told her. The woman became angry and reported the bandit to the authorities. She wept bitterly and said to others, "I married a good man who was killed by bandits. If I had known earlier, I would not have lived with the bandit and these two children, who are the offspring of criminals and should not be allowed to live. After the bandit is punished, I will also drown myself." These two women were examples of righteousness and virtue that inspired others over the centuries.

Wang Zhifu A man from Laiwu, Yan Prefecture, Wang Zhifu, though born into a farming family, possessed a strong and unyielding character. He did not flatter deities or spirits. Whenever his wife and children fell ill, he made every effort to treat them, refusing to engage in any superstitious practices such as rituals to ward off evil. Friends sometimes urged him to do so, but he would reply, "Life and death are determined by fate, and wealth and status are decided by heaven. I have set my lifelong principles and will not change them." In the spring of the first year of the Zhenglong era of the Jin Dynasty, strange and unusual phenomena suddenly arose. During the day, ghosts appeared in the courtyard, peering through windows, howling, moving beds, and shifting pots. They made various noises and moved in many different ways, causing the entire household to be terrified and unable to find peace in eating or sleeping. Wang Zhifu stood firm and unmoved, calling out to the elders and younger members of the family, warning them, "Do not be afraid of these strange things. We are human beings, true forms of heaven and earth, inheriting the positive energies of yin and yang. Those ghosts are merely negative spirits and cannot interfere with the positive. You should remain calm and not be overly fearful." The family's anxiety slightly eased. One day, while sitting calmly in the main hall, he saw a large ghost with a seven-foot-long body, wearing a high hat, a wide sash, and a long robe, with red shoes, standing respectfully before him. Wang Zhifu showed no sign of fear. The ghost, bowing his sleeves, said, "Master Wang, you are truly a righteous man today. We have already admired and respected you. We were just testing you, fearing that you might be outwardly stern but inwardly weak. If you ignore us and do not pay attention, we will not dare to act as we did before." Wang Zhifu dismissed the ghost, who bowed respectfully and disappeared.

Siu Tze-yuen, a man from Fuqiang County in the state of Cai, resided in a place called Gangan Castle, where he gathered young students to support himself. He was known for his unrestrained and unconcerned demeanor, not paying attention to minor details. Once, while returning from a rural estate to his home, he passed by an ancient temple and tomb. He heard voices of living people laughing and talking. He climbed higher to look and saw more than ten figures with the appearance of ancient kings and dukes. They were passing around wine cups and drinking freely. Siu Tze-yuen screamed out in surprise, and they all vanished. Where they had been, he found several cups and dishes, all made of white horn. He took them home and also found an ancient document. Along the way, he read it and discovered it contained esoteric practices. Siu Tze-yuen studied it diligently, even during meals and rest, and after a long time, he fully verified its contents. He could summon clouds and rain, and whatever he desired would instantly appear before him. He was also skilled in divination. In the fourth year of the Zhenglong era of the Jin dynasty, in June, there was a severe drought. The villagers asked for rain and received a response, which said that the next day it would rain heavily. On that day, the sky was filled with fiery clouds, and there were no clouds at all. The elders questioned him thoroughly.

### Final Translation Sui said to the gentlemen, "Please return quickly, for it will rain soon." After they had gone, Sui went to the garden behind the well and took a scale from the well, dipped it in water, and clapped his teeth, throwing it into the well. Soon, a dragon appeared, and the thunder roared, followed by a heavy rain that covered an area of twenty li, causing the crops to flourish. A wicked youth saw this at the beginning and reported it to the authorities, who bound Sui and brought him to the prefectural administration. The prefect ordered him released and asked him with kind words. Sui replied, "I am a poor scholar who seeks far. What can I do? My studies are in mystical arts." The prefect asked him to demonstrate one of his skills. Sui then untied the belt from his waist and placed it on the ground. With a shout, he stood tall and leaped high, dancing and singing down. He then untied the black rope and spread it on the ground, shouting at it, and it suddenly transformed into a black python. The people in the courtyard were terrified and ran away. Sui said, "Do not be afraid." As the python swirled, it grew scales and armor, and a thunderbolt rose, transforming it into a flying dragon. Sui quickly rode it away, and from then on, it was no longer visible. --- P'an Qianshu P'an Qianshu, a scholar from Nankang, lived near the Fishing Terrace on West Lake. He was a man of integrity and sharp wit, often reading books and involved in official matters. During the Chunxi era, he met a group of ruffians on the road while drunk and they argued over right and wrong. They beat him until his head was crushed, and he died shortly after. His wife was weak, and his children were too young to seek justice. In the first year of the Shaoyi era, during the end of summer, in the afternoon, a dozen or so boats gathered at the platform. The boatmen disembarked and drank wine, seeing hundreds of ghosts sitting by the fire, taking small fish and roasting them over the fire. They fought over and ate them. Some ghosts had no heads or feet, and those nearby put the cooked fish into the broken heads. One ghost covered his face and ate the fish. He called out the names of the boatmen, listened carefully, and it was P'an Qianshu. They were all frightened and ran away, and the ghosts also shouted and dispersed.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 9 *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Yijian Zhi* Volume 10 By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty **Poem by Zhongli翁** In the 11th year of the Chunxi era, a granary official in Liyang was branded and exiled for stealing government grain. His family's property was confiscated, and two scrolls of cursive script were discovered. The title on the scrolls read, "Written in the Gengshen year," with a signature resembling a sword, indicating it was by Zhongli翁. The poem reads: Dew drops on red orchids, jade-like plants fill the fields. Leisurely walking with elephant skin sandals to the west of the peak. As long as one's heart is as pure as a lotus flower, Why must one's body be as withered as a dead tree? An ancient ditch with a faint fragrance and old red flowers. Half of a peak covered in snow, a white monkey cries. Although it is not the Peach Blossom Spring, When spring comes, peach blossoms will also cover the stream. Li Cuibo inscribed, "The characters are free and graceful, with the style of flying dragons and dancing phoenixes. Breaking away from conventional paths, only those who have it in their heart and express it through their hands can achieve this. A graceful and ethereal manner like a fairy indeed has its foundation." The original is stored in the military treasury of Jiankang Prefecture. The silk and paper bindings are all broken, but only the characters remain intact. Jing Pei once saw it. Is the Gengshen year not the year when the founding emperor established the dragon dynasty?

Pan Yuan Ning's Turtle Dream Pan Yuan Ning was a man from the Muxi village in Qingtian who enjoyed entertaining guests and eating turtles. Whenever he caught turtles near streams or ponds, he sold them. In the spring of the third year of Shaoxi, a fisherman brought a huge turtle weighing six catties. Pan saw it and was delighted, wanting to cook and eat it. His wife said, "Today is the day of Shangsi, we should not eat this turtle. Let's keep it and wait until tomorrow morning." The children tied a rope around its feet and played with it, dragging it around. By evening, it fell into a ditch and was lost. After more than a month, his wife dreamed of a tall man who cried and said, "Because of your words, I was spared from the cooking pot. But now I have fallen into a ditch and am being eaten by worms, nearly losing one of my feet. I am near death. Please grant me a final favor." She woke up and told Pan, who laughed and said, "This dream is just a figment of the imagination, not worth believing." The next morning, he got up and saw the turtle limping in the mud. He picked it up and had his servant release it into the river. Both husband and wife dreamed that the turtle came to thank them. The Master of the Cherry Garden In the army before the palace in Lin'an, there was a runaway soldier. The official Hou Yan went out to capture him. Passing through the Cherry Garden, he saw a Taoist priest with an ancient appearance, long beard, wearing a black crown with seven stars and a purple cloud robe standing on the side of the road. As Yan passed by, the priest glared at him with anger, gnashing his teeth, and said, "Damn it, a strong man like you acts so arrogantly!" Yan replied, "I am just passing by, what does this have to do with you?" The priest said, "You almost knocked me over. I am the Master of the Supreme Void and the Great Cave, responsible for driving away evil spirits at the North Pole. I can capture all ghosts and demons in the world. Now, all officials in the court respect me."

### Translation of the Source Text How can they bully each other, with the two of them quarreling endlessly? The Taoist slapped Yan's cheek, but Yan did not know the reason and dared not retaliate. Instead, he grasped the Taoist's wrist with his hand. The Taoist could not withstand it and then said, "Let me leave you for now, but tonight after the third watch, I will make you know my divine power." Yan returned home and felt uneasy in mind. At midnight, he suddenly became like someone having a stroke, going mad, crying, and screaming as if possessed by a ghost. The family went to help, but his strength was ten times greater than usual, making it impossible to approach him. So they invited a shaman to examine and treat him, but it was ineffective. After a hundred or so days, he finally recovered, and the Five Thunder Chen Taoist, finding it strange, then left. The so-called Taoist was actually a ghost. --- Li Mengdan's brothers The student Li Mengdan from Raozhou, in the first month of the first year of the Jiaqing era, was afflicted with an epidemic. Only his younger brother Mengshuo escaped. All the household affairs, both inside and out, including buying rice and salt, were taken care of by the younger brother. Before this, when Mengdan was sick, although severely ill, he recovered in five days. In his dream, he saw a divine person speaking. Mengshuo should also have a five-day illness, but if he is not well, who will manage the household affairs? Someone nearby said, "Do not let his elder brother take over." The divine person said, "Then you will immediately feel all over burning, and your illness will return to its original state." After four days, in his dream, he presented a petition to the divine person, only asking to be exempt from the remaining days. It seemed as if he had arrived at the main hall, which was a temple inside the gate of Pingzhou. He hoped that the divine person would grant his request, but he only heard the sound of the divine person giving orders to the attendants to take the petition. Suddenly, he heard a furious rebuke, saying, "You don't take medicine but want to be exempt from the illness." Mengdan told the divine person that there was no medicine, and the divine person said, "The medicine is already prepared; it is the White Peony Heart Decoction." Mengdan again said there was none, and the divine person did not make a judgment on the petition. He did not realize that his younger brother was in front, boiling the medicine. When asked, the younger brother said, "Zou the doctor sent it; it is the White Peony Heart Decoction." He was pleased that it matched his dream and immediately took it.

Soon after, he fell asleep again and dreamed of making a petition to present before the deity. He soon returned to the deity's presence and asked if he had taken the medicine, to which he replied that he had taken it. The deity ordered a clerk to bring a jar and instructed him to vomit, and he vomited out all five organs, which were then put back. Then he took the petition and wrote on it, saying that Li Mengdan should substitute for his brother Mengyue for five days, and now there is a petition requesting exemption because the new official is unfamiliar with the duties. Today, Zhang Wang and Li De are ordered to take care of Li Mengdan's illness, and he is to sweat by noon tomorrow, and the petition is to be presented again. Soon after, another yellow note summoned the four men away. Li Mengdan said, "Let's stay and watch," but I said, "No." Then he returned and said, "Instruct two people to induce a cool sweat, and two people to prepare medicine. However, by noon tomorrow, it will not be for five days, but rather until the first watch. After a long time, someone wearing a yellow silk robe came and said, 'Your family has been afflicted by the plague, and I will escort you to Fengdu.' He then realized that he had sweated all over his body, and he felt light and comfortable immediately. It was mid-September, and the whole process took a hundred days.

### Translation **Jiang You Sweeps the Temple** Jiang You, a man from Poyang City, supported himself through hired labor, never marrying, but remaining strong and healthy even in old age, and never weakening in his ability to carry heavy loads. In the sixteenth year of the Chongning era, on the eighth day of the first month, he began to abandon his old occupation and renounced himself as a servant in the main hall of the temple, providing services of sweeping and cleaning. He ate daily at the temple custodian Sun Yanheng's home and slept at night under the eaves. The Sun family was extremely poor, and Jiang often lacked food, sometimes not having enough for days. In the fourth year of the Shaoxi era, on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, early in the morning, he swept the ground thoroughly while the doors were still closed. He met a scholar who greeted him, and the scholar asked him, "How old are you now?" Jiang replied, "Eighty-four years old." The scholar said, "I know you have no food to eat and no money to spend; I will help you a little." He immediately called his servant and whispered to him. Soon, a measure of rice and three strings of cash appeared beside Jiang. Jiang bowed and thanked him, and the person disappeared. Jiang quickly took the money and rice into his room and then went to the three deities' seats. He faintly saw one of the deities stand up, with a belt tied around his waist, and then return to his original position. Jiang often heard, in the dead of night, the sound of the deity driving a carriage, with the sound of the attendants and the bells of the carriage like those of crows. People living outside the temple also sometimes heard it, but they never saw anything. **Rhododendron Flowers in Pingyang** Wang Shunbo was the magistrate of Pingyang, Wenzhou. In September, he went to the fields to inspect the dry farmland. On the road, he saw a rhododendron bush that was very tall, with several flowers, their color like fresh cinnabar, illuminating the faces of people, making them look red. He was surprised that it bloomed out of season and asked the local people. They all said that this kind only grows in the mountains and valleys, blooming four times a year, most profusely in spring and autumn. Wang wanted to find a smaller one but could not. He suspected that there was only one. He said according to the *Biographies of Immortals* that in Danlin Temple in Runzhou there was such a flower, over ten feet tall, blooming profusely at the end of spring. Sometimes people saw three women in red dresses, beautiful and elegant, playing in the woods. The common people say that these are the flower spirits.

With people collectively preserving the flourishing, which is different from ordinary flowers, the governor Zhou Bao said to the Daoist monk Yin Qiqi, "The flowers in the  forest are extraordinary in the world. I have heard that they can bloom out of season. Can this flower bloom now?" Yin Qiqi replied, "Yes, it can." Zhou Bao said, "Today is the Double Ninth Festival. Can it bloom on this day?" Yin Qiqi then went to the  forest the day before. At night, a woman came and said, "I am commanded by the Supreme One to manage this flower and open it together with the Daoist." The next morning, the flower began to unfold its petals, and by the ninth day, it bloomed like spring, amazing the entire city. Thus, the autumn beauty of the cuckoo lies in Pingyang, naturally not dependent on the power of female immortals and Daoists. As for the monkey skin from Sichuan, Peng Zhongne sent his brother Zhonghe to Lin安置 and held a farewell banquet at Tiantang Temple south of the Poyang River. There, he saw several dozen villagers lined up under the corridor, drawing lots and facing each other as if planning something. Upon closer inspection, they were all fishermen from the riverbank. He asked them what they were discussing. They replied, "There is a guest from the river who brought a monkey skin to sell, priced at thirteen guan. We exactly twenty-six people, each contributing five fen, to buy it, and we will cut it up and take it away." Peng said, "One monkey's skin is so valuable, how can it be bought for this price?" The fishermen said, "The monkey skin from Sichuan is placed on the hook to catch white fish, with no failures. After one use, it becomes even more durable and does not rot. Unlike our local skins, which rot when wet and are only good for two or three uses, we do not hesitate to pay the high price, fearing to miss out." Last year, your brother got a monkey from Yiling, two feet tall, with a fierce and ugly appearance. He brought it back to the stable, where it died after a year. The stable worker skinned and cooked the meat. Fishermen happened to see it and, considering the connection between Chongqing and Sichuan, bought the skin for five hundred coins, which was a great joy, as it was exactly what they needed and its value was hard to estimate.

Wang Lüdao's Advancement In the first year of the Zhenghe era, Wang Lüdao served as the recorder of Xiangzhou. Upon completing his term and returning to the capital, the governor of Xiangzhou, Han Chunyan, highly esteemed him and recommended his younger brother, Cuiyan, to be summoned to the court. Cuiyan was the father-in-law of Cai Xiu, who was in power during the Northern Song Dynasty. When Cuiyan advised his brother, saying, "Do not rush to advance; instead, make Wang Lüdao your priority," Wang Lüdao, known for his literary reputation, had already made a significant impact in the northern regions and was confident in his ability to advance to the three imperial academies. A skilled diviner told him, "You are truly a noble person, but for now, you will only hold a low-ranking position. After three years, you will begin to prosper and rise to high offices, eventually becoming a high-ranking military officer and holding military authority. However, your later years will be lonely and desolate." Wang Lüdao did not initially believe the diviner's words. After arriving in the capital, he was appointed as a scholar-official responsible for teaching the imperial clan. He felt dissatisfied with his position and desired to leave. Someone suggested that there was a scholar named Liu Qichang living in a temple within the Fongchou Gate. Wang Lüdao immediately went to visit him. Liu Qichang invited him into a small study where he saw dozens of scrolls of paintings and calligraphy, all belonging to Wang Lüdao.

He inscribed at the end and then withdrew. Wang had always practiced the calligraphy of Ouyang Xiu, and Liang claimed that he was a talented scholar from the Ouyang family, treasuring it as a priceless gem. He placed the scroll in a box and immediately rode a horse to Liang's residence, showing it to him. The next day, there was an appointment as a Left Zuo Zu, as Liang had already recommended him to the emperor through a direct petition. Cai was not informed in advance. One day, while at the office, Cai sent someone to summon him to the mansion. He did not meet him but instead ordered an old soldier to lead him to a small study behind the long corridor. He took out a yellow bag containing documents and handed them over, which was a test of three external regulations. All the necessary items such as brushes, ink, paper, inkstones, glue boxes, scissors, rulers, and ink droppers were all prepared. Later, a very generous meal was prepared, and the remaining food was given to his family. Once the writing was completed, there was no way to deliver it. He noticed someone peeping through the window and called out to the old soldier to quickly hide, as it was Cai. After a while, the old soldier came to retrieve it, and then he led him out. The next day, the emperor's handwriting appointed him as a Middle Bookkeeper. Cai did not accept the position and instead made a report, stating that it had never happened before that a Zuo Zu was promoted to a court attendant. Therefore, he was changed to the position of Junior Secretary. After only forty days, the previous command was finally carried out.

During this period, there were often officials of high rank or literary officials who were appointed to positions of control, but Wang returned the appointment order, and Emperor Huizong praised him for his boldness in speaking out. Wang was promoted to the position of Vice President of the Censorate. In the early years of the Xuanhe era, Cai lost the emperor's favor. The emperor instructed Wang to criticize those close to him. Cai knew about this and, while Wang was waiting in the palace, Cai knelt and wept before the emperor, saying, "Please do not let Wang Anzhong speak against me." He repeated his plea earnestly without adding any further words. The emperor smiled and said, "Do not worry." Wang then ascended the throne to announce the appointment of a scholar-official, and the matter was thus put to rest. Wang served in office for three years and was promoted to the position of Left Chancellor of the Board of Works. After the conquest of Yan Mountain, he was appointed as the military governor of Qingyuan. In the early years of the Jingkang era, he was punished for failing to defend a city and was demoted and exiled to Xiangzhou, where he died. All the astrologers' predictions came true. In the year 1195, the author's father served as an official in the local area. Wang Roushao, a junior official, provided a detailed account. The author listened attentively to the account. Due to the passage of time, the details were not recorded accurately. More than fifty years have passed, and the names of the astrologers, scholars, and the temple where Wang lived are no longer remembered. Only this much can be recalled and written down.

The Venerable Zheng In the twentieth year of the Shaoxing era, there was a Venerable Zheng in Poyang, whose origin was unknown. He would not enter the Daoist hall, but daily went begging in the market, and at night he went out to sleep in the northern county's communal altar, seven li away from the city gate, where no one lived. Once, in advance of the Spring Sacrifice, he commanded officials to repair the sacrificial hall but they could not return. That night, with sharp cold, he was seen picking up withered branches and leaves and lighting a fire in the corner of the house, as if talking to someone. Almost midnight, he turned and said to him, "Warm yourself by the fire, you can leave now. Do not create strange phenomena or evil karma, enduring through countless eons without hope of liberation." Then he bowed and stood up, soon laying alone by the fire. In the morning, he went out again, staying for several months without anyone considering him a companion. Some thought him a man of the Way, while others believed he was bewitched by demons and his heart fell into the record of ghosts. Only Yong Youwen recognized him and said he was a strange person. Afterward, he was unknown. Wang the Attendant Minister Wang Wenqing, the Attendant Minister, was already recorded in earlier annals. In the early days of the Shaoxing era, he entered Fujian but was not respected by people. He once stayed at the Qingcheng Temple in Fuzhou. The people of the prefecture saw his actions and suspected them to be exaggerated and false, so they sent servants to throw tiles and gravel at the window at night to scare him. Wang remained calm, and later, he hit the tiles twice. He asked, "Are you human or ghost?" One by one, they were sent away and the servants fell to the ground, never to rise again. At that time, Master Zhang Yuanjue was renowned for his Daoist techniques, and the people of Fujian called him a sage. Wang interacted with him and heard that Zhang presided over rituals. He told those he trusted, "Should have a big argument with this bald monk, but Zhang looked around and entered the city as if searching for something." Tian Fengzi was sitting in a teahouse on East Street, so he went over and bowed, saying, "Your wild behavior has returned."

Yé Wang laughed and said, "It will only take a moment." When Zhang arrived at the temple, he cleaned the altar and made the lights as bright as day. Suddenly, a wind came from the northwest, extinguishing everything. After eating for a while, everything suddenly became bright again. Many of the Daoist practitioners were not in agreement with him. Because the governor wanted to pray for rain, he requested that the Daoist practitioners perform a ritual on a platform set up in front of the government office. Afterward, he alone stood below, holding a sword and performing the Yu step, during which the stars filled the sky. Then, a violent wind and clouds came from the northwest, extinguishing all the candles. A loud thunderclap followed, and sweet rain poured down. His disciples were terrified and came down, and Wang had already left. From then on, both the Daoists and the common people began to respect and honor Wang's skills. He practiced the Five Thunder Technique, but he used it for play and to harm ordinary people, which is probably not something the gods and heavens can tolerate. Zhang, the Holy One, was originally a resident of Shuixi Shuangfeng in Fuzhou. One day, while gathering firewood in the mountains, he encountered two people playing chess on a boulder. He sat with them and was offered bamboo shoots, but he could not finish them and left. That day, he abandoned his home and began selling divination. He never bought money to cast the divination, yet people's fortunes, misfortunes, life and death were immediately known to him. He called himself Zhang Chuheng. During the Shaoxing period, Zhang Wei Gong governed Fujian. His mother, Lady Qi, often gave ordination certificates to the East Zen Temple, asking them to choose a disciple to be ordained. One night, the abbot dreamed of a black dragon coiled outside the temple. The next morning, he saw Zhang and asked, "Do you want to become a monk?" Zhang replied, "I would like to." So, he shaved his head and was given the name Yuanjue. He often put both fists in his mouth, and when he laughed, it was almost uncontrollable. Despite not knowing how to read or write, he often composed poetry.

In social interactions, one must speak frankly and offer advice. In the county, there was a man named Lin Dong, who had talent but lacked virtue. Once, he criticized Zhang by saying, "The Bodhisattva's head has grown horns." Zhang responded, "Lin Dong will not survive the winter." As predicted, Lin Dong was punished and assigned to local service, then traveled to Jian'an, where he spoke freely and offended the Deputy Commissioner of Transportation, Ma Ziyue Chun. Ma arrested him, shackled and beat him, but Lin Dong's body remained unharmed. Eventually, he was accused of creating superstition and deluding the people, impeached by the court, and exiled to Meizhou. After a long time, he returned to his hometown. In the winter of Jiyou, someone asked who would be the top scholar in the new year, and he replied, "He is in the home of Liang's tenth brother." No one understood this, but it was later revealed that Liang, the Chancellor of Wenling, was the best in the world, and the tenth brother was named Ke. Zhang encountered a chess player who wore one hat and one bun. The bun-wearer gave him a bamboo sprout, which was said to be from Zhongziyun. **Chen Yuan's Poem to Zigu** Chen Yuan, a resident of Gan Zhou in Houguan, passed the imperial examinations as the second-place candidate but died before taking up his official duties. The *Kui Zhi* (a historical record) mentions that after his death, local scholars asked the deity Zigu for a sign. Two years later, they received two large characters: "Chen Yuan." A poem was then written: "The moon orchid once climbed the second branch; in the green robe, I was proud to brush against the red steps. Unfavored by the emperor's grace, I returned empty-handed. How much sorrow in breaking the chain?" This poem reflects Chen Yuan's disappointment after his death, as he had just married and was separated from his wife for only a few months. Thus, the final lines cannot be forgotten and are indeed sorrowful.

The *Yijian Zhi* (Records of the Supernatural) Volume Ten of the *Ding* Section <Novel Category, Strange Tales Subcategory, *Yijian Zhi*> *Qinding Siku Quanshu* (Imperially Approved Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature) *Yijian Zhi* Volume One of the *Wu* Section By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty The Immortal Li of Shixi Village Li Jia, a villager from Shixi Village in Shunchang County, Nanjian State, was forty years old and had not married. He only ate and slept at his younger brother's wife's house. He often cut wood, burned it into charcoal, and sold it in the market. When he got money, he would buy two sheng of rice daily to support himself. If there was any surplus, he would save it for use when it rained or snowed and he couldn't go out. He never wasted anything else. In the second year of the Shaoxi era (1191 AD), in the ninth month, he entered the mountains a bit deeper and was tired. He rested outside an empty house and heard the sound of people playing chess from outside. He knew that people lived there and saw that there were two scholars playing chess. Li Jia hurried forward and bowed to them, calling them "Master." The chess players laughed and asked him, "What do you do for a living?" He replied, "I sell charcoal." They asked, "Can you take medicine?" He replied, "Yes." They immediately asked their servant to fetch something from a gourd and gave it to him. The servant looked rather reluctant and said, "What is this thing, and why are you giving it so lightly?" The scholar said, "It's not for you to know." The medicine was bright red and had a slightly sour taste. After taking it, he was quickly sent away. They said, "You will return to this mountain in thirty years." When he left, he looked back and saw nothing. He smelled the food in his waistband and found it too stinky to eat. He poured it into water and continued on his way. By the time he returned home, three days had passed. He then had diarrhea for several days. From then on, he no longer ate regular food but only ate wild fruits. The villagers called him Li Xian (the Immortal Li).

Professor Yang's Younger Brother In Shuixi Village in Fuzhou, there is a mountain that rises sheer for a hundred feet, and no one has ever been able to climb it. Professor Yang Yi Zhong's younger brother, who was light and frivolous and not careful about his actions, suddenly dreamed of a yellow-robed Taoist who told him, "Come to the mountain tomorrow morning, and I will meet you there." In the morning, he told several close friends, and they agreed to go together. As they were joking and laughing along the way, they reached the base of the mountain. Yang then lifted his clothes and ascended the sheer cliff, walking with ease and grace as if on level ground. The others stood in amazement, looking up at him. He laughed on the mountain top and searched for the yellow-robed Taoist, but found no one. Instead, he saw white flowers covering the ground. At that time, he was very hungry and ate all the flowers, leaving none behind. He then waved his hand down to call his companions, but none dared to look up. He descended slowly and returned. That night, he dreamed again of the previous person, who was angry and said, "Why did you say so many words?" He then swallowed saliva, and spittle overflowed between his teeth and cheeks, spitting into a large bowl and making him drink it. Yang drank it all in one gulp without any resentment. It was cold and rainy, but he only wore a single layer of clothing and sat on the mountain for several days before returning. His clothes were not wet, and he claimed he had never encountered rain. From then on, he drank and ate only two or three tenths of what he usually did. He wore the clothes of a Taoist and walked through the market, fair-skinned like jade, with a clear and refined demeanor, fluttering away with a transcendent air. He had clearly gained something. During the Chongxi era, he still existed.

Wan Shou Temple Gatekeeper In the early years of the Southern Song Dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong, a man known as a "Liao zi" sold himself into service as a gatekeeper at the Wan Shou Temple in Fuzhou. For many years, he diligently opened and closed the gates, swept, and cleaned, working tirelessly day and night. Among the temple's servants, he was the most diligent and orderly. When the abbot discussed repairing the temple halls and pavilions, it was estimated that five hundred thousand would be needed. The abbot intended to carefully select honest and capable individuals to go out and solicit donations. The Liao zi heard this and entered to say, "I have been at the temple gates for a long time and have nothing to offer. I wish to handle this matter for the temple." Everyone in the temple pointed to him as mad. Shortly after, the money arrived, and everyone was greatly surprised. Someone asked where it came from, and the Liao zi smiled and did not answer. Later, the abbot visited the mountain estate to inspect the crops, and the Liao zi died in the gatehouse. The temple wrapped him in cloth and buried him. Only then did they inform the abbot. The abbot said, "Two days ago, I saw him passing by with a staff, not bowing and leaving. I thought he was displeased with something, but I did not know he had died." He ordered the grave to be opened and found only his clothes remaining. Tian Zhan Tou In the middle of the Southern Song Dynasty, there was a beggar in Fuzhou named Tian Zhan Tou, whose origins were unknown. His clothes were dirty and unkempt, and he did not interact with others. His appearance was extremely disheveled, yet he never combed his hair and had no lice. He never bathed, but his body did not smell. He often stayed in a secluded area west of An Tai Bridge, using a tattered mat as shelter, barely enough to sit on. He often spat out a white, round object from his mouth, which he played with endlessly. If someone saw it, he would laugh and swallow it again. It was said to be an internal elixir. Whether sitting or lying down, he could stay for over a month without going out to beg. Suddenly, he would appear, running without stopping. Zhang Yuan Jue recognized his unusual qualities and left him a poem: "The Buddhist realm of thirty gold worlds, the Daoist twelve jade towers. Where has the cloud gone? Only leaving the beggar to sleep on his mat at night." Several years later, he disappeared without a trace.

Duke Huang and Liu, Two Daoists Mr. Sa from Shu resided in Quanzhou, famous for his Daoist techniques. Hundreds of people followed him. In Futang, there were two Daoists named Liu and Huang, who were also his disciples. Huang was older, and Liu called him elder brother. In the fifth year of the Chongxi era, they planned to visit him together. Liu traveled to Jiangnan and suddenly fell ill. Huang criticized him, saying, "You are so obstructed; how can you enter the Dao? I will take care of myself now." Liu replied, "I have been mediocre all my life, with no significant achievements. I have not seen Mr. Sa and fear I will become a lower ghost." Huang said, "Anyone who studies Daoism may not achieve it in one lifetime. If there is something that can be achieved, I will temporarily record it and wait for the next life to cultivate, which will fulfill my wish." Two days later, Liu died. Huang burned his body. Two nights later, he stayed at an inn, and Liu appeared in no form and cursed him on both sides, saying, "You have misled me. The Book of the Dead has not accepted me. Now, I have nowhere to go. What can I do?" Huang said, "Why not stay with someone?" Liu replied, "I am already a ghost. Where can I go?" So, they followed each other for three days and nights, suffering greatly. Liu then said, "There is a wealthy family; I will send you there." They returned to Fuzhou. Initially, the wealthy man Zheng treated Huang very well. His wife was pregnant. Huang knocked on the door at night and said, "I have an urgent matter and had to enter the city at night. I hope to stay for one night and then leave." He also asked Zheng's wife about the day she would give birth, saying, "Not yet." Huang secretly told Liu, "It can be done." The next morning, his wife gave birth to a boy. Six years later, Huang returned to Zheng's residence. On a winter evening, they sat by the stove, and the baby also sat beside them. Suddenly, the baby laughed and said, "Brother Huang, do you remember when we were in Xiuzhou and practiced alchemy together?" Huang's face changed color. Zheng's father heard and shouted, "My son, why are you delirious?" The baby then stopped talking. In the nineteenth year of the Qingyuan era, the boy's actions, speech, and silence were all like those of Daoists, but Huang died spitting blood.

### 筹洋村鬼 In the village of Guankeng in Luoyuan County, Fuzhou, there lived a woodcutter who regularly went to Cuiyang Village to gather firewood. One day, he returned a bit late and, as he walked along a small bay, he encountered a man descending from the mountains. The man called out to him, saying, "We haven't seen each other for a long time." The woodcutter realized that the man was dead and his expression was one of dejection. Slowly, the man could speak and said, "You are dead. How are you here?" The woodcutter replied, "I should not have died. Now I live here just as I did before." He pointed to a dense forest and said, "I only live in there. Can we go together to talk?" The woodcutter rejected the offer, saying, "The sun is almost setting, and I am afraid that my family will be waiting at the door." The man then said, "If you come to my house, tell my mother that I live here as I did when I was alive. Don't worry about me. Just arrange food and drink outside the door, and I will naturally enjoy them." The woodcutter quickly bowed and left. The next day, he reported the encounter to the ghost's mother, who cried and burned paper money. She set up wine and food and closed the door while praying. After a short while, she went out to see and found only empty containers. The ghost had a wife and three sons, but after his death, the wife took the sons and remarried, frequently suffering beatings from the stepfather. The father often walked alone across the mountain pass, and the ghost blocked the path and scolded, saying, "What can be done about your evil? Having taken my wife and mistreated my children, what kind of reason is this?" He then raised his fists and hit him. This person also fought back, and they chased each other to the cave entrance. A passerby, surprised, asked them, and the ghost disappeared. Upon returning home, the woodcutter had blue marks all over his body and no longer dared to pass by as before.

**The Tiger of Guan Pit** In the village of Guan Pit in Luoyang, there is a hill that is not very high, with a flat top. The residents call it the "Basket Top Field." A woman from this village once returned to visit her parents and passed by this place. She saw a tiger crouching in the grass, and fearing that she could not escape, she stood up and called out to it, saying, "Brother Tiger, I am visiting my parents today. There is no grudge between us. Please go quickly." The tiger lowered its ears and stood on its hind legs, listening attentively. It then dragged its tail and sought a dangerous path, and the woman managed to escape. People say that tigers are spiritual creatures and do not harm humans without cause. However, this woman, seeing a fierce beast, did not fear and was able to reason with it, which is quite remarkable. **The Tiger of Shigu Mountain** In the fourth year of the Shaoxi era during the reign of Emperor Lizong of the Song Dynasty, a woman from a village in Gutian County was picking bamboo shoots on Shigu Mountain when she was grabbed and carried away by a tiger. At night, she had a dream in which she said to her family, "I first came down the mountain and met a black tiger emerging from the opposite rock. It was still far away, so I hurriedly climbed the mountain, but two children forced me to stop and could not move forward. I cried out to heaven for mercy, and the tiger was already beside me, ready to eat me. How terrible!" Her family woke up and lit fires, carrying sticks to search for her. They found her lying on a grassy slope, her body completely covered in wounds. They carried her back to their house. After midnight, the tiger circled the house, roaring and threatening, as if seeking something. This continued for several nights. It is said that the tiger failed to catch the woman and thus became angry. The two children were actually伥鬼, the spirits of people who had been killed by tigers and now serve them.

**Chen Family Girl as Bai Qi** In the first year of the Jingyuan era (1195) in March, a seventeen-year-old girl from the Chen family in Fujian Province fell ill. As she was dying, she spoke to people and said, "I am the ancient Bai Qi." The girl had never known how to read, and her family was greatly surprised. They asked her, "Bai Qi was a general of the State of Qin, and he has been dead for more than one thousand three hundred years. Why do you say this?" The girl replied, "This is because I killed seventy to eighty thousand people in my lifetime. In the underworld, I suffered countless torments. Recently, I have just regained human form, but I will be reborn as a woman repeatedly, and my lifespan will not exceed twenty years. This is my fate today." After finishing speaking, she died. **Zhang Han Ying** Zhang Han Ying was originally from Chang'an but fled south during a period of turmoil and settled in Fuzhou. He lived in poverty and had no other means of support, relying on the monks at Wansui Temple for food. In the fourth year of the Shaoshi era (1193), June, he dreamed that he was led by a yellow-clad officer who tied him with a rope and made him follow. Everywhere was dark, and he did not know where he was going. Suddenly, the rope broke, and he could not take another step. He stood there crying, and the yellow-clad officer suddenly appeared from a small alley, raised his hand, and beckoned him to follow. They arrived at an office whose door was extremely low, with a sign reading "Office for Examining Every Tiny Sin." The person in charge, dressed in white, sat at a desk making judgments. The attendants on both sides were also women, dressed in white. The person in charge shouted loudly, "What sins did you commit while alive?" Zhang replied, "I have always wished to help others, but I lacked the strength. I never had any intention of harming anyone." The person in charge said, "You should not expect fame or fortune. But if you ever betray your heart, everything you do here will be recorded. You must not forget this!" Zhang dared not answer and was filled with fear, waking up in a state of shock. He did not discuss this experience with others. Later, he died while ill. People suspected that there might be something sinister about it.

A Strange Monk of Xuefeng Mountain At Xuefeng Mountain, Elder Master Fachun of Guanyin Temple in Gutian once stayed temporarily with a guardian monk who was from the western part of Zhejiang. This monk behaved in a somewhat eccentric manner and often made exaggerated statements. He would say that people from Fuzhou would call monks bald slaves, and asked what the reason for this was. However, the local custom of practicing Pure Land Buddhism seemed commendable. Later, he accompanied ten monks on a trip to Gushan Mountain. Before reaching five miles, a sudden gust of wind arose between their steps. The group was startled and disoriented, almost unable to stand, but after a short while, the wind calmed down. By then, this monk had already disappeared, and no one knew where he went. Everyone suspected he was a wandering saint mixed among ordinary people, or perhaps an odd character. Sanyang Dragon Pool In the year 1184 during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, there was no rain in Fuzhou during the summer. The governor, Zhao Zizhi, ordered all counties to show utmost respect and pray at all sacred shrines, springs, and other places of spiritual significance within their territories. In Gutian County, there were three pools on Sanyang Mountain, located in a steep and precipitous area. Residents often went there to pray for rain and usually received abundant rainfall. One day, the county magistrate, Mr. Chen, went to the site, burned incense, and offered a prayer. Afterward, he stood by the pool, wearing his official hat and robe, and expressed his wish to see the form of a dragon. Mr. Chen was a sincere and diligent man, and he remained steadfast in his devotion. Suddenly, a black cloud rose from the mountain and covered the entire sky. An object leaped out of the third pool and landed on a rock, revealing itself to be the hind leg of a dragon. It was a bright yellow color and as large as a pillar. Mr. Chen hurriedly descended and found a place to rest in a nearby house. Immediately, it began to rain heavily, covering the entire county, and the rain lasted for three days before stopping. The governor reported this event to the imperial court, which then decreed that the site be honored and a temple be built.

**Strange Monk of Xuefeng** The elder monk Fachun of Gu Tian Guanyin Temple once stayed at Xuefeng Monastery, where he shared lodging with a strange monk from Zhe West. This monk was quite eccentric in his manner and had a tendency to speak boastfully. He often said, "People in Fuzhou like to call monks 'bald slaves'—what is the reason for this?" Yet, despite this, the local custom was largely devoted to the Pure Land tradition, which seemed commendable. Later, they went together into the city, heading toward Gu Mountain, where ten monks were traveling together. Not yet five li from their destination, a sudden whirlwind arose between their steps. The group was thrown into confusion, losing their order, and nearly fell. After a short while, the wind ceased, and the monk was nowhere to be found. All were puzzled, suspecting he might be a wandering sage mingling with ordinary people, or perhaps a strange being. **Shanyang Dragon Pond** In the year Chunxi Jiachen (1184 CE) of the Song dynasty, during the hot summer in Fuzhou, there was no rain. The governor Zhao Zizheng ordered all counties to pay reverence and pray at any local shrines, springs, or places known for their spiritual power. In Gu Tian County, Shanyang Mountain had three ponds nestled among steep rock ridges. Locals often went there to pray for rain, and frequently received timely rain. Thus, the county magistrate Chen went to the site, burned incense, and offered words of supplication. Then, facing the pond, he stood upright with ceremonial cap and staff, vowing to see the dragon’s form. Chen was a man of sincerity and determination, and remained steadfast for a long time. Suddenly, dark clouds arose from the mountain's waist, covering the entire sky. A creature leapt from the third pond and coiled upon the rock—this was the dragon’s thigh, a deep yellow in color, as large as a beam. Chen hastily ran down and took shelter briefly at a nearby civilian's house. Immediately, heavy rain poured over the entire county, drenching it for three days before ceasing. The prefecture reported the event to the imperial court, and the emperor issued an edict to bestow a title and establish a temple.

The Cave of the Floating Moon Lady Temple In the third year of the Shaoxi era, a man from Fuzhou named Zheng Lizi traveled by sea from Panyu back to his hometown. His ship stopped at the Floating Moon Bay in Putian before leaving the harbor. Someone came to report that there were six pirate ships in the nearby ocean. They suggested planning an escape. The crew went to the Temple of the Venerable Lady of Auspiciousness to seek help. They obtained three auspicious yarrow stalks. Although they were happy that there was no danger, they hesitated and could not decide. They gathered and said, "We are few in number and should not openly act during the day to invite disaster. And how do we know the informant is not part of the pirates' lookout group? Do not fall into their trap. It would be better to wait until dawn and attack them unexpectedly, perhaps we can avoid the danger. Especially since the goddess promised us." Everyone agreed. When they reached the harbor, there were indeed six ships flying over the waves. Two of them had already approached, causing the crew to be in a state of distress. They only looked far at the temple, prayed, and put on armor, shooting arrows at them. The arrows were almost all used up, and the pirates had already boarded the ship. One pirate with a long spear was about to jump on, when suddenly smoke and mist arose, and a sudden wind and rain came. The surging waves like mountains made it impossible to see each other, it was completely like the middle of the night. After the storm cleared, the pirate ships all headed southeast. They looked incredibly small, and Zheng Lizi's ship was also drifting dozens of miles away. There was no other fear, it was a gift from the goddess. Her miraculous nature is like this, and now she has been promoted to the rank of a concubine.

A Monk from Fujian Named Zongda Once upon a time, there was a monk from Fujian named Zongda who served as the abbot of Luoyuan Mountain Temple. He was the uncle of a lay practitioner named Lin from Lianjiang, who had received his novice training as a sandali (novice monk). Later, Zongda was stationed in the regions of Jiang and Xiang, where he met Lin in a village market in Nanchang. Zongda saw him and was very pleased, guiding him to a temple where they stayed for three nights. At night, however, Zongda was no longer there, and the novice monk only heard moaning sounds as if they were coming from dozens of miles away. The novice monk was puzzled, and at that time, Zongda had already passed away five years ago. Yet, the novice monk suddenly forgot about it and went to visit and search for Zongda. He found Zongda sitting by the stove, with his feet in the fire, moaning in pain without end. Afterward, Zongda walked and stood as if at the beginning. He said, "In my lifetime, I once cut down two trees behind the temple and gave them to someone. Now I have fallen into a bad state, and every time I encounter boiling water in a cauldron, my feet are used as firewood. Fortunately, in my daily life, I had no other sins, only this one. And so, I was reborn." The number of monks in this temple was slightly large. In the morning, they went to the dining hall to eat porridge three times. First, there was molten copper, then molten iron, and finally porridge. You must remember this. Soon after, a report came that the rope bed had caught fire. The monks gathered and stood in the Dharma Hall. One monk took off his clothes and lay on the bed to be burned. The pain was unbearable. After a while, he got up and put his clothes back on. Others continued to do so, one after another. Zongda said, "These are all people who slander the Prajna (Wisdom) teachings. Generally, those who have not yet understood the truth speak falsely about things. The sin is extremely heavy. You should deeply reflect on this. Fortunately, I did not suffer from this. I can explain this to the world." The novice monk was drenched in sweat and suddenly woke up. It turned out he was in the wilderness. At that time, the temple and the bell tower were shining with gold and jade, but it was all an illusion. What I remember about the events at Wujiao Mountain is quite similar to the events described in this passage.

Tong Gong Ren Tong Gong Ren was a wealthy merchant from Chang Le County in Fuzhou. On the first day of the first month in the first year of the Chunxi era (1174 AD), his concubine dreamed that three people entered the house. Two of them wore green robes and held documents, writing large on the wall: "Tong Gong Ren will die an evil death on the seventh day of the fourth month this year." The concubine, who could read, told her companions the next morning that the dream was extremely ominous. They looked at the wall but found no writing. All kept the matter secret and did not speak of it. After some time, a group of merchants, including Zhang Shixian, He Zhongli, Zhong Ji, and more than ten others, agreed that Mo Shaoyu in the East Wall of Fuqing was preparing a ship to sail to Zhejiang in April. They decided to buy cloth together and depart as planned. They arrived at the state boundary at Fuying Port around midnight. The ship's crew reported that the ship had inexplicably begun to break apart. Zhang Shixian quickly got out of bed and went outside. At that time, the ship was anchored about a zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length) from the shore. He felt as if someone was pushing him and his companions to the shore. The others climbed onto the shore one after another, but Tong Gong Ren and He Zhongli were reluctant to leave their wealth behind. Soon, the ship sank, and the anchor rope rose to the surface. The two men grabbed the rope and were tossed by the waves, crying out for help in distress. The sound was unbearable. Their bellies were shaken by the rope, almost splitting open. They finally fell and drowned in the waves on the seventh day of the fourth month.

**Translation:** A Pig from a Family in Fuzhou In Fuzhou, a poor family in Gutian raised a pig. The pig lived in a narrow and confined space, often kept in the bedroom. The family had a baby who was still in swaddling clothes. One day, the mother went to a neighbor's house to drink tea and left the baby in a basket on the bed to sleep. The pig bit and dragged something down, causing the basket to fall to the ground. The pig then bit the baby’s feet up to the thighs and almost ate the baby’s arm. The baby died after a night. --- **A Monkey on a Sea Vessel** In Guangzhou, near the Hai Shan Tower, a merchant ship had a monkey that was very tame. After being kept for a long time, the monkey became familiar with humans. The merchant’s wife gave birth to a baby who was already three or four years old. The monkey often carried the baby, and this became a common occurrence. The family paid little attention to the monkey. One day, the merchant went ashore, and the wife was in the cabin. The monkey picked up the baby and climbed directly to the top of the mast, which was several meters high. Everyone on the ship was shocked and unable to immediately catch the monkey. They arranged sails, sheets, and curtains around the monkey to prevent it from falling into the water. They sent a poleman to climb up and rescue the baby. Just as the poleman was about to reach the monkey, the monkey suddenly let go, causing the baby to fall and die. The merchant killed the monkey and threw it into the sea, filled with deep sorrow and regret. Zhang Zhengshu, a person from Xiangzhou who was taking refuge there, witnessed the event. The author also remembers a story from a novel where a monkey mimicked a person bathing a baby and placed it in a cooking pot. Such behavior should not be treated as normal or acceptable.

Hu Zhonghui was twice recommended for the local examination during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song. He had consulted both a fortune-teller and a respected scholar, and these experiences were recorded in the *Gengzhi* (庚志), but there were still some details left unmentioned. In the autumn of the year Guiyou, he was preparing to take the examination and stayed at a scholar's residence on a lane near the city隍庙 (City God Temple). There, he met a wealthy old man who was sending his son to take the examination and offered to help Hu Zhonghui by giving him three hundred thousand coins. Hu Zhonghui declined, saying, "I am indeed very poor, but my academic resources are limited. What I can do is to illuminate myself; how could I have the time to extend my help to others?" The old man left, but Hu Zhonghui still thought about the tempting offer and was undecided. That night, he dreamed of a man in yellow robes who climbed up to his room and reported the examination results. Hu Zhonghui asked if he had passed or failed, and the man replied, "Your name is on the list, which is why I came to report." Hu Zhonghui was very happy. The man then descended and returned to say, "Although your name is on the list, you must not meddle in other people's affairs." Hu Zhonghui woke up suddenly, thinking about the old man's offer and firmly decided to refuse it. As a result, he was indeed pre-selected for the examination in the autumn of the year Ji Mao, when he took the combined examination for the classics at the prefectural school. Some scholars dreamed that year that the best in the composition of regulated verse would be the one who could match the sun and moon in brightness and impartiality. The theme of the examination was "The Emperor's Virtue Compared to the Sun and Moon," with the phrase "impartial and bright like the sun and moon" as the rhyme. Among the ten candidates who used the word "明" (bright) in the first rhyme, nine were chosen, but Hu Zhonghui alone used the word "私" (private). His first two lines were: "The sage's virtue is lofty, and the heavenly light descends below." The examiner clapped in admiration and praised his work, and Hu Zhonghui was chosen as the top candidate. Thus, it became clear that even a single word or phrase is not predetermined, and how could one allow for private bias?

### 王彦谟妻 In the year 1133, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong, the Vice Minister Liang Qidao temporarily resided at the Miaofu Temple in Poyang. Accompanying him was Wang Yanmo, a military officer, who brought his wife and children to live in the temple. They earned a living by pawning their property to borrow money with interest. They had four sons named Jinqing, Shunqing, Chuqing, and Yueqing. Wang Yanmo's wife was extremely cruel and harsh, employing two maids whom she treated with great severity. The maids were forced to work day and night without rest. Whenever they were seen sleeping, she would press soapberry juice into their eyes, causing them to be unable to see for days. Some advised her, saying, "If the maids have done wrong, they should be beaten, but if they cannot be forgiven, they should be dismissed. It is not right to harm their eyes and ruin their lives forever." However, she did not stop. After Wang Yanmo died, their movable wealth accumulated to over ten thousand strings of cash. The four sons squandered the money without limit, eventually leading to the deaths of Jinqing, Shunqing, and Yueqing. The cruel wife, suffering from eye disease, became blind in both eyes and lay writhing in pain on her bed, moaning with regret. She could not eat or drink regularly, and after ten years, she died. Her fate was clearly a manifestation of retribution. ### 孙知县妻 Outside Dan阳县, ten li away, there lived a county magistrate named Sun. He married a woman from the same county, the daughter of a certain family. She had three younger sisters. Sun's wife was the youngest, and her beauty was unparalleled. She was fond of the plum makeup style, which she wore regardless of the season, dressing in a simple white shirt with a red vertical stripe. Her appearance and demeanor were like those of a figure in a painting. However, every time she bathed, she would set up a heavy curtain to block the view, not allowing any maids to enter, even to help her scrub her back. Sun often asked her why she did this, but she would only smile and not answer. After ten years, she was about thirty years old. One day, while Sun was drunk, he secretly watched her while she bathed. He saw a large white snake piled in a basin, which was terrifying to look at.

### Translation He ran to the study and set up a separate bed, from then on living apart from her. His wife probably already knew, and as soon as she got out of the bath, she went to him and said, "I am not right, and you are also wrong. Do not let any other suspicions arise. Tonight, returning to the bedroom to sleep together will not harm." Sun was very afraid but had no words to refuse, and he ended up sleeping with her again, as close and intimate as before. But in his heart, he was suspicious and fearful, feeling like he was carrying a thorn, turning restlessly and unable to find peace in bed. Sullenly falling ill and dying within a year, it was the year Ding Wei of the Chongxi era. Zhang Sishun, the supervisor of Jiangkou Town in Zhenjiang, was ordered to oversee the county, and this incident was heard by him. This woman lived until the second year of the Qianyuan era, when she was forty years old. ### Zhang Maoxian's Dream Zhang Maoxian, from Xinyu, Linjiang, went to the provincial examination in the year Yi Wei of the Chongxi era and returned home after completing it. Because he passed by a nearby temple and felt slightly uncomfortable, with his head and eyes feeling drowsy, he decided to stay overnight. At the beginning of the night, he dreamed of being led into an official hall where desks were set up, and officials were present, each holding documents and writing judgments. They said he must wait for rain to come, so they led him to stand in the courtyard, and soon the rain started pouring down. Every time it touched him, it turned red like blood, and then he felt cool, with his head feeling heavy and as if horns were growing. Then he was led back into the hall, and officials came one by one, some holding cases of lawsuits, asking him to judge them, hundreds of items in total. There were those with branches and leaves on their heads, each according to their position, consulting and reporting. They also brought branches and leaves, asking for his signature. Some arranged bamboo sticks like counting rods, too many to count, and he signed each one. He was so tired and exhausted that he woke up at the fifth watch of the night. The next day, the list of names was posted, and he was announced as the first. The county official Ding Juyi, who studied under Zhang Fang, later passed the exams and became the magistrate of Gan County. Zhang Sishun, the deputy magistrate, heard this story.

The Dream of Zheng the Magistrate In the year Gengzi of Chunxi (1180), Zheng Jingshi Fang of Putian followed Chen Weigong, a local official, to Jiankang. His son Yue, at the age of six, already showed great promise. The official invited him into the office and found him to be handsome and spirited, understanding books and showing extraordinary talent. He told his father that one day he would be his son-in-law. Zheng Xun thanked him and that was all. By the year Wushen (1188), Zheng was an official in the military headquarters of the governor of Xiang Prefecture. His son, who was accompanying him, suddenly told someone that he had dreamed of Chen Weigong's office. The furnishings were lavish and splendid, and the wives and concubines brought out a girl adorned with jewels and dressed in fine clothes. The attendants supported him and made him bow, and he woke up with a start, wondering what this meant. The girl's sister laughed and said, "Don't think too much about it. Just focus on your studies, and if you pass the exams and get an official position, you can marry into their family." Another three years later, Zheng was an official in the audit office. The youngest son of Chen Weigong told Zheng that he wanted to follow the wishes of the deceased and arrange a marriage with one sister and one nephew. In the year Gengshen (1190), they planned the wedding, consulted a fortune-teller, and found that the nephew's daughter was auspicious. The matchmaker's proposals were not finalized until the year Kui-Chou of Shaoxi (1193), when Yue passed the imperial examination at the age of nineteen. He was appointed as the magistrate of Jian'an, thus fulfilling his wish. The girl's father was a Langzhong of the Ministry of Works.

The Ancestral Home of Chancellor Ye The ancestral home of Chancellor Ye, located in Xianyou County, Xinghua, was inhabited by over a hundred branches of the Ye family in a village. This home was situated at a strategic position, surrounded by mountains that formed a screen and barrier. During the Song Dynasty, a geomancer named Luo Zhengfu traveled to this place and told the residents, "According to the terrain, this place should produce a chancellor, but it is often plagued by flood disasters. It must regain its original vitality over a long period to be considered auspicious according to feng shui principles. Therefore, it took a hundred years for the floods to subside. The local belief is that floods originate from water bursting out of the mountains, creating holes." At that time, Ye Zha was appointed as the magistrate of Shangyu. Fifteen years later, he was promoted to the position of Secretary of State, exactly a hundred years after the floods subsided. Luo Zhengfu's predictions were proven correct, and many scholars sought him out, but he no longer responded to them. The Tomb of the Father of Chen Wei Gong The tomb of the father of Chen Wei Gong was located near a temple in Putian. Originally, it was the burial site of a wealthy family. Twenty-five years after the tomb was built, the descendants of the wealthy family suffered from eye diseases, some becoming blind. A geomancer said, "This problem originated from the tomb. It should be moved urgently. Selling the land to others would be a solution. Otherwise, the situation will worsen day by day and year by year, becoming unsalvageable." The wealthy family was greatly afraid and relocated the tomb, which later became the property of the Chen family. The wealthy family's descendants recovered, and Chen Wei Gong rose to the position of Chancellor and eventually became a Grand Tutor. Thus, the auspiciousness of a burial site depends on certain factors. Without virtue, one will not receive blessings but will suffer misfortune. One must not make reckless hopes or wishes.

**The Ancestral Residence of Chancellor Ye** The ancestral residence of Chancellor Ye Zi’ang was located in Xiangyou County, Xinghua, where over a hundred families of the Ye clan resided in a single village. This residence occupied a strategic and defensible location, surrounded by mountains that stood like a screen and a barrier. A feng shui expert from Shaoxing, Luo Zhengfu, visited the area while traveling and told the inhabitants: “According to the terrain of the hills and mountains, this place should produce a chancellor. However, it is often subject to the calamity of a flood from the mountains. Only when the vital energy has accumulated over a long time as it was before will it conform to the principles of geomancy.” Therefore, the flood was delayed for a hundred years. The term “flood from the mountains” commonly refers to a flood bursting forth from the hills, breaking through and creating a passage. At that time, Ye Zi’ang was the magistrate of Shangyu, and fifteen years later he was appointed as Pushe. Thus, the time between the flood and his appointment as chancellor was exactly a hundred years. Luo Zhengfu’s prediction proved accurate, and upon hearing this, the literati and officials vied to invite him, but he no longer produced any miraculous responses. **The Tomb of the Father of Chen Weigong** The tomb of the father of Chen Weigong was located near the Southern Temple within the borders of Putian. It was originally the burial site of a wealthy man. Within twenty-five years after the burial, his son and grandson suffered from eye diseases, with some becoming completely blind. A skilled practitioner said, “This calamity originates from the tomb. It must be moved urgently. If you sell this site to someone else, it may be resolved. Otherwise, the suffering will grow worse each day and each year, and the disaster will become increasingly severe, to the point of being beyond remedy.” The wealthy man’s son was greatly alarmed and immediately chose a new site for the tomb. The original burial site was then acquired by the family of Chen Weigong. The previously afflicted members of the wealthy family recovered, while Chen Weigong rose to the position of chancellor, eventually attaining the high title of Shao Shi. Thus, the auspiciousness of a burial site is indeed connected to something—without virtue to inherit it, one not only fails to receive blessings but suffers misfortune. One cannot recklessly hope for fortune.

### Translation #### 郑秀才梦 Once, a talented scholar named Zheng from Xinghua went to take the Zhejiang漕试 (漕试) and stayed at a guesthouse. His younger brother, Jingshi, was studying at a school and would visit him every night. One evening, Zheng called out to his brother and said, "I had a dream where I saw many people bustling around, checking a list of successful candidates. They were searching for someone named Zheng Dacheng, but couldn't find him. What could this mean?" The next day, when the results of the examination in Lin'an were announced, Zheng Dacheng's name was indeed on the list. A few nights later, Zheng had another dream where he found himself at a place with a large banner revealing names. He saw that all the names on the list were highlighted in gold, except for one, Li, which was written in gold. That night, the examination results were announced, and Li was declared the top candidate. Zheng did not pass the examination. This dream seemed to have no direct connection to Zheng himself, yet it came true in the mysterious ways of fate. #### 方翥招紫姑 Fang Zhu, from Putian, was preparing to take the乡举 (乡举) in the autumn of the丁巳 (丁巳) year of the Song Dynasty. He could often summon the spirit of Zigu, a deity associated with divination. One day, he asked the spirit a question using a topic. The spirit refused to answer, saying, "Heavenly secrets cannot be divulged." Fang Zhu then lit incense, poured wine, and prayed several times. Finally, the spirit wrote the characters "和" (harmony) and "中" (center). At the time, Fang Zhu was eighteen years old and was studying poetry and prose. He began to search for topics that embodied the essence of harmony and centrality, such as the idea of the emperor establishing harmony as the ultimate goal, or the concept of harmony in the positioning of heaven and earth, and the teaching of rites and music. He also considered the idea that harmony lies in understanding the people's sentiments. He prepared for all possible topics. That year, the candidates were divided into two sessions. The first part of his essay was titled "The Hope for the Revival of the Nation," and the second part was titled "The Benefits of Peace and Harmony with Foreign Nations." It was then that Fang Zhu understood the significance of the spirit's message. He passed the first part of the examination and was selected as the top candidate. I still remember reciting his essay in my youth, which included the following lines: "The eight directions of the earth are opened, symbolizing the crossing of the Yellow River; the six directions of the sky are illuminated, showing the support of five dragons." He waited to see if his fellow officials would shine again, but he had to endure the hardships and tears of the people in the north. The following year, Fang Zhu passed the examination, but after thirty years of struggle, he only reached the position of a low-ranking official in the Secretariat.

The Cost of Wine for Scholar Ruan Wang Yuqing, a scholar from Putian, once was recommended for the imperial examinations and had a dream in which he entered the underworld. Three figures in formal attire sat above him. One called out to Wang as he approached the steps and asked, "Do you remember me? Have we ever shared the same writing tools?" Wang could not recognize him. The figure then said, "Your fate is not far off, so I wish to inform you." Soon, all three figures rose and disappeared behind a screen. Wang looked at a table and saw a book of records. He examined it and found detailed accounts of people's names and their good and bad deeds. One entry read, "Ruan should not rely on others for wine money, for his lifespan will be reduced by half a decade." Wang did not have time to read further when the three figures reappeared and told him, "In three years, we will meet again." As Wang left the corridor, he saw Zhang, a man from the neighborhood, chained and being interrogated. Wang wanted to visit and console him, but the attendant refused. Upon waking, Wang was not happy. He sent his son to visit Zhang's family, and it turned out that Zhang had suffered a stroke the previous night. After two years, Wang realized he would not live much longer and respectfully visited Scholar Ruan. He spoke calmly, asking, "Have you ever been involved in lawsuits?" Ruan replied that he usually avoided lawsuits. Wang pressed further and learned that Ruan had once run a wine shop where someone owed him fifteen thousand coins. The debtor traveled to another place and did not return, so Ruan falsely accused his brother of owing the money and took the case to court. The debtor was arrested and punished, and Ruan eventually paid the full amount. Wang looked solemn and said, "If so, then the underworld has evidence, and I will surely not escape." He gently informed Ruan of what he had seen, and Ruan also regretted his actions. Wang died as expected.

Sun's Elder and Younger Daughters After the death of Sun TiJu, a high-ranking official from WuXing who lived in LinAn, his widow and two sons and five daughters were left alone and weak. The eldest daughter had already passed away, the fourth daughter was adopted by a relative, and the fifth daughter was destitute and wandered to the Wang HouYuan in Yongyang County.
