In the first year of the Qian Dao era, a severe epidemic broke out in the western part of Zhejiang Province, causing the death of Sun's two sons, their wives, and the second and third daughters. The wife feared that the disaster would continue and believed it was due to the inauspicious tomb of the eldest daughter. She sent her close friend, a young man named Wei Er, who is a government official, to New City to burn the coffin. When Wei Er arrived, he informed the old nun at the temple. The nun advised him to stop and said, "This year, the epidemic is rampant, and who's not affected? The eldest daughter has been buried for several years and is now at peace. How could we harm her bones without reason and stir up her resentment in the afterlife?" Wei Er replied, "I have no heart for this, but I am following the command of the lady. I cannot return empty-handed." The nun closed the door and refused to let him in, but he could not be stopped. She then said, "Wait until tomorrow morning, and I will explain." Wei Er did not understand what she meant but agreed and left. This daughter, after her burial, often came out at night to the nun's room to ask questions and receive answers, listening to her recite scriptures. On this night, she also came to the nun's room.

**Madam Sun** After Inspector Sun of Wu Xing, who resided in Lin'an, passed away, his wife and two sons, along with five daughters, were left in a vulnerable and isolated state. All the daughters were of beautiful appearance. The eldest daughter died first; the fourth daughter was raised by a relative of the same clan, and the fifth daughter had fallen into obscurity in the Wang Hòuyuàn of Yong County. In the first year of the Qiandao era, a great epidemic broke out in western Zhejiang, and both of Sun’s sons, their wives, and the second and third daughters died. His wife, fearing that the calamity would not cease, believed it was caused by the inauspiciousness of the elder daughter’s burial. She therefore sent her close associate, the young gentleman Wei Er, to Xinshi to burn the coffin. When Wei arrived, he informed the old nun who guarded the nunnery. The nun advised him to stop, saying: "This year, the epidemic is raging fiercely; who among us has not suffered the loss of family members? Madam Sun has been buried for several years and has found peace. How could we, without cause, desecrate her decayed bones and awaken the grievances of the dead below?" Wei replied: "I have no ill intent; I am merely obeying Lady’s command. How could I return empty-handed?" The nun closed the door and resisted repeatedly, but he could not prevent her. She then said, "Wait, I will explain. Come back tomorrow, and I will tell you." Wei did not understand her words, but he tentatively agreed, saying "Yes," and left. This daughter had, since her burial, often come out at night to the nun’s room, exchanging greetings and conversation, listening to her recite scriptures, and only returning after the third or fourth watch. That night, too, she came to the nun.

She was informed, "There is a karmic connection that is not aligned. Do you know about it?" The woman replied, "I certainly know about it. Please tell Wei Er for me." She said, "The disaster and misfortune in my family are due to fate. It is not caused by my tomb. I hope you will go back and tell my mother to stop this work. If she does not move the burial pit, she will protect her two younger sisters. Teach and cultivate peace." The nun agreed to it. The next day, she told Wei in detail. Wei laughed but did not believe, saying, "How could there be such a thing? You are fabricating this to scare me." He immediately called workers and servants to put effort into it. The nun asked for one more night. Just as night fell, the woman arrived and said, "Wei Er did not listen to my words but let him do as he pleased." Wei finally went to that place and dug up the tomb, chopping at the coffin and lifting the lid. The woman suddenly sat up, her appearance as if she were alive, looking at Wei intently and laughing loudly. Wei was frightened and collapsed. After a long time, he gradually recovered, quickly burned incense to apologize, and covered it again. The sick person in the Sun family also recovered. Yuan Shan, the official in charge of the relay station in Raozhou, lived next to the Sun family, therefore, he obtained a detailed account of the beginning and end. He still regrets that the old nun and the woman interacted for many years, without probing the reasons why, and what was seen and heard in the dark. Whether she has come out again since then.

**Translation:** **Huang Huizhou** Huang Minzhan, a court official of the rank of Zhaoyin (朝请大夫), was from Xinfeng, Ganzhou. After passing the imperial examinations, he often served in the southern regions, holding positions such as magistrate of Chaoyang, deputy governor of Xunzhou, and governor of Gaizhou. Due to mourning the death of his mother, he resigned and was later appointed governor of Huizhou. In the first year of the Shaoxi era (绍熙元年), during the leap month of the second lunar month, while he was with his wife in the main hall of their residence, he suddenly burst into anger and shouted at those below, "Go away!" His wife was startled and asked him what was wrong. He replied, "A yellow-robed subordinate official dared to enter the main hall of our residence, holding a piece of paper and wanting to present it to me. So I shouted at him to leave, but I regret that I did not have someone catch him. How could you not see?" His wife dared not speak further, but she was deeply worried. On the twenty-ninth day of that month, he fell ill in the evening. On the first day of the third month, his illness improved slightly, and he was able to get up and walk, asking for food. A few days later, he called an old soldier and said, "Manage the official funds, arrange them neatly, and I will take charge of the task myself." Those who heard this were shocked and whispered among themselves, saying that the prefect was on leave, so no one had brought the money or goods. He dared not argue, but said, "The money is not much, and the treasury official has already taken it." Because of this, his illness flared up again. On the ninth day, he suddenly looked outside and shouted loudly, standing up angrily, as if holding or grasping something. When people asked him what was wrong, he said, "The previous time, the subordinate official came again, bringing the same document and urging me to leave early. It's just too much to bear!" His wife knew that what he saw was an omen of misfortune and gathered doctors and shamans to treat him. His condition worsened, and after two months, he died. Initially, Huang had been guarding his mother's coffin on its way back to his hometown from Gaizhou, passing through the Gan River. The boat hit a rock, split apart, and the coffin sank into the water. Huang had only one son who rushed into the swift current to save him, but he too drowned. The bodies of both the deceased and his son were never found, and the tragedy was shocking. At this point, his grandson was only six years old.

The Fishermen of the Freshwater Yuan Shanyu once oversaw the salt field at the Freshwater area in Huizhou, which was near the sea and had several hundred households of fishermen. He observed that catching sea creatures was extremely hard work. Abalone has only one shell to protect itself. Fishermen would row their boats to where the abalone was produced, tie a hemp rope around their waists, and fasten one end to the stern. They would then dive into the water, sometimes reaching depths of fifty to sixty zhang. If they didn't notice, they could pick them up. If they knew someone was coming, the abalone would stick to the rocks and could not be pulled off. Even if they were hit with a hammer until they broke, they still stuck. According to the *Book of the Later Han*, *Biography of Fu Long*, Zhang Bu presented abalone. Guo Pu's annotation in *San Cang* says abalone is like a clam and sticks to the rock. *Guang Zhi* also says abalone has a shell and one side is attached to the rock with fine holes, sometimes seven or nine, which agrees with this statement. Like sea snails and clams, which are very easy to catch, when they want to come out of the water, they climb up by holding onto the rope and stepping with their feet. Sometimes they don't come out for a long time, and bubbles form on the surface of the water. Their wives and children in the boat see this and all cry out in sorrow, it seems they have been taken by a big fish. Those who encounter this often happen.

The *Yijian Zhi* (Wujuan II) Imperially Approved Complete Library The *Yijian Zhi* (Wujuan III) By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Cheng Jun and the Snake Cheng Jun, a military officer of the rank of Wu Gong (Martial Doctor), was stationed in Jiankang as a deputy commander of the central army. He was skilled in exorcism and particularly adept at dealing with snakes. In the 23rd year of the Shaoxing era, several snakes began appearing at night from the bamboo grove near the Four-View Pavilion outside the city gates. These snakes were three feet long, with faces as wide as a pestle, four legs, and covered in fur. They made sounds like pigs and moved swiftly, posing a threat to people. The crowd was alarmed and did not know what to do. Fortunately, there was a horse trough nearby, which they quickly used to cover the snakes. The white commander then sent for Cheng Jun, who arrived and was able to describe the snakes' appearance and warned that they were known as pig-tailed snakes, which could kill a person with a single bite. Cheng Jun immediately performed a ritual involving walking in a specific pattern and manipulating qi to subdue the snakes. After a short while, he ordered the trough to be uncovered, and the snakes were found to be paralyzed and unable to move. He covered them again, allowing them to absorb the sunlight, and then blew three times over the trough. When he uncovered it again, the snakes had transformed into a solid mass of blood. There was also a strange python in Paiwan Mountain, which was deep blue in color and about twenty feet long.

Residents who are harmed by something invite Mr. Jun to perform a ritual. Mr. Jun says, "According to my method, it is not appropriate to act rashly; instead, prepare a formal petition." Once the petition is ready, they submit it to heaven. At dawn, they go to the entrance of the cave and set up an altar, wearing a Taoist priest's robe, barefoot, and with hair loose. They shout at the empty space to summon the divine generals, saying, "Summon the snake quickly; if it does not appear, send two more generals, repeating this process several times." The snake rushes out from the cave with great speed, as if it is about to fight. Mr. Jun loudly rebukes it, saying, "You wicked beast, how dare you be disrespectful?" He then takes his sweatshirt and tears it into pieces. The snake splits into two, and the trouble is thus resolved. A child from a local family, while walking in the grass, is stung by a venomous snake, nearly dying. Mr. Jun goes to treat the child and asks, "Did you accidentally step on it, causing it to bite you? Did you approach it closely?" The child replies, "I did not touch it; I did not feel it bite me." Mr. Jun says, "I have known this for a long time; it has no reason to harm people." He adds, "It cannot be forgiven; clear an area of about a meter and insert small bamboo strips as swords, then perform a ritual to summon the snakes." He commands them, "Those who have committed wrongs must stay under the swords; otherwise, they must leave." The snakes leave one by one, each going to its respective place. Only a small snake, colored like earth, remains near the sword. Mr. Jun calls the judge to check the law, saying, "What punishment should be given to a snake that harms people without cause?" The people gathered around cannot see it; after a long time, he says, "According to the law, the snake will die by touching the sword with its head." Mr. Jun's skill is like this, and he does not seek anything from people. The doctor Liu Dayong wants to learn this skill; Mr. Jun says, "This is not something to be stingy about, but I fear that if you handle it carelessly, you may violate the law and bring disaster upon yourself, so I will not teach you." Mr. Jing Chen's younger brother says that in the village, there was once a snake that was fat and short, unable to move gracefully, so it would only rush forward and attack people, making it impossible to survive if one was bitten. This was likely a type of venomous snake.

A White-Clothed Man from Chizhou Li Miao was a courtesan from Chizhou. In the sixth year of the Chunxi era (1179 CE), a white-clad man visited her home, drank wine, and asked to stay overnight. They got along very well, and this continued for more than three months. Li Miao, due to her mother’s precautions, asked the man for an object. The man said, “Yes, I will go home to get it. I will bring it to you tomorrow.” Li Miao then ordered her servant Yong Ji to follow the man. The man refused, saying, “I have been here for a long time, and my family does not know. I do not wish to reveal my destination. If Yong Ji follows me, it might cause trouble for me.” Li Miao and her mother thought he was lying and finally ordered Yong Ji to follow him. Gradually, they left the city gate to the west and arrived at the Temple of Sanlang. The man told Yong Ji, “Turn around, there is a relative calling you.” Yong Ji turned back but saw no one. He looked forward again and saw a large white snake rushing towards the grassy hill. He was frightened and trembling, almost falling, and returned to tell Li Miao. Both Li Miao and Yong Ji fell gravely ill. After a year, they recovered, but Li Miao’s complexion had become pale and weak, and she no longer resembled her former self. The local authorities allowed her to be deregistered and live freely afterward, and she was later sold to a dye shop as a concubine. Chen’s Ghostly Ailment Seventy miles south of Shaoguan is called Guti. There was a wealthy woman named Chen who suffered from a strange illness. On ordinary days, she had no other discomfort, but whenever a cold wind blew, she would experience an unusual itch between her thighs, which she would scratch incessantly. Soon, the entire body would feel the same way, and she would lose consciousness. This would last for three days before she regained consciousness. When she sat up, she would make a sound like coughing, and her body would move forward and backward as if swaying. This usually happened dozens of times. Just as it slightly calmed down, she would fall into a deep sleep and lose consciousness again. This cycle would repeat for several nights until she finally recovered. She became so afraid that she dared not leave her house. Ten doctors tried to cure her, but none succeeded. Doctor Liu Dayong examined her and said, “I have already diagnosed her condition.” He first gave her a dose of medicine and then ordered to bring a string of prayer beads. The patient’s family did not know why he needed the beads. When the woman was swaying, the doctor noted the rhythm of the beads’ movement and said, “This is the name of the illness.”

A ghostly ailment occurs when one enters a temple to observe and play, and is then possessed by an evil spirit, leading to a disturbed spirit. The method is to use a pillow made from a dead person's head, boil it to make a decoction, and drink it. After drinking, one immediately has several large bowel movements, and the long-standing illness suddenly disappears. Liu Dayong said that after using the pillow, it should be returned to its original place. If it is delayed, it can cause madness. It only borrows its qi (vital energy). I recall in the *Nan Shi* (History of the Southern Dynasties), there is a record of Xu Sibo, who was skilled in medicine. There was an elderly woman suffering from stagnation and coldness. He diagnosed her condition as a corpse-induced ailment. Zhang Jing had abdominal distension and a yellow complexion, and he said it was a stone worm. Shen Sengyi saw ghostly apparitions due to pain and said that evil qi had entered the liver. He instructed them to boil and drink the pillow made from a dead person's head. Finally, it was buried in its original place. Wang Jing asked him, "Why did three different illnesses all improve with the same treatment?" He answered in detail, and Wang Jing deeply admired its miraculous efficacy. This is the same kind of case. The son of Wei Chengwu from Ningguo was a wealthy man, but his only son was young, fond of pleasure, and became ill, becoming emaciated. Many doctors treated him for consumption (tuberculosis), but after three years, the condition worsened. Just as Liu Dayong passed through the county, he was invited to examine him and also diagnosed it as consumption. After taking medicine for over a month, there was no improvement. He asked about the cause of the illness, and after a long time, the patient finally admitted. He said that in June, he drank with a prostitute, got into an argument, became drunk, and fell asleep on a black stand. When he woke up a bit and felt thirsty, he couldn't find water, but there was a clean water pot with water from a water chestnut plant, which he drank. From then on, he fell ill. Liu Dayong was secretly pleased and secretly sent a servant to dig up mud from the fields, wash it with water, and take the clear juice in two bowls to administer.

A command was issued to drink freely, and Wei Zi, who had long been suffering from his illness, did not find this objectionable. He drank it all at once. Soon, his intestines began to churn and prickle, and after a long time, the pain subsided. He then took a hundred grains of purgative medicine, which immediately caused a thorough evacuation, expelling more than sixty leeches. He felt a sense of relief, and Liu said, "This must have been mistakenly swallowed from the basin. Leeches enter the human body, feeding on fat and blood, nourishing and multiplying. They often stick tightly to the five organs, making them impossible to remove. However, after being away from the dirty ditch for a long time, they think of what they desire. It is only by using this substance that they can be gathered." Although Wei Zi's illness was cured, he remained weak and frail. Additional treatments were applied, and it took eighty days for him to fully recover. I recently remembered the case of Zhang Rui treating Wu Shao Shi, which was remarkably similar. --- Cai, the county magistrate, treated a case of inch-long white worms. Cai Kang, the son of Cai Kan, had long suffered from these worms. The physician instructed him to grind Areca catechu into fine powder, take the root of the pomegranate that grows eastward, boil it into a decoction, and consume it. First, roast a large piece of fatty pork, put it in the mouth, and chew the fat and oil, but do not eat it. This worm only has its head facing up before the third day of the month, when it can be treated with medicine. On other days, even if there is medicine, it is of no use. The worm hears the smell of meat and begins to suck and eat, so it will rush to it. He felt as if ten thousand arrows were piercing his chest, which was the moment to take the previous medicine. Cai followed all the instructions, and within two hours, his abdomen roared like thunder. He hurried to the toilet, and the worms came out in a continuous string, growing several zhang long, still moving slightly and able to move. They were thrown into the stream, and the long-standing illness was immediately cured. This formula is also recorded in Yang's Collection of Verified Cases. Cai, who was stationed in Lin'an, spoke to Qian Zhongben, wishing to spread this remedy to benefit future generations.

**The Iron Broom** In Lin'an, there was a fortune-teller whose name has been lost. He often wore Daoist robes and had a signboard that read "Iron Broom." He set up his shop near the walls of the government offices, and many people came to him for divination. Although his predictions sometimes came true, he was not taken seriously by the elite officials. In the year 1184, a commoner came to ask about his fate. The fortune-teller told him, "Your star chart is very bad, and you must be wary of the beginning of spring next year. You are at risk of being punished by law or execution. If you can stay isolated for a month and not interact with people, perhaps you can avoid it." The commoner was unhappy but followed the advice. On the last day of the month, he could no longer bear the isolation and went to the fortune-teller to rebuke him, saying, "You said I would suffer a great disaster in January, and now it is the thirtieth day. I have not broken any laws. How could I have encountered this disaster?" The fortune-teller admitted, "I made a mistake and forgot what I said. Let me re-evaluate the situation." As he was setting up his tools, he said, "Today is still January, and there is still cause for concern." The commoner became angry and accused the fortune-teller of being absurd, and they argued and cursed each other endlessly. Unable to control his anger, the commoner threatened, "I will beat you to death to test your predictions." He kicked the fortune-teller in the side, and the fortune-teller died instantly. The commoner was then captured and received a reduced sentence of branding and exile. Such stories are sometimes found in historical records.

Zhang Zizhi's Destruction of the Temple During the years of the reign of Emperor Ling in the Song Dynasty, when Zhang Zizhi was the governor of Changzhou, a severe epidemic broke out in the spring and summer of 1195. Nine out of ten households were affected by the disease. Zhang prepared a lot of good medicine and distributed it among the neighborhoods, but very few people came to ask for it. He was quite puzzled and consulted the local scholars, who told him that in this area, behind the Dongyue temple, there was a hall where people worshiped the plague god, and four witches held power over the disease. All those who were ill were required to come and pray, and they were forbidden to take medicine. Therefore, even though the government provided medicine, people did not dare to ask for it. Zhang felt deeply dissatisfied. On another day, he visited the Dongyue temple, where the courtyard was quiet and the incense was sparse. He asked about the location of the plague temple, and the officials said they would surely pay homage, but Zhang ordered all the incense and cushions to be removed. At that time, elderly women and children gathered around him, and when he saw the magistrate coming, they crowded around him, looking at him. Zhang pointed to something in the middle.

A dignitary asked, "What is this deity?" The shaman replied, "It is the Lord Tai Sui." He then pointed to several statues on either side, some holding their feet, others glaring, and still others raising their hands. "What are these?" the dignitary asked. "These are the gods of plague," the shaman answered. The dignitary said, "Humans and gods are one and the same. There should be proper etiquette and decorum, regardless of status. Now that we have revered Tai Sui as the most honored, those with crowns and robes sitting in the center while others serve them on the side have clearly acted improperly. Where is the moral justification for this?" He immediately arrested the four shamans and brought them back to his residence. He then selected twenty strong soldiers, gave them wine to drink, and sent them to break the statues and distribute the ritual objects among the temples. After the burning of incense at the Jianshi Temple, there was no Buddha hall, so he ordered the dismantling of the building and gave it to the monks to rebuild. He cleared the area and drove the shamans out. The ignorant masses thought he might bring strange punishment upon them, but the people's illness increasingly recovered, and the customs were slightly reformed. He was eventually summoned to serve as the Minister of Personnel. --- Qian Linzong Qian Yangzhi Linzong lived in the Northern Monastery of the Pure Abode Temple in Huating. He was a man of great integrity. One day, hundreds of snakes appeared in the rooms, even in the pots and pans during cooking, but Qian did not consider it strange. He simply ordered his servant to drive them away. He then walked to the monk's hall and saw a newly placed divine image, a statue of a snake that the common people worshipped as a bodhisattva. The local people revered this demon so much that they did not dare to mention its name, and even the left side of the square characters on the statue were carefully avoided. The temple monk wanted to take advantage of this situation to establish a shrine and lure the people to pray and offer sacrifices to seek profit. Qian realized the cause of the strange occurrences and immediately took an axe and hammer to break the statue and throw it into the water. That night, the monk fled with a bag. From then on, the Qian family lived peacefully in the Northern Monastery. Day by day, the place became desolate, almost no human traces remained, but only the Qian descendants still lived there. The snake had been a plague for a long time, but this year it slightly calmed down. The Geng Record also mentions this matter.

The Witch of Jinshan Temple The Jinshan Temple in Huating, by the sea, is the shrine of General Huo of Han. It is said that during the time when Qian Wusu dominated Wu and Yue, he often received assistance from invisible soldiers, hence the temple was grandly constructed. In the later part of the Chunxi era, people from the county gathered at the temple during festivals. One day, a witch was burning incense and praying, reciting blessings and curses. Shen Hui, the head of the temple, did not believe in the witch and mocked and insulted her. Those who were friends with Shen Hui tried to stop him, fearing that he would bring disaster upon himself. The witch loudly declared and scolded Shen Hui severely. Shen Hui was arguing with her when he suddenly fell down, foaming at the mouth, as if he had fainted. His servants rushed to tell his wife and children, who came to see and bowed to the witch, begging for his life. The witch said, "It is too late to repent. The gods are already very angry. His spirit has been captured and sent to Fengdu, and he will die in an instant, and there is no way to save him." Shen Hui's wife and children were at a loss, just patting his body and crying over him. Shen Hui suddenly stood up, and the bystanders scattered in fear, saying that he was driven by a powerful spirit. Shen Hui laughed and said, "I was just joking with everyone. I didn't see anything." The witch was startled and secretly left, and all the people in the temple also left.

Mute Money Xunzhi In the year Gengchen of the Shaoxing era, Qian Darenkan, who served as the governor of the grain transport from Chengdu, passed away. His son, Qian Xunzhi, around thirty years old, suddenly became mute and unable to speak, but his daily routines and eating were exactly the same as before. He suffered from distress and boredom for eight years. One day, an incident occurred where he was angered by a concubine and struck her with a stick. The concubine ran away, and he scolded her as a mute animal. Qian Xunzhi became even more depressed and shouted, "Look at the mute animal beating people!" He continued to scold without stopping. The family heard his voice and were surprised to see him speaking again. From then on, he spoke as before. Qian Xunzhi believed that his illness was cured by the incident with the concubine and chose to forgive her and reward her. Later, he passed the government examination and was appointed as the magistrate of Jiangyin, but he did not get to take up the position and died. A Small House in Li Lane In the northern part of Raozhou City, there is a small house in Li Langzhong Lane. It has always been dominated by ghosts, and previous residents could not stay there comfortably. Every time they hosted important guests and prepared banquets, strange and unusual forms appeared and sat down to eat and drink. During the Shaoxing era, the house was owned by two families, the Liang and Guan clans. Finally, it was occupied by Judge Dong Yi. After Dong Yi's death, his son sold it to his eastern neighbor, Officer Wang Jiguang. Wang Jiguang was brave and fearless of ghosts and spirits. At the beginning of his ownership, he sent a servant to guard the house overnight, but the servant was troubled by the ghosts and could not sleep all night. The next night, he replaced the servant with two soldiers, but the same thing happened. Wang Jiguang did not deeply believe in the reports and personally went to investigate. He shouted loudly, "I have heard that this place is haunted by many ghosts. If there are any, come out!"

A ghostly sound appeared, and for a short while, it rustled like a broom sweeping dust off the wall, but the dust did not move. Soon after, there was the sound of galloping horses. Wang said, "You only create such a small disturbance; what is there to be afraid of? You must show yourself to me." The entity emerged from the shade of willow trees and stood still without moving. Wang approached and said to it, "If you are the spirit of someone who died violently and has been dead for a long time, it is difficult to find you. Why don't you find your own place of rebirth? If you want, you can ask for offerings of wine and food, but there is no money available. If you persist, I will have sorcerers punish you and send you to the Eastern Peak of Mount Tai and the City of Foul Odors. Do not regret your possessions, for they will disappear with these words, and the house will then be peaceful." 独脚莲 In the mountainous region of Poyang, there grows a type of grass that, when it first sprouts, resembles a lotus bud. It is commonly called "独脚莲" (one-legged lotus). When planted in the gaps of residential areas or gardens, snakes do not dare to pass beneath it. In the backyard of Wang Jiguang's home, there was a thicket where a snake lived, and it often came out to harm people. Wang then planted several of these grasses outside the hole. From then on, the problem ceased. During the hot summer months, a foul smell was detected inside the hole. The gardener dug the soil and found several snakes, likely affected by the grass's scent. Another small snake came from elsewhere and, upon reaching the grass, immediately turned into water. Its efficacy was as such. --- This translation aims to capture the supernatural and ghostly atmosphere of the original text while maintaining clarity and readability in English. It also preserves the cultural and historical context, including the references to the "独脚莲" plant and the mythological places of Mount Tai and the City of Foul Odors.

The Supervisor Li Xing A certain military personnel named Li Xing, due to meritorious service, was released from his military duties and appointed to the position of supervisor in Quanzhou. On his way to take up his new post, he sent his wife and children to travel by land while he himself set off first from Lin'an. Li Xing gathered and made up for the delay in the evening, then rode a horse to catch up on the road. He reached the foot of Long Mountain, where he was blocked by thousands of common people and managed to pass through. That day, a pirate was executed, and after being dismembered and decapitated, his head was displayed. Li Xing scolded the onlookers and said, "This is a vicious criminal who harms the good people. Even a thousand cuts would not be enough to punish his evil deeds. Why do you all stare at him?" Then he kicked the remains and spat on them, cursing them. Blood stained his shoes, but he paid no heed; the crowd gradually dispersed. Li Xing suddenly felt confused and disoriented, not knowing east from west, as if he had lost his mind, and was possessed by a ghost. He got on his horse and returned to the city to stay in a small inn. A servant worried that the family would wait for news and wanted to go report, but Li Xing did not listen. From then on, his madness grew worse each day. He attacked anyone he met. People saw that he was strong and powerful, so they avoided him and did not dare to fight. He even went to the inner city gate, bare-chested and with disheveled hair, saying he wanted to see the imperial family and cry out his grievances. The guards knew he was ill and thought of his former comrades, so they only helped him out. An old friend who lived in Guan Alley forcibly took him back, locked him in a room, and secretly brought him food through a hole in the wall, regardless of how much there was, none was left over. Sometimes he would forget to eat for days and act as if nothing was wrong, shouting and jumping around, greatly disturbing others. After many nights, he finally hanged himself to death. His family almost reached Fujian before they received the news, and they rushed back in a hurry. It was said that the ghost of the pirate was causing this misfortune. Zichan said that even common people who die violently can still haunt and harm others, which is exactly what this is about.

*Yijian Zhi* Section Wu, Volume Three *Qinding Siku Quanshu* *Yijian Zhi* Section Wu, Volume Four Southern Song Dynasty, Hong Mai, compiled Wu Yunlang In a village twenty miles outside Wujiang County, there was a wealthy man named Wu Ze who was about to enter official service. He had a son, whom he nicknamed Yunlang. From a young age, Yunlang was inclined towards learning and had once taken the imperial examination and was registered as a candidate. In the eighth month of the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, Yunlang died due to illness. His parents deeply mourned and felt a deep sense of loss. The following winter, Wu Ze's brother, a tutor named Wu Zi, was traveling to the Dongshan area of Dongting Lake to visit his wife's family. Not far from his destination, a sudden storm hit the boat, and they temporarily anchored at the temple of the Benevolent King. Wu Zi got off the boat and walked around, looking at the half-closed temple gate. He saw Yunlang, dressed in a black silk robe, walking slowly out of the temple. Wu Zi was greatly shocked and approached him, saying, "Your parents have been thinking of you day and night. They could not see you once. Why are you here?" Yunlang replied, "I have a matter that binds me, making me linger. The situation is extremely bitter. Please tell my uncles about this for my parents. If you want to see me, you must come in person." Wu Zi sighed and left. He quickly returned home and told his brother and sister-in-law. They all held each other and cried. The three of them got back on the original boat and returned to the temple. Yunlang was already standing by the riverbank. They ran to their father.

The mother knelt before her father, wept, and recounted the hardships and sufferings of her time in captivity. Before she finished speaking, she suddenly glared at him with furious eyes, grabbed his clothes, and shouted loudly, "You protected my life, but you stole my gold and silk, making me suffer silently with grievances and pain for forty to fifty years. Today, I will not let you go." They then fought each other fiercely and rolled into the water. The servants and boatmen waded into the water to save her, and she was finally pulled out. She lay on the shore, on the verge of death. Initially, no one saw anything; they only saw Ze raise her hand to fight. It was not until evening that the situation was settled. Ze did not know that Ze had a hidden malice. She asked him, and he replied with a frowning expression, "When the enemy cavalry broke through the city, a young man came to seek refuge and stayed. He carried a lot of money in his bag. I coveted his wealth. After several months, I killed him and took the money. I realized that I had a debt of guilt on my conscience. From youth to old age, I was always filled with sorrow. This child was born in the year Renwu. Isn't this the reason?" Since then, she was in a state of depression and stopped eating, and she died after ten days. Hui Nanfu, the son of the Chancellor, was like my cousin Gao. Li, who married into the Wu family, told Wei about this.

**Moral Transformation of Fierce Beasts** In the first year of the Jingyuan era, on the twenty-fourth day of the fifth month, a heavy rain fell over Jiujiang for five consecutive days and nights, causing the river to overflow and all chickens, dogs, and livestock to be swept away. A fruit seller named Huang Er was working in the fields of Dehua County when he encountered a fierce beast approaching along the road. He immediately climbed a tall tree to avoid it. Another woman was passing by with her two children, playing cautiously as usual. The beast remained still. Huang, despite his fear, looked down and found the scene very unusual. Suddenly, a group of more than ten soldiers arrived, sounding gongs and drums. They came with spears and forks, intending to drive away the beast. Only then did the beast leave, crossing the water to the west. Huang finally dared to come down from the tree and reached the city gate, where the woman had already been waiting. She bought ten rice dumplings from a vendor and fed them to her children, walking quickly away. Her eyes were fixed and appeared very menacing. She crossed the river while holding the hem of her robe as if walking on flat ground, and later, no one knew where she went. People suspected she was a tiger spirit, as mentioned in the previous text about the Yangtai. **Shanjian Becomes a Monk** In the fourth year of the Chongxi era, Zhang Zizheng, an official serving as the governor of Taizhou, prayed to the Buddha with burning incense because his wife was ill. He wished to ordain a monk. After his wife recovered, he posted a notice in various temples, instructing all registered novices to gather at the Baoren Guangxiao Temple on the fifteenth day of the fourth month during the summer solstice. Shanjian, a monk from Xiu State, greatly desired to visit the temples. He crossed the river from Jiangyin to Shizhuang and stayed overnight at the Mingxi Zen Temple. Suddenly, his feet became heavy and swollen like stones, preventing him from moving forward. The temple monks told him it was the tenth day, and although he wanted to continue, his physical strength was not sufficient. That night, he dreamed of a temple deity urging him to leave. In the morning, after breakfast, he reluctantly left from Rugao County and headed north toward the tenth day.

After four nights, I arrived at the city of the prefecture and visited the Baoren Temple, where the pain in my thigh had become more severe. I laid down in the monks' selection room. The next day, Zhang and his wife came, and all the officials prepared for the斋供 (a ritual offering). I watched everyone draw lots. At that time, there were more than five hundred people guarding, arranged to stand in the hall. Zhang asked if there were any who had not yet arrived, and answered with 善鉴 (a good mirror), also saying that the road had damaged his feet, and now he was lying sick. Zhang insisted that he come. Jian could not refuse and went with a staff, joining the crowd to pick a small paper scroll. When opened, only Jian received it. On that day, he shaved his head. Zhang made three sets of clothing for him, treating him equally. All raised sighs of admiration. After ten days, Nan Chan Temple lacked a master. Zhang wrote a letter inviting him to take charge of the main lecture hall. Thus, he served in three major temples, highly regarded in the Huai region. Zhang cooked crabs. A commoner named Zhang from Pingjiang sold cooked crabs to support himself. The number of crabs he killed was in the tens of millions. In July of the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, he bought two baskets and placed them in the room, containing hundreds of crabs. At night, he heard the sound of ducks. His father and son held torches and searched but saw nothing. When they returned to sleep, the sound resumed. Carefully listening, they realized it was inside the basket. Then they stood up and scolded it. The crab spoke in human language, saying, "Just died after midnight." Then they felt someone wearing wooden clogs walking around, thinking it was a thief. They ran to inform the neighbors, wanting to arrest, but there was silence and no trace. His daughter, Wujin, was frightened and fell ill, lying in bed. Three days later, she heard someone outside calling, "Wujin, you can go together." She responded, "Wait for me," and died at night. Nine days later, Zhang's wife also fell ill and saw the daughter sitting under the bed. She called her to come up, and then Zhang, his father, and his wife died one after another. Only left a small daughter named A Gan. No one to raise her, a relative named Zhou Er took her to his home. She saw her parents come and call, and she also died. The Zhang family line ended.

In the Huangchi Town, which is part of Taiping State, to the east lies the border of Xuancheng County. Within ten miles, there are settlements inhabited by ruffians, bad elements, and rebellious members of the royal family who gather to slaughter cattle and dogs, brew private alcohol, mint counterfeit coins, and produce counterfeit paper money. They engage in all kinds of illegal and harmful activities. In the 13th year of the Chunxi era during the Southern Song Dynasty (1186 AD), Wang Yuanqing and his cousin Sheng Guozhe traveled together to Ningguo Prefecture. Passing through the area, they saw five water buffaloes tied up in a wild garden. Sheng Guozhe pointed to the second buffalo and said, "This one will die tomorrow." Wang Yuanqing asked, "How do you know?" Sheng Guozhe replied, "The other four are eating grass, but this one has tears in its eyes and is not eating. I asked the people in the tea house, and they said it was bought by Zhao San Shi, who plans to slaughter it at dawn." Indeed, the next day, the buffalo died as predicted. They went back to see, and the fourth buffalo also had the same behavior from the previous day. It looked at the two men, bowed its hooves, and knelt down as if pleading. They asked the people in the tea house again, and they said that a customer had come early that morning and bought three of them, leaving only one for slaughter that night. Sheng Guozhe advised Wang Yuanqing to buy the buffaloes, and they placed them in a nearby village to wait for their death. Wang Yuanqing immediately visited the owner, paid a fair price, and brought the buffaloes back home, where they still exist today.

**Judge Pian of Hui Prefecture** In the first year of the Chunxi era (1174), Judge Pian Shu of Jiaxing embarked on a journey to serve as the magistrate of Huangmei County in Qizhou. He sailed on the Yangtze River, passing through Qikou, and stayed there that night. A fierce wind uprooted trees, and his boat was shattered by huge waves, leaving only the bottom plank intact. Pian happened to be on it, holding on and floating, thinking that all his family had drowned. He waited until dawn when the wind slightly calmed and looked to see that everyone was huddled on the plank, not a single person missing. All bags, servants, and attendants were lost, but a small box containing his imperial edicts remained unharmed. He then called a fisherman's boat to take them to the south bank. The locals saw them and came to comfort them, saying, "Last night, in the darkness of wind and thunder, we heard a divine voice saying, 'Save the entire family of the Pian magistrate.' Isn't this the magistrate Pian?" Pian was known for being honest, upright, and strict, and he was highly regarded in his hometown. Therefore, despite suffering a great disaster, he was saved by divine power. After his term ended, he was indeed appointed as the magistrate of Taizhou. **Monk Ruben of Fujian** Monk Ruben of Fujian, originally from Fuzhou, arrived at Shizhuang Town in Gaoyang County and decided to stay there. In the year 1151 during the Southern Song Dynasty, the Jin army invaded the Huai region. Ruben collected and buried 300 remains and received an official document from the government. In the first year of the Shaosheng era (1190), he became the head monk and went with the other monks to Huanghuagang to perform Buddhist rituals. He left only one old man to guard the temple. At noon, a fire broke out in the Yan Shou Hall and then spread to the monks' hall, turning all to ashes. The monks heard the news and fled in panic. Usually, the monks' vows, ordination documents, and other items were locked in the altar cabinet on the meditation bed, but all were burned and destroyed. Only Ruben's were hung on the beam. When they fell, a large tile covered and protected them, suffering no damage. Ruben was very strict in his conduct, simple, and kind-hearted, reciting scriptures and mantras day and night without ceasing. Therefore, he received good fortune.

A Woman from the Sun Family in Poyang During the month of June in the year Gengxu of the Shaoxing era, a woman from the Wang family in the Sun district of Poyang died from illness. Her father had gone to a neighboring village, and since the body was still warm, the family could not bear to bury her. They sent a servant to call her father back to stay for another night. When he returned, half-drunk, he patted the body and wept loudly. Soon after, the deceased revived. That day, they invited monks from Lotus Temple to recite scriptures and asked the woman about what she had seen. She stood up and said, "I arrived at the bridge of the underworld and saw my old mother. She asked, 'Why are you here?' I replied, 'This is the path of death; you cannot stay here long.' She then bought paper and asked someone to write a statement, which we presented together. The official in purple robes, sitting at the desk making decisions, wrote, 'The person has been filial and respectful to her in-laws, and her life span is not yet complete, so she should be allowed to return.' We crossed the bridge again. Hungry and unable to bear it, she wanted to buy a sesame cake from a vendor, but her mother said, 'This is not something you should eat.' She rested for a while and saw many people passing by, but none of them returned. She asked her mother about the underworld, and the mother said, 'The greatest sin is filial impiety, and the second is killing living beings.' Suddenly, she saw a water pavilion with a pond full of red and white lotuses interspersed, and she admired them. Then someone from behind saw her and pushed her into the water, and she woke up."

The Vice-Magistrate of Chenzhou The Vice-Magistrate of Chenzhou, surnamed Xiang, was from Guixi in Xin Prefecture. He claimed that his father, who had no son, often fasted and prayed to the Heavenly Deity. Afterward, he dreamed of a messenger in yellow robes carrying a large box. When the box was opened, he saw a tray inside containing a distinguished person adorned with a golden seal and purple sash. His mother then conceived. He was born on the seventh day of the seventh lunar month and, at the age of twenty-five, passed the imperial examination. He served in five prefectures and counties, and was dispatched to Chényáng at the age of fifty. He often boasted of his own greatness and was summoned by the Criminal Affairs Department to investigate a case in the neighboring area of Xiāngyáng, where he was accused of concealing official funds amounting to hundreds of thousands of coins while purchasing grain. Xiang accepted bribes totaling three million coins and handled the case carelessly. Before the investigation was complete, he fell ill and rushed back home. During the night, supernatural beings appeared, some snapping their fingers and others sighing, saying, "A pity, a pity." His family often saw these beings, but Xiang did not understand their significance. His illness worsened. The horse stable was located in the rear garden of the prefectural government office, separated by two gates and more than a hundred steps away. Ghosts and spirits moved the horse manure onto his bed at night, removing it and then bringing it back. On the seventh day of the seventh lunar month, he died in office, holding only the rank of Chengyi Lang and leaving no inheritance. The Village Head of Fangzhou In Fangzhou, Fangling, there was a man named Lǐ Zhèng, who served as a village head. He was stubborn, cunning, and fond of litigation, often encroaching on others' farmland and seizing their cattle and horses. The authorities could not control him. In the fourteenth year of the Chúnxī era, he suddenly died. That very day, his family's water buffalo gave birth to a calf. Underneath the calf's belly, the white and black fur alternated to form the four characters "Lǐ Zhèng," which were as clear and legible as those in the Chóngníng currency, worth three coins. Whenever his wife and children approached it, they would shed tears. Just half a year later, the calf was eaten by a tiger, and its blood and bones were all gone. Only the four characters, still with the skin, remained as they had been at first. The supervisor of the prefectural alcohol tax, Zhōngxùn Láng Wáng Sìzōng, personally witnessed this event. It is often recorded that when people die, they become cows, but this case is particularly unusual. The Landlord of Chenzhou

Wang Zhongyin, whose ancestor was Wang Yin in the year Wu Chen of the Shaoxing era, served as a deputy to the provincial inspector of Hubei. He followed his superior, Ma Juzhong, on a tour of subordinate cities up to Chenzhou. Ma stayed in the administrative building's hall in the prefectural capital, while Wang slept in the eastern corner of the defensive setup. In the middle of the night, he dreamed of an old man in a white robe and black hat who introduced himself and approached the guest steps. Wang thought the old man might be a land god and repeatedly invited him to face east. The old man declined, saying that Wang would one day come to this place as the host and it would be inappropriate for him to be above. Wang woke up and wrote down the dream, also recording it on the wall of Zhang's academy three miles away. Thirty years later, Wang became a military governor in Huai East, and Zhao Wei, who was then prime minister, had known him before and appointed him to govern Chenzhou. Initially, Wang never dared to think about governing Chenzhou, but when he got the position, he was surprised and thought of his earlier dream. In the first month of the year Xin Chou during the Chunxi era, Wang went to Chenzhou and passed by Zhang's academy, where the words and paintings on the wall were still as they were before. The family had already covered the wall with green gauze. Wang later served as the governor of Yongzhou and was responsible for the criminal justice of Guangxi and Fujian, and he died in this role. The story of Zhang Gongzhi and his silver: Zhang Gongzhi, a wealthy man from Jiangling, was known for his wealth and influence in the region. During the Zhenghe era, he dreamed of more than twenty white-clad figures bowing to him, asking who they were, but they did not answer and then disappeared into the ground. He found it strange but did not consider it extremely unusual, and every night the same thing happened. He then ordered his servant to dig at the place where they disappeared, and at a depth of three feet, they found twenty large silver pieces, each weighing fifty taels, with very ancient designs. He thought they were from a thousand years ago, recorded them with flower-like inscriptions, and kept them in a chest, not telling his descendants and not reviewing them at inappropriate times. One day, he dreamed again that they came to bid farewell, saying they wanted to go to Changsha to help build a house for Mr. Zhao but could not stay with him for long, and they would eventually return. Zhang was suspicious and opened the chest in the morning, finding it empty with nothing inside. He was greatly frightened and wanted to thoroughly investigate, going specifically to Changsha.

Indeed, near Shanhua County, there was a residence of Zhao, who was just beginning to build a grand mansion. Zhang Lao Na, responsible for administrative duties, visited Zhao's residence. Upon hearing of Zhang's reputation, Zhao hurriedly came out to greet him, and they sat down for a while to settle. Zhang then said, "When your family was building the mansion, what did you find in the ground?" Zhao did not immediately respond and told him, "I found a thousand taels of silver." Zhang replied, "It is from my family's old collection; each piece has floral inscriptions. I took it out to see and indeed it was true." Zhang then mentioned his previous dream and expressed his wish to exchange it for other silver. Zhao gladly agreed. Zhang returned home and called a blacksmith to melt the silver into a large ball. He made a hole in the middle, used an iron chain to bind it, and placed it at the foot of the bed so it could not move again. At night, one often heard crying sounds. Later, after passing through military conflicts and bandits, it is no longer known where it is. According to popular belief, during the time of Zhang Xunwang, his family had a lot of silver, and each time they melted a thousand taels into a ball, considering it as something beyond their control. The temple deity of Yuzhang Wei Daobi, a political figure during the Southern Song Dynasty, served as the governor of Hongzhou in the year Renwu of the Shaoxing era. Behind his residence, there was a small temple dedicated to the land god. The temple's door was connected to a toilet, which was considered impure. Wei ordered the temple to be moved to the corridor of the City God Temple. When the preparations were complete, the vice governor Lin dreamed of a spirit who identified himself as the land god of the governor's residence. The spirit said, "I have been serving as a deity for many years, even though I am close to the toilet, it has not harmed me. If I am moved away, then the entire family, old and young, will have no place to rely on. Additionally, being in the presence of the great deity, my movements and rest will not be at ease. I beg you to grant me one word to reach the governor's seat and allow us to continue as before, if you please." Lin said, "If the spirit can manifest its power, why doesn't it speak for itself?" The spirit replied, "Wei is the Purple Qi Star God, currently holding a high position as a minister. How can he easily come close?" The next day, Lin reported this to Wei, who then stopped the move. Instead, he only moved the door to another direction and renovated the temple, decorating it and offering sacrifices to it. That night, Lin dreamed again of the spirit thanking him.

Wutai Manjushri In the sixth year of Xuanhe (1124 AD), Jiang Xiuju, a professor in Longde Prefecture, was tasked by the educational administration to search for lost books. Accompanied by his family and his eldest son Gui, he traveled to Wutai Mountain and stayed at the Manjushri Temple. The following morning, after finishing his meal, he prayed before the Buddha statue, asking for a miraculous appearance. By the time of the afternoon meal, a cool breeze began to stir, and a pale yellow cloud rose from the west. Soon, the sounds of arhats and heavenly beings could be heard, each holding banners, flags, incense, and flowers, leading the way in an orderly procession. Swords, armor, banners, and flags followed continuously, shining beside the sunlight. Finally, the Bodhisattva appeared riding on a lion throne, followed by leaders of the four barbarian tribes, wearing fur coats and wolf hats, who followed respectfully. Countless and boundless beings followed, each according to their kind, emitting strange fragrances that were fragrant and dense, perceived clearly by the nose. After several moments, the vision disappeared. As the sky began to darken, another time, hundreds of lamps and candles were seen, arranged with golden and purple nobles holding hand warmers and walking ahead. The Bodhisattva walked slowly, and lotuses grew where his feet touched the ground, with a yellow dog wagging its tail behind. Gui, who was thirteen years old at the time, wore a monk's robe and appeared beside the image. Fairies played flutes and pipes, and the sound could be heard. Jiang's family and the temple servants all witnessed this. In the winter of Jingtian, he reported to the emperor that the horses had drunk from the river, resigned his position, and returned to the capital, where he was appointed as a professor at the Imperial Academy. He lived in a bamboo fence alley. Soon after, he dreamed that the Bodhisattva told him to urgently request an external appointment. He then took his family eastward, exiting through the water gate, meeting a convenient boat on the Si River, and arriving at the Bian River bank. Gradually, they returned to Yanling, where the entire household was safe and sound. At that time, the capital had already been besieged, and amidst chaos and separation, he felt no fear or terror. Gui was born in the year Gui Si during the Zhenghe era (1123 AD) and lived until the year Bing Chen during the Qingyuan era (1196 AD), reaching eighty-four years old, still strong and healthy, showing no signs of decline.

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Four Imperially Approved Complete Library The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Five By Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty Liu Yuan Eight-Lang A man from Mingzhou, Xia, the main office, and a wealthy merchant, Lin, jointly bought a government-run distillery. Other stores then sold or distributed alcohol, paying taxes according to the amount sold. After many years, Lin owed Xia 2,000 strings of cash, and Xia could not collect the debt. He complained to the local officials, who accepted bribes and twisted the case, claiming that Xia owed Lin. They first sent eight agents to alter the records to support their claim. Xia was wronged and could not clear his name, suffering imprisonment, beating, and torture, which led to illness. In the county, there was a man named Liu Yuan, who was naturally bold and valiant. He felt it was unfair and proclaimed to the public: "In my hometown, there are such unjust cases. Xia, the main office, presents the case of the wine money but is stuck in prison. What use are the state and county officials? Regret that they did not point to me as a witness. I can explain the situation clearly and make those eight people suffer punishment." Gradually, they heard his words and feared that it would be revealed and cause harm. They sent two people who were good at speaking to invite Liu to drink at the flag pavilion and discuss this case. "Why should Eight-Lang bother with other people's business? Just drink," they said. After drinking, he took out 200,000 strings of official currency and gave it to them, saying, "Knowing that Eight-Lang is poor, I just want to help." Liu angrily cursed, "You started this unjust case!"

The person's heart was stirred by an unjust case, and now they are being defiled by unjust wealth. I would rather die of hunger than accept a single coin as a bribe. This matter of right and wrong, true and false, cannot be resolved in the mortal world. If there is no case in the afterlife, then that is fine. But if there is, there must be a place of justice. He called out to the wine server and asked how much was spent today. The server replied that it cost 1,800 coins. Liu quickly took off his clothes to pawn them and pay the bill. Shortly after, Xia became very ill, was released from prison, and died. On his deathbed, he warned his son, "I die with a grievance unavenged. All the official documents and contracts that others owe me can be placed in my coffin. I will strongly plead my case in the afterlife." A month later, eight people died suddenly. Another month later, Liu was at home and suddenly felt dizzy and trembling. He told his wife, "The scene before me is not good. It seems that Xia's grievance has come to me, and I must provide evidence. I will surely die, but I hope that I did not do anything else wrong in my life. Do not bury me immediately. Give me three days. If I do not rise after three days, then you can do as you wish." That evening, he died. A few nights later, he suddenly sat up and said, "Recently, I was chased by two public officials, traveled a hundred miles to reach the official office, and encountered something green (or possibly a green object)."

The official from the hall came out to see and it was Xia, the county magistrate. He repeatedly thanked and said, "Troubling you, Eight, to come here. The documents are all done, just need a slight proof. Don't worry." Later, he saw eight people wearing a long whip about 15-16 feet long with eight holes to receive the heads. Soon, a report came that the king was sitting on the throne, and officials led him to the courtyard. The king said, "The matters of the Xia family don't need to be discussed, but the incident of drinking upstairs should be clear. I said, 'Two people invited me to drink five cups, bought three dishes, and gave 200 coins for official expenses, which I never accepted.'" The king looked around and sighed, "There are such good people in the world. They are truly worthy of reward. Let's discuss how to reward him. Check his lifespan. An official went out and returned shortly, saying, 'He should live to 79 years.' The king said, 'Poor people don't accept money, so we must reward him by adding a decade to his life.' He ordered Yuan to lead him to see hell first and then return. When they arrived, it was mostly like the human world, with prisoners from the city and its counties. Some were carrying whips, some were bound, and some were being questioned and punished. They cried when they saw him, telling him their names, residences, and asking him to help them."

On the day of returning to the world, one repays the original family or household. It is said that one owes money to some households or rent to others, or has borrowed items from them, or has falsely relied on someone else’s land and property. All debts are to be repaid by family members for the sake of reducing sin. Others may seek money or perform good deeds. I ignored them and left without paying attention, yet I still heard admiration and envy. Returning to the hall, the underworld king said, “Since you have seen this, recount it to the world so that they know about the underworld.” I bowed and left. Upon leaving, I was sent out by an official who demanded money, but I refused to give it. He complained, “After serving you for a few days, how could you not thank me? Give me ten thousand strings of coins!” I replied, “I myself have no food to eat, how could I have spare money?” The official then pulled off my topknot and pushed me to the ground. I regained consciousness and found my topknot on the pillow. The county magistrate from Jinan, who lived in Siming, personally witnessed this account. During the reign of Emperor Chunxi, Liu, who was over eighty years old and sick, was visited by Wang, who was very worried. Liu said, “County magistrate, do not worry. I will not die, and indeed, I recovered.” It was said that the number added by the underworld king was the cause. He died at the age of ninety-one. Wang is now a magistrate in Raoyang.

妙縁寺 In the year of Xichong (1146) on the night of October 25th, my elder brother, Wen'an Gong, who held the position of Deputy Governor of Taizhou, went out to inspect the counties and stayed at the Tiantai Mountains. In his dream, he rested in a mountain with a burden, alone guarding a nearby monastery, and arrived at the Myōgō-ji Temple. As he was about to return, suddenly rain started. A monk pointed west and said, "The sky on that side has such beautiful colors; it will clear up soon." Farmers often use this as an omen. Looking east, the rain was pouring down like a curtain, and on the high peaks, the paths of woodcutters and streams competed. He followed the mountain south and then turned east, coming to a bridge over twenty zhang long, with deep ravines and towering pines reaching the clouds. Every five or seven steps, the branches and trunks of the pines curved around the bridge, resembling dragons. It was called the Winding Dragon Bridge. Straight east to the place where they rested, at that time, a monk came from Myōgō-ji Temple and asked to leave first. My elder brother composed a couplet to send him off, saying, "Looking west, the sky was clear with colorful clouds; looking east, it was overcast. This clearly distinguishes the clear and the overcast. After crossing the small bridge, the Winding Dragon Bridge was dangerous. Turning back, the high person thanked me." Then he woke up, but soon fell asleep again, dreaming of serving his parents and carrying his family ashore, with the sun already high and the children still not ready. Zhongxuan Gong ordered them to hurry, while his wife, Lady Luchuo, was dressing, and his sister opened a chest to give him clothes. He then accompanied Wen'an Gong and I to explore nearby areas, coming to another bridge and wanting to go to a small temple in the west corner, seeing the path was similar to the one we took yesterday. Because of the previous dream, my two younger brothers said this was the Myōen-ji Temple, and as we were about to cross the bridge, it started raining again. We wanted to stop, but Zhongxuan Gong forced us to go. I suspected that there might be an ancestor's grave in that temple. Zhongxuan Gong held a staff and ordered a soldier to hold an umbrella; after landing, Wen'an Gong followed. This bridge was very dangerous, so I turned back and told my two younger brothers not to come. Before reaching Myōen-ji, I woke up. At that time, Zhongxuan Gong was in the countryside, Wen'an Gong was in Pizhou, and I was serving below. This dream is almost incomprehensible.

**Wen Hui Gong's Dream Poem** On the night of July 24, 1177, during the fourth year of the Chongxi era, Wen Hui Gong was in the countryside when he dreamt of reaching a wild temple. There were no monks to be seen, but several feathered immortals were sitting in a circle. One of them recited a poem: "At sixty, one buys a concubine; at seventy, one still gives birth to a child." The others covered their mouths and laughed, asking, "Does the old man know or not?" Wen Hui Gong was born in the year of the Dragon (丁酉) and was 61 years old at the time. This guest seemed to be offering some form of criticism. Wen Hui Gong, who had lived in seclusion for many years and had never kept concubines, responded with a 56-character poem: "As the sun sets in the west, my hair turns gray; the path of old age is busy. I do not devote my energy to the pleasures of a luxurious life, but let others compete in dance and song, vying for new makeup. I sweep away all matters, as if life were a dream, and end my life without losing my composure. Relying on the clear wind and bright moon, I am willing to come and accompany a single incense burner." Everyone laughed, and the one who had been reciting felt ashamed. They drank tea and engaged in clear conversation for a long time before dispersing. After waking, Wen Hui Gong ordered someone to write it down. As for the words about seventy, the public did not count him among those who reached that age.

Ren Dao Yuan Ren Dao Yuan, a native of Fuzhou, was the eldest son of the former Vice President of the Imperial Academy, Wen Jian. In his youth, he was fond of the Dao and studied under Master Ouyang Wenbin, who taught him the "Lian Du" and "Tianxin" methods, which showed great efficacy. During the latter part of Emperor Xiaozong's reign (乾道年间), a person from Yongfu named Ke sought help at the altar due to illness. Ren and his wife's nephew Liang Gun stayed in the斋舍 (a place for fasting and worship). Gun also liked the method. One night, Gun dreamed of a deity who said, "If anyone seeks a response, they should write the character '香' and return home quickly." Gun woke up and told Ren. Ren lit a candle, wrote the character, sealed it, and then went back to sleep. The next morning, Ke arrived and was given the character. Ke returned home and died 18 days later. The character "香" represents the number 18. Afterwards, the Vice President passed away, and Ren took office. He gradually became less diligent in his religious duties, only bowing outside the shrine each morning and having a child light incense inside. Family members often advised him but he did not listen. In the 13th year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign (淳熙年间), during the Lantern Festival night, residents of the northern city built a large yellow talisman ceremony at Zhang Dao's hermitage. They invited Ren to lead the ceremony, and many people gathered to watch. Two girls with braids stood side by side, looking very pretty. Ren looked at them and said, "Little ones, come in and watch." The two girls bowed.

谢复仔细观察后说道：“提起你的襕裙，襕裙是福建习俗中指代擦胸的动作，提起是戏谑的话语。”其中一人问道：“法师做醮时如何却说这样的话？”过了一会儿那人离开，任与他交谈如初，又被女子责备。等到醮礼结束后，任感到左耳后发痒且疼痛，命令仆人查看，发现一处疮口如粟粒大小，内部疼痛难忍。第二天回家，情绪不佳。过了几天，任对绲说：“我做了一个极其恶梦，已秘密写在纸上，等请商日邀请法师来验证。”商到来后说：“这不是我能分辨的，需要圣童到来才能决定。”不久，门外出现一个村童，刚到便跳上梁间，以神语说道：“任道元，诸神保护你许久，而你却不谨慎香火，贪淫并行，罪不可赦。”任深感前非，敲额头谢罪。神又说：“你十五夜所说的话很好。”任拜谢乞命，愿改过自新。神说：“你还有什么要说的？我也不欠你一个奉事，应当作为受法弟子的戒律，并且宽限你二十天的期限。”说完，童子落地。

Awakening in a dazed state with no awareness, Gün tore open the writing and showed it to Shang, which was the number "twenty days three." At that time, it was the twenty-sixth day of the first month. On the next night, Ren dreamed that a deity held an iron whip and chased and circled around the residence at Jiuxian Mountain several times. The back of his head was struck by the whip, causing him to wake up in a state of fear. From then on, the wound grew larger, and his head swelled like a gourd. Every time after the second drumbeat, he would cry out as if being whipped. Those around him wept and bowed; after a while, he suffered again, and his entire body turned blue-black. On the twelfth night of February, Gün returned to his residence; his mother did not allow him to go again. That night, he dreamed that the deity said, "You must arrive at the first drumbeat early in the morning and go to Ren's house to see me beat Dao Yuan." He got up and waited for the time to arrive before going. Ren saw him and wept, saying, "This is the only time we will meet." He was about to get out of bed when he suddenly fell on the mat. Eight servants helped him sit down; it seemed as if something pulled him out and threw him onto the ground. When they looked, he was already dead. Ouyang Shi, who lived in the north of the city, also died on that day. Gün no longer dared to practice the method. My eldest son recorded and showed me the event, and I recorded it in the *Nanbu Huayu Lu*. The name "Xiang Niang" is the same as the "eighteen days" mentioned here. Ren Qing, a good scholar, deserves his descendants to flourish. Later generations, practicing recklessly without caution, bring upon themselves strange punishments. I have seen many such cases.

Guan Wang Pool In the fifth year of Chongxi (1178), Xu Dazhong of Jiaxing followed his father to serve in Zhongdu and rented a residence near the warehouse in Renhe County. To the south of their home was the Guan Wang Pool, which was home to many turtles and softshell turtles. Some of these creatures were large enough to carry a person. The water in the pool was always clear and did not dry up even during droughts. On several consecutive days of overcast and dim weather, they saw an iron coffin floating on the surface of the water. Xu Dazhong then set about tidying up his study. There was a cluster of bamboo plants by the window, which had withered and needed to be removed. Beneath the bamboo, he found a large round object with a glossy surface that could be used as a mirror. Thinking it was a broken gourd, he took a look at it and discovered it was actually a skull. He thought that according to medical books, the parietal bone (天灵盖) could be used as medicine, and this must be the real thing. He carelessly stored it in a bookcase. That night, the family saw a little boy wearing a silk shirt and a green skirt. The boy jumped from the table and leaped through the window. They suspected a neighbor of being a thief, but no traces were found. Xu Dazhong then had a dream where the child came to ask for money to move the body. He did not know how to respond. The child also said to burn 3,000 strings of paper money to turn it into gold.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

In the spring of the fourth year of the Qiandao era, Wenhuigong, a prominent official, requested to return home from his post as governor of Fuyang and was on his way to Yiwu, a county in Wuzhou. The county magistrate Zhang Chong arrived early to clean and prepare the Xiu Chuan Inn, where the local officials in charge of preparations stayed. The night was still young, and the moonlight was hazy. They heard people walking outside, their steps were very heavy, and they suspected it was thieves. They carefully observed over ten people, who appeared to be divine beings, each about a zhang tall. The officials were afraid and dared not go out; they went back to bed and did not have a moment's peace all night. The next day, Wenhuigong arrived, and it was said that when a former prime minister visits, there must be divine beings accompanying him. Hu Tongzhi, a judicial officer in the eastern command of the southern mountains, was a son of a prominent family in Piling. When he was young, he visited the Zhang Wang Temple in Guangde to seek a dream. That night, he dreamed of entering the temple, where everything was grand and different from the daytime. He walked to the west wing, and a clerk came forward to greet him warmly, as if they had met before. Hu Tongzhi thought this was not the human world and asked about his future prospects, but felt dissatisfied. In the tenth year of the Shaoxi era, he first joined the Xiang military camp and after two examinations, his recommendation was rejected. He often thought about his dream, knowing that his official career had limits, and if promoted, he would have fewer years left. On the twenty-first day of the twelfth month of the twelfth year of the Qingyuan era, he attended a banquet with his colleagues and said that last night he dreamed of a guest giving him a money bag, which he counted to be fifty-three coins, and he was now fifty-three years old, suggesting that this omen was not good. The guests tried to comfort him, but he suddenly felt severe abdominal pain and had to leave early. He died the next day and was posthumously granted the title of Tongzhi, as predicted by the dream.

### Translation Li Linfu The *Lóngchéng Lù* attributed to Liu Zihou is actually written by Liu Wuyan and contains parables. One of these parables states that in the first year of the Yuanhe era (806) in June, a prostitute in Huizhou was struck dead in the market. On her side, there was red writing that said, "Li Linfu, who used poison and tyranny to manipulate power." The emperor ordered the immortals to investigate three recent cases of similar phenomena. The most recent occurred in the first year of the Shaoxi era (1190) in spring, when a seven-year-old girl from the Yangtai market in Hanyang was struck dead by lightning. There was writing on her back that resembled a talisman, and those who read it said, "Li Linfu, who has been reborn as a prostitute for seven generations, is now in the form of a human. All thirteen characters are very similar to the previous incident." A Daoist priest from Xiangyang named Li Dafang once saw this. Turtle Possession Jing Chen's eldest son, Gong, was seven years old when he suddenly developed a swelling on his side. He felt something inside his skin that resembled a turtle. It moved and caused severe pain. In Deqing, an ancient village, there was an external medicine doctor named Hong Doufu. When he saw Gong, he instructed him to buy fresh shrimp soup to eat. Everyone suspected that shrimp was a food to avoid for wounds and infections, but the doctor insisted that Gong eat it. Soon after, the pain subsided. The doctor said, "This is the turtle possession. I deliberately sought what he likes to test it." He then prepared a medicine similar to that used for treating the spleen and stomach, ground up two money of aconite powder, and administered it. After several doses, the condition disappeared. The next year, the illness recurred, but it was treated as before, and the root cause was eliminated. This person was skilled in acupuncture and treating abscesses and sores, but he did not pass on his knowledge. Even his son, who asked him about it, received no answer. However, those who served him closely and observed him closely enough to become proficient also became renowned as skilled physicians.

**Translation:** **Records of the Unusual, Volume Five, Section Wu** *From the Subcategory of Strange Tales in the Novels Section of the Four Treasuries* *Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty* **The Wealthy Family of Qiantang** In the past, Qiantang County of Chuzhou once suffered from a flood that completely submerged the houses of the people. A wealthy family, whose surname is unknown, usually kept several boats along the riverbank. When the flood came, the entire family climbed aboard and took refuge on a high place. After being saved, their basic necessities were not fully prepared, and they planned to return to the boats to load their belongings. As they looked out, they saw the water level rising further. The people of the entire county were climbing onto their roofs and shouting, their cries echoing through the fields. The wealthy man said, "The loss of our family's property is something we can recover from. How could we ignore people drowning like fish, leaving them to their fate?" Immediately, he divided the task among his sons, each responsible for a boat. They rescued people from the lowest to the highest levels in order, along with the bags and boxes they carried, allowing them to follow. When they reached a place, they would go back again. They made more than ten round trips, without a doubt, over a thousand people. All escaped the disaster of drowning. The next day, the water receded, but not a single house in the county remained standing. The area became a vast expanse of sand. The sand where the wealthy man lived suddenly rose like a hill. He sent his servants to work together to remove the sand, and the house remained intact. The household items and boxes were as secure as before. Only the books, clothes, and bedding were slightly damp. At that time, the wealthy man's son was studying in Yongjia. He heard of the disaster and hurried back, and returned again. He said that the story was as described. It is regrettable that we do not know the name of the wealthy man. Those who do good deeds will surely receive divine recompense, but people do not know this yet.

A Scholar from Taizhou In the early days of the Chongxi era, the two rivers outside Taizhou City swelled due to heavy rain, nearly overtopping the city walls, and the number of people who died in the flood was incalculable. A scholar, whose name is omitted, lived in the city but had his fields in Huangyan. Before the floodwaters rose, he rowed a small boat to fetch grain, carrying forty baskets, each holding one measure of grain.
