Just as he left the mouth of the stream, the waves were as high as mountains, and people were alternately floating and sinking, continuously in sight. The scholar moored his boat on a high bank and encountered those who were being carried to the side. He wanted to save them, but the boat's power was not enough to carry them. So, he would load one person and throw away one basket of grain. In a short time, fifty people were rescued, and all the grain was gone. Then he took them back to the city. At that time, You Yanzhong was the magistrate of the county and praised his kindness. Immediately, he prepared a grand feast to invite him.

### Final Translation Invited and given a hundred thousand coins, *Chu He Guan Wen* bestowed favors on his retinue and was then appointed as an *登仕郎*. At the same time, there was a young woman from a wealthy family whose family had already perished. She sat alone in a bathing tub, floating aimlessly on the brink of death. A fishing boat passed by her side, and she called out to the fisherman, saying, "I am a girl from a certain lane of a certain family. If you can save me, I will repay you with two gold bracelets from my arm." The fisherman carried her to a place where she had no home. The girl wept bitterly almost to the point of death, unfastened the bracelets, and gave them to him. He replied, "Madam, your family is ruined, and you need this to sustain yourself. I cannot take it." He left her and departed. The fisherman's virtue is evident here. If he had met a wicked person, they would have taken the gold and thrown her into the river, leaving no chance of survival. Tian extended the story but could not learn the fisherman's name. --- **Chen Shizun** In the fifth year of the Qian Dao era, a great fire broke out in Changxi, Fuzhou. A local scholar-official named Chen Shizun lived in the village in harmony with others, but by nature he was upright and principled. As the fire approached his home, the neighbors gathered and asked him to flee. Chen said, "I have never had the slightest selfish thought in my life. Since this disaster is from heaven, it will not affect me." He firmly sat still, only burning incense in the courtyard, dressed in official robes, and prayed, "This house was built with the surplus of my official salary. May the divine fire watch over it." In a moment, all around it was reduced to ashes, but only Chen's house remained intact. This event is very similar to what was recorded in *Zhi Bing* about Li Shou's house, which was protected by a fire offering.

Huang Shixian Prays at Li Mountain In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era (1158 AD), Huang Shixian from Putian set out for the provincial examination. Initially, he had made an agreement with his fellow villager Chen Yingqiu to travel together, but due to certain matters not being completed, he delayed his departure. After several days, he finally set out on the road, passing through Jian'an and visiting the Li Hou Temple on Li Mountain to seek a dream. In the dream, a spirit told him, "You do not need to hear what I say; just see what Chen Junqing said is correct." Upon arriving in Lin'an, Huang met Chen Fang and immediately asked about the results. Chen Fang replied that he did not know. Huang continued to urge him, and Chen Fang, becoming angry, shouted loudly, "Shixian will be the first, Junqing will be second, that is enough." Huang was pleased that it matched his dream and told Chen Fang. When the results were announced, they matched what he had said. The Tai Sui Hall The family of Zhang Bibu in Suzhou was extremely wealthy and famous, their gardens and mansions were the best in the two Zhe regions. During the Chongning era, they built a magnificent hall in the back garden and dug a large pond in front of it, using the soil to build the foundation of the hall. After digging several feet into the ground, they found a snake as thin as a chopstick, coiled and forming a dome, its length was uncountable. Zhang Bibu's son was in charge of this project and did not consider the strange object a problem. He ordered the servants to cut it into hundreds of pieces and remove it. It took transporting eighteen or nineteen loads to finish. People at the time said that Zhang's son was ominous due to the supernatural, and they jokingly called the hall the Tai Sui Hall. However, there was no harm. Later, when they suffered from a military disaster, it became a ruin. Lady Nanjian, the grandmother of Wang Shunbo, was a daughter of the Zhang family.

**能仁长老** In Yongfu County, there was an elder monk named Nengren who used temple funds to buy a temple certificate to ordain one of his disciples as a monk. In the second year of the Shaoxing era, the elder died in the temple. The following year, the ordained monk went to another temple. One day, a calf was born with the name of its master hidden on its back, clearly visible. The monk then asked the head of the temple where the calf was born to buy it, bring it back, and bury it. Soon after, another cow gave birth to a calf with the same inscription on its belly. The monk bought it again and buried it as before. Not long after, a cow from a nearby village gave birth to a calf with the same inscription. Several monks went to see it and found it to be exactly the same as the two they had already buried. They then realized that the karmic retribution was indeed as it should be, and they left it unattended. **香屯渡小童** In Xiangtun Ferry in Dexing, there was a wild ferry where a boatman moored his boat at the shore. A young boy, who was a servant, gave him fifty coins to carry him across. The boatman was surprised by the large amount. The boy said, "I got angry with my master and fled here. I fear he will also come this way to catch me. Please let me hide under the board to avoid him." The boatman agreed. Shortly after, an old man from the village came and just as he boarded the boat, the boy suddenly jumped out from under the board and turned into a giant python, about fifteen feet long. It raised its head and bit the old man's throat directly. The old man quickly raised his hands to grasp the python's neck, but the python could not bite or attack. Instead, it wrapped its body around him. The people on the boat shook with fear and looked at each other. Someone held a long hook and cut the python into five or four pieces. Finally, they released it, and both the man and the python died. This old man was a sorcerer named Cheng, who was known in the village as Cheng the Sorcerer. He was particularly skilled in controlling snakes and had accumulated so many kills that they could not be counted. In his later years, his methods were somewhat weakened, so he suffered this karmic retribution. Zhang Zili's younger brother, who was a tax official in Nankang, once learned the method from him.

A Maid Servant from the Yu Family Dreams of Predicting the Imperial Examination List In the first year of the Qingyuan era, during the seventh month, a maid named Qingnu, servant to Yu Jieqing who supervised the tax office in Lin'an, had a dream in which two men holding yellow flags knocked on the door and shouted, "Come, we are here to announce the imperial examination list." She initially refused them, but one of them said, "This is indeed our household." Upon waking, she told Yu Jieqing about the dream. At the time, Yu's two sons, Yan and Zhuo, were waiting in Poyang for the autumn examination. Yu Jieqing then wrote a letter home, telling them about the dream and urging them to sharpen and encourage their studies in response to the omen. However, neither of them was selected in the examination. Two years later, on the seventh day of the third month, a scholar from Yudan named Shi Ben visited early in the morning. He was a candidate from the previous year's local examination and said that while he was in the examination hall, he had written on the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) and felt quite satisfied with his work. He was concerned that he might have obtained a name or rank undeservedly, as he had heard that those who reported the list could strip people of their clothes. If he were to encounter such a situation, he would have no other clothes to wear, so he wished to hide here. Yu Jieqing agreed, and the next day, when the list was posted, the person who reported it already knew where Shi Ben was and went straight to Yu's house. Shi Ben hid in the hall and did not let the person see his face, but he was given money to appease him. It was then that Yu Jieqing realized the maid's dream and the phrase "our household" from the dream. Shi Ben was ranked seventh in the first category and was the first place in the *Yijing* category. He was in his early twenties.

The Deity of the Three Public Officials --- In the city of E, there is a temple dedicated to the three public officials. The statues are arranged in a tripod-like formation, but their identity as a deity is unknown. People treat this deity with great reverence and caution. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Song Dynasty, Left Commander Zuo Liang, who was stationed at Jin Kou, about thirty miles from the capital, had a dream-like experience one evening. He saw a messenger in yellow robes standing in the courtyard and said that the three public officials had summoned him. Zuo Liang refused, saying, "I am in charge of patrolling, and the three public officials are revered deities. Why have they summoned me?" Soon, another messenger arrived, and his words were the same. Reluctantly, Zuo Liang followed him out and walked with him to a large official building. Upon entering, they entered a hall where he saw several people dressed in scholar's robes and hats sitting. They invited Zuo Liang to sit at the end but did not speak to him. Zuo Liang stood up and said, "I am a lowly official on duty. I was summoned. What is the matter?" One of them replied, "There is no other matter, but a yellow silk screen has been lost in this place. We ask you to investigate." Zuo Liang hesitated and was about to respond when another person said, "The person is currently in Yue Fei's army." Zuo Liang bowed and returned, feeling as if he had just woken from a dream. The next day, he reported the divine words to Yue Fei, who ordered the military officials to investigate. Yue Fei's legal system was very strict, and the officials did not dare to delay. They indeed captured the thief immediately. Half of the screen was already damaged. Yue Fei was surprised and ordered the thief to be executed while ordering the creation of a new screen to be sent to the temple. Zuo Liang then dreamed again and came to thank the deity. According to the "Ding Records," this was the same person as the military official of Wuzhou, whose son served as a tax official in Nankang County in the second year of Qianyuan, and he recounted this story.

Hu Shi, the Custodian A man named Hu Shi from Yangzhou, whose family was quite wealthy, was known as the Custodian. At the end of the Shaoxing period, five scholars came to see him. They did not introduce themselves and did not wait for the host to come out, but walked directly into the courtyard and sat down. Hu immediately put on his belt and invited them with a bow. The conversation was somewhat unusual, and Hu began to suspect them. One of the guests stood up and said, "Sir, do not be suspicious. We are not ordinary people; we are the so-called Five Manifest Deities. We know that you are a good host, which is why we did not come through introductions. We wish to borrow a room where we can rest temporarily and find a place to settle. However, we will also provide occasional assistance to repay your hospitality." Hu was very pleased and invited them in. They drank several cups of wine and were shown to an empty guesthouse. They stayed for a short while, and Hu continued to show them respect in the mornings and evenings, offering them gifts of gold and silk without them having to ask. They stayed with him for five months. Just as Hu's birthday was approaching, they all entered together and said, "We have been troubling your family for a long time. On this occasion of your birthday, we wish to present a cup of wine as a toast." That night, Hu heard the sound of preparations, clinking. The next morning, they visited to congratulate him with splendid curtains and bright new utensils. They raised the cup repeatedly, and Hu noticed that the bottom of the wine vessels were engraved with the characters "Yangzhou official use." He became very anxious, thinking that they had stolen public property and would surely bring trouble to him. The guests noticed his concern and laughed, saying, "Just relax and drink; we will return it to its original place." They enjoyed drinking and singing until past midnight before dispersing. The next day, they were gone.

A Place of Nothingness Suddenly Became a Site of Disturbance In a place that was once empty, someone suddenly brought utensils and joined the Hu people in drinking. Calmly, he said, "We have exerted great effort for you; our efforts are not insignificant. We wish to request that this house be converted into a temple, so that humans and spirits will not be confused or mixed." The Hu person replied, "This is where my family has lived for three generations; how could it be lightly discussed? Let us choose a good spot on a hill and build a shrine there, and I will take on the responsibility of maintaining the incense and fire. How can this be?" All spoke vehemently, saying it cannot be done, and their words became increasingly contrary. From then on, supernatural disturbances occurred. The Hu person could not bear it and consulted with his relatives and old friends, intending to call a Taoist priest to perform rituals before returning. At the gate, five people blocked the road and said, "We heard that you intend to invite a priest to treat us, but we are righteous spirits who enjoy state sacrifices. We only want the house to be converted into a temple, which is not a great fault. What are you doing, priest? Even if the Heavenly Master of Han were to reappear, we would not fear." The Hu person became increasingly worried, disturbed, and confused daily. One day, while entering the market, he encountered a Taoist who was begging. The Taoist said, "You have a worried expression; you must be troubled by ghosts. Proceed from here, and if you encounter a small monk, pray to him; he can definitely save you." The Hu person was surprised and thanked the Taoist, intending to ask for details, but the Taoist suddenly disappeared. The Hu person then walked to the fields and indeed met a monk, to whom he immediately offered earnest prayers. The monk said, "This is a small matter; you may return."

The Leaders of the Two Defense Units in Wuzhou In Wuzhou, it is a local custom to jointly organize a Taoist ritual known as the Huanglu Jiao on the birthday of Zhenwu, the deity of martial arts and water, which falls on the third day of the third lunar month. The ritual is intended to ward off disasters and seek blessings. In the first year of the Shaoxing era, wealthy households led by Chen and Xu organized the event, with Chen serving as the chief leader and Xu as his deputy. Over the years, the preparations for the ritual were consistently orderly and solemn. However, three years later, Chen, due to family matters, had excessively used the communal funds and could not repay them by the time of the ritual. He then pushed Xu to take his place. Xu agreed and covered all the expenses with his own money, without any hesitation. The person hired to write the ritual text relied on the old ritual text, which listed the names of the leaders as either Xu or Chen. The two leaders had not carefully reviewed the text. When they ascended the altar to burn the ritual text, both of them trembled uncontrollably, bowing their hands in greeting as if they were bound. When asked, they did not respond, only calling out in anguish. Hundreds of men and women in the ritual pleaded in distress, but no one knew why they were being punished. Suddenly, one person, seemingly possessed, loudly declared, "I am the Jianshai Shizhe. I have just seen that the ritual text lists the names as either Xu or Chen, showing inconsistency. This is indeed disrespectful to the Three Pure Ones, who will not accept the offering. This violates the pure laws, and the sin has a specific consequence." The crowd continued to plead in sorrow, but after a long while, the possessed person said, "You all have been sincere in your hearts. I will report this to Zhenwu and make a resolution." Shortly after, the possessed person said, "Zhenwu has reported this to the higher authorities and has already been granted forgiveness." Immediately, the two leaders regained consciousness as if nothing had happened, and the ritual was completed.

A Taoist Priest Named Wang In the area near the Yongjin Gate in Lin'an, there lived a Taoist priest named Wang Fa Shi. He practiced the Tianshin method and performed rituals involving writing talismans and burning them, wearing a star crown and ritual robe, though he was not a full-time Taoist monk. The common people regarded him as a true Taoist priest, and the fees and gratuities he received were reduced by about one-third, so he was often employed. Whenever he needed to write documents, he would often have his neighbor, Li Sheng, do it for him. On the fifteenth day of the first month of the second year of the Chingyuan era, a wealthy family invited him to perform a security ritual for the Lantern Festival. Li Sheng, along with his friends, went out to watch the lanterns, drink wine, and eat meat the night before. That day, he did not mention it and wrote the documents while drunk. The handwriting and sealing were all sloppy. Just as the ritual was ending, Wang Fa Shi had a dream in which two officials in red robes chased and captured him, bringing him to the hall of the Heavenly Official. The Heavenly Official, dressed in full regalia, sat majestically, with his attendants standing in perfect order. An official led Wang Fa Shi to stand in front of the Heavenly Official. Soon, several soldiers brought a prisoner, who turned out to be Li Sheng. The Heavenly Official was furious and asked, "How could you dare to eat meat and drink alcohol while writing the green words for the ritual?" After scolding Li Sheng, the Heavenly Official ordered him to be beaten with a club, and then called for Wang Fa Shi to be questioned. Wang Fa Shi was reprimanded as before. He replied, "I merely presided over the ritual and performed the high priest's duties. I had no knowledge of Li Sheng's actions. Please let me withdraw." A soldier struck his heart with the club and said, "Go!" Wang Fa Shi awoke with a start, feeling severe heart pain. Before he could speak to anyone, he heard a loud call from outside. He sent a servant to inquire and found it was Li Sheng's wife, who said, "My husband has suddenly fallen ill and is in a critical condition. Please come to save him, Master." Despite the pain, Wang Fa Shi went to Li Sheng's room. Li Sheng, from afar, knelt down and said, "Just now, I was severely beaten and suffered immense pain. You just witnessed it. I cannot last much longer. There is nothing more I can say. I hope you will consider our long-standing relationship and give me three thousand coins to buy a coffin." Wang Fa Shi, deeply distressed, agreed. Li Sheng died immediately. Wang Fa Shi's heart pain worsened, and he soon began to vomit blood. By the end of the fourth month, he passed away. The Official Huang and the Bird In another story, there was an official named Huang who had a pet bird that could mimic human speech. The bird was known for its ability to draw eyebrows, which was a sign of good fortune and prosperity. The official Huang used the bird to predict the future and provide guidance to those who sought his help. One day, a man came to see the official, hoping to receive a prophecy. The official asked the bird to draw eyebrows on a piece of paper, and the bird did so with precision. The man was impressed and asked what the drawing meant. The official explained that the bird's drawing indicated that the man would soon achieve great success and wealth. The man was overjoyed and thanked the official for his wisdom. The story of the bird and the official Huang illustrates the belief in the power of animals to provide spiritual guidance and predict the future. It also highlights the importance of trust and the role of intermediaries in seeking divine intervention.

Huang Zhuxiao from Yi County served as a minor official in Poyang. In the second month of the Qingyuan era, a thief entered his home, gathered clothes and bedding, and placed them in two bags. Just before leaving, Huang had raised one mountain bluebird, which was quite tame and could understand human speech. That night, the whole family was asleep, but the bird suddenly began to flutter in its cage and cried out incessantly. Those who heard it thought it was being attacked by a cat and rushed up to look at it. The thief saw and was frightened, rushing out and leaving one bag behind. Huang also woke up and sent a servant to chase after the thief, but the thief had already escaped. The bird, which had been raised with care, knew how to repay its debt, and it was indeed a source of shame. --- In the first month of the Qingyuan era, the examination for the imperial examinations in Shaowu was held. The first question in the *Spring and Autumn Annals* section was: "The Duke met Duke Wen of Jin, Duke of Song, Duke of Wei, Duke of Cao, Duke of Ju, Duke of Zhu, Duke of Teng, Duke of Xue, Duke of Qi, and Duke of Xiaozhu in the state of Zha. In the fifth month of summer, on the day Jia Wu, they then destroyed the state of Biyang. The Duke returned from the meeting." The examiner, Wang Yu, the Deputy Magistrate of Huian, selected two scrolls, but the quota for passing was limited, and only one could be chosen. He took them to show his colleagues. The first scroll's opening statement read: "Using their strategy in matters that should not be done, the hegemon will use it to plan against the barbarians. Exerting all their strength in situations that cannot be delayed, the hegemon will use it to secure the peace of the central states." The second scroll's opening statement read: "Leading the feudal lords to participate in matters that should not be participated in, the *Spring and Autumn Annals* already considers the initial action as shameful. Using the feudal lords to govern matters that should not be governed, the *Spring and Autumn Annals* especially considers the final action as fortunate." Everyone said that the first scroll's wording was clear and brisk, surpassing the second, but it did not mention the Duke returning from the meeting.

When it comes to determining who should be retained or dismissed, this is the standard. Thus, 乙 was placed in the selection. The king was restless for several nights and, as a result, read 乙's response again. In his response, 乙 mentioned the influence of fashionable trends and customs, sighing and writing, "Using the story of a person from Qi and a minister from Chu, there is a correct pronunciation. Otherwise, it should be read as a falling tone, which would violate the taboo of the temple." Consequently, 乙 was demoted, and 甲 was promoted. When the sealed envelopes were opened and the list was created, the person referred to as 乙 was Li Hongzu. Previously, he had recommended 甲 for the selection in Taiping. Before the exam, 甲 had asked for a dream at the Daqin Guangyou Temple. In the dream, two dragons were coiled inside the house, and one dragon leaped up and flew out in front. Upon waking, 甲 changed his name. The following year, in the provincial examination in Shaoxing, Mo Zichun came in first, and 甲 was second. Since 甲 had already taken the imperial examination and held an official position, he could not be ranked above the other candidates. Therefore, according to the tradition, 甲 was promoted to the top rank. Thus, 甲's future prospects were secured. The imperial examination system was merely a means to an end.

Zhu Tai, a Military Officer from Xinzhou Zhu Tai, a military officer of the Wei Guo Camp in Xinzhou, served only in the prefectural government. His character was straightforward and he had few faults. On the night of the twenty-first day of the eighth month in the first year of the Qianyuan era, at midnight, he dreamed that he saw a person dressed like a military officer who introduced himself as Zhu Tai and brought a document to obtain a family guarantee. Zhu Tai became aware of his condition and fell ill, becoming very weak despite taking medicine. Two nights later, he dreamed of another person named Zhang who took him to a high and steep place by the riverbank, took his umbrella, and pushed him into the river. Fortunately, he fell on flat ground, stretched his neck to look up, and saw five riders chasing each other, all dismounting and calling out to him, "How did you fall here?" One of them, surnamed Mao, made him raise his hand to pull him up, and as water sprang out nearby, they lifted him up and gathered around, saying, "You are a good person." Zhu Tai thanked them, saying, "I dare not sit in the presence of the commander, but you have saved my life. How can I repay your kindness?" Soon, someone came and said, "A strong man fell into the water and drowned, still holding his umbrella." Before they could exchange pleasantries, he woke up. On the twenty-fifth night after midnight, he suddenly felt his limbs becoming weak, his throat constricted, unable to speak, only gasping for breath. A yellow-robed official arrived and said, "The Eighth Department of the Eastern Yue."

**The Case of Life and Death: Zheng Chao's Response** Zheng Chao, the commanding officer of Xinzhou, was summoned for the case of life and death. He replied, "I only wish to die and do not care for my wife and children; I will not harbor any resentment." Seeing himself lying on the bed, he pointed and said, "Let him die quickly, do not let him suffer." The man in yellow robes said, "The underworld does not take people this way; it only leads them away. If we were to end their life prematurely, it would violate the heavenly laws." They stayed for a meal's time, then reached out and grasped his throat, feeling like pulling a newly forged iron piece from fire and quenching it in a water basin. They held a rope to bind Zheng Chao, but he said, "No need for that, I will not run away to the ends of the earth." He followed the messenger, and the man said, "It is right according to reason," and they continued on their way. Soon, they arrived at the eighth office under the mountain and entered the hall. On the dais, they sang out, "Bring in Zheng Chao, the commanding officer of Xinzhou." When he first left home, his body suddenly grew large like a guardian deity at the temple gate, startling him with its strangeness. By then, he had shrunk to the size of a tea saucer. The judge asked, "What scriptures did you read in the mortal world?" He replied that he often recited the Diamond Sutra. As soon as he finished speaking, golden light emerged and illuminated everything, like the sun's light surrounding everything, with ten thousand ghosts raising their fists and praising. The judge called the official in charge of merit and presented the case, writing the judgment on two lacquered boards. He ordered the official to show the judgment to Zheng Chao, roughly like this.

The text is completely incomprehensible, yet it is said that according to Zheng Chao, there should be a record of past offenses and sins that are all forgiven. Officials guided him to rest on the left side, and from morning until noon, the official ascended the hall again. The official then decided to extend the lifespan by one and a half decades and said to Chao, "I am the Duke of Dongping, the Righteous and Impartial Judge of Human Life and Death. You may speak of this in the world without hindrance." Chao replied, "I will certainly not speak of it in the world, for I fear the leakage of heavenly secrets." The official said, "Just speak truthfully and do not say anything false." The official then ordered the gatekeeper to release him. Upon reaching the gate, two officials stood on either side, wearing formal attire, each wrote a character in the air and blew it into Chao's body. Then, they took a small red container of medicine and sprinkled it on his abdomen, saying, "Let you return from here and you can eat and drink; all idle spirits and ghosts will not dare to invade you." The official released him, and he walked down steps, and woke up feeling cold. His wife and children were asleep, and he called out to them, saying that the Holy King had released him back. He asked his wife to bring him a cup of Ophiopogon water, which he drank and felt a fragrant aroma; he then asked for porridge, and the next day he was well. Chao detailed what he saw in a written account, distributed it at various gates and inns, totaling two thousand words, and summarized the key points here.

The Ancient Tomb at Tiesuo Temple Shí Jùn, the commander of the central army in Jiankang, planned to build a residence in the twenty-fourth year of the Shaoxing era. A fortune-teller advised him that the area near the southern outer region of the city, adjacent to the military training ground and behind Tiesuo Temple, had a winding and twisting mountain range with excellent feng shui, making it suitable for constructing a grand mansion. Shí Jùn followed this advice and ordered workers to prepare the site. They dug about a zhang (approximately 3 meters) into the high slope and uncovered an ancient mound, which turned out to be a tomb from several thousand years ago. Inside the tomb, there were no unusual objects, but a spacious stone chamber remained, measuring about 30 meters in diameter. Inside the chamber, they found a single human shinbone, 4 chi (approximately 1.33 meters) long, and a long iron sword, 6 chi (approximately 2 meters) long. Both the shinbone and the sword were perforated by corrosion. They also discovered a large copper basin, almost the size of a plate, and a large jar filled with oil, which was half-empty. When lit, the oil burned brightly, but it was extinguished by the wind. The wooden beams and rafters in the chamber were completely white, and the stone layers were stacked neatly, all appearing as new. Shí Jùn had everything removed and used the stones to construct the foundation of his new residence. He melted down the iron sword to make weapons for his troops and found many stone vessels, which he stored in boxes. He kept the shinbone and showed it to the commander Wang Quan, who often displayed it to his guests. Scholars recognized the shinbone as belonging to a descendant of Fangfeng. About six or seven years later, Shí Jùn achieved significant military success at Caishi, serving as the military commander of Chizhou, deputy governor of Jiangxi, and eventually reaching the rank of Four Ambassadors of Pacification. He later built another residence in Yuzhang.

**Translation:** The Two Dragons of Cangling In the midst of countless mountains lies Xianju County in Taizhou, where a majestic range stands tall and imposing in the southwest, known as Cangling. To the east, it borders Wen, and to the west, it reaches Dushu. The northern slopes are divided, with the southern part belonging to Kuoch'ang. The unique and extraordinary cliffs and valleys, as well as the deep and lush forests and springs, are all gathered here. Ascending along the northern slope, one reaches the midpoint of the mountain, where a depression forms two ponds, separated by three miles. These ponds are deep beyond measurement, and it is said that dragons dwell within. Those who pray for rain during drought will surely be answered. In the autumn of the fourteenth year of the Ch'unch'i era (1187 AD), the two Zhe regions suffered from severe drought. An imperial decree was issued to the prefectural governors, instructing them to pay homage to famous mountains and rivers to pray for rain. The county magistrate Su Guangting led the local officials and people to fast and stay overnight at the edge of the pond. The higher places were steep and rugged, with no path, and one had to climb rocks and pull vines to reach them. Holding a letter, they imitated the common practice of presenting credentials and threw it into the pond. Soon, a creature emerged, winding its way out—a black dragon and a yellow dragon, circling and bowing their heads, seemingly coming together. Just as they were about to bow and greet, they immediately jumped back into the water. They then welcomed them to the high pavilion below the pond, where they set up incense.

A tea, fruit, and food offering accompanied by the chanting of Buddhist scriptures was completed according to ritual. The night clock had not yet run out ten ticks, and the stars shone brightly. At dawn, clouds suddenly gathered as Su descended the mountain. Rain followed a slight clearing, and there was again a cloud rising from the southeast, pouring for three days, thoroughly soaking the entire area. It was the twenty-sixth day of the seventh month. Before this, Su had a dream of a divine person who said his surname was Cao, carrying two small snakes, crossing the stream and going down, letting them loose on the flat fields, they followed one another up. The color of the dragon, the direction of the clouds, the condition of clearing and then raining again, all matched the dream, but Su did not understand the words of the divine Cao. An old man living in the mountains said that this pool was formerly called Cao Pool because its shape resembled it. Is this it? Su thought of the miraculous response, wishing for future generations to always respect and honor it. Therefore, he donated public money to build ten rooms facing the pool, greatly repairing the incense and fire offerings. Professor Lu Jun of Taizhou wrote an inscription and carved it on stone. Su, now the Tongpan of Wufei Army, showed it to me. --- **黄教授后身** **黄唐佐字尧臣福州人登绍兴四年进士科绍兴乙丑终于奉议郎某州教授其妻王氏悲痛不能释明年二月梦之如生时与之语曰我已在闽清县药山陈五君家出世无用忆我觉以告从子相乡尉楷楷曰楷知彼处有药山但不审所谓陈五君者何等人且居何地当即往访求既至果得其家先折简致问五君不答楷具昨梦因依纳谒乃报云吾儿妇以二月懐姙曽梦** **Translation:** **The Reincarnation of Professor Huang** **Huang Tangzuo, courtesy name Yaochen, was from Fuzhou. He passed the imperial examination in the fourth year of the Shaoxing era and died in the year of the Gengchen (1160) as a Fellow of the Imperial Academy in a certain state. His wife, Lady Wang, was so grief-stricken that she could not bear to let go. The following February, she dreamed of him as if he were alive, and he spoke to her, saying, "I have been reborn in the home of Chen Wujun in Yao Mountain, Minqing County. There is no need to remember me." Upon waking, she told her nephew, Xiang, who was the county magistrate, about the dream. Xiang said, "I know there is a Yao Mountain in that place, but I do not know who Chen Wujun is, what kind of person he is, or where he lives. I should go to visit and inquire." Upon arriving, he indeed found Chen Wujun's family. He first sent a letter asking about Chen Wujun, who did not respond. Xiang then recounted his dream and visited Chen Wujun, who replied, "My daughter-in-law was pregnant in February and had a dream."**

A minor official came and said that he was Professor Huang and would be the guardian of your son. After reviewing the discussion, I found a mysterious coincidence, suggesting a good omen. Kai expressed deep gratitude and requested immediate notification of the birth of the child. The child was born on December 21st at dawn, and the mother gave birth to a male child. The child was named Wanqing and given the courtesy name Mengying to signify the auspicious sign. A letter was sent to Kai, who promptly visited the newborn, and the child greeted him with a smile. As the child grew up, he became known for his excellent studies and was recommended for the provincial exams in 1184. However, he faced setbacks for twenty years before finally passing the exams in 1203 and being appointed as a magistrate in Xinghua. His younger brother, Dayou, wrote a detailed account of the events and shared it with others. Regarding the mole-rat and ant-tiger, the mole-rat is recorded in the *Spring and Autumn Annals* as a pest to oxen, but such occurrences are rare in later records. However, in recent years, we have encountered one in our village. A family named Sun had a yellow ox in their pen that refused to eat or move. Upon inspection, it was found that the ox's skin and flesh were eaten, creating holes. Two mole-rats were seen, much smaller than usual, jumping around and biting the ox. Despite attempts to drive them away, they could not be removed. The ox eventually died, with its horns worn through and its flesh almost completely gone, leaving only its skeleton. When the ox was being harmed, it seemed not to feel pain but only extreme itching. The ant-tiger, on the other hand, was obtained from Huainan and is much larger than white ants. When placed in a wooden pillar infested with ants, it quickly eliminated them, creating small holes in the ants' heads. Within half a day, the entire colony was eradicated. In Poyang, houses are often made of pine, which attracts ants. There is no effective method to control them, and it is unfortunate that there are not more ant-tigers. These two creatures can be considered novel, but their impact on humans is vastly different.

The Dragon of Taoyuan Pond In the Fangjue Mountain of Deqing, which extends for over a hundred li, there are three ponds where dragons are said to reside. The one in Taoyuan Valley occasionally shows strange phenomena. During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, a local resident named Wu Yanrou and his wife, who had always practiced Buddhism, personally went to the pond. They burned incense and made a request, saying, "It has been a long time since it rained, and the crops are drying up. We wish for a single act of divine grace to bring rain." They circled around all sides of the pond and devoutly recited scriptures and mantras. A short while later, a small green snake emerged from the water. Soon, it transformed into a giant carp, and then into a catfish with a snake's head, floating freely and at ease. Wu prayed, "A divine dragon can bring rain to save the crops. We should build a temple with family wealth to ensure the local customs of offering incense and sacrifices continue forever." The dragon then revealed its double horns and stood firmly. Not long after, heavy rain poured down for the whole day and then stopped. The entire county relied on this to have a good harvest. Wu was not very wealthy and only had a thousand acres of land, which he divided into three parts.

A Man and His Wife, Selling One Child for Construction Costs In ancient times, a man had two sons and sold one of them as a laborer for construction costs. After the temple was completed, the couple abandoned their home and moved to live by the temple gate, personally performing the duties of sweeping and cleaning. The spirit of the dragon became increasingly mysterious, and people sometimes drew water from the pond and placed it in a basin, where small fish were abundant and countless. When the water was returned to the pond, not a single fish remained. Paper money was thrown into the water, some sinking and some floating. According to local folklore, those that sank were accepted by the deity, while those that floated were not. If the offering did not please the deity, even if it was tied with a stone, it would still crack and float to the surface. Wu's wife arrived there and after a year, she sat upright and passed away. Wu lived alone, and thieves took advantage of his absence to steal his clothes and belongings and carry them down the mountain. He heard two strong men chasing behind and shouting, "These are Wu's possessions; how dare you steal them? Quickly return them to their original place, and I will let you go." The thief regretted and feared as commanded, and from then on, there were no more thieves. In the year of Shaoxi, during the drought, famine-stricken people entered the mountains to dig for fern roots, suffering greatly from the lack of breakfast. Wu cooked rice into porridge every day to feed them, providing a continuous supply. He worried that it would not last, but as soon as his reserves were depleted, outsiders would come to help. If there were those who followed the right path, they would eventually complete the task. Wu still lives today, and the village society calls him the Twenty-One Old Man.

A Daoist from Hedong In the year 1127, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, a Daoist from Hedong came to visit Money Gongzai, who was in charge of Chang'an. They had a long-standing relationship. Money asked why he came, and the Daoist replied that he originally lived in a certain county, but the atmosphere was extremely bad, and the people of the county would suffer severe disasters if they did not leave. He added that at that time, the troubles of the Jin were just beginning, and he intended to move to avoid the danger. A month later, a report from a neighboring prefecture came, saying that a county had experienced a daytime ground collapse, and all the residents were buried. Money was amazed by the Daoist's foresight and wanted to call him and send him somewhere to avoid the disaster. However, by the time he tried to reach him, it was from sunset to dawn, and the shopkeeper said the Daoist's room was right under the store wall, and last night, after half the wall suddenly collapsed, he was buried in the soil. They had to wait until reporting to the authorities before they could dig him out. Money was greatly amazed and said that this person could foresee the disaster in advance but could not predict the outcome. Is it that fate is already determined, and even with intelligence and calculation, one cannot escape?

**Title:** Qian's Trained Wolf **Text:** Qian Zhongben, while serving as a Judge of Dali, had a servant who spent 500 coins to buy a trained wolf at the market. The wolf would often take food from people's hands and playfully disturb them nearby, as if it were a well-trained pet. Once, a cat approached the wolf, intending to attack it. The wolf leapt forward and met the cat head-on, causing the cat to retreat in fear. From that day on, the cat dared not approach the wolf again. The wolf, regardless of size or distance, would pursue and capture its prey, always tracking it to its den and capturing it. Official residences often used pine boards to cover the ground to prevent animals from digging. Any creature that was bitten through by the wolf and passed by would also be searched and captured. Within several months, the wolf's pack was almost completely eradicated. A neighboring official named Zhu, whose servant raised several chickens, was once careless in his vigilance, and at midnight, a creature climbed onto the back of one of the chickens and pecked at it. The servant, upon hearing the noise, jumped up and made a sound, only to find that the creature had already died. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be the trained wolf. The servant took the opportunity to capture and kill it, stripping its skin and nailing it to the wall. Qian Zhongben lamented the loss of this fierce animal without end. After a long time, the wolf's ferocity returned to its former state. **Title:** Xu Dalang **Text:** Xu Dalang, a man from the capital, made a living selling noodles but could barely support himself. By the time he was an old man, he began to pay more attention to managing his affairs. He built three mills, bought thirty to forty donkeys, and purchased wheat from neighboring counties, always striving to get as much as possible and never taking a break. In this way, after ten or so years, his family's wealth grew steadily, and he became increasingly prosperous. Every night, he would assign his servants to guard the mills. One night, two young servants fell asleep. In the middle of the night, they heard a call for help. The moonlight was bright, and they could clearly see everything around them. They looked around and found that there was no one nearby. After a while, the sound grew louder, and they listened carefully. It turned out that one of the donkeys had stuck its head into the millstone and was speaking in human language. At that moment, all the other donkeys were resting in the stable, and none were in the mill room. The millstone was narrow and could not accommodate the donkey's head, which was extremely unusual.

### Translation I was too afraid to ask, trembling until dawn, and ran to tell the owner, saying, "A monster has entered the room, and it is no longer safe to go there." The owner asked about the reason and laughed, saying, "You are delirious and talking nonsense. How can there be such a thing? I will verify it myself." Until night, I went there alone to stay, and immediately heard someone calling "Big Brother" three times. I got up and scolded, saying, "You beast, what kind of monster are you?" The donkey replied, "Well, let's just end it." I then scolded again, saying, "You beast, if you are here, stay here, but why scare me? I am not afraid of you." Then it became silent and had no further effect. By the next day, all the grinding stones were cracked in the middle like they had been cut, and they could no longer be used, so the livelihood gradually declined. Xu also died. His son, being a good archer, enlisted as a palace guard. His grandson, through a transfer, was appointed to an official position. In the early years of the Qianyuan era, he became the commander of the patrol in Rao and Xinzhou. --- ### 许客还债 许元惠是乐平的一位乡绅，他的父亲梦见一位穿着黑衣的客人来对他说：“我昨天借了您三百钱，今天来归还。”还没来得及问他是谁以及何时借的，就醒了。第二天回想起来，却完全不明白。平时家里养了十多只鸭子，那天回来时，发现有一只黑色的鸭子在外面，小童以为是别人家的鸭子，把它赶了出来。鸭子绕着圈儿歇息在旁边，掉下一颗蛋就离开了。从此以后，一个月里每天都是这样，总共下了三十颗蛋，然后就不再来了。最终不知道是谁家的鸭子，计算这些蛋的价值正好是三百钱。

### Translation #### Cheng Didigong's Blindness Cheng Juedi Gong, from Hangqiao, LePing, was a man of letters named Le Dao. He lived a life of diligent and painful study, repeatedly taking the imperial examination for the position of Jinshi (advanced scholar). He failed four times in the Ministry of Rites' examination but was later granted a special imperial favor to become an assistant teacher in a state. However, he did not accept the position. During the grand imperial pardon of Emperor Shaoxi in the year Jia Yin (1194), he was selected in the official examination of the year Bing Mao (1195) during the reign of Emperor Qingyuan. He was then transferred to oversee the wine tax in Ezhou (Hubei). After receiving the order, he returned home. On the way back, he stayed at an inn and was in the middle of sleep one night when he heard an unusual sound rising from inside his right ear. The sound was like thunder, and he woke up in shock. His black eyeball had already burst, and clear fluid flowed and filled the room, though he did not feel much pain. By morning, his eye had dried up. The inn's guests said that he must have seen something extraordinary in his dream, but Cheng did not speak of it to anyone. #### Lu Daogui Lu Daogui was a woman from Jinling who, since childhood, liked to recite Buddhist scriptures. She left home and became a nun at Bai Zhang Mountain. After becoming a nun, she returned to secular life and married. She had a son, and her husband went out to do business for many years without any news. She entrusted her son to a relative and, dressed in a cloth robe and straw shoes, went alone to another place to search for her husband. On the road, she met a monk and asked about his destination, telling him everything. The monk said, "Your husband has been dead for a long time. There is no need to go." She hesitated and doubted, but she was already on the road and continued as before. The monk strongly urged her to return and still asked for travel expenses. She had only three thousand coins and feared he would take all of it, so she gave him most of it. She realized she would not have enough to sustain herself and also returned. After one day, she saw the monk again. The monk said, "The remaining money should be given to me entirely." She emptied her purse, and the monk gave her the fan he held as a gift, saying, "My fan is not ordinary. If you meet someone who is sick, wave it at them, and they will be cured without taking medicine." He then left. A family happened to hear of an epidemic, and those who were ill and lying in bed recovered. As soon as she left the door, the monk was there again. She angrily said, "I taught you to heal people's illnesses, not to heal their lives." Many people fell ill due to the monk's actions.

**Cheng Digu Loses His Sight** Cheng Jue Di Gong, a man from Hangqiao, LePing, was known by the courtesy name Le Dao. Throughout his life, he diligently studied the classics and was repeatedly nominated as a jinshi (imperial scholar-official), but he failed four times in the Ministry of Rites examination. He was later granted a special imperial favor to serve as an assistant teacher in a state school, but he declined the post. During the year Jiayin of the Shaoxi era, when the emperor ascended the throne and issued a great imperial decree, he was appointed to official service. In the year Yimao of the Qingyuan era, he passed the铨试 (civil service examination) and was assigned to supervise the wine administration of E Prefecture. After accepting the appointment, he returned home, but before reaching halfway, he stopped at an inn. In the middle of the night, while he was sleeping, he heard a strange sound coming from his right ear, a sound like thunder. He was startled awake, and upon opening his eyes, he found that his black pupils had burst open, with clear fluid flowing and filling the mat. Though it was not very painful, by morning his eyes had completely dried up. Those around him believed that he must have seen something in his dream, but Cheng never spoke of the strange occurrence. **Lu Daogu** Lu Daogu was a woman from Jinling. From a young age, she loved reciting Buddhist scriptures and became a nun at Bai Zhang Mountain. Later, after childhood, she returned to lay life and married a husband, with whom she had a son. Her husband went out to do business and for many years there was no news of him. Lu entrusted her son to relatives and, wearing a coarse robe and straw sandals, went alone to another place to search for her husband. On the road, she met a monk and asked him where she was going. She told him everything. The monk said, "Your husband has been dead for a long time. There is no need for you to go on." Lu was doubtful and continued on her way as before. The monk insisted that she return and asked for travel expenses. Lu had only three thousand coins with her, and fearing his violence, she gave him most of it. As she traveled, she had no means to support herself and so returned. A day later, she met the monk again. He said, "The money I had yesterday should be given entirely to me." She emptied her purse for him. As a gift, the monk gave her the fan he was holding and said, "My fan is not ordinary. When someone is sick, if you wave it over them, they can recover without taking medicine." Then he left. As he passed by a house, they just heard that there was a plague. Those who were sick and lying in bed under the fan all stood up. As soon as Lu stepped out of the house, the monk was there again and angrily said, "I taught you to cure illnesses, not to cure lives. These people were suffering from plague."

All is in accordance with heavenly will; how can it be disobeyed? They commanded with a shout to return, and the family returned to fan the affected person. All who rose fell again, so they took the original fan and kept it, saying, "From now on, only use the hand to fan it; exhale and blow on it, and it is sufficient." After returning to their hometown, the deaf, blind, lame, and crippled flocked to their residence. Those who relied on it for recovery were about seven or eight out of ten. In the first month of the ninth month of the first year of the Jingyuan era, they came to Xin'an, about ten miles from the city, and found Shiyi Mountain. They quickly built a stone room to live in, and those who heard of it came in succession, numbering daily in the hundreds. Yu Chiguo, a scholar from Dexing, married the daughter of Hong Yingxian. Yu Chiguo participated in the local examination of the Renzi year, and guests came to congratulate him. Until winter, it did not cease. Hong went to the kitchen to see the food, fell, and injured his foot, with the tendons contracting and unable to be stretched. He was treated for three years without effect, so he went to visit his aunt. The aunt welcomed him with joy and spoke with him, saying, "The lady has a good heart, so she should have no suffering." She fed her tea, fruits, and rice, all of which she took first and blew on. In a short while, she stood up as if she had never been ill, without the use of medicine, acupuncture, or moxibustion. She gave her money as a gift, but she smiled and did not accept. Holding money and rice to give to others, the number increased gradually, and they parted.

A man from the Yu family exerted great effort to engage in affairs and intended to build a Buddhist hall. He ascended from the foot of the mountain to its peak, climbing along the steep and rugged terrain, which was extremely difficult. The workers transported timber and stones, as if there were divine protection. Wealthy individuals with significant social status began to harbor evil thoughts and wanted to go and question and challenge him. Before reaching his seat, they saw two dragons coiling around on his side, with radiant and terrifying appearances. They immediately felt remorse and fear and performed a ritual, willing to donate one hundred and sixty thousand coins to carve a Buddha statue. The man firmly refused and did not agree. He said, "If you really want it, do not use the craftsmen from Wuyuan." Zhu Su, who was friendly with Tang, eventually accepted their workers. After more than a month, the work was completed, and a hundred workers were gathered to carry the statue up the mountain. Tang was angry about the man's previous words and hosted a banquet, getting drunk and saying disrespectful words. Soon, a strong wind arose from all sides, and sand and stones flew everywhere. The bearers fell down one after another, and for a whole day, they could not proceed. From then on, outsiders who came to visit could see that they were not good people. Describing his usual conduct without any concealment, the man was about fifty years old and often said, "I have taken a vow and will leave after nineteen years." It is unknown what will happen in the end.

**Lu Jiuling Passes the Imperial Examination** A scholar from Pingyang, Zhou Xiuzai, dreamed that someone told him, "You will pass the examination." The person took out the list of successful candidates from his sleeve and showed it to him. After reviewing the entire list, he could not find his name. The person pointed to Jiuling and said, "This is you." Upon waking up, he did not believe it, but he pondered the meaning and could not let it go. His heart suddenly became clear, and he thought, "I should not change my surname; perhaps I can take 'Jiuling' as my name." Those who heard this laughed at him. Indeed, in the eighth year of the Qian Dao era (1172), he was selected for a high position on the Huang Ding list. **An Auspicious Omen in Xiangxiang** Wang Nanqiang, originally named Wuchun, was from Xiangxiang, Hunan. In the year of Ren Yin (1172), he studied at the Yuelu Academy. He once had a minor dispute with his classmates, but later regretted it and decided to change his name to reflect the virtue of self-discipline and restraint. His brothers also supported this decision, and he changed his name to Rongzhi, taking the meaning of "teaching with kindness and gentleness" and avoiding unorthodox teachings. In the spring of the year of Gui Mao (1173), he was waiting at the academy for the provincial examination. His brother went to the county to submit the family guarantee form, and just a few days before the exam, when he was about to submit his examination paper, he saw that the name on the county's submission list was Wang Rong, which made him doubt. His brother came and said, "I have changed my name to Rong, so you don't need to use two names. I have already removed the character 'Nan' from your name. This is you." So he used this name to take the exam. This time, he was recommended, and after the provincial examination, he heard that his brother had passed away and returned home. When he arrived, the announcement of the results came, and he had been named. His old teachers, Shuyi and Zhou Ren, came to congratulate him. Two years earlier, a fortune-teller had come to Xiangxiang and visited the county school, saying that he could predict the future by examining the image of the master and knew that the scholar would pass the examination.

The number of successful candidates in the imperial examination is such that the sacred image smiles, and two scholars have passed. Such an event is rare, with only one person succeeding, but there will be more successors. Last spring, the head of the school, Wang Zhongyan (Wang Fensou), personally wrote a couplet on peach wood, saying that people are discussing the good fortune of the "plain king" and that the top scholars will pass first. I have often thought about it. The "king" is a title, and "颜" (face) is part of the name, which is the name of the "plain king" during the period of mourning. This year, due to an obstacle in the imperial examination, the top scholars will still pass in the next attempt, bringing good news. This idea has spread among scholars. In the spring of "乙巳," during a supplementary examination at the county school, Wang Renbo changed his name to "颜" and became the top candidate. In the spring of "丙午," Shu Zhouren entered the government and spoke to Nanqiang, saying that Wang Fensou wrote another couplet on peach wood, which is even more surprising, with the "plain king" smiling and the top scholars coming in great numbers. After writing the couplet, Wang Fensou suddenly realized that what he wrote was a premonition for both Nanqiang and Wang Renbo. I, however, do not agree with this interpretation.

Nanqiang Xianfang, a man of three prominent names, was once a champion in the imperial examinations, achieving a high rank that he was very satisfied with. However, he ultimately failed. Nanqiang Xianfang indeed became the champion of the world. The practitioner of a certain skill did not return, and it is regrettable that his hometown and name are not recorded. There is an ancient saying in Changsha that a top scholar would emerge from the breaking of a mountain shaped like a camel's snout. The camel's snout is a mountain, and its shape resembles a camel's snout, located north of the city, directly at the water mouth. Below it is called Ma Tan, where all are huge stones standing upright. In the seventh year of Chunxi (1178), Mou You'an served as the governor and established the Flying Tiger Camp, widely opening up streets and paths, allowing monks and civilians to redeem their sins with stones. All were carved from the stones taken from the pond, too many to count. Later, General Lin Huangzhong further developed the southern street, taking more and more stones. By the summer of Bingwu (1186), the camel's snout was broken into two parts, and within a year, Nanqiang Xianfang responded to it. The peachwood charm evidence is already recorded in the Gui Records. From this, I learned the beginning and end of the story, and realized that the previous records were incomplete. Therefore, I record it again here, and since the Gui Records have already been published in the Mashasha bookshop, they cannot be removed.

In the year 1183, during the late winter of the Chongning era, Wang Nanzhang traveled from Changsha to take the provincial examination. On his way, he passed through Yuanzhou and prayed at the Yangshan Palace. That night, he stayed at a guesthouse in Xinshi Village, east of the city. In his dream, he heard someone singing a poem titled "Yulou Chun," which went: "The fragrance of flowers will be warm as you depart from the jade hall, Just as the willow fluff is flying and fluttering in front of the horse, The moon goddess has cut green cloud-like clothes, Awaiting your arrival at the moon palace to exchange." Only half of the poem was sung before it stopped. Then, another person in white clothes, riding a horse, came from afar, dismounted, bowed to Wang, and stood talking. He said, "This morning, I visited you and then rode away again." Wang sent him off for about half a mile when another person passed by and said, "This is a person from the Yangshan Temple." Wang woke up in surprise, realizing that the statue of the deity in the temple was indeed dressed in white robes. The following year, Wang submitted his name for the exam but did not take it due to his brother's death. The meaning of the unfinished song was verified. In the first month of the 14th year, he went to take the imperial examination and prayed at Yuan. In his dream, he drank with a friend named Sun in Luxi Market. Sun said, "You drink with me."

A Person Ranked Second but Not a King The king said, "I never expected to be second." Sun replied hastily, "I only wish you could become the top scholar." Then he woke up and saw an idol under the temple eaves, wearing a monk's hat, called the "one who answers dreams." Sun was born bald, with a completely monk-like appearance. Therefore, the god used that person to convey a message: In the first year of spring during the Shaoxing era, the king went to the eastern command post, visited the temple, and prepared sacrificial meat and wine to offer thanks at the offering pavilion. In a dream, he saw a deity drinking at the top, bowing and making him sit in the guest position, with several guests sitting. The wind was cold and solemn, dark like dusk, and still not setting up lamps or candles. Feeling his hair stand on end, he could not identify who was sitting next to him. He heard a stern voice from the hall saying, "Why are you late?" Before he could answer, he said, "Hurry up, hurry up." Awakening in a daze, the king should have taken office in April of last year but was delayed for a month because of family matters. Finally, he took office, but after only two months, he was summoned to the imperial library. This is evidence of the 'slow-fast' two words.

**The Two Scholars Huang and Dai** In the prefecture of Putian, there were two scholars, Huang Shang with the courtesy name Bo Hua, and his friend Dai Song. Both were born at the hour of Yin on a certain day in the year of the rabbit during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, and they lived in the same prefecture. As children, they were close friends. When they grew up, both became famous for their studies. A guest skilled in astrology was invited by the two scholars to determine their fates and compare their destinies. After asking about their family backgrounds and life experiences, the guest concluded that their fates were largely similar. However, he noted that Huang was born at the exact hour of Yin, while Dai's birth time was close to but not exactly at the hour of Yin, suggesting that Dai's time was closer to the hour of Chou. The guest predicted that Dai's rise to prominence would come later. Later, the guest told others that their moral character was extremely low, and even though Huang was superior, it was still not worth mentioning. Subsequently, Dai was recommended for an examination but did not pass before turning fifty and died. Huang, on the other hand, entered the Imperial Academy and was selected for official service. In the year 1182 (the year of Ren Yin during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song), Huang was exempted from the provincial examination and returned to Fujian to serve as an official. Upon hearing of Dai's death, Huang was greatly alarmed. Huang, a lecturer in Fuzhou, was appointed as the governor of Chaozhou. He visited Chaozhou and drank excessively. In the middle of the night, he contracted a severe illness due to excessive indulgence. The date for the imperial examination in 1194 (the year of Jia Chen) was approaching, so he was forced to go to the county seat despite being ill. Local people bribed the guards and gatekeepers to allow him to enter the examination hall, where he was carried by three or four people, limping, to the hall. When his name was called, he was still carried in the same manner. Although he was fortunate to be listed in the fourth rank, he could not participate in the selection process and instead sought a position in a temple. After completing his term, he did not recover and died after serving in three positions. He was only fifty-eight years old. Although he passed the imperial examination and held an official position, compared to Dai, who died young, Huang's life was less fortunate. His disability and near-death experience truly made his life unworthy of mention. **The Chicken Struck by Lightning** On June 8, 1196, during the reign of Emperor Ningzong of Song, a great thunderbolt struck in Raozhou. At the Shuanggang military camp, Zhang Fa, a military officer, had raised a rooster. He intended to use it as a sacrificial animal for the Dragon Hall. On that day, the rooster was struck by lightning. It was first cut at the foot and then split from head to tail. The chicken's offense against the divine is impossible to ask about, but the thunderbolt used its power lightly and was not severe.

Xu Zijiao Xu Zijiao, a poor scholar from Nankang Dàyǔ, visited Baoji Temple in the eighth year of the Qian Dao era. He stayed overnight at the temple and there happened to be a physician named Liu Dayong who was sleeping in the front room. Xu stayed in the main hall and kept making startling noises in his sleep during the night. Liu came out and called to him, and the monk also came. Xu then woke up and said to the others, "There was something very heavy that climbed onto my bed, pressing down on my stomach. It felt cold as ice, and in the darkness, I could barely see any hands or feet. I was so exhausted that I couldn't resist it until I heard you all coming, and then it left." The monk said, "This is a huge stone behind the temple. It often appears with strange phenomena and causes trouble for anyone who stays overnight. No one has been able to sleep soundly because of its weight and stubborn nature. It is difficult to remove, so we just leave it alone." Xu sat up until dawn, hurriedly left the temple, and never dared to return. Someone said, "The stone demon is so powerful that unless the corners of the stone are chiseled off, it will continue to cause trouble." The monk, lacking the strength to do so, refused to take action. Xie Jun, the Preserver of Loyalty Xie Jun, a preserver of loyalty and a descendant of a former military commander from Jingnan, was a military officer in charge of the Nan'an army in the seventh year of the Qian Dao era. There was a guest who was about to arrive, and the county magistrate was going to meet the guest at Baoji Temple. Xie was in charge of the arrangements. By evening, the guest had not arrived, so they stayed overnight. Just past midnight, the candle had not yet gone out, and a woman suddenly came, moving gracefully and seductively. Her appearance was very beautiful. Half drunk, Xie offered some words of flirtation, and she laughed happily and said, "I came here precisely to be with you and to establish a close relationship." She then climbed onto the bed and asked him about his name and where he lived. He replied, "Don't say too much; I live behind the temple. Can you still make it here tomorrow night?" Xie was especially pleased and said, "I will keep my promise." From then on, he came every day and also borrowed a room from the temple monk for long-term accommodation. After more than a month, the monk did not suspect anything, and no one else knew about it. Xie often gave her gifts of gold, silver, hairpins, and earrings. He gained both beauty and wealth and was very happy, beyond his expectations.

He said to him, "I have not yet married. I wish to send a matchmaker to your house with gifts to marry you. What do you think?" The woman replied, "My father holds a high official rank. How could he agree to marry me off? But if we just continue as we are, it is sufficient." Jun trusted it to be true, but his energy and strength gradually declined. The medicine seller Liu Dayong lived with him and was also surprised by him. Jun did not tell him about it. Once, the two of them went out of the city together and encountered a man selling talismans and water by the roadside. He pulled Liu's ear and whispered to him, "How can that official carry a dead ghost with him? He will die within three months." Liu told Jun, who at first still denied it. Then he suddenly realized, "How did he know that there must be something strange?" So he dragged Liu to a guesthouse. The man laughed and said, "Would officials seek me out? Otherwise, it would nearly ruin my life." He left him to stay in the same guesthouse but in a different room, and looked at Liu and let him stay together. He burned ten or more pieces of paper talisman and had Jun swallow them. Liu peeked at him secretly and saw him perform a ritual and scold. Around the second watch of the night, they heard the sound of a woman crying outside the door. It was silent until the third watch. The next morning, Jun left and warned him not to go again. Later, the monks in the temple learned about the matter and said, "There were no such spirits or ghosts in the temple. Last year, when Vice Minister Zhao Hongyuan was exiled, he lost a daughter. She was buried behind the wall. It must be this." From then on, it often appeared and troubled the monks, greatly troubling them. They sent a servant to Wu Ling to report to Zhao and request a reburial. Zhao agreed and then buried it on the side of a field five li north of the city gate. It appeared again and troubled the residents, so they moved it to a deep mountain. The ghost finally ceased. As recorded in the *Jia Zhi*, the daughter of the governor Zhang was a ghost in Jiaoyu Temple in Nan'an, troubling the grandson of Xie Qin. It is very similar to this. The two cases are about thirteen to fourteen years apart, and both are sons of the Xie family. It is suspected that it is the same event with different accounts, but the doctor Liu claimed to have seen it. One should verify it with the people there.

**Translation:** The Magistrate of Longyang County In the aftermath of the ravages of bandits, the villages and towns of Longyang County in Ding Prefecture lay desolate, with few residents. Strange and supernatural phenomena appeared and disappeared, even manifesting in broad daylight. In the first year of the Qingyuan era, a man from Wu named Zhang came to serve as magistrate. He took office for a short time but soon seemed as if he were infected or attached to something mysterious. Every day after his official duties, he would order officials to set up a room in the west wing of the hall, hang heavy curtains and multiple layers of screens, and have it appear completely dark. No one was allowed to peek in, and he would prepare food for two people with chopsticks but no guests would enter. Only laughter and conversation could be heard, and when wine and roasted meat arrived, he would take them out himself. This continued until evening when it ended. His wife and children asked him, but he did not answer. His appearance became increasingly gaunt and haggard, and by winter he had passed away. After the funeral, On the day of the burial, a stable hand dreamed of him coming to the stable and telling him the whip was in the main hall but could not be found. Zhang said, "I took it myself." That night, the whole family dreamed of him entering the room and taking the whip with him. The next morning, the horse he had raised died without reason, and the whip disappeared. Zhang’s son could not bear to skin the horse and had it buried in the garden. Soon after, it also appeared as a ghost, circling the county hall, neighing and jumping. Everything showed signs of change or transformation. After the family had left, my nephew, Yan, became the county magistrate. He had always suffered from blood disease, and from then on, it worsened. Every night he was plagued by ghosts and demons, knocking and banging on doors. His wife advised him to move, but he did not have time to listen. In February of the following year, he finally passed away.

### Final Translation **The Strange Tales of Yi Qianying: Volume Eight** **The Four Treasuries, as Mandated by the Emperor** **The Strange Tales of Yi Qianying: Volume Nine** **Compiled by Hong Mai of the Song Dynasty** **The White Snake of Tongzhou** Since the Yuanfu era, Tongzhou has frequently been plagued by supernatural beings causing harm, all of which were said to be the spirits of white snakes. Officials and civilians often suffered from these calamities, and even the prefectural governor sometimes met untimely deaths due to these strange occurrences. Those who knew about the situation dared not request the position of governor. During the Zhenghe era, a son-in-law of a high-ranking official, eager to gain the generous salary, insisted on taking the post. The chancellor advised him, saying, "The white snake in Fengyi is very malevolent; do not risk your life by accepting this position." Despite the warning, the son-in-law was determined and eventually accepted the appointment. Three days after taking office, he held a grand banquet for the officials. Suddenly, he looked at the entertainers and asked, "I am just beginning to handle affairs and have prepared this feast. You should be dressed in festive attire to celebrate. Why are you all wearing white?" The entertainers, knowing the reason, dared not answer. After the banquet, he fell ill. The next day, he inquired of the guest commander, who replied, "Perhaps your lordship has just arrived and is seeing things incorrectly. There is no one here." The family sent a rider to report this to the chancellor, who then informed Emperor Huizong. The emperor summoned Zhang Tianshi, the Master of the Void and Stillness, to deal with the situation. When Zhang arrived, the son-in-law had disappeared. He had only been in the prefecture for ten days when Zhang summoned all the gods inside and outside the city to ask where the white snake was. None of them could answer. Zhang then called upon the city god, who also claimed not to know. Zhang became furious and severely punished the spirits with the command of the underworld, using all kinds of torture. The spirits pleaded, "That creature is very powerful and wise."

Communicating with heaven and speaking aloud will bring immediate disaster. Zhang said, "My magical power is more than enough to destroy it, but I only want to find its lair. If you do not reveal it, you will be killed first." The god then lowered his head and secretly revealed the location. Zhang chose a day to go there, three miles from the lair, and built a five-tiered altar spanning dozens of zhang. When the altar was completed, all the officials and civilians of the city were gathered and made to stay on top of it while Zhang led the道士 in performing rituals. First, he flew a white符, but there was no response. Next, he flew a red符, followed by a yellow符. After a long time, clouds and wind surged up, and lightning and thunder erupted from all sides. Blue mist and black smoke filled the valleys, and those who saw it were filled with fear. Soon, the smoke dispersed, and Zhang continued his rituals as before. Suddenly, white vapor rose into the sky, sometimes yellow, sometimes purple, for four or five changes. The people on the altar all fell down in fear and cried, waiting to be devoured. Zhang made everyone hold a piece of soil in their mouths to protect against evil forces. He sent someone to fetch the state seal and placed it in front of him, saying to the crowd, "The white snake's spirit is all here. It will surely emerge. If it passes through the five altars, even I will have no more life. If it does not confront me, it will stop at the third layer. Evil cannot overcome righteousness; this city will be safe." Soon, a fierce fire erupted from the hole, gradually reaching the side of the altar. A large snake opened its mouth wide, intending to...

The creature, with its head raised and body coiled, reached up into the sky, looking out over the horizon. It moved in a winding path, close to the ground, drawing its body around the lower levels several times. Standing upright, the figure held a state seal in his left hand and a jade seal in his right, facing the creature. The snake, trembling and hesitant, could not decide whether to advance or retreat, its body gradually lowering as if crushed by a mountain. It rushed to the third level and stopped. Then, a flying sword was launched to kill it. Afterward, many more creatures emerged, the smaller ones resembling pillars or columns, numbering in the tens of thousands. The figure said, "The chief evil lies with the female kind, whose species is indeed numerous. It is difficult to eliminate them all, but they cannot be pardoned. Choose those that are evil and remove them." The elderly and brave, armed with their swords, cut down the pillar-like creatures, more than twenty in total, all of whom were bound by supernatural power and submitted to the sword. The rest were driven out of the area by a talisman given to a divine general. Several days later, the figure led the local people to inspect the den, where they found a stone bed in the center, apparently the creature's resting place. Piles of bones accumulated around it, all the remains of those the creature had devoured, their stench reaching for miles. It took a month for the foul odor to dissipate. The figure was a hermit, the thirty-third generation descendant of the Han Celestial Master, who never married. As the capital was about to fall, he secretly left the city and returned to his hometown, undergoing a spiritual transformation and hiding in Mount Emei. People in Shu sometimes saw him, but the direct lineage of the Celestial Master ended. Now, his descendants continue the line.

In the second year of the Xuanhe era, a strange and ominous spirit afflicted the granddaughter-in-law of Grand Tutor Cai Jing. She would dress elaborately and sit quietly outdoors every evening as if waiting for someone. Once inside, she would whisper and speak softly to someone, laughing and talking all night, then falling into a deep sleep due to exhaustion. She treated her family members as strangers, completely neglecting food and drink. Cai Jing was very worried and angry, and summoned a Daoist priest from the palace named Bao Lu to treat her. Many famous Daoists and priests from the capital city, numbering in the dozens, all suffered great humiliation and left in a desperate and shameful manner. At that time, Zhang Xujing was in the capital city and secretly reported and summoned him. Just as he arrived at the hall, a ghost howled from the beam. Zhang said, "This demon has immense power; it was born at the time when chaos first separated. It is not easy to quickly eliminate it. Allow two days, and I will secretly perform rituals.
